The Operating System cannot "live" there. 2. **Flashing the OS image** is the equivalent of **delivering a pre-fabricated house (the OS)** onto that surveyed land using a truck, complete with its blueprint. 3. **Booting** is the act of **turning on the lights, opening the front door, and starting the generator (kernel + systemd)** so that the house (OS) becomes a *living, functioning home* for your applications (IoT programs). When you press the power button on a Raspberry Pi, the tiny CPU sends an electrical signal to the SD card, asking: *"Where is my house?"* The bootloader on the SD card then guides the CPU room-by-room (driver-by-driver) until the OS is fully alive. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Engineering Constants The following quantitative parameters govern SD card preparation and OS booting in IoT systems: - **Recommended Minimum SD Card Capacity:** **8 GB** (Class 10 / UHS-I) - **Standard Cluster (Block) Size for FAT32 boot partition:** **4096 bytes (4 KiB)** or **8192 bytes (8 KiB)** - **Raspberry Pi Bootloader Default Read Block Size:** **512 bytes** (legacy) or **4096 bytes** (modern) - **Typical Linux Kernel Image Size (`kernel8.img` for Pi 4/5):** **~6 MB to 12 MB** compressed - **Standard Boot Partition Size for Raspberry Pi OS:** **256 MB** (FAT32) + remaining as ext4 root - **Boot Time Benchmark (Cold Boot, Pi 4, Raspberry Pi OS Lite):** **~15–25 seconds** - **SD Card Speed Class Requirement for OS Booting:** **Class 10 / A1 / V30 minimum** (write ≥ 10 MB/s, sustained random IOPS ≥ 1500) - **Voltage Level for SD Card Interface:** **3.3 V DC** (logic level) - **Default Clock Frequency (SD mode):** **25 MHz** (up to 50 MHz in High-Speed mode) > [!NOTE] > **Why Class 10 matters for Booting:** The kernel, Device Tree, and `initramfs` are read *sequentially* during the first ~3 seconds of boot. A Class 4 card (4 MB/s write) can cause kernel load to exceed the watchdog timeout on some SoCs, leading to a non-booting system. --- ## 1.4 Why This Topic is High-Yield for KTU 2024 > [!IMPORTANT] > Module 1 of PBCIT504 establishes the **physical-layer foundation** of every IoT lab exercise. The KTU 2024 Scheme examiner frequently tests: > - Identification of correct file systems (`FAT32` vs `ext4`) > - Order of the boot sequence (Stage 1 → Stage 2 → Kernel → init) > - Configuration files (`config.txt`, `cmdline.txt`) > - Tools used (Raspberry Pi Imager, `dd` command, balenaEtcher) --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two-Tier Preparation Model An SD card destined for an IoT single-board computer (SBC) undergoes **two distinct preparation phases**: ### Phase A — Formatting (Logical Structuring) This creates an *empty but well-organized* address space. 1. **Physical-Level Erase** — All NAND flash cells are reset to the erased state (all bits = 1, since NAND flash erases to logic high). 2. **Partition Table Creation** — Either **MBR** (Master Boot Record, 512 bytes at sector 0) or **GPT** (GUID Partition Table, 92 bytes header at LBA 1). 3. **File System Initialization** — Writing the superblock, inode tables (for ext4), and FAT tables (for FAT32). 4. **Volume Label Assignment** — E.g., `BOOT`, `RECOVERY`, `rootfs`. ### Phase B — OS Image Flashing (Population) This writes the actual OS files into the partitions. 1. **Image Download** — Acquire a `.img`, `.iso`, or `.zip` file (e.g., `2024-11-19-raspios-bookworm-arm64.img.xz`). 2. **Image Decompression** — `.xz` → `.img` using `xz -d` or `7-Zip`. 3. **Bit-for-Bit Flash** — Write the entire image (partition table + boot partition + root partition) to the SD card using a tool that understands raw block writes. ## 2.2 File System Selection Matrix | File System | Typical Use in IoT | Max File Size | Journaling | OS Native Support | |-------------|---------------------|---------------|------------|-------------------| | **FAT32** | Boot partition (`/boot/firmware`) | 4 GiB − 1 B | No | Universal (Windows, Linux, macOS, SoC ROM) | | **exFAT** | Large media on IoT gateways | 16 EiB | No | Windows/Linux (with `exfat-fuse`) | | **ext4** | Root partition (`/`) | 16 TiB | Yes (metadata) | Linux native | | **squashfs** | Read-only IoT firmware overlays | 16 EiB | No | Linux native | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pearl:** The **boot partition must always be FAT32** for Raspberry Pi 1/2/3/Zero and for most Allwinner / Broadcom SoCs because the on-chip ROM bootloader has *no native ext4 parser*. It only understands FAT16/FAT32 signatures. ## 2.3 The Raspberry Pi Boot Sequence (Stage-by-Stage) This is the **most examined diagram** for IoT Programming courses. | Stage | Executed From | Binary / File | Function | |-------|---------------|---------------|----------| | **Stage 0** | SoC Mask ROM (immutable, in silicon) | ROM code | Looks for `bootcode.bin` on SD card (Pi 1/2/3) or on SPI EEPROM (Pi 4/5) | | **Stage 1** | SDRAM (L2 cache as SRAM) | `bootcode.bin` | Loads `start.elf` into RAM | | **Stage 2** | GPU L2 cache | `start.elf` (`firmware`) | Reads `config.txt`, `cmdline.txt`, loads `kernel.img` and Device Tree Blobs (`*.dtb`, `*.dtbo`) | | **Stage 3** | CPU ARM cores | `kernel8.img` (Pi 4/5, 64-bit) or `kernel7.img` (Pi 3) | Decompresses itself, initializes RAM, mounts rootfs | | **Stage 4** | User space | `/sbin/init` → `systemd` | Starts services, mounts filesystems, reaches login prompt | For Raspberry Pi 4 and 5, Stages 0 and 1 are **merged** because the bootloader resides in an SPI EEPROM on the board itself — the SD card only needs to provide **Stage 2+** (i.e., the `start.elf`, `kernel`, and `config.txt`). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Constant | Description | Unit | |---|--------------------|-------------|------| | 1 | $$C_{\text{usable}} = C_{\text{raw}} \times (1 - O_{\text{overhead}})$$ | Usable capacity after partition + file-system overhead | GB | | 2 | $$O_{\text{overhead}} \approx 0.03 \text{ to } 0.08$$ | Typical FAT32/ext4 metadata overhead (3–8%) | dimensionless | | 3 | $$T_{\text{flash}} = \frac{S_{\text{image}}}{R_{\text{write}}}$$ | Time to flash an image (image size ÷ write rate) | seconds | | 4 | $$R_{\text{write}}^{\min} = \frac{S_{\text{kernel}}}{T_{\text{boot-budget}}}$$ | Minimum SD card write rate for kernel load | MB/s | | 5 | $$N_{\text{cycles}} = 10{,}000 \text{ to } 100{,}000$$ | NAND flash P/E cycles (endurance, SD card) | cycles | | 6 | $$T_{\text{boot}}^{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=0}^{4} T_i$$ | Total cold-boot latency (sum of stage latencies) | seconds | | 7 | $$f_{\text{SD-clock}}^{\max} = 50 \text{ MHz (HS mode)}$$ | SD interface max clock (SDR50) | MHz | | 8 | $$R_{\text{theoretical}}^{\max} = 50 \text{ MB/s}$$ | SD 3.0 UHS-I bus ceiling | MB/s | | 9 | $$\text{Sector size} = 2^9 = 512 \text{ bytes}$$ | Logical block size in MBR | bytes | | 10 | $$\text{Boot sector offset} = 0 \times 0000 \text{ (LBA 0)}$$ | MBR / FAT boot record location | bytes | | 11 | $$\text{ext4 inode ratio} = 1 \text{ inode per } 16{,}384 \text{ bytes}$$ | Default bytes-per-inode on `mkfs.ext4` | bytes/inode | > [!NOTE] > **Pipe-Symbol Safety Note:** In the table above, all conditional magnitudes use the `≈` symbol (not the vertical pipe `\vert`) to maintain markdown table integrity. ## 2.5 Engineering Utility Understanding SD card formatting and OS booting is **foundational** to: - **Edge AI deployment** (loading TensorFlow Lite models on Coral + Pi). - **Headless IoT nodes** (no monitor, so SSH must be enabled at boot). - **Over-The-Air (OTA) firmware updates** via A/B root partition swapping. - **Industrial gateways** running Yocto-built custom Linux distros. - **Reproducible Lab Submissions** for KTU practical examinations (PBCIT504L). --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Usable Capacity After Formatting A student purchases a **32 GB** SD card. Compute the **usable capacity** for the root partition after formatting with `ext4`. **Given:** - $C_{\text{raw}} = 32 \text{ GB}$ - $C_{\text{boot}} = 256 \text{ MB}$ (reserved FAT32 boot partition) - $O_{\text{overhead}} = 0.05$ (5% ext4 metadata overhead) **Step 1:** Convert raw capacity into MiB for partition arithmetic. $$ C_{\text{raw}}^{\text{MiB}} = 32 \times 1000 = 32{,}000 \text{ MB} \quad \text{(using SI definition, since SD card marketing uses SI)} $$ **Step 2:** Subtract boot partition size. $$ C_{\text{root}} = 32{,}000 - 256 = 31{,}744 \text{ MB} $$ **Step 3:** Apply ext4 metadata overhead. $$ C_{\text{usable}} = C_{\text{root}} \times (1 - O_{\text{overhead}}) $$ **Step 4:** Substitute values. $$ \begin{aligned} C_{\text{usable}} &= 31{,}744 \times (1 - 0.05) \\ &= 31{,}744 \times 0.95 \\ &= 30{,}156.8 \text{ MB} \\ &\approx 29.45 \text{ GiB} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 (Sanity check via binary conversion):** $$ 29.45 \text{ GiB} \times 1.0737 \approx 31.62 \text{ GB (decimal)} \quad \checkmark $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** A "32 GB" card yields approximately **29.45 GiB** of usable user space after reserving a boot partition and formatting as ext4. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Image Flashing Time A Raspberry Pi OS image is **1.8 GB** in size. The SD card reader writes at an average of **20 MB/s**. Compute the time to flash. **Step 1:** Apply the flashing-time formula. $$ T_{\text{flash}} = \frac{S_{\text{image}}}{R_{\text{write}}} $$ **Step 2:** Substitute. $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{flash}} &= \frac{1.8 \text{ GB} \times 1024 \text{ MB/GB}}{20 \text{ MB/s}} \\ &= \frac{1843.2 \text{ MB}}{20 \text{ MB/s}} \\ &= 92.16 \text{ seconds} \\ &\approx 1 \text{ minute } 32 \text{ seconds} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 (KTU Practical Insight):** Add a 15% safety margin for filesystem verification: $$ T_{\text{realistic}} = 92.16 \times 1.15 \approx 106 \text{ seconds} $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Boot Time Decomposition A Raspberry Pi 4 cold-boots into the login prompt. Decompose the boot time into stages using the published latency profile. | Stage | Component | Typical Latency | |-------|-----------|-----------------| | $T_0$ | Mask ROM → EEPROM handshake | 0.2 s | | $T_1$ | `start.elf` load from SD | 1.3 s | | $T_2$ | `config.txt` parsing + DT overlay | 0.5 s | | $T_3$ | Kernel decompression + initramfs | 4.0 s | | $T_4$ | systemd services + login target | 8.0 s | **Step 1:** Sum the stages. $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{boot}}^{\text{total}} &= T_0 + T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + T_4 \\ &= 0.2 + 1.3 + 0.5 + 4.0 + 8.0 \\ &= 14.0 \text{ seconds} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 (Critical-path analysis):** The dominant stage is $T_4$ (systemd init) at **8.0 s ≈ 57% of total boot time**. Optimization must target the systemd service graph (mask unused services like `ModemManager`, `avahi-daemon` on headless nodes). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pearl:** The kernel decompression ($T_3$) is **CPU-bound**, not I/O-bound, for most modern Pis. The systemd stage ($T_4$) is **I/O-bound** on slow SD cards. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Tool Selection for Formatting/Flashing | Tool | Platform | Formats SD? | Flashes OS? | Verifies? | KTU-Lab Recommended? | |------|----------|-------------|-------------|-----------|----------------------| | **Raspberry Pi Imager** | Win/Mac/Linux | Yes (auto) | Yes | Yes (sha256) | ✅ **Strongly Yes** | | **balenaEtcher** | Win/Mac/Linux | No (auto) | Yes | Yes | ✅ Yes | | **SD Card Formatter** (SD Assoc.) | Win/Mac | Yes (full/quick) | No | No | ✅ For formatting only | | **`dd` (Linux CLI)** | Linux | Yes (manual) | Yes (raw) | No | ⚠️ Advanced only | | **Win32 Disk Imager** | Windows | No | Yes | No | ❌ Legacy | --- ## 3.5 Step-by-Step Procedure (Lab Notebook Format) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Practical Examination Tip:** The following 6-step procedure frequently appears as a 7-mark short answer. **Step 1 — Acquire the Image:** Download `Raspberry Pi OS Lite (64-bit)` from the official `raspberrypi.com/software` URL. **Step 2 — Verify Checksum:** Compute `sha256sum` and compare with the published value to detect corruption. **Step 3 — Insert SD Card & Launch Imager:** Open Raspberry Pi Imager → Click *Choose OS* → *Choose Storage* → Click the gear icon (⚙) for **OS Customisation** → Enable *SSH* and *Set username/password*. **Step 4 — Write the Image:** Click *Write*. The tool performs a **format → write → verify** cycle. **Step 5 — Safe Eject:** Use the OS's *Safely Remove* feature to prevent filesystem journaling corruption. **Step 6 — First Boot Validation:** Insert the SD card into the Pi, power on, observe the green LED activity, and connect via SSH on the reserved IP. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — SD Card Preparation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw SD Card
32 GB Class 10] --> B[Physical Format
SD Card Formatter] B --> C[Partition Table
MBR or GPT] C --> D[Boot Partition
FAT32 256 MB] C --> E[Root Partition
ext4 Remaining] D --> F[Stage 2 Files
start.elf kernel8.img] E --> G[OS Filesystem
usr etc var home] F --> H[OS Image Flash
Raspberry Pi Imager] G --> H H --> I[Bootable SD Card
Ready for Pi] I --> J[Insert into Raspberry Pi] J --> K[Power On] K --> L[Mask ROM Stage 0] L --> M[Bootloader Stage 1 and 2] M --> N[Kernel Stage 3] N --> O[systemd Stage 4] O --> P[Login Prompt Reached] ``` > [!NOTE] > The above flow renders a **functional block architecture**. Each block represents a transformation state of the SD card or a system state of the booting IoT device. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — The Raspberry Pi 4 Boot Stage State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> PowerOn PowerOn --> MaskROM : Apply 3.3V to SoC MaskROM --> EEPROM : Read SPI EEPROM bootloader EEPROM --> SDRAMInit : Initialize LPDDR4 SDRAMInit --> StartELF : Load start.elf from SD StartELF --> ParseConfig : Read config.txt cmdline.txt ParseConfig --> LoadDTB : Mount Device Tree Blobs LoadDTB --> KernelLoad : Read kernel8.img into RAM KernelLoad --> KernelExec : Decompress and jump to entry KernelExec --> RootMount : Mount ext4 on rootfs RootMount --> SystemdStart : Execute /sbin/init SystemdStart --> LoginPrompt : Reached multi-user target LoginPrompt --> [*] : OS is fully booted ``` --- ## 4.3 Logical Memory Map of a Formatted SD Card ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SECTOR_0["Sector 0 LBA 0"] MBR["MBR 512 bytes
Partition Table"] end subgraph SECTOR_1["Sector 1 to 524287"] BOOT["Boot Partition FAT32
256 MB
start.elf, kernel8.img, config.txt, cmdline.txt, overlays/"] end subgraph SECTOR_2["Sector 524288 onwards"] ROOT["Root Partition ext4
~29 GiB
bin, sbin, usr, etc, var, home, opt, tmp"] end MBR --> BOOT --> ROOT ``` --- ## 4.4 Command-Flow Diagram — Linux CLI Method (for Advanced Students) ```mermaid flowchart TD X1[Step 1: Identify SD card
lsblk or fdisk -l] --> X2[Step 2: Unmount partitions
umount dev/sdX1 dev/sdX2] X2 --> X3[Step 3: Write image
sudo dd if=raspios.img of=dev/sdX bs=4M status=progress conv=fsync] X3 --> X4[Step 4: Sync buffers
sync] X4 --> X5[Step 5: Eject safely
sudo eject dev/sdX] X5 --> X6[Step 6: Boot Raspberry Pi] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Question Paper]** **List the four stages of the Linux boot process on a Raspberry Pi and mention the file/binary invoked at each stage.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Raspberry Pi boot process consists of four stages: 1. **Stage 0 – Mask ROM (immutable, in SoC):** The first instruction is hardwired in the silicon. It locates the boot medium (SD card for older Pis, SPI EEPROM for Pi 4/5). 2. **Stage 1 – Bootloader (`bootcode.bin` + `start.elf`):** Loaded into the GPU's L2 cache. It reads `config.txt` and prepares the SDRAM. 3. **Stage 2 – Kernel (`kernel8.img` for 64-bit Pi 4/5):** Decompresses itself, initializes hardware drivers from Device Tree Blobs (DTBs), and mounts the root file system. 4. **Stage 3 – Init (`/sbin/init` → `systemd`):** The first user-space process (PID 1). It starts system services and brings the system to the multi-user target (login prompt). **(3 marks)** --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Supplementary Paper]** **Why is the boot partition of a Raspberry Pi SD card formatted as FAT32 and not ext4?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The boot partition must be FAT32 for the following reasons: 1. **ROM Bootloader Limitation:** The on-chip Mask ROM bootloader in Broadcom SoCs has *no ext4 parser* — it can only read FAT12, FAT16, and FAT32 signatures. **(+1 mark)** 2. **Cross-Platform Accessibility:** FAT32 is readable by Windows, macOS, and Linux, allowing users to easily edit `config.txt` and `cmdline.txt` from any host. **(+1 mark)** 3. **Low Overhead:** FAT32 has a small, simple metadata structure ideal for the small 256 MB boot partition. **(+1 mark)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module-1 Internal Choice Pattern:** The student answers *either* Question A *or* Question B. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** #### **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the step-by-step procedure to format an SD card and flash the Raspberry Pi OS for headless IoT deployment. Mention at least two configuration parameters set via Raspberry Pi Imager's advanced menu. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Downloading the OS Image:** Obtain the latest Raspberry Pi OS Lite (64-bit) `.img.xz` file from the official `raspberrypi.com` server. This image is a bit-for-bit clone of a pre-built filesystem. **(1 mark)** **Step 2 — Image Verification:** Before flashing, compute the SHA-256 checksum of the downloaded file and compare it against the value published on the official download page. This prevents flashing a corrupted or tampered image. **(1 mark)** **Step 3 — Physical Card Identification:** Insert the SD card into the host PC. Use `lsblk` (Linux), *Disk Utility* (macOS), or *This PC → Manage* (Windows) to identify the device node (e.g., `/dev/sdb`). **Critical:** Confirming the correct device prevents accidental overwriting of the host's hard disk. **(1 mark)** **Step 4 — Launching Raspberry Pi Imager:** Open the Imager, click the **gear icon (⚙)** to access the *OS Customisation* menu, and set the following two parameters for headless deployment: - **Enable SSH** with *Password Authentication* (set a strong password). This allows remote command-line access without a monitor. **(0.5 mark)** - **Configure Wi-Fi** with SSID and PSK (Pre-Shared Key) for the target network. This is essential for headless nodes with no Ethernet. **(0.5 mark)** **Step 5 — Flashing the Image:** Click *Choose OS → Raspberry Pi OS (other) → Raspberry Pi OS Lite (64-bit)*, then *Choose Storage → [SD card]*, then *Write*. The Imager internally invokes a `format → write → verify` cycle. **(1 mark)** **Step 6 — Safe Ejection and First Boot:** Eject the SD card safely, insert it into the Raspberry Pi, and power on. Within ~20 seconds, the Pi obtains an IP via DHCP. The user can `ssh pi@` from a terminal. **(1 mark)** **Step 7 — Post-Boot Validation:** Run `systemctl status ssh` to confirm SSH is active, `ip addr` to confirm network connectivity, and `df -h` to confirm the root partition is mounted with the expected capacity. **(1 mark)** #### **(b) [7 marks]** With the help of a block diagram, describe the file-system hierarchy created on a typical Raspberry Pi OS SD card after flashing. Identify the purpose of `config.txt` and `cmdline.txt`. **Model Solution:** **Block Diagram (Mermaid rendered in the answer script):** ```mermaid flowchart TB SD["SD Card 32 GB"] --> MBR["MBR at Sector 0
512 bytes"] MBR --> P1["Partition 1: /boot/firmware
FAT32, 256 MB"] MBR --> P2["Partition 2: /
ext4, ~29 GiB"] P1 --> F1["config.txt"] P1 --> F2["cmdline.txt"] P1 --> F3["start.elf + bootcode.bin"] P1 --> F4["kernel8.img"] P1 --> F5["overlays/*.dtbo"] P2 --> D1["/bin /sbin"] P2 --> D2["/usr /opt"] P2 --> D3["/etc /var"] P2 --> D4["/home /root"] ``` **Description of the hierarchy:** The SD card contains an MBR partition table with two partitions. Partition 1 is a **256 MB FAT32** boot volume mounted at `/boot/firmware` containing all firmware-stage artifacts. Partition 2 is the **ext4 root filesystem** containing the full Linux directory tree (FHS 3.0 compliant). **(2 marks)** **Purpose of `config.txt`:** This is the Raspberry Pi's *BIOS-equivalent* configuration file, parsed by `start.elf` *before* the kernel is loaded. It contains directives such as `arm_freq=1800` (overclock), `gpu_mem=128`, `enable_uart=1`, and `dtoverlay=enable-bt`. It controls **hardware parameters at firmware stage**, not at kernel stage. **(2.5 marks)** **Purpose of `cmdline.txt`:** This file holds the **kernel command-line arguments** passed to `kernel8.img`. A typical line is: $$ \texttt{console=serial0,115200 console=tty1 root=PARTUUID=abcd-ef01 rootfstype=ext4 fsck.repair=yes rootwait} $$ It tells the kernel **which device to mount as root (`root=`)**, the **root filesystem type (`rootfstype=`)**, and whether to **run fsck on boot**. It is read *after* `config.txt`, at kernel stage. **(2.5 marks)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** #### **(a) [7 marks]** Compare the following three tools for SD card preparation: Raspberry Pi Imager, balenaEtcher, and the Linux `dd` command. Tabulate the comparison along seven parameters. **Model Solution:** | Parameter | Raspberry Pi Imager | balenaEtcher | `dd` command | |-----------|---------------------|--------------|--------------| | **Operating System Support** | Windows, macOS, Linux | Windows, macOS, Linux | Linux (and macOS) only | | **Graphical User Interface** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (CLI only) | | **OS Customisation (SSH, Wi-Fi)** | ✅ Built-in (via ⚙ menu) | ❌ Not built-in (post-flash) | ❌ Manual (mount + edit) | | **Automatic Format Step** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (must pre-format) | | **Verification Step (checksum)** | ✅ SHA-256 built-in | ✅ Hash check | ❌ No (manual `sha256sum`) | | **Risk of Misuse** | Low (GUI protection) | Low | **High** (can overwrite host disk with one typo) | | **Recommended User Level** | Beginner → Advanced | Beginner → Intermediate | Advanced / Embedded engineers | **Justification (extra 1 mark):** Raspberry Pi Imager is the **KTU-recommended** tool for PBCIT504 lab work because it integrates formatting, flashing, *and* post-flash customisation (SSH keys, locale, Wi-Fi credentials) in a single click, mirroring the workflow expected in KTU-supplied lab manuals. #### **(b) [7 marks]** A team is deploying a Raspberry Pi-based air quality monitoring IoT node in a remote industrial site with intermittent power. Discuss three engineering considerations for SD card reliability and boot resilience in this scenario. **Model Solution:** **Consideration 1 — Use an Industrial-Grade SD Card with Power-Loss Protection (PLP).** Industrial cards (e.g., Swissbit, ATP, Transcend Industrial) have a built-in tantalum capacitor that flushes the NAND write buffer to flash on sudden power loss. This prevents **filesystem journal corruption** in ext4, which is the #1 cause of non-booting Linux systems after power cuts. The team should budget for cards rated at **pSLC mode** (pseudo-SLC) for **100,000 P/E cycles** versus consumer cards at **3,000 cycles**. **(2.5 marks)** **Consideration 2 — Configure a Read-Only Root Filesystem (OverlayFS).** Since the air quality node runs a *fixed* Python application that does not need to write to `/usr` or `/etc`, the team can use `overlayroot` (Raspberry Pi OS) or a custom initramfs to mount the root as read-only with a tmpfs overlay. This makes the system **immune to sudden power loss** because no writes ever reach the SD card during normal operation. Only `/var/log` and sensor data (typically written to a separate microSD or transmitted via MQTT) remain writable. **(2.5 marks)** **Consideration 3 — Enable Watchdog Timer + A/B Partition Failover.** The BCM2711 SoC has a hardware watchdog that can be enabled via `dtparam=watchdog=on` in `config.txt` and serviced by `systemd` using `RuntimeWatchdogSec=15`. If the system hangs during boot (e.g., due to corrupted kernel image), the watchdog will **reboot the Pi within 15 seconds**. For higher resilience, an **A/B root partition layout** (popular in OpenWrt and Yocto) allows the bootloader to fall back to a known-good partition if the primary fails to mount. **(2 marks)** --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake 1 (–1 mark):** Writing "FAT32 is faster than ext4" — **Wrong.** ext4 is faster on Linux-native workloads; FAT32 is chosen for *bootloader compatibility*, not speed. > - **Common Mistake 2 (–1 mark):** Saying the Linux kernel is loaded by the BIOS — **Wrong for Raspberry Pi.** There is *no BIOS/UEFI*; the SoC Mask ROM is the equivalent. UEFI appears on x86 IoT gateways (UP Squared, ASUS Tinker Board 2). > - **Common Mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Confusing `config.txt` with `cmdline.txt`. The first is **firmware-level** (parsed by `start.elf` *before* the kernel), the second is **kernel-level** (parsed *by* the kernel). > - **Common Mistake 4 (–1 mark):** Forgetting to convert GB (decimal) to GiB (binary) in capacity derivations. Always use $1 \text{ GiB} = 1.0737 \text{ GB}$. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist** — Read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall. - [x] **SD card preparation = Format (logical) + Flash (populate).** Formatting alone does not install an OS; flashing alone does not create a valid partition table. - [x] **Two-partition layout is canonical:** FAT32 boot (`/boot/firmware`, 256 MB) + ext4 root (`/`, remaining). - [x] **Boot partition MUST be FAT32** for Raspberry Pi (broadcom SoC ROM limitation). - [x] **Four boot stages:** Mask ROM → Bootloader (`bootcode.bin` + `start.elf`) → Kernel (`kernel8.img`) → init (`systemd`). - [x] **`config.txt` = firmware parameters** (clock speed, GPU mem, overlays). **`cmdline.txt` = kernel parameters** (root device, console). - [x] **Device Tree Blobs (DTB/DTBO):** Hardware description files read by the kernel to enumerate peripherals *without* hardcoding in the kernel image. - [x] **Raspberry Pi Imager is the KTU-recommended tool** for PBCIT504 labs (integrates format, flash, verify, customisation). - [x] **`dd` is dangerous** — always triple-check `of=/dev/sdX` to avoid wiping the host disk. - [x] **Minimum SD card spec for OS boot:** Class 10 / A1 / V30 (≥10 MB/s sustained write, ≥1500 IOPS). - [x] **Default MBR sector size = 512 bytes; FAT32 default cluster size for 256 MB = 4 KiB.** - [x] **Boot time decomposition** (Pi 4): ≈ 14 s (Mask ROM 0.2s + Firmware 1.8s + Kernel 4s + systemd 8s). - [x] **ext4 inode ratio:** 1 inode per 16,384 bytes by default → ≈ 1.9 million inodes on a 30 GiB partition. - [x] **Power-loss resilience strategies:** Industrial SD card with PLP, read-only rootfs via OverlayFS, hardware watchdog, A/B partition. - [x] **Cold-boot vs warm-boot:** Cold = power off → on (full Mask ROM chain). Warm = `sudo reboot` (skips Mask ROM, jumps to EEPROM/firmware). - [x] **SD card wear-out math:** $T_{\text{lifetime}} = N_{\text{cycles}} \times \frac{C_{\text{card}}}{W_{\text{rate}}}$, where $W_{\text{rate}}$ is the average daily write volume. --- **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — "Format an SD Card and Booting the OS"**

topics.title: Interfacing Hardware
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT504 - PROGRAMMING FOR INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Interfacing Hardware

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Internet of Things (IoT)** under the KTU 2024 NEP-2020 aligned syllabus, **Hardware Interfacing** is formally defined as the systematic process of establishing electrical, electronic, and logical communication between a **microcontroller/microprocessor unit** (the "brain") and the **external physical world** through **Input/Output (I/O) peripherals** such as sensors, actuators, displays, and communication modules.

Hardware interfacing essentially bridges the **digital domain** (where the controller operates using binary logic states: `0` and `1`, corresponding to **0 V** and **3.3 V / 5 V**) and the **analog/physical domain** (where real-world phenomena like temperature, pressure, light, and motion exist as continuous signals).

The fundamental elements of hardware interfacing include:

- **GPIO (General Purpose Input/Output) Pins** — the basic digital communication endpoints.
- **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)** — converts continuous analog signals into discrete digital values.
- **DAC (Digital-to-Analog Converter)** — converts discrete digital values back into continuous analog signals.
- **Communication Protocols** — standardized electrical and logical rules such as **UART**, **SPI**, **I²C**, and **1-Wire** that allow orderly data exchange between devices.
- **Level Shifters and Pull Resistors** — auxiliary circuitry ensuring voltage compatibility and stable logic states.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Hardware interfacing is the foundational layer (the **"Perception Layer"** in the standard IoT architecture model) upon which all higher-layer IoT logic — networking, data processing, and cloud communication — is built. A solid grasp of this module is non-negotiable for the End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Universal Translator

Imagine a **diplomat** stationed between two countries that speak completely different languages and follow different social customs. The diplomat does not change who they are, but they **interpret, translate, and enforce the protocol** of communication so that trade and conversation can happen smoothly.

In this analogy:
- The **diplomat** = the **microcontroller** (e.g., ESP32, Arduino Uno, Raspberry Pi Pico).
- **Country A (analog world)** = real-world physical signals (temperature in °C, light in lux, voltage from a potentiometer).
- **Country B (digital world)** = the binary world of the processor (bits, registers, logic levels).
- **Translation manuals / diplomatic protocols** = **UART, SPI, I²C**.
- **Currency exchange counters** = **voltage level shifters** (e.g., converting 5 V logic to 3.3 V logic).

Without this diplomat (interfacing), the two "countries" would be utterly isolated — and that is precisely what an IoT node becomes: a *blind island* unable to perceive or influence the physical world.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Terminology Every KTU Student Must Memorize:**
> - **Logic HIGH:** A voltage level recognized by the controller as binary `1` (commonly **3.3 V** or **5 V**).
> - **Logic LOW:** A voltage level recognized as binary `0` (commonly **0 V** / GND).
> - **Vih / Vil:** Minimum input voltage guaranteed to be read as HIGH / LOW respectively.
> - **Voh / Vol:** Output voltage levels guaranteed by the device when driving HIGH / LOW.
> - **Sink Current:** Current flowing *into* the pin (toward ground).
> - **Source Current:** Current flowing *out of* the pin (toward the load).
> - **Fan-out:** The number of digital inputs a single output can reliably drive.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Hardware Interfacing

Hardware interfacing, when dissected, rests on three functional pillars:

#### Pillar 1 — **Digital Interfacing**
- Uses only two discrete voltage states.
- Applications: Reading a push-button, toggling an LED, controlling a relay.
- **Voltage Threshold Rule:** A digital input is guaranteed HIGH only when the applied voltage exceeds **0.7 × Vcc** (for most CMOS logic families), and guaranteed LOW when below **0.3 × Vcc**.

#### Pillar 2 — **Analog Interfacing**
- Uses a continuous range of voltages (typically **0 V – 3.3 V** for most modern ARM-based controllers).
- Requires **ADC** for sensing and **DAC** (or PWM) for actuation.
- **Resolution:** Determined by the number of bits — an **n-bit ADC** divides the reference voltage into **2ⁿ** discrete steps.

#### Pillar 3 — **Bus / Protocol Interfacing**
- Multiple devices share a common communication channel governed by a strict protocol.
- The four dominant protocols in IoT hardware are **UART, SPI, I²C, and 1-Wire**.

### 2.2 Communication Protocols — Comparative Anatomy

| Feature | UART | SPI | I²C | 1-Wire |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Full Name | Universal Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter | Serial Peripheral Interface | Inter-Integrated Circuit | One-Wire (Dallas) |
| Wires Required | 2 (TX, RX) | 4 (MOSI, MISO, SCK, SS) | 2 (SDA, SCL) | 1 (DQ) + GND |
| Clock | Asynchronous (no shared clock) | Synchronous (SCK line) | Synchronous (SCL line) | Asynchronous (master-driven) |
| Speed | Up to ~1 Mbps typical | Up to ~10–50 Mbps | 100 kbps (Standard) / 400 kbps (Fast) / 3.4 Mbps (High-Speed) | ~16 kbps typical |
| Device Addressing | Point-to-point (1-to-1) | SS line per slave (chip select) | 7-bit / 10-bit address | 64-bit ROM ID |
| Multi-master | Not natively | No (single master typical) | Yes | Yes |
| Typical Use | Debug console, GPS, Bluetooth modules | SD cards, OLED displays, high-speed sensors | EEPROMs, RTCs, slow sensors | DS18B20 temperature sensor |

### 2.3 GPIO Configuration Modes

Every modern microcontroller GPIO pin can typically be configured into several modes. The KTU examiner expects familiarity with these:

- **Input Floating** — High-impedance input; no internal resistor. Used when an external pull-up/pull-down is provided.
- **Input Pull-up** — Internal resistor (~30–50 kΩ) connects pin to Vcc.
- **Input Pull-down** — Internal resistor (~30–50 kΩ) connects pin to GND.
- **Output Push-Pull** — Can actively drive the line HIGH or LOW. Standard digital output.
- **Output Open-Drain** — Can only pull LOW actively; requires an **external pull-up resistor** to reach HIGH. Essential for I²C bus lines.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Voltage Divider (output across R₂) | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $R_{LED} = \dfrac{V_s - V_f}{I_f}$ | Current-limiting resistor for an LED | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 3 | $R_{p(min)} = \dfrac{V_{cc} - 0.4}{I_{sink(max)}}$ | Minimum I²C pull-up resistor | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 4 | $R_{p(max)} = \dfrac{t_r}{0.8473 \cdot C_b}$ | Maximum I²C pull-up resistor (rise time constraint) | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 5 | $\text{Step Size} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$ | ADC quantization step (LSB voltage) | Volts (V) |
| 6 | $\text{Resolution} = \log_2(\text{Step Count}) = n$ | Number of bits required for a given resolution | Bits |
| 7 | $f_{SCL(max)} = \dfrac{1}{T_{high} + T_{low}}$ | Maximum I²C clock frequency | Hertz (Hz) |
| 8 | $P = V \cdot I$ | DC power dissipation in interfacing circuit | Watts (W) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** In markdown tables, **never** use the vertical pipe `|` symbol for absolute value or "such that" notation. Always use `\vert` or `\mid`. For example, write $\vert x \vert$ instead of $|x|$ inside table cells.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hardware interfacing is the cornerstone of every physical computing domain:

- **Wearable Health Devices:** ECG electrodes (analog) → ADC → microcontroller → BLE → smartphone.
- **Smart Agriculture:** Soil moisture sensor (analog) → ADC → ESP32 → MQTT broker → cloud dashboard.
- **Industrial Automation:** PLC digital inputs (24 V) → opto-isolated level shifter → 3.3 V microcontroller GPIO.
- **Automotive IoT:** CAN bus interfacing for OBD-II port data extraction.

Without mastering interfacing, an IoT engineer cannot translate a sensor datasheet into a working prototype — a skill the KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests in the laboratory component.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Voltage Divider Equation

The voltage divider is the most fundamental interface circuit — used for **scaling down** a sensor output voltage to fit within the microcontroller's ADC input range.

**Starting Point — Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the series loop:**

$$V_{in} = V_{R_1} + V_{R_2}$$

**Apply Ohm's Law** ($V = I \cdot R$) to each resistor, noting the same current $I$ flows through both (series connection):

$$V_{in} = I \cdot R_1 + I \cdot R_2$$

**Factor out the common current** $I$:

$$V_{in} = I \cdot (R_1 + R_2)$$

**Solve for the loop current** $I$:

$$I = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**The output voltage is defined as the voltage across R₂**, again using Ohm's Law:

$$V_{out} = I \cdot R_2$$

**Substitute the expression for $I$ obtained earlier**:

$$V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot R_2$$

**Final Compact Form:**

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

> **Interpretation:** If $R_1 \gg R_2$, then $V_{out} \ll V_{in}$ (step-down). If $R_2 \gg R_1$, then $V_{out} \approx V_{in}$ (passthrough). The **Thevenin equivalent output impedance** of this divider is $R_{th} = R_1 \parallel R_2$, which must be considered when driving a non-ideal ADC input.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: LED Current-Limiting Resistor

When interfacing an LED to a digital output pin, a series resistor is mandatory to prevent **thermal runaway** and destruction of the LED.

**Define the circuit constraint** (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the loop: Vcc → Resistor → LED → GND):

$$V_s = V_R + V_f$$

where $V_s$ is the supply voltage at the GPIO pin, $V_f$ is the LED forward voltage, and $V_R$ is the voltage drop across the series resistor.

**Rearrange to isolate the resistor voltage:**

$$V_R = V_s - V_f$$

**Apply Ohm's Law** $V_R = I_f \cdot R$:

$$I_f \cdot R = V_s - V_f$$

**Solve for the resistance R:**

$$R = \dfrac{V_s - V_f}{I_f}$$

**Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style):**

Given: $V_s = 5$ V (Arduino digital HIGH), $V_f = 2.0$ V (red LED), $I_f = 15$ mA (desired LED current).

$$R = \dfrac{5 - 2.0}{15 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$R = \dfrac{3.0}{0.015}$$

$$R = 200 \, \Omega$$

**Nearest standard E12 value: 220 $\Omega$.**

> **Sanity Check:** The actual LED current with 220 $\Omega$ is $I = 3.0 / 220 \approx 13.6$ mA — well within safe operating limits.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: I²C Bus Pull-Up Resistor Sizing

The I²C bus uses **open-drain** outputs, meaning devices can only pull the SDA/SCL lines LOW. To return the line HIGH, an external **pull-up resistor** $R_p$ to $V_{cc}$ is mandatory. Choosing $R_p$ involves two bounds:

**Lower Bound — Current Sinking Constraint:**

The I²C specification allows a maximum sink current of **3 mA** per device at $V_{OL} = 0.4$ V. Beyond this, the LOW voltage may exceed the guaranteed LOW threshold.

$$R_{p(min)} = \dfrac{V_{cc} - V_{OL(max)}}{I_{sink(max)}}$$

**Numerical Example:** With $V_{cc} = 3.3$ V:

$$R_{p(min)} = \dfrac{3.3 - 0.4}{3 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{2.9}{0.003} \approx 967 \, \Omega$$

**Upper Bound — Rise Time Constraint:**

The bus capacitance $C_b$ (sum of all device pin capacitances + trace capacitance) must be charged through $R_p$ within the maximum allowed rise time $t_r$ (300 ns for Standard-mode I²C, per the Philips/NXP specification):

$$R_{p(max)} = \dfrac{t_r}{0.8473 \cdot C_b}$$

**Numerical Example:** With $t_r = 300$ ns and $C_b = 100$ pF (typical for a small bus):

$$R_{p(max)} = \dfrac{300 \times 10^{-9}}{0.8473 \times 100 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$R_{p(max)} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{-7}}{8.473 \times 10^{-11}} \approx 3.54 \, k\Omega$$

**Final Selection Rule:** Choose $R_p$ such that $R_{p(min)} \le R_p \le R_{p(max)}$. A standard **2.2 kΩ** or **4.7 kΩ** resistor is commonly chosen in practice.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: ADC Resolution Step Size

An ADC works by dividing a reference voltage range into discrete intervals. For an **n-bit** ADC:

$$\text{Number of discrete levels} = 2^n$$

The **Least Significant Bit (LSB)** voltage — the smallest distinguishable voltage change — is:

$$\Delta V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$$

**Numerical Example:** A **12-bit ADC** (like the one in ESP32) with $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V:

$$\Delta V_{LSB} = \dfrac{3.3}{2^{12} - 1} = \dfrac{3.3}{4095} \approx 0.806 \, mV$$

**Practical Implication:** This 12-bit ADC can theoretically distinguish voltage changes as small as ~0.8 mV — sufficient for precision sensor interfacing in industrial IoT applications.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix: Selecting an Interface Protocol

| Selection Criterion | UART | SPI | I²C |
|---|---|---|---|
| Need only 2 wires? | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (4 wires) | ✅ Yes |
| Need multiple slaves on one bus? | ❌ Point-to-point | ✅ Via SS lines | ✅ Via addressing |
| Highest throughput required? | ⚠️ Moderate | ✅ Fastest | ⚠️ Moderate |
| Cable length > 1 m? | ✅ Robust | ❌ Short-distance only | ✅ Good up to a few m |
| Need multi-master? | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ Yes |
| Hot-swap of devices required? | ⚠️ Risky | ❌ | ✅ Designed for it |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examination Tip:** Questions on protocol selection frequently appear in the form of *"Justify why I²C is preferred over SPI for connecting an RTC and an EEPROM on the same bus."* The answer revolves around **pin count, addressing, and multi-device support**, not raw speed.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 IoT Hardware Interfacing — Layered Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **end-to-end flow** of data and control signals through the hardware interfacing layer of a typical IoT node. Each block represents a functional module, and the arrows depict the **direction of signal flow**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Physical World Signals] --> B[Sensor Transducer]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning Block]
    C --> D[ADC Module]
    D --> E[GPIO Bus of Microcontroller]
    F[Microcontroller Firmware Logic] --> G[DAC Module]
    G --> H[Driver Circuit]
    H --> I[Actuator Device]
    I --> J[Physical World Action]
    K[UART Interface] --> E
    L[SPI Interface] --> E
    M[I2C Interface] --> E
    N[1-Wire Interface] --> E
    E --> O[Higher Layer Communication Module]
    O --> P[Network Gateway or Cloud]

    classDef sensor fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef act fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#c2185b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef proto fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff

    class A,B,C,D sensor
    class E,F,O,P ctrl
    class G,H,I,J act
    class K,L,M,N proto
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **left column (blue)** represents the **perception / input chain**: physical phenomena → transducer → signal conditioning → ADC → microcontroller.
- The **center (yellow)** represents the **intelligence layer**: firmware executing on the MCU.
- The **right column (pink)** represents the **actuation / output chain**: DAC → driver → actuator → physical action.
- The **purple blocks** are the **alternative protocol entry points** through which peripherals (sensors, displays, memories) may communicate with the MCU.

---

### 4.2 Protocol Comparison — Communication Topology

The next diagram contrasts the **bus topology** of UART, SPI, and I²C. This is essential for understanding *why* a particular protocol is chosen in an IoT design.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UART_TOPO[UART Point-to-Point Topology]
        MCU1[Microcontroller A] --- TX1[TX Pin]
        MCU2[Microcontroller B] --- RX1[RX Pin]
        TX1 -.TX to RX.-> RX1
        RX1 -.RX to TX.-> TX1
    end

    subgraph SPI_TOPO[SPI Single-Master Multi-Slave Topology]
        SPIMST[SPI Master]
        SPIS1[SPI Slave 1 - SS1]
        SPIS2[SPI Slave 2 - SS2]
        SPIS3[SPI Slave 3 - SS3]
        SPIMST --- SCKL[Shared SCK Line]
        SPIMST --- MOSIL[Shared MOSI Line]
        SPIMST --- MISOL[Shared MISO Line]
        SPIMST --- SS1L[SS Line 1]
        SPIMST --- SS2L[SS Line 2]
        SPIMST --- SS3L[SS Line 3]
        SS1L --- SPIS1
        SS2L --- SPIS2
        SS3L --- SPIS3
    end

    subgraph I2C_TOPO[I2C Multi-Master Multi-Slave Topology]
        I2CM1[I2C Master Node A]
        I2CS1[I2C Slave - Addr 0x50]
        I2CS2[I2C Slave - Addr 0x68]
        I2CS3[I2C Slave - Addr 0x27]
        I2CM1 --- SDAL[Shared SDA Line]
        I2CM1 --- SCLL[Shared SCL Line]
        I2CS1 --- SDAL
        I2CS1 --- SCLL
        I2CS2 --- SDAL
        I2CS2 --- SCLL
        I2CS3 --- SDAL
        I2CS3 --- SCLL
        RPU1[Pull-up Rp] --- SDAL
        RPU2[Pull-up Rp] --- SCLL
    end

    classDef mcu fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef slave fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef line fill:#fff,stroke:#666,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    class MCU1,MCU2,SPIMST,I2CM1 mcu
    class SPIS1,SPIS2,SPIS3,I2CS1,I2CS2,I2CS3 slave
    class TX1,RX1,SCKL,MOSIL,MISOL,SS1L,SS2L,SS3L,SDAL,SCLL,RPU1,RPU2 line
```

**Key Visual Insight:**
- **UART** requires a dedicated pair of wires between *every* two devices — scaling poorly.
- **SPI** shares three wires but needs a *new chip-select line* per slave — moderate scalability.
- **I²C** uses only **two wires** with **software addressing** — excellent scalability and is the protocol of choice for low-speed sensor networks in IoT.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q1.** Differentiate between **UART** and **SPI** communication protocols in terms of signaling type, number of wires, and device addressing. *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | UART | SPI |
|---|---|---|
| Signaling | Asynchronous (no shared clock) | Synchronous (shared SCK) |
| Wires | 2 (TX, RX) | 4 (MOSI, MISO, SCK, SS) |
| Addressing | Not addressed (1-to-1 only) | Hardware chip-select per slave |

*(Any valid technical differences covering the three parameters earn full marks.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]**

**Q2.** Why are **pull-up resistors** mandatory on the SDA and SCL lines of an I²C bus? What could happen if they are omitted? *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

I²C devices use **open-drain output drivers** which can only pull the line **LOW**; they cannot actively drive it HIGH. An external **pull-up resistor** to Vcc is therefore required to return the line to a logic HIGH state when no device is pulling it down.

**Consequences of omission:**
- The SDA/SCL lines would **float** at an undefined voltage when released, causing the bus to **hang** or produce spurious START/STOP conditions.
- Communication between master and slave(s) would fail completely.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]**

#### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Choose A OR B*

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **internal architecture of a typical GPIO pin** of a modern ARM-based microcontroller. Discuss the role of the **input buffer, output driver, pull-up/pull-down resistors, and alternate function multiplexer**. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

A GPIO pin is a configurable multi-purpose terminal whose internal structure comprises the following blocks:

1. **Protection Diodes:** Two ESD protection diodes — one to Vcc and one to GND — clamp voltage transients outside the supply range, protecting the internal CMOS stages.

2. **Input Buffer (Schmitt Trigger):** A Schmitt trigger with hysteresis converts the possibly noisy external voltage into a clean digital logic level. The upper threshold is typically **0.7 × Vcc** (Vih) and the lower threshold is **0.3 × Vcc** (Vil).

3. **Pull-up / Pull-down Resistors:** Weak internal resistors (~30–50 kΩ) selectable via software to bias floating inputs and prevent undefined states.

4. **Output Driver (Push-Pull):** A pair of complementary MOSFETs (one P-channel sourcing HIGH, one N-channel sinking LOW) capable of actively driving the line in both directions.

5. **Alternate Function Multiplexer:** Routes peripheral signals (UART TX, SPI MOSI, I²C SDA, PWM output, etc.) to the pin instead of the GPIO register.

6. **Data Latch / Bit-Band Register:** A memory-mapped register that holds the pin's output value or reads its input value.

The configuration is selected through registers such as `GPIOx_MODER`, `GPIOx_OTYPER`, `GPIOx_PUPDR`, and `GPIOx_IDR`/`GPIOx_ODR` in the STM32 family.

*(Diagram description accepted in lieu of drawing; mention of Schmitt trigger hysteresis and alternate function mux carries high weight.)*

---

**(b)** A **TMP36 temperature sensor** outputs an analog voltage of **750 mV at 25 °C**, with a linear scale factor of **10 mV/°C** and a **500 mV offset at 0 °C**. The sensor output is fed to a **10-bit ADC** with a **3.3 V reference**. Determine:
   (i) The actual temperature corresponding to a digital ADC output of **512**.
   (ii) The minimum resolvable temperature change (in °C).
   *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- ADC resolution $n = 10$ bits
- $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V
- ADC reading $D = 512$
- Sensor offset $V_0 = 500$ mV
- Scale factor $k = 10$ mV/°C

**(i) Temperature corresponding to D = 512:**

Step 1 — Compute LSB voltage:

$$\Delta V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1} = \dfrac{3.3}{1023} \approx 3.226 \, mV$$

Step 2 — Convert ADC reading to voltage:

$$V_{ADC} = D \cdot \Delta V_{LSB} = 512 \times 3.226 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$V_{ADC} = 512 \times 0.003226 \approx 1.652 \, V$$

Step 3 — Convert voltage to temperature using sensor transfer function:

$$V_{out} = V_0 + k \cdot T$$

$$T = \dfrac{V_{out} - V_0}{k} = \dfrac{1.652 - 0.500}{0.010}$$

$$T = \dfrac{1.152}{0.010} = 115.2 \, °C$$

**(ii) Minimum resolvable temperature change:**

The minimum resolvable voltage change is one LSB = **3.226 mV**.

$$\Delta T_{min} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{LSB}}{k} = \dfrac{3.226 \, mV}{10 \, mV/°C}$$

$$\Delta T_{min} \approx 0.3226 \, °C$$

**Conclusion:** A 10-bit ADC with a 3.3 V reference resolves a temperature change of approximately **0.32 °C** when paired with a TMP36 sensor.

---

#### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Compare and contrast **I²C** and **SPI** protocols across **eight (8)** parameters. Explain with a block diagram why **I²C requires external pull-up resistors** whereas SPI does not. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| # | Parameter | I²C | SPI |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of wires | 2 (SDA, SCL) | 4 (MOSI, MISO, SCK, SS) |
| 2 | Clock type | Synchronous | Synchronous |
| 3 | Data direction | Half-duplex | Full-duplex |
| 4 | Addressing | 7-bit / 10-bit software address | Hardware chip-select per slave |
| 5 | Maximum devices | Up to 127 (7-bit) / 1023 (10-bit) | Limited by available GPIO pins |
| 6 | Speed | 100 kbps / 400 kbps / 3.4 Mbps | Up to 50 Mbps or higher |
| 7 | Multi-master | Yes | No (typically) |
| 8 | Output driver | Open-drain (needs pull-up) | Push-pull (no pull-up needed) |

**Why I²C Needs Pull-up Resistors (Block Diagram Explanation):**

In I²C, every device's SDA and SCL pins are connected to the drain of an **N-channel MOSFET** whose source is tied to GND. There is **no P-channel pull-up transistor inside the device**. Therefore:
- When the MOSFET is OFF → the line must be pulled HIGH *externally* by $R_p$ to Vcc.
- When the MOSFET is ON → the line is pulled LOW (sinking current).

This **open-drain (or open-collector) topology** is essential for I²C because it allows **wired-AND** behavior: any device can pull the line LOW, and the line goes HIGH only when *all* devices release it.

In contrast, SPI uses **push-pull** outputs with both P-channel and N-channel transistors, allowing the master/slave to actively drive the line both HIGH and LOW — hence no external pull-up is required.

*(Block diagram of open-drain vs push-pull driver should accompany this answer.)*

---

**(b)** Design a **voltage divider** to scale down a sensor output range of **0 – 24 V** to **0 – 3.3 V** suitable for a microcontroller ADC. Assume the Thevenin resistance of the source is negligible, and choose standard **E24 resistor values**. Justify your selection. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Target:** Scale $V_{in}$ from 0–24 V down to $V_{out}$ = 0–3.3 V.

**Step 1 — Determine the divider ratio:**

$$\dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \dfrac{3.3}{24} = 0.1375$$

**Step 2 — Apply the voltage divider formula** and pick $R_2$ first. Choose $R_2 = 3.3$ kΩ (a convenient starting value).

Then:

$$\dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = 0.1375$$

$$R_1 + R_2 = \dfrac{R_2}{0.1375} = \dfrac{3300}{0.1375} \approx 24000 \, \Omega$$

$$R_1 = 24000 - 3300 = 20700 \, \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Convert to E24 standard value:**

E24 standard values in the 20 kΩ decade: **20 kΩ** is the nearest standard value.

**Verification with $R_1 = 20$ kΩ, $R_2 = 3.3$ kΩ:**

$$V_{out} = 24 \cdot \dfrac{3.3}{20 + 3.3} = 24 \cdot \dfrac{3.3}{23.3} \approx 3.40 \, V$$

This is slightly above 3.3 V — within tolerance for most ADCs (which typically tolerate ±5% of Vref).

**Step 4 — Refine for tighter accuracy:**

Using $R_1 = 22$ kΩ, $R_2 = 3.3$ kΩ:

$$V_{out} = 24 \cdot \dfrac{3.3}{22 + 3.3} = 24 \cdot \dfrac{3.3}{25.3} \approx 3.13 \, V$$

This yields 3.13 V at 24 V input — well within the 3.3 V safe limit.

**Final Selection: $R_1 = 22$ kΩ, $R_2 = 3.3$ kΩ (both E24 standard).**

**Justification:**
- Both values are commercially available E24 series resistors.
- The current drawn from the sensor at full scale is $I = 24 / (22000 + 3300) \approx 0.95$ mA — negligible loading effect on the sensor.
- The Thevenin output impedance $R_{th} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \approx 2.87$ kΩ is acceptable provided the ADC input impedance is at least **~287 kΩ** (the typical 10× rule).

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always specify E-series standard values** — random values like 20.7 kΩ will lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Compute the Thevenin impedance** and verify it does not excessively load the source.
> 3. **Forget the safety margin** — never let $V_{out(max)}$ exceed $V_{ref}$ by more than 5%, or the ADC may saturate and the pin may be damaged.
> 4. **Watch the divider current** — too low a current (< 0.1 mA) makes the output vulnerable to noise pickup; too high a current wastes power.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE**

- **Hardware Interfacing** = bridge between **physical signals** and **digital controller logic**.
- The three functional pillars are: **Digital I/O, Analog I/O (ADC/DAC), and Bus Protocols**.
- **GPIO modes** to memorize: Input Floating, Input Pull-up, Input Pull-down, Output Push-Pull, Output Open-Drain, Alternate Function.
- **UART** is asynchronous, point-to-point, 2-wire → best for debug consoles and module-to-MCU links.
- **SPI** is synchronous, full-duplex, 4-wire, fast → best for displays, SD cards, high-speed sensors.
- **I²C** is synchronous, half-duplex, 2-wire, open-drain → best for multiple low-speed peripherals sharing a bus.
- **Pull-up resistors** are **mandatory** on I²C SDA/SCL because of the open-drain driver topology.
- **Voltage divider** formula: $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$.
- **LED current-limiting resistor:** $R = (V_s - V_f) / I_f$.
- **ADC step size:** $\Delta V = V_{ref} / (2^n - 1)$ — use $2^n - 1$, **not** $2^n$.
- **I²C pull-up bounds:** $R_{p(min)}$ from sink-current limit, $R_{p(max)}$ from rise-time limit.
- **ESD protection diodes** inside every GPIO pin clamp voltage transients — never expose a pin beyond the abs-max rating (typically Vcc + 0.3 V).
- **Schmitt trigger** input hysteresis is what allows noise-immune digital input reading.
- For **mixed-voltage interfacing** (e.g., 5 V Arduino ↔ 3.3 V ESP32), always use a **level shifter** or at minimum a **voltage divider** on the logic lines.

> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Interfacing Hardware**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Remote Access
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT504 - PROGRAMMING FOR INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Remote Access

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Remote Access** in the context of the Internet of Things (IoT) is formally defined as the ability of an authorized user, application, or service to **interact with, monitor, configure, and control** an IoT device, sensor node, or embedded system over a communication network without requiring physical proximity to the device.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PBCIT504, Remote Access encompasses the protocols, software tools, network configurations, and security mechanisms that allow a developer or end-user to:

1. Reach a headless (no monitor/keyboard) embedded board (e.g., Raspberry Pi, ESP32, Arduino with shields) from a remote host.
2. Execute shell commands, transfer files, and stream telemetry.
3. Deploy firmware updates over-the-air (OTA).
4. Debug live applications across geographically separated networks.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1)**
> Remote Access is a foundational enabler of *ubiquitous computing*. The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to differentiate between **local access** (direct cable connection like USB/UART) and **remote access** (network-mediated), and to identify the protocol stack involved in each layer of the OSI model that facilitates this access.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Home from Office" Analogy

Imagine you are sitting in your office in **Kochi**, and you suddenly remember that you left the air conditioner running in your flat in **Trivandrum**.

- **Without Remote Access:** You would have to physically drive 200+ km back home, walk to the switchboard, and turn it off. This is **local access** — you must *be there*.
- **With Remote Access:** You open an app on your phone, tap the "AC Off" button, and a signal travels through cellular towers, through the internet, into your home Wi-Fi router, and finally to a smart plug that cuts power to the AC. You never left your chair.

**In this analogy:**
- **You** = The Remote Client (PC/Laptop/Mobile)
- **The Internet** = The Communication Channel
- **Your Home Wi-Fi Router** = The Gateway/Port Forwarder
- **The Smart Plug** = The IoT Device (Raspberry Pi/ESP32)
- **The App** = The Remote Access Software (SSH Client, MQTT Dashboard, etc.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Distinction for KTU Exams**
> - **Local Access:** Device-to-Computer via USB, UART (Serial), JTAG, or direct Ethernet cable. No IP routing involved.
> - **Remote Access:** Computer-to-Device over **TCP/IP** (LAN, WAN, or Internet). Requires addressing (IP), a transport protocol (TCP/UDP), and an application protocol (SSH, HTTP, MQTT).

### 1.3 Key Metrics & Constants in Remote Access

| Metric | Standard Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **SSH Default Port** | **22** | Standard TCP port for Secure Shell |
| **HTTP Default Port** | **80** | Unencrypted web access |
| **HTTPS Default Port** | **443** | Encrypted web access (TLS) |
| **MQTT Default Port** | **1883** | IoT messaging protocol (plain) |
| **MQTTS Default Port** | **8883** | MQTT over TLS |
| **Telnet Default Port** | **23** | Insecure remote terminal (legacy) |
| **RDP Default Port** | **3389** | Microsoft Remote Desktop |
| **VNC Default Port** | **5900** | Virtual Network Computing |
| **Latency Tolerance (IoT)** | **< 100 ms** | Acceptable for control loops |
| **CoAP Default Port** | **5683** | Constrained Application Protocol |

> [!TIP]
> **Why Ports Matter (Examiner's Favourite):** When you configure port forwarding on a router for a Raspberry Pi, you must know that the SSH service listens on **port 22** by default. KTU exam questions frequently test this mapping between *service* and *port number*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Remote Access

Remote access in IoT rests on three architectural pillars that the KTU 2024 module explicitly emphasizes:

**Pillar 1 — Connectivity Layer (How do we reach the device?)**
- The device must be assigned a routable address.
- Mechanisms include: Static Public IP, Dynamic DNS (DDNS), Port Forwarding, VPN Tunnels, Reverse SSH Tunnels, and Relay Services (ngrok, Dataplicity, Remote.it).

**Pillar 2 — Protocol Layer (What language do we speak?)**
- Application protocols: **SSH** (secure shell), **HTTP/HTTPS** (web), **MQTT** (pub/sub), **CoAP** (constrained REST), **WebSockets** (full-duplex), **Telnet** (legacy).
- Transport protocols: **TCP** (reliable, used by SSH/HTTP/MQTT) and **UDP** (lightweight, used by CoAP).

**Pillar 3 — Security Layer (How do we ensure only authorized access?)**
- **Authentication:** Passwords, Public Key Cryptography (RSA/Ed25519), Certificates (X.509), OAuth 2.0 tokens.
- **Encryption:** TLS 1.2/1.3, AES-256 for data-at-rest.
- **Authorization:** Role-Based Access Control (RBAC), Access Control Lists (ACL).

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Flow

When a developer types `ssh pi@192.168.1.50` from a laptop to access a Raspberry Pi on the same Wi-Fi:

1. **DNS Resolution:** The hostname is converted to an IP via the local DNS resolver (skipped for raw IPs).
2. **TCP Three-Way Handshake:** SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK establishes a connection.
3. **Protocol Negotiation:** SSH client and server agree on encryption algorithms (e.g., `chacha20-poly1305`).
4. **Host Key Verification:** The server presents its public key fingerprint; the client checks it against `known_hosts`.
5. **User Authentication:** Password or public-key challenge.
6. **Encrypted Tunnel Established:** All subsequent traffic is encrypted.
7. **Shell Session:** A pseudo-terminal (PTY) is opened, and command I/O flows through the tunnel.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Protocol Comparison Table

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These concepts are foundational for **Smart City** projects (Kerala Smart City Mission), **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** for Kerala's cashew/coir industries, and **precision agriculture** in Wayanad's spice plantations.

| Feature | SSH | Telnet | HTTP/REST | MQTT | RDP/VNC |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Default Port** | 22 | 23 | 80 / 443 | 1883 / 8883 | 3389 / 5900 |
| **Encryption** | Built-in (AES) | None | TLS (HTTPS) | TLS (MQTTS) | TLS optional |
| **Transport** | TCP | TCP | TCP | TCP | TCP |
| **Use Case** | Shell + file transfer | Legacy terminal | Web dashboard | Sensor telemetry | GUI control |
| **IoT Suitability** | High | Low (deprecated) | Medium | Very High | Low (heavy) |
| **Bandwidth** | Low | Low | Medium | Very Low | Very High |

### 2.4 Mathematical Model of Remote Access Performance

The **end-to-end latency** of a remote access session is critical. The total round-trip time can be modeled as:

$$ T_{total} = T_{prop} + T_{trans} + T_{proc} + T_{queue} $$

Where:
- $T_{prop}$ = Propagation delay = $\dfrac{D}{v}$ (Distance / signal velocity)
- $T_{trans}$ = Transmission delay = $\dfrac{L}{R}$ (Packet length / Bandwidth rate)
- $T_{proc}$ = Processing delay at each node (routing, encryption)
- $T_{queue}$ = Queuing delay in router buffers

For a typical Kerala home network with fiber to the home (FTTH) and an IoT device in the same LAN:

$$ T_{prop} \approx 0.5 \, \text{ms} \quad \vert \quad T_{trans} = \frac{64 \times 8}{10^9} = 0.512 \, \mu s $$

Since $T_{trans} \ll T_{prop}$ on a local LAN, the dominant component is **propagation** and **processing** (especially TLS handshake). This is why a *reverse SSH tunnel over a relay* (like ngrok) feels "laggy" — it adds an extra hop through a remote server.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Effective Throughput of an Encrypted SSH Session

When a developer runs a remote command like `scp file.txt pi@raspberry.local:/home/pi/`, the *effective throughput* observed by the user is less than the raw network bandwidth due to encryption overhead.

**Step 1 — Define the raw link capacity.**

Let $R$ be the raw bandwidth in bits per second. For a typical home Wi-Fi:

$$ R = 100 \times 10^6 \, \text{bits/s} \quad (100 \, \text{Mbps}) $$

**Step 2 — Account for the TCP/IP header overhead.**

Each packet has a 20-byte IP header, 20-byte TCP header, and SSH adds a 16-byte MAC plus a 4-byte packet length field. Total overhead per packet = $20 + 20 + 16 + 4 = 60$ bytes.

If the Maximum Transmission Unit (MTU) is $M = 1500$ bytes, the *payload efficiency* is:

$$ \eta_{payload} = \frac{M - 60}{M} = \frac{1500 - 60}{1500} = 0.96 $$

**Step 3 — Account for encryption padding.**

AES-256 in CBC mode requires 16-byte block alignment. If the payload is not a multiple of 16, padding is added. The worst-case padding waste is 15 bytes per packet, giving:

$$ \eta_{encrypt} = \frac{1440 - 15}{1440} \approx 0.9896 $$

**Step 4 — Account for the Acknowledgment (ACK) overhead.**

TCP ACKs are sent for every few data packets. Typically, an ACK is sent for every 2 data packets. Each ACK is 40 bytes (IP + TCP header, no payload) plus the reverse-path bandwidth consumption. The reverse ACK overhead fraction is approximately:

$$ \eta_{ack} = 1 - \frac{40}{2 \times 1500} = 1 - 0.0133 = 0.9867 $$

**Step 5 — Compute the effective throughput.**

The total effective throughput is the product of all efficiency factors multiplied by the raw bandwidth:

$$ R_{eff} = R \times \eta_{payload} \times \eta_{encrypt} \times \eta_{ack} $$

$$ R_{eff} = 100 \times 10^6 \times 0.96 \times 0.9896 \times 0.9867 $$

$$ R_{eff} = 100 \times 10^6 \times 0.9375 $$

$$ R_{eff} \approx 93.75 \, \text{Mbps} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that on a clean 100 Mbps link, the user sees only ~93.75 Mbps of *useful* data because of protocol overhead. On a slower IoT link like **LoRaWAN** (50 kbps) or **NB-IoT** (200 kbps), this overhead becomes catastrophic — which is why protocols like **CoAP** and **MQTT-SN** (with smaller headers) were designed specifically for constrained IoT devices.

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Remote Access Methods

| Method | Setup Complexity | Security | NAT Traversal | Latency | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct Port Forwarding** | Low | Low (exposed port) | No (need static IP) | Very Low | Home servers with public IP |
| **VPN (WireGuard/OpenVPN)** | Medium | Very High | No (LAN-only) | Low | Corporate networks |
| **Reverse SSH Tunnel** | Medium | High | Yes (via relay) | Medium | Cloud VPS as jump host |
| **ngrok / Dataplicity** | Very Low | Medium | Yes | Medium-High | Demos, hackathons, KTU labs |
| **MQTT Broker (HiveMQ/Mosquitto)** | Medium | High (with TLS) | Yes | Low | Telemetry & sensor data |
| **BaaS (AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub)** | High | Very High | Yes | Low | Production IoT products |

> [!TIP]
> **NAT Traversal Explained Simply:** Most home routers use **NAT (Network Address Translation)**, meaning the Raspberry Pi has a *private* IP (like 192.168.1.50) hidden behind the router's *public* IP. A packet from the internet cannot directly reach 192.168.1.50 without the router forwarding it. This is why a developer in KTU's lab cannot simply SSH into a Pi at home — the Pi is "invisible" from the outside world. Port forwarding, reverse tunnels, and relay services all solve this problem differently.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Remote Access Architecture in IoT

The following Mermaid block illustrates the complete end-to-end architecture of a typical IoT remote access scenario, from the developer's laptop to the embedded device.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RemoteUser[Remote User Domain]
        laptop[Developer Laptop or Mobile]
        clientApp[SSH Client or Web Browser]
    end

    subgraph PublicNet[Public Internet]
        routerHome[Home Router with NAT]
        relaySvc[Reverse Tunnel Relay like ngrok]
        mqttBrk[Public MQTT Broker]
    end

    subgraph IoTDomain[IoT Device Domain]
        rpi[Raspberry Pi or ESP32]
        sensor[Sensor like DHT22]
        actuator[Actuator like Relay]
    end

    laptop --> clientApp
    clientApp -->|HTTPS Request| routerHome
    clientApp -->|MQTT Publish| mqttBrk
    routerHome -->|Port 22 Forwarded| rpi
    clientApp -.->|Tunnel via TCP| relaySvc
    relaySvc -.->|Decapsulated Traffic| routerHome
    mqttBrk -->|MQTT Subscribe| rpi
    rpi --> sensor
    rpi --> actuator
```

### 4.2 Sequence Diagram: SSH Handshake for Remote Access

This sequence diagram shows the exact message exchange that happens during a typical SSH connection establishment.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Dev as Developer Laptop
    participant Rtr as Internet Router
    participant Pi as Raspberry Pi SSH Daemon

    Dev->>Rtr: TCP SYN to port 22
    Rtr->>Pi: Forwarded SYN to port 22
    Pi-->>Rtr: TCP SYN-ACK
    Rtr-->>Dev: TCP SYN-ACK
    Dev->>Rtr: TCP ACK
    Rtr->>Pi: TCP ACK
    Note over Dev,Pi: TCP Connection Established
    Dev->>Pi: SSH Protocol Version Exchange
    Pi-->>Dev: SSH Server Version String
    Dev->>Pi: Key Exchange Init (KEXINIT)
    Pi-->>Dev: KEXINIT Response
    Dev->>Pi: Diffie-Hellman Public Key
    Pi-->>Dev: Diffie-Hellman Public Key
    Note over Dev,Pi: Shared Secret Derived
    Dev->>Pi: Encrypted User Auth Request (pi)
    Pi-->>Dev: Auth Method Accepted (publickey)
    Dev->>>Pi: Encrypted Public Key Signature
    Pi-->>Dev: Authentication Success
    Note over Dev,Pi: Encrypted Shell Session Active
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow: Choosing the Right Remote Access Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Need Remote Access to IoT Device]) --> q1{Is device on same LAN?}
    q1 -->|Yes| sameLAN[Use Direct SSH via Local IP]
    q1 -->|No| q2{Do you have a public static IP?}
    q2 -->|Yes| pf[Configure Port Forwarding on Router]
    q2 -->|No| q3{Need persistent secure connection?}
    q3 -->|Yes| vpn[Set up WireGuard VPN on Pi]
    q3 -->|No| q4{Is it for a demo or short term?}
    q4 -->|Yes| ngrok[Use ngrok or Dataplicity]
    q4 -->|No| cloud[Use Cloud IoT Platform like AWS IoT]
    sameLAN --> done([Connection Established])
    pf --> done
    vpn --> done
    ngrok --> done
    cloud --> done
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Lab Tip:** In your PBCIT504 lab, you will most likely use the **Direct SSH via Local IP** path because both your laptop and the Raspberry Pi will be on the KTU lab Wi-Fi. The IP will typically be something like `192.168.50.42`. Use `ssh pi@192.168.50.42` with the default password `raspberry`.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Style]**

**Q1. [3 Marks] Define Remote Access in the context of IoT. List any two protocols used for achieving remote access to embedded devices.**

**Model Answer:**
Remote Access in IoT refers to the capability of a user to interact with, monitor, and control an embedded device or sensor node over a communication network from a geographically distant location, without requiring physical presence near the device.

Two commonly used protocols are:
1. **SSH (Secure Shell)** — operates on TCP port **22**, provides encrypted terminal access and secure file transfer (SCP/SFTP).
2. **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** — operates on TCP port **1883**, provides lightweight publish/subscribe messaging ideal for IoT telemetry.

*(Other acceptable: HTTP/HTTPS, CoAP, Telnet, VNC, RDP)*

---

**Q2. [3 Marks] Differentiate between Telnet and SSH as remote access protocols. Why is Telnet not recommended for IoT devices?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Telnet | SSH |
|---|---|---|
| **Default Port** | 23 | 22 |
| **Encryption** | None (plaintext) | Full encryption (AES, ChaCha20) |
| **Authentication** | Password in clear text | Password or public-key cryptography |
| **Data Integrity** | Not guaranteed | MAC-based integrity check |
| **Modern Usage** | Deprecated | Industry standard |

**Reason Telnet is not recommended:** Telnet transmits all data, including **usernames and passwords, in plaintext** over the network. An attacker on the same network can use a packet sniffer (e.g., Wireshark) to capture credentials. For IoT devices exposed to the internet, this is a critical security vulnerability that can lead to device hijacking and botnet recruitment (e.g., the Mirai botnet attack).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the architecture of remote access in IoT systems with a neat block diagram. Describe the function of each layer.**

**Model Answer:**

The architecture of remote access in IoT can be understood as a **four-layer stack**:

**Layer 1 — Device Layer (Bottom)**
This is the physical IoT hardware: microcontrollers (ESP32, Arduino), single-board computers (Raspberry Pi), sensors (DHT22, BME280), and actuators (relays, motors). These devices run lightweight services like `sshd` (SSH daemon) or `mosquitto` (MQTT broker client) to listen for incoming connections.

**Layer 2 — Network Layer**
This layer handles IP addressing, routing, and transport. The device gets a private IP (e.g., 192.168.1.50) from the router's DHCP server. The transport protocol is either **TCP** (reliable, used for SSH/HTTP/MQTT) or **UDP** (lightweight, used for CoAP). NAT and port forwarding operate at this layer.

**Layer 3 — Protocol Layer**
Application protocols define *how* the remote client communicates with the device:
- **SSH** for shell access
- **HTTP/REST** for web-based control panels
- **MQTT** for sensor telemetry
- **CoAP** for constrained devices

**Layer 4 — Application Layer (Top)**
The user-facing software: an SSH client (PuTTY, OpenSSH), a web dashboard (Node-RED, Grafana), or a mobile app (Blynk, custom React Native app). The user enters commands, sees sensor graphs, and triggers actuator actions.

**Block Diagram (drawn in exam):**

```
+----------------------------------+
|   APPLICATION LAYER              |
|   (SSH Client, Web Dashboard)    |
+----------------------------------+
|   PROTOCOL LAYER                 |
|   (SSH, HTTP, MQTT, CoAP)        |
+----------------------------------+
|   NETWORK LAYER                  |
|   (TCP/UDP, IP, NAT, Routing)    |
+----------------------------------+
|   DEVICE LAYER                   |
|   (RPi, ESP32, Sensors)          |
+----------------------------------+
```

**(b) [7 Marks] With a neat diagram, explain how a Reverse SSH Tunnel works for accessing an IoT device behind a NAT router. Mention any one real-world service that implements this concept.**

**Model Answer:**

**Problem Context:** A Raspberry Pi in a home network has a private IP (192.168.1.50) and the home router has a dynamic public IP. A developer wants to SSH into the Pi from outside, but cannot do so directly because:
1. The Pi's IP is private (not routable on the internet).
2. The home's public IP changes periodically (dynamic).
3. The router blocks unsolicited incoming connections (firewall/NAT).

**Solution: Reverse SSH Tunnel**

In a reverse tunnel, the *IoT device* (the Pi) initiates the connection to a publicly accessible **relay server** (a cloud VPS with a static IP). The Pi opens an SSH session to the relay, requesting that the relay listen on a specific port (e.g., 9000) and forward any traffic on that port back through the existing tunnel to the Pi.

**Diagram:**

```
Developer Laptop                  Relay VPS                 Home Router              Raspberry Pi
(Remote Client)              (Public IP: a.b.c.d)        (NAT: 203.0.113.x)         (192.168.1.50)
      |                              |                          |                          |
      |                              |                          |   ssh -R 9000:localhost:22 |
      |                              |<----------------------------------------------------------|  [1. Pi initiates tunnel]
      |                              |                          |                          |
      |       ssh -p 9000            |                          |                          |
      |----------------------------->|                          |                          |
      |       [2. Dev connects       |   [3. Forwards via       |                          |
      |        to relay port 9000]   |    existing tunnel]      |                          |
      |                              |------------------------->|<-------------------------|  [4. Reaches Pi's port 22]
      |                              |                          |                          |
      |<============ Encrypted SSH Session Across Tunnel ================================|
```

**Steps:**
1. The Raspberry Pi runs: `ssh -R 9000:localhost:22 user@relay-server.com`
2. This opens a persistent SSH connection to the relay and instructs it to listen on port 9000.
3. The developer, from anywhere in the world, runs: `ssh -p 9000 user@relay-server.com`
4. The relay forwards this connection back through the original tunnel to the Pi's local port 22.

**Real-world service:** **Dataplicity** (dataplicity.com) implements exactly this concept for Raspberry Pi remote access. It provides a web-based terminal and file manager without requiring router configuration. Another popular service is **ngrok**, which provides reverse tunnels for any TCP service.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks] Compare and contrast MQTT and HTTP as remote access protocols for IoT. Which is more suitable for battery-powered sensor nodes and why?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | MQTT | HTTP/REST |
|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | Publish/Subscribe (broker-based) | Request/Response (client-server) |
| **Default Port** | 1883 (plain) / 8883 (TLS) | 80 (HTTP) / 443 (HTTPS) |
| **Header Size** | 2 bytes (fixed) | Hundreds of bytes (variable) |
| **Transport** | TCP | TCP |
| **Connection Model** | Persistent (keep-alive) | New connection per request (typically) |
| **Data Push** | Server can push anytime (push-based) | Client must poll (pull-based) |
| **Power Efficiency** | Excellent (few transmissions) | Poor (polling wastes energy) |
| **Suitability for Sensors** | High | Low-Medium |

**Suitability for Battery-Powered Sensor Nodes:**

MQTT is more suitable because:
1. **Small header overhead (2 bytes)** vs HTTP's hundreds of bytes, meaning less airtime and lower power consumption.
2. **Persistent TCP connection** avoids the expensive TCP handshake (which involves multiple radio transmissions) on every message.
3. **Publish/Subscribe model** allows the sensor to send data only when a change occurs (event-driven), keeping the radio off most of the time.
4. **Quality of Service (QoS) levels 0, 1, 2** allow tuning reliability vs power trade-off.

A temperature sensor publishing via MQTT might send 50 bytes of payload and consume ~5 mA for 100 ms. An HTTP equivalent would send ~400 bytes and consume ~5 mA for 800 ms, resulting in **8x more energy per reading**.

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain the role of Dynamic DNS (DDNS) in IoT remote access. How does it solve the problem of dynamic public IP addresses in home networks?**

**Model Answer:**

**The Problem:** Most home internet connections in India (BSNL, Jio Fiber, Asianet) provide a **dynamic public IP** that changes every time the router reconnects (which can be daily, weekly, or on every reboot).
