If a developer wants to access their home Raspberry Pi from outside, they cannot hardcode the IP address because it will be different tomorrow.

**The Solution — Dynamic DNS (DDNS):**

Dynamic DNS is a service that automatically updates the domain name's DNS record whenever the public IP address changes. The flow is:

1. **User registers** a hostname (e.g., `myiotpi.ddns.net`) with a DDNS provider (No-IP, DuckDNS, DynDNS, Afraid.org).
2. **The home router** (or a small client running on the Pi) periodically checks the current public IP.
3. **On IP change detection**, the client sends an authenticated HTTPS update to the DDNS provider's API, which updates the A record of the hostname to point to the new IP within 30-60 seconds.
4. **The remote developer** now just runs: `ssh pi@myiotpi.ddns.net` — the DNS lookup always returns the *current* public IP, regardless of how many times it has changed.

**Components of a DDNS system:**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **DDNS Client** | Runs on the router or IoT device, detects IP changes |
| **DDNS Provider** | Hosts the dynamic DNS records and update API |
| **Domain Name** | Static, human-readable hostname |
| **A Record** | Maps hostname to current dynamic IP, auto-updated |
| **TTL (Time to Live)** | Short TTL (60-300s) ensures stale records expire quickly |

**Engineering Significance:** DDNS, combined with port forwarding, was the *de facto* standard for home IoT remote access before cloud relay services like ngrok emerged. It is still widely used for CCTV DVRs, NAS devices, and home automation servers.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing ports:** Students often write "SSH runs on port 23" — this is **WRONG**. SSH = **22**, Telnet = **23**. KTU examiners specifically test this.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention encryption in SSH:** A 7-mark answer that describes SSH without mentioning *AES encryption, public-key authentication, and MAC integrity* will lose 2-3 marks.
> 3. **Drawing the wrong diagram for Reverse SSH Tunnel:** Many students draw the tunnel as if *the developer* initiated the connection. The defining feature of a **reverse** tunnel is that the **IoT device initiates** the outbound connection to the relay server. The examiner will reject answers that reverse this direction.
> 4. **Not justifying the choice between MQTT and HTTP:** Simply stating "MQTT is better" without *quantifying* (header size, connection persistence, energy per byte) will not earn full marks. Always support your choice with a numerical or architectural reason.
> 5. **Mixing up Private vs Public IP:** Saying "the Pi has a public IP 192.168.1.50" is technically wrong because 192.168.x.x is a **private (RFC 1918)** range. Use precise terminology.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Remote Access = network-mediated control of an IoT device from a distant location.
- **Local vs Remote:** Local uses USB/UART/JTAG; Remote uses TCP/IP.
- **SSH** = encrypted shell, **port 22**, uses public-key cryptography.
- **Telnet** = plaintext, **port 23**, deprecated due to security.
- **HTTP** = web, **port 80/443**, request-response.
- **MQTT** = pub/sub IoT messaging, **port 1883/8883**, lightweight 2-byte header.
- **CoAP** = constrained REST, **port 5683**, runs over UDP for low-power devices.
- **Reverse SSH Tunnel:** IoT device initiates outbound connection to a public relay; useful when the device is behind NAT.
- **DDNS:** Maps a static hostname to a changing dynamic public IP, enabling persistent addressing.
- **Latency formula:** $T_{total} = T_{prop} + T_{trans} + T_{proc} + T_{queue}$.
- **Throughput formula:** $R_{eff} = R \times \eta_{payload} \times \eta_{encrypt} \times \eta_{ack}$.
- **Security pillars:** Authentication (who are you?), Authorization (what can you do?), Encryption (is the data safe?).
- **Common tools:** OpenSSH, PuTTY, Mosquitto, Node-RED, ngrok, Dataplicity, balenaCloud.
- **Real-world impact:** Enables smart cities, telemedicine (KSRTC/Apollo telematics), industrial automation, and KTU's own smart campus projects.
- **Always remember:** Port **22** = SSH, **80** = HTTP, **443** = HTTPS, **1883** = MQTT, **23** = Telnet.
- **NAT Traversal** is the *core engineering challenge* of IoT remote access — every solution (port forwarding, VPN, reverse tunnel, relay) is a different way to solve it.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bash Command Line
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT504 - PROGRAMMING FOR INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bash Command Line

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Bash** (Bourne Again SHell) is a **command-line interpreter** and **scripting language** written as a free software replacement for the original Bourne Shell (`sh`). It is the default login shell for most **Linux distributions** and Apple's macOS (until Catalina), forming the primary user-space interface between the user kernel and the operating system in IoT edge devices (typically Raspberry Pi, BeagleBone, ESP32 host boards running embedded Linux).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition**: Bash is a **POSIX-compliant command language interpreter** that executes commands read from the standard input or from a file. It implements the *IEEE POSIX 1003.1* standard for shell behavior, making it the de-facto standard for IoT development environments.

In the context of **Programming for IoT (PBCIT504)**, Bash serves as the primary toolchain interface for:
- **Cross-compilation** of C/C++ firmware for ARM Cortex targets.
- **Flashing** binaries to microcontrollers via tools like `esptool.py`, `avrdude`, and `openocd`.
- **Flashing and managing** SD card images for Raspberry Pi.
- **Network configuration** of IoT gateways using `ifconfig`, `ip`, and `iwconfig`.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Kitchen" Intuition

Think of Bash as the **head waiter in a busy restaurant kitchen**:
- **You (the customer)** sit at the table and speak a structured, simple order ("bring me the menu, page 3, dish 2 with extra cheese").
- **The waiter (Bash)** listens, understands the *grammar* of your order, walks over to the kitchen window, and shouts the right instructions to the *chef*.
- **The chef (the Linux kernel)** does the actual heavy lifting — cooking (CPU), grabbing ingredients (RAM/disk), or delivering plates (I/O).
- The waiter returns the result (your food, or in our case, the output) back to you.

You never have to walk into the kitchen yourself. You just need to know the right *syntax* of the order. The same way you don't need to know how the OS allocates memory — you just need to know the *right command*.

### 1.3 The Anatomy of a Bash Prompt

When you open a terminal, you see a *prompt* — a piece of text that signals Bash is ready to receive input. The default prompt in most Linux distributions looks like this:

```
user@hostname:~/current_directory$
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Prompt Symbols you MUST remember for KTU exams:**
> - `$` — Indicates a **normal user** (unprivileged) shell. Most IoT device logins land here.
> - `#` — Indicates the **root/superuser** shell. Dangerous! Can wipe the entire filesystem.
> - `~` — A shorthand for the current user's **home directory** (`/home/username`).

### 1.4 Standard Bash Command-Line Structure

Every Bash command follows a strict three-token structure:

```
command [options] [arguments]
```

For example:

```
ls -la /var/log
```

- `ls` → the **command** (list directory).
- `-la` → combined **options** (long format + show hidden).
- `/var/log` → the **argument** (the target directory).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Bash Operation

Bash can be analyzed as a system with three independent processing stages. Understanding this is the key to scoring full marks in KTU theory questions.

**Pillar 1 — Command Parsing (Lexical Analysis)**
Bash reads the line character by character and breaks it into tokens. It identifies:
- **Metacharacters**: reserved symbols that have special meaning (e.g., `|`, `>`, `<`, `&`, `;`).
- **Words**: sequences of non-metacharacter characters.
- **Operators**: control operators like `&&`, `||`, `;`, `|`.

**Pillar 2 — Expansion (Preprocessing Stage)**
Before any command is executed, Bash performs a *chain of substitutions* on the parsed tokens:
1. **Brace expansion** → `file{1..3}.txt` becomes `file1.txt file2.txt file3.txt`.
2. **Tilde expansion** → `~` expands to the home directory path.
3. **Parameter and variable expansion** → `$HOME`, `$PATH`, `$?`.
4. **Command substitution** → `` `cmd` `` or `$(cmd)` is replaced by the command's output.
5. **Arithmetic expansion** → `$((2+2))` becomes `4`.
6. **Word splitting** → splits the result of unquoted expansions on whitespace.
7. **Pathname expansion (Globbing)** → `*.log` becomes a list of all matching files.
8. **Quote removal** → strips the quoting characters that protected the text.

**Pillar 3 — Execution (Redirection + Fork/Exec)**
The final expanded command line is executed. If redirection operators (`>`, `<`, `>>`, `2>`) are present, file descriptors are rewired first. Then the shell *forks* a child process and uses `execve()` to replace it with the target program.

### 2.2 Quoting Rules — The "Shield Mechanics"

Quotes are essential in Bash because the shell aggressively splits and expands tokens. There are three levels of "shielding":

- **Backslash `\`** — Escapes the *single next* character. Lightest shield.
- **Single quotes `'...'`** — Strongest shield. NOTHING is expanded inside, not even `$`.
- **Double quotes `"..."`** — Medium shield. Variable expansion and command substitution still happen, but word splitting is suppressed.

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Table — Memorize this entire table for Module 1.**

| Concept | Symbol / Syntax | Expansion Result / Behavior | Used In IoT Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| Home directory | `~` | `/home/user` | Navigating to project workspace |
| Previous directory | `~-` | Last working directory | Quickly toggling between dirs |
| Current directory | `.` | The directory itself | Running local scripts `./build.sh` |
| Parent directory | `..` | One level up | Cross-compiler include paths |
| Variable | `$VAR` | Value of `VAR` | `$DEVICE_IP`, `$PORT` |
| Command substitution | `$(cmd)` | Output of `cmd` | `ID=$(esptool read_mac)` |
| Process exit status | `$?` | 0=success, non-zero=error | `if [ $? -eq 0 ]` |
| Background process | `&` | Runs async, returns PID | `minicom &` to free terminal |
| Pipe | `|` | Chains stdout of cmd1 to stdin of cmd2 | `cat log | grep ERROR` |
| Redirect stdout | `>` | Overwrite file | `dmesg > boot.log` |
| Redirect stdout (append) | `>>` | Append to file | `echo "OK" >> status.txt` |
| Redirect stderr | `2>` | Send errors to file | `make 2> build.err` |
| Logical AND | `&&` | Run cmd2 only if cmd1 succeeds | `make && flash` |
| Logical OR | `||` | Run cmd2 only if cmd1 fails | `ping -c1 host || echo DOWN` |
| Glob (any chars) | `*` | Matches any string | `rm *.tmp` |
| Glob (single char) | `?` | Matches one character | `ls file?.txt` |
| Char class | `[abc]` | Matches a, b, or c | `cp img[12].png ./` |
| History expansion | `!!` | Re-runs the last command | `sudo !!` to retry as root |
| Last argument | `!$` | The last word of previous cmd | `mkdir test && cd !$` |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**, the same shell command pattern is used in **DevOps pipelines** to flash thousands of edge sensors:

```bash
for DEVICE in $(cat device_list.txt); do
  avrdude -c arduino -p m328p -P /dev/ttyUSB$DEVICE -U flash:w:firmware.hex
done
```

This is the same Bash the KTU examiner expects you to know. Mastering it is the difference between *writing code* and *shipping hardware*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: How Bash Evaluates a Compound Pipeline

Let's trace, step by step, how Bash converts a *user-typed pipeline* into a *running process graph*. This is a guaranteed **14-mark analytical question** in KTU ESE.

**The typed command line:**

```
ls -l *.log 2> errors.txt | grep "ERROR" > matches.txt
```

**Step 1 — Tokenization (Lexical Analysis)**
Bash scans left-to-right and produces a token list:

| Token | Type |
|---|---|
| `ls` | Word (Command) |
| `-l` | Word (Option) |
| `*.log` | Word (Argument, will be globbed) |
| `2>` | Operator (Redirection) |
| `errors.txt` | Word (Filename) |
| `|` | Operator (Pipe) |
| `grep` | Word (Command) |
| `ERROR` | Word (Argument, double-quoted) |
| `>` | Operator (Redirection) |
| `matches.txt` | Word (Filename) |

**Step 2 — Expansion Chain**
Before execution, Bash applies substitutions in this fixed order:

1. **Brace expansion** — no braces present, skip.
2. **Tilde expansion** — none.
3. **Parameter expansion** — none.
4. **Command substitution** — none.
5. **Arithmetic expansion** — none.
6. **Word splitting** — `*.log` is unquoted, so the *result* of the next step will be split.
7. **Pathname expansion (Globbing)** — `*.log` is replaced by the list of matching files. Suppose the directory contains `a.log` and `b.log`. The token list now reads `a.log b.log` (a list, not one token).
8. **Quote removal** — The double quotes around `ERROR` are stripped, but the content is preserved.

**Step 3 — Pipeline Decomposition**
Bash recognizes `|` and breaks the line into **two sub-pipelines**:

- **Left pipeline**: `ls -l a.log b.log 2> errors.txt`
- **Right pipeline**: `grep ERROR > matches.txt`

**Step 4 — File Descriptor Setup**
For each sub-pipeline, Bash prepares the redirections:
- `2> errors.txt` opens `errors.txt` for writing and binds it to **fd 2** (stderr) of the `ls` process.
- `> matches.txt` opens `matches.txt` for writing and binds it to **fd 1** (stdout) of the `grep` process.

**Step 5 — Process Graph Construction (The Fork/Exec Model)**

The mathematical expression for the final process graph is:

$$
P_{\text{final}} = \text{exec}_{\text{grep}} \circ \text{pipe} \circ \text{exec}_{\text{ls}}
$$

Where the `pipe` operator creates an anonymous kernel pipe (a unidirectional byte stream with two file descriptors: a read end and a write end).

The kernel-level topology is:

$$
\text{fd}_1(\text{ls}) \;\to\; \text{pipe}_{\text{write}} \;\Big\Vert\; \text{pipe}_{\text{read}} \;\to\; \text{fd}_0(\text{grep})
$$

**Step 6 — Execution**
Bash calls `fork()` to create child processes. The shell waits for the entire pipeline to finish, then reaps zombie processes and restores the terminal.

### 3.2 Variable Expansion Walkthrough (Mathematical Modeling)

Consider a user typing:

```
echo "$USER has $(( 1 + 2 )) files in ~/projects"
```

**Step A — Initial token: `echo`** (command)
**Step B — Argument token (double-quoted):**

$$
\text{arg} = "\$USER \text{ has } \$((1+2)) \text{ files in } \textasciitilde\text{/projects}"
$$

**Step C — Inside double quotes, the following are still expanded:**

1. `$USER` → parameter expansion → suppose the value is `root`. So `\$USER` becomes `root`.
2. `$((1+2))` → arithmetic expansion → evaluates to `3`.
3. `~` → tilde expansion → `/root`.

**Step D — Word splitting inside double quotes is SUPPRESSED.** This is a critical rule: the argument remains *one* token.

**Step E — Final argument passed to `echo`:**

$$
\text{arg} = \text{"root has 3 files in /root/projects"}
$$

**Step F — The output printed to stdout is exactly that string, with no further word splitting applied to the *output*.**

### 3.3 The Exit Status Algebra

Every command, when it finishes, returns a numeric status `s` to the shell. The rules are:

$$
s = \begin{cases}
0 & \text{if the command succeeded} \\
1 \text{ to } 255 & \text{if the command failed (specific value depends on the program)}
\end{cases}
$$

The exit status is stored in the special variable `$?`. The Boolean evaluation for `&&` and `||` follows this logic:

$$
\text{AND}(\text{cmd}_1, \text{cmd}_2) = \begin{cases}
\text{run cmd}_2 & \text{if } s_1 = 0 \\
\text{skip cmd}_2 & \text{if } s_1 \neq 0
\end{cases}
$$

$$
\text{OR}(\text{cmd}_1, \text{cmd}_2) = \begin{cases}
\text{skip cmd}_2 & \text{if } s_1 = 0 \\
\text{run cmd}_2 & \text{if } s_1 \neq 0
\end{cases}
$$

This algebraic property is why `make && flash` is a *safe* build-and-flash sequence in IoT toolchains — the firmware is only flashed if compilation succeeded.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Trap Commands

| Typed by Student | What Student Thinks Happens | What Bash ACTUALLY Does | Marks Lost If Wrong |
|---|---|---|---|
| `echo Hello World` | Prints "Hello World" | Prints "Hello" and "World" (two args) | 0 (still correct) |
| `echo "Hello World"` | Prints "Hello World" | Prints "Hello World" (one arg) | 0 |
| `A=5; echo $A` | Prints 5 | Prints 5 (semicolon is a statement separator) | 0 |
| `A=5 echo $A` | Prints 5 | Prints nothing (variable not yet set in subshell) | **2 marks** |
| `cd /tmp; ls *.txt` | Lists txt files | Lists txt files in /tmp | 0 |
| `cd /tmp || ls *.txt` | Lists txt files in /tmp | `cd` runs in *subshell*, current shell still in original dir! | **2 marks** |
| `echo *.c` when no `.c` files exist | Prints `*.c` | Prints literal `*.c` (null glob default in bash) | 1 mark |

> [!WARNING]
> The `cd || echo failed` pattern is the **#1 KTU trap**. The `||` is a *command operator*, not a grouping. The `cd` runs in a subshell and any directory change is lost. Use `if` blocks or `&&` for sequential logic.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block: Bash Command Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[user types raw line] --> B1{tokenize}
    B1 --> B2{alias substitution}
    B2 --> B3[brace expansion]
    B3 --> B4[tilde expansion]
    B4 --> B5[parameter expansion]
    B5 --> B6[command substitution]
    B6 --> B7[arithmetic expansion]
    B7 --> B8[word splitting]
    B8 --> B9[pathname globbing]
    B9 --> B10[quote removal]
    B10 --> C1{redirect file descriptors}
    C1 --> C2{fork child process}
    C2 --> C3[execve target program]
    C3 --> D1[wait for exit status]
    D1 --> D2[store exit in special variable]
    D2 --> E0[shell prints next prompt]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block: File System Hierarchy as Seen in IoT

```mermaid
graph TD
    root1["root directory slash"] --> bin1["bin essential user binaries"]
    root1 --> etc1["etc system configuration"]
    root1 --> home1["home user home dirs"]
    root1 --> var1["var variable data"]
    root1 --> dev1["dev device files"]
    root1 --> proc1["proc kernel and process info"]
    root1 --> tmp1["tmp temporary files"]
    home1 --> user1["user pi or developer"]
    user1 --> iotproj["iotproj project workspace"]
    iotproj --> src1["src c source files"]
    iotproj --> build1["build compiled binaries"]
    iotproj --> tools1["tools esptool avrdude"]
    dev1 --> ttyUSB0["ttyUSB0 serial port for arduino"]
    dev1 --> ttyACM0["ttyACM0 serial port for esp32"]
    dev1 --> mmcblk0["mmcblk0 raspberry pi sd card"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block: Shell I/O Redirection Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[keyboard] -->|fd 0 stdin| SH[bash process]
    SH -->|fd 1 stdout| B[terminal screen]
    SH -->|fd 2 stderr| C[terminal screen]
    SH -->|fd 1 to file| D[output dot log]
    SH -->|fd 2 to file| E[errors dot log]
    SH -->|fd 0 from file| F[input dot txt]
    G[pipe left cmd] -->|fd 1 write end| H[kernel pipe buffer]
    H -->|fd 0 read end| I[pipe right cmd]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block: Exit Status Decision Tree for `&&` and `||`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[run cmd1] --> S1{exit status}
    S1 -->|zero success| S2[run cmd2 because of and operator]
    S1 -->|nonzero failure| S3[skip cmd2 because of and operator]
    S0 --> T1[run cmd1] --> T2{exit status}
    T2 -->|zero success| T3[skip cmd2 because of or operator]
    T2 -->|nonzero failure| T4[run cmd2 because of or operator]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These four diagrams together form the **complete mental model** of Bash for KTU Module 1. Memorize the *order* of expansion steps — it is the single most-tested diagram in the question bank.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define Bash. List any four features of the Bourne Again Shell.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Bash (Bourne Again SHell)** is a free, POSIX-compliant command-line interpreter and scripting language that serves as the default login shell for most Linux distributions. It is the successor to the original Bourne Shell (`sh`) created by Stephen Bourne at Bell Labs in 1977.

**Four key features of Bash (½ mark each):**

1. **Command-line editing** — full readline support with Emacs and Vi editing modes.
2. **Command history** — the `.bash_history` file stores previously typed commands, accessible via arrow keys or `!n`.
3. **Job control** — ability to send processes to the background (`&`), suspend them (`Ctrl+Z`), and resume them (`fg`/`bg`).
4. **Shell scripting** — full programming constructs like `if`, `for`, `while`, `case`, and functions.
5. **Aliases and functions** — user-defined command shortcuts.
6. **POSIX compliance** — guarantees standard behavior across Unix-like systems.

*(Any 4 features for 2 marks, definition for 1 mark = 3 marks total.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Differentiate between single quotes and double quotes in Bash with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Single Quotes `'...'` | Double Quotes `"..."` |
|---|---|---|
| Variable expansion | **Suppressed** — `$VAR` printed literally | **Performed** — `$VAR` replaced by its value |
| Command substitution | **Suppressed** | **Performed** |
| Escape sequences | **All suppressed** except the single quote itself | **Mostly preserved** (e.g., `\\n` is NOT converted to a newline) |
| Word splitting | N/A (single token always) | **Suppressed** when wrapped in quotes |

**Examples (1 mark each):**

- Suppose `NAME=IoT`.
- `echo '$NAME device'` → outputs literally: `$NAME device` (no expansion).
- `echo "$NAME device"` → outputs: `IoT device` (variable expanded).

*(1 mark definition, 1 mark differences, 1 mark examples = 3 marks.)*

---

### Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the step-by-step process of how Bash executes the following command line. Mention all expansion stages and the final process structure.

```
cat *.txt 2> err.log | sort | uniq -c > summary.txt
```

**(b) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the standard Linux filesystem hierarchy. Identify which directories are most relevant for IoT firmware development.

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Step-by-step execution [7 marks]

The compound command consists of **two pipes** creating a three-stage pipeline: `cat` → `sort` → `uniq -c`.

**Step 1 — Tokenization [1 mark]**
Bash parses the line and recognizes:
- Commands: `cat`, `sort`, `uniq`
- Operators: `2>`, `|`, `|`, `>`
- Arguments: `*.txt`, `err.log`, `-c`, `summary.txt`

**Step 2 — Expansion [2 marks]**
- `*.txt` is unquoted → pathname expansion occurs → replaced by the alphabetically sorted list of matching files, e.g., `a.txt b.txt c.txt` (assuming three files).
- `2> err.log` is not expanded (redirection operands are literal).
- `> summary.txt` is not expanded.

**Step 3 — Pipeline Construction [1 mark]**
The kernel creates **two anonymous pipes**:
- Pipe P1 connects `cat`'s stdout to `sort`'s stdin.
- Pipe P2 connects `sort`'s stdout to `uniq`'s stdin.

**Step 4 — Redirection Setup [1 mark]**
- `cat`'s stderr (fd 2) is rewired to `err.log`.
- `uniq`'s stdout (fd 1) is rewired to `summary.txt`.

**Step 5 — Fork/Exec [1 mark]**
Bash calls `fork()` three times (once per process in the pipeline) and uses `execve()` to load each binary.

**Step 6 — Synchronization [1 mark]**
Bash calls `wait()` on the last process in the pipeline. The exit status of the *rightmost* command (`uniq`) becomes `$?` of the whole compound.

---

#### Part (b) — Linux File System Hierarchy for IoT [7 marks]

| Directory | IoT Relevance | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| `/bin`, `/sbin` | Core binaries; `mount`, `ifconfig` for embedded Linux | 1 |
| `/etc` | Network and service config; `wpa_supplicant`, `interfaces` | 1 |
| `/dev` | Device files — `ttyUSB0`, `ttyACM0`, `mmcblk0` for SD card | 1 |
| `/home` | Developer's project workspace | 0.5 |
| `/opt` | Optional third-party SDKs (e.g., ESP-IDF toolchain) | 0.5 |
| `/var` | Logs (`/var/log`), MQTT broker data | 1 |
| `/proc`, `/sys` | Live kernel and device info; used by `sysfs` in GPIO control | 1 |
| `/tmp` | Scratch space during firmware builds | 0.5 |
| `/mnt`, `/media` | Mount points for SD cards and USB drives | 0.5 |

**Diagram (to be drawn in exam — 1 mark):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    r[slash] --> b1[bin]
    r --> s1[sbin]
    r --> e1[etc]
    r --> h1[home]
    r --> d1[dev]
    r --> v1[var]
    r --> p1[proc]
    r --> sy1[sys]
    r --> t1[tmp]
    r --> m1[mnt]
    r --> o1[opt]
    h1 --> u1[user pi]
    u1 --> iot[iot workspace]
    d1 --> tty1[ttyUSB0 arduino]
    d1 --> tty2[ttyACM0 esp32]
    d1 --> mmc[mmcblk0 sd card]
```

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain redirection operators in Bash. Describe input, output, append, and error redirections with examples. How do you redirect both stdout and stderr of a command to the same file?

**(b) [7 marks]** What are environment variables? Explain `PATH`, `HOME`, `USER`, and `SHELL` with their purpose. How do you export a variable permanently to child processes?

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Redirection Operators [7 marks]

Bash associates every process with three standard streams, identified by **file descriptors (fd)**:

| Stream | File Descriptor | Default Target |
|---|---|---|
| stdin | 0 | Keyboard |
| stdout | 1 | Terminal |
| stderr | 2 | Terminal |

**The four core redirection operators [4 marks]:**

| Operator | Meaning | Example | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| `>` | Redirect stdout (overwrite) | `ls > out.txt` | `ls` output goes to `out.txt` (file is created/overwritten) |
| `<` | Redirect stdin | `sort < raw.txt` | `sort` reads from `raw.txt` instead of keyboard |
| `>>` | Redirect stdout (append) | `date >> log.txt` | Current date appended to `log.txt` |
| `2>` | Redirect stderr | `make 2> err.log` | Error messages go to `err.log` |

**Combining stdout AND stderr to the same file [3 marks]:**

There are two common ways:

1. **POSIX way** — use the `2>&1` notation. Order matters: `2>&1` must come *after* `>`.

```
command > all.log 2>&1
```

This first redirects stdout to `all.log`, then duplicates stderr to whatever stdout is *currently pointing to* (which is `all.log`).

2. **Bash shorthand** — use `&>`:

```
command &> all.log
```

This is **NOT** POSIX-portable; it is a Bash extension. For `ksh` or `dash` scripts, always use method 1.

---

#### Part (b) — Environment Variables [7 marks]

An **environment variable** is a named dynamic value that affects the behavior of running processes. They are inherited by child processes via the `execve()` call when a new program is launched.

**The four key variables [4 marks]:**

| Variable | Example Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `PATH` | `/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin` | Colon-separated list of directories Bash searches when you type a command |
| `HOME` | `/home/pi` | Current user's home directory; the value `~` expands to this |
| `USER` | `pi` | Username of the currently logged-in user |
| `SHELL` | `/bin/bash` | Absolute path of the user's login shell |

**Exporting a variable [3 marks]:**

In Bash, a variable is *local* to the current shell by default. To make it visible to all child processes, use the `export` builtin.

```
# Wrong: child processes will not see VAR
VAR=hello
./script.sh
```

```
# Correct: child processes inherit VAR
export VAR=hello
./script.sh
```

The keyword `export` marks the variable to be placed in the *environment* of subsequently executed commands. To see all exported variables, run `env`. To remove an export, use `unset`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout** — Where students commonly lose marks:
>
> 1. **Confusing `$` and `&` in front of `?`** — `$?` is the exit status, NOT `&?`. Forgetting the dollar sign is a 1-mark loss.
> 2. **Forgetting the space** in `A=5` (writing `A = 5`) — Bash parses this as running the command `A` with arguments `=` and `5`. No assignment happens.
> 3. **Order of redirections** — `command 2>&1 > file` is **WRONG**. It sends stderr to the *original* terminal, then stdout to `file`. The correct order is `command > file 2>&1`.
> 4. **Tilde expansion only works at the *beginning* of a word.** `~user/projects` works, but `/path/~user` does NOT.
> 5. **In `for` loops, the variable is NOT preceded by `$` when assigning** (`for i in 1 2 3`) but **IS preceded by `$` when used** (`echo $i`). Forgetting this is a 1-mark loss.
> 6. **Single quotes do NOT escape single quotes** — you cannot embed a single quote inside a single-quoted string. Use `'\''` (close, escape, reopen) or switch to double quotes.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Bash = Bourne Again SHell** — GNU project, POSIX-compliant, default on Linux.
- [ ] **Three-stage execution**: parsing → expansion → execution.
- [ ] **Expansion order is FIXED**: brace → tilde → parameter → command → arithmetic → word-splitting → pathname → quote-removal.
- [ ] **Prompt symbols**: `$` = user, `#` = root.
- [ ] **Standard structure**: `command [options] [arguments]`.
- [ ] **Quotes**: single = strongest shield, double = medium, backslash = single-char escape.
- [ ] **Redirections**: `<` (stdin), `>` (stdout overwrite), `>>` (stdout append), `2>` (stderr).
- [ ] **Combined redirect**: `cmd > out 2>&1` is POSIX; `cmd &> out` is Bash-only.
- [ ] **Pipes** `|` connect stdout of LHS to stdin of RHS — both run as **parallel** processes.
- [ ] **`&&` and `||`** are **short-circuit** operators, controlled by `$?`.
- [ ] **`$?`** holds the last command's exit status (0 = success).
- [ ] **`export VAR=value`** is required for a variable to be inherited by child processes.
- [ ] **IoT-relevant paths**: `/dev/ttyUSB*`, `/dev/ttyACM*`, `/dev/mmcblk*`, `/etc/network/interfaces`.
- [ ] **Tilde `~`** expands only at the *start* of a word; never in the middle.
- [ ] **No spaces around `=`** in variable assignments: `A=5` (correct), `A = 5` (wrong).
- [ ] **Glob `*`** matches any string, `?` matches one character, `[abc]` matches a class.
- [ ] **Null glob** in Bash: if `*.tmp` matches nothing, the literal `*.tmp` is passed (not an error).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip**: Module 1 questions on Bash are *almost always* either (a) tracing the order of expansion, (b) explaining redirection, or (c) writing short pipelines. Practice all three. The 14-mark question will combine theory + a small diagram of the file system or pipeline.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operating Without a GUI Interface and Python Programming on Raspberry Pi
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCIT504 - PROGRAMMING FOR INTERNET OF THINGS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Operating Without a GUI Interface and Python Programming on Raspberry Pi # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Headless Operation** is the methodology of configuring, controlling, and programming a Raspberry Pi (or any single-board computer) **without a physical display, keyboard, or mouse attached**, by accessing its operating system and command-line interface over a **network connection** using protocols such as **SSH (Secure Shell)** on **Port 22**. In the context of the KTU 2024 IoT syllabus, headless operation represents the **deployment-grade configuration** of the Raspberry Pi, where the device is treated as a remote embedded node on a network, communicating with a developer workstation or a cloud server using encrypted terminal sessions. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The TV with a Hidden Remote" Imagine a large smart TV mounted on a wall in a hotel lobby: - The TV has no buttons, no visible screen controls, and no attached keyboard. - Yet, the technician sitting in a back room can change channels, adjust volume, install apps, and update firmware. - All of this is done through a **wireless remote control (the SSH client)** sending **encrypted infrared/radio signals (the SSH protocol over TCP/IP)** to the TV (the Raspberry Pi). The **Raspberry Pi** is the TV — a powerful computer that does not *need* a screen to be useful. The **SSH client** (e.g., PuTTY on Windows, or the native `ssh` command on Linux/macOS) is the remote control. The **local network cable or Wi-Fi** is the invisible link carrying instructions. ## 1.3 Why Headless? — The IoT Reality > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In real-world IoT deployments, a Raspberry Pi is typically installed in a weatherproof enclosure on a rooftop, inside a factory machine, or beneath a streetlight. **Physically attaching a monitor is impossible.** Therefore, the headless paradigm is the *primary* mode of operation, not an optional convenience. ### Key Advantages of Headless Operation | Advantage | Engineering Significance | |---|---| | **Lower Power Budget** | No HDMI controller, GPU rendering, or USB peripherals — saves **15-25%** power | | **Reduced BOM Cost** | Eliminates the need for a dedicated monitor, keyboard, and mouse in production | | **Remote Accessibility** | Engineers can manage fleets of Pi devices from a single workstation across continents | | **Faster Boot Time** | Skips the desktop environment, reaching a usable shell in **~5-8 seconds** | | **Automation-Ready** | Directly interfaces with CI/CD pipelines, cron jobs, and Ansible playbooks | ## 1.4 Python on Raspberry Pi — The Native IoT Language > [!IMPORTANT] > **Python** is the **de facto high-level language** for Raspberry Pi IoT development because it ships pre-installed in **Raspberry Pi OS (formerly Raspbian)** and provides direct, Pythonic bindings to the **GPIO (General Purpose Input/Output)** pins through libraries such as `RPi.GPIO` and `gpiozero`. Python serves three roles on the Pi: 1. **Scripting Layer** — Orchestrating sensors, actuators, and network calls. 2. **Rapid Prototyping Layer** — Testing logic without compiling C/C++ toolchains. 3. **System Glue Layer** — Wrapping shell commands, MQTT clients, and REST APIs. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 The SSH Protocol — Theoretical Foundation **SSH (Secure Shell)** is a cryptographic network protocol defined in **RFC 4253** that operates on the **Application Layer (Layer 7)** of the OSI model, typically riding on **TCP Port 22**. It replaces the insecure **Telnet** protocol (Port 23) by encrypting all traffic using asymmetric (public-key) and symmetric (session-key) cryptography. ### SSH Connection Lifecycle (Logical Steps) 1. **TCP Handshake** — The SSH client opens a TCP socket to the server on **Port 22**. 2. **Protocol Negotiation** — Client and server exchange supported SSH versions (e.g., `SSH-2.0-OpenSSH_8.4`). 3. **Key Exchange** — Algorithms such as **Diffie-Hellman (DH)** or **Elliptic Curve DH (ECDH)** generate a shared session key. 4. **Server Authentication** — The server presents its **RSA/Ed25519 public key fingerprint**; the client verifies it (against `known_hosts`). 5. **User Authentication** — Either password-based or **public-key-based** (recommended). 6. **Encrypted Channel Established** — All subsequent commands, file transfers (`scp`, `sftp`), and port forwards (tunneling) are encrypted. > [!NOTE] > **Default Port: 22 (TCP)**. Always use this when answering KTU questions unless a custom port is explicitly mentioned. ## 2.2 Enabling SSH on a Fresh Raspberry Pi For security, since the **November 2016** release, SSH is **disabled by default** in Raspberry Pi OS. There are two standard methods to enable it: | Method | Procedure | Use Case | |---|---|---| | **`raspi-config` (On-Device)** | `sudo raspi-config` → `Interface Options` → `SSH` → `Yes` | When a temporary monitor is available | | **Headless (No Monitor)** | Create an empty file named `ssh` (no extension) in the **boot partition** of the SD card before first boot | The standard KTU lab procedure | ### Detailed Headless Setup Workflow 1. Flash Raspberry Pi OS onto a microSD card using **Raspberry Pi Imager** (or Balena Etcher). 2. Open the boot partition in a file manager. 3. Create an empty file named exactly `ssh` (this triggers the SSH daemon to start on first boot). 4. *(Optional, for Wi-Fi headless setup)* Create a `wpa_supplicant.conf` file containing the SSID and PSK of the local Wi-Fi network. 5. Insert the SD card, power the Pi, and wait **~30-60 seconds** for boot. 6. Discover the Pi's IP address using your router's admin page, `ping raspberrypi.local` (mDNS), or a network scanner like `Advanced IP Scanner`. 7. Connect via SSH: `ssh pi@192.168.1.42` (default credentials: user `pi`, password `raspberry`). ## 2.3 Essential Linux Shell Commands for Headless IoT Work A KTU student **must** memorize the following commands, as they form the backbone of every headless exam answer: | Command | Purpose | Example | |---|---|---| | `pwd` | Print current working directory | `pwd` → `/home/pi` | | `ls -la` | List all files (including hidden) with permissions | `ls -la /home/pi` | | `cd ` | Change directory | `cd /home/pi/iot_project` | | `mkdir ` | Create a new directory | `mkdir sensors` | | `cat ` | Display the full contents of a file | `cat script.py` | | `nano ` | Open the simple terminal text editor | `nano app.py` | | `chmod +x ` | Make a file executable | `chmod +x app.py` | | `sudo apt update` | Refresh the package index | Run before any install | | `sudo apt install ` | Install a software package | `sudo apt install python3-gpiozero` | | `ifconfig` / `ip a` | View network interfaces and IP | `ip a` shows `inet 192.168.x.x` | | `ping ` | Test network connectivity | `ping 8.8.8.8` | | `ps aux` | List running processes | Find runaway Python scripts | | `kill ` | Terminate a process by ID | `kill 1234` | | `scp ` | Securely copy files over SSH | `scp app.py pi@192.168.1.42:~` | | `top` / `htop` | Real-time process/resource monitor | Diagnose CPU/memory load | ## 2.4 Python on Raspberry Pi — The Developer's Toolkit > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Raspberry Pi OS (Buster / Bullseye / Bookworm) ships with **Python 3** pre-installed. The legacy `python` (Python 2) command was removed in Raspberry Pi OS Bullseye (2021). Always invoke `python3` explicitly. ### Available Python Editors and Environments | Tool | Type | Best For | |---|---|---| | **Thonny** | GUI IDE (pre-installed) | Beginners, debugging, step-through execution | | **IDLE** | GUI IDE (pre-installed) | Quick scripts, learning | | **Mu Editor** | GUI IDE | MicroPython on Pico, education | | **nano / vim** | Terminal text editors | Headless editing over SSH | | **VS Code (Remote-SSH)** | Remote IDE | Professional multi-file projects | | **Jupyter Notebook** | Browser-based REPL | Data analysis, sensor logging | ### The `RPi.GPIO` Library — The Core IoT API > [!IMPORTANT] > **`RPi.GPIO`** is a Python module that provides a class-based interface to the Pi's **40-pin GPIO header**. It is the *primary* library tested in KTU 2024 examinations. An alternative, more modern library is `gpiozero` (recommended for beginners). #### Key Functions of `RPi.GPIO` | Function | Signature | Purpose | |---|---|---| | `setmode()` | `GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BOARD)` or `GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)` | Select pin numbering scheme | | `setup()` | `GPIO.setup(pin, GPIO.IN)` or `GPIO.setup(pin, GPIO.OUT)` | Configure pin as input or output | | `input()` | `state = GPIO.input(pin)` | Read HIGH (1) or LOW (0) from a pin | | `output()` | `GPIO.output(pin, GPIO.HIGH)` | Drive a pin to 3.3V or 0V | | `cleanup()` | `GPIO.cleanup()` | Reset all pins to a safe state | | `add_event_detect()` | `GPIO.add_event_detect(pin, GPIO.RISING, callback=my_cb)` | Attach an interrupt (ISR) | ## 2.5 Pin Numbering Schemes — BOARD vs BCM The Raspberry Pi header has **40 physical pins**, but they are referred to in code using **two different schemes**: - **BOARD Mode** — Numbers the physical pins **1 through 40** in the order they appear on the header. Intuitive for hardware engineers reading schematics. - **BCM Mode** — Numbers the pins according to the **Broadcom SOC channel numbers** (e.g., `GPIO17`, `GPIO27`). Matches the `gpio` column in the official pinout diagram. > [!NOTE] > **Physical pin 11** is **GPIO17** (BCM). A student **must** know this mapping because KTU questions often give the BCM number and expect the BOARD pin (or vice versa). ## 2.6 Running a Python Script Headlessly Once you have written a Python file (e.g., `blink.py`), execute it in any of these ways over an SSH session: - `python3 blink.py` — Runs in the foreground; closing SSH will terminate it. - `nohup python3 blink.py &` — Runs in the **background**, immune to SIGHUP (logout). - `sudo python3 blink.py` — Runs with **root privileges** (required for direct GPIO access on some OS versions). - Add to **`crontab`** for automatic startup at boot. - Configure as a **`systemd` service** for production-grade daemonization. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Code Walkthrough ## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Choosing an Access Method > [!NOTE] > Use this matrix to **justify your design choice** in 14-mark KTU answers. Examiners reward reasoning, not just memorization. ``` ┌──────────────────────────┐ │ Do you have a monitor? │ └────────────┬─────────────┘ │ ┌────────────────────┴────────────────────┐ │ │ YES NO │ │ ┌───────▼────────┐ ┌────────▼────────┐ │ Use Desktop │ │ Headless Setup │ │ + VNC Viewer │ │ + SSH (Port 22) │ └───────┬────────┘ └────────┬────────┘ │ │ ┌───────▼────────┐ ┌────────▼────────┐ │ For visual │ │ For maintenance, │ │ debugging & │ │ automation, & │ │ education │ │ IoT deployment │ └────────────────┘ └─────────────────┘ ``` ## 3.2 Step-by-Step: SSH Login from a Windows / Linux Workstation ### Scenario A developer at a workstation with IP `192.168.1.10` wants to remotely log into a Raspberry Pi at IP `192.168.1.42` over a local Wi-Fi network. **Step 1 — Verify network reachability** The developer opens a terminal and pings the Pi to confirm it is online. ``` ping 192.168.1.42 ``` Expected response: `64 bytes from 192.168.1.42: icmp_seq=1 ttl=64 time=2.43 ms`. If the ping fails, check the Pi's power, the SD card, and the Wi-Fi credentials. **Step 2 — Initiate the SSH handshake** ``` ssh pi@192.168.1.42 ``` **Step 3 — Trust the host key (first connection only)** The terminal displays the **ECDSA key fingerprint**: ``` The authenticity of host '192.168.1.42 (192.168.1.42)' can't be established. ED25519 key fingerprint is SHA256:aBcDeFgHiJkLmNoPqRsTuVwXyZ1234567890. Are you sure you want to continue connecting (yes/no/[fingerprint])? ``` The developer types `yes`. The key is saved to `~/.ssh/known_hosts`. **Step 4 — Authenticate** ``` pi@192.168.1.42's password: ******** ``` **Step 5 — Welcome banner appears, shell prompt ready** ``` pi@raspberrypi:~ $ ``` The developer is now inside the Pi's Linux shell. Every command typed runs *on the Pi*, not the workstation. ## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Writing and Running a Python LED Blink Script The "Hello World" of IoT — blinking an LED on **GPIO 17** (Physical Pin 11). ### Hardware Wiring (for context in exam answers) ``` Raspberry Pi Header ┌──────────────────────────────┐ Pin 9 │ GND ──── Cathode of LED ──┤ │ │ Pin 11 │ GPIO17 ──── 330Ω Resistor ──┴── Anode of LED └──────────────────────────────┘ ``` > [!NOTE] > Always use a **current-limiting resistor** (typically **220Ω to 330Ω**) between the GPIO and the LED anode. The Pi's GPIO pins are **3.3V** tolerant and source/sink up to **16 mA** per pin (50 mA total across all pins). Exceeding these limits **permanently damages** the SoC. ### Python Source Code (Exhaustive Walkthrough) ```python # ------------------------------------------------------- # Filename : blink.py # Purpose : Blink an LED on GPIO 17 (Physical Pin 11) # Run as : sudo python3 blink.py # ------------------------------------------------------- import RPi.GPIO as GPIO # (1) Import the GPIO control library import time # (2) Import time for sleep/delay functions # --- (3) Pin Numbering Configuration --- # BOARD = physical pin numbers (1-40) # BCM = Broadcom SoC channel numbers (e.g., GPIO17) LED_PIN = 17 # Using BCM numbering for GPIO17 GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM) # (4) Tell the library we will use BCM names GPIO.setwarnings(False) # (5) Suppress "channel already in use" warnings # --- (6) Pin Setup --- GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT) # Configure pin 17 as an OUTPUT pin # --- (7) Main Loop --- try: while True: GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.HIGH) # (8) Drive pin HIGH → 3.3V → LED ON print("LED ON") # Log to stdout (visible in SSH) time.sleep(1) # Wait 1 second GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.LOW) # (9) Drive pin LOW → 0V → LED OFF print("LED OFF") time.sleep(1) # Wait 1 second # --- (10) Graceful Shutdown on Ctrl+C --- except KeyboardInterrupt: print("Stopping the program...") GPIO.cleanup() # (11) Reset all pins to a safe INPUT state print("GPIO pins cleaned up. Exiting.") ``` ### Line-by-Line Analytical Explanation 1. `import RPi.GPIO as GPIO` — Loads the C-extension Python bindings to `/dev/gpiomem`, exposing a high-level API. 2. `import time` — Standard library; provides `time.sleep(seconds)` for timing. 3. `LED_PIN = 17` — A constant for readability and easy modification. 4. `GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)` — **Mandatory** before any other GPIO call. Choosing BCM means `17` refers to `GPIO17`, not physical pin 17. 5. `GPIO.setwarnings(False)` — Cosmetic; suppresses runtime warnings that confuse beginners. 6. `GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT)` — Allocates the pin and configures the internal pull-up/pull-down resistors (none, in output mode). 7. `while True:` — Infinite loop — the heart of every embedded polling program. 8. `GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.HIGH)` — Internally writes a `1` to the GPIO output register; the pin's voltage rises to **3.3V**. 9. `GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.LOW)` — Internally writes a `0`; the pin's voltage falls to **0V (GND)**. 10. `except KeyboardInterrupt:` — Catches the `SIGINT` signal sent by `Ctrl+C`, allowing a graceful exit. 11. `GPIO.cleanup()` — **Critical** for a KTU answer. Releases all pin allocations and resets them to INPUT to prevent the LED from staying latched ON after the script exits. ## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Reading a Digital Input (Push Button) ```python import RPi.GPIO as GPIO import time BUTTON_PIN = 27 # Physical Pin 13, BCM GPIO27 LED_PIN = 17 # Physical Pin 11, BCM GPIO17 GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM) GPIO.setup(BUTTON_PIN, GPIO.IN, pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_UP) # Enable internal pull-up GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT) try: while True: if GPIO.input(BUTTON_PIN) == GPIO.LOW: # Button pressed (active-low) GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.HIGH) print("Button Pressed → LED ON") else: GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.LOW) time.sleep(0.1) # Software debounce delay except KeyboardInterrupt: GPIO.cleanup() ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pull-up resistors** are essential because the GPIO pins **float** when not connected to anything, picking up stray electromagnetic noise. The internal pull-up (`PUD_UP`) ties the pin to **3.3V** through a **~50 kΩ** resistor inside the SoC, ensuring a stable **HIGH** when the button is open. The button press pulls the pin to **GND (LOW)** — this is the **active-low** wiring convention. ## 3.5 The `gpiozero` Alternative — A Modern, Object-Oriented API ```python from gpiozero import LED, Button from signal import pause led = LED(17) # Auto-uses BCM numbering button = Button(27, pull_up=True) button.when_pressed = led.on button.when_released = led.off pause() # Blocks forever, processing events in the background ``` > [!NOTE] > `gpiozero` is recommended by the Raspberry Pi Foundation for **new projects**. It encapsulates `RPi.GPIO` in a clean object-oriented wrapper and is **exam-relevant** — KTU may ask you to compare the two libraries. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Headless Raspberry Pi Deployment Topology ```mermaid flowchart LR devWS[Developer Workstation
IP 192.168.1.10] router[Local Wi-Fi Router
192.168.1.1] rpi[Raspberry Pi 4B
IP 192.168.1.42] sensor[Sensor Array
DHT22 / PIR / MQ-2] actuator[Actuator Bank
Relay / Motor / LED] cloud[Cloud MQTT Broker
test.mosquitto.org] devWS -- SSH Port 22
Encrypted Terminal --> router router -- Wi-Fi 802.11n --> rpi rpi -- GPIO Wires --> sensor rpi -- GPIO Wires --> actuator rpi -- Publish MQTT
Port 1883 --> cloud cloud -- Subscribe
Dashboard --> devWS ``` ## 4.2 The Python-on-Pi Software Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB hw[Physical Hardware Layer
CPU, GPIO, I2C, SPI, UART] kernel[Linux Kernel
gpio-mem driver] lib1[RPi.GPIO C Extension] lib2[gpiozero Library] py[User Python Scripts
blink.py, sensor_log.py] cron[Cron / systemd] ssh[SSH Daemon sshd] hw --> kernel kernel --> lib1 lib1 --> lib2 lib2 --> py kernel --> ssh cron --> py ssh --> py ``` ## 4.3 GPIO Pin Numbering Mapping (BCM ↔ BOARD) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph BCM_Mode ["BCM Numbering - Software View"] b1[GPIO17] b2[GPIO27] b3[GPIO22] b4[GPIO4] end subgraph BOARD_Mode ["BOARD Numbering - Physical View"] p1[Pin 11] p2[Pin 13] p3[Pin 15] p4[Pin 7] end b1 -- Same pin --> p1 b2 -- Same pin --> p2 b3 -- Same pin --> p3 b4 -- Same pin --> p4 ``` ## 4.4 SSH Authentication Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client as SSH Client
PuTTY / Terminal participant Server as Raspberry Pi
sshd on Port 22 Client->>Server: TCP SYN to Port 22 Server-->>Client: TCP SYN-ACK Client->>Server: Protocol Version Exchange Server-->>Client: Supported Algorithms List Client->>Server: Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange Server-->>Client: Server Public Key (RSA/Ed25519) Client->>Server: User Authentication Request Server-->>Client: Password / Public Key Prompt Client->>Server: Encrypted Credentials Server-->>Client: Authentication Accepted Client->>Server: Encrypted Command Stream Server-->>Client: Encrypted Output Stream ``` ## 4.5 LED Blink Program State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Initialize Initialize --> LED_ON: setup complete LED_ON --> Wait_ON: GPIO.HIGH + sleep 1s Wait_ON --> LED_OFF: timeout LED_OFF --> Wait_OFF: GPIO.LOW + sleep 1s Wait_OFF --> LED_ON: timeout LED_ON --> Cleanup: Ctrl+C SIGINT LED_OFF --> Cleanup: Ctrl+C SIGINT Cleanup --> [*]: GPIO.cleanup ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PBCIT504 Module 1]** Define **headless operation** of a Raspberry Pi. List any **two** advantages of running a Pi in headless mode for IoT deployments. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Headless Operation** is the method of configuring, accessing, and programming a Raspberry Pi **without physically attaching a display (monitor), keyboard, or mouse**. It is achieved primarily through a **Secure Shell (SSH)** connection over the local network on **TCP Port 22**, allowing the developer to issue Linux commands and run Python scripts on the Pi from a remote workstation. **Two Advantages:** 1. **Reduced Power and Hardware Cost** — Without an HDMI display and USB peripherals, the Pi consumes less power and the deployment cost drops significantly, which is critical when scaling to **hundreds of IoT nodes**. 2. **Remote Manageability and Automation** — Engineers can deploy firmware updates, monitor logs, and restart services from anywhere with network access, enabling **24/7 unattended operation** in industrial or outdoor environments. *(Any two valid points from: lower BOM cost, faster boot, easier automation via cron/systemd, suitability for production enclosures, etc., are acceptable.)* --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PBCIT504 Module 1]** Differentiate between **`BOARD`** and **`BCM`** pin numbering modes in Raspberry Pi GPIO programming. Give one example mapping. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | **BOARD Mode** | **BCM Mode** | |---|---|---| | Reference | Physical pin position on the 40-pin header (1 to 40) | Broadcom SoC channel number (e.g., GPIO17) | | Audience | Hardware engineers, schematic readers | Software developers, Python coders | | Library Call | `GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BOARD)` | `GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)` | **Example Mapping:** The physical **Pin 11** on the Raspberry Pi header corresponds to **BCM GPIO17**. Thus, `GPIO.setup(11, GPIO.OUT)` (BOARD) and `GPIO.setup(17, GPIO.OUT)` (BCM) both refer to the **same physical pin**. *(Award full marks if the student clearly states the mapping with correct numbers.)* --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) > [!IMPORTANT] > Following the **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, each 14-mark question carries **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. Students are expected to attempt **ONE of TWO choices**. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PBCIT504 Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain with a neat diagram how a Raspberry Pi can be **configured and accessed headlessly** using SSH from a developer workstation. Mention the **default port number**, the **steps to enable SSH** before the first boot, and the **command to connect** from a Linux terminal. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write a complete Python script using the **`RPi.GPIO` library** to interface a **push button** (connected to **GPIO 27, Pin 13**) and an **LED** (connected to **GPIO 17, Pin 11**). The LED should glow **only while the button is pressed**. Explain each section of your code. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A **(a) Headless Configuration and SSH Access (7 Marks)** **Step 1 — Flashing the OS:** Use **Raspberry Pi Imager** to write Raspberry Pi OS to a microSD card. **Step 2 — Enabling SSH Before First Boot (Headless Trick):** Mount the **boot partition** of the SD card on the workstation and create an **empty file named `ssh`** (no extension, lowercase). This file acts as a one-time flag that tells the Pi's first-boot service to start the **SSH daemon (`sshd`)**. **Step 3 — Powering the Pi:** Insert the SD card into the Pi, connect the power supply, and wait **30-60 seconds** for the boot sequence to complete. The Pi will acquire an IP address from the router's DHCP server (e.g., `192.168.1.42`). **Step 4 — Discovering the IP Address:** Use any of the following: - Router admin page (look at the connected devices list). - `ping raspberrypi.local` (relies on mDNS / Bonjour). - Network scanner apps like **Fing** or **Advanced IP Scanner**. **Step 5 — Initiating the SSH Connection:** From a Linux/macOS terminal (or PowerShell on Windows 10+): ``` ssh pi@192.168.1.42 ``` On first connection, type `yes` to trust the host key, then enter the default password `raspberry`. **Default Port: TCP 22** **Diagram (already shown in Section 4.1):** The developer workstation connects to the Pi's `sshd` over the local Wi-Fi router. **Key Points to Remember:** - Change the default password immediately using `passwd`. - Consider setting up **key-based authentication** for production. **(b) Python Script for Button-Controlled LED (7 Marks)** ```python import RPi.GPIO as GPIO import time # --- Pin Definitions (BCM numbering) --- BUTTON_PIN = 27 # Physical Pin 13 LED_PIN = 17 # Physical Pin 11 # --- Library Configuration --- GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM) GPIO.setwarnings(False) # --- Pin Setup --- # Internal pull-up resistor enabled so pin reads HIGH by default GPIO.setup(BUTTON_PIN, GPIO.IN, pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_UP) GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT) # --- Main Logic Loop --- try: while True: input_state = GPIO.input(BUTTON_PIN) if input_state == GPIO.LOW: # Button pressed (active-low) GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.HIGH) print("Button Pressed → LED ON") else: GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.LOW) time.sleep(0.1) # Debounce delay except KeyboardInterrupt: print("Exiting cleanly...") GPIO.cleanup() ``` **Code Explanation (Exam-Worthy Points):** 1. **Pin selection (BCM 27, 17)** — Uses Broadcom numbering; alternative `GPIO.BOARD` would use 13 and 11. 2. **`pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_UP`** — Enables the internal ~50 kΩ pull-up resistor. The pin floats HIGH when the button is open. 3. **`input_state == GPIO.LOW`** — The button connects the pin to **GND** when pressed, so the logic is **active-low**. 4. **`time.sleep(0.1)`** — A 100 ms software debounce to filter out mechanical switch bounce noise. 5. **`GPIO.cleanup()`** — Releases all pin resources on exit, preventing the LED from latching in an undefined state. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, PBCIT504 Model Paper]** **(a)** Compare the **RPi.GPIO** and **gpiozero** libraries for Raspberry Pi Python programming. Which one is recommended for **beginners**, and why? List any **four essential Linux commands** used in headless Raspberry Pi operation and state their purpose. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write a Python script that reads the state of a **PIR motion sensor** connected to **GPIO 4 (Pin 7)**. When motion is detected, the script should print `"Motion Detected"` to the terminal and also log a **timestamped entry** to a file named `motion_log.txt`. Use the `datetime` module. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B **(a) RPi.GPIO vs gpiozero + Essential Linux Commands (7 Marks)** | Feature | **RPi.GPIO** | **gpiozero** | |---|---|---| | **API Style** | Procedural / function-based | Object-Oriented | | **Learning Curve** | Steeper (manual `setmode`, `setup`, `cleanup`) | Gentle (one-liner device abstractions) | | **Code Verbosity** | More lines for the same task | Concise (e.g., `led = LED(17)`) | | **Built-in Features** | Manual interrupt setup | Event-driven (`when_pressed`, `when_motion`) | | **Recommendation** | Low-level, fine-grained control | **Beginner-friendly** (recommended by Pi Foundation) | **gpiozero is recommended for beginners** because: - It hides the complexity of pin mode setup and cleanup. - Devices like `LED`, `Button`, `MotionSensor`, `Buzzer` are pre-modeled as Python classes. - The `when_pressed` / `when_released` event syntax reads almost like English. **Four Essential Linux Commands:** 1. **`ls -la`** — Lists all files (including hidden) in the current directory with permissions, owner, size, and timestamp. 2. **`cd /home/pi`** — Changes the working directory to `/home/pi` (absolute path navigation). 3. **`sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade`** — Refreshes the package index and upgrades all installed software to the latest versions. 4. **`nano blink.py`** — Opens the `nano` terminal text editor to create or modify the `blink.py` file. 5. **`scp blink.py pi@192.168.1.42:~`** — Securely copies a file from the local workstation to the Pi's home directory over SSH. *(Any four from: `pwd`, `mkdir`, `cat`, `chmod +x`, `ifconfig`, `ping`, `ps aux`, `kill`, `top` are also acceptable.)* **(b) PIR Motion Sensor Logger (7 Marks)** ```python import RPi.GPIO as GPIO from datetime import datetime PIR_PIN = 4 # Physical Pin 7, BCM GPIO4 LOG_FILE = "motion_log.txt" GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM) GPIO.setwarnings(False) GPIO.setup(PIR_PIN, GPIO.IN) # PIR output is a digital HIGH/LOW def log_motion(): timestamp = datetime.now().strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S") message = f"Motion Detected at {timestamp}\n" print(message, end="") with open(LOG_FILE, "a") as f: f.write(message) print(f"PIR sensor armed on GPIO {PIR_PIN}. Waiting for motion...") try: GPIO.add_event_detect(PIR_PIN, GPIO.RISING, callback=log_motion, bouncetime=300) while True: pass # Main thread sleeps; interrupt handles the rest except KeyboardInterrupt: print("\nShutting down.") GPIO.cleanup() ``` **Explanation of Key Constructs:** - **`datetime.now().strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")`** — Captures the current time and formats it as a human-readable string, e.g., `2024-12-15 14:32:10`. - **`with open(..., "a") as f`** — Opens the log file in **append mode**, ensuring previous entries are not overwritten. The `with` block guarantees the file is closed even on exception. - **`add_event_detect(..., bouncetime=300)`** — Registers a **hardware interrupt** so the callback fires *only* on the rising edge (LOW → HIGH transition) and ignores re-triggers within **300 ms** (debounce). - **`while True: pass`** — A low-CPU idle loop. The actual work is done by the interrupt service routine, so the main thread does nothing but wait. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Forgetting `GPIO.setmode()`** — Calling `GPIO.setup()` before `setmode()` raises a `RuntimeError`. This is the **#1 reason students lose 2 marks** in code-based questions. > 2. **Skipping `GPIO.cleanup()`** — Examiners check for graceful shutdown. Omitting `cleanup()` results in **partial credit loss**. > 3. **Confusing `BOARD` and `BCM` numbers** — Writing `GPIO.setup(11, ...)` after `setmode(GPIO.BCM)` is a **logic error**. Always cross-check the pin number. > 4. **Using `python` instead of `python3`** — On modern Raspberry Pi OS, `python` does not exist or points to a non-IoT-aware environment. Use `python3` explicitly. > 5. **Forgetting `sudo` for GPIO scripts** — On some OS images, direct GPIO memory access requires **root** privileges. > 6. **Not mentioning the `ssh` file trick** — For headless questions, the **empty `ssh` file in the boot partition** is the expected KTU answer, not a vague "enable SSH in settings". --- ## Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print or bookmark this section — it covers 90% of exam marks on this topic.** - **Headless Operation** = Using a Raspberry Pi **without a monitor/keyboard/mouse** by accessing it over a network using **SSH (Port 22)**. - The two prerequisites for a truly headless setup are: (1) an **empty `ssh` file** in the boot partition, and (2) a **`wpa_supplicant.conf`** file for Wi-Fi credentials. - **Default credentials** are user `pi`, password `raspberry` — **change immediately** in production. - The SSH command syntax is `ssh @`. - **Essential Linux commands**: `ls`, `cd`, `pwd`, `mkdir`, `cat`, `nano`, `chmod +x`, `sudo apt update`, `ifconfig / ip a`, `ping`, `scp`, `ps aux`, `kill`, `top`. - **Python 3** is the default on Raspberry Pi OS (Bullseye/Bookworm); always invoke with `python3`. - The **two GPIO numbering modes** are `GPIO.BOARD` (physical 1-40) and `GPIO.BCM` (Broadcom names like GPIO17). - **Physical Pin 11 = GPIO17 (BCM)**, **Pin 13 = GPIO27**, **Pin 7 = GPIO4**. - GPIO voltage levels: **HIGH = 3.3V**, **LOW = 0V**; max current per pin = **16 mA**. - **Pull-up resistors** prevent floating inputs; use `pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_UP` for active-low buttons. - The five `RPi.GPIO` essentials: `setmode`, `setup`, `input`, `output`, `cleanup`. - `RPi.GPIO` is **procedural**; `gpiozero` is **object-oriented** and **beginner-friendly**. - `add_event_detect()` enables **hardware interrupts**, avoiding busy-wait loops. - **`time.sleep(0.1)`** is the standard software debounce delay for mechanical switches. - **`GPIO.cleanup()`** is **mandatory** at the end of every script — failure to call it loses marks. - Headless scripts can be backgrounded with `nohup ... &` or set to auto-start via `crontab -e` / `systemd`. - The `datetime` module provides `datetime.now().strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")` for timestamped logging. - **File modes**: `"w"` overwrites, `"a"` appends; always use `with open(...)` for safe closure. - **SSH runs on TCP Port 22**; SFTP/SCP run on the **same port** by default; Telnet (Port 23) is insecure and deprecated. - **Total GPIO pins**: 40 on the header, of which **26 are usable GPIO**; the rest are GND, 5V, 3.3V, and reserved pins. - **LEDs always need a current-limiting resistor** (220-330 Ω) to prevent GPIO damage. - The `signal.pause()` function in `gpiozero` is a clean way to keep the main thread alive without burning CPU.

topics.title: Python Programming Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCIT504 - PROGRAMMING FOR INTERNET OF THINGS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Python Programming Environment ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Python Programming Environment** in the context of IoT refers to the complete ecosystem of tools, interpreters, libraries, and configuration frameworks required to develop, test, deploy, and execute Python code across heterogeneous IoT devices — ranging from full-stack edge gateways to resource-constrained microcontrollers. As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PBCIT504, Module 1)**, this environment comprises four primary pillars: 1. **Python Interpreter** (CPython, MicroPython, CircuitPython) 2. **Integrated Development Environment (IDE)** (Thonny, IDLE, VS Code, PyCharm) 3. **Package Management System** (pip, venv, conda) 4. **Target Execution Platform** (Raspberry Pi, ESP32, Raspberry Pi Pico, PC) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PBCIT504 – Module 1):** > Students are expected to understand the **differences between CPython, MicroPython, and CircuitPython**, configure a working IoT programming environment, and demonstrate proficiency in using at least one microcontroller-targeted Python variant for sensor/actuator interfacing. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Workshop" View Think of building an IoT project like **cooking a meal in a smart kitchen**: | Kitchen Component | Python Environment Equivalent | Role | |---|---|---| | Chef (you) | Programmer | Writes the recipe (code) | | Recipe book | Source code (.py files) | Contains instructions | | Kitchen stove | Python Interpreter | Executes the recipe | | Blender / Mixer | IDE (Thonny / VS Code) | Helps prepare & test recipes | | Pantry (salt, oil) | Standard Library (`os`, `time`, `json`) | Pre-available ingredients | | Online grocery store | PyPI + `pip` | Downloads extra ingredients | | Smart oven / Tandoor | MicroPython / CircuitPython | Specialized cooking for small devices | | Smart fridge / Mixer grinder | Raspberry Pi / ESP32 | The hardware that runs it | Just as a chef chooses the right tool for the dish, an IoT developer must choose the right **Python flavour** for the target hardware. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The *same Python code* can run on a powerful cloud server (full CPython) and on a tiny 4 MB microcontroller (MicroPython) — but with very different setup procedures and library availability. The "environment" bridges this gap. ### 1.3 Why Python for IoT? | Reason | Engineering Benefit | |---|---| | **High-level abstraction** | Faster prototyping of IoT prototypes | | **Dynamic typing** | Rapid iteration during sensor testing | | **Huge ecosystem** | `paho-mqtt`, `Adafruit_DHT`, `RPi.GPIO` etc. | | **Interpreted execution** | REPL-based live debugging on hardware | | **MicroPython / CircuitPython** | Runs directly on microcontrollers (no OS) | | **Industry adoption** | AWS IoT, Google Cloud IoT Core both support Python SDKs | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix ### 2.1 The Three Python Flavours for IoT #### 2.1.1 CPython (Standard Python) - **Maintainer:** Python Software Foundation (PSF) - **Use case:** Edge gateways, Raspberry Pi (Linux), cloud backends - **Memory footprint:** ~25 MB minimum (interpreter only) - **Execution model:** Compiles `.py` → **bytecode** (`.pyc`) → executed by **PVM (Python Virtual Machine)** - **Package manager:** `pip` (default) #### 2.1.2 MicroPython - **Maintainer:** Damien George (2014) - **Use case:** Microcontrollers (ESP32, STM32, Raspberry Pi Pico, pyboard) - **Memory footprint:** ~256 KB RAM, ~1 MB flash - **Execution model:** Lean CPython re-implementation with a subset of standard library - **Package manager:** `mip` (MicroPython package manager), `upip` (older) #### 2.1.3 CircuitPython - **Maintainer:** Adafruit Industries - **Use case:** Adafruit boards (Circuit Playground, Feather, Qt Py, RP2040) - **Memory footprint:** Similar to MicroPython - **Execution model:** Fork of MicroPython emphasizing **ease of use** and **USB drag-and-drop** - **Key feature:** The board appears as a **USB mass storage drive**; editing `code.py` on the drive auto-runs the program > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner's Distinguishing Point:** > When asked *"Compare MicroPython and CircuitPython"*, the expected answer must highlight: **CircuitPython = MicroPython + Adafruit libraries + beginner-friendly USB drive workflow**. CircuitPython is *not* a complete superset; it removes some advanced features in exchange for simpler APIs. ### 2.2 The Python Execution Pipeline (CPython) The standard CPython execution flow can be broken down as follows: 1. **Lexical Analysis** — Source code is tokenized into tokens (`if`, `x`, `==`, `5`). 2. **Parsing** — Tokens are arranged into an **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)**. 3. **Compilation** — AST is compiled into **bytecode** (`.pyc` file in `__pycache__/`). 4. **Interpretation** — **PVM** reads bytecode instructions and executes them via the underlying C runtime. This is a **hybrid compile-interpret** model — often called *"interpreted"* colloquially, but technically CPython *does* compile to bytecode first. ### 2.3 The Interactive REPL **REPL = Read → Eval → Print → Loop** $$ \text{REPL}_{\text{cycle}} = (R \rightarrow E \rightarrow P \rightarrow L)_{n=1}^{\infty} $$ Each cycle: - **R:** Reads a single user expression - **E:** Evaluates it - **P:** Prints the result (with `>>>` and `...` prompts) - **L:** Loops back, ready for the next input > [!NOTE] > **Why REPL matters in IoT:** On a MicroPython board, the REPL connects over the **USB serial port** (e.g., `ttyUSB0`, `COM3`) and lets the developer run live commands on the hardware — read sensor values, toggle GPIO pins, test network connectivity — **without flashing firmware**. ### 2.4 Virtual Environments & Dependency Isolation A **virtual environment** (`venv`) is an **isolated Python directory tree** containing a particular Python version and a set of installed packages. $$ E_{\text{project}_i} = \langle P_{\text{interpreter}}, L_{\text{packages}_i}, S_{\text{scripts}_i} \rangle $$ Where: - $E_{\text{project}_i}$ = the isolated environment for project $i$ - $P_{\text{interpreter}}$ = the chosen Python binary - $L_{\text{packages}_i}$ = the libraries installed only for project $i$ - $S_{\text{scripts}_i}$ = the activation scripts This solves the classic **"dependency hell"** problem: $$ \text{Conflict} = \text{ProjA}(\text{lib}_v = 2.0) \cap \text{ProjB}(\text{lib}_v = 1.5) $$ With venv, $\text{ProjA} \perp \text{ProjB}$ (independent, no shared global state). ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Reference Table | Component | Standard (CPython) | MicroPython | CircuitPython | |---|---|---|---| | **Official Maintainer** | PSF | Damien George | Adafruit | | **Target Hardware** | PCs, Servers, Pi (Linux) | ESP32, Pico, STM32 | Adafruit boards | | **Min RAM Required** | ~25 MB | ~128 KB | ~128 KB | | **Filesystem Mount** | Standard FS (ext4/NTFS) | LittleFS / FAT | FAT (USB Drive) | | **Package Manager** | `pip` | `mip` / `upip` | `circup` | | **REPL Access** | Terminal / IDE | UART serial | UART + USB Drive | | **Standard Library** | Full | Subset | Smaller subset | | **Key Missing Modules** | — | `numpy`, `pandas` (large) | `asyncio` (partial) | | **Auto-run File** | `__main__.py` | `main.py` / `boot.py` | `code.py` | | **Async Support** | Full (`asyncio`) | Partial (`uasyncio`) | Limited | | **Use Case in IoT** | Cloud, Gateway | Sensor Node | Beginner / Education | ### 2.6 Common IDEs for IoT Python | IDE | Best For | KTU-Relevant Note | |---|---|---| | **Thonny** | Beginners, MicroPython on ESP32/Pico | Default in KTU labs; bundled Python 3.x | | **Mu Editor** | MicroPython / CircuitPython | Built-in REPL pane, file sync | | **IDLE** | Quick scripts, default with Python | Comes pre-installed with CPython | | **VS Code + Pymakr** | Professional IoT development | Industry standard; intellisense for MicroPython | | **PyCharm** | Large IoT gateway projects | Heavy; not suitable for microcontrollers | | **Arduino Lab for MicroPython** | MicroPython on ESP32 | Official Arduino IDE variant | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** In industry, the typical IoT developer pipeline is **VS Code (write code) → REPL (test on hardware) → OTA update (deploy to field devices)**. Universities and KTU labs prefer **Thonny/Mu** for the same reason — minimal setup, maximal learning. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Setup, Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Setting Up a Standard CPython Environment (Windows / Linux / macOS) #### Step 1 — Install Python 3.x **Windows:** ```bash # Download installer from python.org # ✅ Tick "Add Python to PATH" during installation python --version # Expected: Python 3.11.x or later ``` **Linux (Debian/Ubuntu):** ```bash sudo apt update sudo apt install python3 python3-pip python3-venv python3 --version ``` **macOS (using Homebrew):** ```bash brew install python python3 --version ``` #### Step 2 — Verify the Interpreter Path $$ \text{Resolution} = \text{where} \rightarrow \text{PATH} \rightarrow \text{Python.exe} $$ ```bash # Windows where python # Linux/macOS which python3 ``` #### Step 3 — Create a Virtual Environment $$ E_{\text{project}} = \text{isolate}(\text{lib}_{\text{system}}, \text{lib}_{\text{project}}) $$ ```bash # Create python -m venv iot_lab_env # Activate (Windows CMD) iot_lab_env\Scripts\activate.bat # Activate (Windows PowerShell) iot_lab_env\Scripts\Activate.ps1 # Activate (Linux/macOS) source iot_lab_env/bin/activate ``` #### Step 4 — Install IoT-Relevant Packages via pip ```bash pip install paho-mqtt # MQTT client for IoT messaging pip install Adafruit-DHT # Temperature/humidity sensors pip install RPi.GPIO # Raspberry Pi GPIO control pip install requests # HTTP APIs pip install pyserial # Serial port communication ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** `pip` uses the **Python Package Index (PyPI)** — a global repository hosted at `pypi.org`. Always mention this in viva questions about `pip`. #### Step 5 — Verify Installation ```bash pip list # Expected output: paho-mqtt, RPi.GPIO, requests, ... python -c "import paho.mqtt.client as mqtt; print('MQTT OK')" ``` ### 3.2 Setting Up MicroPython on ESP32 (Full Walkthrough) This is the **most likely practical question** in the KTU lab exam. #### Step 1 — Install esptool (Firmware Flashing Utility) ```bash pip install esptool ``` #### Step 2 — Download MicroPython Firmware Download the appropriate `.bin` file for **ESP32** from: `https://micropython.org/download/esp32/` #### Step 3 — Erase Flash (Mandatory First Step) $$ \text{Flash}_{\text{erase}} = \text{esptool.py} - \text{chip esp32} - \text{erase\_flash} $$ ```bash esptool.py --chip esp32 --port /dev/ttyUSB0 erase_flash # Windows users replace /dev/ttyUSB0 with COM3, COM4, etc. ``` > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall:** Skipping the erase step causes inconsistent firmware. KTU evaluators deduct 1 mark for this. #### Step 4 — Flash MicroPython Firmware ```bash esptool.py --chip esp32 --port /dev/ttyUSB0 \ --baud 460800 write_flash -z 0x1000 esp32-20240101-v1.22.1.bin ``` The `0x1000` is the **default partition offset** for ESP32 firmware. Do **not** change it unless using a custom partition table. #### Step 5 — Connect via REPL Use any serial terminal at **115200 baud**: ```bash # Using PuTTY, Tera Term, Thonny, or screen screen /dev/ttyUSB0 115200 ``` You should see the banner: ``` MicroPython v1.22.1 on 2024-01-01; ESP32 module with ESP32 Type "help()" for more information. >>> ``` #### Step 6 — Test GPIO Blinking (Hello World of IoT) ```python from machine import Pin import time led = Pin(2, Pin.OUT) # Onboard LED on most ESP32 dev boards while True: led.value(1) # LED ON time.sleep(1) led.value(0) # LED OFF time.sleep(1) ``` > [!NOTE] > **File Persistence:** If you save this as `main.py` on the board, MicroPython will **auto-execute it on every boot** — analogous to `__main__.py` in CPython. ### 3.3 Setting Up CircuitPython on Adafruit Boards #### Step 1 — Download UF2 Firmware From `circuitpython.org/board//`, download a `.uf2` file. #### Step 2 — Enter Bootloader Mode - Double-press the **RESET** button quickly. - The board mounts as a USB drive named `BOOT` (e.g., `CPLAYBOOT`). #### Step 3 — Drag-and-Drop Firmware Copy the `.uf2` file into the `BOOT` drive. The board **auto-reboots** and appears as `CIRCUITPY`. #### Step 4 — Edit `code.py` Open `CIRCUITPY/code.py` in any text editor and write: ```python import board import digitalio import time led = digitalio.DigitalInOut(board.LED) led.direction = digitalio.Direction.OUTPUT while True: led.value = True time.sleep(0.5) led.value = False time.sleep(0.5) ``` Save the file → the board **auto-restarts and runs the new code**. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Environment Selection Decision Tree | If your target device is... | Use this Python flavour | IDE Recommended | Package Manager | |---|---|---|---| | Raspberry Pi (full Linux) | CPython 3.11+ | VS Code / Thonny | `pip` + `venv` | | Cloud Server / Docker | CPython | VS Code / PyCharm | `pip` + `venv` / `conda` | | ESP32 / ESP8266 | MicroPython | Thonny / uPyCraft / VS Code | `mip` / `upip` | | Raspberry Pi Pico (RP2040) | MicroPython / CircuitPython | Thonny / Mu | `mip` / `circup` | | Adafruit Feather / QT Py | CircuitPython | Mu / VS Code | `circup` | | STM32 Nucleo | MicroPython | uPyCraft / STM32CubeIDE | `mip` | ### 3.5 Mathematical Model: Memory Footprint Estimation For a constrained IoT device, the total memory requirement is: $$ M_{\text{total}} = M_{\text{interpreter}} + M_{\text{firmware}} + M_{\text{RAM}_{\text{user code}}} + M_{\text{heap}} $$ For a typical MicroPython on ESP32: - $M_{\text{interpreter}} \approx 1.1$ MB (flash) - $M_{\text{firmware}} \approx 300$ KB - $M_{\text{RAM}_{\text{user code}}} \leq 100$ KB - $M_{\text{heap}} \leq 50$ KB $$ M_{\text{total}} \approx 1.5 \text{ MB (flash)}, \quad \leq 200 \text{ KB (RAM)} $$ This is why **`numpy` cannot run on raw MicroPython** — it would exceed the heap limit by orders of magnitude. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Layered Architecture of the Python IoT Environment ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Application Layer
User Python Scripts] --> B[Standard Library
time, json, os, socket] A --> C[Third-Party Libraries
paho-mqtt, RPi.GPIO, requests] A --> D[Hardware Abstraction Layer
machine.Pin, board.LED] B --> E[Python Interpreter
CPython, MicroPython, CircuitPython] C --> E D --> E E --> F[Operating System / Firmware
Linux, FreeRTOS, Bare-metal] F --> G[Hardware Platform
Raspberry Pi, ESP32, Pico] ``` ### 4.2 CPython Execution Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A[source.py] --> B[Tokenizer
Lexical Analysis] B --> C[Parser
Builds AST] C --> D[Compiler
AST to Bytecode] D --> E[Bytecode .pyc
Stored in __pycache__] E --> F[PVM
Python Virtual Machine] F --> G[Hardware Execution
