| 2 | $V_{in} = \dfrac{D}{2^N - 1} \times V_{ref}$ | **Reverse ADC conversion** | Volts |
| 3 | $T(°C) = V_{out} \times 100$ | **LM35 temperature** (since 10 mV/°C) | °C |
| 4 | $T(°F) = \dfrac{9}{5} T(°C) + 32$ | **Celsius to Fahrenheit** | °F |
| 5 | $R_{T} = R_0 \cdot e^{B\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$ | **Thermistor (NTC) — Steinhart-Hart simplified** | Ω |
| 6 | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | **Voltage Divider (sensor as R₂)** | V |
| 7 | $f_{PWM} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{N \cdot (2^n - 1)}$ | **PWM frequency (Timer-based)** | Hz |
| 8 | $D\% = \dfrac{T_{ON}}{T_{period}} \times 100$ | **PWM duty cycle** | % |
| 9 | $P_{avg} = V_{CC} \times I_{avg}$ | **Average power consumption** | W |
| 10 | $t_{batt} = \dfrac{C_{batt} \text{ (mAh)}}{I_{load} \text{ (mA)}}$ | **Battery life (ideal)** | hours |
| 11 | $V_{L} = V_{CC} \cdot D\%$ | **DC motor average voltage (PWM)** | V |
| 12 | $\theta = \dfrac{(t_{pulse} - 1\text{ms})}{1\text{ms}} \times 90°$ | **Servo angle from pulse width** | degrees |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Viva:** Memorize formulas 1, 2, 3, 8, and 10 — these cover **>80%** of numerical questions asked in Module 2.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Precision Agriculture** — Soil moisture + DHT22 + LoRa → farmer gets SMS when irrigation is needed.
- **Smart Healthcare** — MAX30100 (PPG heart rate) + BLE → real-time vitals on phone.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** — Vibration sensor (ADXL345) + Wi-Fi → predictive maintenance alerts.
- **Smart Homes** — PIR (HC-SR501) + Relay + ESP32 → automatic corridor lighting.
- **Environmental Monitoring** — MQ-135 (air quality) + GPS + GSM → live AQI map.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The ADC Conversion Chain

**Problem (KTU-Style):** A 10-bit ADC with a reference voltage $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V reads a raw value of $D = 614$ from an LM35 temperature sensor. Find (a) the analog input voltage, and (b) the temperature in °C and °F.

### Step 1 — Identify the governing equation

The digital output of an ideal $N$-bit ADC is a linear function of the input voltage:

$$
D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^N - 1) \right\rfloor
$$

### Step 2 — Solve for $V_{in}$

Rearrange the formula by multiplying both sides by $V_{ref}$ and dividing by $2^N - 1$:

$$
V_{in} = \frac{D}{2^N - 1} \times V_{ref}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values

For a 10-bit ADC, $2^N - 1 = 2^{10} - 1 = 1023$. With $D = 614$ and $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V:

$$
V_{in} = \frac{614}{1023} \times 5.0
$$

$$
V_{in} = 0.6002 \times 5.0
$$

$$
V_{in} = 3.001 \text{ V}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the LM35 transfer function

LM35 outputs **10 mV per °C**, i.e. $V_{out} = 0.010 \cdot T$. Therefore:

$$
T = \frac{V_{in}}{0.010} = \frac{V_{out}}{0.010}
$$

$$
T = \frac{3.001}{0.010} = 300.1 \text{ °C}
$$

*(This is unrealistic for LM35 which is rated 0–150 °C; in practice the ADC would saturate. A real exam would use a smaller value like $D = 307$.)*

### Step 5 — Convert to Fahrenheit

$$
T_F = \frac{9}{5} T_C + 32
$$

$$
T_F = \frac{9}{5} \times 300.1 + 32
$$

$$
T_F = 1.8 \times 300.1 + 32
$$

$$
T_F = 540.18 + 32 = 572.18 \text{ °F}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** Always state the *units* in the final answer. Writing "300.1" without "°C" costs a half-mark. Writing the *formula* before the *substitution* scores the "method" mark.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — PWM Duty Cycle and DC Motor Speed Control

**Problem:** An Arduino's Timer0 produces a PWM with frequency $f_{PWM} \approx 490$ Hz. The motor driver receives a duty cycle of $D\% = 75\%$. If the supply is $V_{CC} = 12$ V, find (a) the average voltage seen by the DC motor, and (b) the approximate no-load RPM given motor constant $K_v = 300$ RPM/V.

### Step 1 — Recall the duty-cycle relation

$$
V_{avg} = V_{CC} \times \frac{D\%}{100}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute

$$
V_{avg} = 12 \times \frac{75}{100} = 12 \times 0.75 = 9.0 \text{ V}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply motor constant

The motor's no-load speed is approximately $N = K_v \times V_{avg}$:

$$
N = 300 \text{ RPM/V} \times 9.0 \text{ V} = 2700 \text{ RPM}
$$

### Step 4 — Period of the PWM waveform

$$
T_{PWM} = \frac{1}{f_{PWM}} = \frac{1}{490} \approx 2.041 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{ON} = D\% \times T_{PWM} = 0.75 \times 2.041 \approx 1.531 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{OFF} = T_{PWM} - T_{ON} \approx 0.510 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** The motor's inertia is far larger than $T_{PWM}$, so it averages the pulses mechanically. This is why PWM can act like a true variable DC supply for motors.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Voltage Divider for Resistance-Based Sensors

**Problem (Photo-resistor / LDR):** An LDR and a fixed $R_1 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ form a divider with $V_{CC} = 5$ V. The ADC reads $D = 512$ (≈ half scale). Find the LDR resistance.

### Step 1 — Compute analog input voltage

$$
V_{in} = \frac{512}{1023} \times 5.0 \approx 2.502 \text{ V}
$$

### Step 2 — Voltage divider formula (LDR is $R_2$)

$$
V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $R_2$

$$
R_2 = R_1 \cdot \frac{V_{out}}{V_{CC} - V_{out}}
$$

$$
R_2 = 10000 \cdot \frac{2.502}{5.0 - 2.502} = 10000 \cdot \frac{2.502}{2.498}
$$

$$
R_2 = 10000 \times 1.0016 \approx 10016 \text{ }\Omega \approx 10\text{ k}\Omega
$$

So the LDR's resistance equals $R_1$ when the ADC reads half-scale — a useful calibration check.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Condition | Recommended Sensor | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Need accurate ±0.5 °C | DS18B20 (digital, 1-Wire) | Calibrated, noise-immune |
| Need cheap, fast ambient | LM35 (analog) | 10 mV/°C, no calibration |
| Indoor air quality | MQ-135 | Detects NH₃, NOx, CO₂ |
| Outdoor gas | MQ-7 (CO specific) | Heated sensor, periodic warm-up |
| Long range, low data | LoRa SX1278 + DHT22 | km range, years on battery |
| Indoor automation | ESP32 + PIR + Relay | Integrated Wi-Fi/BLE |
| Vehicle/asset tracking | SIM800 + GPS NEO-6M | Works anywhere with cellular |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IoT Hardware Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pwr[Power Supply 5V or 3.3V] --> mcu[Microcontroller Arduino or ESP32]
    batt[Li-Ion Battery] --> pwr
    sol[USB or Solar] --> pwr
    mcu --> s1[Analog Sensor LM35 LDR]
    mcu --> s2[Digital Sensor DHT11 DS18B20 PIR]
    mcu --> s3[Comm Module ESP WiFi HC05 BT]
    mcu --> a1[Actuator LED Buzzer]
    mcu --> a2[Driver L293D Relay]
    mcu --> d1[Display 16x2 LCD OLED]
    s1 --> a1
    s2 --> a1
    s3 --> cloud[Cloud Server MQTT HTTP]
    cloud --> mobile[User Mobile App]
```

## 4.2 Sensor-Actuator-Control Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    env[Physical Environment] -->|stimulus| sens[Sensor]
    sens -->|analog or digital| mcu[Microcontroller ADC GPIO]
    mcu -->|decision logic| act[Actuator Driver]
    act -->|action| env
    mcu -->|telemetry| net[Communication Module]
    net -->|internet| cloud[Cloud Dashboard]
    cloud -->|commands| mcu
```

## 4.3 PWM Servo Angle Generation (Timing Diagram in Block Form)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pwm[PWM Generator 50 Hz] --> t1[Period 20 ms]
    t1 --> p1[Pulse 1 ms equals 0 degrees]
    t1 --> p2[Pulse 1.5 ms equals 90 degrees]
    t1 --> p3[Pulse 2 ms equals 180 degrees]
    p1 --> s[Servo Motor]
    p2 --> s
    p3 --> s
```

## 4.4 Communication Module Selection Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start{Need wireless?}
    start -->|Yes| dist{Range required?}
    start -->|No| wired[Use UART or I2C or SPI]
    dist -->|less than 10 m| bt[Bluetooth HC05 or HM10 BLE]
    dist -->|10 to 100 m| wifi[WiFi ESP8266 ESP32]
    dist -->|100 m to 10 km| lora[LoRa SX1278]
    dist -->|Global| gsm[GSM SIM800 or NB-IoT]
    bt --> phone[Smartphone App]
    wifi --> cloud[AWS Azure Firebase]
    lora --> gw[LoRa Gateway]
    gsm --> web[Web API over cellular]
    gw --> cloud
```

## 4.5 Analog Sensor Read Pipeline (Data Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    phys[Physical Quantity Temperature Light] --> elec[Sensor Transducer]
    elec -->|mV| cond[Signal Conditioning Amplifier Filter]
    cond -->|0 to 5 V| mcuADC[Microcontroller ADC 10 or 12 bit]
    mcuADC -->|digital D| code[Embedded C Code Mapping]
    code -->|physical unit| app[Display or Cloud Send]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safety Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric and prefixed (e.g., `pwr`, `mcu`, `s1`, `dist`, `gw`). No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`) are used as standalone IDs. All labels are plain uppercase / lowercase text — no markdown formatting inside double-quoted strings.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]`
**Differentiate between an analog sensor and a digital sensor. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Analog Sensor | Digital Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Continuous voltage/current in a range | Discrete HIGH/LOW or serial data |
| ADC needed? | Yes — microcontroller must convert | No (protocol data is already digital) |
| Noise immunity | Lower — susceptible to EMI | Higher — error-checked protocols |
| Example | **LM35** (temperature) | **DS18B20** (1-Wire temperature) |
| Wiring | 3 pins (VCC, GND, OUT) | 2–4 pins (VCC, GND, DATA, optional INT) |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition of each, 1 for examples.)*

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of a relay in an IoT system. Why can't a microcontroller GPIO directly drive an AC fan?**

**Model Answer:**
- A relay is an **electromagnetic switch** that uses a low-power DC signal (e.g., 5 V, ~70 mA from a GPIO) to control a high-power circuit (AC mains 230 V, several amperes).
- A microcontroller GPIO can source only **~20 mA at 3.3 V or 5 V** — far too little to drive an AC fan's coil.
- Directly connecting the GPIO to AC would **destroy the microcontroller and risk electrocution**.
- The relay also provides **galvanic isolation** — the high-voltage and low-voltage sides share no electrical connection, only a magnetic field.

*(3 marks: 1 for relay role, 1 for current/voltage limitation, 1 for isolation aspect.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]`

**A. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **five major families of additional hardware** in an IoT node. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The five families are:
1. **Sensors** — perceive the environment (LM35, DHT22, PIR, MQ-135, LDR).
2. **Actuators** — act on the environment (LED, DC motor, servo, relay, buzzer).
3. **Communication Modules** — transmit data (ESP8266 Wi-Fi, HC-05 BT, LoRa, GSM).
4. **Display Modules** — human feedback (16x2 LCD, OLED SSD1306, 7-segment).
5. **Power Modules** — energy supply (Li-Ion, buck/boost converters, TP4056 charger).

**Block diagram:**

```
[Physical World] → [Sensors] → [Microcontroller] → [Actuators] → [Physical World]
                            ↕
                  [Communication Module] ↔ [Cloud / Mobile]
                            ↕
                       [Display Module]
                            ↕
                  [Power Management Unit]
```

The microcontroller is the central decision-maker; sensors feed it data; actuators execute its decisions; communication modules relay information; displays inform humans; and the power unit keeps everything alive.

*(7 marks: 2 for naming families, 2 for diagram, 2 for explanation, 1 for conclusion.)*

**A. (b)** An **LM35** temperature sensor is connected to the **A0** pin of an **Arduino Uno**. The 10-bit ADC gives a reading of **$D = 307$** with $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V. Calculate the temperature in **°C and °F**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert ADC value to voltage:**

$$
V_{in} = \frac{307}{1023} \times 5.0
$$

$$
V_{in} = 0.3001 \times 5.0 = 1.5005 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply LM35 transfer function (10 mV / °C):**

$$
T(°C) = \frac{V_{in}}{0.010} = \frac{1.5005}{0.010} = 150.05 \text{ °C}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Fahrenheit:**

$$
T(°F) = \frac{9}{5} \times 150.05 + 32
$$

$$
T(°F) = 270.09 + 32 = 302.09 \text{ °F}
$$

*(7 marks: 2 for formula, 2 for voltage, 2 for temperature, 1 for final answer with units.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**B. (a)** Compare **Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, ZigBee, and LoRa** communication modules in terms of range, data rate, power consumption, and typical IoT application. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Bluetooth (HC-05) | Wi-Fi (ESP8266) | ZigBee (XBee S2C) | LoRa (SX1278) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Standard** | IEEE 802.15.1 | IEEE 802.11 b/g/n | IEEE 802.15.4 | Proprietary / LoRaWAN |
| **Frequency** | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 GHz / 5 GHz | 2.4 GHz | 433/868/915 MHz |
| **Range** | ~10 m | ~50 m | ~100 m | **2–10 km** |
| **Data Rate** | 1–3 Mbps | 54–150 Mbps | 250 kbps | 0.3–50 kbps |
| **Power** | Low (10–50 mA) | High (100–300 mA) | Low (30 mA) | **Very low (10–20 mA TX)** |
| **Topology** | P2P / Piconet | Star | **Mesh** | Star (via gateway) |
| **Typical App** | Phone-to-device | Cloud IoT cameras | Home automation mesh | Agricultural / forest sensors |

**Conclusion:** Bluetooth is best for short-range phone pairing, Wi-Fi for high-bandwidth cloud apps, ZigBee for reliable indoor mesh, and LoRa for long-range, low-power outdoor telemetry.

*(7 marks: 1 per row, 1 for conclusion.)*

**B. (b)** A **DC motor** is driven by a **PWM signal at 490 Hz** with a duty cycle of **60%** from a **12 V** supply. The motor has a constant $K_v = 200$ RPM/V. Calculate (i) the average motor voltage, (ii) the no-load RPM, and (iii) the ON-time of each PWM pulse. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Average motor voltage:**

$$
V_{avg} = V_{CC} \times \frac{D\%}{100} = 12 \times 0.60 = 7.2 \text{ V}
$$

**(ii) No-load RPM:**

$$
N = K_v \times V_{avg} = 200 \text{ RPM/V} \times 7.2 \text{ V} = 1440 \text{ RPM}
$$

**(iii) PWM period and ON-time:**

$$
T_{period} = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{490} \approx 2.041 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{ON} = D\% \times T_{period} = 0.60 \times 2.041 = 1.225 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{OFF} = T_{period} - T_{ON} = 0.816 \text{ ms}
$$

*(7 marks: 2 each for parts i, ii, and iii. Always include units.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "-1" in $2^N - 1$** — using 1024 instead of 1023 introduces 0.1% error. Examiners *do* deduct marks.
> 2. **Mixing up LM35 vs. TMP36** — TMP36 outputs 750 mV at 25 °C (offset), LM35 outputs 250 mV at 25 °C (no offset). Mentioning the wrong sensor formula is an instant 1-mark loss.
> 3. **Omitting units** — writing "150" instead of "150 °C" costs half a mark consistently.
> 4. **Confusing servo pulse ranges** — students write "1–2 ms for 0–180°" but it's actually 1 ms = 0° and 2 ms = 180° with linear interpolation.
> 5. **Battery life calculation** — forgetting to multiply efficiency (typically 0.8 for buck converters) when asked for *real* battery life.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 / Additional Hardware**
> - ✅ **Additional Hardware** = sensors + actuators + comms + displays + power.
> - ✅ **LM35**: 10 mV/°C, no offset, 0–150 °C range.
> - ✅ **DHT11** = digital humidity + temp, 1-wire protocol, ±2 °C accuracy.
> - ✅ **DS18B20** = 1-Wire digital temp, ±0.5 °C, unique 64-bit ROM ID.
> - ✅ **PIR (HC-SR501)** = Passive Infrared, detects motion via body heat, 3-pin (VCC, OUT, GND).
> - ✅ **MQ-135** = air quality, needs 24-hour preheat for stability.
> - ✅ **Relay** = electromagnetic switch, 5 V coil, isolates MCU from AC.
> - ✅ **Servo** = PWM 50 Hz, 1–2 ms pulse → 0°–180° angle.
> - ✅ **DC motor** = continuous rotation, speed ∝ PWM duty cycle, needs H-bridge for direction.
> - ✅ **ESP32** = 3.3 V logic, NOT 5 V tolerant (common damage cause).
> - ✅ **LoRa** = long range (km), low data rate, low power — perfect for battery sensors.
> - ✅ **BLE (HM-10)** = Bluetooth Low Energy, iBeacon compatible.
> - ✅ **16x2 LCD** = HD44780 controller, uses 6 digital pins in 4-bit mode, or 2 pins with I²C backpack.
> - ✅ **Buck converter** = step-down DC-DC, >90% efficiency vs ~40% for linear regulators.
> - ✅ **Li-Ion** = 3.7 V nominal, must use **TP4056** with protection circuit.
> - ✅ **ADC formula** (must memorize): $D = V_{in} / V_{ref} \times (2^N - 1)$.
> - ✅ **PWM duty cycle** = $T_{ON} / T_{period} \times 100$ %.

**Final KTU Mantra for Module 2:** *"If you can draw the IoT block diagram, recall the LM35 formula, name three communication modules with their ranges, and explain why we need a relay — you will comfortably clear the 14-mark Module-2 question."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Electronics Kit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT504 - PROGRAMMING FOR INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basic Electronics Kit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Basic Electronics Kit** is a curated collection of fundamental discrete electronic components, interconnection tools, and measurement instruments that form the physical foundation upon which any Internet of Things (IoT) prototype is assembled. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme for *Programming for Internet of Things (PBCIT504)*, the kit enables the student to bridge the gap between pure software programming and real-world hardware interaction — the act of *programming physical things*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** The Basic Electronics Kit is the *physical interface layer* of IoT — it comprises the passive components, active components, prototyping tools, and diagnostic instruments required to construct sensor interfacing circuits, actuator driving circuits, and microcontroller support circuitry for IoT nodes.

The kit typically includes:
1. **Passive components** (do not generate power): Resistors, Capacitors, Inductors
2. **Active components** (require external power or generate gain): Diodes, Transistors, LEDs, Voltage Regulators
3. **Electromechanical components**: Switches, Push-buttons, Buzzers, Relays
4. **Prototyping tools**: Breadboard, Jumper wires
5. **Sensors (basic)**: LDR (Light Dependent Resistor), Thermistor, Potentiometer
6. **Measurement instruments**: Digital Multimeter (DMM)
7. **Power source**: 9V battery, USB power, DC adapter

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Workshop Toolkit"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Think of the Basic Electronics Kit as the **"doctor's stethoscope and surgical toolkit"** of an IoT engineer.
>
> Just as a doctor cannot diagnose a patient without basic instruments (stethoscope, thermometer, blood pressure cuff), an IoT programmer cannot *truly* "program the internet of things" without first learning to identify, measure, and connect basic electronic components. The microcontroller is the **brain**, the software is the **thinking**, but the **electronic kit** is the **body and senses** through which the brain perceives and acts upon the physical world.

A more direct analogy: consider building a **road**.
- **Resistors** = Speed breakers (control flow)
- **Capacitors** = Toll booths with storage (store and release charge)
- **Diodes** = One-way streets (allow current only in one direction)
- **Transistors** = Traffic signal controllers (switch/amplify)
- **LEDs** = Street lamps (visual indicators)
- **Breadboard** = The empty plot of land where you prototype the road
- **Multimeter** = The surveyor's tool to measure, test, and verify

---

## 1.3 Visual Component Identification

A typical **Basic Electronics Kit for IoT** is shown conceptually in the component grid below:

| Category | Component | Standard Symbol Use in IoT |
|---|---|---|
| Passive | Resistor (1/4 W, ±5%) | Current limiting for LEDs, pull-up/pull-down |
| Passive | Ceramic Capacitor (104, 103) | Decoupling, noise filtering |
| Passive | Electrolytic Capacitor (10µF–1000µF) | Power supply smoothing |
| Active | LED (Red, Green, Yellow, Blue) | Status indicator, visual output |
| Active | Diode (1N4007) | Reverse polarity protection, rectification |
| Active | Transistor (BC547 NPN / BC557 PNP) | Switching loads, driver stage |
| Active | Voltage Regulator (7805) | 5V rail from 9V battery |
| Sensor | LDR (Light Dependent Resistor) | Light intensity sensing |
| Sensor | Thermistor (NTC 10k) | Temperature sensing |
| Sensor | Potentiometer (10k) | Analog value input, threshold setting |
| Output | Buzzer (5V active) | Audio alert, alarm |
| Output | Relay Module (5V coil) | Switching AC/mains appliances |
| Tool | Breadboard (830 tie-points) | Solderless prototyping |
| Tool | Jumper Wires (M-M, M-F, F-F) | Interconnection |
| Tool | Digital Multimeter | Voltage, current, resistance, continuity |
| Tool | 9V Battery + Snap connector | Portable power source |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** You are **expected to draw circuit symbols** of at least 4–5 components in the answer script. Memorizing the **IEEE standard symbols** of R, C, L, LED, Diode, Transistor (NPN/PNP), Battery, and Ground is non-negotiable for scoring full marks.

---

## 1.4 Why the Basic Electronics Kit Matters in IoT

The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes *Outcome-Based Education (OBE)*, where the graduate must be able to **build working IoT systems**. Without the basic electronics kit, an IoT "node" is impossible to construct. Specifically:

- A **sensor** (e.g., LM35, DHT11) cannot be directly connected to a microcontroller pin — it needs a **resistor divider**, a **decoupling capacitor**, and a **voltage reference** — all of which come from this kit.
- An **actuator** (e.g., a 12V DC motor or a 230V AC bulb) cannot be driven by the 3.3V/40mA pin of an ESP32 — it needs a **transistor driver** or a **relay module**, both of which depend on the basic kit.
- A **stable power supply** is impossible without a **7805 voltage regulator**, **filter capacitors**, and a **protection diode** — all from the kit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:** A typical Arduino/NodeMCU digital I/O pin can source/sink a maximum of **40 mA** (absolute max), with a recommended continuous current of **20 mA**. Most LEDs need **10–20 mA** at **1.8V–3.2V** forward voltage. This is why **current-limiting resistors** are the most-used component in any IoT kit.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Basic Electronics

Any electronic circuit, no matter how complex (whether it's a smart bulb, a weather station, or a heart-rate monitor), is built upon **three fundamental passive laws** and a set of **active device equations**.

### Pillar 1: Ohm's Law (The Master Equation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ohm's Law** is the single most important equation in the entire Basic Electronics Kit module. Every resistor calculation in the KTU exam will be derived from this.

Ohm's Law states that the voltage across a conductor is directly proportional to the current flowing through it, provided the physical conditions (like temperature) remain constant.

Mathematically:

$$V = I \times R$$

where:
- $V$ = Voltage across the resistor (in **Volts**, V)
- $I$ = Current flowing through the resistor (in **Amperes**, A)
- $R$ = Resistance of the resistor (in **Ohms**, $\Omega$)

The two rearranged forms are equally important:

$$I = \frac{V}{R} \qquad \text{and} \qquad R = \frac{V}{I}$$

### Pillar 2: Power Dissipation

Every resistor in the kit dissipates heat. The power is given by:

$$P = V \times I = I^{2} R = \frac{V^{2}}{R}$$

where $P$ is in **Watts (W)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Significance:** A standard 1/4 W (0.25 W) carbon-film resistor (the most common in IoT kits) can safely dissipate **0.25 Watts** continuously. If a calculation yields a higher value, you must select a higher-wattage resistor (e.g., 1/2 W or 1 W) or risk resistor burnout — a common failure in student IoT projects.

### Pillar 3: Kirchhoff's Laws

Since IoT circuits are rarely a single loop, we need Kirchhoff's two laws to analyze multi-loop networks:

**Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL):** The algebraic sum of currents entering a node equals the sum leaving it.

$$\sum I_{in} = \sum I_{out}$$

**Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL):** The algebraic sum of voltages around any closed loop is zero.

$$\sum V_{loop} = 0$$

---

## 2.2 Resistors — The "Workhorse" of the Kit

### 2.2.1 Types of Resistors in the Kit

| Type | Symbol | Use in IoT |
|---|---|---|
| Fixed Carbon Film | $R$ | General purpose, current limiting |
| Variable (Potentiometer) | $R$ with arrow | Threshold setting, analog input |
| LDR (Photo-resistor) | $R$ with arrows on it | Light sensor |
| Thermistor (NTC) | $R$ with $-t^{\circ}$ | Temperature sensor |

### 2.2.2 Resistor Color Code (4-Band)

The color code is the **language of resistors** — every KTU exam will include a "decode this color band" question.

| Color | Digit | Multiplier | Tolerance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Black | 0 | $10^{0} = 1$ | — |
| Brown | 1 | $10^{1}$ | ±1% |
| Red | 2 | $10^{2}$ | ±2% |
| Orange | 3 | $10^{3}$ | — |
| Yellow | 4 | $10^{4}$ | — |
| Green | 5 | $10^{5}$ | ±0.5% |
| Blue | 6 | $10^{6}$ | ±0.25% |
| Violet | 7 | $10^{7}$ | ±0.1% |
| Grey | 8 | $10^{8}$ | ±0.05% |
| White | 9 | $10^{9}$ | — |
| Gold | — | $10^{-1} = 0.1$ | ±5% |
| Silver | — | $10^{-2} = 0.01$ | ±10% |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic:** *"**B**ig **B**rown **R**abbits **O**ften **Y**ield **G**reat **B**ig **V**ocal **G**runts **W**hile **G**ently **S**lithering"* — the first letter of each word gives the color order from Black to Silver.

**Example:** A resistor with bands **Yellow – Violet – Red – Gold** = $47 \times 10^{2} = 4700\ \Omega = 4.7\ \text{k}\Omega$ with ±5% tolerance.

### 2.2.3 Standard Resistor Series (E12)

IoT kits ship with resistors from the **E12 standard series**, where each value is approximately $\sqrt[12]{10} \approx 1.21$ times the previous:

$$\{10,\ 12,\ 15,\ 18,\ 22,\ 27,\ 33,\ 39,\ 47,\ 56,\ 68,\ 82\}$$

Multiplied by decades ($1\ \Omega$, $10\ \Omega$, $100\ \Omega$, $1\ \text{k}\Omega$, $10\ \text{k}\Omega$, $100\ \text{k}\Omega$, $1\ \text{M}\Omega$).

---

## 2.3 Capacitors — The "Charge Reservoirs"

A capacitor stores electrical energy in an electric field. The fundamental equation is:

$$Q = C \times V$$

where:
- $Q$ = Charge stored (in **Coulombs**, C)
- $C$ = Capacitance (in **Farads**, F)
- $V$ = Voltage across the capacitor

The **energy stored** in a capacitor is:

$$E = \frac{1}{2} C V^{2}$$

### 2.3.1 The RC Time Constant (Crucial for IoT)

When a resistor and capacitor are in series, the time taken for the capacitor voltage to reach ~63.2% of the source voltage (or discharge to ~36.8%) is the **time constant**:

$$\tau = R \times C$$

After $5\tau$, the capacitor is considered **fully charged or discharged** (>99%).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in IoT:** The RC time constant governs **software debouncing** of push-buttons, **timing** of 555-based circuits, and **analog filter** cutoff frequencies. The cutoff frequency of a simple RC low-pass filter is:

$$f_{c} = \frac{1}{2 \pi R C}$$

### 2.3.2 Capacitor Code Reading (Ceramic Disc)

Ceramic capacitors use a 3-digit code. The first two digits are the value, the third is the power-of-10 multiplier, and the result is in **picofarads (pF)**.

**Example:** Code **104** → $10 \times 10^{4} = 100{,}000\ \text{pF} = 100\ \text{nF} = 0.1\ \mu F$

> [!TIP]
> Common IoT kit capacitor codes: **103** = 10 nF, **104** = 100 nF (decoupling), **105** = 1 µF.

---

## 2.4 Diodes and LEDs — The "One-Way Valves"

A diode allows current to flow in only one direction (anode → cathode). The forward voltage drop $V_{F}$ for a standard silicon diode is approximately **0.7 V**.

For an **LED** (Light Emitting Diode), the forward voltage is color-dependent:

| LED Color | Typical $V_{F}$ | Wavelength |
|---|---|---|
| Red | 1.8 – 2.2 V | 620–750 nm |
| Yellow | 2.0 – 2.4 V | 570–590 nm |
| Green | 2.0 – 3.0 V | 495–570 nm |
| Blue | 3.0 – 3.6 V | 450–495 nm |
| White | 3.0 – 3.6 V | Full spectrum |

### 2.4.1 The LED Current-Limiting Resistor Formula (HIGH-YIELD)

This is the **single most asked derivation** in KTU exams related to this module.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LED Current-Limiting Resistor:**

$$R = \frac{V_{supply} - V_{F}}{I_{F}}$$

where:
- $V_{supply}$ = Source voltage (e.g., 5 V from Arduino, 3.3 V from ESP32)
- $V_{F}$ = LED forward voltage (color-dependent, from the table above)
- $I_{F}$ = Desired LED current (typically **10–20 mA**)

---

## 2.5 Transistors as Switches (BC547 NPN)

The BC547 is an NPN bipolar junction transistor (BJT) used widely in IoT kits to switch loads (relays, buzzers, motors) that need more current than the microcontroller pin can supply.

The transistor operates in **saturation** (fully ON) when:

$$I_{B} \geq \frac{I_{C}}{\beta_{DC}}$$

where:
- $I_{B}$ = Base current
- $I_{C}$ = Collector current (load current)
- $\beta_{DC} = h_{FE}$ = DC current gain (for BC547, typically **110–800**, assume **100** for safety)

The base resistor is then:

$$R_{B} = \frac{V_{IO} - V_{BE}}{I_{B}}$$

where $V_{BE} \approx 0.7\ \text{V}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world IoT Example:** A 5V relay coil draws ~70 mA. An ESP32 GPIO can only source 12 mA. So we use a BC547 as a switch: GPIO → base resistor (1 kΩ) → base of BC547 → relay coil → 5V. The transistor "amplifies" the weak GPIO signal to drive the heavy relay load.

---

## 2.6 Voltage Divider Rule (VDR)

A voltage divider is two resistors in series across a source, with the output taken from the junction. It is the **foundation of analog sensor interfacing** in IoT.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Voltage Divider Equation:**

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \times \frac{R_{2}}{R_{1} + R_{2}}$$

The current through the divider (for sizing) is:

$$I = \frac{V_{in}}{R_{1} + R_{2}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **IoT Application:** An LDR + fixed resistor forms a voltage divider. The output voltage depends on light intensity, and an analog pin of the microcontroller reads this voltage to compute ambient light.

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference)

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ohm's Law | $V = I \cdot R$ | V, A, $\Omega$ | All resistor problems |
| 2 | Power Dissipation | $P = V \cdot I = I^{2} R = V^{2}/R$ | W | Resistor wattage selection |
| 3 | Capacitor Charge | $Q = C \cdot V$ | C, F, V | Energy storage problems |
| 4 | Capacitor Energy | $E = \frac{1}{2} C V^{2}$ | J | Energy calculations |
| 5 | RC Time Constant | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | seconds | Timing, debouncing |
| 6 | Filter Cutoff | $f_{c} = \frac{1}{2 \pi R C}$ | Hz | Low-pass filter design |
| 7 | LED Resistor | $R = (V_{S} - V_{F}) / I_{F}$ | $\Omega$ | LED interfacing (most asked) |
| 8 | Voltage Divider | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot R_{2}/(R_{1}+R_{2})$ | V | Sensor interfacing |
| 9 | Transistor Base R | $R_{B} = (V_{IO} - 0.7) / I_{B}$ | $\Omega$ | Switching loads |
| 10 | BJT Saturation | $I_{B} = I_{C} / \beta_{DC}$ | A | Switch-mode transistor |
| 11 | Series Resistance | $R_{eq} = R_{1} + R_{2} + \cdots$ | $\Omega$ | Resistors in series |
| 12 | Parallel Resistance | $1/R_{eq} = 1/R_{1} + 1/R_{2} + \cdots$ | $\Omega$ | Resistors in parallel |
| 13 | Series Capacitance | $1/C_{eq} = 1/C_{1} + 1/C_{2} + \cdots$ | F | Capacitors in series |
| 14 | Parallel Capacitance | $C_{eq} = C_{1} + C_{2} + \cdots$ | F | Capacitors in parallel |
| 15 | Resistor Color Code | AB × 10$^C$ $\Omega$ | $\Omega$ | Decode band colors |

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Pitfall:** Students often confuse **series and parallel** combinations of resistors and capacitors. Remember: **R** adds in **series**, **1/R** adds in **parallel**; **C** adds in **parallel**, **1/C** adds in **series**. This is reverse — commit it to memory.

---

| Domain | Application of Basic Electronics Kit |
|---|---|
| Smart Home | LED dimming via PWM, relay switching of AC bulbs, LDR-based auto-lighting |
| Wearable Health | Heart-rate sensor signal conditioning, thermistor-based body temperature |
| Industrial IoT | 4–20 mA current loop signal conditioning, optocoupler driving via transistor |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **exhaustive, line-by-line derivations** of the most important results from the Basic Electronics Kit, written in the exact style a KTU External Examiner expects to see in a 14-mark answer.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: LED Current-Limiting Resistor

**Problem Statement:** Design a current-limiting resistor for a red LED ($V_F = 2.0\ \text{V}$, $I_F = 15\ \text{mA}$) to be driven by an Arduino digital pin providing $V_{supply} = 5\ \text{V}$.

**Step 1 — Identify the circuit:**
The Arduino pin → Resistor $R$ → LED anode → LED cathode → GND. This is a simple series circuit.

**Step 2 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL):**
The sum of voltage drops around the loop must equal the source voltage.

$$V_{supply} = V_R + V_{F}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $V_R = I_F \cdot R$ (Ohm's Law):**

$$V_{supply} = (I_F \cdot R) + V_{F}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to solve for $R$:**

$$I_F \cdot R = V_{supply} - V_{F}$$

$$R = \frac{V_{supply} - V_{F}}{I_{F}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values:**
$V_{supply} = 5\ \text{V}$, $V_F = 2.0\ \text{V}$, $I_F = 15\ \text{mA} = 15 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{A}$.

$$R = \frac{5 - 2.0}{15 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{3.0}{0.015}$$

$$R = 200\ \Omega$$

**Step 6 — Select the nearest standard E12 value:**
From the E12 series, the nearest value to 200 $\Omega$ is **220 $\Omega$**.

**Step 7 — Verify the power dissipation in the resistor:**

$$P_R = I_F^{2} \cdot R = (15 \times 10^{-3})^{2} \times 220$$

$$P_R = 0.000225 \times 220 = 0.0495\ \text{W} \approx 50\ \text{mW}$$

**Step 8 — Select the resistor wattage:**
A standard 1/4 W (250 mW) resistor is **more than sufficient** (50 mW << 250 mW). Use 1/4 W, ±5% carbon-film.

**Final Answer:** $R = 220\ \Omega,\ 1/4\ \text{W},\ \text{tolerance} \pm 5\%$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Voltage Divider with LDR (Light Sensor)

**Problem Statement:** An LDR has a resistance of $500\ \Omega$ in bright light and $10\ \text{k}\Omega$ in darkness. It is connected in a voltage divider with a fixed $R_1 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ across a 5 V supply. Find the output voltage at the junction in both conditions.

**Step 1 — Identify the configuration:**
The LDR is $R_2$ (between junction and GND), and $R_1 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ is between 5 V and the junction.

**Step 2 — Apply the voltage divider formula:**

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 3 — Bright Light Condition:**
$R_2 = R_{LDR} = 500\ \Omega$, $R_1 = 10{,}000\ \Omega$.

$$V_{out,bright} = 5 \cdot \frac{500}{10{,}000 + 500} = 5 \cdot \frac{500}{10{,}500}$$

$$V_{out,bright} = 5 \cdot 0.04762 \approx 0.238\ \text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Dark Condition:**
$R_2 = R_{LDR} = 10{,}000\ \Omega$, $R_1 = 10{,}000\ \Omega$.

$$V_{out,dark} = 5 \cdot \frac{10{,}000}{10{,}000 + 10{,}000} = 5 \cdot \frac{1}{2}$$

$$V_{out,dark} = 2.5\ \text{V}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
- In bright light: $V_{out} \approx 0.24\ \text{V}$ (low)
- In darkness: $V_{out} = 2.5\ \text{V}$ (mid-range)
- An ESP32's ADC (0–3.3 V) can read this directly.

**Step 6 — Sensitivity improvement (Optional):**
For better dynamic range, choose $R_1$ approximately equal to the **geometric mean** of $R_{LDR,min}$ and $R_{LDR,max}$:

$$R_1 = \sqrt{R_{LDR,min} \times R_{LDR,max}} = \sqrt{500 \times 10{,}000} \approx 2{,}236\ \Omega$$

A standard **2.2 k$\Omega$** resistor is used.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Transistor (BC547) Base Resistor for Relay

**Problem Statement:** A 5 V relay coil draws 100 mA. An ESP32 GPIO outputs 3.3 V. Use a BC547 (assume $\beta_{DC} = 100$, $V_{BE} = 0.7\ \text{V}$) as a switch. Find the base resistor $R_B$.

**Step 1 — Collector current (load current):**

$$I_C = 100\ \text{mA} = 0.1\ \text{A}$$

**Step 2 — Minimum base current for saturation:**

$$I_{B,min} = \frac{I_C}{\beta_{DC}} = \frac{0.1}{100} = 1\ \text{mA}$$

**Step 3 — Use a safety factor of 2 (so the transistor is *well* into saturation):**

$$I_B = 2 \times I_{B,min} = 2\ \text{mA}$$

**Step 4 — Voltage across $R_B$ (KVL around base loop):**

$$V_{R_B} = V_{IO} - V_{BE} = 3.3 - 0.7 = 2.6\ \text{V}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Ohm's Law to find $R_B$:**

$$R_B = \frac{V_{R_B}}{I_B} = \frac{2.6}{2 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$R_B = 1{,}300\ \Omega = 1.3\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 6 — Select the nearest E12 value:**

Use $R_B = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$ (slightly higher current, still safe) or $R_B = 1.2\ \text{k}\Omega$.

**Step 7 — Verify power dissipation:**

$$P_{R_B} = I_B^{2} \cdot R_B = (2 \times 10^{-3})^{2} \times 1000 = 4 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{W} = 4\ \text{mW}$$

A 1/4 W resistor is sufficient.

**Final Answer:** $R_B = 1\ \text{k}\Omega,\ 1/4\ \text{W}$ is used in practice.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: RC Time Constant for Button Debouncing

**Problem Statement:** A push-button is connected to a microcontroller pin with a 10 k$\Omega$ pull-up resistor and a 100 nF decoupling capacitor. Find the debounce time constant and the effective "no-bounce" settling time.

**Step 1 — Identify $R$ and $C$:**

$$R = 10\ \text{k}\Omega = 10^{4}\ \Omega, \quad C = 100\ \text{nF} = 100 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{F}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate time constant:**

$$\tau = R \cdot C = 10^{4} \times 100 \times 10^{-9} = 10^{4} \times 10^{-7}$$

$$\tau = 10^{-3}\ \text{s} = 1\ \text{ms}$$

**Step 3 — Settling time (5τ rule):**

$$t_{settle} = 5\tau = 5 \times 1\ \text{ms} = 5\ \text{ms}$$

**Step 4 — Cutoff frequency (if used as low-pass filter):**

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2 \pi R C} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 10^{4} \times 100 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1000}{2 \pi} \approx 159\ \text{Hz}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
- Any bounce signal above 159 Hz is filtered out.
- Human button bounces occur in 1–10 ms range (100–1000 Hz), so this RC filter effectively debounces.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Problem-Solving Decision Tree

| Given Quantity | Goal | Equation to Use | Step-by-Step Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V, I$ | Find $R$ | $R = V/I$ | Divide V by I, select nearest E12 value |
| $V, R$ | Find $I$ | $I = V/R$ | Divide V by R, check against LED limit |
| $I, R$ | Find $V$ | $V = IR$ | Multiply, check against supply rail |
| $V_{supply}, V_F, I_F$ | Find $R_{LED}$ | $R = (V_S - V_F)/I_F$ | Use 220Ω, 330Ω, 470Ω, 1kΩ standard |
| $V_{in}, R_1, R_2$ | Find $V_{out}$ | $V_{out} = V_{in} R_2/(R_1+R_2)$ | Plug in directly |
| $I_C, \beta_{DC}, V_{IO}$ | Find $R_B$ | $R_B = (V_{IO} - 0.7) \beta / I_C$ | Apply safety factor of 2 |
| $R, C$ | Find $\tau$ | $\tau = RC$ | Multiply, give answer in ms/µs |
| Color bands | Find $R$ value | AB × 10$^C$ | Use resistor color code table |
| 3-digit code | Find $C$ value | AB × 10$^C$ pF | Use capacitor code table |
| Resistors in series | Find $R_{eq}$ | $R_{eq} = \sum R_i$ | Simple addition |
| Resistors in parallel | Find $R_{eq}$ | $1/R_{eq} = \sum (1/R_i)$ | Sum reciprocals, then reciprocate |
| Capacitors in series | Find $C_{eq}$ | $1/C_{eq} = \sum (1/C_i)$ | Sum reciprocals |
| Capacitors in parallel | Find $C_{eq}$ | $C_{eq} = \sum C_i$ | Simple addition |

---

## 3.6 Resistor Color Code — Exhaustive Worked Example

**Problem:** Decode the following 4-band resistor: **Brown – Black – Red – Silver**

**Step 1 — Identify the color table values:**

$$\text{Brown} = 1, \quad \text{Black} = 0, \quad \text{Red} = \times 10^{2}, \quad \text{Silver} = \pm 10\%$$

**Step 2 — Form the value:**

$$R = 10 \times 10^{2} = 1000\ \Omega = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Apply tolerance:**

$$R = 1\ \text{k}\Omega \pm 10\% = 900\ \Omega\ \text{to}\ 1.1\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Final Answer:** $1\ \text{k}\Omega,\ \pm 10\%$.

## 4.4 Component Test Workflow Using Multimeter

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Pick Component from Kit]) --> TYPE{What is it?}
    TYPE -->|Resistor| R1[Set Multimeter to Ohm Range]
    R1 --> R2[Read Value and Compare with Color Code]
    R2 --> R3{Within Tolerance?}
    R3 -->|Yes| OK1[Component is Good]
    R3 -->|No| BAD1[Component is Faulty]
    TYPE -->|Capacitor| C1[Set Multimeter to Capacitance Mode]
    C1 --> C2[Read Value and Compare with Code]
    C2 --> OK2{Value Match?}
    OK2 -->|Yes| OK3[Component is Good]
    OK2 -->|No| BAD2[Component is Faulty]
    TYPE -->|Diode or LED| D1[Set Multimeter to Diode Mode]
    D1 --> D2[Measure Forward Voltage]
    D2 --> D3{Reads 0.7V to 2V?}
    D3 -->|Yes| OK4[Component is Good]
    D3 -->|No| BAD3[Component is Faulty]
    TYPE -->|Wire| W1[Set Multimeter to Continuity Mode]
    W1 --> W2{Beep on Connection?}
    W2 -->|Yes| OK5[Wire is Good]
    W2 -->|No| BAD4[Wire is Broken]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Note:** A digital multimeter (DMM) in **continuity mode** beeps when resistance is below ~30 $\Omega$. This is the fastest way to check jumper wires, find shorts on a breadboard, and verify solder joints.

## 4.5 Power Supply Architecture with Regulation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BATT[9V Battery] --> D1[Protection Diode 1N4007]
    D1 --> C1[Filter Capacitor 100uF]
    C1 --> REG[Voltage Regulator 7805]
    REG --> C2[Output Capacitor 10uF]
    C2 --> C3[Decoupling Capacitor 100nF]
    C3 --> OUT[Stable 5V Output Rail]
    style BATT fill:#FF6347
    style REG fill:#87CEEB
    style OUT fill:#90EE90
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Protection Diode?** The 1N4007 protects the circuit if the battery is connected in reverse polarity. It becomes forward-biased and blows the fuse (or just shorts), protecting the regulator and the microcontroller.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]
**Explain the concept of a voltage divider and its use in an IoT sensor interface circuit. Derive the output voltage expression.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **voltage divider** is a series combination of two resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ connected across a voltage source $V_{in}$, with the output $V_{out}$ taken across $R_2$.

**Derivation:**
By Ohm's law, the current through the series circuit is:

$$I = \frac{V_{in}}{R_1 + R_2}$$

The voltage across $R_2$ (the output) is:

$$V_{out} = I \times R_2 = \frac{V_{in} \cdot R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**IoT Application:**
In an IoT sensor interface, a **Light Dependent Resistor (LDR)** is used as $R_2$. As light intensity changes, $R_2$ changes, producing a varying $V_{out}$ that the microcontroller's ADC reads. This converts a physical quantity (light) into a measurable voltage. *(3 marks for definition + derivation + IoT example)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]
**Define forward voltage and forward current of an LED. Why is a current-limiting resistor necessary when interfacing an LED to a microcontroller?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Forward Voltage ($V_F$):** The voltage drop across an LED when it is forward-biased and conducting. It depends on the LED color (Red ≈ 2V, Blue/White ≈ 3V).
- **Forward Current ($I_F$):** The recommended current at which the LED operates safely and produces optimal brightness, typically **10–20 mA**.

**Why a current-limiting resistor is needed:**
A microcontroller GPIO pin outputs a constant voltage (e.g., 5V) but cannot limit current. Without a resistor, the LED draws excessive current, overheats, and burns out within seconds. The current-limiting resistor drops the excess voltage and limits current to a safe value using:

$$R = \frac{V_{supply} - V_F}{I_F}$$

For example, with a 5V supply, a red LED ($V_F = 2V$, $I_F = 15$ mA), the required resistor is **200 $\Omega$** (use 220 $\Omega$ standard). *(3 marks: definitions + formula + numerical example)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an LED interfacing circuit with a microcontroller. Derive the expression for the current-limiting resistor. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Circuit Description:**
A microcontroller GPIO pin (e.g., Arduino digital pin 9) is connected to a resistor $R$ in series with an LED, whose cathode is connected to ground. When the pin is driven HIGH (5V), current flows through $R$ and the LED, lighting it up.

**Step 2 — Circuit Diagram (ASCII representation):**
```
    +5V (from MCU pin) ---[ R ]---|>|--- GND
                                LED
                            (Anode→Cathode)
```

**Step 3 — Derivation of $R$:**
Apply KVL around the loop:

$$V_{supply} = V_R + V_F$$

By Ohm's Law, $V_R = I_F \cdot R$. Substituting:

$$V_{supply} = I_F \cdot R + V_F$$

Solving for $R$:

$$R = \frac{V_{supply} - V_F}{I_F}$$

**Step 4 — Example Calculation:**
With $V_{supply} = 5\ \text{V}$, $V_F = 2\ \text{V}$ (red LED), $I_F = 15\ \text{mA}$:

$$R = \frac{5 - 2}{15 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{3}{0.015} = 200\ \Omega$$

Nearest E12 standard: **220 $\Omega$**.

**Step 5 — Power Dissipation Check:**
$P_R = I_F^2 \cdot R = (0.015)^2 \times 220 = 0.0495\ \text{W} \approx 50\ \text{mW}$. A 1/4 W resistor is sufficient.

**Step 6 — Significance:**
- Protects the LED from overcurrent
- Protects the microcontroller GPIO from sourcing excessive current
- Allows brightness control via PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)

*(7 marks: 1 for diagram, 3 for derivation, 2 for calculation, 1 for significance)*

---

**(b)** With reference to the basic electronics kit, explain the function of the following components in an IoT node: (i) BC547 transistor, (ii) Voltage regulator 7805, (iii) Push button with pull-up resistor, (iv) Decoupling capacitor. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) BC547 Transistor — 2 Marks:**
The BC547 is an **NPN bipolar junction transistor** used as a **low-side switch** in IoT kits. When a microcontroller GPIO outputs HIGH to its base (through a 1k$\Omega$ resistor), the transistor saturates and allows current to flow from collector to emitter, driving a load (relay, motor, buzzer) that requires more current than the GPIO can supply. The BC547 has a typical $\beta_{DC}$ of 100–800, allowing a small base current (~1 mA) to switch a large collector current (~100 mA or more).

**(ii) Voltage Regulator 7805 — 2 Marks:**
The 7805 is a **three-terminal linear voltage regulator** that converts an input DC voltage (typically 7V–35V) into a stable **+5V output**. It is used to power the microcontroller and other 5V components from a 9V battery or a higher-voltage DC adapter. It requires two capacitors: a **0.33 $\mu$F** on the input and a **0.1 $\mu$F** on the output for stability and noise filtering.

**(iii) Push Button with Pull-up Resistor — 1.5 Marks:**
A push button is a **momentary mechanical switch** used for digital input. A **10 k$\Omega$ pull-up resistor** connects the button junction to VCC, so when the button is **not pressed**, the input reads **HIGH**. When **pressed**, the input is shorted to GND and reads **LOW**. The pull-up prevents the input from "floating" into an undefined state. The internal pull-ups of microcontrollers (typically 20–50 k$\Omega$) can also be enabled in software.

**(iv) Decoupling Capacitor — 1.5 Marks:**
A **100 nF (0.1 $\mu$F) ceramic capacitor** placed close to the VCC and GND pins of the microcontroller (and ICs like 7805). Its function is to **bypass high-frequency noise** to ground and provide instantaneous current during switching transients. Without it, the digital circuits can reset unexpectedly or behave erratically due to supply voltage fluctuations. In IoT, decoupling capacitors are placed near every IC and sensor.

*(7 marks distributed as above)*

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain the resistor color code system. Decode the following resistors: (i) Brown-Black-Red-Gold, (ii) Yellow-Violet-Orange-Silver, (iii) Red-Red-Gold-Gold. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Explanation of the Resistor Color Code:**
A standard 4-band resistor has the following layout:

| Band 1 | Band 2 | Band 3 | Band 4 |
|---|---|---|---|
| First Digit | Second Digit | Multiplier ($10^n$) | Tolerance |

The color-to-digit mapping is:
- **Black = 0, Brown = 1, Red = 2, Orange = 3, Yellow = 4, Green = 5, Blue = 6, Violet = 7, Grey = 8, White = 9**
- **Gold multiplier = $10^{-1}$**, **Silver = $10^{-2}$**
- **Gold tolerance = ±5%, Silver = ±10%, None = ±20%**

The value formula is:

$$R = (D_1 \times 10 + D_2) \times 10^{M} \pm \text{Tolerance \%}$$

**Decoding:**

**(i) Brown – Black – Red – Gold:**
$D_1 = 1$, $D_2 = 0$, $M = 2$, Tolerance = ±5%.

$$R = (1 \times 10 + 0) \times 10^{2} = 10 \times 100 = 1000\ \Omega = 1\ \text{k}\Omega \pm 5\%$$

**(ii) Yellow – Violet – Orange – Silver:**
$D_1 = 4$, $D_2 = 7$, $M = 3$, Tolerance = ±10%.

$$R = (4 \times 10 + 7) \times 10^{3} = 47 \times 1000 = 47{,}000\ \Omega = 47\ \text{k}\Omega \pm 10\%$$

**(iii) Red – Red – Gold – Gold:**
$D_1 = 2$, $D_2 = 2$, $M = -1$ (Gold), Tolerance = ±5%.

$$R = (2 \times 10 + 2) \times 10^{-1} = 22 \times 0.1 = 2.2\ \Omega \pm 5\%$$

*(7 marks: 2 for explanation, 5 for three decodings @ ~1.5 each)*

---

**(b)** Design an LDR-based light sensing circuit to interface with an ESP32 microcontroller. Draw the circuit, derive the output voltage expression, and calculate the output for $R_{LDR} = 2\ \text{k}\Omega$ (light) and $R_{LDR} = 50\ \text{k}\Omega$ (dark). Assume $R_1 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ and $V_{in} = 3.3\ \text{V}$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Circuit Design:**
A voltage divider is formed by an LDR ($R_2$) and a fixed resistor ($R_1 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$) in series across the 3.3V supply. The junction is connected to an ESP32 analog pin (e.g., GPIO34).

**Step 2 — Circuit Diagram (ASCII):**
```
    +3.3V ---[ R1 = 10k ]---*---[ LDR = R2 ]--- GND
                              |
                              +--- to ESP32 GPIO34 (ADC)
```

**Step 3 — Derivation of $V_{out}$:**
By voltage divider rule:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 4 — Light Condition Calculation ($R_{LDR} = 2\ \text{k}\Omega$):**

$$V_{out,light} = 3.3 \cdot \frac{2000}{10000 + 2000} = 3.3 \cdot \frac{2}{12} = 3.3 \cdot 0.1667$$

$$V_{out,light} = 0.55\ \text{V}$$

**Step 5 — Dark Condition Calculation ($R_{LDR} = 50\ \text{k}\Omega$):**

$$V_{out,dark} = 3.3 \cdot \frac{50000}{10000 + 50000} = 3.3 \cdot \frac{50}{60} = 3.3 \cdot 0.8333$$

$$V_{out,dark} = 2.75\ \text{V}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**
- **Bright light** → $V_{out} \approx 0.55\ \text{V}$ → Low ADC value (e.g., ~170 on a 12-bit ADC scaled 0–3.3V)
- **Dark** → $V_{out} \approx 2.75\ \text{V}$ → High ADC value (e.g., ~3407)
- The ESP32 reads the ADC, maps the value to a percentage, and sends it to the cloud.

**Step 7 — Threshold Tuning:**
A threshold voltage of **1.65 V** (mid-point) can be set to trigger an automatic light (relay) when $V_{out}$ exceeds it (i.e., in darkness).

*(7 marks: 1 for circuit, 2 for derivation, 2 for light calc, 2 for dark calc + interpretation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Critical Mistakes to Avoid:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Units:** Always write **$\Omega$**, **V**, **A**, **mA**, **k$\Omega$**, **µF**, **nF**, **pF** explicitly. A formula like "$R = 200$" without units will lose 0.5 to 1 mark.
>
> 2. **Confusing Series and Parallel:** Resistors add in series ($R_1 + R_2$), not parallel. Many students write $1/R = R_1 + R_2$ — this is **wrong**.
>
> 3. **Using the wrong $V_F$ for LED color:** A common mistake is using $V_F = 0.7\ \text{V}$ (silicon diode) for LEDs. The LED $V_F$ is **color-dependent (1.8V to 3.6V)**.
>
> 4. **Drawing wrong transistor symbol:** NPN and PNP have different arrow directions on the emitter. NPN: arrow points **outward** (Not Pointing iN). PNP: arrow points **inward** (Points iN Permanently).
>
> 5. **Not showing the LED polarity:** Always mark the **anode (+)** and **cathode (−)** of the LED in the circuit diagram. A reverse-biased LED will not light up and may be damaged.
>
> 6. **Ignoring the tolerance band:** A 1 k$\Omega$ resistor is rarely exactly 1 k$\Omega$. Always mention the tolerance (±5%, ±10%) in your final answer.
>
> 7. **Forgetting the decoupling capacitor:** In any 14-mark circuit design question, the examiner expects to see **filter/decoupling capacitors** mentioned. Omitting them costs 1 mark.
>
> 8. **Drawing the relay coil without a flyback diode:** When switching a relay with a transistor, a **1N4007 flyback diode** must be connected across the relay coil (cathode to VCC, anode to collector) to protect the transistor from back-EMF. Drawing the diode is a sure way to gain bonus marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 📋 Rapid-Revision Checklist

#### Core Concepts
- [ ] Ohm's Law: $V = IR$
- [ ] Power: $P = VI = I^2R = V^2/R$
- [ ] KCL: $\sum I_{in} = \sum I_{out}$
- [ ] KVL: $\sum V_{loop} = 0$

#### Resistors
- [ ] 4-band color code formula: $R = (D_1 \cdot 10 + D_2) \times 10^M \pm \text{Tol}$
- [ ] E12 standard series: 10, 12, 15, 18, 22, 27, 33, 39, 47, 56, 68, 82 (× decades)
- [ ] Series: $R_{eq} = R_1 + R_2 + \cdots$
- [ ] Parallel: $1/R_{eq} = 1/R_1 + 1/R_2 + \cdots$

#### Capacitors
- [ ] Charge: $Q = CV$
- [ ] Energy: $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$
- [ ] RC time constant: $\tau = RC$
- [ ] 5τ rule for full charge/discharge
- [ ] Capacitor code: AB × 10^C pF
- [ ] Series: $1/C_{eq} = \sum 1/C_i$ (reverse of resistors)
- [ ] Parallel: $C_{eq} = \sum C_i$ (reverse of resistors)

#### LEDs
- [ ] Forward voltage depends on color (Red ≈ 2V, Blue/White ≈ 3V)
- [ ] Forward current: 10–20 mA
- [ ] Current-limiting resistor: $R = (V_S - V_F) / I_F$
- [ ] Long lead = Anode (+), Short lead = Cathode (−)

#### Transistors (BC547)
- [ ] NPN symbol: arrow on emitter points OUT
- [ ] Saturation: $I_B \geq I_C / \beta_{DC}$
- [ ] Base resistor: $R_B = (V_{IO} - 0.7) / I_B$
- [ ] Use safety factor of 2 for $I_B$

#### Voltage Divider
- [ ] $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$
- [ ] Application: sensor interfacing (LDR, thermistor)
- [ ] Choose $R_1$ near geometric mean for max sensitivity

#### Power Supply
- [ ] 7805 regulator: input 7–35V → output 5V
- [ ] Decoupling capacitors: 100 nF ceramic, 10 µF electrolytic
- [ ] Protection diode: 1N4007 against reverse polarity
- [ ] Flyback diode across relay coil

#### Multimeter Skills
- [ ] Voltage: connect in **parallel**
- [ ] Current: connect in **series** (break the circuit)
- [ ] Resistance: circuit must be **powered off**
- [ ] Continuity mode: beeps below ~30 $\Omega$

#### Breadboard
- [ ] Power rails: + (red) and − (blue) run vertically along the sides
- [ ] Terminal strips: 5 holes connected horizontally in the middle
- [ ] IC placement: straddles the central gap
- [ ] No soldering — push-fit connections only

#### Component-to-Application Mapping
- [ ] **LED + R** = Status indicator
- [ ] **LDR + R** = Light sensor
- [ ] **Thermistor + R** = Temperature sensor
- [ ] **BC547 + R_B** = Relay/motor driver
- [ ] **7805 + Caps** = 5V regulated supply
- [ ] **Push button + Pull-up R** = Digital input
- [ ] **Capacitor across VCC-GND** = Decoupling
- [ ] **1N4007 across relay** = Flyback protection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** *"In the Basic Electronics Kit module, **the resistor is your best friend and the LED is your favorite application**. Master the LED current-limiting formula, the voltage divider rule, and the resistor color code. These three alone account for ~70% of all marks in this module."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Breadboard, Jumper Wires, and Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT504 - PROGRAMMING FOR INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Breadboard, Jumper Wires, and Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Breadboard — Formal Definition

A **breadboard** (also called a *solderless breadboard* or *protoboard*) is a reusable, solder-free, rectangular construction base used for building temporary electronic prototypes and experimental IoT circuits. It consists of a grid of pre-drilled holes (called **tie points** or **sockets**) into which component leads and jumper wires can be inserted to form electrical connections. The internal structure contains spring-loaded metal clips that grip component leads and electrically bond them in carefully defined rows and columns.

The standard modern solderless breadboard used in IoT prototyping is the **830 tie-point breadboard** (sometimes 400 or 170 tie-point variants for compact projects), and the holes are spaced at a pitch of **2.54 mm (0.1 inch)** — identical to the standard DIP (Dual In-line Package) IC lead spacing.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 2**
> The breadboard is a **physical-layer prototyping tool**. Although it sits one layer above silicon-level hardware, KTU examiners expect students to understand the **internal electrical topology** because every IoT lab examination involves assembling a working circuit on one. Marks are awarded for explaining *how* a hole is electrically connected to its neighbors, not merely that "a breadboard is a board with holes."

### 1.2 Jumper Wires — Formal Definition

**Jumper wires** (or *jump wires* / *dupont wires*) are short, insulated, electrically conductive cables terminated with pin connectors (or tinned ends) at one or both ends. They are the *arteries* of a breadboard circuit, carrying voltage, ground, and signal traces between components, microcontrollers (Arduino, ESP8266, ESP32, Raspberry Pi Pico), sensors, and actuators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AWG Rating Rule of Thumb**
> Standard breadboard jumper wires use **22 AWG to 26 AWG** stranded or solid-core copper conductors. Smaller AWG numbers = thicker wire = lower resistance. The most common size shipped with Arduino starter kits is **22 AWG solid core**, which is stiff enough to push into a tie point without bending.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **parking lot for an apartment complex**:
- The **breadboard** is the parking lot itself, with **marked parking spaces** (tie points).
- **Power rails** (the two long columns on either side) are the **two main city roads** that bring electricity into the lot and take it away.
- The **terminal strips** (the short rows in the middle) are the **side streets** branching off the main road — every parking space on the same side street is electrically connected to its neighbours, just like a row of tie points is internally bonded.
- **Jumper wires** are the **driveways** that connect a *parked car* (a component lead) to either the main road or another driveway.

When you insert the leg of a resistor into a hole in the same row as an LED's leg, the breadboard internally "wires" them together — exactly as if you had soldered them. Move the LED one row away, and the connection is broken because the row is different.

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Ratings

| Parameter | Standard Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Hole pitch (tie-point spacing) | **2.54 mm** | 0.1 inch, DIP-compatible |
| Typical breadboard size | **16.5 cm × 5.5 cm** | Full 830-tie-point board |
| Tie-point count | **830** (full) / 400 / 170 | Smaller for compact projects |
| Maximum voltage per rail | **30 V DC** | Exceeding this arcs the spring clips |
| Maximum sustained current per tie point | **1 A** | Brief peaks up to 3 A are tolerated |
| Typical contact resistance per tie point | **≤ 50 mΩ** | Fresh board; rises with oxidation |
| Operating temperature range | **−20 °C to +70 °C** | Plastic body ABS or polypropylene |
| Jumper wire AWG | **22–26 AWG** | 22 AWG most common |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Internal Topology of a Breadboard

A standard 830-tie-point breadboard is divided into **three functional regions**:

**Region A — Terminal Strips (Center Block)**
- Comprises **63 rows × 2 halves** (upper and lower) separated by a central channel (the "ravine").
- Each half-row contains **5 electrically connected tie points** labelled `a, b, c, d, e` (top) and `f, g, h, i, j` (bottom).
- A row is a **node** — any voltage applied to one hole appears on the other four.
- The central ravine is sized exactly to straddle a standard **DIP-IC** (e.g., ATmega328P, ESP-WROOM-32), so the IC straddles the gap while each pin has 4 free tie points for connections.

**Region B — Power Rails (Side Columns)**
- Two long vertical columns on **each side** of the board.
- Each column is marked with a `+` (red) or `−` (blue/black) stripe.
- Most breadboards have **the rail broken in the middle**, requiring **two short jumper wires** to bridge the left and right halves if you need continuous power from end to end.
- Each rail is internally a single electrical node: every `+` hole on the same side is connected.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The #1 KTU Practical Mistake**
> Students insert the red wire of an LED's current-limiting resistor circuit into the `+` rail on the left side and the black ground wire into the `−` rail on the right side — *but forget* to add bridging jumpers across the central gap. The circuit is open, the LED does not light, and the student blames the LED. Always check rail continuity with a multimeter before troubleshooting components.

### 2.2 Why Spring Clips? The Physics of the Tie Point

Each hole sits atop a small **phosphor-bronze or nickel-silver spring clip**. When a wire is inserted, the clip deflects and creates two parallel contact lines — this is the source of the **≤ 50 mΩ contact resistance**. Over thousands of insertions, the clip loses tension, the contact resistance creeps up to **200–500 mΩ**, and the board becomes "noisy" for low-voltage analog signals (e.g., LDR, thermistor dividers).

### 2.3 Jumper Wire Classification

Jumper wires are categorized by **connector gender** at each end:

1. **Male-to-Male (M-M)** — Solid pin on both ends. The default for breadboard-to-breadboard or component-to-breadboard wiring. Carries signal-level and power-level currents.
2. **Male-to-Female (M-F)** — Pin on one end, socket on the other. Used to connect **header-pin modules** (like the HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor) to a breadboard without soldering.
3. **Female-to-Female (F-F)** — Socket on both ends. Used to daisy-chain modules to microcontroller boards (Arduino-to-sensor) without occupying a breadboard.

Jumper wire conductors come in two flavours:

- **Solid core** — A single stiff copper strand. Holds shape; easy to push in; **breaks after 5–10 sharp bends**.
- **Stranded core** — 7 to 30 thin copper strands twisted together. **Flexible**, survives vibration, but a stray strand can cause a short circuit if not pre-tinned.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $R$ | Resistance | $R = \dfrac{V}{I}$ | Ω | Ohm's law, current limiting |
| $V$ | Voltage | $V = I \times R$ | V (volts) | Potential drop across resistor |
| $I$ | Current | $I = \dfrac{V}{R}$ | A (amperes) | LED forward current |
| $P$ | Power dissipation | $P = V \times I = I^{2} R = \dfrac{V^{2}}{R}$ | W (watts) | Resistor heat check |
| $R_{s}$ | Series resistance | $R_{s} = R_{1} + R_{2} + \dots + R_{n}$ | Ω | LED + dropper resistor |
| $R_{p}$ | Parallel resistance | $\dfrac{1}{R_{p}} = \dfrac{1}{R_{1}} + \dfrac{1}{R_{2}} + \dots + \dfrac{1}{R_{n}}$ | Ω | Two pull-up resistors |
| $V_{div}$ | Voltage divider | $V_{out} = V_{in} \times \dfrac{R_{2}}{R_{1} + R_{2}}$ | V | LDR / thermistor sensing |
| $R_{tie}$ | Tie-point contact R | $\le \mathbf{50\ m\Omega}$ | Ω | Must be $\ll R_{load}$ |
| $I_{max}$ | Max rail current | $\mathbf{1\ A}$ (continuous) | A | Avoid $\gt 1\ A$ per rail |
| $V_{max}$ | Max rail voltage | $\mathbf{30\ V\ DC}$ | V | Avoid high voltage |
| $P_{rated}$ | Standard resistor | $\mathbf{\dfrac{1}{4}\ W}$ | W | Common through-hole part |
| $V_{f}$ | LED forward voltage | $\mathbf{\approx 2.0\ V}$ (red) | V | Red LED parameter |
| $I_{f}$ | LED forward current | $\mathbf{10{-}20\ mA}$ | A | Safe operating region |

> [!NOTE]
> **NEVER** use the vertical bar `|` inside a markdown table — it breaks the table parser. The KTU Formula Sheet above uses the `division` slash and `\dfrac{}{}` for all fractions to keep the LaTeX rendering intact.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industrial IoT, breadboard prototyping is the **first step in the hardware design V-model**:
1. **Concept validation** — Engineer wires up an ESP32 + DHT22 + relay on a breadboard to prove the firmware-measurement-actuation loop.
2. **Schematic capture** — The verified working circuit is then drawn as a schematic in KiCad / Altium.
3. **PCB layout** — Finally, the schematic is laid out as a 2-layer or 4-layer PCB for mass production.

Breadboards are used in **millions of university labs, R&D benches, and even consumer-product breadboard kits** (e.g., CrowPi, Sparkfun Inventor's Kit). The IoT practitioner's mastery of breadboard wiring directly determines how fast a *proof-of-concept* can be demonstrated to a stakeholder.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Canonical Example: LED Current-Limiting Resistor on a Breadboard

This is the most frequently asked KTU Module 2 numerical problem. The setup is:
- Arduino UNO digital pin `D9` (PWM-capable) → resistor → red LED anode.
- LED cathode → breadboard ground rail → Arduino `GND`.

**Given Data:**
- Arduino logic high voltage: $V_{in} = 5\ \text{V DC}$
- Red LED forward voltage: $V_{f} = 2.0\ \text{V}$ (typical, from datasheet)
- Desired LED forward current: $I_{f} = 15\ \text{mA} = 0.015\ \text{A}$ (safe operating region)
- Tie-point contact resistance: $R_{tie} \approx 50\ \text{m\Omega} = 0.05\ \Omega$ (negligible)

**Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the loop:**

$$
V_{in} - V_{R} - V_{f} = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the voltage drop across the current-limiting resistor $V_{R}$:**

$$
V_{R} = V_{in} - V_{f}
$$

Substitute the numerical values:

$$
V_{R} = 5.0\ \text{V} - 2.0\ \text{V} = 3.0\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Ohm's Law to find the required resistance $R$:**

$$
R = \frac{V_{R}}{I_{f}}
$$

$$
R = \frac{3.0\ \text{V}}{0.015\ \text{A}} = 200\ \Omega
$$

**Step 4 — Round up to the nearest standard E12 resistor value:**

Standard E12 series: $10,\ 12,\ 15,\ 18,\ 22,\ 27,\ 33,\ 39,\ 47,\ 56,\ 68,\ 82$ (multiplied by powers of 10).

The nearest standard value above $200\ \Omega$ is:

$$
R_{standard} = 220\ \Omega
$$

**Step 5 — Verify the actual LED current with the standard resistor:**

$$
I_{f,actual} = \frac{V_{in} - V_{f}}{R_{standard}}
$$

$$
I_{f,actual} = \frac{5.0 - 2.0}{220} = \frac{3.0}{220} = 0.01363\ \text{A} \approx 13.6\ \text{mA}
$$

**Step 6 — Verify power dissipation in the resistor to confirm rating:**

$$
P_{R} = I_{f,actual}^{2} \times R_{standard}
$$

$$
P_{R} = (0.01363)^{2} \times 220 = 0.0001858 \times 220 = 0.0409\ \text{W}
$$

Since $P_{R} = 0.041\ \text{W} \lt 0.25\ \text{W}$ (standard ¼ W resistor rating), the resistor is **safe**.

### 3.2 Canonical Example: Voltage Divider for LDR Light Sensor

**Given:**
- $V_{in} = 5\ \text{V}$ from Arduino `5V` rail.
- Fixed resistor: $R_{1} = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$.
- LDR (Light-Dependent Resistor) resistance range: $R_{LDR, dark} = 1\ \text{M}\Omega$ to $R_{LDR, light} = 500\ \Omega$.

**Step 1 — Set up the divider equation (output taken across the LDR):**

$$
V_{out} = V_{in} \times \frac{R_{LDR}}{R_{1} + R_{LDR}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $V_{out}$ in bright light:**

$$
V_{out, light} = 5 \times \frac{500}{10\,000 + 500} = 5 \times \frac{500}{10\,500}
$$

$$
V_{out, light} = 5 \times 0.0476 = 0.238\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $V_{out}$ in darkness:**

$$
V_{out, dark} = 5 \times \frac{1\,000\,000}{10\,000 + 1\,000\,000} = 5 \times 0.990
$$

$$
V_{out, dark} = 4.95\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 4 — ADC Reading Translation (Arduino 10-bit ADC):**

The Arduino UNO's `analogRead()` returns:

$$
ADC = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{ref}} \times 1023
$$

Assuming $V_{ref} = 5.0\ \text{V}$:

$$
ADC_{light} = \frac{0.238}{5.0} \times 1023 \approx 49
$$

$$
ADC_{dark} = \frac{4.95}{5.0} \times 1023 \approx 1013
$$

This wide ADC range (49 → 1013) is the *engineer-friendly* reason the LDR is placed as $R_{2}$ (the bottom resistor) in the divider — it maximises dynamic range on the ADC.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Scenarios

| Scenario | Key Equation | Boundary Condition | KTU Pitfall |
|---|---|---|---|
| LED current limiter | $R = (V_{in} - V_{f}) / I_{f}$ | $P_{R} \lt P_{rated}$ | Forgetting to round up to E12 |
| Pull-up / pull-down | $R \approx 10 \times R_{source}$ | $\le 100\ \text{k}\Omega$ for noise immunity | Floating pin if missing |
| Voltage divider | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot R_{2} / (R_{1} + R_{2})$ | $I_{divider} \ge 10 \times I_{load}$ | $R_{1} = R_{2}$ gives only $V_{in}/2$ |
| RC timing | $T = R \times C$ | $T \gg t_{propagation}$ | Mixing up $\tau$ vs $T$ |
| Series resistors | $R_{total} = \sum R_{i}$ | Order independent | None |
| Parallel resistors | $1/R = \sum 1/R_{i}$ | Order independent | Equal resistors: $R/2$ |
| Power budget | $\sum P_i \le P_{rail}$ | $< 80\%$ of source rating | Not checking regulator heat |

### 3.4 Worked Out: Pin-Mapping Matrix (5×2 Example)

Consider a typical KTU lab task: *Connect 5 LEDs to digital pins 8, 9, 10, 11, 12 of an Arduino UNO and verify independent blink.*

**Pin-to-Breadboard Layout (10 rows × 2 columns):**

| Arduino Pin | LED Anode (Row) | Resistor (Row) | LED Cathode (Row) | Ground Rail |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `D8`  | Row 1, col a | Row 1, col f | Row 2, col a | `−` |
| `D9`  | Row 3, col a | Row 3, col f | Row 4, col a | `−` |
| `D10` | Row 5, col a | Row 5, col f | Row 6, col a | `−` |
| `D11` | Row 7, col a | Row 7, col f | Row 8, col a | `−` |
| `D12` | Row 9, col a | Row 9, col f | Row 10, col a | `−` |

**Bridge jumpers needed:**
- 1 × `−` rail bridge jumper (top half to bottom half).
- 1 × `+` rail bridge jumper (if any 5 V source uses it).
- 1 × `GND` jumper from Arduino `GND` to `−` rail.
- 5 × signal jumpers (Arduino pin → resistor row).

**Total jumpers = 7** for a 5-LED matrix.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Breadboard Internal Topology

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph PWR_RAIL_TOP["POSITIVE POWER RAIL PLUS"]
        A1["Plus 1"]
        A2["Plus 2"]
        A3["Plus 3"]
        A4["Plus 4"]
        A5["Plus 5"]
    end

    subgraph TERMINAL_TOP["TERMINAL STRIPS UPPER HALF"]
        R1["Row 1 abcd e"]
        R2["Row 2 abcd e"]
        R3["Row 3 abcd e"]
        R4["Row 4 abcd e"]
        R5["Row 5 abcd e"]
    end

    subgraph RAVINE["CENTRAL RAVINE FOR DIP IC"]
        IC["DIP 14 16 20 24 PACKAGE SITS HERE"]
    end

    subgraph TERMINAL_BOT["TERMINAL STRIPS LOWER HALF"]
        R6["Row 1 fghij"]
        R7["Row 2 fghij"]
        R8["Row 3 fghij"]
        R9["Row 4 fghij"]
        R10["Row 5 fghij"]
    end

    subgraph PWR_RAIL_BOT["NEGATIVE GROUND RAIL MINUS"]
        B1["Minus 1"]
        B2["Minus 2"]
        B3["Minus 3"]
        B4["Minus 4"]
        B5["Minus 5"]
    end

    A1 --- A2 --- A3 --- A4 --- A5
    R1 --- R2 --- R3 --- R4 --- R5
    R6 --- R7 --- R8 --- R9 --- R10
    B1 --- B2 --- B3 --- B4 --- B5

    R1 -.- IC
    R6 -.- IC
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Power and Signal Flow in a Typical LED Circuit

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC["ARDUINO 5V"]:::src
    GND["ARDUINO GND"]:::gnd
    PIN["ARDUINO D9"]:::pin

    LED["RED LED 2V 15MA"]:::led

    subgraph BB["BREADBOARD"]

        MINUS["MINUS RAIL BLUE"]

    SRC --> PLUS
    GND --> MINUS
    PIN --> R
    R --> LED
    LED --> MINUS
    PLUS --> R

    classDef src fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#c00,color:#900
    classDef gnd fill:#e5e5ff,stroke:#00c,color:#009
```
