[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Explain the role of WSGI in Python web frameworks. How is it different from ASGI?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **WSGI (Web Server Gateway Interface)** is the synchronous Python specification (PEP 3333) that defines a standard interface between web servers and Python web applications. It enables interoperability — a Flask or Django app can run on any WSGI-compliant server (Gunicorn, uWSGI, mod_wsgi) without code changes. **Difference between WSGI and ASGI:** | Aspect | WSGI | ASGI | |---|---|---| | **Standard** | PEP 3333 | PEP 484/489 | | **Execution Model** | Synchronous (one request per worker thread) | Asynchronous (concurrent via `asyncio`) | | **Protocol Support** | HTTP/1.1 only | HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, **WebSockets** | | **Frameworks** | Flask, Django (default) | FastAPI, Django Channels, Starlette | | **IoT Fit** | HTTP REST APIs | Real-time telemetry, WebSocket streams | --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Flask + IoT REST API** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]** **(a)** Explain the **MVC architectural pattern** in the context of Python web frameworks. Compare it with Django's **MVT pattern** with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Design and implement a **Flask-based REST API** for an IoT smart home gateway that exposes the following endpoints: - `GET /api/sensors` — list all registered sensors. - `POST /api/sensors` — register a new sensor (accepts `id`, `type`, `location`). - `GET /api/sensors//latest` — return the latest reading of a specific sensor. - `POST /api/sensors//reading` — submit a new reading (accepts `value` and optional `unit`). Include proper status codes, JSON serialization, and error handling. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) MVC vs MVT Pattern (7 marks):** **Model-View-Controller (MVC):** - **Model:** Represents data and business logic; interacts with database (e.g., SQLAlchemy model class). - **View:** User Interface layer (HTML, JSON, XML output). - **Controller:** Receives user input, processes requests, and coordinates Model ↔ View updates. **MVT (Model-View-Template) — Django:** - **Model:** ORM data layer (`models.py`). - **View:** Business logic (`views.py`) — equivalent to the Controller in MVC. - **Template:** Presentation layer (`templates/*.html`) — equivalent to the View in MVC. - The **Controller is the framework itself** (URL dispatcher + middleware). | Aspect | MVC (Flask) | MVT (Django) | |---|---|---| | Controller | Written manually by developer | Implicit (framework) | | View | HTML/UI output | Business logic (Python function) | | Template | Jinja2 (optional) | Django Template Language (DTL) | | ORM | External (SQLAlchemy) | Built-in | *(Include the Mermaid architecture diagram from Section 4.3 here for full marks.)* --- **(b) Flask REST API Implementation (7 marks):** ```python # File: smart_home_api.py from flask import Flask, jsonify, request from datetime import datetime app = Flask(__name__) # In-memory database (use SQLite/PostgreSQL in production) sensors_db = {} readings_db = {} @app.route('/api/sensors', methods=['GET']) def list_sensors(): """List all registered sensors.""" return jsonify({"count": len(sensors_db), "sensors": list(sensors_db.values())}), 200 @app.route('/api/sensors', methods=['POST']) def register_sensor(): """Register a new sensor.""" data = request.get_json(silent=True) or {} sid = data.get('id') if not sid: return jsonify({"error": "Missing 'id' field"}), 400 if sid in sensors_db: return jsonify({"error": f"Sensor {sid} already exists"}), 409 sensors_db[sid] = { "id": sid, "type": data.get('type', 'generic'), "location": data.get('location', 'unknown'), "registered_at": datetime.utcnow().isoformat() } readings_db[sid] = [] return jsonify({"message": "Sensor registered", "sensor": sensors_db[sid]}), 201 @app.route('/api/sensors//latest', methods=['GET']) def latest_reading(sid): """Return the latest reading of a sensor.""" if sid not in sensors_db: return jsonify({"error": "Sensor not found"}), 404 readings = readings_db.get(sid, []) if not readings: return jsonify({"error": "No readings available"}), 404 return jsonify(readings[-1]), 200 @app.route('/api/sensors//reading', methods=['POST']) def submit_reading(sid): """Submit a new sensor reading.""" if sid not in sensors_db: return jsonify({"error": "Sensor not found"}), 404 data = request.get_json(silent=True) or {} value = data.get('value') if value is None: return jsonify({"error": "Missing 'value' field"}), 400 reading = { "value": value, "unit": data.get('unit', ''), "timestamp": datetime.utcnow().isoformat() } readings_db[sid].append(reading) return jsonify({"message": "Reading recorded", "reading": reading}), 201 if __name__ == '__main__': app.run(host='0.0.0.0', port=5000, debug=True) ``` **HTTP Status Code Usage Justification:** | Endpoint | Success | Error Cases | |---|---|---| | `GET /api/sensors` | `200 OK` | — | | `POST /api/sensors` | `201 Created` | `400` missing field, `409` duplicate | | `GET /api/sensors//latest` | `200 OK` | `404` sensor/no data | | `POST /api/sensors//reading` | `201 Created` | `400` missing value, `404` unknown sensor | --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Django IoT Cloud Dashboard** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Django request-response lifecycle** from URL reception to template rendering. Compare Django's **MTV (Model-Template-View)** architecture with the traditional **MVC** pattern. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Develop a **Django application** named `iot_devices` to manage IoT devices and their telemetry. The application must: - Define a `Device` model with fields: `device_id` (unique), `device_type`, `location`, `is_online` (boolean), `last_seen` (datetime). - Define a `Telemetry` model with a ForeignKey to `Device`, fields: `metric_name`, `metric_value`, `recorded_at`. - Register both models in Django Admin with appropriate list display and filters. - Implement a Django REST Framework view `DeviceListCreate` that lists all devices (`GET`) and creates a new device (`POST`), returning proper JSON responses. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Django Request-Response Lifecycle (7 marks):** ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Browser / IoT Client sends HTTP Request"] --> B["Django WSGI Handler
wsgi.py"] B --> C["Middleware Chain
CommonMiddleware,
CSRF, Auth, Session"] C --> D["URL Resolver
urls.py - ROOT_URLCONF"] D --> E["App URL Router
iot_devices/urls.py"] E --> F["View Function
views.py - def device_list()"] F --> G{"Needs Data?"} G -- Yes --> H["ORM Query
models.py"] H --> I[("Database")] I --> H H --> F G -- No --> F F --> J["Template Render
device_list.html"] J --> K["HTTP Response
HTML / JSON"] K --> L["Client Receives Response"] ``` **MVC vs MTV Comparison (4 marks):** | Component | MVC | MTV (Django) | |---|---|---| | Model | Data | `models.py` | | View | UI Output | `templates/*.html` | | Controller | Application Logic | **Framework (URL dispatcher + middleware)** | | What developer writes | All three | Model, Template, View (function) | **Note for exam:** In Django's terminology, the **"View" is actually the Controller** in MVC, and the **"Template" is the View**. This is a common point of confusion in KTU valuation. --- **(b) Django IoT Application Implementation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Models (`iot_devices/models.py`):** ```python from django.db import models from django.utils import timezone class Device(models.Model): DEVICE_CHOICES = [ ('SENSOR', 'Environmental Sensor'), ('ACTUATOR', 'Actuator/Relay'), ('CAMERA', 'IP Camera'), ('GATEWAY', 'Edge Gateway'), ] device_id = models.CharField(max_length=50, unique=True) device_type = models.CharField(max_length=10, choices=DEVICE_CHOICES) location = models.CharField(max_length=100) is_online = models.BooleanField(default=False) last_seen = models.DateTimeField(null=True, blank=True) created_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True) class Meta: ordering = ['-last_seen'] verbose_name_plural = "Devices" def __str__(self): return f"{self.device_id} @ {self.location}" class Telemetry(models.Model): device = models.ForeignKey(Device, on_delete=models.CASCADE, related_name='telemetry') metric_name = models.CharField(max_length=50) metric_value = models.FloatField() recorded_at = models.DateTimeField(default=timezone.now) def __str__(self): return f"{self.device.device_id}: {self.metric_name}={self.metric_value}" ``` **Step 2 — Admin Registration (`iot_devices/admin.py`):** ```python from django.contrib import admin from .models import Device, Telemetry @admin.register(Device) class DeviceAdmin(admin.ModelAdmin): list_display = ('device_id', 'device_type', 'location', 'is_online', 'last_seen') list_filter = ('device_type', 'is_online') search_fields = ('device_id', 'location') actions = ['mark_online', 'mark_offline'] @admin.action(description="Mark selected devices as online") def mark_online(self, request, queryset): queryset.update(is_online=True) @admin.register(Telemetry) class TelemetryAdmin(admin.ModelAdmin): list_display = ('device', 'metric_name', 'metric_value', 'recorded_at') list_filter = ('metric_name', 'device__device_type') date_hierarchy = 'recorded_at' ``` **Step 3 — REST API View (`iot_devices/views.py`):** ```python # pip install djangorestframework from rest_framework import status, generics from rest_framework.response import Response from .models import Device, Telemetry class DeviceListCreate(generics.ListCreateAPIView): queryset = Device.objects.all() def list(self, request, *args, **kwargs): devices = self.get_queryset() data = [{ "id" : d.id, "device_id" : d.device_id, "device_type": d.device_type, "location" : d.location, "is_online" : d.is_online, "last_seen" : d.last_seen } for d in devices] return Response({ "count" : len(data), "devices": data }, status=status.HTTP_200_OK) def create(self, request, *args, **kwargs): d = Device.objects.create( device_id = request.data.get('device_id'), device_type = request.data.get('device_type', 'SENSOR'), location = request.data.get('location', 'unknown'), is_online = request.data.get('is_online', False) ) return Response({ "id" : d.id, "message" : "Device registered successfully", "device" : { "device_id": d.device_id, "location": d.location } }, status=status.HTTP_201_CREATED) ``` **Step 4 — URL Configuration (`iot_devices/urls.py`):** ```python from django.urls import path from .views import DeviceListCreate urlpatterns = [ path('api/devices/', DeviceListCreate.as_view(), name='device-list-create'), ] ``` **Step 5 — Project URL inclusion (`iot_cloud/urls.py`):** ```python from django.contrib import admin from django.urls import path, include urlpatterns = [ path('admin/', admin.site.urls), path('', include('iot_devices.urls')), ] ``` **Step 6 — Migration & Run:** ```bash python manage.py makemigrations iot_devices python manage.py migrate python manage.py createsuperuser python manage.py runserver 0.0.0.0:8000 ``` --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing Django's "View" with MVC's "View":** In Django, `views.py` contains the **controller logic** (request handling), NOT the HTML template. The template is in `templates/`. Examiners specifically look for this distinction — writing the wrong definition costs 2–3 marks. > > 2. **Forgetting HTTP Status Codes:** Returning a dict without explicit status codes (e.g., `200`, `201`, `400`, `404`) loses 1 mark per endpoint. > > 3. **Not mentioning `methods=['GET', 'POST']` in Flask routes:** The default Flask route accepts only GET. A POST request will return `405 Method Not Allowed` — students often forget this and lose marks. > > 4. **Hardcoding `localhost` instead of `0.0.0.0`:** The IoT gateway must be reachable from other devices on the network. Using `app.run(host='127.0.0.1')` blocks external IoT device access — examiner deducts 1 mark. > > 5. **Missing `related_name` in ForeignKey:** Without `related_name='telemetry'`, you cannot do `device.telemetry.all()` in Django — a common ORM pitfall. > > 6. **Not using `request.get_json(silent=True)`:** Without `silent=True`, a malformed JSON payload raises a 500 error instead of returning a graceful 400 response. > > 7. **Skipping migrations:** Writing models but not running `makemigrations` + `migrate` — model is never created in the database, full functional marks lost. --- ### 5.3 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **🎯 High-Yield KTU 2024 Module 3 — Python Web Framework Quick Recall:** - ✅ **Definition:** A Python Web Framework is a reusable code library providing routing, templating, ORM, and middleware for HTTP/IoT applications. - ✅ **Three Top Frameworks:** **Flask** (micro), **Django** (full-stack), **FastAPI** (async). - ✅ **WSGI** = Synchronous gateway (PEP 3333); **ASGI** = Asynchronous gateway (WebSockets, HTTP/2). - ✅ **MVC** = Model-View-Controller (Flask style). **MVT** = Model-View-Template (Django style) — Django's "View" = Controller in MVC. - ✅ **Flask Core Decorator:** `@app.route('/path', methods=['GET','POST'])`. - ✅ **Django Project Layout:** `project/` (settings) + `app/` (models, views, urls, admin). - ✅ **Django ORM Command Sequence:** `makemigrations` → `migrate` → `createsuperuser` → `runserver`. - ✅ **HTTP Status Codes for IoT APIs:** `200` (OK), `201` (Created), `400` (Bad Request), `404` (Not Found), `409` (Conflict), `500` (Server Error). - ✅ **JSON Serialization:** Use `jsonify()` in Flask, `Response()` in DRF Django, automatic in FastAPI via Pydantic. - ✅ **CORS:** Cross-Origin Resource Sharing is mandatory for cross-domain IoT dashboards. - ✅ **Blueprint (Flask):** Modular route grouping for large IoT projects. - ✅ **IoT Hosting Best Practice:** Always bind Flask to `host='0.0.0.0'` to allow external devices (ESP32, ESP8266) to reach the gateway. - ✅ **Production Server:** Never use Flask's built-in server in production — use **Gunicorn**, **uWSGI**, or **uvicorn** (FastAPI). - ✅ **Security:** Use **JWT tokens** or **API keys** to secure IoT control endpoints; enable CSRF for Django POST forms. - ✅ **Databases:** SQLite (development), PostgreSQL (cloud), **InfluxDB** (time-series IoT data), **Redis** (caching). - ✅ **Common IoT Endpoints Pattern:** `/api/sensors` (CRUD), `/api/actuators/control`, `/api/telemetry/stream`. - ✅ **REST Verbs Mapping:** `GET` (read), `POST` (create), `PUT` (update), `DELETE` (remove). - ✅ **Request Object Methods:** `request.args` (query), `request.form` (form), `request.get_json()` (JSON body), `request.headers` (auth tokens). - ✅ **Jinja2 Syntax:** `{{ variable }}` (output), `{% for item in list %}` (loop), `{% if condition %}` (branch). - ✅ **Asynchronous Advantage:** FastAPI with `async def` can handle 10,000+ concurrent WebSocket connections — ideal for high-frequency IoT telemetry.

topics.title: Designing a RESTful Web API
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT504 - PROGRAMMING FOR INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Designing a RESTful Web API

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Designing a RESTful Web API - Foundational Concepts

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **REST (Representational State Transfer)** is an architectural style, proposed by **Roy Fielding** in his **2000 PhD dissertation**, that defines a set of constraints to be used for creating web services. A **RESTful Web API** is an Application Programming Interface that conforms to the architectural constraints of REST, enabling stateless, cacheable, client-server communication over standard HTTP/HTTPS protocols.

In the context of the **Internet of Things (IoT)**, a RESTful Web API serves as the standardized communication bridge between resource-constrained edge devices, gateways, cloud backends, and end-user applications. It uses **HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH)** as verbs to manipulate **resources** (identified by **URIs/URLs**) which are represented in formats like **JSON (JavaScript Object Notation)** or **XML**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology to Remember for KTU Exams:**
> - **Resource**: Any piece of information that can be named (e.g., a sensor, a reading, a device).
> - **URI (Uniform Resource Identifier)**: The unique address of a resource (e.g., `/api/v1/sensors/temp01`).
> - **Representation**: The current or desired state of a resource (typically JSON).
> - **Statelessness**: Each request from client to server must contain all the information necessary to understand and complete the request.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy - The "Smart Restaurant" Model

Imagine a RESTful API as a **highly organized restaurant**:

- **The Menu (API Documentation)** is the contract. It lists all the dishes (endpoints) you can order and describes each (parameters, expected output).
- **The Waiter (HTTP Protocol)** is the messenger. You (the client) make a request through the waiter. The waiter doesn't remember what you ordered yesterday — every visit is a fresh interaction (**stateless**).
- **The Kitchen (Server/Database)** prepares the dish (response) based purely on the order ticket (request).
- **The Dish (Resource Representation in JSON)** is what arrives at your table. It is *not* the actual ingredient in the kitchen; it is a *representation* of it.
- **Your Order Slip (HTTP Request)** has a clear structure: a table number (**URI**), a verb (**GET dish, POST order, DELETE item**), and any special instructions (headers, body).

Just as a restaurant doesn't need to know your history to serve you lunch today, a RESTful server doesn't retain client session state — every request is self-contained.

## 1.3 Why RESTful APIs Matter in IoT

In an IoT ecosystem, thousands of sensors continuously generate data. A RESTful Web API provides a **standardized, language-agnostic, lightweight, and scalable** mechanism to:
1. **Ingest** telemetry data from sensors to the cloud (e.g., `POST /api/sensors/{id}/readings`).
2. **Retrieve** historical data for dashboards (e.g., `GET /api/sensors/{id}/readings?from=...&to=...`).
3. **Control** actuators remotely (e.g., `PUT /api/actuators/{id}/state`).
4. **Register/Deregister** devices dynamically (e.g., `POST /api/devices`, `DELETE /api/devices/{id}`).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Guiding Constraints of REST (Fielding's Dissertation)

A system is "truly RESTful" only if it satisfies these six architectural constraints. KTU questions frequently ask students to **list and explain** these.

1. **Client-Server Separation**
   - The client (mobile app, IoT device firmware) and server (cloud backend) are decoupled. They evolve independently. The client does not need to know how the server stores data, and vice versa.

2. **Statelessness** 
   - Each request from client to server must contain **all** the information needed to process it. The server stores **no client context** between requests. This makes scaling (horizontal scaling across multiple servers) trivial.

3. **Cacheability**
   - Responses must define themselves as cacheable or non-cacheable. If a response is cacheable, the client may reuse that response data for equivalent future requests, reducing load on the IoT cloud.

4. **Uniform Interface**
   - This is the cornerstone. It is decomposed into four sub-constraints:
     - **Identification of Resources** (via URIs)
     - **Manipulation of Resources through Representations** (JSON/XML)
     - **Self-Descriptive Messages** (HTTP methods, status codes, headers)
     - **Hypermedia as the Engine of Application State (HATEOAS)**

5. **Layered System**
   - The architecture is composed of hierarchical layers (e.g., Load Balancer → API Gateway → Auth Service → Business Logic → Database). Each layer knows only about the immediate layer it interacts with.

6. **Code on Demand (Optional)**
   - Servers can temporarily extend client functionality by transferring executable code (e.g., JavaScript applets). This is the *only optional* constraint and is rarely used in pure IoT REST APIs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Statelessness + Uniform Interface are the two most heavily tested constraints. Always quote Roy Fielding (2000) when defining REST.

## 2.2 HTTP Methods as Verbs (The Action Layer)

The HTTP protocol provides a rich vocabulary. Mapping them correctly to CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) operations is a high-weightage KTU topic.

| HTTP Method | CRUD Action | Idempotent? | Safe? | Purpose in IoT Context |
|-------------|-------------|-------------|-------|------------------------|
| **GET** | Read | Yes | Yes | Fetch sensor data, device list |
| **POST** | Create | No | No | Register a new device, submit a reading |
| **PUT** | Update/Replace | Yes | No | Update full device config |
| **PATCH** | Partial Update | No | No | Update only one field (e.g., threshold) |
| **DELETE** | Delete | Yes | No | Remove a device from registry |
| **HEAD** | Read Headers | Yes | Yes | Check if a resource exists |
| **OPTIONS** | Discover | Yes | Yes | List allowed methods on resource |

> [!NOTE]
> **Idempotent** means performing the operation multiple times produces the same result. **Safe** means the operation does not modify server state. KTU often asks: *"Why is GET safe but POST is not?"*

## 2.3 HTTP Status Codes - The Server's Reply Slip

A well-designed REST API never returns just a body — it always returns a **standardized 3-digit HTTP status code**.

| Class | Range | Meaning | Common Codes |
|-------|-------|---------|--------------|
| **1xx Informational** | 100–199 | Request received, continuing process | 100 Continue |
| **2xx Success** | 200–299 | Request succeeded | **200 OK**, 201 Created, 204 No Content |
| **3xx Redirection** | 300–399 | Further action needed | 301 Moved Permanently, 304 Not Modified |
| **4xx Client Error** | 400–499 | Malformed request | **400 Bad Request**, **401 Unauthorized**, **404 Not Found**, 409 Conflict |
| **5xx Server Error** | 500–599 | Server failed | **500 Internal Server Error**, 503 Service Unavailable |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Design Rules Cheat Sheet

| Design Rule | Correct Form | Incorrect Form | Reason |
|-------------|--------------|----------------|--------|
| Use **Nouns**, not Verbs in URI | `/api/devices/101` | `/api/getDevice/101` | HTTP method already conveys the verb |
| Use **Plural** for collections | `/api/sensors` | `/api/sensor` | REST represents collections |
| Use **Hierarchical URIs** for relationships | `/api/devices/101/sensors/3` | `/api/sensors?device=101&sensor=3` | URI structure shows ownership |
| Versioning in URI | `/api/v1/devices` | `/api/devices?v=1` | Cleanest, easiest to route |
| Use **kebab-case** for multi-word | `/api/sensor-readings` | `/api/sensorReadings` | URL-friendly convention |
| Filter with **Query Strings** | `/api/sensors?type=temp&status=active` | `/api/sensors/temp/active` | Filters are not sub-resources |
| Use **JSON** for representation | `{"id":101,"value":24.5}` | XML (legacy) | Lightweight, native to JavaScript/IoT |
| Use **HTTPS** always | `https://api.iot.com/...` | `http://...` | Encryption for IoT security |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

RESTful API design is the **de facto industry standard** for IoT platforms. Real-world examples:
- **AWS IoT Core** exposes REST APIs to manage things, shadows, and rules.
- **Google Cloud IoT** uses REST for device registry management.
- **Philips Hue Smart Lighting** uses a RESTful API to control bulbs (PUT `/lights/1/state`).
- **Tesla Vehicle API** uses REST endpoints for telemetry and control.

Mastering RESTful design enables a B.Tech graduate to integrate with virtually any modern cloud IoT service.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Analytical Derivation - Designing a RESTful API for an IoT System

## 3.1 Problem Statement (Typical KTU Scenario)

> *"Design a RESTful Web API for a Smart Agriculture IoT system that monitors soil moisture and temperature sensors deployed in a field. The system should allow users to register sensors, push readings, retrieve historical data, and configure alert thresholds."*

## 3.2 Step 1 - Identify Resources (The "Noun Extraction" Phase)

**Logic:** REST is built around *resources*, not actions. We extract every noun from the problem domain.

**Extracted Resources:**
- **Sensor** (a physical IoT device with a unique ID)
- **Reading** (a single data point from a sensor)
- **Threshold** (the alert configuration for a sensor)
- **Field** (the geographical area containing sensors)

**Conversion Logic:** Each noun becomes a URI collection.

## 3.3 Step 2 - Map Resources to URI Hierarchies

We design the URI tree top-down, using **plural nouns** and **hierarchy** to show ownership.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Root API} &= \texttt{/api/v1} \\[4pt]
\text{Field Collection} &= \texttt{/api/v1/fields} \\[4pt]
\text{Single Field} &= \texttt{/api/v1/fields/\{fieldId\}} \\[4pt]
\text{Sensors within a Field} &= \texttt{/api/v1/fields/\{fieldId\}/sensors} \\[4pt]
\text{Single Sensor} &= \texttt{/api/v1/fields/\{fieldId\}/sensors/\{sensorId\}} \\[4pt]
\text{Readings from a Sensor} &= \texttt{/api/v1/fields/\{fieldId\}/sensors/\{sensorId\}/readings} \\[4pt]
\text{Threshold of a Sensor} &= \texttt{/api/v1/fields/\{fieldId\}/sensors/\{sensorId\}/threshold}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why hierarchical?** It naturally enforces the parent-child relationship (a reading *belongs to* a sensor, a sensor *belongs to* a field), enabling scoped authorization (e.g., "user A can access field 1, but not field 2").

## 3.4 Step 3 - Map CRUD to HTTP Methods on Each URI

We now build the **Endpoint Matrix** — the most important deliverable of RESTful API design.

| # | HTTP Method | URI Template | Action | Request Body (JSON) | Success Response | Failure Codes |
|---|-------------|--------------|--------|---------------------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | `POST` | `/api/v1/fields` | Register a new field | `{"name":"North Field","area":2.5}` | `201 Created` + Location header | 400, 409 |
| 2 | `GET` | `/api/v1/fields` | List all fields | (none) | `200 OK` + JSON array | 401 |
| 3 | `GET` | `/api/v1/fields/{fieldId}` | Get one field | (none) | `200 OK` | 404, 401 |
| 4 | `PUT` | `/api/v1/fields/{fieldId}` | Update a field | `{"name":"N-Field","area":3.0}` | `200 OK` | 400, 404 |
| 5 | `DELETE` | `/api/v1/fields/{fieldId}` | Delete a field | (none) | `204 No Content` | 404 |
| 6 | `POST` | `/api/v1/fields/{fieldId}/sensors` | Register a new sensor | `{"type":"soil_moisture","model":"SM-100"}` | `201 Created` | 400, 404 |
| 7 | `GET` | `/api/v1/fields/{fieldId}/sensors` | List sensors in field | (none) | `200 OK` | 404 |
| 8 | `POST` | `/api/v1/fields/{fieldId}/sensors/{sensorId}/readings` | **Push sensor data** | `{"value":24.5,"unit":"°C","timestamp":"2025-01-15T10:30:00Z"}` | `201 Created` | 400, 404 |
| 9 | `GET` | `/api/v1/fields/{fieldId}/sensors/{sensorId}/readings` | Fetch historical data | (none; uses `?from=...&to=...`) | `200 OK` + array | 404 |
| 10 | `GET` | `/api/v1/fields/{fieldId}/sensors/{sensorId}/threshold` | Get threshold | (none) | `200 OK` | 404 |
| 11 | `PUT` | `/api/v1/fields/{fieldId}/sensors/{sensorId}/threshold` | Set threshold | `{"min":18,"max":30,"alertLevel":"HIGH"}` | `200 OK` | 400, 404 |

## 3.5 Step 4 - Design the JSON Representation (Schema)

A *good* REST API publishes its data models. For the **Reading** resource:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{Reading JSON Schema:} \quad \{ \\
\quad \texttt{"id"} &: \texttt{"read\_a1b2c3"} \quad \text{(server-generated UUID)} \\
\quad \texttt{"sensorId"} &: \texttt{"sensor\_007"} \quad \text{(foreign key)} \\
\quad \texttt{"value"} &: 24.5 \quad \text{(float, actual measurement)} \\
\quad \texttt{"unit"} &: \texttt{"°C"} \quad \text{(SI unit, string)} \\
\quad \texttt{"timestamp"} &: \texttt{"2025-01-15T10:30:00Z"} \quad \text{(ISO 8601 UTC)} \\
\quad \texttt{"quality"} &: \texttt{"GOOD"} \quad \text{(enum: GOOD, SUSPECT, BAD)} \\
\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Timestamps are always ISO 8601 in UTC (`Z` suffix). Units are explicit to prevent ambiguity (e.g., Celsius vs Fahrenheit). IDs are opaque server-generated strings (UUIDs) — clients should never parse them for meaning.

## 3.6 Step 5 - Implement Stateless Authentication

Since REST is stateless, we cannot use server-side sessions. We use **tokens**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{Authentication Header Format:} \quad \texttt{Authorization: Bearer <jwt\_token>}
\end{aligned}
$$

The flow:
1. Client sends `POST /api/v1/auth/login` with username/password.
2. Server returns a signed **JWT (JSON Web Token)**.
3. Every subsequent request includes the JWT in the header.
4. Server **does not** store the session — it just verifies the JWT signature (stateless).

## 3.7 Step 6 - Error Response Standardization

A well-designed REST API returns a consistent JSON error envelope:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{Error Response:} \quad \{ \\
\quad \texttt{"status"} &: 404 \\
\quad \texttt{"code"} &: \texttt{"SENSOR\_NOT\_FOUND"} \\
\quad \texttt{"message"} &: \texttt{"Sensor with id sensor\_007 does not exist."} \\
\quad \texttt{"timestamp"} &: \texttt{"2025-01-15T10:35:21Z"} \\
\quad \texttt{"path"} &: \texttt{"/api/v1/fields/f1/sensors/sensor\_007"} \\
\}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is critical for IoT device firmware that needs to programmatically decide retry behavior.

## 3.8 Step 7 - Apply HATEOAS (Hypermedia Links)

The response for `GET /api/v1/fields/{fieldId}/sensors/{sensorId}` should include **hyperlinks** to related actions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{HATEOAS Response:} \quad \{ \\
\quad \texttt{"id"} &: \texttt{"sensor\_007"} \\
\quad \texttt{"type"} &: \texttt{"soil\_moisture"} \\
\quad \texttt{"\_links"} &: \{ \\
\qquad \texttt{"self"} &: \{ \texttt{"href"} : \texttt{"/api/v1/fields/f1/sensors/sensor\_007"} \} \\
\qquad \texttt{"readings"} &: \{ \texttt{"href"} : \texttt{"/api/v1/fields/f1/sensors/sensor\_007/readings"} \} \\
\qquad \texttt{"threshold"} &: \{ \texttt{"href"} : \texttt{"/api/v1/fields/f1/sensors/sensor\_007/threshold"} \} \\
\quad \} \\
\}
\end{aligned}
$$

This allows the client to **discover** all available actions by following links, rather than hardcoding URIs.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 REST Architectural Flow in an IoT Context

The following diagram illustrates how a sensor reading travels from a physical device to a cloud dashboard through a RESTful API.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical IoT Sensor] -->|1. Reads value 24.5°C| B[Device Firmware MCU]
    B -->|2. Constructs JSON| C[HTTP Client on Device]
    C -->|3. POST /api/v1/fields/f1/sensors/s7/readings| D[TLS Termination / Load Balancer]
    D --> E[API Gateway]
    E -->|4. Validates JWT| F[Auth Service]
    F -->|5. Token Valid| G[Business Logic Layer]
    G -->|6. Persist to DB| H[(Time-Series Database)]
    G -->|7. Trigger if threshold crossed| I[Alert Engine]
    I -->|8. Notify user| J[Mobile App / Dashboard]
    K[Client App] -->|9. GET /api/v1/fields/f1/sensors/s7/readings| D
    D --> E
    E --> G
    G --> H
    H --> G
    G -->|10. 200 OK + JSON array| K
```

## 4.2 CRUD-to-HTTP Method Mapping Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CRUD_Operations
        C1[Create]
        R1[Read]
        U1[Update]
        D1[Delete]
    end
    subgraph HTTP_Methods
        H1[POST]
        H2[GET]
        H3[PUT / PATCH]
        H4[DELETE]
    end
    C1 -.maps to.-> H1
    R1 -.maps to.-> H2
    U1 -.maps to.-> H3
    D1 -.maps to.-> H4
    H1 --> URI1[/api/v1/fields]
    H2 --> URI2[/api/v1/fields/fieldId]
    H3 --> URI3[/api/v1/fields/fieldId]
    H4 --> URI4[/api/v1/fields/fieldId]
```

## 4.3 REST API Request-Response Cycle (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client as IoT Device / Mobile App
    participant Gateway as API Gateway
    participant Server as REST Server
    participant DB as Database
    Client->>Gateway: HTTP Request (Method + URI + Headers + Body)
    Gateway->>Gateway: Authenticate (Verify JWT)
    Gateway->>Server: Forward validated request
    Server->>DB: Query / Insert / Update / Delete
    DB-->>Server: Return data
    Server-->>Gateway: HTTP Response (Status Code + JSON Body)
    Gateway-->>Client: Response delivered to caller
    Note over Client,DB: Every request is independent (Stateless)
```

## 4.4 Richardson Maturity Model (RMM) - Block View

The RMM grades an API on a 4-level scale of REST compliance. KTU may ask you to identify the level of a given API design.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Level 0: The Swamp of POX\nPlain Old XML, single URI, POST only]
    L1[Level 1: Resources\nMultiple URIs, one per resource\nHTTP still used as transport]
    L2[Level 2: HTTP Verbs\nCorrect use of GET, POST, PUT, DELETE\n+ Status Codes]
    L3[Level 3: Hypermedia Controls\nHATEOAS: responses include links\nDiscoverability of actions]
    L0 --> L1 --> L2 --> L3
    style L0 fill:#fbb
    style L1 fill:#fdb
    style L2 fill:#cfc
    style L3 fill:#9f9
```

> [!NOTE]
> A fully RESTful API sits at **Level 3** of the Richardson Maturity Model. Most real-world IoT APIs operate at **Level 2** due to the complexity of HATEOAS in resource-constrained devices.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A - Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Explain the concept of statelessness in RESTful Web APIs. Why is it important for IoT systems?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Statelessness is one of the core architectural constraints of REST, as defined by **Roy Fielding (2000)**. It means that **each request from a client to the server must contain all the information necessary for the server to understand and process that request**. The server does **not store any client context (session state)** between consecutive requests.

For example, in an IoT scenario, when a sensor sends `POST /api/v1/sensors/s007/readings` with its JWT token, the server does not need to remember that this sensor sent data 5 minutes ago. It processes the current request in isolation.

**Importance in IoT (1 mark):**
- Enables **horizontal scalability** — any server in a cluster can handle any request, allowing millions of IoT devices to be served by adding more servers.
- Improves **fault tolerance** — if a server crashes, no session is lost.
- Simplifies **load balancing** — no need for sticky sessions.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
*Differentiate between PUT and PATCH HTTP methods with a suitable IoT example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Both `PUT` and `PATCH` are used to update a resource, but they differ in scope.

| Aspect | PUT | PATCH |
|--------|-----|-------|
| Operation Type | Full replacement | Partial update |
| Idempotency | **Idempotent** (same result on multiple calls) | **Not idempotent** (may differ) |
| Body Required | Yes (complete resource) | Yes (only changed fields) |
| Safety | Not safe (modifies state) | Not safe (modifies state) |

**IoT Example (1.5 marks):**
- `PUT /api/v1/sensors/s007/threshold` with `{"min":18, "max":30, "alertLevel":"HIGH"}` — replaces the *entire* threshold object.
- `PATCH /api/v1/sensors/s007/threshold` with `{"max":35}` — updates *only* the `max` field, leaving `min` and `alertLevel` unchanged.

---

## 5.2 Part B - Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** List and briefly explain the **six architectural constraints of REST** as defined by Roy Fielding. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design the RESTful API endpoints for a **Smart Home Lighting IoT system**. The system has Houses, Rooms, and smart Bulbs. Users must be able to:
   - Register a new house.
   - Add rooms to a house.
   - Install bulbs in a room.
   - Switch bulbs on/off.
   - Retrieve the current state of all bulbs in a house.
   - Set brightness level (0-100) for a bulb.
   
   Provide a complete **endpoint matrix** (URI + HTTP method + sample JSON body). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) - 7 Marks:**

1. **Client-Server Constraint (1 mark):** Separation of concerns. The client UI and server data storage evolve independently. Example: An IoT mobile app and a Python cloud backend can be developed by different teams.

2. **Statelessness (1.5 marks):** Each HTTP request from the client must include all the data needed to process it. The server holds **no session state**. This allows any server in a cluster to handle any request, enabling massive scalability — critical for IoT where millions of devices connect.

3. **Cacheability (1 mark):** Responses must explicitly state whether they are cacheable via HTTP headers (`Cache-Control`, `ETag`). A GET response for sensor data with `Cache-Control: max-age=60` can be reused by the client for 60 seconds, reducing network traffic and server load.

4. **Uniform Interface (1.5 marks):** The single most important constraint. It standardizes how resources are named (URIs), how they are represented (JSON), how they are manipulated (HTTP methods), and how the client navigates (HATEOAS). This decouples client and server implementations.

5. **Layered System (1 mark):** The client cannot tell whether it is communicating with the end server or an intermediary (e.g., API Gateway, load balancer, cache). Each layer only knows the layer below it. This improves scalability and security.

6. **Code on Demand (1 mark):** *(Optional constraint)* Servers can transfer executable code (e.g., JavaScript) to the client to extend its functionality. Rarely used in IoT due to device constraints.

---

**Part (b) - 7 Marks - Endpoint Matrix:**

| # | HTTP Method | URI Template | Action | Sample JSON Body | Success Code |
|---|-------------|--------------|--------|------------------|--------------|
| 1 | `POST` | `/api/v1/houses` | Register a house | `{"name":"Villa","owner":"Anand"}` | 201 |
| 2 | `GET` | `/api/v1/houses` | List all houses | (none) | 200 |
| 3 | `GET` | `/api/v1/houses/{houseId}` | Get one house | (none) | 200 |
| 4 | `POST` | `/api/v1/houses/{houseId}/rooms` | Add a room | `{"name":"Bedroom","floor":1}` | 201 |
| 5 | `GET` | `/api/v1/houses/{houseId}/rooms` | List rooms in a house | (none) | 200 |
| 6 | `POST` | `/api/v1/houses/{houseId}/rooms/{roomId}/bulbs` | Install a bulb | `{"model":"Hue-A19","wattage":9}` | 201 |
| 7 | `GET` | `/api/v1/houses/{houseId}/bulbs` | **Get all bulb states in a house** | (none) | 200 |
| 8 | `GET` | `/api/v1/houses/{houseId}/rooms/{roomId}/bulbs/{bulbId}` | Get state of one bulb | (none) | 200 |
| 9 | `PATCH` | `/api/v1/houses/{houseId}/rooms/{roomId}/bulbs/{bulbId}/state` | **Switch ON/OFF** | `{"isOn":true}` | 200 |
| 10 | `PUT` | `/api/v1/houses/{houseId}/rooms/{roomId}/bulbs/{bulbId}/brightness` | **Set brightness** | `{"level":75}` | 200 |
| 11 | `DELETE` | `/api/v1/houses/{houseId}/rooms/{roomId}/bulbs/{bulbId}` | Remove a bulb | (none) | 204 |

**Design Justifications (1 mark for quality of explanation):**
- **Hierarchical URIs** enforce ownership (a bulb *belongs to* a room *within* a house).
- **PATCH** is used for binary state changes (ON/OFF) — only one field changes.
- **PUT** is used for brightness — represents the *full* state of the brightness resource.
- **Versioning** in URI (`v1`) allows future API evolution without breaking existing clients.
- **Plural nouns** are used for collections (`houses`, `rooms`, `bulbs`).
- All endpoints require **JWT Bearer token** in `Authorization` header for security.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **Richardson Maturity Model (RMM)**. Identify the maturity level of a typical IoT dashboard API that:
   - Uses a single endpoint `/api/iot` with `POST` for all operations.
   - Sends commands as XML payloads.
   - Does not use any HTTP status codes (always returns 200).
   
   Justify your answer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a RESTful API for an **Industrial IoT (IIoT) Machine Monitoring** system with the following requirements:
   - Each machine has a unique ID and emits temperature, vibration, and pressure readings.
   - Operators should be able to register machines, fetch the latest reading, and query historical data within a time range.
   - Maintenance teams should be able to schedule predictive maintenance jobs and mark a job as complete.
   - Provide the **resource hierarchy**, **endpoint table**, and a **sample request-response** for fetching the latest reading of machine `M-101`. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) - 7 Marks:**

The **Richardson Maturity Model (RMM)**, developed by **Leonard Richardson**, grades a web API on its REST compliance across four levels:

- **Level 0 — The Swamp of POX (Plain Old XML):** Single URI, single HTTP method (usually POST). The API uses HTTP only as a transport mechanism. The described API is exactly here:
  - Single endpoint `/api/iot` → violates multiple URIs principle.
  - Uses POST for everything → does not leverage HTTP verbs.
  - Uses XML → POX-style.

- **Level 1 — Resources:** Multiple URIs, one per resource. The API still uses HTTP POST as the only verb, but distinguishes operations by URI.

- **Level 2 — HTTP Verbs:** Correct use of `GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE` and proper HTTP status codes. The described API is *not* at Level 2 because it ignores status codes (always returns 200) and does not use HTTP verbs meaningfully.

- **Level 3 — Hypermedia Controls (HATEOAS):** Responses contain hyperlinks to related actions, enabling discoverability.

**Conclusion (2 marks):** The described IoT API sits at **Level 0** of the RMM. It uses HTTP as a mere transport, ignoring resources, verbs, and status codes. It is **not RESTful** at all.

**Improvements to reach Level 2 (1 mark):**
- Use distinct URIs per resource: `/api/v1/machines/{id}/readings`.
- Use `GET` for reads, `POST` for creation.
- Return appropriate status codes: `200 OK` for successful reads, `404 Not Found` for missing machines.

---

**Part (b) - 7 Marks:**

**Resource Hierarchy (1 mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{Machine} &\rightarrow \texttt{Reading} \\
\texttt{Machine} &\rightarrow \texttt{MaintenanceJob}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Endpoint Table (4 marks):**

| # | HTTP Method | URI Template | Purpose |
|---|-------------|--------------|---------|
| 1 | `POST` | `/api/v1/machines` | Register a new machine |
| 2 | `GET` | `/api/v1/machines` | List all machines |
| 3 | `GET` | `/api/v1/machines/{machineId}` | Get machine details |
| 4 | `GET` | `/api/v1/machines/{machineId}/readings/latest` | **Get latest reading** |
| 5 | `GET` | `/api/v1/machines/{machineId}/readings` | Get historical readings (`?from=...&to=...`) |
| 6 | `POST` | `/api/v1/machines/{machineId}/readings` | Push a new reading (from machine) |
| 7 | `POST` | `/api/v1/machines/{machineId}/maintenance-jobs` | Schedule a maintenance job |
| 8 | `GET` | `/api/v1/machines/{machineId}/maintenance-jobs` | List all jobs for a machine |
| 9 | `PATCH` | `/api/v1/maintenance-jobs/{jobId}` | Mark job as complete |
| 10 | `DELETE` | `/api/v1/machines/{machineId}` | Decommission a machine |

**Sample Request-Response (2 marks):**

**Request:**
$$
\texttt{GET /api/v1/machines/M-101/readings/latest} \\
\texttt{Host: api.iiot.example.com} \\
\texttt{Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9...} \\
\texttt{Accept: application/json}
$$

**Response (HTTP/1.1 200 OK):**
```json
{
  "machineId": "M-101",
  "timestamp": "2025-01-15T11:45:30Z",
  "sensors": {
    "temperature": { "value": 78.4, "unit": "°C", "status": "NORMAL" },
    "vibration":   { "value": 2.31,  "unit": "mm/s", "status": "WARNING" },
    "pressure":    { "value": 5.8,   "unit": "bar",   "status": "NORMAL" }
  },
  "_links": {
    "self":      { "href": "/api/v1/machines/M-101/readings/latest" },
    "historical":{ "href": "/api/v1/machines/M-101/readings" },
    "machine":   { "href": "/api/v1/machines/M-101" }
  }
}
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Verbs in URIs** (e.g., `/getSensor/101`): NEVER use verbs in URIs. HTTP methods are the verbs. **−1 to 2 marks.**
> 2. **Wrong status code**: Returning `200 OK` for every situation, including errors. **−1 mark per error.**
> 3. **Forgetting Statelessness**: Building a RESTful design but then mentioning "server stores session". **−1 mark.**
> 4. **Singular nouns for collections**: `/api/device` instead of `/api/devices`. **−1 mark.**
> 5. **No version in URI**: `/api/sensors` instead of `/api/v1/sensors`. **−1 mark.**
> 6. **Confusing PUT vs PATCH**: Using PUT for partial updates. **−1 to 2 marks.**
> 7. **Omitting Roy Fielding reference** when defining REST. Examiners explicitly look for the year **2000** and the dissertation title. **−0.5 to 1 mark.**
> 8. **Ignoring authentication**: A complete design must mention JWT/OAuth2/API keys. **−1 mark.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — High-Density Summary**

- **REST** = **R**epresentational **S**tate **T**ransfer. Defined by **Roy Fielding in 2000**.
- REST is an **architectural style**, not a protocol or standard. It uses **HTTP** as its transport.
- The **6 constraints** are: Client-Server, **Stateless**, Cacheable, **Uniform Interface**, Layered System, Code on Demand (optional).
- **Stateless** means: *every request carries all info; server stores no client context.*
- **Uniform Interface** = URIs identify resources + HTTP methods act on them + JSON represents them.
- HTTP Methods to remember cold: **`GET`** (read, safe, idempotent), **`POST`** (create, not safe, not idempotent), **`PUT`** (replace, idempotent), **`PATCH`** (partial update, not idempotent), **`DELETE`** (remove, idempotent).
- Status Code Cheat: **2xx** = success, **3xx** = redirect, **4xx** = client's fault, **5xx** = server's fault.
- URI Design Golden Rules: **Nouns only, plural, hierarchical, kebab-case, versioned** (`/api/v1/...`).
- **Richardson Maturity Model** has **4 levels** (0 to 3). True REST is Level 3 (with HATEOAS).
- **HATEOAS** = responses include `_links` to related actions, enabling API discoverability.
- **JSON** is the preferred representation format (over XML) for IoT REST APIs due to its lightweight nature.
- Authentication is achieved via **Bearer tokens / JWT** in the `Authorization` header.
- An **endpoint matrix** (URI × Method × Body × Response) is the standard KTU deliverable for API design questions.
- Always use **HTTPS** for IoT APIs to encrypt telemetry data in transit.
- **Content Negotiation** uses `Accept` (client) and `Content-Type` (server) headers with MIME types like `application/json`.
- For **IoT specifically**, REST is best for *control-plane* operations (device management, config). For *high-frequency telemetry*, MQTT or CoAP are often preferred due to lower overhead.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Connecting to APIs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT504 - PROGRAMMING FOR INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Connecting to APIs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Application Programming Interface (API)** is a formally defined communication contract that allows two software systems to exchange structured data over a network using standardized protocols, request formats, and response semantics.

In the context of **Internet of Things (IoT)**, *Connecting to APIs* refers to the mechanism by which resource-constrained embedded devices, edge gateways, or backend services invoke remote web services to **publish sensor telemetry, retrieve configuration parameters, trigger actuators, or synchronize state** with a cloud platform.

The most prevalent API paradigm used in IoT systems is the **REpresentational State Transfer (REST)** architectural style, which rides on top of the **HyperText Transfer Protocol (HTTP/HTTPS)** and exchanges data encoded primarily in **JavaScript Object Notation (JSON)**.

| Term | Standard Definition |
|---|---|
| **API** | Application Programming Interface |
| **REST** | REpresentational State Transfer |
| **HTTP** | HyperText Transfer Protocol |
| **JSON** | JavaScript Object Notation |
| **MQTT** | Message Queuing Telemetry Transport |
| **URI / URL** | Uniform Resource Identifier / Locator |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Waiter in a Restaurant

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of an API as a **waiter in a restaurant**.

- You (the **IoT device / client**) sit at a table with a menu (the **API documentation**).
- The kitchen (the **cloud server / database**) prepares what you ask for.
- The **waiter (API)** is the *only* channel through which your order travels to the kitchen and the food returns to you.
- You never enter the kitchen; you never touch the stove. You simply speak a structured language (the **request**) and receive a structured reply (the **response**).

This is exactly how a **NodeMCU / ESP32 / Raspberry Pi** talks to **ThingSpeak, Blynk, Firebase, or AWS IoT Core** — it never directly accesses the server's internal database; it sends a properly formatted **HTTP request** and parses the **JSON response** it gets back.

## 1.3 Why APIs Matter in IoT — The Engineering Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** APIs are the *backbone of every cloud-connected IoT system*. Without APIs, an IoT device is an isolated island of data.

Engineering justifications for using APIs in IoT:

- **Interoperability** — A temperature sensor in Kerala can push data to a dashboard in Bengaluru using a standardized API.
- **Scalability** — Cloud providers expose APIs that auto-scale to millions of devices (e.g., **AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT**).
- **Modularity** — Smart home systems use APIs to let the *lighting subsystem* talk to the *HVAC subsystem* without one knowing the internal code of the other.
- **Data persistence** — Sensor readings are stored in time-series databases accessible via REST endpoints.
- **Remote actuation** — A mobile app sends an `HTTP POST` to a cloud API, which then triggers a relay on a faraway device.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard IoT Performance Metric:** Most public IoT cloud APIs operate with an **availability SLA of 99.9 %** (the "three-nines" rule), translating to a maximum allowed downtime of approximately **8.76 hours per year**.

## 1.4 The IoT API Ecosystem — Common Platforms

| Platform | Primary API Style | Typical IoT Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **ThingSpeak** | REST over HTTP | Academic sensor logging |
| **Blynk** | REST + WebSocket | Mobile-controlled prototypes |
| **Firebase Realtime DB** | REST + WebSocket | Low-latency dashboards |
| **AWS IoT Core** | MQTT + REST + Shadow | Industrial-scale fleets |
| **Azure IoT Hub** | MQTT / AMQP / HTTPS | Enterprise telemetry |
| **IFTTT** | REST Webhooks | Event-driven automations |
| **OpenWeatherMap** | REST over HTTPS | Weather-aware IoT |
| **Telegram Bot API** | REST over HTTPS | Alert notifications |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The REST Architectural Constraints (Fielding, 2000)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A RESTful API is identified by six core constraints.** KTU frequently tests *which* constraint is violated by a given design.

1. **Client–Server** separation of concerns.
2. **Statelessness** — every request carries all information the server needs; no session memory.
3. **Cacheability** — responses must declare whether they can be cached.
4. **Uniform Interface** — standardized resource URIs and representations.
5. **Layered System** — client cannot tell if it is talking to the origin server or an intermediary.
6. **Code-on-Demand (optional)** — server can send executable code (e.g., JavaScript).

## 2.2 HTTP Request/Response — The Anatomical Breakdown

An **HTTP transaction** is a text-based, line-delimited conversation. For a B.Tech IoT project sending a temperature reading to a cloud API, the request line is:

$$\text{Request Line} = \underbrace{\text{METHOD}}_{\text{verb}} \; \underbrace{\text{URI /path}}_{\text{resource}} \; \underbrace{\text{HTTP/version}}_{\text{protocol header}}$$

### 2.2.1 HTTP Methods Used in IoT (Verbs)

| Method | CRUD Equivalent | Idempotent? | Typical IoT Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| `GET` | Read | ✅ Yes | Fetch latest sensor value |
| `POST` | Create | ❌ No | Upload new telemetry |
| `PUT` | Update / Replace | ✅ Yes | Overwrite device config |
| `PATCH` | Partial Update | ❌ No | Change a single field |
| `DELETE` | Delete | ✅ Yes | Remove a device record |
| `HEAD` | Metadata only | ✅ Yes | Check API health |

> [!NOTE]
> **Idempotency definition:** An operation is *idempotent* if performing it multiple times produces the same observable result as performing it once. `POST` is **not** idempotent because each call creates a new record; `PUT` **is** idempotent because it overwrites the same resource.

### 2.2.2 Anatomy of an HTTP Request

```
POST /api/v1/devices/esp32_01/telemetry HTTP/1.1        ← Request Line
Host: api.thingSpeak.com                                 ← Header
Content-Type: application/json
Authorization: Bearer <API_TOKEN>
Content-Length: 87
                                                         ← Blank line
{"device_id":"esp32_01","temp":28.4,"hum":62.1}        ← Body (JSON)
```

### 2.2.3 Anatomy of an HTTP Response

```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK                                          ← Status Line
Content-Type: application/json
Date: Mon, 22 Jan 2026 10:30:00 GMT
                                                         ← Blank line
{"status":"ok","entry_id":12345}                        ← Body
```

## 2.3 HTTP Status Codes — The Engineer's Cheat Sheet

| Class | Range | Meaning | Common IoT Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1xx** Informational | 100–199 | Request received, continue | `100 Continue` |
| **2xx** Success | 200–299 | Action completed | `200 OK`, `201 Created`, `204 No Content` |
| **3xx** Redirection | 300–399 | Further action needed | `301 Moved Permanently`, `304 Not Modified` |
| **4xx** Client Error | 400–499 | Fault in the request | `400 Bad Request`, `401 Unauthorized`, `404 Not Found`, `429 Too Many Requests` |
| **5xx** Server Error | 500–599 | Server failed to fulfill valid request | `500 Internal Server Error`, `502 Bad Gateway`, `503 Service Unavailable` |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student pitfall:** Writing `404` for "server crashed" — the *client* is responsible for `4xx` errors; the *server* is responsible for `5xx` errors. A device that sends a malformed JSON will get a `400`, not a `500`.

## 2.4 Data Interchange Formats in IoT APIs

### 2.4.1 JSON (JavaScript Object Notation)

JSON is the de-facto standard for IoT APIs because it is **lightweight**, **human-readable**, and **natively understood by JavaScript / Python / MicroPython** runtimes.

A JSON document is a tree of:

- **Objects** — unordered key/value collections enclosed in `{}`.
- **Arrays** — ordered lists enclosed in `[]`.
- **Primitives** — `string`, `number`, `boolean`, `null`.

Example — A typical IoT telemetry payload:

```json
{
  "device_id": "esp32_lab_07",
  "timestamp": 1737543000,
  "sensors": {
    "temperature_c": 28.4,
    "humidity_pct": 62.1,
    "lux": 412.7
  },
  "location": { "lat": 10.0261, "lon": 76.3125 },
  "battery_mv": 3720,
  "online": true
}
```

### 2.4.2 XML (Extensible Markup Language)

Older industrial IoT systems (SCADA, industrial automation) still use XML. It is **verbose but self-describing**.

```xml
<sensor>
  <id>esp32_lab_07</id>
  <temperature unit="C">28.4</temperature>
  <humidity unit="%">62.1</humidity>
</sensor>
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Verdict for modern IoT:** Use **JSON** unless the platform mandates XML — JSON payloads are typically **30–40 % smaller** than equivalent XML, a critical factor over LoRaWAN / NB-IoT links where every byte costs power and airtime.

## 2.5 API Authentication Mechanisms

Most IoT cloud APIs are *not* open. Devices must prove their identity.

| Mechanism | How It Works | IoT Suitability | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **API Key** | Single shared secret in URL or header | Prototyping only | ThingSpeak `?api_key=...` |
| **Bearer Token (JWT)** | Signed token in `Authorization` header | Production-grade | Firebase, AWS |
| **Basic Auth** | Base64 of `user:pass` | Legacy, never over plain HTTP | Internal admin |
| **OAuth 2.0** | Delegated auth with refresh tokens | Mobile + Cloud apps | Google IoT |
| **mTLS (X.509)** | Mutual TLS with client certificates | Industrial IoT, AWS IoT | Hardware-rooted identity |
| **HMAC Signature** | Cryptographic signature of payload | High-security telemetry | AWS SigV4 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Security Rule:** An API key embedded in plain HTTP is *not* encrypted. For production IoT, always use **HTTPS (TLS 1.2+)** and prefer **mTLS or short-lived JWTs** over long-lived static keys.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Symbol / Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | URI structure | `scheme://host:port/path?query` | `https://api.x.com/v1/dev/01?fields=temp` |
| 2 | Idempotency | $\text{POST} \neq \text{idempotent}$ | Repeating POST creates duplicates |
| 3 | JSON size estimate | $S_{\text{json}} \approx 2N_{keys} + N_{vals} + 4$ | Rough byte cost for tiny payloads |
| 4 | HTTPS overhead | $O_{tls} \approx 5\text{–}15\,\text{KB}$ per handshake | Critical for NB-IoT |
| 5 | API rate-limit guard | $T_{\text{min}} = \frac{3600}{N_{\text{max}}}$ | Min seconds between calls |
| 6 | HTTP status class | $4xx = \text{client}, \; 5xx = \text{server}$ | 4xx = your fault, 5xx = their fault |
| 7 | Battery budget (LoRa) | $E = V \cdot I \cdot t_{\text{airtime}}$ | More bytes ⇒ more energy |
| 8 | Cache freshness | $C = \frac{t_{\text{cached}}}{t_{\text{total}}}$ | Higher ⇒ lower API load |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real IoT deployments, the *battery-life equation* above is the *single most important* reason to choose JSON over XML and to keep API calls to a minimum. Every byte transmitted over a cellular or LoRa link costs the device energy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Building an HTTP API Request from First Principles

We are given an IoT device (an ESP32) that must send **one temperature reading** to a cloud endpoint. Let us *derive* the complete request line, headers, and body.

### Step 1 — Identify the resource

The cloud stores telemetry under the path:

$$\text{path} = \dfrac{\text{API version}}{\text{resource class}} \big/ \text{device id} \big/ \text{action}$$

For our case:

$$\text{path} = \dfrac{\text{api}}{\text{v1}} \big/ \text{devices} \big/ \text{esp32\_01} \big/ \text{telemetry}$$

### Step 2 — Choose the HTTP verb

Because we are *creating* a new telemetry record, the correct verb is `POST`. We **cannot** use `GET` for writing (it would violate REST semantics and be cached by proxies).

$$\text{verb} = \text{POST}$$

### Step 3 — Assemble the Request Line

$$\text{Request Line} = \text{POST} \; /api/v1/devices/esp32\_01/telemetry \; \text{HTTP}/1.1$$

### Step 4 — Compute the JSON Body

The sensor reading is `temp = 28.4 °C`, `hum = 62.1 %`, taken at unix-time `t = 1737543000`.

$$\text{body} = \text{JSON}\big(\{\, \text{temp}: 28.4, \, \text{hum}: 62.1, \, \text{t}: 1737543000 \,\}\big)$$

$$\Rightarrow \text{body} = \{ \text{"temp"}: 28.4, \text{"hum"}: 62.1, \text{"t"}: 1737543000 \}$$

### Step 5 — Compute `Content-Length`

The body as a UTF-8 string is:

```
{"temp":28.4,"hum":62.1,"t":1737543000}
```

Counting the bytes explicitly:

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{body}} &= \underbrace{8}_{\{"temp":\}} + 4 + 1 \\
&\quad + \underbrace{7}_{\text{"hum":\}} + 4 + 1 \\
&\quad + \underbrace{6}_{\text{"t":\}} + 10 + 2 \\
&= 8 + 4 + 1 + 7 + 4 + 1 + 6 + 10 + 2 \\
&= 43 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{Content-Length} = 43$$

### Step 6 — Final Assembled Request

```
POST /api/v1/devices/esp32_01/telemetry HTTP/1.1
Host: api.example.com
Content-Type: application/json
Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9...
Content-Length: 43

{"temp":28.4,"hum":62.1,"t":1737543000}
```

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Rate-Limit Back-Off Calculation

Many free IoT APIs (e.g., **OpenWeatherMap** free tier) cap requests to **N = 60 calls/minute**. We need the minimum *safe* time interval between consecutive requests.

### Step 1 — Define the variables

- $N_{\max} = 60$ — maximum allowed requests.
- $T_{\text{window}} = 60\,\text{s}$ — the time window.

### Step 2 — Compute the average safe interval

$$T_{\text{safe}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{window}}}{N_{\max}} = \dfrac{60}{60} = 1.0\,\text{s}$$

### Step 3 — Add safety margin

To absorb network jitter, we apply a **20 % safety factor**:

$$T_{\text{deploy}} = T_{\text{safe}} \cdot (1 + \sigma) = 1.0 \cdot 1.2 = 1.2\,\text{s}$$

### Step 4 — Final deployment rule

$$\boxed{\; T_{\text{deploy}} \geq 1.2\,\text{s between successive API calls \;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** If your ESP32 sends telemetry faster than once every **1.2 seconds** to OpenWeatherMap's free tier, you will receive `HTTP 429 Too Many Requests` and your data will be silently dropped by the gateway.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — JSON Size vs. XML Size Comparison

Suppose a sensor produces three readings: `{temp=28.4, hum=62.1, lux=412.7}`.

### JSON version (as built above for a slightly different payload)

$$\text{body}_{\text{json}} = \{ \text{"temp"}: 28.4, \text{"hum"}: 62.1, \text{"lux"}: 412.7 \}$$

Counting bytes: `{"temp":28.4,"hum":62.1,"lux":412.7}` →

$$S_{\text{json}} = 8 + 4 + 1 + 7 + 4 + 1 + 7 + 5 + 2 = 39 \text{ bytes}$$

### XML equivalent

```xml
<s><t>28.4</t><h>62.1</h><l>412.7</l></s>
```

Counting: `<s><t>28.4</t><h>62.1</h><l>412.7</l></s>`

$$S_{\text{xml}} = 3 + 7 + 3 + 7 + 3 + 7 + 4 = 65 \text{ bytes}$$

### Overhead ratio

$$R_{\text{overhead}} = \dfrac{S_{\text{xml}} - S_{\text{json}}}{S_{\text{json}}} \cdot 100\%$$

$$R_{\text{overhead}} = \dfrac{65 - 39}{39} \cdot 100\% = \dfrac{26}{39} \cdot 100\% \approx 66.7\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** XML is **~66.7 % larger** than JSON for the *same* data. Over a 1-year deployment with 1 reading per minute:

$$N_{\text{reads}} = 60 \cdot 24 \cdot 365 = 525\,600 \text{ readings / year}$$

$$B_{\text{wasted}} = 26 \text{ bytes} \cdot 525\,600 = 13.66 \text{ MB / year}$$

Wasted energy and bandwidth — exactly why IoT platforms standardized on JSON.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right API Method

| Use-Case in IoT | Method | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Read current sensor value | `GET /sensors/temp` | Idempotent, cacheable |
| Upload a new reading | `POST /telemetry` | Creates a new record |
| Update device firmware URL | `PUT /devices/01/firmware` | Full replacement, idempotent |
| Change Wi-Fi SSID only | `PATCH /devices/01` | Partial update |
| Remove a retired device | `DELETE /devices/01` | Idempotent removal |
| Check if API is alive | `HEAD /health` | Headers only, no body |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student pitfall:** Using `GET` to "send" data by appending it to the URL (e.g., `GET /set?temp=28.4`). This **violates REST semantics**, can be cached by intermediate proxies, exposes the data in server logs, and is rejected by most modern IoT platforms.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid blocks follow the **alphanumeric-node-ID rule** and avoid reserved keywords as standalone node names. All labels are raw uppercase text without markdown formatting inside quotes.

## 4.2 Diagram A — End-to-End IoT Device ⇄ Cloud API Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph fieldLayer["FIELD LAYER SENSORS AND ACTUATORS"]
        sensorA["SENSOR DHT22 TEMP HUMIDITY"]
        sensorB["SENSOR LDR LIGHT"]
        actA["ACTUATOR RELAY PUMP"]
    end

    subgraph edgeLayer["EDGE LAYER MCU"]
        mcuA["MCU ESP32 MICROPYTHON"]
        wifiA["WIFI TLS STACK"]
    end

    subgraph cloudLayer["CLOUD LAYER API"]
        gwA["API GATEWAY AUTH RATE LIMIT"]
        apiA["REST API ENDPOINT POST TELEMETRY"]
        dba["TIME SERIES DATABASE"]
        ruleA["RULE ENGINE ALERT"]
    end

    subgraph clientLayer["CLIENT LAYER"]
        appA["MOBILE APP GET DASHBOARD"]
        webA["WEB DASHBOARD GET HISTORICAL"]
    end

    sensorA --> mcuA
    sensorB --> mcuA
    mcuA --> wifiA
    wifiA -->|HTTPS POST JSON| gwA
    gwA --> apiA
    apiA --> dba
    dba --> ruleA
    ruleA -->|ALERT| apiA
    apiA -->|HTTPS GET JSON| appA
    apiA -->|HTTPS GET JSON| webA
    appA -->|HTTPS POST COMMAND| gwA
    gwA -->|MQTT DOWNLINK| mcuA
    mcuA --> actA
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **Field Layer** collects physical phenomena.
- The **Edge Layer (ESP32)** packages readings into a **JSON payload** and pushes them over **HTTPS POST** to the API Gateway.
- The **Cloud Layer** authenticates, stores, and runs alert rules.
- The **Client Layer** pulls historical data via `GET` and can issue commands via `POST` that are mirrored back to the device using **MQTT downlink** (a common pattern in AWS IoT).

## 4.3 Diagram B — HTTP Request/Response Lifecycle (Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant dev as IOT DEVICE ESP32
    participant net as NETWORK TLS
    participant api as CLOUD API SERVER
    participant db as DATABASE

    dev->>dev: STEP 1 SERIALIZE SENSOR DATA TO JSON
    dev->>net: STEP 2 OPEN TCP CONNECTION PORT 443
    net->>api: STEP 3 TLS HANDSHAKE CERT VERIFY
    dev->>api: STEP 4 SEND HTTP REQUEST LINE HEADERS BODY
    api->>api: STEP 5 PARSE JSON VALIDATE TOKEN
    api->>db: STEP 6 INSERT TELEMETRY ROW
    db-->>api: STEP 7 CONFIRM WRITE
    api-->>dev: STEP 8 HTTP 200 OK JSON RESPONSE
    dev->>dev: STEP 9 PARSE RESPONSE LOG ENTRY ID
    dev->>net: STEP 10 CLOSE OR KEEP ALIVE TLS SOCKET
```

## 4.4 Diagram C — Block-Level Decision Flow: When to Use POST vs GET vs MQTT

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["START DECISION DATA DIRECTION AND FREQUENCY"]
    q1{"Q1 IS DATA BIDIRECTIONAL OR REAL TIME STREAM"}
    q2{"Q2 ARE PACKETS SMALLER THAN 1 KB"}
    q3{"Q3 IS LATENCY BUDGET BELOW 1 SECOND"}
    q4{"Q4 IS POWER BUDGET STRICT BATTERY OPERATED"}
    outMqtt["OUT USE MQTT OVER TCP TLS PORT 8883"]
    outHttpPost["OUT USE HTTPS POST INTERMITTENT UPLOADS"]
    outRest["OUT USE REST GET FOR DASHBOARD POLLING"]

    startA --> q1
    q1 -->|YES| q3
    q1 -->|NO| q2
    q3 -->|YES| outMqtt
    q3 -->|NO| outHttpPost
    q2 -->|YES| q4
    q2 -->|NO| outHttpPost
    q4 -->|YES| outMqtt
    q4 -->|NO| outRest
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, Part-A questions test *Remember* and *Understand* levels of **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy**. Answers should be concise (≈80–120 words).

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**Define REST API. List any four HTTP methods used in IoT applications.**

**Model Answer:**

A **REST (REpresentational State Transfer) API** is an architectural style for designing networked applications that use the stateless **HTTP** protocol and standard HTTP methods to manipulate resources identified by URIs.

The four most common HTTP methods used in IoT are:

1. **GET** — to read/retrieve sensor data.
2. **POST** — to upload new telemetry readings.
3. **PUT** — to update or replace device configuration.
4. **DELETE** — to remove a device record from the cloud.

**CO Mapping:** CO3 — *Apply* | **Bloom Level:** L2 (Understand) | **Marks:** 3

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]

**Differentiate between JSON and XML as data interchange formats for IoT.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | JSON | XML |
|---|---|---|
| **Syntax** | Lightweight key-value | Tag-based, verbose |
| **Size** | ~40–60 % smaller | Larger overhead |
| **Parsing** | Native in JS / Python | Requires DOM/SAX parser |
| **Data types** | Native (number, bool, null) | All strings |
| **IoT use** | Cloud APIs, mobile | Legacy industrial SCADA |

For modern IoT, **JSON is preferred** because of its smaller payload, faster parsing on microcontrollers, and direct mapping to programming language objects.

**CO Mapping:** CO3 — *Apply* | **Bloom Level:** L4 (Analyze) | **Marks:** 3

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** *Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, has internal choice, and must be answered for a total of two questions. Sub-parts (a) and (b) typically carry 7 marks each.*

---

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an IoT system that connects a temperature sensor to a cloud REST API. List the layers and the protocol used at each interface. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** An ESP32 with a DHT22 sensor is programmed to send temperature readings to a cloud API at **api.weather-iot.in**. The endpoint accepts JSON via `POST /v1/readings` and authenticates using a **Bearer token**. Construct the complete **HTTP request** (request line, headers, JSON body) for a reading of `temp = 30.2 °C`, `hum = 58.4 %`, taken at Unix timestamp `1737543000`. Show every step of how the body and `Content-Length` are calculated. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

The **four-layer IoT-API architecture** is:

1. **Perception / Field Layer** — physical sensors (DHT22) and actuators.
2. **Network / Edge Layer** — microcontroller (ESP32) with Wi-Fi and TLS stack that formats the JSON payload.
3. **Cloud / API Layer** — API Gateway, REST endpoint, time-series database, rule engine.
4. **Application / Client Layer** — mobile app and web dashboard that poll data via `GET` requests.

**Protocols used at each interface:**

- Sensor ⇄ ESP32 → **GPIO / I²C / One-Wire** (proprietary sensor bus)
- ESP32 ⇄ Cloud → **HTTPS (HTTP over TLS 1.2)** on **port 443**
- Cloud ⇄ Mobile App → **HTTPS / WebSocket**

A Mermaid block diagram is shown in **Section 4.2** above for reference.

**Mark split:** Diagram = 3, Layer explanation = 2, Protocols = 2. **(Total 7)**

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Choose the verb**

We are *creating* a new reading → use **POST**.

**Step 2 — Build the request line**

```
POST /v1/readings HTTP/1.1
```

**Step 3 — Build the JSON body**

The body must be a single JSON object containing the readings:

```json
{"temp":30.2,"hum":58.4,"ts":1737543000}
```

**Step 4 — Compute Content-Length byte by byte**

$$\begin{aligned}
L &= \underbrace{1}_{\{} + \underbrace{8}_{\text{"temp":\}} + \underbrace{4}_{30.2} + \underbrace{1}_{,} \\
&\quad + \underbrace{7}_{\text{"hum":\}} + \underbrace{4}_{58.4} + \underbrace{1}_{,} \\
&\quad + \underbrace{6}_{\text{"ts":\}} + \underbrace{10}_{1737543000} + \underbrace{1}_{\}} \\
&= 1 + 8 + 4 + 1 + 7 + 4 + 1 + 6 + 10 + 1 = 43 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Final assembled HTTP request**

```
POST /v1/readings HTTP/1.1
Host: api.weather-iot.in
Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9...
Content-Type: application/json
Content-Length: 43
Connection: close

{"temp":30.2,"hum":58.4,"ts":1737543000}
```

**Mark split:** Verb = 1, Request line = 1, JSON = 1, Byte calculation = 2, Full request = 2. **(Total 7)**

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (INTERNAL CHOICE)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

> **(a)** Explain the four common **API authentication mechanisms** used in IoT systems. For each, state one advantage and one disadvantage. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A free-tier IoT API enforces a rate limit of **60 requests per minute**. Calculate the **minimum safe interval** between two consecutive requests. If a sensor node sends a payload of **256 bytes JSON** every 30 seconds, compute the **total bytes uploaded in 24 hours** and the **percentage overhead** of HTTP headers (assume 400 bytes of fixed HTTP headers per request). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

| Mechanism | Description | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **API Key** | Shared secret string in URL or header | Simple, easy to use | Visible in logs; no per-user revocation |
| **Bearer Token (JWT)** | Signed token in `Authorization` header | Stateless, expires automatically | Must refresh periodically |
| **OAuth 2.0** | Delegated auth via access/refresh tokens | User can revoke 3rd-party access | Complex flow; overkill for sensors |
| **mTLS (X.509)** | Mutual TLS with client certificates | Strongest device identity | Requires secure element on device |

**Mark split:** 4 mechanisms × 1.5 marks = 6, Conclusion 1 mark = **7**

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Rate limit interval**

$$T_{\text{safe}} = \frac{60\,\text{s}}{60} = 1.0\,\text{s/req}$$

**Step 2 — Number of requests in 24 h at 30 s interval**

$$N = \frac{24 \cdot 60 \cdot 60}{30} = \frac{86400}{30} = 2880 \text{ requests}$$

**Step 3 — Total JSON payload in 24 h**

$$B_{\text{payload}} = 256 \cdot 2880 = 737\,280 \text{ bytes} \approx 720\,\text{KB}$$

**Step 4 — Total HTTP header overhead in 24 h**

$$B_{\text{header}} = 400 \cdot 2880 = 1\,152\,000 \text{ bytes} \approx 1.10\,\text{MB}$$

**Step 5 — Total data transferred**

$$B_{\text{total}} = B_{\text{payload}} + B_{\text{header}} = 720 + 1125 = 1845\,\text{KB}$$

**Step 6 — Percentage overhead of headers**

$$\% \text{overhead} = \frac{B_{\text{header}}}{B_{\text{total}}} \cdot 100\% = \frac{1125}{1845} \cdot 100\% \approx 60.98\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Headers account for **~61 % of the total bandwidth** in this typical IoT use-case. For battery-powered NB-IoT or LoRa nodes, batching multiple readings into one request is the standard mitigation.

**Mark split:** Interval = 1, Request count = 1, Payload = 1, Header = 1, Overhead % = 3. **(Total 7)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in "Connecting to APIs" questions:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up verbs** — using `GET` to *send* data, or `POST` to *fetch* a value. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting the blank line** between HTTP headers and body — the parser will reject the request. (–0.5 mark)
> 3. **Wrong status-code class** — answering `404` for a server crash, instead of `500`. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Quoting keys with single quotes** in JSON — JSON *requires* double quotes. (–0.5 mark)
> 5. **Omitting `Content-Length`** — required for `POST` requests over `Content-Length`-strict servers. (–0.5 mark)
> 6. **Confusing authentication types** — Bearer Token is *not* Basic Auth. (–1 mark)
> 7. **Missing units in JSON payload** — KTU examiners reward schemas like `"temp_c": 28.4`. (–0.5 mark)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for "Connecting to APIs" (Module 3):**

- ☐ **REST** = architectural style on top of **HTTP**, **stateless**, **resource-based**.
- ☐ **HTTP methods** for IoT: `GET` (read), `POST` (create), `PUT` (replace), `DELETE` (remove), `PATCH` (partial update).
- ☐ **Idempotent verbs** = `GET, PUT, DELETE`; **non-idempotent** = `POST, PATCH`.
- ☐ **Status codes** — remember **2xx = success**, **4xx = client error**, **5xx = server error**.
- ☐ **JSON** is preferred over XML in IoT — **~40–60 % smaller**, **natively typed**, **easy to parse on MCUs**.
- ☐ A complete HTTP request = **Request Line** + **Headers** + **Blank line** + **Body**.
- ☐ `Content-Length` = exact byte count of the JSON body.
- ☐ **Authentication ladder** (weakest → strongest): API Key → Basic Auth → Bearer Token / JWT → OAuth 2.0 → mTLS.
- ☐ **Always prefer HTTPS (port 443)** — never send API keys over plain HTTP.
- ☐ **Rate limits** must be respected: $T_{\text{safe}} = T_{\text{window}} / N_{\max}$ plus a **20 % safety margin**.
- ☐ **JSON schema discipline** — always include `device_id`, `timestamp`, units, and a status flag.
- ☐ For **real-time bidirectional** IoT, prefer **MQTT (port 8883)**; for **intermittent telemetry**, prefer **HTTPS POST**.
- ☐ **Headers can be ~60 % of total bytes** in small-payload IoT traffic — batch your data.
- ☐ Cloud platforms tested in KTU: **ThingSpeak, Blynk, Firebase, AWS IoT Core, OpenWeatherMap**.
- ☐ **Webhooks** = "reverse APIs" — the cloud calls *your* endpoint when an event occurs (e.g., IFTTT).

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Connecting to APIs**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Baking Pi: Powering Raspberry Pi
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT504 - PROGRAMMING FOR INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Baking Pi: Powering Raspberry Pi

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **"Baking Pi: Powering Raspberry Pi"** refers to the systematic hardware initialization, power budgeting, operating system provisioning, and first-boot configuration of a Raspberry Pi single-board computer (SBC) in preparation for IoT application development. The term *"Baking Pi"* is borrowed from the eponymous ARM Assembly tutorial series (Cambridge) and metaphorically represents "booting up" or "bringing the Pi to life" by establishing a stable power rail, writing a bootable image to non-volatile storage, and configuring the on-board firmware/bootloader (VideoCore GPU + BCM2835/2836/2711/2712 SoC).

Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBCIT504 – Programming for IoT)**, this topic falls under *Module 3: Raspberry Pi Architecture and Programming*, and is considered a foundational **Cognitive Level: Understand (L2) / Apply (L3)** learning outcome mapped to **CO3**: *"Develop IoT applications using Raspberry Pi by configuring hardware interfaces, GPIO, and embedded programming tools."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — "Lighting a Pilot Flame on an Industrial Boiler"**
>
> Imagine you have just received a brand-new industrial boiler. Before you can run the steam engine inside, you must:
> 1. Connect it to a **regulated gas/electric supply** (the power rail),
> 2. Light the **pilot flame** safely (the firmware boot),
> 3. Pour in **fuel and water** (the OS image on the SD card),
> 4. Verify the **gauges and safety valves** (peripherals, network, SSH).
>
> *"Baking Pi"* is exactly this ritual for the Raspberry Pi. The **"baking"** metaphor emphasizes that the Pi is *not* a plug-and-play appliance like a laptop — it is a **bare circuit board** that needs careful, deliberate preparation before it becomes useful.

### 1.3 The Raspberry Pi Power Equation — A First Look

The single most important electrical relationship governing "Baking Pi" is Ohm's Power Law:

$$P = V \times I$$

where **P** is power in **Watts (W)**, **V** is voltage in **Volts (V)**, and **I** is current in **Amperes (A)**.
