For a Raspberry Pi 4 Model B, the official specification mandates a **5.1 V** supply capable of delivering at least **3.0 A** through a **USB-C** connector, giving a theoretical maximum power budget of approximately **15.3 W**.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard KTU Metric to Memorize:**
> - Raspberry Pi Zero / Zero W → **5.0 V, 1.2 A, micro-USB**
> - Raspberry Pi 3 Model B+ → **5.1 V, 2.5 A, micro-USB**
> - Raspberry Pi 4 Model B → **5.1 V, 3.0 A, USB-C**
> - Raspberry Pi 5 → **5.0 V, 5.0 A, USB-C (PD compliant)**

### 1.4 Why Powering Correctly Matters in IoT

A Raspberry Pi deployed as an IoT edge node is often expected to run **24 × 7** from remote locations — greenhouses, traffic junctions, industrial PLC cabinets, weather stations. An unstable power rail produces the infamous **"lightning bolt" under-voltage icon** 🌩️, causes SD card corruption, watchdog reboots, and silent GPIO misreads. Hence, *powering* the Pi correctly is not a peripheral concern — it is the **first non-negotiable engineering step** in any KTU-evaluated IoT project.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of the Power-Up Sequence

When you press the **ON button** (Pi 4/5) or apply power (older models), the following deterministic chain of events occurs:

1. **Voltage Regulation Stage** — The external 5.1 V enters through the USB-C / micro-USB port and is fed to the PMIC (Power Management IC), typically a **RT9742 / RT9743 / DA9090** (Pi 5) hot-swap controller.
2. **Polyfuse F1 Trip Check** — A 1.1 A (Pi 3) or 2.5 A (Pi 4) resettable polyfuse protects against sustained overcurrent.
3. **PMIC Output Rails** — Multiple buck regulators generate the SoC core voltage (typically **0.8 V – 1.2 V**), DRAM voltage (**1.2 V / 1.35 V** for LPDDR2/4), and peripheral rails (**3.3 V for GPIO, 1.8 V for HDMI**).
4. **GPU/CPU Boot Hand-off** — The **VideoCore GPU** powers up first, reads the boot ROM (one-time programmable mask ROM on the SoC), and begins executing stage 1 of the bootloader (`bootcode.bin` from SD card in legacy mode, or directly from OTP in newer models).
5. **Firmware Loading** — `start.elf` and `fixup.dat` initialize RAM, GPU, and clocks. The device tree blob (`.dtb`) describes connected hardware to the kernel.
6. **Kernel Handoff** — The Linux kernel (`kernel8.img` for 64-bit Pi 4) is loaded into RAM, the root filesystem is mounted, and systemd begins service initialization.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **only reference you need** during the KTU ESE for power and OS-image calculations. Commit it to memory.

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula / Standard Value** | **Unit** | **Boundary / Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Electrical Power | $P$ | $P = V \times I$ | Watts (W) | Steady-state budget calculation |
| Energy Consumption | $E$ | $E = P \times t$ | Watt-hours (Wh) | Battery sizing for remote Pi |
| Image Size on SD | $S_{img}$ | $S_{img} \approx S_{os} + S_{swap} + S_{data}$ | Gigabytes (GB) | Always over-provision by **10 %** |
| SD Card Required Speed | $C_{sd}$ | $C_{sd} \geq 10 \text{ MB/s}$ (Class 10 / A1) | MB/s | Recommended: **A2 rated** |
| Power Efficiency | $\eta$ | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{load}}{P_{input}} \times 100$ | Percent (%) | Typical Pi 4 ≈ **85 % – 90 %** |
| Battery Backup Time | $t_{backup}$ | $t_{backup} = \dfrac{E_{battery} \times \eta}{P_{load}}$ | Hours (h) | $E_{battery}$ in Wh |
| Under-voltage Threshold | $V_{uvlo}$ | $V_{uvlo} \le 4.63 \text{ V}$ | Volts (V) | Triggers 🌩️ icon + throttle |
| Boot Time (Cold) | $T_{boot}$ | $T_{boot} \approx 20$ – $45$ | Seconds (s) | Lower with SSD boot |
| Safe Shutdown Time | $T_{sd}$ | $T_{sd} \approx 5$ | Seconds (s) | Use `sudo shutdown -h now` |
| Recommended PSU Current | $I_{psu}$ | $I_{psu} \geq I_{pi} + I_{usb}$ | Amperes (A) | USB peripherals add **0.5 A** each |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Critical Pitfall:** In markdown tables, the vertical bar `|` is a structural delimiter. KTU students have historically lost marks when their answer sheets contain broken tables. Inside any formula, use `\vert` (e.g., $V \vert_{min}$) instead of `\vert` typed directly, or wrap the formula in a code-block if necessary. The table above is the **gold standard format** for KTU valuation.

### 2.3 Powering Methodologies — Engineering Trade-offs

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to know **at least three** power-delivery methods. Each carries a distinct engineering trade-off:

| **Method** | **Voltage / Current** | **Use-Case** | **Pros** | **Cons** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Official PSU (Wall Adapter) | **5.1 V / 3.0 A** | Lab bench, fixed installations | Stable, regulated, recommended | Not portable |
| Micro-USB / USB-C from PC | **5.0 V / 0.5 A** (USB 2.0) | Quick code-flash, low load | Ubiquitous, cheap | **Insufficient** under load → under-voltage |
| Power Bank (Li-Po) | **5.0 V / 2.0 – 3.0 A** | Mobile demos, field IoT | Portable, on-the-go | Auto-shutoff if current < threshold |
| PoE (Power over Ethernet) | **48 V → 5 V** (PoE HAT) | Wall-mounted / ceiling nodes | Single-cable deployment | Requires **PoE HAT** (Pi 3B+/4) |
| Solar + Battery + BMS | **12 V → 5 V (buck)** | Remote weather stations | Off-grid, long-term | Needs MPPT controller, weatherproofing |
| Bench Power Supply (Lab) | Adjustable, current-limited | R&D, current profiling | Precise, has current limit knob | Bulky, mains-bound |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility in Engineering:** In any **KTU B.Tech Final-Year IoT Project**, your external examiner will *always* ask: *"What is your powering strategy? Have you measured inrush current?"* The expected answer involves: **(a)** a regulated SMPS, **(b)** a fuse or polyfuse, **(c)** a buck converter with at least **20 %** current headroom, and **(d)** measurement with a USB inline meter.

### 2.4 The OS-Image Provisioning Workflow

"Baking Pi" also includes **installing the operating system**. The canonical 2024 workflow is:

1. **Acquire the OS Image** — `Raspberry Pi OS Lite` (32-bit, headless, ~430 MB) or `Raspberry Pi OS with Desktop` (64-bit, ~1.1 GB). Download from `https://www.raspberrypi.com/software/operating-systems/`.
2. **Flash to SD Card** — Use **Raspberry Pi Imager** (the official cross-platform tool) or `balenaEtcher`. Avoid Win32DiskImager for new projects.
3. **Pre-Configure (Headless Setup)** — Before ejecting the SD card, mount the `boot` partition and create:
   - An empty file named `ssh` (enables SSH on first boot)
   - A `wpa_supplicant.conf` file (configures Wi-Fi)
   - A `userconf.txt` file (sets username + password hash) for newer Pi OS releases.
4. **First Boot** — Insert SD card, apply power, wait **30 – 90 seconds** for the green LED to settle.
5. **Locate the Pi on Network** — Use `ping raspberrypi.local` (mDNS) or scan with `nmap -p 22 192.168.1.0/24`.
6. **SSH In & Update** — `sudo apt update && sudo apt full-upgrade -y`.

### 2.5 Why "Baking Pi" is a High-Yield KTU Topic

The phrase is a **gateway concept**: it tests whether the student can connect *electrical engineering* (power calculations, voltage rails) with *computer science* (bootloaders, filesystems, SSH) with *systems thinking* (reliability, monitoring). A 14-mark KTU question on this topic will typically demand a numeric power-budget calculation **plus** a diagram of the boot process.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Power Budget for a Pi-Based IoT Node

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU-style 7-mark):**
> A Raspberry Pi 4 is deployed in a smart-agriculture IoT node. The Pi is connected to: a 7-inch touchscreen (300 mA), a USB LoRa modem (250 mA), and a USB SSD enclosure (500 mA). Compute: (a) the total worst-case current draw, (b) the minimum PSU rating required, and (c) the energy consumed per day in Wh.

#### Step 1 — Identify the Base Pi Current

The Raspberry Pi 4B official specification states a maximum board current of **1.2 A** at full CPU + GPU + Ethernet load, before peripherals.

$$I_{pi} = 1.2 \text{ A}$$

#### Step 2 — Sum Peripheral Currents

$$I_{peri} = I_{screen} + I_{lora} + I_{ssd}$$

$$I_{peri} = 0.300 + 0.250 + 0.500$$

$$I_{peri} = 1.050 \text{ A}$$

#### Step 3 — Compute Total Worst-Case Current

$$I_{total} = I_{pi} + I_{peri}$$

$$I_{total} = 1.200 + 1.050$$

$$I_{total} = 2.250 \text{ A}$$

#### Step 4 — Apply the 20 % Engineering Headroom Rule

A **best engineering practice** (and a KTU favourite!) is to never operate a PSU at > 80 % of its rated capacity. Therefore:

$$I_{psu} \geq \dfrac{I_{total}}{0.80}$$

$$I_{psu} \geq \dfrac{2.250}{0.80}$$

$$I_{psu} \geq 2.8125 \text{ A}$$

#### Step 5 — Round Up to the Nearest Standard PSU Rating

The next-standard PSU above 2.8125 A is the official **3.0 A PSU**.

$$\boxed{I_{psu,chosen} = 3.0 \text{ A at } 5.1 \text{ V}}$$

#### Step 6 — Compute the Power

$$P_{system} = V \times I_{total}$$

$$P_{system} = 5.1 \times 2.250$$

$$P_{system} = 11.475 \text{ W}$$

#### Step 7 — Compute Daily Energy Consumption

$$E_{daily} = P_{system} \times t_{day}$$

$$E_{daily} = 11.475 \text{ W} \times 24 \text{ h}$$

$$\boxed{E_{daily} = 275.4 \text{ Wh/day}}$$

#### Step 8 — Compute Monthly Energy and Cost (KTU extension)

Assuming an Indian industrial tariff of **₹ 7.50 / kWh**:

$$E_{monthly} = 275.4 \times 30 = 8262 \text{ Wh} = 8.262 \text{ kWh}$$

$$\text{Cost} = 8.262 \times 7.50 = \text{₹ } 61.965 \approx \text{₹ } 62$$

This is the *operational expense* number KTU evaluators love to see in the conclusion of an IoT deployment answer.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Battery Sizing for an Off-Grid Pi Node

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU-style 7-mark):**
> Design a battery pack using a **12 V, 7 Ah sealed lead-acid (SLA)** battery to power a Pi 4B consuming **11.475 W** through a buck converter with efficiency **η = 88 %**. How many hours of continuous operation are possible before the battery reaches its **50 % depth-of-discharge (DoD)** safety threshold?

#### Step 1 — Compute Usable Battery Energy

A SLA battery must not be discharged below 50 % to preserve cycle life.

$$E_{batt,usable} = V_{batt} \times C_{batt} \times \text{DoD}$$

$$E_{batt,usable} = 12 \text{ V} \times 7 \text{ Ah} \times 0.50$$

$$E_{batt,usable} = 42.0 \text{ Wh}$$

#### Step 2 — Compute Effective Energy at the Load

The buck converter efficiency is 88 %, meaning the load receives only 88 % of the energy drawn from the battery:

$$E_{load} = E_{batt,usable} \times \eta$$

$$E_{load} = 42.0 \times 0.88$$

$$E_{load} = 36.96 \text{ Wh}$$

#### Step 3 — Compute Backup Time

$$t_{backup} = \dfrac{E_{load}}{P_{system}}$$

$$t_{backup} = \dfrac{36.96}{11.475}$$

$$\boxed{t_{backup} \approx 3.22 \text{ hours}}$$

#### Interpretation

A **12 V 7 Ah SLA** can keep the IoT node alive for just over **3 hours**. To extend this to **24 hours**, the required battery capacity is:

$$C_{req} = \dfrac{P_{system} \times 24}{V_{batt} \times \text{DoD} \times \eta}$$

$$C_{req} = \dfrac{11.475 \times 24}{12 \times 0.50 \times 0.88}$$

$$C_{req} = \dfrac{275.4}{5.28} \approx 52.16 \text{ Ah}$$

This means a **12 V 50 Ah deep-cycle battery** would be required. This kind of sizing exercise is a **favourite 14-mark KTU Part-B question**.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — SD Card Capacity Planning

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU-style):**
> You are provisioning a Raspberry Pi 4 for a kiosk application. Requirements: Raspberry Pi OS Lite 64-bit (**1.2 GB**), swap partition (**1.0 GB**), data logging area (**8.0 GB**), with **15 %** over-provisioning reserved for logs and journal. What is the minimum SD card capacity you should purchase?

#### Step 1 — Sum Base Requirements

$$S_{base} = 1.2 + 1.0 + 8.0 = 10.2 \text{ GB}$$

#### Step 2 — Add Over-Provisioning

$$S_{total} = S_{base} \times 1.15$$

$$S_{total} = 10.2 \times 1.15 = 11.73 \text{ GB}$$

#### Step 3 — Round Up to the Next Standard SD Card Size

Standard sizes: 16 GB, 32 GB, 64 GB, 128 GB, 256 GB, 512 GB.

$$\boxed{S_{sd,chosen} = 16 \text{ GB (A2 rated)}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick:** Students often pick **32 GB** here, which is *not wrong* but is *not optimal*. The correct engineering answer is the **smallest standard size ≥ requirement** that satisfies the speed class. Always write the *next standard size* and justify it.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Powering Errors

| **Error** | **Symptom** | **Root Cause** | **Fix** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Using a 5 V / 1 A phone charger | Lightning bolt, random reboots | Insufficient current headroom | Upgrade to official **3.0 A** PSU |
| Long / thin micro-USB cable | Voltage drop > 0.3 V | $V_{drop} = I \times R_{cable}$ | Use **20 AWG** or shorter cable |
| No `shutdown` before unplugging | SD card corruption, lost data | Filesystem journaling interrupted | `sudo shutdown -h now` always |
| Pulling power during `apt upgrade` | Bricked boot partition | Interrupted bootloader write | Use UPS / allow completion |
| Powered USB hub on Pi USB-A | Peripheral not detected | Pi 4 limits USB output to **1.2 A** shared | Use externally-powered hub |
| Battery auto-shutoff on power bank | Pi restarts in 30 s | Power bank turns off when I < 100 mA | Add a dummy load (e.g., USB LED) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Power Rail

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AC Mains 230V] --> B[SMPS Wall Adapter]
    B --> C["USB-C Connector 5.1V"]
    C --> D[Polyfuse F1 Protection]
    D --> E[PMIC - Power Mgmt IC]
    E --> F["Buck 3.3V GPIO Rail"]
    E --> G["Buck 1.8V HDMI Rail"]
    E --> H["Buck 0.85V SoC Core"]
    E --> I["Buck 1.2V LPDDR4"]
    J[USB Peripherals] --> K[USB Power Switch 1.2A Limit]
    K --> E
    L[Ethernet PoE HAT Optional] --> M[PoE to 5V Buck]
    M --> E
    E --> N[BCM2711 SoC + GPU + CPU]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Power flows top-down from the AC mains, through an isolated SMPS adapter (which converts 230 V AC to 5.1 V DC), through a resettable polyfuse for safety, into the PMIC which generates all the lower-voltage rails needed by the SoC, memory, and peripherals. The PoE path is an alternative input for installations where a single Ethernet cable is preferred.

### 4.2 Boot Sequence — State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PowerOn
    PowerOn --> PolyfuseCheck : Current OK
    PolyfuseCheck --> PMICInit : Voltage Stable
    PMICInit --> GPUROM : SoC Reset Released
    GPUROM --> Stage1Bootcode : Reads bootcode.bin
    Stage1Bootcode --> Stage2Firmware : start.elf loaded
    Stage2Firmware --> KernelLoad : kernel8.img into RAM
    KernelLoad --> RootMount : /dev/mmcblk0p2 mounted
    RootMount --> SystemdInit : systemd PID 1
    SystemdInit --> LoginPrompt : getty on tty1
    LoginPrompt --> [*]
    SystemdInit --> LowVoltageFlag : Under 4.63V
    LowVoltageFlag --> ThrottleCPU : GPU downclock
    ThrottleCPU --> SystemdInit
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each node represents a well-defined state. Arrows are labelled with the event causing the transition. The feedback loop from `LowVoltageFlag` → `ThrottleCPU` represents the Pi's protective under-voltage behaviour — a critical concept for KTU power-related questions.

### 4.3 Powering Methods Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start{Need portable or fixed?}
    Start -->|Fixed indoor| Fixed[Official USB-C 5.1V 3A PSU]
    Start -->|Fixed outdoor, single cable| PoE[PoE HAT via Ethernet]
    Start -->|Portable, short demo| PB[10,000 mAh Power Bank]
    Start -->|Remote, off-grid| Solar[Solar Panel + MPPT + SLA Battery]
    Fixed --> Verify1{Voltage drops below 4.63V?}
    PoE --> Verify2{Pi under voltage?}
    PB --> Verify3{Power bank auto off?}
    Solar --> Verify4{Cloudy days > 3?}
    Verify1 -->|Yes| Fix1[Upgrade to thicker 20 AWG cable]
    Verify2 -->|Yes| Fix2[Use PoE+ 802.3at not 802.3af]
    Verify3 -->|Yes| Fix3[Add dummy 100 mA USB load]
    Verify4 -->|Yes| Fix4[Oversize battery 2x]
    Fix1 --> Done[System Reliable]
    Fix2 --> Done
    Fix3 --> Done
    Fix4 --> Done
```

**Reading the diagram:** A practical decision-support flowchart that an examiner can use to test a student's *practical engineering judgement*. The four terminal nodes (`Fix1` – `Fix4`) are typical 3-mark sub-question answer anchors.

### 4.4 Component-Level Interconnection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SBC[Raspberry Pi 4 Single Board Computer]
        CPU[BCM2711 Quad Core Cortex A72]
        GPU[VideoCore VI GPU]
        MEM[LPDDR4 4GB RAM]
        GPIO[40 pin GPIO Header]
        ETH[Gigabit Ethernet]
        USB[2x USB 3.0 + 2x USB 2.0]
        HDMI[2x micro HDMI]
        CSI[CSI Camera Port]
        DSI[DSI Display Port]
    end
    subgraph Storage[Non Volatile Storage]
        SD[Micro SD Card A2]
        SSD[USB 3.0 SSD Optional]
    end
    subgraph Power[Power Subsystem]
        PSU[Official PSU 5.1V 3A]
        PMIC[PMIC RT9743]
    end
    PSU --> PMIC
    PMIC --> CPU
    PMIC --> MEM
    SD --> CPU
    SSD --> USB
    CPU --- GPU
    CPU --- GPIO
    CPU --- ETH
    CPU --- HDMI
    CPU --- CSI
    CPU --- DSI
```

**Reading the diagram:** A clean component map. Note the **PMIC** sits between the **PSU** and **CPU/MEM** — it is the heart of the power system. The CPU is a *SoC* that integrates the GPU, so they share a single block in this abstraction.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Define "Baking Pi" in the context of IoT edge-node deployment. List any four preparatory steps involved.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
"Baking Pi" refers to the complete hardware and software initialization procedure required to bring a Raspberry Pi single-board computer from an unpowered, unconfigured state to a fully functional, network-attached, ready-to-program IoT edge node. It metaphorically represents "booting up" or "bringing the Pi to life."

The four preparatory steps are:
1. **Stable Power Provisioning** — Connecting a regulated 5.1 V / 3.0 A USB-C PSU with adequate current headroom.
2. **OS Image Flashing** — Writing the Raspberry Pi OS image onto a Class-10 / A2-rated micro-SD card using Raspberry Pi Imager or balenaEtcher.
3. **Headless Pre-Configuration** — Creating the `ssh` and `wpa_supplicant.conf` files on the boot partition to enable remote login and Wi-Fi at first boot.
4. **Network Discovery & Update** — Locating the Pi on the LAN via mDNS (`raspberrypi.local`) and running `sudo apt update && sudo apt full-upgrade -y` to bring firmware and packages up to date.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **What is the under-voltage threshold for a Raspberry Pi 4? What visible symptom does it produce and what is the corrective action?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The under-voltage threshold for a Raspberry Pi 4 is **4.63 V ± 5 %** at the input of the board. When the supply voltage drops below this threshold, the SoC firmware displays a **lightning-bolt icon (🌩️)** in the top-right corner of the desktop (or logs a `Under-voltage detected` message via `vcgencmd get_throttled`). The CPU and GPU are also throttled down to reduce current draw, which degrades performance.

**Corrective action:** Replace the power supply with the official **5.1 V / 3.0 A USB-C adapter**, use a short and thick (≥ 20 AWG) USB cable, and remove any peripherals that draw excessive current from the Pi's USB ports. For permanent installations, deploy a **PoE HAT** or a **regulated buck converter** fed from a higher-capacity supply.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Students frequently lose marks on "Baking Pi" questions for three reasons:
> 1. **Forgetting the engineering headroom** (20 % rule) when computing PSU ratings — examiner deducts **1 mark**.
> 2. **Omitting the shutdown procedure** when discussing reliability — examiner deducts **1 mark**.
> 3. **Drawing the boot diagram without labelling the transitions** — examiner deducts **up to 2 marks**.
> Always remember: *power → OS → network → reliability* is the KTU-mandated four-pillar answer structure.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Power Budgeting & Boot Process*

> **Q3a. [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]**
> **(7 marks)** A Raspberry Pi 4-based IoT gateway must run 24 × 7 from a solar-charged 12 V battery. The system connects to: an LTE USB modem (400 mA), a USB SSD (500 mA), and a 5-inch touchscreen (350 mA). Compute the total worst-case current, recommend a properly sized PSU (with 20 % headroom), and the minimum 12 V battery capacity (Ah) needed for **48 hours** of autonomy at 50 % DoD and 88 % converter efficiency.

> **Q3b. (7 marks)** With a neat state-transition diagram, describe the boot sequence of a Raspberry Pi from power-on to login prompt. Mention the role of `bootcode.bin`, `start.elf`, the device tree blob (`.dtb`), and the Linux kernel image.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q3a (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Identify Pi base current (0.5 mark):**
From the official Raspberry Pi 4B datasheet, worst-case board current (CPU + GPU + Ethernet) is:

$$I_{pi} = 1.2 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 — Sum peripheral currents (1 mark):**

$$I_{peri} = 0.400 + 0.500 + 0.350 = 1.250 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 — Total worst-case current (1 mark):**

$$I_{total} = 1.200 + 1.250 = 2.450 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4 — Apply 20 % headroom for PSU (1 mark):**

$$I_{psu} \geq \dfrac{2.450}{0.80} = 3.0625 \text{ A}$$

**Step 5 — Choose the next-standard PSU (0.5 mark):**

$$I_{psu,chosen} = 3.5 \text{ A at } 5.1 \text{ V} \quad (\text{or} \ 4.0 \text{ A for added safety})$$

**Step 6 — Compute system power (0.5 mark):**

$$P_{sys} = 5.1 \times 2.450 = 12.495 \text{ W}$$

**Step 7 — Compute required battery capacity for 48 h (2.5 marks):**

$$C_{req} = \dfrac{P_{sys} \times t}{V_{batt} \times \text{DoD} \times \eta}$$

$$C_{req} = \dfrac{12.495 \times 48}{12 \times 0.50 \times 0.88}$$

$$C_{req} = \dfrac{599.76}{5.28} \approx 113.6 \text{ Ah}$$

**Final Answer:** Use a **12 V 120 Ah deep-cycle lead-acid battery** (next-standard size) with the official 3.5 A USB-C PSU. *(Examiner's note: full marks only if headroom, DoD, and efficiency are all explicitly stated.)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Q3b (7 marks)**

**Boot Sequence (state diagram as in Section 4.2 — 4 marks):**

1. **Power-On (0.5 mark):** 5.1 V enters the PMIC. PMIC asserts the SoC's reset line.
2. **`bootcode.bin` (1 mark):** The VideoCore GPU's mask ROM loads this first-stage bootloader from the SD card's FAT32 boot partition into the SoC's 128 KB SRAM. It is responsible for SDRAM initialisation.
3. **`start.elf` and `fixup.dat` (1 mark):** The second-stage firmware is loaded next. It initialises the GPU, sets up clocks, and reads the device tree blob (`.dtb`) which describes the hardware peripherals to the OS.
4. **Linux Kernel (`kernel8.img`) (1 mark):** The 64-bit ARM kernel is loaded into RAM and executed. It mounts the root filesystem (typically `ext4` on `/dev/mmcblk0p2`).
5. **systemd → Login Prompt (1 mark):** systemd (PID 1) starts system services, brings up networking, and spawns a getty on `tty1` which displays the login prompt.

> **Closing Statement (0.5 mark):** A clean shutdown must be performed using `sudo shutdown -h now` before disconnecting power to prevent SD-card journal corruption — a critical reliability practice for any 24 × 7 IoT deployment.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — *OS Flashing & Powering Methodologies*

> **Q4a. (7 marks)** Compare **Raspberry Pi Imager**, **balenaEtcher**, and the legacy **NOOBS** installer. Which is the official KTU-recommended tool in 2024, and why? Outline the steps to perform a **headless** first-boot configuration with Wi-Fi and SSH enabled.

> **Q4b. (7 marks)** Discuss **at least four** methods of powering a Raspberry Pi in IoT deployments. For each method, state the typical voltage, current, one advantage, and one limitation.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q4a (7 marks)**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| **Feature** | **Raspberry Pi Imager** | **balenaEtcher** | **NOOBS (legacy)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Official? | **Yes** (Raspberry Pi Foundation) | No (third-party) | Yes (deprecated) |
| OS catalogue | Built-in, customisation options | Manual image download | Built-in |
| Pre-config (SSH, Wi-Fi, hostname) | **Yes (advanced menu)** | No | Yes |
| Auto-eject | Yes | Yes | No |
| Cross-platform | Win / macOS / Linux | Win / macOS / Linux | Win / macOS / Linux |
| **2024 recommendation** | **⭐ PREFERRED** | Good alternative | ❌ Discontinued |

**Headless First-Boot Steps (3 marks):**
1. Open **Raspberry Pi Imager** → choose OS (e.g., Raspberry Pi OS Lite 64-bit) → choose storage (SD card) → click the **gear icon** (or `Ctrl+Shift+X`) to open **Advanced Options**.
2. In Advanced Options: tick **Enable SSH** → set a password → tick **Configure Wi-Fi** → enter SSID and password → set **hostname** (e.g., `iot-node-01`) → enable **locale settings** → click **Save**.
3. Click **Write**. After flashing completes, insert the SD card into the Pi and power it on. The Pi will boot, connect to Wi-Fi, and enable SSH automatically.
4. From your laptop, run `ssh iotuser@iot-node-01.local` (or use the IP from your router) and complete the welcome wizard.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q4b (7 marks)**

| **#** | **Method** | **V / I** | **Advantage** | **Limitation** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Official Wall PSU | **5.1 V / 3.0 A** | Stable, surge-protected, KTU-recommended | Not portable |
| 2 | Power Bank (Li-Po) | **5.0 V / 2.0 – 3.0 A** | Portable, 8 – 20 h runtime | Auto-shuts-off at low draw |
| 3 | PoE HAT | **48 V → 5 V** | Single-cable install (data + power) | HAT cost, extra heat |
| 4 | Solar + SLA + MPPT | **12 V → 5 V buck** | Off-grid, scalable | Cloudy days, MPPT tuning needed |

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** The choice of powering method is dictated by *deployment environment* (indoor/outdoor), *autonomy requirement* (hours/days/weeks), and *reliability budget* (cost of downtime). For KTU final-year projects, examiners expect a *justified* selection with a power-budget table.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — "Baking Pi: Powering Raspberry Pi"**
>
> ☐ **"Baking Pi"** = powering + OS-flashing + first-boot + network setup of the Raspberry Pi.
> ☐ Official Pi 4 PSU = **5.1 V / 3.0 A USB-C** (not 5.0 V — the 0.1 V headroom matters).
> ☐ Under-voltage threshold = **4.63 V** → 🌩️ icon + CPU throttling.
> ☐ Always apply the **20 % headroom rule**: $I_{psu} \geq I_{total} / 0.80$.
> ☐ Power equation: $P = V \times I$; Energy: $E = P \times t$ in Wh.
> ☐ Battery sizing: $C_{req} = (P_{sys} \times t) / (V_{batt} \times \text{DoD} \times \eta)$.
> ☐ Official 2024 flashing tool = **Raspberry Pi Imager** (with `Ctrl+Shift+X` advanced options).
> ☐ Headless setup needs three pre-boot files: `ssh` (empty), `wpa_supplicant.conf`, `userconf.txt`.
> ☐ Boot sequence: PMIC → GPU ROM → `bootcode.bin` → `start.elf` + `.dtb` → `kernel8.img` → systemd → getty.
> ☐ Always `sudo shutdown -h now` before unplugging power to prevent SD-card corruption.
> ☐ PoE HAT delivers **48 V → 5 V** through a single Ethernet cable (Pi 3B+/4 only).
> ☐ Use **Class 10 / A2-rated** SD cards for reliable boot and fast I/O.
> ☐ Wi-Fi can be pre-configured by editing `wpa_supplicant.conf` on the `boot` partition.
> ☐ Examiner tip: always end your answer with a **power-budget table** and a **shut-down reliability statement**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Mantra for KTU ESE 2024:**
> *"Power the Pi safely, flash the OS correctly, configure the network headlessly, and never yank the cable — that is Baking Pi."* 🍰

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Formatting SD Cards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT504 - PROGRAMMING FOR INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Formatting SD Cards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**SD Card Formatting** in the context of the Internet of Things (IoT) is the process of preparing a Secure Digital (SD) memory card by establishing (or re-establishing) a valid **file system structure** on the flash storage medium so that an embedded operating system, microcontroller firmware, or data-logging application can reliably **read, write, seek, and persist** data blocks across the non-volatile NAND flash cells.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *PBCIT504 – Programming for Internet of Things* (Module 3), formatting an SD card is a foundational preparatory step before deploying the card as a boot medium (e.g., for a Raspberry Pi) or as a **data-logging sink** for sensor nodes (e.g., ESP32, Arduino, NodeMCU with SD shield).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PBCIT504, Module 3)**
> Formatting an SD card is the first step before mounting it on a Raspberry Pi, a microcontroller-based sensor node, or any edge device that requires persistent storage. The student must understand the **file-system types (FAT16, FAT32, exFAT, ext4)**, the **partition table schemes (MBR vs GPT)**, the role of the **boot sector**, and how to choose the correct format for the target IoT hardware.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a brand-new, blank **notebook** that you just bought from a shop. The pages are completely empty and there are **no headings, no table of contents, and no page numbers**. If you try to "find Chapter 5," the notebook has no idea what you mean — it is just a stack of blank paper.

**Formatting is like creating the table of contents, numbering every page, and dividing the notebook into chapters *before* you start writing.** Once formatted, your notebook knows exactly:

- Where Chapter 1 begins and ends,
- Which pages are reserved for the index,
- How much space (how many pages) each chapter can hold, and
- What to do when a chapter runs out of room (start a new one, link them).

In the same way, an SD card is just a mass of **NAND flash cells** until it is formatted. Formatting installs a **file system** (FAT32, exFAT, or ext4) that gives the card an organized address book, allowing your IoT device to save sensor readings, retrieve configuration files, and boot an operating system without getting lost.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does an IoT engineer care about formatting?**
> Choosing the *wrong* file system can render the card unreadable by the device. For example, a Raspberry Pi 4 will not boot from a card formatted as **NTFS** (Windows default), and an Arduino SD library will not recognize **ext4** (Linux native). Picking the correct format is therefore a **prerequisite engineering decision**, not just a housekeeping chore.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Constants

The following technical parameters are used throughout this topic and must be memorized for KTU examinations:

- **Logical Block Size (Sector Size):** typically **512 bytes** (legacy standard, mandated by SD specification v1.x–v6.x for SDSC/SDHC).
- **Default Cluster / Allocation Unit Size:** depends on card capacity (e.g., **4 KiB** for cards between 8 GB and 16 GB in FAT32).
- **Master Boot Record (MBR) Size:** exactly **512 bytes**, located at sector 0.
- **Volume Boot Record (VBR):** located at the start of the partition.
- **File Allocation Table (FAT) entry width:** 12 bits (FAT12), **16 bits (FAT16)**, or **32 bits (FAT32)**.
- **Maximum file size in FAT32:** **4 GiB − 1 byte** ($2^{32} - 1$ bytes).
- **Maximum partition size in FAT32:** **2 TiB** (theoretical), although many tools cap it at **32 GiB** for compatibility.
- **SD Card Capacity Classes (per SD spec):** **SDSC** (≤ 2 GB), **SDHC** (4–32 GB), **SDXC** (64 GB – 2 TB), **SDUC** (2 TB – 128 TB).
- **Wear-Leveling Endurance:** typical consumer SD cards rated for **10,000 to 100,000 write/erase cycles per MLC/TLC block**.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU viva voce: If the examiner asks *"Why does Windows refuse to format a 64 GB card as FAT32?"*, the answer is Microsoft's artificial limit, **not** a technical limit. Use the official **SD Association's SD Card Formatter** to bypass it.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of an SD Card

An SD card is, internally, a small printed circuit board carrying:

1. **NAND Flash memory die(s)** – the actual storage, organized in **blocks** and **pages**.
2. **A controller chip** – performs **wear leveling**, **bad-block management**, **ECC (Error-Correcting Code)**, and translates logical block addresses (LBAs) to physical pages.
3. **An interface** – either **SPI mode** (used by most microcontrollers) or the **SD bus mode** (1-bit or 4-bit) used by Raspberry Pi's BCM2711 SoC.

When we "format" the card from the host's perspective, we are **not** touching the NAND cells directly. We are writing a few hundred kilobytes of **metadata structures** at the beginning of the visible logical address space.

## 2.2 The Three Logical Regions Created by Formatting

When an SD card is formatted, the OS/tool writes three major regions:

1. **Partition Table Region**
   - Sits in the first sector (LBA 0).
   - Two schemes exist: **MBR (Master Boot Record)** and **GPT (GUID Partition Table)**.
   - For SD cards ≤ 2 TiB, **MBR is standard**. MBR describes up to **4 primary partitions**, with the active/bootable flag stored in a single byte (offset 0x1BE).

2. **Boot Sector Region**
   - For FAT-family file systems, the first sector of the partition contains the **Volume Boot Record (VBR)**.
   - VBR holds the **BPB (BIOS Parameter Block)**, which describes the geometry: bytes per sector, sectors per cluster, reserved sectors, number of FATs, root directory size (FAT16/32 only), total sectors, and media descriptor.

3. **Data Region (Clusters)**
   - The remainder of the card is carved into **clusters** (also called *allocation units*).
   - Each cluster is a fixed number of consecutive sectors (commonly 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, or 64 sectors).
   - The **FAT** (a linked-list / bitmap hybrid) tracks which clusters belong to which files and which are free.

## 2.3 File System Comparison – Which One to Pick?

| File System | Max File Size | Max Volume Size | Bootable by RPi | Supported by Arduino SD.h | Journaling | OS Native To |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| FAT16 | 2 GiB | 2 GiB | Yes (old Pi) | Yes | No | DOS / Windows |
| FAT32 | 4 GiB − 1 B | 2 TiB (theoretical) | **Yes (RPi 3/4/Zero)** | **Yes (most common)** | No | Windows 95 OSR2+ |
| exFAT | 16 EiB | 128 PiB | Yes (RPi 4 UEFI) | Limited (SdFat library) | No | Windows / Android |
| ext4 | 16 TiB | 1 EiB | **Yes (RPi Linux)** | No (needs ext2 lib) | **Yes (journaled)** | Linux |
| NTFS | 16 TiB | 256 TiB | **No (read-only on Pi)** | No | Yes | Windows NT |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** When asked *"Which file system should you use to format an SD card for a Raspberry Pi IoT project?"*, the textbook-correct answer is **FAT32 for the boot partition** (because the GPU's first-stage bootloader only understands FAT) and **ext4 for the root filesystem** (created automatically by Raspberry Pi Imager or `raspi-config`).

## 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Cluster size (bytes) | $C = S \times N$ | bytes | $S$ = bytes per sector, $N$ = sectors per cluster |
| Total clusters | $K = \dfrac{D}{C}$ | dimensionless | $D$ = usable data region in bytes |
| FAT size in sectors | $F = \dfrac{K \times E}{8 \times S}$ | sectors | $E$ = FAT entry width (16, 32 bits) |
| Usable capacity | $U = K \times C$ | bytes | Always less than nominal capacity due to overhead |
| Overhead fraction | $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{U}{D_\text{nominal}}$ | dimensionless | Typically $0.02$ to $0.05$ for FAT32 |
| Max files in root (FAT32) | $M_\text{root} = 65536$ | files | Fixed by spec (cluster chain for root) |
| Max clusters (FAT32) | $2^{28} = 268\,435\,456$ | clusters | 28 bits used; top 4 bits reserved |
| Max clusters (FAT16) | $2^{16} - 1 \approx 65\,534$ | clusters | Practical limit |
| Wear-leveling lifetime | $L = \dfrac{B \times P}{W}$ | years | $B$ = blocks, $P$ = PE cycles, $W$ = writes/day |

> [!NOTE]
> In the wear-leveling lifetime formula, $B$ is the number of physical blocks in the NAND die, $P$ is the rated program-erase cycles per block (e.g., 10,000 for MLC), and $W$ is the projected number of writes per day from the IoT application. For a temperature sensor logging every 10 seconds, $W \approx 8\,640$ writes/day.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industrial IoT deployments — for example, a **Vibration Monitoring Node** mounted on a motor — the SD card is formatted as **FAT32 with a 32 KiB cluster size** to minimize FAT churn when writing large contiguous log buffers. In contrast, a **Raspberry Pi acting as an MQTT broker gateway** is best formatted with a small **64 MiB FAT32 boot partition** and a large **ext4 root partition**, because Linux benefits from journaling for power-cut resilience.

> [!TIP]
> For the KTU exam, always mention **journaling** as the reason to prefer ext4 for Linux-based IoT gateways: journaling writes metadata changes to a log *before* applying them, so a sudden power loss does not corrupt the root filesystem.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 – Computing Cluster Count and FAT Size

Suppose we have a **16 GB SDHC card** that we want to format as **FAT32** with the following parameters:

- Bytes per sector: $S = 512$ bytes
- Sectors per cluster: $N = 64$ (giving a 32 KiB cluster, common for cards in the 8–16 GB range)
- Reserved sectors: $R = 32$ (standard for FAT32, used for backup boot sector)
- Number of FATs: $T = 2$ (primary + mirror)
- Root directory sectors: $D_\text{root} = 0$ (in FAT32, root is a cluster chain, not a fixed area)

**Step 1 — Convert nominal capacity to bytes.**

$$
D_\text{nominal} = 16 \text{ GB} = 16 \times 1024 \times 1024 \times 1024 = 17\,179\,869\,184 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert bytes to total sectors.**

$$
S_\text{total} = \dfrac{D_\text{nominal}}{S} = \dfrac{17\,179\,869\,184}{512} = 33\,554\,432 \text{ sectors}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute bytes per cluster.**

$$
C = S \times N = 512 \times 64 = 32\,768 \text{ bytes/cluster} = 32 \text{ KiB}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute total clusters in the data region.**

$$
K = \dfrac{S_\text{total} - R}{N} = \dfrac{33\,554\,432 - 32}{64} = 524\,288 \text{ clusters}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute size of one FAT in sectors.**

For FAT32, the entry width is $E = 32$ bits $= 4$ bytes per cluster pointer.

$$
F = \dfrac{K \times E}{S} = \dfrac{524\,288 \times 4}{512} = 4\,096 \text{ sectors per FAT}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute total sectors occupied by both FATs.**

$$
S_\text{FAT} = T \times F = 2 \times 4\,096 = 8\,192 \text{ sectors}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute usable data capacity.**

$$
U = K \times C = 524\,288 \times 32\,768 = 17\,179\,869\,184 \text{ bytes} = 16 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 8 — Compute overhead fraction.**

$$
\eta = 1 - \dfrac{U}{D_\text{nominal}} = 1 - \dfrac{16 \text{ GB}}{16 \text{ GB}} \approx 0.0
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The overhead is negligible in the *approximation* because we assumed the entire card is one big data region. In reality, the FATs themselves, the reserved sectors, and the boot record occupy $8\,224$ sectors, i.e., $4.2 \text{ MB}$. The **true** overhead is therefore:

$$
\eta_\text{true} = \dfrac{8\,224 \times 512}{17\,179\,869\,184} \approx 0.000245 \;\; (\approx 0.0245\%)
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 – Wear-Leveling Lifetime Estimate

Consider an **ESP32 + SD card module** logging temperature once every **10 seconds** to a 32 GB SD card with the following specs:

- Card has $B = 2^{32}$ addressable logical blocks (typical SDXC maximum).
- Rated program-erase cycles per block: $P = 10\,000$ (TLC NAND).
- Writes per day from the sensor: $W = 8640$.

**Step 1 — Compute total write budget across the card's life (without wear leveling).**

$$
\text{Total writes} = B \times P = 2^{32} \times 10\,000 \approx 4.295 \times 10^{13} \text{ writes}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the number of days the card can survive.**

$$
\text{Days} = \dfrac{\text{Total writes}}{W} = \dfrac{4.295 \times 10^{13}}{8\,640} \approx 4.97 \times 10^{9} \text{ days}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to years.**

$$
\text{Years} = \dfrac{4.97 \times 10^{9}}{365} \approx 1.36 \times 10^{7} \text{ years}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> This number is **misleadingly high** because in practice the *same* physical blocks are written far more often than others (FAT table, log index, etc.). The controller's wear-leveling algorithm distributes writes evenly, but the **effective** endurance for a logging application is more realistically on the order of **3–10 years**, not millions. For KTU exams, always state the wear-leveling caveat explicitly.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Decision Flow for IoT Projects

| If the IoT use case is… | Then format the card as… | Cluster Size | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Raspberry Pi OS boot + data | FAT32 boot + ext4 root | 4 KiB (ext4), 32 KiB (FAT) | GPU first-stage only reads FAT |
| Arduino / ESP32 sensor logger | FAT32 | 32 KiB or 64 KiB | SD library defaults; large clusters reduce FAT writes |
| High-speed camera on RPi | exFAT (or ext4) | 128 KiB | exFAT supports files > 4 GiB without fragmentation |
| Industrial gateway with journaling | ext4 | 4 KiB | Power-cut safety via journal |
| Tiny 1–2 GB card in old Pi | FAT16 | 32 KiB | Small card → small FAT |
| Windows-readable data dump | NTFS or exFAT | 4 KiB | Windows default; cross-platform handoff |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Formatting a Card via the Official SD Card Formatter

1. **Download** the *SD Card Formatter* from the official SD Association website (sdcard.org). Do **not** use the generic Windows "Format" dialog — it often produces sub-optimal cluster sizes and may not write a clean boot sector.
2. **Insert** the SD card into the host PC's card reader.
3. **Select** the correct drive letter. Double-check capacity to avoid formatting the wrong disk.
4. **Choose** *Overwrite Format* for a thorough wipe, or *Quick Format* to merely re-initialize the file-system structures.
5. **Set Volume Label** (e.g., `RPI_BOOT` or `SENSORLOG`).
6. **Click** *Format*. Wait until the tool reports "Formatting was completed."
7. **Verify** by reinserting the card and checking that it mounts and shows the correct free space.

> [!TIP]
> On Linux, the equivalent one-liner is: `sudo mkfs.vfat -F 32 -n SENSORLOG /dev/sdX1` (replace `/dev/sdX1` with the actual partition). To format the whole card with a fresh MBR + FAT32 partition, use `sudo parted /dev/sdX mklabel msdos mkpart primary fat32 1MiB 100%` followed by the `mkfs.vfat` command.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram – SD Card Logical Layout After FAT32 Formatting

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[LBA 0: MBR Sector 512 bytes] --> B[Partition Table 4 entries 64 bytes]
    A --> C[Boot Signature 0x55AA 2 bytes]
    B --> D[LBA 2048: FAT32 Partition Start]
    D --> E[Volume Boot Record VBR 1 sector]
    D --> F[FSInfo Sector 1 sector]
    D --> G[Backup Boot Sector 1 sector]
    D --> H[Reserved Sectors 32 total]
    H --> I[FAT 1 Mirror Primary]
    H --> J[FAT 2 Backup Copy]
    I --> K[Cluster 2: Data Region Start]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Cluster 3 Next Data Cluster]
    L --> M[Cluster N: Root Directory Anchor]
    M --> N[File A Sensor Log csv]
    M --> O[File B Config json]
    M --> P[Cluster 268435454: FAT32 Max]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram – Formatting Decision Flow for IoT Projects

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start([Choose IoT Target Device]) --> Q1{What is the host OS or MCU}
    Q1 -->|Raspberry Pi Linux| RPi[RPi: FAT32 boot + ext4 root]
    Q1 -->|ESP32 or Arduino| MCU[FAT32 only]
    Q1 -->|Industrial Linux gateway| GW[ext4 with journaling]
    Q1 -->|Windows handoff needed| WIN[exFAT or NTFS]
    RPi --> Step1[Use Raspberry Pi Imager]
    MCU --> Step2[Use SD Card Formatter]
    GW --> Step3[Use mkfs.ext4 with journal]
    WIN --> Step4[Use SD Card Formatter exFAT mode]
    Step1 --> Verify[Test boot on Pi]
    Step2 --> Verify
    Step3 --> Verify
    Step4 --> Verify
    Verify --> End([Card ready for deployment])
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram – Block-Level Architecture of an SD Card Controller

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Host[Host MCU or SoC SPI or SD bus] --> IFR[Interface Logic SPI SD or UHS I]
    IFR --> CTRL[Flash Controller SoC]
    CTRL --> ECC[ECC Engine Hamming or BCH]
    CTRL --> WL[Wear Leveling Algorithm]
    CTRL --> BBM[Bad Block Management Table]
    ECC --> NAND[Raw NAND Flash Die TLC or MLC]
    WL --> NAND
    BBM --> NAND
    NAND --> PHY[Physical Pages and Blocks]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram – File-System Layering in the IoT Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    App[IoT Application Sensor Logger] --> Lib[SD Library SD.h or SdFat]
    Lib --> FS[File System Layer FAT32 or ext4]
    FS --> Block[Block Device Driver SPI or SDIO]
    Block --> HW[Physical SD Card Hardware]
    HW --> NAND[Internal NAND Flash]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each layer in the diagram above is a **separate software abstraction** that the IoT programmer interacts with. When you "format" the card, you are populating the **File System Layer** so that the SD library can find clusters, read directory entries, and create new files on behalf of your sensor code.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**  
*"What is meant by formatting an SD card? List any two file systems commonly used for formatting SD cards in IoT applications."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Formatting an SD card is the process of initializing the card by writing a **file system structure** (such as FAT32, exFAT, or ext4) onto the raw NAND flash, so that an operating system or microcontroller firmware can organize data into files and directories. (2 marks)

Two commonly used file systems in IoT are:
1. **FAT32** – used for boot partitions on Raspberry Pi and for SD card data logging with Arduino/ESP32. (½ mark)
2. **ext4** – used for the root filesystem of Raspberry Pi OS because of journaling and Linux compatibility. (½ mark)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**  
*"Differentiate between MBR and GPT partition table schemes in the context of SD card formatting."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | MBR | GPT |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Maximum addressable capacity | 2 TiB (32-bit LBA) | 9.4 ZB (64-bit LBA) |
| Maximum partitions | 4 primary (or 3+1 extended) | 128 by default |
| Location | Single sector at LBA 0 | Primary at LBA 1, backup at last LBA |
| CRC protection | None | CRC32 on header and entries |
| Use in IoT | Standard for SD cards ≤ 2 TiB | Used for SDXC/SDUC > 2 TiB |

(1½ marks for MBR explanation, 1½ marks for GPT)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the internal structure of a FAT32-formatted SD card. With a neat diagram, describe the roles of the MBR, VBR, FAT, and Data Region. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 32 GB SDHC card is to be formatted as FAT32 with a sector size of 512 bytes and 64 sectors per cluster. Compute the number of clusters, the size of one FAT in sectors, and the effective overhead percentage. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Introduction (1 mark):**  
A FAT32-formatted SD card is logically divided into four major regions: the **Master Boot Record (MBR)**, the **Reserved Region** (containing the VBR), the **FAT Region** (two copies), and the **Data Region** (clusters).

**Step 2 — MBR (1 mark):**  
The MBR occupies the first sector (LBA 0) of the card. It contains the bootstrap code, the disk signature, and a 64-byte partition table describing up to four primary partitions. For SD cards used with Raspberry Pi, the active partition entry points to the boot partition.

**Step 3 — Reserved Region and VBR (2 marks):**  
The reserved region begins at the start of the partition (typically LBA 2048 after a 1 MiB alignment). The first sector holds the **Volume Boot Record (VBR)**, which contains the BPB describing the geometry: bytes per sector, sectors per cluster, reserved sector count, number of FATs, root entry count, total sectors, and media type. Subsequent reserved sectors contain the **FSInfo sector** and a **backup boot sector**.

**Step 4 — FAT Region (1.5 marks):**  
Two identical File Allocation Tables (FAT1 primary, FAT2 backup) follow the reserved region. Each FAT is an array of 32-bit entries; each entry represents one cluster in the data region. A value of `0x00000000` means free, `0x0FFFFFF8` is the end-of-chain marker, and intermediate values point to the next cluster of a file.

**Step 5 — Data Region (1.5 marks):**  
The data region is divided into clusters (allocation units). Each cluster is a fixed number of consecutive sectors (e.g., 64 sectors × 512 bytes = 32 KiB). Cluster number 2 is the first usable cluster, and the root directory is itself a cluster chain (not a fixed area as in FAT16).

**Diagram (allocated in part of the 7 marks):** Refer to **Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram** of these notes.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
- Capacity: $D = 32 \text{ GB}$
- Bytes per sector: $S = 512$
- Sectors per cluster: $N = 64$

**Step 1 — Total sectors (1 mark):**

$$
S_\text{total} = \dfrac{32 \times 1024^3}{512} = 67\,108\,864 \text{ sectors}
$$

**Step 2 — Bytes per cluster (0.5 mark):**

$$
C = S \times N = 512 \times 64 = 32\,768 \text{ bytes/cluster} = 32 \text{ KiB}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of clusters (2 marks):**  
Assuming reserved sectors $R = 32$ and that the entire data region is used (ignoring the small FAT overhead for the count, since it is negligible compared to $S_\text{total}$):

$$
K = \dfrac{S_\text{total} - R}{N} = \dfrac{67\,108\,864 - 32}{64} = 1\,048\,575 \text{ clusters}
$$

This is well below the FAT32 maximum of $2^{28} = 268\,435\,456$, so the format is valid.

**Step 4 — FAT size in sectors (2 marks):**

$$
F = \dfrac{K \times 4 \text{ bytes}}{512 \text{ bytes/sector}} = \dfrac{1\,048\,575 \times 4}{512} \approx 8\,193 \text{ sectors}
$$

Rounded up to the next whole sector: $F = 8\,193$ sectors per FAT (often padded to 8\,192).

**Step 5 — Overhead percentage (1.5 marks):**

$$
\text{Total FAT sectors} = 2 \times 8\,193 = 16\,386
$$

$$
\text{Overhead bytes} = (R + 16\,386) \times 512 \approx 8.4 \text{ MB}
$$

$$
\eta = \dfrac{8.4 \text{ MB}}{32\,768 \text{ MB}} \times 100\% \approx 0.0256\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students forget to **multiply by 2** when computing total FAT space (because there are two copies of the FAT). They also confuse the **total card capacity** with the **data region capacity**. Always clearly state *what* you are counting.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Compare FAT32, exFAT, and ext4 file systems with respect to maximum file size, journaling support, and compatibility with Raspberry Pi and Arduino. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the step-by-step procedure to format an SD card for a Raspberry Pi using the official Raspberry Pi Imager. Why is it preferred over the Windows "Format" dialog? (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (a) — 7 marks

| Property | FAT32 | exFAT | ext4 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Max file size | 4 GiB − 1 B | 16 EiB | 16 TiB |
| Max volume size | 2 TiB (theoretical) | 128 PiB | 1 EiB |
| Journaling | No | No | **Yes** |
| RPi bootable | **Yes** (boot partition) | Yes (RPi 4 UEFI only) | **Yes** (root partition) |
| Arduino SD.h | **Yes** | Limited (SdFat lib) | No |
| Cluster size range | 512 B – 32 KiB | Up to 32 MiB | 1 KiB – 64 KiB |
| OS support | Universal | Win/macOS/Android | Linux native |

(Each row carries 1 mark. Award 7 marks for a well-completed table with brief justifications.)

**Conclusion (½ mark):** For a Raspberry Pi IoT project, the **FAT32 + ext4** combination offers the best of both worlds — broad boot compatibility and a journaled root filesystem for reliability.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Download and install (1 mark):**  
Download the **Raspberry Pi Imager** from the official raspberrypi.com website. Install it on a Windows, macOS, or Linux host. The tool is the recommended method endorsed by the Raspberry Pi Foundation.

**Step 2 — Insert the SD card (1 mark):**  
Insert the SD card into the host PC's card reader. Verify the correct drive letter/capacity in the Imager's "Storage" dropdown to avoid accidentally formatting the wrong disk.

**Step 3 — Choose the OS (1.5 marks):**  
Click "Operating System" and select the desired Raspberry Pi OS variant (e.g., *Raspberry Pi OS 64-bit Desktop*). For an IoT project, *Raspberry Pi OS Lite* is preferred because it omits the desktop and runs headless.

**Step 4 — Configure advanced options (1.5 marks):**  
Click the gear icon to set hostname, enable SSH, configure Wi-Fi credentials, and set a username/password. This pre-configures the card *before* first boot, saving time.

**Step 5 — Write the image (1.5 marks):**  
Click "Write". The Imager will:
1. Wipe the card,
2. Create a **FAT32 boot partition** (typically 256 MiB or 512 MiB) containing the firmware, kernel (`kernel8.img`), and configuration files,
3. Create a **second ext4 partition** for the root filesystem containing the OS libraries and user data.

**Step 6 — Verify and eject (0.5 mark):**  
Once the Imager reports success, safely eject the card, insert it into the Raspberry Pi, and power on.

**Why is it preferred over the Windows "Format" dialog? (bonus 1 mark — included in the 7):**  
The Windows dialog only formats a single partition as FAT32 or NTFS and does not lay down the **two-partition boot/root layout** that the Raspberry Pi bootloader expects. It also does not write the proprietary **Raspberry Pi firmware blobs** (`bootcode.bin`, `start.elf`, `fixup.dat`) that the GPU's first-stage bootloader needs to hand off control to the ARM CPU. The Imager performs all of this in one step.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** A common mistake is to answer *"format with FAT32 only and copy NOOBS files"* — this describes the **legacy** NOOBS installer workflow, which is **deprecated** as of 2024. State the modern Imager-based workflow to earn full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Formatting installs a file system on the raw NAND flash so the host OS/firmware can organize data into files and directories.
- **Three key regions:** MBR (or GPT) → Reserved Region (VBR) → FAT(s) → Data Region (clusters).
- **FAT32** is the universal IoT file system: max 4 GiB files, max 2 TiB volume, no journaling, supported by both Raspberry Pi and Arduino SD library.
- **ext4** is journaled and is the standard Linux root filesystem; preferred for the root partition of Raspberry Pi OS.
- **exFAT** is the bridge between Windows and large cards (>32 GB) when FAT32's 4 GiB file limit is a problem.
- **Sector size is 512 bytes**; **cluster size** ranges from 512 B up to 32 MiB; choose larger clusters to reduce FAT churn for log files.
- **Wear leveling** is handled by the SD card's internal controller; choose industrial-grade cards for high-write IoT workloads.
- **MBR vs GPT:** Use MBR for cards ≤ 2 TiB (almost all IoT cards); use GPT only for SDXC > 2 TiB or UEFI boot scenarios.
- **Raspberry Pi Imager** is the KTU-recommended tool — it creates a FAT32 boot partition + ext4 root partition automatically.
- **SD Card Formatter** from sdcard.org is the most reliable cross-platform formatter for plain FAT32/exFAT.
- **Overhead** from FAT metadata is typically **< 0.1%** of nominal capacity; never confuse it with usable capacity.
- **Common 3-mark question:** *"List two file systems and state the maximum file size of each."* — answer: FAT32 → 4 GiB; ext4 → 16 TiB; exFAT → 16 EiB.
- **Common 7-mark question:** *"Compute the number of clusters and FAT size for a given card capacity, sector size, and sectors-per-cluster."* — use the formulas in §3.1.
- **Common 14-mark question:** *"Explain FAT32 layout with diagram and perform a sizing calculation."* — combine the diagram from §4.1 with the derivation in §3.1.
- **Examiner's red flags** that cost marks: (i) formatting a 64 GB card as FAT32 using the Windows dialog, (ii) stating that formatting erases the NAND permanently, (iii) omitting journaling as the reason for ext4 preference, (iv) forgetting to multiply FAT size by 2 in calculations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Installing and Working with Raspberry Pi
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT504 - PROGRAMMING FOR INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Installing and Working with Raspberry Pi

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Raspberry Pi** is a series of small, single-board computers (SBCs) developed by the **Raspberry Pi Foundation** (UK) to promote the teaching of basic computer science in schools and developing countries. From an IoT perspective, it functions as a low-cost, low-power **edge computing node** capable of running a full Linux-based operating system (typically **Raspberry Pi OS**, formerly Raspbian), interfacing with sensors/actuators via **General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO)** pins, and connecting to networks via Ethernet/Wi-Fi.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition to Memorize:** A Raspberry Pi is a *credit-card sized, ARM-based single-board computer* used in IoT as a gateway or edge device, capable of running a full OS, executing Python/C/C++ programs, and communicating with physical hardware through its 40-pin GPIO header.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Pocket-Sized Brain"

Imagine you have a **full desktop computer** — bulky, power-hungry, needs a hard drive, monitor, and keyboard. Now, imagine shrinking that entire computer down to the **size of a credit card**, and making it consume about as much power as a **5W LED bulb**. That is exactly what a Raspberry Pi is.

Think of the IoT ecosystem as a **human nervous system**:
- The **Sensors** (eyes, ears, skin) collect data from the environment.
- The **Raspberry Pi** acts as the **brain/spinal cord** — it processes the sensory data, makes decisions, and sends commands back.
- The **Actuators** (muscles, hands) carry out the physical action (e.g., turning on a fan, unlocking a door).
- The **Cloud** is the *memory and higher reasoning* — where data is stored long-term and complex analytics happen.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Raspberry Pi for IoT?** Unlike microcontrollers (e.g., Arduino), the Raspberry Pi runs a **full multitasking OS** (Linux). This means you can browse the internet, run a Python script, host a small web server, and read sensor data — *all simultaneously* — on a device that costs roughly **₹3,000 – ₹6,000** (USD 35–60).

## 1.3 Key Technical Specifications (Latest Generation)

| Parameter | Raspberry Pi 4 Model B | Raspberry Pi 5 |
|---|---|---|
| **SoC** | Broadcom BCM2711 | Broadcom BCM2712 |
| **CPU** | Quad-core Cortex-A72 @ 1.8 GHz | Quad-core Cortex-A76 @ 2.4 GHz |
| **RAM** | **2 / 4 / 8 GB LPDDR4** | 4 / 8 GB LPDDR4X |
| **GPIO Pins** | 40-pin header | 40-pin header |
| **Connectivity** | Wi-Fi, Bluetooth 5.0, Gigabit Ethernet | Wi-Fi, Bluetooth 5.0, Gigabit Ethernet |
| **Power** | **5V / 3A via USB-C** | 5V / 5A via USB-C |
| **Storage** | microSD card | microSD card / PCIe |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard GPIO Voltage:** All GPIO pins operate at **3.3V logic levels**. Applying **5V** to a GPIO pin will permanently damage the board. This is a frequent KTU exam question and a common student mistake.

## 1.4 Hardware Components on the Board

A Raspberry Pi board is a complete ecosystem. The major components are:

1. **SoC (System on Chip):** The "brain" — contains CPU, GPU, and memory controller.
2. **GPIO Header (40 pins):** The interface to the physical world.
3. **USB Ports:** For keyboard, mouse, or external storage.
4. **HDMI Port(s):** For connecting a monitor/display.
5. **Ethernet Port (RJ45):** For wired network connectivity.
6. **microSD Card Slot:** Acts as the **hard drive** — stores the OS and all user files.
7. **Power Input (USB-C):** **5V DC**, minimum **3A** for Pi 4, **5A** for Pi 5.
8. **CSI Camera Port:** For connecting the official camera module.
9. **DSI Display Port:** For connecting the official touchscreen.
10. **Power LED & Activity LED:** Status indicators.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 40-Pin GPIO Header — Decoded

The 40-pin header is the most exam-relevant feature. It contains multiple types of pins, each with a specific function. The pin numbering follows two standards: **Board Pin Numbering** (physical location) and **BCM (Broadcom) Numbering** (the internal SoC pin number). The `RPi.GPIO` Python library accepts both, but **BCM is the preferred professional standard**.

### 2.1.1 Pin Categories

- **Power Pins (5 total):**
  - **2 × 5V pins** (Pins 2, 4)
  - **2 × 3.3V pins** (Pins 1, 17)
  - **Ground (GND) pins** (Pins 6, 9, 14, 20, 25, 30, 34, 39)

- **GPIO Data Pins (26 usable):** GPIO2, GPIO3, GPIO4, GPIO5, GPIO6, GPIO7, GPIO8, GPIO9, GPIO10, GPIO11, GPIO12, GPIO13, GPIO14, GPIO15, GPIO16, GPIO17, GPIO18, GPIO19, GPIO20, GPIO21, GPIO22, GPIO23, GPIO24, GPIO25, GPIO26, GPIO27.

- **Special Function Pins:**
  - **I2C:** GPIO2 (SDA), GPIO3 (SCL) — used for sensors, RTC, OLED displays.
  - **SPI:** GPIO9 (MISO), GPIO10 (MOSI), GPIO11 (SCLK), GPIO8 (CE0) — used for high-speed peripherals.
  - **UART (Serial):** GPIO14 (TXD), GPIO15 (RXD) — used for serial debugging.
  - **PWM (Hardware):** GPIO12, GPIO13, GPIO18, GPIO19 — used for motor speed control, LED dimming.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board vs BCM Numbering Example:** Physical Pin 11 on the board is **GPIO17** in BCM numbering. In Python, when using `RPi.GPIO`, you must choose exactly one: `GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BOARD)` or `GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)`. Mixing them is a common KTU exam pitfall.

## 2.2 The Raspberry Pi OS — Architecture Overview

Raspberry Pi OS is a **Debian-based Linux distribution** optimized for the ARM architecture of the Pi. The OS is structured in layers:

- **Hardware Layer:** Broadcom SoC (BCM2711/BCM2712).
- **Kernel Layer:** Linux Kernel (custom-built for ARM, with Broadcom hardware drivers).
- **User Space:** Desktop environment (PIXEL on legacy, Wayfire on Bookworm), Python 3, libraries, and applications.
- **Application Layer:** User programs (Python scripts, web servers, Node-RED, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Linux?** Linux is **open-source, free, and highly stable**. It supports multitasking, networking, and a vast library ecosystem. The Pi Foundation uses Debian because of its package management (`apt`), which makes installing software as simple as `sudo apt install <package>`.

## 2.3 Installation Workflow — The Standard Procedure

The official installation method recommended by the Raspberry Pi Foundation uses a tool called **Raspberry Pi Imager**. The process is:

1. **Download Raspberry Pi Imager** from the official website onto a host PC (Windows/macOS/Linux).
2. **Insert a microSD card** (minimum **8GB**, Class 10 recommended) into the PC.
3. **Select the OS** (Raspberry Pi OS 64-bit) inside the Imager.
4. **Select the Storage** (the microSD card).
5. **Configure Advanced Options** (OS Customization):
   - Set hostname
   - Enable **SSH** (Secure Shell) with password/public-key authentication
   - Configure **Wi-Fi credentials** (SSID and password)
   - Set username and password
6. **Click "Write"** — this flashes the OS image onto the microSD card.
7. **Insert the microSD card** into the Raspberry Pi.
8. **Power on** the Pi using a **5V / 3A** USB-C power supply.

> [!WARNING]
> **Power Supply Warning:** Using a low-quality phone charger (e.g., 5V/1A) is the **#1 reason for boot failures and SD card corruption**. The Pi 4 strictly requires **5V/3A**, and the Pi 5 requires **5V/5A** with a proper USB-C PD supply.

## 2.4 Headless Setup — No Monitor Required

In many IoT deployments, the Pi runs without a display, keyboard, or mouse — this is called a **headless setup**. The procedure is:

1. Flash the OS using Raspberry Pi Imager with **SSH enabled** and **Wi-Fi pre-configured**.
2. Insert the microSD card into the Pi and power it on.
3. Find the Pi's IP address on the local network (using tools like `nmap`, `Advanced IP Scanner`, or by checking the router's DHCP lease table).
4. Connect remotely via:
   - **SSH:** `ssh username@<pi-ip-address>` from any terminal.
   - **VNC (Virtual Network Computing):** Install `sudo apt install realvnc-vnc-server` and connect using the RealVNC Viewer for a full graphical desktop.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Hint:** The "headless setup" is a frequently asked 14-mark question. The expected answer should include: enabling SSH during flashing, configuring Wi-Fi via the `wpa_supplicant.conf` file (legacy method) or Imager's OS customization, and then accessing the Pi via SSH/VNC.

## 2.5 The Linux Command Line — Core Commands for KTU

Students must be familiar with the following essential Linux commands. These are extremely high-yield for Part A (3-mark) questions.

| Command | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `pwd` | Print current working directory | `pwd` → `/home/pi` |
| `ls` | List files and directories | `ls -la` |
| `cd` | Change directory | `cd /home/pi/Documents` |
| `mkdir` | Create a new directory | `mkdir iot_project` |
| `rm` | Remove a file or directory | `rm -rf old_folder` |
| `sudo` | Execute a command as superuser | `sudo apt update` |
| `apt` | Package manager (Debian) | `sudo apt install python3-gpiozero` |
| `nano` | Terminal text editor | `nano hello.py` |
| `ifconfig` | Display network interface configuration | `ifconfig` or `ip a` |
| `ping` | Test network connectivity | `ping google.com` |
| `reboot` | Restart the system | `sudo reboot` |
| `shutdown` | Power off the system safely | `sudo shutdown -h now` |

## 2.6 The GPIO Programming Library — `RPi.GPIO` & `gpiozero`

Two Python libraries dominate Raspberry Pi GPIO programming:

### 2.6.1 `RPi.GPIO` (Low-Level)

This is the foundational library. It gives direct control over pin modes (INPUT/OUTPUT), pull-up/pull-down resistors, and event detection.

```python
import RPi.GPIO as GPIO
import time

LED_PIN = 17  # BCM numbering

GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)
GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT)

try:
    while True:
        GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.HIGH)
        time.sleep(1)
        GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.LOW)
        time.sleep(1)
finally:
    GPIO.cleanup()
```

### 2.6.2 `gpiozero` (High-Level, Beginner-Friendly)

The `gpiozero` library is a wrapper around `RPi.GPIO`, designed for clean, object-oriented syntax. It is the **recommended library** by the Raspberry Pi Foundation for new projects.

```python
from gpiozero import LED
from signal import pause

led = LED(17)  # BCM pin 17

led.blink(on_time=1, off_time=1)  # Blink forever
pause()
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The `GPIO.cleanup()` function in `RPi.GPIO` resets all pins to their default state. Forgetting to call it leaves pins in an undefined state, which can cause issues for subsequent programs. This is a common 3-mark question.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **GPIO Logic HIGH Voltage** | $V_{HIGH} = 3.3\text{ V}$ | NEVER exceed **3.3V** on data pins |
| **GPIO Logic LOW Voltage** | $V_{LOW} = 0\text{ V}$ | Same as ground reference |
| **Max GPIO Current (per pin)** | $I_{max} = 16\text{ mA}$ | Recommended: **≤ 8 mA** per pin |
| **Max Total GPIO Current** | $I_{total} = 50\text{ mA}$ | Sum of all GPIO pins combined |
| **Power Budget (Pi 4)** | $P = 5\text{V} \times 3\text{A} = 15\text{ W}$ | USB-C input |
| **Power Budget (Pi 5)** | $P = 5\text{V} \times 5\text{A} = 25\text{ W}$ | USB-C PD input |
| **SD Card Requirement** | $\geq 8\text{ GB, Class 10}$ | microSDHC / microSDXC |
| **Board → BCM Mapping** | Pin 11 (Board) = GPIO17 (BCM) | Physical pin 11, 6th pin on top row |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Power Budget Derivation — Can a USB Charger Power the Pi?

A critical engineering analysis students must master is whether a given power supply can safely operate a Raspberry Pi. Let us derive the answer step-by-step.

### Problem Statement

> *You have a USB power bank rated at 5V / 2.4A. Can you use it to reliably power a Raspberry Pi 4B with a single USB Wi-Fi dongle, an LED, and a DHT22 temperature sensor connected?*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify the load's current requirements.**

A Raspberry Pi 4B at full CPU load (no peripherals) draws approximately **3.0A** at 5V. Adding peripherals increases this demand. We tabulate the current draw:

| Component | Typical Current Draw |
|---|---|
| Raspberry Pi 4B (baseline) | 600 mA (idle) – 1.2A (typical load) |
| Raspberry Pi 4B (with peripherals) | up to **1.5 A** |
| USB Wi-Fi Dongle | 200 mA |
| LED (with 330Ω resistor) | ~5 mA |
| DHT22 Sensor (peak during read) | 2.5 mA |

**Step 2: Sum the total current requirement.**

$$
I_{total} = I_{Pi} + I_{WiFi} + I_{LED} + I_{DHT22}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
I_{total} = 1500\text{ mA} + 200\text{ mA} + 5\text{ mA} + 2.5\text{ mA}
$$

$$
I_{total} = 1707.5\text{ mA} \approx 1.71\text{ A}
$$

**Step 3: Compare to the supply capacity.**

The power bank provides $I_{supply} = 2.4\text{ A}$ at $V = 5\text{ V}$. The requirement is $I_{total} = 1.71\text{ A}$.

$$
I_{supply} > I_{total} \implies 2.4\text{ A} > 1.71\text{ A} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 4: Apply the engineering safety margin.**

A professional engineer always applies a **20% safety margin** to account for transients (inrush current at boot, Wi-Fi transmission peaks, etc.):

$$
I_{required\_safe} = I_{total} \times 1.2
$$

$$
I_{required\_safe} = 1.71\text{ A} \times 1.2 = 2.05\text{ A}
$$

**Step 5: Final verdict.**

Since $I_{supply} = 2.4\text{ A} > I_{required\_safe} = 2.05\text{ A}$, the **2.4A power bank is marginally sufficient**. However, for production-grade reliability, a **3A supply is strongly recommended**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The Pi draws a large inrush current (~1A spike for ~100ms) at boot when charging the USB ports and initializing peripherals. Cheap power supplies cannot handle this transient, leading to brownouts and random reboots — a classic symptom of an under-spec'd power supply.

## 3.2 LED Current-Limiting Resistor Calculation

When connecting an LED to a GPIO pin, a current-limiting resistor is **mandatory** to prevent the LED from drawing too much current and burning out (or worse, damaging the GPIO pin).

### Problem Statement

> *You connect a red LED (forward voltage $V_f = 2.0\text{ V}$, desired current $I_f = 5\text{ mA}$) between GPIO17 (3.3V) and GND. Calculate the required series resistor value.*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL).**

The sum of voltage drops around the loop must equal the source voltage:

$$
V_{GPIO} = V_R + V_f
$$

Where $V_{GPIO} = 3.3\text{ V}$ is the GPIO output HIGH voltage, $V_R$ is the voltage drop across the resistor, and $V_f = 2.0\text{ V}$ is the LED's forward voltage.

**Step 2: Solve for $V_R$.**

$$
V_R = V_{GPIO} - V_f
$$

$$
V_R = 3.3\text{ V} - 2.0\text{ V} = 1.3\text{ V}
$$

**Step 3: Apply Ohm's Law.**

$$
R = \frac{V_R}{I_f}
$$

Substituting:

$$
R = \frac{1.3\text{ V}}{5 \times 10^{-3}\text{ A}}
$$

$$
R = \frac{1.3}{0.005} = 260\text{ }\Omega
$$

**Step 4: Select the nearest standard E12 resistor value.**

The nearest standard value above 260Ω is **330 Ω**. Using 330Ω gives a slightly dimmer LED but a safer operating current:

$$
I_{actual} = \frac{V_R}{R} = \frac{1.3\text{ V}}{330\text{ }\Omega} \approx 3.94\text{ mA}
$$

This is well within the safe range for a standard LED.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget that the **GPIO output is 3.3V, not 5V**. Using 5V in the calculation gives a different (and incorrect) resistor value. Always use **3.3V** for Raspberry Pi GPIO calculations.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Installation Scenarios

| Scenario | Method | Key Steps | Tools Required |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Desktop setup (with monitor)** | Direct HDMI connection | Flash SD card → Insert → Connect HDMI + KB/Mouse → Power on → Walk through setup wizard | Monitor, HDMI cable, keyboard, mouse |
| **Headless (Wi-Fi only)** | SSH + VNC | Flash with Imager → Enable SSH + Wi-Fi → Boot → Find IP → `ssh user@ip` | Pi, power supply, Wi-Fi network |
| **Headless (Ethernet only)** | SSH | Same as above but with Ethernet cable instead of Wi-Fi | Pi, Ethernet cable, router |
| **Headless (no network)** | Serial Console | Connect USB-to-TTL cable to GPIO14/15 → Use `minicom` or PuTTY on host PC | USB-TTL cable, host PC |
| **Recovery from corrupt SD** | Reflash | Remove SD card → Re-flash using Imager → Restore from backup | Another PC, microSD reader |

## 3.4 Configuration Files — The Backbone of the Headless Setup

When the Pi boots, several configuration files control its behavior. Understanding these is key to troubleshooting:

| File | Location | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `config.txt` | `/boot/config.txt` | GPU memory, HDMI settings, overclocking |
| `cmdline.txt` | `/boot/cmdline.txt` | Kernel boot parameters (e.g., root filesystem location) |
| `wpa_supplicant.conf` | `/etc/wpa_supplicant/wpa_supplicant.conf` | Wi-Fi network credentials (legacy) |
| `ssh` | `/boot/ssh` | Empty file whose **presence** enables SSH (legacy) |
| `dhcpcd.conf` | `/etc/dhcpcd.conf` | Static IP configuration |
| `crontab` | `/etc/crontab` | Scheduled task execution |

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Approach (Bookworm+):** Since the release of Raspberry Pi OS Bookworm (2023), the recommended way to configure SSH, Wi-Fi, and credentials is through **Raspberry Pi Imager's OS Customization menu** at the time of flashing. The legacy method of manually creating `wpa_supplicant.conf` and `ssh` files on the boot partition is deprecated.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Raspberry Pi Boot Sequence Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power On - 5V USB-C] --> B[PMU Initialized]
    B --> C[BCM SoC Starts Core 0]
    C --> D[GPU reads bootcode.bin from SD]
    D --> E[GPU loads start.elf]
    E --> F[Kernel image loaded to RAM]
    F --> G[cmdline.txt parsed]
    G --> H[Root filesystem mounted on SD]
    H --> I[systemd initialized]
    I --> J[Network services start]
    J --> K[SSH daemon launched]
    K --> L[Login prompt or Desktop GUI]
    L --> M[User Session Active]

    style A fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style M fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style D fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style H fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The boot process begins with power application. The GPU (not the CPU) is the first to execute code, reading the small `bootcode.bin` binary from the SD card. This is a unique aspect of the Raspberry Pi architecture — the GPU is the "boot master."

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    R --> B[LED Anode - Positive Terminal]
    B --> C[LED Cathode - Negative Terminal]

    style A fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Circuit:** Current flows from GPIO17 (HIGH = 3.3V) through the 330Ω resistor, through the LED (which lights up), and then to ground. The resistor limits the current to approximately **3.94 mA**, protecting both the LED and the GPIO pin from excessive current draw.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Raspberry Pi. List its key features.
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Raspberry Pi is a low-cost, credit-card sized single-board computer (SBC) developed by the Raspberry Pi Foundation, based on ARM architecture, widely used as an edge computing device in IoT applications.

**Key Features:**
1. **Compact size** — credit-card form factor (~85mm × 56mm).
2. **ARM-based SoC** (Broadcom BCM2711/BCM2712) with quad-core CPU.
3. **40-pin GPIO header** for interfacing with sensors and actuators at **3.3V logic**.
4. **Multiple connectivity options** — Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, Ethernet, USB.
5. **Runs a full Linux OS** (Raspberry Pi OS / Debian-based) supporting multitasking.
6. **HDMI output** for connecting a display.
7. **microSD card** for storage (acts as the hard drive).
8. **Low power consumption** (~15W for Pi 4).

---

### Question 2: What is the difference between BOARD numbering and BCM numbering in Raspberry Pi GPIO? Give an example.
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | BOARD Numbering | BCM Numbering |
|---|---|---|
| **Reference** | Physical pin position on the header | Internal Broadcom SoC pin number |
| **Pin Identification** | Sequential count from top-left (1 to 40) | Refers to the SoC's GPIO controller number |
| **Example** | Physical Pin **11** | **GPIO17** (same physical pin) |
| **Library Code** | `GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BOARD)` | `GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)` |

**Example:** The physical pin at position 11 on the GPIO header is referenced as **Pin 11** in BOARD mode and **GPIO17** in BCM mode. Both refer to the same physical pin, but the numbering scheme is different. Mixing the two modes in code is a common source of errors.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Module Structure:** Each 14-mark question has internal choice. Students answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B. Sub-parts (a) and (b) carry **7 marks each**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure to install Raspberry Pi OS on a microSD card using Raspberry Pi Imager. How do you configure SSH and Wi-Fi during the installation process for a headless setup? **[7 Marks]**

**Q. (b)** Write a Python program using the `gpiozero` library to read the state of a push button connected to GPIO17 and toggle an LED connected to GPIO27. Draw the corresponding circuit diagram. **[7 Marks]**

### Model Answer for Q. A(a) — Installation Procedure

**Step 1: Download and Install Raspberry Pi Imager**
Download Raspberry Pi Imager from the official website (https://www.raspberrypi.com/software/) and install it on a Windows/macOS/Linux PC.

**Step 2: Insert the microSD Card**
Insert a microSD card (minimum 8GB, Class 10) into the PC's card reader. Ensure all data is backed up, as the card will be erased.

**Step 3: Launch the Imager**
Open Raspberry Pi Imager. The interface presents three dropdowns:
- **Operating System**
- **Storage** (select the microSD card)
- **Next button**

**Step 4: Select the Operating System**
Click on "Operating System" → Choose **"Raspberry Pi OS (64-bit)"** from the list. This is the recommended full desktop version for beginners.

**Step 5: Select the Storage**
