    C->>S: Encrypted password
    S->>S: Verify against /etc/shadow
    S-->>C: Authentication success
    C->>S: Channel open request - session
    S-->>C: Channel open confirmation
    C->>S: Encrypted shell commands
    S-->>C: Encrypted command output
```

## 4.4 Decision Flow — Choosing an Authentication Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start - need remote access to Pi] --> B{Is this a one-off lab demo?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use password authentication]
    B -- No --> D[Use public-key authentication Ed25519]
    C --> E[Set strong password and change default]
    D --> F[Run ssh-keygen on client]
    F --> G[Run ssh-copy-id to push public key]
    G --> H[Disable PasswordAuthentication in sshd_config]
    H --> I[Restart sshd]
    E --> J[Production-ready IoT node]
    I --> J
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Headless Boot Sequence Enabling SSH

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Write Raspberry Pi OS to microSD via Imager] --> B[Create empty ssh file in boot partition]
    B --> C[Insert SD card and power on Pi]
    C --> D[First-boot service detects ssh file]
    D --> E[raspi-config enables SSH service permanently]
    E --> F[systemd starts sshd on port 22]
    F --> G[Pi announces itself via mDNS as raspberrypi.local]
    G --> H[Client can now ssh pi@raspberrypi.local]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PBCIT504]**
**What is SSH? Mention its default port number and two key features.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** SSH (Secure Shell) is a cryptographic network protocol used to securely access and manage a remote computer over an unsecured network. It provides encrypted communication between a client and a server.
- **Default port (1 mark):** **TCP port 22**.
- **Two key features (1 mark):** (i) **Confidentiality** through symmetric encryption (e.g., AES-256, ChaCha20), and (ii) **Authentication** through public-key cryptography or passwords, ensuring only authorized users gain access.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PBCIT504]**
**How do you enable SSH on a freshly imaged Raspberry Pi without using a monitor or keyboard?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. After writing the Raspberry Pi OS image to the microSD card, **re-open the boot partition** on a PC (1 mark).
2. Create an **empty file with the exact name `ssh` (no extension, no content)** in the root of that partition (1 mark).
3. Eject the card, insert it into the Pi, and power it on. The first-boot service (`raspi-config`) detects this file, **enables the SSH daemon permanently**, and starts the `sshd` service on port 22 (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations: a 14-mark question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Below are **two full alternative questions** to satisfy the internal-choice pattern.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **SSH-2 protocol architecture** and the function of each layer. (7 marks)

**(b)** List and explain the steps to **set up password-less SSH access** from a Linux laptop to a Raspberry Pi using `ed25519` keys. Include the hardening step that disables password login. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (a)

**SSH-2 Protocol Architecture (7 marks):**

SSH-2 is a layered protocol (defined across **RFC 4251 to 4254**) consisting of three primary layers stacked on top of TCP/IP:

1. **Transport Layer [RFC 4253] – 2 marks**
   - Responsible for **server authentication, encryption, and integrity** of the connection.
   - Handles **TCP-level connection** (port 22), **key exchange** (Diffie–Hellman, ECDH, Curve25519), and negotiation of session algorithms.
   - Provides **bulk encryption** (AES-128/256, ChaCha20) and **MAC-based integrity** (HMAC-SHA-256/512, ETM).
   - Re-keys the connection periodically to maintain forward secrecy.

2. **User Authentication Layer [RFC 4252] – 2 marks**
   - Authenticates the **client to the server** after the transport layer is established.
   - Methods supported: `publickey`, `password`, `keyboard-interactive`, `hostbased`.
   - Multiple methods can be attempted; the server accepts the first one that succeeds.

3. **Connection Layer [RFC 4254] – 2 marks**
   - Multiplexes the single, encrypted SSH tunnel into **multiple logical channels** for different services.
   - Each channel can run a **shell session, a single command, an SFTP subsystem, X11 forwarding, or arbitrary TCP port forwarding** (tunnelling).
   - Channels are independent; closing one does not affect the others.

**(Architecture diagram: 1 mark)** — A clear three-tier block diagram (Application → Connection → Authentication → Transport → TCP) as drawn in **Section 4.2** above.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (b)

**Password-less SSH Setup using `ed25519` (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Generate the key pair on the client (2 marks)**
- Open a terminal on the Linux/macOS laptop and run:
  ```
  ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "laptop-to-pi"
  ```
- Press `Enter` to accept the default file location (`~/.ssh/id_ed25519`).
- Optionally enter a **passphrase** for an additional layer of security on the private key.
- Result: two files — `id_ed25519` (private, mode `600`) and `id_ed25519.pub` (public, shareable).

**Step 2: Copy the public key to the Raspberry Pi (2 marks)**
- Ensure the Pi is reachable (`ping raspberrypi.local`).
- Run:
  ```
  ssh-copy-id pi@192.168.1.42
  ```
- Enter the Pi's password **one last time**.
- This appends the public key to `/home/pi/.ssh/authorized_keys` on the Pi with correct file permissions (`700` on `.ssh`, `600` on the file).

**Step 3: Test and disable password login (2 marks)**
- Test: `ssh pi@192.168.1.42` → should log in **without a password**.
- On the Pi, edit `/etc/ssh/sshd_config`:
  ```
  PasswordAuthentication no
  PermitRootLogin no
  ```
- Restart the SSH daemon: `sudo systemctl restart ssh`.

**Step 4: Verification (1 mark)**
- Log out, log back in. Confirm that **password-based login is rejected** and only the key works. This protects the Pi against brute-force attacks, which is a best-practice KTU lab takeaway.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **Diffie–Hellman key exchange** algorithm as used during SSH connection establishment. Why is it said to be secure against passive eavesdropping? (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the procedure to **connect a Raspberry Pi to a laptop using SSH on a Windows 10 machine using PuTTY**. Include the steps for **file transfer** using `pscp`. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (a)

**Diffie–Hellman Key Exchange (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Public parameters (1 mark)**
- Both client and server publicly agree on a large prime number $p$ and a primitive root $g$.

**Step 2: Private and public values (2 marks)**
- The **client (laptop)** picks a private secret $a$ and computes its public value:
$$
A = g^{a} \bmod p
$$
- The **server (Raspberry Pi)** picks a private secret $b$ and computes:
$$
B = g^{b} \bmod p
$$

**Step 3: Exchange and shared secret (2 marks)**
- The two sides exchange $A$ and $B$ over the (insecure) network.
- Each side computes the **shared secret**:
$$
S_{\text{client}} = B^{a} \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p
$$
$$
S_{\text{server}} = A^{b} \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p
$$
- Both arrive at the **same** $S$, which is then used to derive symmetric session keys for encryption and MAC.

**Step 4: Why it is secure (2 marks)**
- A passive eavesdropper sees only $p, g, A, B$.
- Recovering $a$ from $A = g^{a} \bmod p$ requires solving the **Discrete Logarithm Problem**, which is computationally infeasible for sufficiently large primes ($p \geq 2048$ bits) or for elliptic-curve variants (ECDH).
- Hence the shared secret is established **without ever being transmitted**, providing **perfect forward secrecy** when combined with ephemeral keys.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (b)

**Connecting to Raspberry Pi via PuTTY on Windows 10 (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Download and install PuTTY (1 mark)**
- Download PuTTY from the official site (`putty.org`). It is a single `.exe` — no installation required.
- (Optional) Download `pscp.exe` and place it in the same folder or add it to `PATH`.

**Step 2: Find the Pi's IP address (1 mark)**
- On the Pi (with temporary monitor) or via the router's admin page, note the IP (e.g., `192.168.1.42`), or use `ping raspberrypi.local` from a Windows command prompt.

**Step 3: Configure PuTTY (2 marks)**
- Open PuTTY → *Session* category:
  - **Host Name:** `192.168.1.42`
  - **Port:** `22`
  - **Connection type:** `SSH`
  - **Saved Sessions:** type `MyRaspberryPi` → click *Save*.
- (For key auth) *Connection → SSH → Auth → Credentials* → browse to your `.ppk` private key file.
- If you have an OpenSSH `id_ed25519` key, first convert it to `.ppk` using `puttygen` (*Conversions → Import key → Save private key*).

**Step 4: Connect and authenticate (1 mark)**
- Click **Open**. On the first connection, accept the host key fingerprint warning.
- A terminal window opens prompting for `login as:` — type `pi` and the password.

**Step 5: File transfer with pscp (2 marks)**
- Open Windows Command Prompt in the folder containing `pscp.exe`.
- Upload a file:
  ```
  pscp C:\Users\Student\project.py pi@192.168.1.42:/home/pi/project.py
  ```
- Download a file:
  ```
  pscp pi@192.168.1.42:/home/pi/sensor_log.csv C:\Users\Student\
  ```
- Enter the password when prompted. The file transfer happens **inside the encrypted SSH tunnel**, ensuring confidentiality on the local Wi-Fi network.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Writing "SSH works on UDP"** — it does **not**. SSH is strictly **TCP-based, port 22**. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Confusing SSH with SSL/TLS** — they share concepts (asymmetric + symmetric crypto) but are **different protocols** at **different layers**. SSH operates at the application layer for remote shell; TLS secures HTTP/SMTP etc. (–2 marks)
> 3. **Forgetting to change the default `pi:raspberry` credentials** — examiners deduct marks because the default credentials are a **critical security flaw**.
> 4. **Not mentioning `mDNS/Avahi`** when asked to "find the Pi's IP" — `.local` hostname resolution is the *headless* standard answer.
> 5. **Writing `ssh-keygen` without specifying the algorithm** (`-t ed25519` or `-t rsa -b 4096`) — marks are awarded for **modern, secure** algorithm choice.
> 6. **Skipping the `chmod 600` permissions on private keys and `authorized_keys`** — SSH **refuses to use** keys with permissive permissions. A question about "why login fails after key copy" is a KTU favourite.
> 7. **Confusing `ssh` (the client) with `sshd` (the server daemon)** — they are different binaries. The Pi runs `sshd`; the laptop runs `ssh`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **SSH = Secure Shell**, a cryptographic protocol for **secure remote command-line access**.
- Default port: **22/TCP**. Protocol version: **SSH-2**.
- Three layers: **Transport** (encryption + server auth), **User Authentication** (client auth), **Connection** (channel multiplexing).
- Default Raspberry Pi credentials (`pi` / `raspberry`) **must be changed**.
- To enable SSH **headlessly**, drop an **empty `ssh` file** in the SD card's `boot` partition *before* first boot.
- To enable SSH with a monitor, use `sudo raspi-config` → *Interface Options* → *SSH* → *Yes*.
- SSH uses **TCP port 22** — confirm with `sudo systemctl status ssh`.
- Discover the Pi via `hostname -I` (on Pi) or `ping raspberrypi.local` (mDNS, on client).
- Connect from Linux/macOS/Windows 10+: `ssh pi@<ip-address>`.
- Connect from legacy Windows: use **PuTTY** (GUI) or `ssh` from PowerShell.
- File transfer: `scp`/`rsync` from CLI, **WinSCP** or `pscp` on Windows GUI clients.
- **Public-key authentication** is the secure, KTU-recommended method:
  - Generate: `ssh-keygen -t ed25519`
  - Push: `ssh-copy-id pi@<ip>`
  - Verify file permissions: `chmod 700 ~/.ssh` and `chmod 600 ~/.ssh/authorized_keys`.
- **Hardening:** Set `PasswordAuthentication no` and `PermitRootLogin no` in `/etc/ssh/sshd_config`, then `sudo systemctl restart ssh`.
- SSH key exchange uses **Diffie–Hellman (or ECDH)** for forward secrecy; user authentication uses **RSA/Ed25519** signatures.
- Troubleshooting: "*Connection refused*" → check `sshd` is running; "*Permission denied*" → check user/key/permissions; "*Host key verification failed*" → re-image or `ssh-keygen -R`.
- SSH is the **foundation** for every later KTU module: GPIO control, MQTT brokers, Flask servers, and OTA updates all rely on it.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creating Simple Raspberry Pi Application
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCIT504 - PROGRAMMING FOR INTERNET OF THINGS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Creating Simple Raspberry Pi Application # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Simple Raspberry Pi Application** refers to a lightweight, embedded software program developed and executed on the **Raspberry Pi (RPi)** single-board computer (SBC) that interfaces with physical hardware components — such as LEDs, sensors, buzzers, or actuators — through the **General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO)** pins, typically programmed using the **Python** language and libraries like `RPi.GPIO` or `gpiozero`. In the context of **Internet of Things (IoT)**, a Raspberry Pi application acts as the *edge node* or *gateway*, performing: - **Data acquisition** (sensing environmental parameters), - **Local computation** (decision-making at the edge), - **Actuation control** (driving physical devices), and - **Network communication** (publishing data to the cloud/MQTT broker). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic falls under **Module 4 – Raspberry Pi & Edge Programming** and tests the Course Outcomes **CO3 (Design IoT applications using Raspberry Pi and Arduino)** and **CO4 (Implement edge-side data handling and cloud offloading).** ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Mini-Brain Controlling a House" Analogy** > Imagine a Raspberry Pi as a **tiny, credit-card-sized brain** sitting on your desk. Just like a human brain controls the body: > - The **GPIO pins** are the *nerves* that connect to muscles (LEDs, motors) and senses (temperature, light). > - The **Python program** is the *thought* telling the brain what to do — "if it's dark, turn on the light." > - The **operating system (Raspberry Pi OS)** is the *consciousness* that keeps everything alive. > - The **network cable/Wi-Fi** is the *telephone line* letting the brain talk to other brains (cloud servers, other devices). > So, when we say "create a simple Raspberry Pi application," we essentially mean **writing a small Python script that makes this tiny brain sense the world, make decisions, and control things around it.** ## 1.3 Core Components of a Simple RPi Application | Component | Role in Application | Common Example | |---|---|---| | **Raspberry Pi Board** | Hardware platform (CPU + RAM + GPIO) | Raspberry Pi 4 Model B, Pi Zero 2 W | | **Power Supply** | Provides stable **5V / 3A** via USB-C | Official RPi 15W PSU | | **microSD Card** | Stores OS + user code (typically **16–64 GB**, Class 10) | SanDisk Extreme 32 GB | | **Raspberry Pi OS** | Linux-based operating system (Debian) | Bookworm, Bullseye | | **GPIO Header** | 40-pin interface for hardware | Pins 1–40 (BCM numbering) | | **Python Libraries** | Software abstraction for hardware | `RPi.GPIO`, `gpiozero`, `adafruit_*` | | **Sensors / Actuators** | Physical world interface | DHT22, LED, PIR, Relay | ## 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU Standard Glossary) - **GPIO (General Purpose Input/Output):** Configurable digital pins on the RPi header capable of reading (input) or driving (output) a **3.3 V** logic signal. *Hard limit: **3.3 V max** — exceeding this damages the SoC.* - **BCM Pin Numbering:** Broadcom chip-level pin reference (e.g., GPIO17). - **BOARD Pin Numbering:** Physical header pin number (e.g., Pin 11). - **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation):** Technique to simulate analog output by varying duty cycle (0–100 %). - **Edge Detection:** Triggering an action on a *rising* (LOW→HIGH) or *falling* (HIGH→LOW) transition. - **Pull-up / Pull-down Resistor:** Internal (or external) resistor that holds a pin at a known default state. > [!WARNING] > **RPi GPIO Voltage Warning:** The Raspberry Pi GPIO operates at **3.3 V logic**. Connecting a **5 V** Arduino-level signal directly to a GPIO pin will permanently damage the Broadcom SoC. Always use a **level shifter** for voltage translation. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 Architecture of a Typical RPi IoT Application A simple RPi IoT application can be decomposed into **four functional layers**, flowing from the physical world to the network: ### Layer 1 — Physical / Hardware Layer - Sensors (DHT22, LDR, PIR, Ultrasonic HC-SR04) generate analog/digital signals. - Actuators (LEDs, relays, motors, buzzers) receive digital commands. - Signal conditioning (resistors, voltage dividers) ensures compatibility with **3.3 V** logic. ### Layer 2 — Interface Layer (GPIO + Libraries) - Python libraries abstract low-level memory-mapped register access. - `RPi.GPIO` provides low-level pin control; `gpiozero` provides high-level object-oriented abstraction. - I²C, SPI, and UART buses allow multi-device communication (e.g., BME280 over I²C). ### Layer 3 — Application Logic Layer (Python Script) - Implements the **sense → decide → actuate** loop. - Uses standard Python constructs: `while True`, `if-else`, `try-except` for robustness. - May include `threading` or `asyncio` for concurrent operations. ### Layer 4 — Network / Cloud Layer - Publishes data via **MQTT** (`paho-mqtt`), **HTTP/REST** (`requests`), or **CoAP**. - Subscribes to commands for remote actuation (smart home, industrial control). > [!TIP] > **The "Sense-Decide-Act" Loop** is the *heart* of every IoT application. In a KTU exam, always draw this triangle when asked to design an RPi application. ## 2.2 GPIO Pin Operating Modes | Mode | Direction | Function | Python Configuration | |---|---|---|---| | **INPUT** | Read external signal | Button, sensor digital out | `GPIO.setup(pin, GPIO.IN)` | | **OUTPUT** | Drive external device | LED, buzzer, relay | `GPIO.setup(pin, GPIO.OUT)` | | **INPUT + PULL-UP** | Read with internal resistor | Tactile button (active-low) | `GPIO.setup(pin, GPIO.IN, pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_UP)` | | **INPUT + PULL-DOWN** | Read with internal resistor | Motion sensor | `GPIO.setup(pin, GPIO.IN, pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_DOWN)` | | **PWM OUTPUT** | Simulated analog | LED dimming, servo motor | `GPIO.PWM(pin, freq)` | ## 2.3 The Ohm's Law Cheat Sheet for RPi Circuits The single most important equation for any simple RPi application: $$V = I \times R$$ For an LED current-limiting resistor (the **most common** RPi circuit): $$R = \frac{V_{source} - V_{LED}}{I_{LED}}$$ > **Example:** For a red LED (V_LED = 2 V, I_LED = 20 mA) on a 3.3 V GPIO: > $$R = \frac{3.3 - 2.0}{0.020} = \frac{1.3}{0.020} = 65 \, \Omega$$ > Use the nearest standard value: **68 Ω** or **100 Ω** (safer). ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Reference Table | Parameter | Value / Range | Unit | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | GPIO Logic HIGH Voltage | **3.3** | V | Hard maximum; 5 V destroys pin | | GPIO Logic LOW Voltage | **0** | V | Ground reference | | Max Current per GPIO pin | **16** | mA | Recommended 2–4 mA continuous | | Max Total Current (all pins) | **50** | mA | Bank limit on 3V3 rail | | PWM Frequency Range | **1 – 1000** | Hz | Default for servo: **50 Hz** | | PWM Duty Cycle Resolution | **0 – 100** | % | Use `ChangeDutyCycle(n)` | | I²C Bus Speed | **100 / 400** | kHz | Standard / Fast mode | | SPI Clock Speed | Up to **125** | MHz | On Pi 4 with `spidev` | | UART Baud Rates | 9600, 115200 | bps | Default console: 115200 | ## 2.5 Software Stack — From Metal to Cloud A complete "simple application" typically uses the following layered software stack: - **Layer 0 — Kernel Drivers:** `/dev/gpiomem`, `/dev/i2c-1`, `/dev/spidev0.0` - **Layer 1 — Python Library:** `RPi.GPIO`, `gpiozero`, `adafruit-circuitpython-*` - **Layer 2 — User Script:** Custom `.py` file with `import` + `while True` loop - **Layer 3 — System Service:** `systemd` unit file for auto-start on boot - **Layer 4 — Network:** `paho-mqtt`, `requests`, `flask` (mini web server) ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > Simple RPi applications form the foundation of: > - **Smart agriculture** (auto-irrigation using soil moisture + relay), > - **Home automation** (voice/PIR-triggered lighting), > - **Weather stations** (BME280 + ThingSpeak cloud upload), > - **Industrial monitoring** (vibration + temperature trending), > - **Health IoT** (pulse sensor + MQTT to caregiver dashboard). > Every commercial IoT product starts from a *simple* RPi prototype. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Design Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — LED Blinking Application (Hello World of IoT) ### Problem Statement Design and develop a simple Raspberry Pi application that blinks an LED connected to **GPIO 17 (physical pin 11)** at an interval of **1 second** (0.5 s ON, 0.5 s OFF) using Python. Draw the circuit and write the complete program. ### Step 1: Hardware Specification (Circuit Design) | Component | Value / Part Number | Quantity | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | Raspberry Pi (any model) | Pi 4 / Pi 3 / Zero | 1 | Controller | | LED | 5 mm red, V_f = 2 V | 1 | Visual output | | Resistor | **330 Ω** (¼ W) | 1 | Current limit | | Breadboard + jumper wires | — | 1 set | Prototyping | **Calculating the current-limiting resistor:** $$R = \frac{V_{source} - V_{LED}}{I_{LED}} = \frac{3.3 - 2.0}{0.010} = \frac{1.3}{0.010} = 130 \, \Omega$$ (We choose **330 Ω** for safety → I_LED ≈ 3.94 mA, well within safe range.) **Circuit diagram (textual schematic):** ``` GPIO 17 (Pin 11) ──[ 330 Ω ]──►|─── GND (Pin 6) LED (Anode → Cathode) ``` ### Step 2: Software Flow (Algorithm in Pseudocode) ``` BEGIN IMPORT RPi.GPIO as GPIO IMPORT time SET pin 17 as OUTPUT LOOP forever: SET pin 17 = HIGH (LED ON) SLEEP 0.5 seconds SET pin 17 = LOW (LED OFF) SLEEP 0.5 seconds END ``` ### Step 3: Complete Python Program ```python import RPi.GPIO as GPIO import time LED_PIN = 17 GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM) # Use Broadcom chip numbering GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT) # Configure pin as output try: while True: GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.HIGH) # LED ON time.sleep(0.5) # Wait 0.5 s GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.LOW) # LED OFF time.sleep(0.5) # Wait 0.5 s except KeyboardInterrupt: pass # Catch Ctrl+C gracefully finally: GPIO.cleanup() # Reset all pins to safe state ``` ### Step 4: Execution Logic Explained - `GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)` → tells the library to interpret pin numbers as Broadcom labels (GPIO17 = physical pin 11). - `GPIO.setup()` → configures the SoC's internal pin multiplexer to output mode. - `GPIO.output(pin, HIGH)` → drives the pin to **3.3 V**, forward-biasing the LED. - `GPIO.output(pin, LOW)` → pulls the pin to **0 V**, turning the LED off. - `GPIO.cleanup()` → critical for preventing pin damage if the script crashes. > [!TIP] > **Always use `try/except/finally` in any RPi application.** The `finally` block calls `GPIO.cleanup()` which resets every pin used by your script to a high-impedance (safe) state — this is **non-negotiable** in production code. ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Push-Button Controlled LED (Sense-Decide-Act) ### Problem Statement Design a Raspberry Pi application where pressing a push-button (connected to **GPIO 27**, active-LOW) toggles an LED (connected to **GPIO 17**) ON/OFF. The button must be debounced for **200 ms**. ### Step 1: Hardware Connection Table | Pin (BCM) | Physical Pin | Direction | Component | Wire Color (Suggested) | |---|---|---|---|---| | GPIO17 | Pin 11 | OUTPUT | LED + 330 Ω → GND | Yellow | | GPIO27 | Pin 13 | INPUT (PUD_UP) | Push-button → GND | Blue | | GND | Pin 6 | — | Common ground | Black | ### Step 2: Application Logic (Flow Description) 1. Initialize GPIO17 as OUTPUT, GPIO27 as INPUT with pull-up enabled. 2. Read button state continuously. 3. If button is pressed (reads LOW due to active-LOW wiring) **and** debounce timer has elapsed: - Toggle LED state (use a boolean flag `led_state`). - Update GPIO17 output. - Update debounce timestamp. 4. Repeat every **50 ms** (polling interval) to balance responsiveness and CPU load. ### Step 3: Complete Python Program ```python import RPi.GPIO as GPIO import time LED_PIN = 17 BTN_PIN = 27 DEBOUNCE_MS = 200 GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM) GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT) GPIO.setup(BTN_PIN, GPIO.IN, pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_UP) led_state = False last_press = 0 try: while True: now = time.time() if GPIO.input(BTN_PIN) == GPIO.LOW: # Active-LOW press if (now - last_press) * 1000 > DEBOUNCE_MS: led_state = not led_state # Toggle GPIO.output(LED_PIN, led_state) last_press = now print("LED state:", "ON" if led_state else "OFF") time.sleep(0.05) # 50 ms polling except KeyboardInterrupt: pass finally: GPIO.cleanup() ``` ### Step 4: Analytical Insights - **Why debounce?** Mechanical push-buttons physically *bounce* between HIGH and LOW for ~5–20 ms. Without debouncing, a single press would be registered as multiple presses. - **Why polling instead of interrupts?** Polling at 50 ms is simple and reliable for human-scale interaction. Interrupts (`GPIO.add_event_detect`) are better for high-frequency or time-critical events. - **Edge-triggered interrupt alternative** (cleaner design): ```python def toggle_led(channel): global led_state led_state = not led_state GPIO.output(LED_PIN, led_state) GPIO.add_event_detect(BTN_PIN, GPIO.FALLING, callback=toggle_led, bouncetime=200) ``` ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — DHT22 Temperature Logger with MQTT (Full IoT Pipeline) ### Problem Statement Create a Raspberry Pi IoT application that reads **temperature and humidity** from a **DHT22** sensor every **10 seconds** and publishes the data to an **MQTT broker** on topic `home/livingroom/dht22`. ### Step 1: Hardware | Component | Connection | |---|---| | DHT22 DATA pin | GPIO 4 (Pin 7) | | DHT22 VCC | 3.3 V (Pin 1) | | DHT22 GND | GND (Pin 6) | | 10 kΩ pull-up | Between DATA and VCC | ### Step 2: Python Program ```python import Adafruit_DHT import paho.mqtt.client as mqtt import time, json SENSOR = Adafruit_DHT.DHT22 PIN = 4 BROKER = "test.mosquitto.org" TOPIC = "home/livingroom/dht22" client = mqtt.Client() client.connect(BROKER, 1883, 60) try: while True: humidity, temperature = Adafruit_DHT.read_retry(SENSOR, PIN) if humidity is not None and temperature is not None: payload = json.dumps({ "temp_c": round(temperature, 2), "hum_pct": round(humidity, 2), "ts": time.time() }) client.publish(TOPIC, payload) print("Published:", payload) else: print("Sensor read failure — retrying") time.sleep(10) except KeyboardInterrupt: pass finally: client.disconnect() ``` ## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Flask Web Server for Remote LED Control ### Complete Application ```python from flask import Flask, render_template_string import RPi.GPIO as GPIO app = Flask(__name__) LED_PIN = 17 GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM) GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT) state = {"led": False} HTML = """

KTU IoT LED Control

Current state: {{ s }}

""" @app.route("/") def index(): return render_template_string(HTML, s="ON" if state["led"] else "OFF") @app.route("/toggle", methods=["POST"]) def toggle(): state["led"] = not state["led"] GPIO.output(LED_PIN, state["led"]) return index() if __name__ == "__main__": app.run(host="0.0.0.0", port=5000) ``` **Mathematical model — request handling latency:** $$T_{response} = T_{network} + T_{flask} + T_{GPIO} \approx 5 \text{ ms} + 2 \text{ ms} + 0.1 \text{ ms} \approx 7.1 \text{ ms}$$ This is well within real-time acceptability for human-controlled IoT. ## 3.5 Comparative Analysis Matrix | Feature | `RPi.GPIO` | `gpiozero` | `pigpio` (remote) | |---|---|---|---| | Abstraction Level | Low | High | Medium | | Learning Curve | Moderate | Easy | Moderate | | Pin Modes | Manual | Auto-configured | Manual | | PWM Support | Software | Hardware + Software | Hardware (DMA) | | Remote GPIO | No | No | **Yes (network)** | | Threading | Manual | Built-in | Manual | | Best For | Low-level control | Rapid prototyping | Distributed Pi clusters | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Architecture — Generic RPi IoT Application ```mermaid flowchart TB PWR[Power Supply 5V 3A] --> RPI[Raspberry Pi SoC] RPI --> OS[Raspberry Pi OS Linux] OS --> PYLIB[Python Libraries RPi.GPIO gpiozero] PYLIB --> SENSE[Sensors DHT22 PIR LDR] SENSE -->|digital analog| PYLIB PYLIB --> DECIDE{Decision Logic Sense Decide Act} DECIDE -->|command| ACT[Actuators LED Relay Motor Buzzer] DECIDE -->|publish| NET[Network MQTT HTTP WebSocket] NET --> CLOUD[Cloud Server AWS IoT ThingSpeak] CLOUD -->|remote command| DECIDE ``` ## 4.2 GPIO Pin Mode State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Unconfigured Unconfigured --> InputMode : GPIO.setup pin IN Unconfigured --> OutputMode : GPIO.setup pin OUT InputMode --> InputPullUp : pull_up_down PUD_UP InputMode --> InputPullDown : pull_up_down PUD_DOWN InputPullUp --> EdgeDetect : add_event_detect InputPullDown --> EdgeDetect : add_event_detect OutputMode --> PWMMode : GPIO.PWM pin freq PWMMode --> OutputMode : stop PWM EdgeDetect --> InputMode : remove_event_detect InputMode --> Unconfigured : GPIO.cleanup OutputMode --> Unconfigured : GPIO.cleanup PWMMode --> Unconfigured : GPIO.cleanup Unconfigured --> [*] ``` ## 4.3 LED Blinking Program Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A([Start]) --> B[Import RPi.GPIO time] B --> C[Set BCM Mode] C --> D[Setup Pin 17 as OUTPUT] D --> E{Loop True} E --> F[Pin 17 HIGH] F --> G[Sleep 0.5s] G --> H[Pin 17 LOW] H --> I[Sleep 0.5s] I --> E E -. Ctrl+C .-> J[Finally cleanup] J --> K([Exit Safely]) ``` ## 4.4 Button-Controlled LED — Event Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User participant B as Push Button GPIO27 participant R as Raspberry Pi participant L as LED GPIO17 U->>B: Press B-->>R: Falling Edge LOW R->>R: Debounce 200ms check alt Valid press R->>L: Toggle Output L-->>U: Visual Feedback else Bounce ignored R->>R: Discard end Note over R,L: finally block GPIO.cleanup on exit ``` ## 4.5 Full IoT Pipeline — Sensor to Cloud ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PHYS [Physical World] S1[DHT22 Temp Humidity] S2[LDR Light] S3[PIR Motion] end subgraph RPI [Raspberry Pi Edge Node] GPIO[GPIO Pins 4 17 27] PY[Python Script] MQTT_CLIENT[MQTT Client] end subgraph CLOUD [Cloud Tier] BROKER[MQTT Broker Mosquitto] DB[Time Series DB InfluxDB] DASH[Grafana Dashboard] APP[Mobile App] end S1 --> GPIO S2 --> GPIO S3 --> GPIO GPIO --> PY PY --> MQTT_CLIENT MQTT_CLIENT -->|publish| BROKER BROKER --> DB DB --> DASH BROKER --> APP APP -->|subscribe| BROKER BROKER -->|control commands| MQTT_CLIENT MQTT_CLIENT --> PY PY --> GPIO GPIO --> A1[Relay Light] GPIO --> A2[Buzzer Alarm] ``` ## 4.6 Systemd Auto-Start Service — Boot Sequence ```mermaid flowchart TB BOOT[Pi Power On] --> KERNEL[Linux Kernel Boot] KERNEL --> SYSTEMD[systemd PID 1] SYSTEMD --> MOUNT[Mount microSD rootfs] SYSTEMD --> NETUP[Network Manager bring up wlan0] SYSTEMD --> SVC[Load myapp.service] SVC --> EXEC[ExecStart usr bin python3 home pi app.py] EXEC --> APP[Python Application Running] APP --> GPIOINIT[GPIO.setup all pins] GPIOINIT --> MAINLOOP[Enter Main while loop] MAINLOOP -. watchdog .-> SVC ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **List any three important features of the Raspberry Pi board that make it suitable for IoT applications.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Integrated GPIO header (40-pin):** Direct interface to sensors/actuators without additional microcontroller. 2. **Built-in networking:** Wi-Fi and Ethernet on-board enable cloud connectivity out-of-the-box. 3. **Linux-based OS (Raspberry Pi OS):** Supports full networking stack (TCP/IP, MQTT, HTTP), multi-tasking, and Python libraries for rapid prototyping. 4. *(Bonus)* Low cost (~$35–60) and compact form factor (credit-card size) make it ideal for edge deployment. ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **What is the difference between BCM and BOARD pin numbering in Raspberry Pi GPIO programming? Give one example.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | BCM | BOARD | |---|---|---| | Full Form | Broadcom SoC channel | Physical header position | | Reference | Internal chip pin | Physical pin on the 40-pin header | | Example | GPIO17 | Physical Pin 11 | In `RPi.GPIO`, the choice is made via `GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)` or `GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BOARD)`. The **same physical pin** has **two different numbers** depending on the mode — confusing beginners but offering flexibility. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > Common mistakes students make in this topic (and lose 2–4 marks each): > 1. Forgetting to call `GPIO.cleanup()` in the `finally` block → pin lock-up on next run. > 2. Using the **wrong numbering** (BCM vs BOARD) and writing the wrong pin number. > 3. Exceeding **3.3 V** on GPIO pins (5 V sensor directly connected = burnt board). > 4. Skipping **pull-up/pull-down** resistor configuration for floating inputs (causes random LED triggers). > 5. No `try/except` for `KeyboardInterrupt` → script must be killed via `kill -9`. > 6. Writing **incorrect PWM frequencies** (e.g., 50 Hz for LED instead of 1000 Hz). > Always present: (i) **circuit diagram**, (ii) **complete executable code**, (iii) **algorithm steps**, (iv) **applications**. ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified] **a)** Design and explain a Raspberry Pi application to **read the temperature and humidity from a DHT22 sensor** and display the values on the terminal. Draw the circuit diagram and write the complete Python program using `Adafruit_DHT` library. **(7 Marks)** **b)** Extend the above application to **publish the sensor data to an MQTT broker** every 10 seconds. Explain the role of MQTT in IoT. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Circuit Diagram (textual):** ``` VCC (3.3V, Pin 1) ──┬── VCC of DHT22 │ [10kΩ pull-up] │ GPIO4 (Pin 7) ───────┴── DATA pin of DHT22 GND (Pin 6) ──────────── GND of DHT22 ``` **Algorithm:** 1. Import `Adafruit_DHT` and `time` modules. 2. Define sensor type as `DHT22` and data pin as GPIO4. 3. Loop indefinitely: call `read_retry()` (handles sensor's timing-sensitive protocol). 4. Print temperature in °C and humidity in %. 5. Handle `KeyboardInterrupt` for graceful exit. **Python Program:** ```python import Adafruit_DHT import time SENSOR = Adafruit_DHT.DHT22 PIN = 4 print("KTU DHT22 Reader — Press Ctrl+C to exit") try: while True: humidity, temperature = Adafruit_DHT.read_retry(SENSOR, PIN) if humidity is not None and temperature is not None: print(f"Temp = {temperature:0.1f}°C Humidity = {humidity:0.1f}%") else: print("Sensor failure — check wiring") time.sleep(2) except KeyboardInterrupt: print("\nExiting...") ``` **Mark Split:** Circuit (2) + Algorithm (1) + Code (3) + Explanation (1) = **7 Marks** --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Role of MQTT in IoT:** - **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** is a lightweight **publish/subscribe** messaging protocol designed for constrained devices and unreliable networks. - Uses a **broker** (e.g., Mosquitto) as a central hub; clients **publish** to topics or **subscribe** to receive messages. - Ideal for IoT due to: low bandwidth (~2 bytes header), QoS levels (0/1/2), retained messages, and last-will-and-testament for device liveness. **Architecture:** ``` [RPi Publisher] ──publish──▶ [MQTT Broker Mosquitto] ──deliver──▶ [Subscriber / Cloud] topic: home/livingroom/dht22 ``` **Extended Python Program:** ```python import Adafruit_DHT import paho.mqtt.client as mqtt import time, json SENSOR = Adafruit_DHT.DHT22 PIN = 4 BROKER = "test.mosquitto.org" PORT = 1883 TOPIC = "ktu/iot/dht22" client = mqtt.Client("rpi_publisher_01") client.connect(BROKER, PORT, 60) client.loop_start() print("Publishing to MQTT broker...") try: while True: h, t = Adafruit_DHT.read_retry(SENSOR, PIN) if h is not None and t is not None: payload = json.dumps({ "temperature_c": round(t, 2), "humidity_pct": round(h, 2), "timestamp": time.time() }) client.publish(TOPIC, payload, qos=1) print("→", payload) time.sleep(10) except KeyboardInterrupt: client.loop_stop() client.disconnect() ``` **Mark Split:** MQTT concept (2) + Architecture (1) + Code (3) + Explanation (1) = **7 Marks** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified] **a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a Raspberry Pi-based smart home lighting system**. Identify the sensors, actuators, and communication protocol. **(7 Marks)** **b)** Write a complete Python program to implement the system using **Flask web server** so that the user can toggle the room light via a mobile browser. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Block Diagram:** ``` [PIR Motion Sensor] ──► GPIO17 [LDR Light Sensor] ──► GPIO27 (via ADC or voltage divider) │ ▼ [Raspberry Pi — Python Script] │ ┌─────────────┼─────────────┐ ▼ ▼ ▼ GPIO22 (Relay1) GPIO23 (Relay2) GPIO24 (Buzzer) │ │ │ [Room Light 1] [Room Light 2] [Alarm] ``` **System Components Table:** | Subsystem | Component | Function | |---|---|---| | Input Sensor 1 | PIR (HC-SR501) | Detect human presence | | Input Sensor 2 | LDR + Voltage Divider | Detect ambient light | | Actuator 1 | 5V Relay Module | Switch AC bulb | | Actuator 2 | Piezo Buzzer | Audible alert | | Controller | Raspberry Pi 4 | Edge decision-making | | Communication | Wi-Fi + HTTP (Flask) | Remote control via phone | | Protocol | MQTT or REST | Cloud optional sync | **Working Principle:** - If **PIR = HIGH** *and* **LDR value < threshold** (dark room) → turn ON the light via relay. - If user toggles from mobile web app → override the sensor decision. - All events logged to a local SQLite/MySQL database for analytics. **Mark Split:** Block diagram (2) + Components table (2) + Working (2) + Protocol choice (1) = **7 Marks** --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Complete Flask + GPIO Program:** ```python from flask import Flask, request, render_template_string import RPi.GPIO as GPIO import sqlite3 from datetime import datetime app = Flask(__name__) LIGHT_RELAY = 22 PIR_PIN = 17 LDR_PIN = 27 GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM) GPIO.setup(LIGHT_RELAY, GPIO.OUT) GPIO.setup(PIR_PIN, GPIO.IN, pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_UP) GPIO.setup(LDR_PIN, GPIO.IN) GPIO.output(LIGHT_RELAY, GPIO.LOW) db = sqlite3.connect("smart_home.db", check_same_thread=False) db.execute("CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS log(ts TEXT, state TEXT)") PAGE = """ KTU Smart Light

🏠 KTU Smart Home Light Control

Current state: {{ s }}

Access from phone: http://<pi-ip>:5000

""" state = "OFF" @app.route("/") def home(): return render_template_string(PAGE, s=state) @app.route("/toggle", methods=["POST"]) def toggle(): global state action = request.form["action"] state = action GPIO.output(LIGHT_RELAY, GPIO.HIGH if action == "ON" else GPIO.LOW) db.execute("INSERT INTO log VALUES (?,?)", (datetime.now().isoformat(), state)) db.commit() return home() if __name__ == "__main__": try: app.run(host="0.0.0.0", port=5000) finally: GPIO.cleanup() db.close() ``` **Mathematical latency analysis (good-to-write for full marks):** $$T_{round\_trip} = 2 \times T_{network} + T_{flask} + T_{GPIO} \approx 2(3) + 5 + 0.1 = 11.1 \text{ ms}$$ This is excellent for human-perceived real-time control (<100 ms threshold). **Mark Split:** Code structure (3) + GPIO integration (2) + DB logging (1) + Explanation (1) = **7 Marks** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Creating Simple Raspberry Pi Applications** - **RPi is a Linux SBC, not a microcontroller** — it runs a full OS and is best for high-level edge processing. - **GPIO voltage is 3.3 V — never apply 5 V** to a pin directly. - **Two numbering schemes:** BCM (chip) vs BOARD (physical) — pick ONE and stick to it. - **Always begin scripts with:** `import RPi.GPIO as GPIO`, `GPIO.setmode(...)`, `GPIO.setup(...)`. - **Always end scripts with:** `GPIO.cleanup()` inside a `finally` block to release pin ownership. - **Current-limiting resistor for LED:** use $R = \frac{V_{source} - V_{LED}}{I_{LED}}$ → typically **220 Ω – 1 kΩ** for 3.3 V. - **Debouncing buttons:** mechanical switches need **5–200 ms** software debounce or hardware RC filter. - **Pull-up resistors:** use `GPIO.PUD_UP` for active-LOW buttons; `GPIO.PUD_DOWN` for active-HIGH. - **PWM uses software timing** in `RPi.GPIO` → precision degrades above ~1 kHz. For servo motors, use exactly **50 Hz**. - **For multi-device buses:** I²C (2 wires, addressable, up to 127 devices), SPI (4+ wires, faster), UART (point-to-point). - **MQTT** is the de-facto IoT protocol — uses **publish/subscribe** with a broker; topic design matters (e.g., `home/room/sensor`). - **Flask** turns the Pi into a mini web server — bind to `0.0.0.0` for LAN/mobile access. - **Systemd service** files auto-start the app on every boot — use `sudo systemctl enable myapp.service`. - **Logging:** always use `try/except` for `KeyboardInterrupt` and log failures to file/DB. - **Typical exam answer structure:** (1) Aim, (2) Components table, (3) Circuit diagram, (4) Algorithm, (5) Code, (6) Output, (7) Applications. - **Common libraries to remember:** `RPi.GPIO`, `gpiozero`, `adafruit-circuitpython-*`, `paho-mqtt`, `flask`, `requests`. - **Final memory aid:** *Sense → Decide → Actuate → Communicate* — the four pillars of any RPi IoT app. ---

topics.title: Project on Java: Bill of Materials
Getting Started with NetBeans
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT504 - PROGRAMMING FOR INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Getting Started with NetBeans

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

**NetBeans IDE** (Integrated Development Environment) is an open-source, modular, and extensible software development platform officially maintained by the **Apache Software Foundation** under the Apache License 2.0. It provides developers with a unified graphical interface to write, compile, debug, test, and deploy applications primarily in **Java**, but also supports **C/C++**, **PHP**, **HTML5**, **JavaScript**, and languages relevant to **IoT** such as embedded Java (Java ME) and scripting bindings used with Raspberry Pi / Arduino workflows.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For the course **PBCIT504**, NetBeans is treated as the *canonical desktop IDE* used to develop, simulate, and package Java-based IoT applications that communicate with sensors, actuators, and cloud services via protocols such as **MQTT**, **CoAP**, and **HTTP**.

From an architectural standpoint, NetBeans is built upon a **NetBeans Platform** — a generic Swing-based desktop application framework on which any large-scale desktop application can be built. The IDE itself is simply one such application built atop this platform.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of NetBeans as an **"electronics workbench for a programmer"**.

- A hardware engineer has a **workbench** with a soldering iron, multimeter, oscilloscope, and screwdrivers — all in one place.
- A programmer using NetBeans has a **code editor, compiler, debugger, project explorer, terminal, GUI designer, and version control** — all wired into a single dashboard.

When you "open NetBeans," you are not opening one program; you are opening an **orchestra of tools** coordinated by a central runtime called the **NetBeans Platform**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy (continued):** If writing code is like cooking, then:
> - **Notepad** = an empty kitchen with only a gas stove.
> - **NetBeans** = a fully-equipped modular kitchen with pre-measured ingredients (templates), smart timers (build automation), recipe books (documentation), and even a self-cleaning sink (auto-formatting & refactoring).

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

For KTU lab examinations and project work, the following **standard configuration metrics** are considered baseline:

| Parameter | Recommended Value |
|---|---|
| **JDK Version** | **JDK 8 / JDK 11 / JDK 17 LTS** |
| **Minimum RAM** | **2 GB** (4 GB recommended) |
| **Minimum Disk Space** | **~1.5 GB** for IDE + JDK |
| **Supported OS** | Windows, Linux, macOS, Solaris |
| **Default Port for Java DB** | **1527** |
| **Build Tool** | **Apache Ant** / **Maven** / **Gradle** |
| **Current Stable Release Line** | **Apache NetBeans 18+** (2024 cycle) |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** The historical name **"Oracle NetBeans IDE"** was used during 2010–2016 when Oracle sponsored it. After Oracle handed it to Apache in 2016, the product was rebranded to **"Apache NetBeans"**. Either name is acceptable in answer sheets, but **Apache NetBeans** is the up-to-date 2024-scheme terminology.

## 1.4 Why NetBeans for IoT Programming?

In the context of **PBCIT504**, NetBeans is preferred over plain text editors because:

1. **Java ME (Micro Edition) Plugin Support** — historically used to develop embedded IoT applications for constrained devices.
2. **Palette-based GUI Designer** — drag-and-drop widgets for IoT dashboards (using JavaFX Swing).
3. **Built-in MQTT Client Libraries** — easy to add via Maven/Ant libraries.
4. **Integration with TinkerForge, Pi4J, and jFrog** — for Raspberry Pi and sensor programming.
5. **Debugging** of network calls — vital when developing IoT communication logic.
6. **Project Templates** — pre-built IoT starter templates accelerate prototyping.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of NetBeans Architecture

NetBeans follows a **modular architecture** rooted in the OSGi-like NetBeans Module System. The entire IDE is a collection of cooperating **modules (.nbm files)** running inside the **NetBeans Runtime Container**.

### 2.1.1 Core Layers (Bottom-Up Logic)

- **Layer 1 — OS / Hardware Layer:** The host operating system provides JVM support.
- **Layer 2 — JDK / JVM Layer:** The Java Runtime executes NetBeans bytecode. NetBeans itself is a Java application.
- **Layer 3 — NetBeans Platform / Runtime Container:** Provides lookup, module lifecycle, file system abstraction, and window system.
- **Layer 4 — NetBeans Modules:** Independent JARs (Java ARchives) that contribute specific capabilities — e.g., `org.netbeans.modules.java.source`, `org.netbeans.modules.debugger.jpda`.
- **Layer 5 — IDE Functionality (User-Visible Features):** Editor, debugger, profiler, GUI builder, version control, project manager.
- **Layer 6 — User Plugins & Third-Party Extensions:** Installable via Plugin Manager.

### 2.1.2 The 'Why' Behind the Modular Design

- **Hot Reload:** Modules can be enabled/disabled without restarting the IDE.
- **Lightweight Deployment:** Only required modules load, reducing RAM footprint.
- **Extensibility:** Third-party developers (e.g., for IoT hardware vendors) can ship their own `.nbm` plugins.
- **Decoupling:** A bug in the debugger does not crash the editor.

## 2.2 Key Components Inside NetBeans

| Component | Functional Role | IoT Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Projects Window** | Hierarchical view of source files, libraries, dependencies | Manages IoT project folders cleanly |
| **Files Window** | Underlying filesystem view | Direct access to config files like `pom.xml` |
| **Navigator** | Outlines class members & methods | Quick navigation in large IoT handler classes |
| **Editor Window** | Syntax-highlighted code editor with auto-completion | Speeds up writing MQTT/CoAP client code |
| **Palette (GUI Builder)** | Drag-and-drop widget palette | Designing IoT dashboard interfaces |
| **Output Window** | Compilation logs, runtime output | Monitoring serial-port sensor data |
| **Debugger Window** | Breakpoints, watches, call stack | Tracing MQTT callback execution |
| **Services Window** | Databases, servers, HTTP clients | Connecting to cloud IoT brokers |
| **Tasks Window** | TODO markers, compiler warnings | Tracking project milestones |

## 2.3 Project Anatomy in NetBeans

Every NetBeans project follows a **standardized folder hierarchy**. Knowing this is critical for KTU practical exams.

```
MyIoTProject/
├── build.xml              ← Apache Ant build script
├── manifest.mf            ← JAR manifest metadata
├── nbproject/             ← NetBeans-specific metadata
│   ├── build-impl.xml
│   ├── genfiles.properties
│   ├── project.properties
│   └── project.xml
├── src/                   ← Java source files (.java)
│   └── iot/
│       └── SensorClient.java
├── test/                  ← JUnit test classes
├── lib/                   ← Third-party JARs (e.g., MQTT library)
├── dist/                  ← Generated output (JAR, Javadoc)
└── README.md
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall to Avoid:** Do **not** manually edit files inside `nbproject/` unless you know exactly what you're doing — these are auto-generated configuration files. Always use the **Project Properties** dialog (right-click project → Properties).

## 2.4 Build Tools Supported by NetBeans

NetBeans abstracts build complexity through three automation tools:

1. **Apache Ant** — Default in older versions. Uses `build.xml`.
2. **Apache Maven** — Uses `pom.xml` (Project Object Model). Preferred for IoT projects due to dependency management.
3. **Gradle** — Uses `build.gradle`. Lightweight and modern.

### 2.4.1 Maven Coordinates (Exam-Favorite Concept)

A Maven library is identified by three coordinates:

$$
\text{GAV} = (\text{GroupId}, \text{ArtifactId}, \text{Version})
$$

Example for Eclipse Paho MQTT (an IoT messaging library):

```xml
<dependency>
    <groupId>org.eclipse.paho</groupId>
    <artifactId>org.eclipse.paho.client.mqttv3</artifactId>
    <version>1.2.5</version>
</dependency>
```

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Use Case for NetBeans |
|---|---|
| **Smart Home IoT** | JavaFX dashboards controlling MQTT-enabled lights & thermostats |
| **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** | Modbus/OPC-UA clients reading sensor data from PLCs |
| **Healthcare IoT** | Wearable device data aggregation apps |
| **Smart Agriculture** | Field-sensor network controllers |
| **Edge Computing** | On-device analytics gateways using Java Embedded |
| **Academic / KTU Labs** | Submitting working IoT Java projects for evaluation |

# 3. Step-by-Step Procedural Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section provides **exhaustive, step-by-step walkthroughs** for all foundational NetBeans operations required in KTU Module 4. Every action is explicitly enumerated — no shortcuts, no skipped steps.

## 3.1 Walkthrough 1 — Installing Apache NetBeans (Full Procedure)

### Step 1 — Verify Java Installation
Open a terminal / command prompt and execute:

```bash
java -version
javac -version
```

If a Java version is displayed (e.g., `javac 17.0.7`), Java is already installed. If not, download and install **JDK 11** or **JDK 17 LTS** from the official Oracle or Adoptium (Eclipse Temurin) website.

### Step 2 — Download Apache NetBeans
Navigate to the official Apache NetBeans download page and download the latest stable **Apache NetBeans 18.x** bundle. Choose the installer matching your operating system:
- **Windows:** `.exe` installer
- **Linux:** `.sh` installer or `.zip` archive
- **macOS:** `.dmg` installer

### Step 3 — Run the Installer
On Windows, double-click the `.exe` file. On Linux, execute:

```bash
chmod +x Apache-NetBeans-18.0-bin-linux-x64.sh
sudo ./Apache-NetBeans-18.0-bin-linux-x64.sh
```

### Step 4 — Accept License Agreement
The installer displays the Apache License 2.0. Click **Next → Accept → Next**.

### Step 5 — Choose Installation Directory
Default location on Windows:
$$
\text{C:\Program Files\NetBeans-18.0}
$$
Accept the default or browse to a custom path.

### Step 6 — Select JDK
The installer auto-detects installed JDKs. Ensure the correct JDK (matching your `java -version` output) is selected. Click **Next**.

### Step 7 — Install GlassFish and Apache Tomcat (Optional)
For IoT projects with web-based dashboards, you may optionally install:
- **GlassFish Server 7.x** — full Java EE / Jakarta EE server
- **Apache Tomcat 10.x** — lightweight servlet container

### Step 8 — Complete Installation
Click **Install**. Wait for the progress bar to complete. Click **Finish**.

### Step 9 — Launch NetBeans
Start NetBeans via:
- **Windows:** Start Menu → Apache NetBeans → Apache NetBeans IDE 18.0
- **Linux:** Terminal → `netbeans`
- **macOS:** Applications → NetBeans.app

## 3.2 Walkthrough 2 — Creating Your First IoT Java Project

### Step 1 — Invoke the New Project Wizard
Menu path: **File → New Project…** (Shortcut: `Ctrl + Shift + N` on Windows/Linux, `⌘ + Shift + N` on macOS).

### Step 2 — Choose Project Category
From the **Categories** panel, select **Java with Maven** (or **Java with Ant** for legacy). From the **Projects** panel, select **Java Application**. Click **Next >**.

### Step 3 — Configure Project Metadata
Enter the following details:

$$
\text{Project Name} = \text{KTUIoTDemo}
$$

$$
\text{Project Location} = \text{C:\Users\Student\NetBeansProjects}
$$

$$
\text{Group ID} = \text{com.ktu.iot}
$$

$$
\text{Version} = \text{1.0-SNAPSHOT}
$$

$$
\text{Package} = \text{com.ktu.iot.sensor}
$$

$$
\text{Create Main Class} = \text{checked}
$$

Click **Finish**.

### Step 4 — Explore the Generated Structure
NetBeans creates the folder hierarchy described in **Section 2.3**. The IDE opens `KTUIoTDemo.java` inside the editor.

### Step 5 — Add an MQTT Dependency
Right-click **Dependencies** in the Projects window → **Add Dependency…** → search for `org.eclipse.paho.client.mqttv3` → select version **1.2.5** → **Add**.

This auto-edits your `pom.xml` as shown in **Section 2.4.1**.

### Step 6 — Write Your First IoT Code
Replace the contents of `KTUIoTDemo.java` with the following:

```java
package com.ktu.iot.sensor;

import org.eclipse.paho.client.mqttv3.MqttClient;
import org.eclipse.paho.client.mqttv3.MqttMessage;

public class KTUIoTDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        // Step 1: Define the broker URL (HiveMQ public test broker)
        String broker  = "tcp://broker.hivemq.com:1883";
        String clientId = "KTUStudentClient_" + System.currentTimeMillis();

        // Step 2: Create an MQTT client instance
        MqttClient client = new MqttClient(broker, clientId);

        // Step 3: Connect to the broker
        client.connect();
        System.out.println("Connected to MQTT Broker: " + broker);

        // Step 4: Publish a simulated sensor reading
        String topic   = "ktu/iot/sensor/temperature";
        String payload = "{\"device\":\"KTU-LAB-01\",\"temp\":28.5}";
        MqttMessage message = new MqttMessage(payload.getBytes());
        message.setQos(1);

        client.publish(topic, message);
        System.out.println("Published: " + payload);

        // Step 5: Disconnect cleanly
        client.disconnect();
        System.out.println("Disconnected from broker.");
    }
}
```

### Step 7 — Build the Project
Press **F11** or right-click project → **Build**. The output window displays:

```
BUILD SUCCESS
Total time: 3 seconds
```

### Step 8 — Run the Project
Press **F6** or right-click project → **Run**. The Output window displays:

```
Connected to MQTT Broker: tcp://broker.hivemq.com:1883
Published: {"device":"KTU-LAB-01","temp":28.5}
Disconnected from broker.
```

## 3.3 Walkthrough 3 — Using the Debugger

### Step 1 — Set a Breakpoint
Click on the left margin (line-number gutter) next to `client.connect();`. A red square appears, indicating a breakpoint.

### Step 2 — Start Debugging
Press **Ctrl + F5** or click the **Debug Project** button. Execution halts at the breakpoint.

### Step 3 — Inspect Variables
Hover over the `broker` variable. The IDE displays its runtime value in a tooltip.

### Step 4 — Step Through Code
Use **F8 (Step Over)**, **F7 (Step Into)**, and **Ctrl + F8 (Step Out)** to navigate line-by-line.

### Step 5 — View Call Stack
Open **Window → Debugging → Call Stack** to observe the execution hierarchy.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — NetBeans vs Other IDEs

| Feature | **NetBeans** | **Eclipse** | **IntelliJ IDEA** | **VS Code** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **First-Release Year** | **1996** (as Xelfi) | 2001 | 2001 | 2015 |
| **Primary Language** | Java | Java (multi) | Java/Kotlin (multi) | Multi-language |
| **GUI Builder** | **Built-in (Matisse)** | WindowBuilder plugin | Scene Builder external | Limited |
| **Maven Support** | **Native** | Native | Native | Extension |
| **Memory Footprint** | Medium | Medium-High | High | Low |
| **IoT Plugin Ecosystem** | Java ME, Pi4J | Comparable | Smaller | Large (Arduino) |
| **License** | **Apache 2.0** | EPL 2.0 | Community/Commercial | MIT |
| **KTU Recommendation** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ⚠ Partial | ⚠ Not recommended for exams |

## 3.5 Common Pitfalls During KTU Practical Exams

1. **Forgetting to set the JDK** — NetBeans may use JRE instead of JDK, causing `javac` errors.
2. **Wrong package declaration** — Java source file location must match the `package` statement.
3. **Not refreshing project** — After adding libraries, press **F5** or right-click → **Clean and Build**.
4. **Mixing Ant and Maven** in the same project — choose **one** build tool per project.
5. **Ignoring the `nbproject/private/` folder** — never commit it to version control.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper Cycle]`
**Define NetBeans IDE. List any four salient features that make it suitable for IoT application development.**

**Model Answer:**

**NetBeans IDE** is an open-source, modular Integrated Development Environment primarily used for Java development, built atop the **NetBeans Platform** and currently maintained by the **Apache Software Foundation** under Apache License 2.0.

Four salient features for IoT development:

1. **Native Maven & Ant Support** — simplifies dependency management for IoT libraries (MQTT, CoAP, jSerialComm).
2. **Built-in GUI Builder (Matisse)** — facilitates rapid dashboard prototyping for IoT monitoring applications.
3. **Java ME Module Support** — historically enabled embedded Java development for resource-constrained IoT devices.
4. **Integrated Debugger (JPDA)** — essential for tracing network callbacks, MQTT event handling, and sensor data parsing logic.

*(Word Count: ~80, Optimal for 3-mark answer)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model Paper Cycle]`
**Explain the role of the following NetBeans components: (i) Projects Window (ii) Navigator (iii) Services Window.**

**Model Answer:**

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **(i) Projects Window** | Displays a hierarchical logical view of the project — source folders, libraries, dependencies, and build scripts. It is the primary entry-point for accessing all project artifacts. |
| **(ii) Navigator** | Provides a structural outline of the currently-open file — listing classes, methods, fields, and inner members. Enables quick navigation in large IoT handler classes. |

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **modular architecture of Apache NetBeans**. List any **six major modules** and their roles. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **step-by-step procedure to create, build, and run a Java IoT Maven project** in NetBeans that publishes an MQTT message to a public broker. Include relevant `pom.xml` snippet. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

The modular architecture of Apache NetBeans consists of **six hierarchical layers**:

**Layer 1 — Operating System Layer:** Windows/Linux/macOS host.

**Layer 2 — JVM Layer:** The Java Virtual Machine that executes all NetBeans bytecode.

**Layer 3 — NetBeans Platform / Runtime Container:** Provides core services — module lifecycle, file system abstraction, lookup mechanism, windowing system.

**Layer 4 — NetBeans Modules (.nbm):** Independent, hot-swappable JARs each contributing specific functionality.

**Layer 5 — IDE Functionality:** Editor, debugger, profiler, GUI builder, version control.

**Layer 6 — User Plugins:** Third-party extensions (IoT plugins, themes, etc.).

**Six Major Modules:**

| # | Module | Role |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `org.netbeans.modules.java.source` | Java language parsing & indexing |
| 2 | `org.netbeans.modules.projectapi` | Project metadata handling |
| 3 | `org.netbeans.modules.debugger.jpda` | Java Platform Debugger Architecture integration |
| 4 | `org.netbeans.modules.form` | Matisse GUI Builder |
| 5 | `org.netbeans.modules.versioning` | Git/SVN/Mercurial support |
| 6 | `org.netbeans.modules.profiler` | Performance & memory profiling |

**(Refer to the architecture diagram in Section 4.1 for the visual schematic — full marks require a labeled diagram.)**

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1:** Open NetBeans → **File → New Project → Java with Maven → Java Application**.

**Step 2:** Enter project details:

- Project Name: `KTUIoTDemo`
- Group ID: `com.ktu.iot`
- Artifact ID: `ktu-iot-demo`
- Version: `1.0-SNAPSHOT`
- Package: `com.ktu.iot`

**Step 3:** Modify `pom.xml` to add the MQTT dependency:

```xml
<dependencies>
    <dependency>
        <groupId>org.eclipse.paho</groupId>
        <artifactId>org.eclipse.paho.client.mqttv3</artifactId>
        <version>1.2.5</version>
    </dependency>
</dependencies>
```

**Step 4:** Write `KTUIoTDemo.java` (refer to full code in Section 3.2, Step 6).

**Step 5:** Build via **F11** (or right-click → Build). Check Output window for `BUILD SUCCESS`.

**Step 6:** Run via **F6**. Observe Output window for:
```
Connected to MQTT Broker
Published: {sensor data}
Disconnected
```

**Step 7:** Verify by subscribing to topic `ktu/iot/sensor/temperature` using any MQTT client (e.g., MQTTBox, Mosquitto CLI).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Compare **Apache NetBeans** with **Eclipse IDE** and **IntelliJ IDEA** under the following heads: **(7 Marks)**
   (i) License Type
   (ii) GUI Builder Support
   (iii) Memory Footprint
   (iv) Maven/Gradle Support
   (v) Plugin Ecosystem
   (vi) KTU Exam Suitability

**(b)** Describe the **Maven build lifecycle** with a neat state diagram. Explain each phase in 1–2 lines. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

| Head | **Apache NetBeans** | **Eclipse IDE** | **IntelliJ IDEA** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) License** | Apache License 2.0 (Open Source) | Eclipse Public License 2.0 | Community Edition: Apache 2.0; Ultimate: Commercial |
| **(ii) GUI Builder** | Built-in Matisse — drag-and-drop Swing/JavaFX | WindowBuilder plugin required | Scene Builder external tool |
| **(iii) Memory Footprint** | ~400–700 MB RAM | ~500–900 MB RAM | ~700–1200 MB RAM |
| **(iv) Maven/Gradle Support** | Native, integrated wizard | Native via m2e plugin | Native, superior dependency resolution |
| **(v) Plugin Ecosystem** | Moderate; strong Java ME & IoT | Very large; multi-language | Large; focused on JVM ecosystem |
| **(vi) KTU Exam Suitability** | ✅ **Highly Recommended** (syllabus-aligned) | ✅ Suitable | ⚠ Acceptable but heavy |

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

The **Maven Build Lifecycle** consists of **eight sequential phases**. Each phase executes the goals bound to it. (Refer to the state diagram in Section 4.4.)

**Phase-by-Phase Explanation:**

1. **Validate** — Validates the project structure and ensures all required information is available.
2. **Compile** — Compiles `.java` source files into `.class` bytecode.
3. **Test** — Runs unit tests (typically JUnit) in the `src/test/java` directory.
4. **Package** — Bundles compiled code into a distributable format (JAR, WAR, or EAR).
5. **Integration-Test** — Runs integration tests in a deployed environment.
6. **Verify** — Runs any checks to verify the package meets quality criteria.
7. **Install** — Installs the package into the **local Maven repository** (`~/.m2/repository`) for use by other local projects.
8. **Deploy** — Copies the final artifact to a **remote repository** for sharing with other developers.
