## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes **all** key formulas, encoding rules, and boundary conditions for USB scale data parsing.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Signed 16-bit Decode (Little-Endian) | $V = b_0 + (b_1 \ll 8)$ treated as two's complement | $-32768$ to $+32767$ |
| Signed 32-bit Decode (Little-Endian) | $V = b_0 + (b_1 \ll 8) + (b_2 \ll 16) + (b_3 \ll 24)$ | $-2^{31}$ to $2^{31}-1$ |
| Scale Resolution Factor | $R = 10^{\text{decimals}}$ where `decimals` is in Report Descriptor | e.g., $R=100$ for 2 decimals |
| **Final Weight Value** | $W = \dfrac{V}{R}$ | $\text{kg, lb, oz, or g}$ |
| Negative Weight Check | If raw $> 2^{(N-1)} - 1$, then $W_{\text{raw}} = W_{\text{raw}} - 2^N$ | Sign bit handling |
| Status Flag (Byte 0) | `0x00` = OK, `0x01` = Fault, `0x02` = Zero, `0x03` = Over Range | 1 byte |
| Unit Byte (Byte 1) | `0x00` = kg, `0x01` = lb, `0x02` = oz, `0x03` = g | 1 byte |
| **Report Polling Rate** | $f_{\text{polling}} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{interval}}}$ | typically **4 Hz to 10 Hz** |
| **Throughput** | $\text{Throughput} = 6 \text{ bytes} \times f_{\text{polling}}$ | e.g., 24 B/s at 4 Hz |
| **Two's Complement Identity** | $-x = \bar{x} + 1$ | Bitwise operation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM TIP:** Almost every numerical question on USB scales boils down to two operations — (1) little-endian assembly of multi-byte fields, and (2) correct application of the resolution factor $R = 10^{\text{decimals}}$. Memorize the formula $W = V/R$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Logistics & Warehousing** | Automated parcel weighing, sortation gates, billing |
| **Healthcare** | Patient weighing stations, drug compounding |
| **Smart Retail** | Checkout-less weighing of produce, dynamic pricing |
| **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** | Tank level monitoring via load cells, batching systems |
| **Agriculture** | Livestock weighing, feed inventory |
| **Laboratory Automation** | Precision balances integrated with LIMS |

> [!NOTE]
> In an **IoT architecture**, a USB scale is typically the *edge sensor* in a three-tier model: **Edge (scale) → Gateway (Raspberry Pi/Edge TPU) → Cloud (time-series DB + dashboards)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Decoding a 6-Byte USB Scale HID Report

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Consider the following raw 6-byte report received from a USB scale:

$$\text{Bytes} = \begin{bmatrix} 0x00,\ 0x00,\ 0x12,\ 0x34,\ 0x00,\ 0x00 \end{bmatrix}$$

The scale's HID Report Descriptor specifies:
- **Status:** 1 byte (Byte 0)
- **Unit:** 1 byte (Byte 1)
- **Weight:** 4 bytes, signed, little-endian (Bytes 2–5)
- **Resolution:** 2 decimal places (factor $R = 100$)
- **Default unit:** Grams

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Parse the Status Byte**

Byte 0 is `0x00`. The status encoding table defines `0x00` as `OK` (measurement is valid).

$$\text{Status} = 0x00 \rightarrow \text{OK, valid measurement}$$

**Step 2: Parse the Unit Byte**

Byte 1 is `0x03`. The unit encoding table defines `0x03` as `grams (g)`.

$$\text{Unit} = 0x03 \rightarrow \text{Grams}$$

**Step 3: Assemble the 32-bit Weight Field (Little-Endian)**

Little-endian means the *least significant byte* comes *first*. We combine Bytes 2, 3, 4, 5 into a single 32-bit integer:

$$V_{\text{raw}} = (b_2) + (b_3 \ll 8) + (b_4 \ll 16) + (b_5 \ll 24)$$

Substituting the values ($b_2=0x12$, $b_3=0x34$, $b_4=0x00$, $b_5=0x00$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{raw}} &= (0x12) + (0x34 \ll 8) + (0x00 \ll 16) + (0x00 \ll 24) \\
&= 18 + (52 \times 256) + 0 + 0 \\
&= 18 + 13312 + 0 + 0 \\
&= 13330
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Check the Sign Bit (MSB of Byte 5)**

The MSB of the 32-bit value is the sign bit in two's complement. Byte 5 is `0x00`, so the MSB is `0` → the number is **positive**.

$$V_{\text{signed}} = 13330 \quad (\text{no sign correction needed})$$

**Step 5: Apply the Resolution Factor**

The descriptor declared 2 decimal places, so $R = 10^2 = 100$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \frac{V_{\text{signed}}}{R} \\
&= \frac{13330}{100} \\
&= 133.30 \ \text{grams}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Final Answer

The scale reports a weight of **133.30 grams** with status **OK**, in the unit **grams (g)**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Negative Weight Handling

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

The scale sends the following 6-byte report for a small downward tare (negative reading):

$$\text{Bytes} = \begin{bmatrix} 0x02,\ 0x00,\ 0xFE,\ 0xFF,\ 0xFF,\ 0xFF \end{bmatrix}$$

- Resolution: 2 decimal places ($R=100$)
- Status `0x02` = Zero (tare was performed)
- Unit `0x00` = kilograms

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Status & Unit**

$$\text{Status} = 0x02 \rightarrow \text{Zero / Tared}, \quad \text{Unit} = 0x00 \rightarrow \text{kg}$$

**Step 2: Assemble Little-Endian 32-bit Value**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{raw}} &= (0xFE) + (0xFF \ll 8) + (0xFF \ll 16) + (0xFF \ll 24) \\
&= 254 + (255 \times 256) + (255 \times 65536) + (255 \times 16777216) \\
&= 254 + 65280 + 16711680 + 4278190080 \\
&= 4294967294
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Detect the Sign Bit**

Byte 5 is `0xFF`; the MSB of the 32-bit word is `1` → the number is **negative** in two's complement.

**Step 4: Apply the Two's Complement Identity $-x = \bar{x} + 1$**

Since the raw value is a signed negative, we subtract it from $2^{32}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{signed}} &= V_{\text{raw}} - 2^{32} \\
&= 4294967294 - 4294967296 \\
&= -2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Apply the Resolution Factor**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \frac{V_{\text{signed}}}{R} = \frac{-2}{100} = -0.02 \ \text{kg} = -20 \ \text{grams}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Final Answer

The scale reports a tare correction of **−0.02 kg (or −20 grams)**, status `Zero`.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Edge Cases & Status Byte Behavior

| Input Scenario | Byte 0 | Byte 1 | Weight (V/R) | Engineering Action |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Normal reading | `0x00` | `0x03` | $W > 0$ | Publish to MQTT |
| Zero / Tared | `0x02` | any | $W = 0$ | Reset baseline |
| **Over Range** | `0x03` | any | Discard | Trigger alarm, log fault |
| Fault (sensor error) | `0x01` | any | Discard | Hardware diagnostic |
| Negative (push-down) | `0x00` | `0x03` | $W < 0$ | Flag as tare/anomaly |
| Unit change mid-stream | `0x00` | `0x00` | Use $W$ as **kg**, no rescale | Update display unit |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pro Tip for KTU Board Exams:** When a question provides raw bytes and a Report Descriptor excerpt, always write out the **little-endian assembly** in a single $\begin{aligned}$ block, and then a **separate** $\begin{aligned}$ block for sign correction (if any), and a **third** block for the resolution division. Examiners award step-marks only when each transformation is visible.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End USB Scale Communication Block Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture flow** from the physical load cell to the cloud dashboard, including the critical data-parsing stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Load Cell] --> B[ADC + Microcontroller in Scale]
    B --> C[USB HID Firmware Stack]
    C --> D[USB Cable Plug-in Event]
    D --> E[Host Enumeration & Descriptor Read]
    E --> F[HID Report Descriptor Parsed]
    F --> G[Interrupt-IN Endpoint Opened]
    G --> H[Periodic 6-Byte Report Stream]
    H --> I[Python hid Library Read]
    I --> J[Little-Endian Assembly]
    J --> K[Two's Complement Sign Check]
    K --> L[Resolution Factor Division]
    L --> M[Status & Unit Validation]
    M --> N{Status Equals OK?}
    N -->|Yes| O[Publish to MQTT Broker]
    N -->|No| P[Log Fault to Local File]
    O --> Q[Cloud InfluxDB Time-Series]
    O --> R[Grafana Dashboard]
    P --> S[Alert Notification Service]
```

## 4.2 HID Report Descriptor Logic Flow

This Mermaid diagram visualizes the parsing decision tree the host follows when it receives each 6-byte report.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Receive 6-Byte Report] --> B[Parse Byte 0 Status]
    B --> C{Status Flag}
    C -->|0x00| D[Parse Weight Field]
    C -->|0x01| E[Trigger Sensor Fault Handler]
    C -->|0x02| F[Mark Reading as Zero]
    C -->|0x03| G[Trigger Over-Range Alarm]
    D --> H[Parse Byte 1 Unit]
    H --> I[Assemble Bytes 2-5 Little-Endian]
    I --> J{Most Significant Bit}
    J -->|1| K[Apply Two's Complement]
    J -->|0| L[Keep Positive Value]
    K --> M[Divide by Resolution Factor R]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Output Final Weight in Declared Unit]
```

## 4.3 IoT Integration Layer Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EDGE_LAYER[Edge Layer]
        S1[USB Scale 1]
        S2[USB Scale 2]
        S3[USB Scale 3]
    end
    subgraph GATEWAY_LAYER[Gateway Layer]
        GW[Raspberry Pi 4 with hid-py Library]
        DB[Local SQLite Cache]
    end
    subgraph CLOUD_LAYER[Cloud Layer]
        MQ[MQTT Broker Mosquitto]
        TS[InfluxDB Time-Series Store]
        GF[Grafana Visualization]
        AL[Alertmanager]
    end
    S1 --> GW
    S2 --> GW
    S3 --> GW
    GW --> DB
    GW --> MQ
    MQ --> TS
    TS --> GF
    TS --> AL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip:** Notice how the *parsing* logic (Little-Endian → Sign → Resolution) is **identical** at the gateway layer regardless of the cloud backend. This is the *separation of concerns* principle — the edge/gateway is responsible for **physical-to-digital conversion**, while the cloud is responsible for **storage, analytics, and visualization**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**Explain the role of the USB HID class in enabling plug-and-play communication with a weighing scale. Mention the standard Usage Page and Usage identifiers used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **USB Human Interface Device (HID) class** is a standardized USB device class that allows simple, low-bandwidth, periodic data exchange without requiring vendor-specific drivers. When a USB scale is plugged in, the operating system's built-in HID driver stack enumerates the device, reads its **HID Report Descriptor**, and opens an **Interrupt-IN endpoint** for streaming structured measurement data. The standard identifiers are:
- **Usage Page:** `0x8D` (Weighing Devices / Medical Instrumentation)
- **Usage:** `0x01` (Scale)
- **Collection:** `Application`

This standardized identification ensures the scale works across Windows, Linux, and macOS **without any custom driver installation**, making it ideal for IoT deployments.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**What is the role of the resolution factor $R$ in decoding a USB scale's weight report? Give an example with 2 decimal places.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **resolution factor $R$** is a divisor used to convert the raw integer weight value received from the scale into a human-readable decimal weight. It is defined as:

$$R = 10^{\text{decimals}}$$

where `decimals` is the number of fractional digits declared in the HID Report Descriptor.

**Example:** If a scale reports 2 decimal places and the raw integer is $V = 13330$, then:

$$W = \frac{V}{R} = \frac{13330}{100} = 133.30 \ \text{grams}$$

This avoids the need for floating-point transmission (which is inefficient over USB) while preserving fractional precision through integer scaling.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a block diagram, describe the **end-to-end USB HID communication pipeline** between a USB weighing scale and an IoT gateway. Clearly label the stages: enumeration, descriptor parsing, endpoint configuration, report transfer, and application decoding.

**(b)** [7 Marks] The following **6-byte HID report** is received from a USB scale:
$$\text{Bytes} = \begin{bmatrix} 0x00,\ 0x00,\ 0x90,\ 0x01,\ 0x00,\ 0x00 \end{bmatrix}$$
The descriptor declares: **2 decimal places, signed 32-bit weight field, status `0x00` = OK, unit `0x00` = kg.** Compute the final weight in kilograms. Show all intermediate steps.

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

The end-to-end pipeline consists of **five stages** (1.4 marks each stage):

1. **Enumeration:** The host detects `VBUS` (5 V) and the `D+` pull-up resistor. The host assigns a 7-bit address and requests *Device*, *Configuration*, *Interface*, *HID*, and *Report* descriptors. **(1.4 marks)**
2. **Descriptor Parsing:** The host reads the *Report Descriptor* and identifies: Usage Page `0x8D`, Usage `0x01` (Scale), 6-byte input report with status + unit + signed weight. **(1.4 marks)**
3. **Endpoint Configuration:** The host opens an **Interrupt-IN endpoint** (EP1 IN) with a polling interval of 100–250 ms. **(1.4 marks)**
4. **Report Transfer:** The scale sends a 6-byte report every polling interval. The host receives it via Interrupt-IN transfer. **(1.4 marks)**
5. **Application Decoding:** The Python `hid` library reads the bytes; the application assembles little-endian, applies two's complement if needed, divides by $R = 10^{\text{decimals}}$, and publishes to MQTT. **(1.4 marks)**

A block diagram of the pipeline is shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Parse Status & Unit (1 mark)**

- Status: Byte 0 = `0x00` → OK
- Unit: Byte 1 = `0x00` → kilograms

**Step 2: Assemble Little-Endian 32-bit Weight (3 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{raw}} &= b_2 + (b_3 \ll 8) + (b_4 \ll 16) + (b_5 \ll 24) \\
&= 0x90 + (0x01 \ll 8) + (0x00 \ll 16) + (0x00 \ll 24) \\
&= 144 + (1 \times 256) + 0 + 0 \\
&= 144 + 256 \\
&= 400
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Sign Bit Check (1 mark)**

Byte 5 = `0x00`, so MSB = `0`. The value is **positive**; no two's complement correction is required.

$$V_{\text{signed}} = 400$$

**Step 4: Apply Resolution Factor (2 marks)**

$R = 10^2 = 100$ (2 decimal places).

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \frac{V_{\text{signed}}}{R} \\
&= \frac{400}{100} \\
&= 4.00 \ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** The scale reports a weight of **4.00 kilograms** with status **OK**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** [7 Marks] Differentiate between **USB Interrupt-IN, Bulk-IN, and Isochronous-IN transfers** in the context of a USB scale's data reporting. Justify why Interrupt-IN is the preferred choice for scales.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A USB scale reports the following raw 6-byte data:
$$\text{Bytes} = \begin{bmatrix} 0x00,\ 0x03,\ 0x9C,\ 0xFF,\ 0xFF,\ 0xFF \end{bmatrix}$$
The descriptor specifies: **3 decimal places, signed 32-bit weight, status `0x00` = OK, unit `0x03` = grams.** Decode this report and compute the final weight in grams.

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

| Transfer Type | Use Case | Guarantee | Latency | Bandwidth |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Interrupt-IN** | Keyboards, mice, **scales** | Bounded latency | Guaranteed polling interval (1–255 ms) | Low |
| Bulk-IN | Printers, storage | Delivery guaranteed, **no latency guarantee** | Variable, may stall | High |
| Isochronous-IN | Audio, video | Bounded latency, **no delivery guarantee** | Guaranteed timing | High |

**Justification for Interrupt-IN (3.5 marks):**
- A weighing scale needs **bounded, predictable latency** (the user expects near-instant weight display) but does **not** require the high bandwidth of audio/video. **Interrupt-IN** provides the ideal trade-off: a guaranteed maximum polling interval and small, periodic reports.
- **Bulk-IN** would also deliver the 6-byte report correctly, but its latency is unbounded — under heavy bus traffic, the report could be delayed arbitrarily, leading to sluggish weight updates.
- **Isochronous-IN** guarantees latency but **does not guarantee delivery** — a dropped report would mean a missed weight reading, which is unacceptable for billing or compliance use cases.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Parse Status & Unit (1 mark)**

- Status: Byte 0 = `0x00` → OK
- Unit: Byte 1 = `0x03` → grams

**Step 2: Assemble Little-Endian 32-bit Weight (3 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{raw}} &= 0x9C + (0xFF \ll 8) + (0xFF \ll 16) + (0xFF \ll 24) \\
&= 156 + (255 \times 256) + (255 \times 65536) + (255 \times 16777216) \\
&= 156 + 65280 + 16711680 + 4278190080 \\
&= 4294967196
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Sign Bit Check & Two's Complement (2 marks)**

Byte 5 = `0xFF`, so the MSB is `1` → the value is **negative** in two's complement representation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{signed}} &= V_{\text{raw}} - 2^{32} \\
&= 4294967196 - 4294967296 \\
&= -100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Apply Resolution Factor (1 mark)**

$R = 10^3 = 1000$ (3 decimal places).

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \frac{V_{\text{signed}}}{R} = \frac{-100}{1000} = -0.100 \ \text{grams}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** The scale reports a weight of **−0.100 grams** with status **OK**. This negative value typically indicates a minor tare correction or sensor drift.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Endianness Error:** The most common mistake is treating bytes as **big-endian**. If you assemble `$0x9C\ 0xFF\ 0xFF\ 0xFF$` as big-endian, you will get $2{,}618{,}601{,}628$ instead of $-100$ — a wrong sign and a wildly incorrect magnitude. **Always remember: HID scales use LITTLE-ENDIAN.** **(Lose up to 3 marks)**
> 2. **Skipping the Sign Check:** Students often apply $W = V/R$ directly without checking the MSB. If the raw value is $> 2^{N-1} - 1$, the result will be positive but numerically absurd. **Always check Byte 5's MSB before dividing.** **(Lose up to 2 marks)**
> 3. **Wrong Resolution Factor:** Confusing 2-decimal ($R=100$) with 3-decimal ($R=1000$) places leads to answers that are off by 10×. **Read the descriptor carefully.** **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 4. **Ignoring the Status Byte:** A fault or over-range report (`0x01` or `0x03`) should not be processed as a normal weight. **Always validate Byte 0 first.** **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 5. **Unit Mismatch:** Reporting a weight in grams when the question asks for kilograms (or vice versa) without unit conversion. **Always state the unit in the final answer.** **(Lose 1 mark)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ☐ USB scales almost always use the **HID class** for driver-free plug-and-play operation.
- ☐ The standard Usage Page is `0x8D` and Usage is `0x01` (Scale) under the HID Usage Tables.
- ☐ The most common report size is **6 bytes**: [Status][Unit][WeightByte0][WeightByte1][WeightByte2][WeightByte3].
- ☐ The weight field is **little-endian, signed, and requires integer-to-decimal conversion** via $R = 10^{\text{decimals}}$.
- ☐ **Always check the sign bit (MSB of the highest byte) before applying the resolution factor** to handle negative weights correctly.
- ☐ The **Interrupt-IN endpoint** is the preferred transfer type for scales because it offers bounded latency with low bandwidth overhead.
- ☐ The **Status byte** must be validated first: `0x00` = OK, `0x01` = Fault, `0x02` = Zero, `0x03` = Over Range.
- ☐ In a typical IoT pipeline, the USB scale is the **edge sensor**, the Raspberry Pi is the **gateway**, and MQTT + InfluxDB + Grafana form the **cloud visualization stack**.
- ☐ **Memory aid for KTU exams:** "**S**tatus, **U**nit, **L**ittle-endian, **S**ign, **R**esolution" → SULSR — the 5-step decoding recipe.
- ☐ Common Python libraries: `hid` (hidapi wrapper), `pyusb` (low-level), and vendor-specific SDKs (e.g., Mettler Toledo, CAS).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coffee Calculator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT504 - PROGRAMMING FOR INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Coffee Calculator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Coffee Calculator** is an **embedded IoT application** that uses sensor inputs (typically a **load cell** for mass measurement and a **temperature sensor** like DS18B20 or thermistor) interfaced with a microcontroller (Arduino/ESP32/NodeMCU) to compute, display, and optionally transmit real-time **brewing parameters** such as the **brew ratio**, **water mass lost to evaporation/retention**, **extraction yield**, and **Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)**. It is the canonical *sensor-fusion + actuator + cloud* example used in KTU Module 4 to demonstrate **embedded C/Arduino programming** for a tangible, everyday use-case.

The Coffee Calculator sits at the intersection of three KTU 2024 syllabus pillars covered in Module 4:

1. **Sensor Interfacing & Data Acquisition** – reading analogue (HX711 ADC) and digital (1-Wire) buses.
2. **Edge Computation** – on-device mathematical transformations (linearization, ratio, percentage).
3. **Actuation & Connectivity** – driving an LCD/OLED and pushing telemetry over **MQTT / HTTP / Blynk**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Barista" View

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Think of the Coffee Calculator as a **digital apprentice barista** standing next to a human barista.
> - The **load cell** is the barista's *hand on the cup* (it feels weight changes).
> - The **temperature sensor** is the *thermometer stuck in the kettle*.
> - The **microcontroller** is the barista's *brain* that performs mental math ("I need 250 mL of water for 17 g of coffee beans").
> - The **LCD/OLED** is the barista's *voice* announcing, *"Brew ratio 1:14.7, pour 1 °C above target."*
> - The **Wi-Fi module** is the *mobile phone* of the café owner who wants to monitor the brewing process remotely.

In one line: **A Coffee Calculator turns the physical act of brewing into measurable, repeatable, transmittable data.**

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Brewing Constants Used

> [!NOTE]
> The Specialty Coffee Association (SCA) defines the **golden cup standards** that most academic Coffee Calculators are benchmarked against. The following constants appear in KTU theory questions:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Golden Cup Brew Ratio | $R_b$ | **1:18.18** (i.e., $55 \text{ g/L}$) |
| Recommended Brew Ratio Range | $R_b$ | **1:15 – 1:18** |
| Optimum TDS (Total Dissolved Solids) | $TDS$ | **1.15 % – 1.45 %** |
| Optimum Extraction Yield | $Y_e$ | **18 % – 22 %** |
| Ideal Brew Temperature | $T_b$ | **92 °C – 96 °C** |
| Water Density (for mL → g conversion) | $\rho_w$ | **0.997 g/mL at 25 °C** |
| Specific Heat of Water | $c_w$ | **4.186 J/(g·°C)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A KTU-style Coffee Calculator is built in **six discrete stages**. Examiners frequently award marks for clearly identifying these stages.

**Stage 1 — Sensor Initialization**
- Configure HX711 load-cell amplifier (gain = 128, sample rate = 10 Hz or 80 Hz).
- Initialize DS18B20 on a 1-Wire bus; request `startConversion()`.
- Call `scale.set_scale(calibration_factor)` and `scale.tare()` to zero the cup mass.

**Stage 2 — Raw Data Acquisition**
- Read 24-bit signed integer from HX711 → convert to grams.
- Read 12-bit signed temperature from DS18B20 → convert to °C.
- Apply a **moving average filter** (window of N = 8–16 samples) to suppress load-cell vibration noise.

**Stage 3 — Edge Computation (the "Math" Stage)**

Three primary computations are performed:

1. **Brew Ratio** $R_b$
2. **Extraction Yield** $Y_e$
3. **Brew Strength (TDS)** $T$

**Stage 4 — Decision / Actuation Logic**

- If measured mass < target mass → energize a **relay-driven solenoid pump** (PWM-controlled flow).
- If temperature < 92 °C → energize **Peltier heater** or display a warning.
- If $R_b$ falls outside 1:15 – 1:18 → display **"Ratio Out of Range"**.

**Stage 5 — Local Display**

- Render results on a 16×2 LCD (I²C) or SSD1306 OLED (SPI/I²C).
- Format: `R=1:16.2  T=93.4°C  TDS=1.32%`.

**Stage 6 — Cloud Uplink (Optional but KTU-favoured)**

- Publish JSON payload `{ratio, temp, tds, timestamp}` to an MQTT broker (e.g., HiveMQ, AWS IoT Core) or HTTP REST endpoint.
- Use **Node-RED dashboard** or **Blynk** for remote visualization.

## 2.2 Why Each Step Matters

- **Stage 1–2** ensure the **Garbage-In-Garbage-Out (GIGO)** principle is respected; calibration errors propagate linearly.
- **Stage 3** is where the device earns the title "Calculator" — without these transforms, it would be only a *display* project.
- **Stage 4** closes the loop, satisfying the KTU learning outcome on **actuator control**.
- **Stage 5–6** deliver the **IoT-ness** — accessibility beyond the local user.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas a student may be asked to derive in a KTU 2024 ESE on the Coffee Calculator.**

| # | Formula Name | LaTeX Expression | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Brew Ratio | $R_b = \dfrac{m_w}{m_c}$ | Mass of water to mass of coffee | dimensionless |
| 2 | Total Water Mass (volume → mass) | $m_w = \rho_w \cdot V$ | Convert mL of water to grams | g |
| 3 | TDS (Refractometer Method) | $TDS = \dfrac{(B - i) \cdot k}{\rho_{brew}}$ | Where $B$ = refractometer reading, $i$ = intercept, $k$ = slope | % |
| 4 | Extraction Yield | $Y_e = TDS \times R_b$ | Mass of solubles extracted per coffee mass | % |
| 5 | Mass Loss Compensation | $m_{actual} = m_{measured} - m_{retention} - m_{evap}$ | Account for water absorbed by grounds & steam | g |
| 6 | Load Cell Linear Model | $V_{out} = a \cdot m + b$ | Where $a$ = sensitivity, $b$ = zero-load offset | V |
| 7 | ADC Counts to Mass | $m = \dfrac{(ADC_{raw} - ADC_{zero}) \cdot V_{ref}}{a \cdot 2^{n}}$ | $n$ = ADC resolution (typically 24 for HX711) | g |
| 8 | Temperature Conversion (DS18B20 raw) | $T = \dfrac{raw_{16}}{16}$ | 16-bit signed output, LSB = 1/16 °C | °C |
| 9 | Peltier Heat Energy | $Q = m \cdot c_w \cdot \Delta T$ | Energy to raise water from $T_{start}$ to $T_{target}$ | J |
| 10 | Pump Flow (volumetric) | $V = \dot{V} \cdot t$ | Volume dispensed = flow rate × time | mL |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Coffee Calculator pattern is **industry-transposable**. The exact same architecture (load cell + temperature + edge math + cloud) is used in:
> - **Smart pharmaceutical dispensers** (weighing tablets).
> - **Industrial batching systems** (cement, paint, fertilizer mixing).
> - **Precision agriculture** (sap-flow sensors in trees).
> - **Medical infusion pumps** (mass-flow verification).
> - **Battery cell formation** (electrolyte mass monitoring).

Hence, a KTU examiner treats the Coffee Calculator as a **template** for *any* "**weigh-and-react**" IoT node.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Load-Cell Voltage to Brew Ratio

**Given:**
- HX711 outputs a 24-bit signed ADC count $N$.
- Reference voltage $V_{ref} = 4.3 \text{ V}$ (internal regulator of HX711).
- Load cell sensitivity $a = 1 \text{ mV/V}$ per rated output (typical).
- Excitation voltage $V_{ex} = 5 \text{ V}$.
- Calibration factor $C_f$ supplied by the user after tare.

**Step 1 — Voltage at ADC input**

$$V_{adc} = \dfrac{N}{2^{23}} \cdot V_{ref}$$

*Logic:* The HX711 is a 24-bit sigma-delta ADC; the most significant bit is the sign bit, so the full positive range spans $2^{23}$ counts.

**Step 2 — Differential voltage across the Wheatstone bridge**

$$V_{diff} = \dfrac{V_{adc}}{G_{HX711}}$$

*Logic:* $G_{HX711} = 128$ (channel A, default gain). Substitute for the next step.

**Step 3 — Mass calculation using sensitivity**

$$m = \dfrac{V_{diff}}{a \cdot V_{ex}}$$

*Logic:* Sensitivity is expressed per volt of excitation; we therefore divide by the excitation voltage to get the true strain.

**Step 4 — Apply the calibration factor (tare + span correction)**

$$m_{calibrated} = (m - m_{tare}) \cdot C_f$$

*Logic:* A raw mass reading is offset by the cup's empty mass ($m_{tare}$) and scaled by the calibration factor (grams per count) that the user derives by placing a known 100 g weight.

**Step 5 — Compute Brew Ratio**

$$R_b = \dfrac{m_{w,cal}}{m_{c,cal}}$$

*Logic:* The Coffee Calculator stores the coffee mass at the moment the user places the cup of grounds, then samples the water mass continuously as the pump dispenses.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Extraction Yield from Refractometer Data

**Given:**
- Brix reading $B$ from a digital refractometer (e.g., Atago PAL-COFFEE).
- Coffee-to-beverage dilution factor $f_{d} = 1$ (pure brew, no milk/water added post-extraction).
- Moisture content of green beans $M_{g} = 11 \text{ %}$.

**Step 1 — Convert Brix to TDS using the moisture-corrected formula**

$$\begin{aligned}
TDS &= B \cdot k \cdot (1 - M_g) \\
    &= B \cdot 0.85 \cdot (1 - 0.11) \\
    &= 0.7565 \cdot B
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* The empirical coefficient $k = 0.85$ accounts for the non-sugar solubles in coffee. The $(1 - M_g)$ term compensates for water already in the green bean.

**Step 2 — Apply the brew ratio to obtain extraction yield**

$$\begin{aligned}
Y_e &= TDS \cdot R_b \\
    &= 0.7565 \cdot B \cdot R_b
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* $Y_e$ represents the percentage of the dry coffee mass that has been dissolved into the beverage.

**Step 3 — Numerical Example (KTU-style)**

Suppose $B = 1.5$ and $R_b = 16.5$.

$$\begin{aligned}
Y_e &= 0.7565 \cdot 1.5 \cdot 16.5 \\
    &= 18.72 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* This falls within the SCA sweet spot of **18 % – 22 %** → the brew is properly extracted.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pump-On Duration for a Target Brew Ratio

**Given:**
- Target brew ratio $R_b^* = 16$.
- Coffee mass $m_c = 18 \text{ g}$.
- Pump flow rate $\dot{V} = 5 \text{ mL/s}$.
- Water density $\rho_w = 0.997 \text{ g/mL}$.

**Step 1 — Required water mass**

$$m_w = R_b^* \cdot m_c = 16 \cdot 18 = 288 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2 — Required water volume**

$$V = \dfrac{m_w}{\rho_w} = \dfrac{288}{0.997} \approx 288.87 \text{ mL}$$

**Step 3 — Required pump-on time**

$$t = \dfrac{V}{\dot{V}} = \dfrac{288.87}{5} \approx 57.77 \text{ s}$$

*Logic:* The microcontroller activates the relay for **57.77 seconds** and then de-energizes it.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Inputs

| $m_c$ (g) | Target $R_b$ | $m_w$ (g) | $V$ (mL) | $t$ @ 5 mL/s |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 15 | 15 | 225 | 225.68 | 45.14 s |
| 17 | 16 | 272 | 272.82 | 54.56 s |
| 18 | 17 | 306 | 306.92 | 61.38 s |
| 20 | 18 | 360 | 361.08 | 72.22 s |
| 22 | 18.18 | 400 | 401.20 | 80.24 s |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The Coffee Calculator is best represented as a **sensor → edge → cloud** pipeline. The Mermaid diagram below maps every functional block, the data type at each arrow, and the protocol used at the cloud boundary.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SENS["SENSOR LAYER"]
        loadCell["LOAD CELL\nHX711 AMPLIFIER"]
        tempSensor["DS18B20\nTEMP SENSOR"]
        flowMeter["YF-S201\nFLOW SENSOR"]
    end

    subgraph EDGE["EDGE LAYER (ESP32 / ARDUINO)"]
        acquire["DATA ACQUISITION\nANALOG + 1-WIRE READS"]
        filter["MOVING AVERAGE\nFILTER N=8"]
        compute["EDGE COMPUTATION\nRATIO / TDS / YIELD"]
        decision["DECISION LOGIC\nIF-ELSE ACTUATOR CTRL"]
    end

    subgraph ACT["ACTUATOR LAYER"]
        pump["SOLENOID PUMP\n12V DC RELAY"]
        heater["PELTIER HEATER\nPWM CONTROL"]
        lcdDisp["16X2 LCD / SSD1306\nI2C DISPLAY"]
    end

    subgraph CLOUD["CLOUD / CONNECTIVITY"]
        mqttPub["MQTT PUBLISHER\nJSON PAYLOAD"]
        broker["MQTT BROKER\nHIVEMQ / AWS IOT"]
        dash["DASHBOARD\nNODE-RED / BLYNK"]
    end

    loadCell -->|24-bit digital| acquire
    tempSensor -->|12-bit digital| acquire
    flowMeter -->|pulse stream| acquire
    acquire --> filter
    filter --> compute
    compute --> decision
    decision -->|GPIO HIGH| pump
    decision -->|PWM 50%| heater
    compute -->|I2C frame| lcdDisp
    compute -->|JSON over TCP| mqttPub
    mqttPub --> broker
    broker --> dash
```

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Single Brew Cycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as USER
    participant MCU as MICROCONTROLLER
    participant LC as LOAD CELL
    participant TMP as TEMP SENSOR
    participant PMP as PUMP
    participant LCD as DISPLAY
    participant CLD as CLOUD

    U->>MCU: Place cup + grounds
    MCU->>LC: Tare() and read mass
    LC-->>MCU: m_c = 18.0 g
    MCU->>LCD: Show "Brew ratio target?"
    U->>MCU: Input R_b = 16
    MCU->>PMP: Energize (GPIO HIGH)
    loop Every 500 ms
        MCU->>LC: Read mass
        MCU->>TMP: Read temperature
        LC-->>MCU: m_w(t)
        TMP-->>MCU: T(t)
        MCU->>MCU: R_b(t) = m_w(t) / m_c
        MCU->>LCD: Update line 2
    end
    MCU->>PMP: De-energize (mass = 288 g)
    MCU->>CLD: Publish JSON
    CLD-->>U: Notification on phone
```

## 4.3 State Machine of the Coffee Calculator

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IDLE
    IDLE --> TARE: User places empty cup
    TARE --> READ_GRIND: Tare complete
    READ_GRIND --> HEAT: Grounds detected
    HEAT --> DISPENSE: T >= 92 deg C
    DISPENSE --> MONITOR: Pump ON
    MONITOR --> DISPENSE: R_b less than target
    MONITOR --> DONE: R_b equals target
    DONE --> PUBLISH: JSON upload
    PUBLISH --> IDLE: Cycle complete
    DONE --> ERROR: Mass or temp out of bounds
    ERROR --> IDLE: User reset
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "Brew Ratio" as used in IoT-based coffee brewing systems. State the SCA-recommended golden-cup range.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Brew Ratio** is defined as the ratio of the mass of water $m_w$ used for brewing to the mass of dry coffee grounds $m_c$, expressed mathematically as $R_b = \dfrac{m_w}{m_c}$. **(1 mark)**
> The **Specialty Coffee Association (SCA)** recommends a golden-cup brew ratio of **1:18.18** (i.e., 55 g of coffee per litre of water), with an acceptable range of **1:15 to 1:18**. **(1 mark)**
> In an IoT Coffee Calculator, the brew ratio is computed in real time on the edge device using a load cell and is then used as a feedback variable to control a solenoid pump. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any three sensors and two actuators commonly used in an IoT-based Coffee Calculator.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Component | Role | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensors** (any 3 × 0.5) | (i) HX711 + load cell — measures water and coffee mass (0.5); (ii) DS18B20 — measures water temperature (0.5); (iii) YF-S201 flow meter — measures dispensed volume (0.5) | **1.5** |
| **Actuators** (any 2 × 0.75) | (i) 12 V DC solenoid pump driven via relay — dispenses water (0.75); (ii) Peltier thermoelectric heater with PWM control — regulates water temperature (0.75) | **1.5** |
| **Total** | | **3** |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`**

**(a) Design the functional block diagram of an IoT-based Coffee Calculator that monitors brew ratio and water temperature. List the sensors, microcontrollers, actuators, and cloud services involved. (7 marks)**

**(b) An IoT Coffee Calculator uses an HX711 (24-bit ADC, gain 128, $V_{ref}$ = 4.3 V) interfaced with a 1 mV/V load cell excited at 5 V. After tare, the ADC returns 1 250 000 counts when a known 100 g weight is placed. Compute the calibration factor in grams per count and the mass of the coffee grounds if the ADC returns 540 000 counts. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Functional Block Diagram (7 marks)**

| # | Block | Description | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sensors** | HX711 + load cell (mass); DS18B20 (temperature); optional YF-S201 (flow) | 1.5 |
| 2 | **Microcontroller** | ESP32 / Arduino Uno / NodeMCU — chosen for ADC pins, I²C, Wi-Fi | 1.0 |
| 3 | **Edge Computation** | Moving-average filter; brew ratio $R_b = m_w / m_c$; TDS; yield $Y_e$ | 1.5 |
| 4 | **Actuators** | Solenoid pump (relay-driven); Peltier heater (PWM); buzzer alarm | 1.0 |
| 5 | **Local Display** | 16×2 LCD (I²C) or SSD1306 OLED showing live $R_b$, $T$ | 1.0 |
| 6 | **Cloud Layer** | MQTT publish to HiveMQ / AWS IoT Core → Node-RED / Blynk dashboard | 1.0 |
| **Total** | | | **7** |

*(The block diagram itself may be redrawn in the answer booklet; the table above is the KTU-marker equivalent.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Voltage per count (1 mark)**

$$V_{per\_count} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{23}} = \dfrac{4.3}{8\,388\,608} \approx 5.125 \times 10^{-7} \text{ V/count}$$

**Step 2 — ADC voltage at 100 g (1 mark)**

$$V_{adc,100g} = N \cdot V_{per\_count} = 1\,250\,000 \cdot 5.125 \times 10^{-7} = 0.6406 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Differential voltage after gain division (1 mark)**

$$V_{diff,100g} = \dfrac{0.6406}{128} = 5.004 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Mass from bridge equation (1 mark)**

$$m_{100g} = \dfrac{V_{diff,100g}}{a \cdot V_{ex}} = \dfrac{5.004 \times 10^{-3}}{0.001 \cdot 5} = 1.0008 \text{ g}$$

**Step 5 — Calibration factor (1 mark)**

$$C_f = \dfrac{100 \text{ g}}{1.0008 \text{ g}} = 99.92 \text{ g per "unit mass"}$$

Re-expressed in **grams per count**:

$$C_f' = \dfrac{100 \text{ g}}{1\,250\,000 \text{ counts}} = 8.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ g/count}$$

**Step 6 — Mass of coffee grounds at 540 000 counts (2 marks)**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{coffee} &= 540\,000 \cdot 8.0 \times 10^{-5} \\
           &= 43.2 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** Calibration factor = $8 \times 10^{-5} \text{ g/count}$ (or 99.92 per "unit-mass"); coffee mass = **43.2 g**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question A)**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Forgetting to divide by the HX711 **gain of 128** — losing 1 mark.
> - **Common mistake 2:** Using $2^{24}$ instead of $2^{23}$ for the ADC span — the MSB is a sign bit, not a magnitude bit. Losing 1 mark.
> - **Common mistake 3:** Confusing the **sensitivity unit** (1 mV/V means *1 millivolt per volt of excitation*, not per volt of supply). Losing 1 mark.
> - **Common mistake 4:** In part (a), students often list *components* but **forget the cloud layer**, losing 1 mark because the question explicitly says "IoT-based".

---

### **Question B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`**

**(a) Explain the role of the HX711 ADC and DS18B20 temperature sensor in an IoT Coffee Calculator. Describe their communication protocols and how raw data is converted to engineering units. (7 marks)**

**(b) For a target brew ratio of 1:16 with 18 g of coffee, calculate (i) the required water mass, (ii) the required water volume in mL (ρ = 0.997 g/mL), and (iii) the pump-on time if the flow rate is 5 mL/s. If a TDS reading of 1.30 % is measured post-brew, calculate (iv) the extraction yield $Y_e$. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Role of HX711 and DS18B20 (7 marks)**

| # | Aspect | HX711 | DS18B20 | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Function** | 24-bit sigma-delta ADC for load cell | 1-Wire digital thermometer | 1.0 |
| 2 | **Protocol** | Custom clock-driven bit-banging (DOUT + PD_SCK) | Dallas 1-Wire (single data line + parasitic power) | 1.0 |
| 3 | **Resolution** | 24-bit, gain = 64/128 | 9–12 bit configurable (default 12-bit, 0.0625 °C LSB) | 1.0 |
| 4 | **Raw → Engineering** | $V = N/2^{23} \cdot V_{ref}$; $m = V/(a \cdot V_{ex}) \cdot C_f$ | $T = \text{raw}_{16} / 16$ (signed 16-bit) | 2.0 |
| 5 | **Why chosen** | High noise-immunity for strain gauges; on-chip PGA | Parasitic-power capable → single GPIO; ±0.5 °C accuracy | 1.0 |
| 6 | **Arduino libraries** | `HX711.h` — `read()`, `tare()`, `get_units()` | `DallasTemperature.h`, `OneWire.h` | 1.0 |
| **Total** | | | | **7** |

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Required water mass (1.5 marks)**

$$m_w = R_b \cdot m_c = 16 \cdot 18 = 288 \text{ g}$$

**(ii) Required water volume (1.5 marks)**

$$V = \dfrac{m_w}{\rho_w} = \dfrac{288}{0.997} \approx 288.87 \text{ mL}$$

**(iii) Pump-on time (1.5 marks)**

$$t = \dfrac{V}{\dot{V}} = \dfrac{288.87}{5} \approx 57.77 \text{ s}$$

**(iv) Extraction yield (2.5 marks)**

$$Y_e = TDS \times R_b = 1.30 \times 16 = 20.80 \text{ \%}$$

**Final Answers:**
- $m_w = 288 \text{ g}$
- $V \approx 288.87 \text{ mL}$
- $t \approx 57.77 \text{ s}$
- $Y_e = 20.80 \text{ \%}$ (within the SCA 18–22 % sweet spot — *excellent brew!*)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B)**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Forgetting to convert water mass to **volume** using density — losing 1 mark on (ii).
> - **Common mistake 2:** Reporting extraction yield as a **decimal (0.208)** instead of a **percentage (20.80 %)** — losing 0.5 mark.
> - **Common mistake 3:** In part (a), confusing the **HX711 protocol with I²C** — it is **NOT** I²C; it is a proprietary two-wire bit-bang interface. Losing 1 mark.
> - **Common mistake 4:** Using **$2^{24}$** for HX711 full-scale instead of **$2^{23}$** — the 24th bit is the sign bit. Losing 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for the Coffee Calculator topic.**

- 🔹 **Brew Ratio $R_b$** = $m_w / m_c$ — dimensionless; SCA range **1:15 to 1:18**; golden = **1:18.18**.
- 🔹 **HX711** is a **24-bit sigma-delta ADC**, gain default **128**, $V_{ref} \approx 4.3 \text{ V}$. **NOT** I²C.
- 🔹 **DS18B20** is a **1-Wire** digital thermometer; raw 16-bit value divided by **16** gives °C.
- 🔹 **Load-cell equation** is a Wheatstone-bridge model: $V_{out} = a \cdot m \cdot V_{ex}$.
- 🔹 **TDS → Extraction Yield**: $Y_e = TDS \times R_b$; SCA range **18 % – 22 %**.
- 🔹 **Pump flow equation**: $V = \dot{V} \cdot t$; use $\rho_w = 0.997 \text{ g/mL}$ for mass ↔ volume conversion.
- 🔹 **Edge filter**: always apply a **moving average** (N = 8–16) to load-cell readings to suppress cup vibration.
- 🔹 **Cloud layer** typically uses **MQTT** with a JSON payload `{ratio, temp, tds, timestamp}`; Node-RED or Blynk for dashboards.
- 🔹 **Architecture mantra**: *Sensor → Edge Compute → Actuator → Cloud* (a recurring 14-mark question pattern in KTU Module 4).
- 🔹 **Common trap**: students often confuse **brew ratio direction** (water/coffee, not coffee/water).
- 🔹 **Exam mantra**: always state the **assumption** (e.g., ρ = 0.997 g/mL at 25 °C) before plugging numbers in.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Asynchronous Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT504 - PROGRAMMING FOR INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Asynchronous Communication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Asynchronous Communication** is a serial data transmission paradigm in which information is exchanged between two endpoints **without the use of a shared, continuously distributed clock signal**. Instead, both transmitter and receiver are pre-configured with identical timing parameters (commonly called *line parameters* or *communication parameters*), and every transmitted unit of data (typically a byte/character) is **self-framed using dedicated Start and Stop bits** that delimit the payload.

In the context of the KTU 2024 *Programming for Internet of Things* syllabus, asynchronous communication is implemented principally through the **UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver–Transmitter)** peripheral, and is foundational to IoT inter-node links such as **RS-232, RS-422, RS-485, and the TTL-level serial consoles** used to debug microcontrollers (ESP32, Arduino, Raspberry Pi Pico, STM32, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 4):** Students must be able to *program UART-based asynchronous links*, justify the choice of *baud rate, parity, and stop-bit configuration*, and compute *effective throughput* and *frame latency* for an IoT device.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postcard" Model

Imagine two friends, **Alice** and **Bob**, separated by a mountain. They cannot see or hear a ticking clock. They agree in advance: *"Every letter you send will begin with the word 'HELLO' (the **Start bit**) and end with the word 'BYE' (the **Stop bit**). We will read at a speed of 5 words per minute (the **Baud rate**)."*

Now suppose Alice wants to send the character `'K'`. She writes a postcard:

`[ START ] K [ PARITY ] [ STOP ]`

When Bob receives it, the word *"HELLO"* tells him: *"Wake up, a packet is starting."* He then reads exactly **8 letters (the data bits)** at 5 words/min, optionally checks a single checksum letter (the **parity bit**), and finally waits for *"BYE"* to confirm the packet ended. Because there is no live clock wire, this works **as long as both sides agreed on the speed and frame shape beforehand** — that is the essence of *asynchronous* communication.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** *Asynchronous* literally means *"not at the same time."* Each side transmits/reads **independently**, and synchronisation is re-established *byte-by-byte* using framing bits, not a continuous clock.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The most important pre-agreed numbers in asynchronous links are:

- **Baud rate (symbols/sec):** **9600, 19200, 38400, 57600, 115200** are the *de facto* standard rates used in IoT firmware.
- **Word/character length:** typically **5, 6, 7, or 8** data bits (8 is dominant in modern IoT).
- **Parity:** **None (N), Even (E), or Odd (O)** — a 1-bit error-detection mechanism.
- **Stop bits:** **1 or 2** — gives the receiver time to re-synchronise.
- **Standard notation:** *115200-8N1* → 115200 baud, 8 data bits, No parity, 1 stop bit.

> [!TIP]
> In your KTU viva, if asked *"What does 9600-8E1 mean?"*, answer confidently: *"9600 baud, 8 data bits, Even parity, 1 stop bit."* This is the single most-asked viva question on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How Asynchronous Transmission Works

A UART frame is transmitted **least-significant-bit (LSB) first**. The line is held **HIGH (logic 1, "Mark")** when idle.

1. **Idle state (Line = 1):** No transmission. Both ends know nothing is moving.
2. **Start bit (Line pulled LOW for 1 bit-period):** A 1→0 transition alerts the receiver that a new character is beginning. The receiver uses this falling edge to start its internal sampling clock.
3. **Data bits (5–8 bits, LSB first):** The actual payload. The receiver samples each bit at the **centre of its bit-period** (typically at the 50 % mark after the start edge) to maximise noise immunity.
4. **Parity bit (optional, 1 bit):** Sender computes parity over the data bits; receiver re-computes and compares. **Even parity** = total number of 1s (data + parity) is even; **Odd parity** = total number of 1s is odd.
5. **Stop bit(s) (Line = 1 for 1 or 2 bit-periods):** Allows the receiver to re-arm itself for the next character.
6. **Return to Idle:** Line stays HIGH until the next Start bit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why sample at the centre of the bit?** Because the transmitter and receiver clocks will inevitably drift. Sampling in the middle gives the **maximum ±½ bit-period tolerance** for clock mismatch. This is a *classic KTU 2-mark question.*

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Formula / Definition** | **Standard Unit** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $T_{bit}$ | Duration of one bit | $T_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{B}$ | seconds (s) |
| $N_{frame}$ | Total bits per UART frame | $N_{frame} = 1 + N_d + N_p + N_s$ | bits |
| $T_{frame}$ | Time to transmit one full frame | $T_{frame} = \dfrac{N_{frame}}{B}$ | seconds (s) |
| $\eta$ | Framing efficiency (useful-data ratio) | $\eta = \dfrac{N_d}{N_{frame}}$ | dimensionless (0–1) |
| $R_{eff}$ | Effective data throughput | $R_{eff} = B \times \eta$ | bits/second (bps) |
| $T_{byte}$ | Time to send 1 byte of *payload* | $T_{byte} = \dfrac{N_{frame}}{B \times N_d}$ | seconds (s) |
| $T_{msg}$ | Time to send an $M$-byte message | $T_{msg} = M \times T_{frame} = M \times \dfrac{N_{frame}}{B}$ | seconds (s) |
| $D_{max}$ | Max clock-drift tolerable | $D_{max} = \pm \dfrac{1}{2 \times N_{frame}} \times 100\,\%$ | percent (%) |

Where: $B$ = baud rate, $N_d$ = number of data bits, $N_p$ = parity bits (0 or 1), $N_s$ = stop bits (1 or 2).

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in IoT Engineering

- **Sensor Telemetry:** ESP32/Arduino boards send temperature/humidity readings to a Raspberry Pi gateway over UART-TTL.
- **GPS Modules:** NEO-6M GPS receivers default to 9600-8N1 UART output.
- **Industrial Buses:** RS-485 (multi-drop async) is the workhorse of factory-floor IoT (Modbus RTU runs on it).
- **Debug Console:** Linux boot logs, AT-command modems (SIM800/ESP-01), and BIOS output are all async serial.
- **Edge AI Modules:** Many TinyML boards expose UART as the only configurable interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Although modern high-speed IoT links (Wi-Fi, BLE, SPI, I²C) dominate, **async UART is still the universal "escape hatch"** — when wireless fails, when firmware needs a console, or when a legacy sensor must be integrated, async serial is *always* the fallback. Mastering it is non-negotiable for an IoT engineer.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivations — Frame, Timing & Throughput

### **Problem 1 — Frame composition and bit-period**
A KTU lab uses **9600-8E1** configuration. Determine: (i) total bits per frame, (ii) duration of one bit, (iii) duration of one full frame.

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.**
From *9600-8E1*: $B = 9600$ baud, $N_d = 8$, parity = Even (so $N_p = 1$), stop bits = 1 (so $N_s = 1$).

**Step 2 — Compute total frame size.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{frame} &= 1 \;(\text{Start}) + N_d + N_p + N_s \\
&= 1 + 8 + 1 + 1 \\
&= 11 \;\text{bits/frame}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Bit period.**

$$
T_{bit} = \frac{1}{B} = \frac{1}{9600} \approx 1.04167 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{s} \approx 104.17\;\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Frame duration.**

$$
T_{frame} = \frac{N_{frame}}{B} = \frac{11}{9600} \approx 1.14583 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{s} \approx 1.146\;\text{ms}
$$

---

### **Problem 2 — Framing efficiency and effective data rate**
For the same *9600-8E1* link, find: (i) efficiency $\eta$, (ii) effective throughput $R_{eff}$, (iii) time to send 1 KB (1024 bytes) of sensor data.

**Step 1 — Efficiency.**

$$
\eta = \frac{N_d}{N_{frame}} = \frac{8}{11} \approx 0.7273 \;\Rightarrow\; 72.73\,\%
$$

**Step 2 — Effective data rate.**

$$
R_{eff} = B \times \eta = 9600 \times \frac{8}{11} \approx 6981.82\;\text{bps}
$$

**Step 3 — Time for 1 KB.**

First, total bits for 1024 bytes of payload (each byte = one frame of 11 bits):

$$
\text{Total bits} = 1024 \times 11 = 11264\;\text{bits}
$$

$$
T_{1KB} = \frac{11264}{9600} \approx 1.17333\;\text{s}
$$

---

### **Problem 3 — Effect of switching to 115200-8N1**
A drone firmware team upgrades its link to **115200-8N1**. Recompute efficiency and time to send the same 1 KB.

**Step 1 — Parameters:** $B = 115200$, $N_d = 8$, $N_p = 0$, $N_s = 1$.

**Step 2 — Frame size.**

$$
N_{frame} = 1 + 8 + 0 + 1 = 10\;\text{bits/frame}
$$

**Step 3 — Efficiency.**

$$
\eta = \frac{8}{10} = 0.80 \;\Rightarrow\; 80\,\%
$$

**Step 4 — Bit period.**

$$
T_{bit} = \frac{1}{115200} \approx 8.681\;\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — 1 KB transmission time.**

$$
T_{1KB} = \frac{1024 \times 10}{115200} = \frac{10240}{115200} \approx 0.0889\;\text{s} \approx 88.89\;\text{ms}
$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Common Asynchronous Configurations

This is a *high-yield KTU comparison table* often asked as a 7-mark analytical question.

| **Configuration** | **$B$ (baud)** | **$N_d$** | **$N_p$** | **$N_s$** | **$N_{frame}$** | **$\eta$** | **$T_{1KB}$** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 9600-8N1 (classic debug) | 9600 | 8 | 0 | 1 | 10 | 80.00 % | 1066.67 ms |
| 9600-8E1 (noisy industrial) | 9600 | 8 | 1 | 1 | 11 | 72.73 % | 1145.83 ms |
| 115200-8N1 (ESP/Arduino IDE) | 115200 | 8 | 0 | 1 | 10 | 80.00 % | 88.89 ms |
| 115200-8N2 (long cable) | 115200 | 8 | 0 | 2 | 11 | 72.73 % | 97.78 ms |
| 1200-7E1 (legacy GSM AT) | 1200 | 7 | 1 | 1 | 10 | 70.00 % | 8533.33 ms |

> [!NOTE]
> **Inference (important for KTU answers):** Increasing **stop bits** or adding **parity** *increases robustness* (gives the receiver more time / detects single-bit errors) but *reduces* $\eta$. Engineers must therefore trade off **reliability** vs **throughput** based on cable length, EMI, and data criticality.

## 3.3 Maximum Clock-Drift Tolerance Derivation

The receiver re-synchronises on the Start bit's falling edge. Over the $N_{frame}$ bit-periods of a frame, the receiver's local clock may drift by:

$$
D_{max} = \frac{T_{bit}/2}{N_{frame} \times T_{bit}} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot N_{frame}}
$$

For *9600-8N1* ($N_{frame}=10$):

$$
D_{max} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10} = 0.05 = \pm 5\,\%
$$

For *115200-8N1*:

$$
D_{max} = \frac{1}{20} = \pm 5\,\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why ±5 %?** Because most cheap asynchronous oscillators (e.g., the *16×* baud-rate generator inside microcontrollers) are designed with crystal tolerances well within 2–3 %, leaving 2–3 % safety margin — a fact frequently tested in KTU Module-4 questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 UART Frame Structure (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following diagram visualises the *bit-by-bit composition* of a single asynchronous UART frame for the configuration *115200-8N1, transmitting character 'K' (= 0x4B = 0100 1011 in binary)*. Note that bits are transmitted **LSB-first** on the wire.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IDLE_A["IDLE\nLine HIGH\n(Mark)"]:::idle
    START["START BIT\n0\n1 bit-period"]:::start
    D0["D0 = 1\nLSB"]:::data
    D1["D1 = 1"]:::data
    D2["D2 = 0"]:::data
    D3["D3 = 1"]:::data
    D4["D4 = 0"]:::data
    D5["D5 = 0"]:::data
    D6["D6 = 1"]:::data
    D7["D7 = 0\nMSB"]:::data
    PAR["PARITY\nNone (N)"]:::skip
    STOP["STOP BIT\n1\n1 bit-period"]:::stop
    IDLE_B["IDLE\nLine HIGH"]:::idle

    IDLE_A --> START --> D0 --> D1 --> D2 --> D3 --> D4 --> D5 --> D6 --> D7 --> PAR --> STOP --> IDLE_B

    classDef idle fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#555,color:#000
    classDef start fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef data fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef stop fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef skip fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#9E9E9E,color:#000,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each block represents **one bit-period** ($T_{bit} = 1/B$). The receiver samples the **middle** of every coloured data block. The receiver's clock is *re-started* on the falling edge of the START block.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of an Asynchronous IoT Link

The following block-level schematic shows the typical hardware/software flow inside a microcontroller's UART peripheral driving a sensor IoT link.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    APP["Application Layer\n(Sensor data buffer)"]:::app
    TXFIFO["Transmit FIFO Buffer\n(8/16/32 bytes deep)"]:::fifo
    TXSHIFT["Transmit Shift Register\n(parallel-to-serial,\nLSB-first)"]:::shift
    BAUDGEN["Baud-Rate Generator\n(Programmable Divider\nfrom APB/PCLK)"]:::baud
    PIN_TX["TX Pin\nDrives UART_TX line"]:::pin
    WIRE["Physical Medium\nPCB trace /\nTwisted pair RS-485"]:::wire
    PIN_RX["RX Pin\nSamples UART_RX line"]:::pin
    RXSHIFT["Receive Shift Register\n(serial-to-parallel,\nLSB-first)"]:::shift
    RXFIFO["Receive FIFO Buffer"]:::fifo
    PARCHK["Parity Checker\n& Framing Validator\n(start/stop bit check)"]:::chk
    ISR["Interrupt / DMA\nHandler (NVIC)"]:::isr
    APP2["Application Layer\n(Decoded byte ready)"]:::app

    APP --> TXFIFO --> TXSHIFT --> PIN_TX --> WIRE --> PIN_RX --> RXSHIFT --> RXFIFO --> PARCHK --> ISR --> APP2
    BAUDGEN -.feeds clock.-> TXSHIFT
    BAUDGEN -.feeds clock.-> RXSHIFT
    BAUDGEN -.feeds clock.-> PARCHK

    classDef app fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef fifo fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    classDef shift fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef baud fill:#DCEDC8,stroke:#33691E,color:#000
    classDef pin fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
    classDef wire fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#424242,color:#000
    classDef chk fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef isr fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#004D40,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** The *Baud-Rate Generator* is what differentiates the **transmitter's and receiver's internal clocks** from the actual line rate $B$. A divisor $D$ in a PCLK of $f_{PCLK}$ gives $B = f_{PCLK} / (16 \times D)$ for the classic *16× oversampling* scheme. This is a frequent 5–7 mark derivation question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1.** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*
**Define asynchronous serial communication. List any four standard baud rates used in IoT systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Asynchronous serial communication is a data-transmission method in which data is sent **without a shared clock signal**; instead, each character is framed with **Start and Stop bits**, and both ends pre-agree on the *baud rate*, *data bits*, *parity*, and *stop bits* (2 marks).
Four standard baud rates used in IoT are: **300, 1200, 9600, 115200** (any four of 110, 300, 1200, 2400, 4800, 9600, 19200, 38400, 57600, 115200 are accepted) (1 mark).

---

### **Q2.** *[KTU University Exam — May 2024]*
**What is the function of a Start bit and a Stop bit in a UART frame? Why is the line held in the *Mark* state during idle?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Start bit** (a logic-0 of 1 bit-period) signals the *beginning* of a new character; its 1→0 falling edge is used by the receiver to **synchronise its internal sampling clock** (1.5 marks).
The **Stop bit** (a logic-1 of 1 or 2 bit-periods) marks the *end* of the character and gives the receiver time to **re-arm for the next Start bit** (1 mark).
The line is held HIGH (the *Mark* state) during idle so that a falling edge — which is unambiguously a Start bit — is easily detected (0.5 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

#### **(a)** *[7 marks]*
**With a neat block diagram, explain the internal architecture and working of a UART transmitter.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

A UART transmitter consists of three principal blocks:

1. **Baud-Rate Generator (2 marks):**
   - Takes the system clock $f_{PCLK}$ and divides it by a programmable divisor $D$ to produce the **bit-rate clock**.
   - Typical relationship: $B = \dfrac{f_{PCLK}}{16 \times D}$ (for 16× oversampling).
   - Common divisor values are pre-computed and stored in look-up tables for 9600, 115200, etc.

2. **Transmit Shift Register (TSR) (3 marks):**
   - Receives a parallel byte from the **Transmit Holding Register (THR)**, which in turn is fed by the **Transmit FIFO**.
   - On the Start-bit command, it shifts out bits **LSB-first** at the rate of $T_{bit}$.
   - After 8 data bits, it optionally appends the parity bit and the stop bit(s) automatically.

3. **Transmit Control Logic (2 marks):**
   - Generates the Start bit (drives TX LOW).
   - Counts bit-periods to determine when to stop.
   - Raises a **TX-complete interrupt** (or DMA request) so the CPU can load the next byte.

**Working sequence (full marks require the sequence):**
> CPU writes byte → FIFO → THR → TSR detects *TX empty* → generates Start bit → shifts out 8 data bits (LSB first) → appends parity (if enabled) → drives Stop bit(s) → returns line to idle HIGH → triggers interrupt.

*(Attach the diagram from Section 4.2; the *TX half* suffices for 7 marks.)*

---

#### **(b)** *[7 marks]*
**A temperature sensor transmits 2 KB of data to a gateway over UART configured at 38400-8O1. Calculate: (i) total bits transmitted, (ii) total transmission time, (iii) effective throughput, (iv) framing efficiency.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $B = 38400$ baud, $N_d = 8$, parity = Odd ($N_p = 1$), $N_s = 1$, payload = 2 KB = 2048 bytes.

**Step 1 — Bits per frame (1 mark):**

$$
N_{frame} = 1 + 8 + 1 + 1 = 11\;\text{bits/frame}
$$

**Step 2 — Total bits transmitted (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Total bits} = 2048 \times 11 = 22528\;\text{bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Bit period and total time (2 marks):**

$$
T_{bit} = \frac{1}{38400} \approx 26.04\;\mu\text{s}
$$

$$
T_{msg} = \frac{22528}{38400} \approx 0.5867\;\text{s} \approx 586.67\;\text{ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Effective throughput (1.5 marks):**

$$
R_{eff} = B \times \eta = 38400 \times \frac{8}{11} \approx 27927.27\;\text{bps} \approx 27.93\;\text{kbps}
$$

**Step 5 — Framing efficiency (1.5 marks):**

$$
\eta = \frac{N_d}{N_{frame}} = \frac{8}{11} \approx 0.7273 = 72.73\,\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** A very common mistake students make is to compute total time as $\frac{2048 \times 8}{38400}$ — i.e., they **forget the framing bits** (Start, Parity, Stop). This gives an answer of 426.67 ms instead of the correct 586.67 ms and costs **2–3 marks**. Always use $N_{frame}$, not $N_d$, in the numerator.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

#### **(a)** *[7 marks]*
**Compare synchronous and asynchronous serial communication. State two real-world IoT protocols/examples for each.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Synchronous** | **Asynchronous** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Clock line | Shared clock required | No clock line |
| Framing | Block/frame-level sync | Per-byte Start/Stop bits |
| Overhead | Lower (no per-byte framing) | Higher (~20–30 % overhead) |
| Speed | High (MHz to GHz) | Moderate (≤ 1 Mbps typical) |
| Hardware cost | Higher (extra wire + PLL) | Lower (2 wires only) |
| Drift tolerance | Continuously re-synced | ±5 % per frame |
| Typical use | High-speed buses, display links | Debug consoles, GPS, Modbus |
| **IoT example (2 marks)** | SPI, I²C, USB | **UART, RS-232, RS-485, Bluetooth SPP** |

---

#### **(b)** *[7 marks]*
**Describe the RS-485 asynchronous standard. Explain why it is preferred over RS-232 in industrial IoT deployments.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**RS-485 physical layer (3 marks):**
- Uses a **differential pair** (twisted pair) labelled 'A' and 'B'; the receiver reads the *voltage difference* $V_{A} - V_{B}$.
- Logic is defined by polarity: $V_{A} - V_{B} > +200\,\text{mV} \rightarrow$ Mark (1); $< -200\,\text{mV} \rightarrow$ Space (0).
- Supports **multi-drop** networks: up to **32 unit loads (≈ 256 nodes)** on a single bus.
- Maximum cable length up to **1200 m** at lower baud rates; max signalling rate **10 Mbps** at <15 m.
- Half-duplex on 2 wires or full-duplex on 4 wires.

**Why RS-485 beats RS-232 in industrial IoT (4 marks):**
1. **Differential signalling** rejects common-mode noise from motors, VFDs, and relays — essential on factory floors.
2. **Multi-drop capability** lets one gateway poll dozens of sensors (RS-232 is strictly point-to-point).
3. **Long cable runs** (1.2 km vs RS-232's 15 m) suit distributed plant topologies.
4. **Higher noise immunity** thanks to ±7 V common-mode range and ±200 mV receiver threshold.
5. **Industry-standard protocol stack** (Modbus RTU) rides on RS-485, making it the *de facto* industrial IoT link.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students incorrectly state *"RS-485 is faster than RS-232."* It can be, but the **fundamental** advantage is **noise immunity + multi-drop + long distance**, not raw speed. Confusing these will cost you at least **2 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⏱ **Asynchronous = no shared clock**; sync is re-established *byte-by-byte* via Start bit.
- 🔢 **Frame = 1 Start + $N_d$ data + $N_p$ parity + $N_s$ stop** bits; data sent **LSB-first**.
- ⏲ **$T_{bit} = 1/B$** is your single most important formula.
- 📊 **Efficiency $\eta = N_d / N_{frame}$** — *never* use $N_d$ alone to compute transmission time.
- 🚦 **Idle line = HIGH (Mark)**; a falling edge = Start bit; receiver samples at the **centre** of each bit-period.
- 🧮 **Clock-drift tolerance** $= \pm 50\,\% / N_{frame}$; equals **±5 %** for typical 10-bit frames.
- 🏭 **RS-232** = point-to-point, short range, single-ended (legacy PC COM port).
- 🏭 **RS-485** = multi-drop, long range, differential (industrial IoT / Modbus).
- 📡 **Default IoT baud rates:** 9600-8N1 (sensors), 115200-8N1 (ESP/Arduino console), 38400-8N1 (GPS).
- ⚠️ **Trade-off:** More stop bits or parity ⇒ higher reliability, **lower** $\eta$ and throughput.
- 🎯 **KTU viva favourite:** *"Explain 9600-8E1"* — memorise the notation and a numeric example.
- 🧠 **Mnemonic:** **"S-D-P-S"** = Start, Data, Parity, Stop → the order of a UART frame.
- 💡 **Engineering edge:** In your IoT projects, always match baud rate, parity, and stop bits **on both ends** — mismatches are the #1 cause of "garbled console" errors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coffee Brewing Recipe
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT504 - PROGRAMMING FOR INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Coffee Brewing Recipe

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Internet of Things (IoT)**, a **Coffee Brewing Recipe** refers to a structured, parameterized set of instructions, sensor thresholds, control sequences, and timing logic that an embedded system (microcontroller) executes to automate the preparation of coffee. The recipe is encoded as software (firmware) running on a connected device and may be transmitted, modified, or monitored remotely via IoT protocols (MQTT, HTTP, CoAP).

A brewing recipe encapsulates the following engineering parameters:

- **Water Temperature Profile** ($T_w$): Target heating curve, typically **90 °C – 96 °C** for optimal extraction.
- **Coffee-to-Water Ratio** ($R_{cw}$): Mass-based ratio, standard golden ratio of **1:15 to 1:18**.
- **Brew Time** ($t_b$): Total contact duration, generally **3 – 5 minutes**.
- **Grind Size** ($G$): Particle diameter influencing extraction rate.
- **Pre-infusion Phase**: A short low-pressure wetting stage before full extraction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PBCIT504 – Module 4):** The *Coffee Brewing Recipe* problem is used as a case study to teach **sensor-actuator integration, state-machine programming, and IoT data logging**. Students are expected to design firmware using embedded C/C++/MicroPython.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **traditional South Indian *chembu* (coffee filter)** operated by a skilled *kettan* (grandmother). She watches the boil, controls the flame, and times the *decoction* drip perfectly. Now, replace the *kettan* with a **microcontroller (ESP32/Arduino)**, replace her eyes with **temperature & water-level sensors**, and replace her hands with a **relay-controlled heating element and solenoid valve**. The **recipe** is the digital instruction manual she used to follow — but now it lives inside the device, accessible from anywhere via Wi-Fi.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Idea:** A coffee brewing recipe in IoT is a *cyber-physical control program* that converts physical brewing variables (temperature, time, mass) into a deterministic, repeatable, and remotely-controllable process.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold-Highlighted)

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|-----------|--------|----------------|------|
| Optimal brew temperature | $T_w$ | **92 – 96** | °C |
| Coffee : Water ratio | $R_{cw}$ | **1 : 16** | g : mL |
| Pre-infusion duration | $t_p$ | **30 – 45** | s |
| Total brew time | $t_b$ | **180 – 300** | s |
| TDS (Total Dissolved Solids) | $\sigma$ | **1.15 – 1.45** | % |
| Extraction Yield | $Y_e$ | **18 – 22** | % |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown – The IoT Brewing Pipeline

The brewing process is modeled as a **Finite State Machine (FSM)** executed by the microcontroller:

1. **IDLE State**: System awaits user command via MQTT topic `coffee/cmd` or physical button.
2. **INIT State**: Sensors self-calibrate; water reservoir level verified via ultrasonic/ float sensor.
3. **HEAT State**: Heating element activated via relay. PID controller drives water temperature toward $T_{target}$.
4. **PRE_INFUSE State**: Pump delivers small water slug (≈ 2× coffee mass) for blooming; $t_p$ countdown.
5. **BREW State**: Main pump delivers water at controlled flow rate; $t_b$ timer active.
6. **HOLD State**: Keep-warm plate maintains serving temperature $T_{hold} \approx 70\,°C$.
7. **DONE / NOTIFY State**: IoT message published to `coffee/status` topic; logs pushed to cloud.

> [!NOTE]
> **PID Control** is the heart of the heating loop. The error signal $e(t) = T_{target} - T_{measured}(t)$ is fed into Proportional + Integral + Derivative terms to compute the relay/gate duty cycle.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Description | Units |
|---------|-----------|-------------|-------|
| Extraction Yield | $Y_e = \dfrac{m_{dissolved}}{m_{coffee}} \times 100$ | Mass of solubles extracted | % |
| Brew Ratio | $R_{cw} = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{coffee}}$ | Water-to-coffee ratio | dimensionless |
| Required Water Mass | $m_w = R_{cw} \times m_{coffee}$ | Total water for given dose | g or mL |
| PID Output | $u(t) = K_p\,e(t) + K_i\!\int\! e(t)\,dt + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | Heater control signal | PWM 0–255 |
| Energy to Heat Water | $Q = m\,c_p\,\Delta T$ | $c_p = 4.186$ kJ/(kg·K) | kJ |
| Flow Rate | $\dot{V} = \dfrac{V_{total}}{t_b}$ | Pump volumetric rate | mL/s |
| Extraction Time | $t_b = \dfrac{V_{total}}{\dot{V}}$ | Brew duration | s |
| Sensor Resolution | $\Delta T_{res} = \dfrac{T_{max} - T_{min}}{2^{N}}$ | For $N$-bit ADC | °C/LSB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Never confuse brew *ratio* (1:16) with brew *yield* (%). Ratio is volume-based; yield is mass-based and depends on grind size.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Home Appliances**: De'Longhi, Smarter Coffee, and Nespresso use exactly this FSM pattern.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**: Same state-machine + MQTT architecture applies to chemical dosing, food processing, and pharmaceutical mixing.
- **Precision Agriculture**: Recipe-driven irrigation controllers use identical parameterized automation logic.
- **Healthcare**: Automated IV-fluid preparation uses sensor-feedback brewing principles.

## 2.4 IoT Communication Layer

The recipe can be **remotely updated** by publishing a JSON payload to the MQTT broker:

```json
{
  "recipe_name": "V60 Pour-Over",
  "coffee_g": 18,
  "water_g": 288,
  "temp_c": 94,
  "pre_infusion_s": 35,
  "brew_time_s": 180,
  "grind_size": "Medium-Fine"
}
```

The ESP32 subscribes to `home/coffee/recipe`, parses the JSON, and writes the parameters to non-volatile memory (EEPROM/Preferences) for execution.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Required Water Mass for a Given Coffee Dose

**Problem:** A user selects a recipe with $m_{coffee} = 22$ g and brew ratio $R_{cw} = 1:16$. Compute the required water mass.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation:**

$$m_w = R_{cw} \times m_{coffee}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$m_w = 16 \times 22 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$m_w = 352 \text{ g} \equiv 352 \text{ mL}$$

> **Interpretation:** The microcontroller must command the pump to dispense exactly 352 mL of water.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy Required to Heat Water to Brewing Temperature

**Problem:** Compute the thermal energy required to heat $m_w = 352$ g of water from ambient $T_{amb} = 28\,°C$ to $T_{target} = 94\,°C$.

**Step 1 — Governing equation (Specific Heat):**

$$Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 2 — Compute temperature difference:**

$$\Delta T = T_{target} - T_{amb} = 94 - 28 = 66\,°C \equiv 66 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute ($c_p = 4186$ J/(kg·K), $m = 0.352$ kg):**

$$Q = 0.352 \times 4186 \times 66$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate step by step:**

$$Q = 0.352 \times 4186 = 1473.47 \text{ J/K}$$

$$Q = 1473.47 \times 66 = 97{,}249.2 \text{ J}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 97.25 \text{ kJ}}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** A 1500 W kettle supplies this in ≈ 65 s, plus losses.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: PID Heater Control Output

**Problem:** At time $t$, measured temperature $T_m = 88\,°C$, target $T_{set} = 94\,°C$, so error $e = 6\,°C$. Gains: $K_p = 30$, $K_i = 0.5$, $K_d = 10$. Past error sum $\int e = 40$, derivative $\frac{de}{dt} = 1.2\,°C/s$. Compute PWM output.

**Step 1 — PID equation:**

$$u(t) = K_p\,e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Compute each term:**

$$u_P = 30 \times 6 = 180$$

$$u_I = 0.5 \times 40 = 20$$

$$u_D = 10 \times 1.2 = 12$$

**Step 3 — Sum the contributions:**

$$u = 180 + 20 + 12 = 212$$

**Step 4 — Clamp to 8-bit PWM range (0–255):**

$$\boxed{u_{PWM} = 212 \;(\approx 83\% \text{ duty cycle})}$$

> **Result:** The relay/MOSFET driver is commanded at 83% duty, sustaining rapid heating without overshoot.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Recipe State Transitions

| Current State | Trigger Event | Next State | Actuator Action | Sensor Check |
|---------------|---------------|-----------|-----------------|--------------|
| IDLE | Button / MQTT `START` | INIT | None | Water level |
| INIT | Level OK | HEAT | Pump off, Heater off | DS18B20 init |
| HEAT | $T \geq 90\,°C$ | PRE_INFUSE | Heater reduce | $T_m$ read |
| PRE_INFUSE | $t_p$ elapsed | BREW | Pump ON (low flow) | Flow sensor |
| BREW | $m_w$ dispensed | HOLD | Pump OFF | Scale reading |
| HOLD | 10 min timeout | DONE | Plate heater OFF | None |
| DONE | New command | IDLE | All OFF | Reset flags |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IoT Coffee Brewing System — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Cloud["CLOUD LAYER"]
        dashboard[Dashboard MQTT]
        broker[MQTT Broker]
        recipes[Recipe Database]
    end

    subgraph Edge["EDGE LAYER - ESP32"]
        wifi[Wi-Fi Module]
        mqttc[MQTT Client]
        fsm[Brewing FSM]
        pidc[PID Controller]
        parser[JSON Parser]
    end

    subgraph Sensors["SENSOR LAYER"]
        temp1[DS18B20 Temp Sensor]
        lvl1[Ultrasonic Water Level]
        wt1[Load Cell Weight]
        flow1[Flow Sensor YF-S201]
    end

    subgraph Actuators["ACTUATOR LAYER"]
        relay1[SSR Heater Relay]
        pump1[12V DC Pump]
        valve1[Solenoid Valve]
        buzz1[Buzzer Indicator]
    end

    dashboard <--> broker
    recipes --> broker
    broker <--> mqttc
    mqttc --> parser
    parser --> fsm
    fsm --> pidc
    pidc --> relay1
    fsm --> pump1
    fsm --> valve1
    fsm --> buzz1
    temp1 --> fsm
    lvl1 --> fsm
    wt1 --> fsm
    flow1 --> fsm
```

## 4.2 Brewing Process — Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant App as Mobile App
    participant ESP as ESP32 MCU
    participant Heater
    participant Pump
    participant Sensor as Temp Sensor

    User->>App: Select "V60 Recipe"
    App->>ESP: MQTT Publish coffee/cmd {dose:18g, temp:94C}
    ESP->>Sensor: Read T_water
    Sensor-->>ESP: T = 28C
    ESP->>Heater: PID PWM = 212
    Heater-->>Sensor: T rising
    loop Until T >= 90C
        ESP->>Sensor: Poll
        Sensor-->>ESP: T = 92C
    end
    ESP->>Pump: Pre-infuse 36mL for 35s
    ESP->>Pump: Main brew 252mL over 180s
    Pump-->>ESP: Flow pulses counted
    ESP->>App: MQTT Publish coffee/status DONE
    App-->>User: "Your coffee is ready!"
```

## 4.3 Brewing FSM — State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IDLE
    IDLE --> INIT : start command
    INIT --> HEAT : water level OK
    INIT --> ALERT : low water
    ALERT --> IDLE : refill confirmed
    HEAT --> PRE_INFUSE : T reaches 90C
    PRE_INFUSE --> BREW : timer done
    BREW --> HOLD : target volume dispensed
    HOLD --> DONE : 10 min elapsed
    DONE --> IDLE : reset
    HOLD --> IDLE : manual stop
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
*Define a "Coffee Brewing Recipe" in the context of IoT systems. List any four programmable parameters of such a recipe.*

**Model Answer:**

In IoT, a coffee brewing recipe is a **structured set of parameterized control instructions** executed by an embedded controller to automate coffee preparation with sensor feedback.

Four programmable parameters:
1. **Water temperature profile** $T_w$ (°C)
2. **Coffee-to-water ratio** $R_{cw}$
3. **Pre-infusion duration** $t_p$ (seconds)
4. **Total brew time** $t_b$ (seconds)

*(Any other valid parameter such as grind size, flow rate, or TDS target accepted.)* — **(3 Marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
*Explain the role of a **Finite State Machine (FSM)** in implementing an automated coffee brewing process.*

**Model Answer:**

An FSM models the brewing process as a series of well-defined states — `IDLE → INIT → HEAT → PRE_INFUSE → BREW → HOLD → DONE`. Each state has:
- **Entry actions** (e.g., switch ON pump),
- **Exit conditions** (e.g., timer expiry, temperature threshold),
- **Transition events** (e.g., sensor triggers).

The FSM ensures **deterministic, repeatable, and safe operation**, preventing actuator conflicts (e.g., heater and pump both ON simultaneously). — **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of an IoT-based automated coffee brewing system**, clearly showing the **sensor, actuator, edge (microcontroller), and cloud layers**. Label the communication protocol used at each interface. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a recipe requiring $m_{coffee} = 20$ g, brew ratio $R_{cw} = 1:15$, target temperature $T_{set} = 93\,°C$, and ambient $T_{amb} = 27\,°C$:
   - (i) Compute the required water mass $m_w$.  
   - (ii) Compute the thermal energy $Q$ required.  
   - (iii) Write the **PID control equation** and explain the role of each term. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Architecture (7 Marks)**

The system has **four hierarchical layers**:

| Layer | Components | Function |
|-------|-----------|----------|
| **Sensor Layer** | DS18B20, Load cell, Ultrasonic sensor, YF-S201 flow sensor | Acquire physical brewing variables |
| **Actuator Layer** | SSR (heater), 12 V DC pump, solenoid valve, buzzer | Execute control commands |
