| **Edge Layer (ESP32)** | MQTT client, JSON parser, PID, FSM | Local decision-making & control |
| **Cloud Layer** | MQTT broker, Node-RED dashboard, Firebase | Remote monitoring, recipe update |

**Protocols:**
- Sensor ↔ ESP32: **1-Wire / I²C**
- ESP32 ↔ Cloud: **MQTT over TCP/IP (Wi-Fi)**
- Cloud ↔ User: **HTTP / WebSocket**

*(A clean block diagram as in Section 4.1 must accompany the answer.)* — **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical + PID (7 Marks)**

**(i) Required water mass:**

$$m_w = R_{cw} \times m_{coffee} = 15 \times 20 = 300 \text{ g} \equiv 300 \text{ mL}$$

**(ii) Thermal energy required:**

$$\Delta T = 93 - 27 = 66\,°C, \quad m = 0.300 \text{ kg}$$

$$Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 0.300 \times 4186 \times 66 = 82{,}882.8 \text{ J} \approx 82.88 \text{ kJ}$$

**(iii) PID equation:**

$$u(t) = K_p\,e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

- **Proportional ($K_p e$)** — Reacts to current error; provides immediate correction.
- **Integral ($K_i \int e\,dt$)** — Eliminates steady-state offset.
- **Derivative ($K_d \dfrac{de}{dt}$)** — Anticipates future error; dampens overshoot.

— **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Design a **Finite State Machine (FSM)** for an IoT coffee brewer. List all states, transitions, and triggers. Explain how sensor failures are handled. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a relevant example, explain how **MQTT protocol** is used to dynamically update a brewing recipe on an ESP32-based coffee machine. Include a sample JSON payload. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### **Part (a) — FSM Design (7 Marks)**

States: `IDLE → INIT → HEAT → PRE_INFUSE → BREW → HOLD → DONE → IDLE`

| Transition | Trigger | Guard Condition |
|------------|---------|-----------------|
| IDLE → INIT | MQTT/Btn START | None |
| INIT → HEAT | Sensors init OK | Water level > 200 mL |
| HEAT → PRE_INFUSE | $T \geq 90\,°C$ | Timeout 5 min fail-safe |
| PRE_INFUSE → BREW | Timer 35 s elapsed | None |
| BREW → HOLD | Volume dispensed = $m_w$ | Flow total ≥ target |
| HOLD → DONE | 10 min elapsed | None |

**Failure handling:**
- **Sensor timeout** → FSM enters `ERROR` state, halts actuators, raises MQTT alarm.
- **Over-temperature** ($T > 100\,°C$) → Emergency heater cutoff, publish to `coffee/alarm`.
- **Low water** → Reverse to `IDLE`, request refill via cloud notification.

— **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Part (b) — MQTT Recipe Update (7 Marks)**

**Workflow:**

1. Mobile app publishes JSON to topic `home/coffee/recipe` on broker `broker.hivemq.com:1883`.
2. ESP32 subscribes to this topic via `mqttClient.subscribe("home/coffee/recipe")`.
3. On message arrival, ArduinoJson library parses payload.
4. Values stored in `Preferences` (non-volatile flash) for next brew.

**Sample Payload:**

```json
{
  "recipe_name": "Espresso Light",
  "coffee_g": 18,
  "water_g": 270,
  "temp_c": 92,
  "pre_infusion_s": 30,
  "brew_time_s": 150
}
```

**Code snippet (ESP32 / Arduino):**

```cpp
#include <PubSubClient.h>
#include <ArduinoJson.h>

void callback(char* topic, byte* payload, unsigned int len) {
  StaticJsonDocument<256> doc;
  deserializeJson(doc, payload, len);
  int coffee = doc["coffee_g"];
  int water  = doc["water_g"];
  int temp   = doc["temp_c"];
  // store & execute
}
```

— **(7 Marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. Confusing **brew ratio** (mass ratio) with **extraction yield** (%) — costs 1 mark.
> 2. Forgetting to **clamp PID output** to 0–255 PWM range — device may saturate.
> 3. Omitting **sensor-failure handling** in FSM diagrams — full 2-mark deduction in design questions.
> 4. Writing brew time as $t_b$ in seconds but using minutes in numerical substitution — unit-mismatch.
> 5. Not mentioning **MQTT QoS level** (e.g., QoS 1) for recipe updates — partial loss of credit in 7-mark questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Coffee Brewing Recipe in IoT** = parameterized firmware controlling a cyber-physical process.
- **Core parameters**: $T_w$ (°C), $R_{cw}$, $t_p$ (s), $t_b$ (s), $G$ (grind).
- **Golden brew ratio** = **1:16** (water : coffee by mass).
- **Optimal brew temperature** = **92–96 °C**.
- **Architecture** = 4 layers: Sensor → Actuator → Edge (ESP32 + FSM + PID) → Cloud (MQTT).
- **Energy to heat water**: $Q = m c_p \Delta T$, with $c_p = 4186$ J/(kg·K).
- **PID equation** has three terms: P (present), I (past), D (future).
- **FSM states** to remember: `IDLE → INIT → HEAT → PRE_INFUSE → BREW → HOLD → DONE`.
- **MQTT** is the de-facto IoT protocol for recipe updates; subscribe to `home/coffee/recipe`.
- **JSON payload** carries recipe data; `ArduinoJson` parses it on ESP32.
- **Safety**: Always include timeout, over-temperature cutoff, and low-water guard in FSM.
- **Conversion reminder**: 1 g water ≈ 1 mL at room temperature; 1 kJ = 1000 J.
- **Don't confuse**:
  - Brew *ratio* (1:16) vs *yield* (20%) — both dimensionless but different meaning.
  - *Pre-infusion* (low-water, short) vs *Brew* (full-water, longer).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Commercial Licensing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT504 - PROGRAMMING FOR INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Commercial Licensing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Commercial Licensing** in the context of the Internet of Things (IoT) refers to the legal, contractual, and economic framework that governs how software, firmware, hardware designs, intellectual property (IP), and embedded code are permitted to be used, distributed, modified, and monetized by individuals, enterprises, and end-users in commercial (non-academic, revenue-generating) environments.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Commercial Licensing is studied as a cross-cutting concern of the IoT software stack, sitting alongside operating systems (like Contiki, RIOT, Zephyr), communication protocols (MQTT, CoAP), and cloud integration. It determines **who owns the code**, **what rights are transferred**, and **what obligations the licensee must honor** during deployment at scale.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Theme Park Pass" Analogy**
> Imagine an IoT firmware is a *theme park*.
> - A **Proprietary License** is like a private park — only ticket holders (paying customers) can enter; they cannot see how the rides work or build a copy.
> - An **Open Source License** is like a *public park* — anyone can enter, but rules apply (e.g., MIT = "do anything but keep the credit"; GPL = "share your improvements back with everyone").
> - A **Dual License** is the park offering both options — free entry for individuals, paid VIP entry for businesses that want to keep their modifications private.
> - A **Subscription (SaaS-style) License** is like an annual park membership — recurring fee, continuous updates.

Just as a park owner must clearly post rules at the gate, a software developer must attach a **license file** at the root of every IoT project repository so that every downstream user knows their rights.

### Why It Matters in IoT (Syllabus Highlight)

In IoT, commercial licensing is uniquely critical because:
1. **Hardware + Software Coupling** — Licenses govern whether an IoT firmware binary can be flashed onto **millions of devices** without per-unit royalty fees.
2. **Supply Chain Compliance** — A single GPL-tainted library inside an otherwise proprietary firmware can force the entire codebase to be open-sourced under the **viral copyleft** effect.
3. **Patent Encumbrance** — Standards-essential patents (SEP) in IoT (e.g., LoRa, Zigbee, BLE) require **FRAND (Fair, Reasonable, and Non-Discriminatory)** licensing.
4. **Data Monetization** — Modern IoT licensing increasingly bundles **data rights** alongside software rights.

> [!TIP]
> **Mandatory Vocabulary for KTU Answers**
> - **Copyright** — Automatic protection of original source code (e.g., `Copyright © 2025, Acme IoT Pvt. Ltd.`).
> - **Copyleft** — Licenses that require derivative works to carry the *same* license (e.g., GPL).
> - **Permissive License** — Allows derivative works under *any* license (e.g., MIT, Apache 2.0).
> - **Royalty** — Recurring per-unit/per-time payment to the licensor.
> - **FRAND** — Standard-essential patent licensing principle.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 The Taxonomy of Commercial Licenses

Commercial licenses for IoT can be classified along **five orthogonal axes**:

1. **Source Visibility** — *Proprietary (Closed)* vs *Open Source*
2. **Distribution Obligation** — *Permissive* vs *Copyleft* vs *Network Copyleft (AGPL)*
3. **Payment Model** — *Perpetual* vs *Subscription* vs *Per-unit Royalty* vs *Free (Monetization via support/services)*
4. **Scope of Rights Granted** — *Use only* vs *Use + Modify* vs *Use + Modify + Redistribute*
5. **IP Coverage** — *Copyright only* vs *Copyright + Patent grant* (e.g., Apache 2.0 includes explicit patent grants)

### 2.2 The "Five-Why" Logic of License Selection in IoT

- **Why a license?** — Because copyright law **automatically** restricts all use; without an explicit grant, *no one* legally has the right to copy, modify, or deploy the code.
- **Why open source in IoT?** — To leverage community contributions, accelerate interoperability, and reduce BOM cost in mass-produced sensor nodes.
- **Why proprietary?** — To protect differentiated algorithms (e.g., proprietary ML model on edge), enforce vendor lock-in, and capture recurring revenue.
- **Why dual licensing?** — To monetize commercial users while keeping an open community ecosystem (e.g., MySQL, MongoDB, Qt).
- **Why subscription?** — To fund continuous OTA (Over-The-Air) security updates — a **mandatory** feature in any production-grade IoT deployment.

### 2.3 IoT-Relevant License Profiles

The following table is the **high-yield cheat sheet** KTU examiners expect you to reproduce from memory:

| License | Type | Commercial Use | Modification | Redistribution | Patent Grant | Copyleft Strength | Typical IoT Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MIT** | Permissive | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | None | Lightweight sensor firmware, edge scripts |
| **BSD 2/3-Clause** | Permissive | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | None | Networking stacks, RTOS layers |
| **Apache 2.0** | Permissive | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ **Yes (explicit)** | None (file-level) | Patent-sensitive IoT protocols (e.g., MQTT brokers) |
| **GPL v3** | Strong Copyleft | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ⚠️ Must disclose source | ✅ Yes | **Strong (viral)** | Avoid in proprietary firmware |
| **LGPL v3** | Weak Copyleft | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (dynamic link OK) | ✅ Yes | Weak | Linking proprietary code to open libraries |
| **AGPL v3** | Network Copyleft | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ⚠️ Network use triggers source disclosure | ✅ Yes | **Strongest** | Cloud IoT platforms (e.g., MongoDB) |
| **MPL 2.0** | File-level Copyleft | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | File-level | Mixed proprietary/open hybrid firmware |
| **Proprietary / EULA** | Closed | ⚠️ Per contract | ❌ Usually no | ❌ Usually no | ⚠️ Per contract | N/A | Vendor-locked industrial IoT gateways |
| **Dual License** | Hybrid | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (open track) | ✅ Yes (open track) | ⚠️ Per track | Depends on track | Qt, MariaDB, ElasticSearch |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Home OEMs (e.g., Philips Hue, Xiaomi)** predominantly use **proprietary** firmware with **per-unit royalty** contracts to chipset vendors (e.g., Qualcomm, Nordic).
- **Industrial IoT gateways (e.g., Bosch, Siemens)** combine **MIT/Apache**-licensed OS layers with **proprietary** application layers.
- **Open-source RTOS projects (e.g., Zephyr, Apache Mynewt)** rely on **Apache 2.0** to attract commercial contributors while protecting them with patent grants.
- **Cloud IoT platforms (AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub)** use a combination of **AGPL-licensed backing services** (e.g., MongoDB) and proprietary front-end layers.

### 2.5 The Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) Equation

For KTU numerical/analytical questions on commercial licensing, the canonical cost model is:

$$\text{TCO} = C_{\text{license}} + C_{\text{maintenance}} + C_{\text{support}} + C_{\text{training}} + C_{\text{compliance}} + C_{\text{audit}}$$

Where:
- $C_{\text{license}}$ — One-time or recurring license fee.
- $C_{\text{maintenance}}$ — Annual patch/update cost (typically **15%–22%** of license fee).
- $C_{\text{support}}$ — Tier-based SLA cost (Bronze / Silver / Gold).
- $C_{\text{training}}$ — Developer certification and onboarding.
- $C_{\text{compliance}}$ — Legal review of OSS dependencies (SBOM generation).
- $C_{\text{audit}}$ — External audit cost if a copyleft audit is required.

For **per-unit royalty** models deployed on $N$ devices:

$$\text{Total Royalty Cost} = N \times R_{\text{per-unit}} \times T_{\text{deployment}}$$

For **subscription models** with annual fee $S$ over $Y$ years:

$$\text{Subscription Total} = S \times Y$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold Rule for License Conversion**: When the recurring subscription total exceeds the perpetual license cost, organizations typically switch to perpetual ownership. Mathematically:
> $$\text{Break-even Year} = \frac{C_{\text{perpetual}}}{S_{\text{annual}}}$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Royalty vs Subscription Decision

**Problem (KTU-style):**
An IoT startup plans to deploy **50,000** smart energy meters. Vendor A offers a perpetual license at **₹8,00,000** with **₹1,20,000/year** maintenance. Vendor B offers a subscription at **₹3,00,000/year** (all-inclusive). Vendor C charges a per-unit royalty of **₹40/meter** plus **₹2,00,000/year** platform fee. Determine the **cheapest option** over a **5-year** horizon.

**Step 1: Compute Vendor A's 5-year TCO.**

$$\text{TCO}_A = 8{,}00{,}000 + (5 \times 1{,}20{,}000)$$

$$\text{TCO}_A = 8{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 = 14{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}$$

**Step 2: Compute Vendor B's 5-year TCO.**

$$\text{TCO}_B = 5 \times 3{,}00{,}000 = 15{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}$$

**Step 3: Compute Vendor C's 5-year TCO.**

$$\text{Royalty}_C = 50{,}000 \times 40 \times 5 = 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}$$

$$\text{Platform}_C = 5 \times 2{,}00{,}000 = 10{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}$$

$$\text{TCO}_C = 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}10{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}$$

**Step 4: Compare.**

$$\text{TCO}_A = 14{,}00{,}000 \;\; < \;\; \text{TCO}_B = 15{,}00{,}000 \;\; < \;\; \text{TCO}_C = 1{,}10{,}00{,}000$$

**Conclusion:** Vendor A's perpetual model is the cheapest, by a margin of **₹1,00,000** over Vendor B and a massive **₹96,00,000** over Vendor C's per-unit royalty model.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Copyleft Contamination Analysis

**Problem:**
An IoT gateway firmware contains **150 third-party libraries**. An SBOM (Software Bill of Materials) audit reveals that **12** are GPL-licensed and **5** are AGPL-licensed. The product is distributed in **binary form** to **100,000 industrial customers**.

**Step 1: Classify obligations.**
- **GPL**: Distributing binary *requires* offering the *complete corresponding source code* to all recipients under the **same GPL terms**.
- **AGPL**: Even *network-accessible* use (e.g., remote gateway management) triggers source disclosure to *all network users*.

**Step 2: Identify legal exposure.**

The 12 GPL libraries are **statically linked** into the firmware. Under GPL §6, this forces the entire combined work to be distributed under GPL — i.e., the proprietary IoT gateway code must be **open-sourced**.

**Step 3: Mitigation strategies.**
- **Replace** GPL libraries with permissive (MIT/Apache) equivalents.
- **Dynamically link** LGPL libraries and provide object files for re-linking.
- **Negotiate commercial dual-license** from the GPL copyright holder (CTO fee).
- **Isolate** GPL code into a separate process communicating via IPC.

**Step 4: Calculate potential CTO (Commercial Total Owner) cost.**

If commercial relicensing costs **₹5,00,000 per GPL library**:

$$C_{\text{CTO}} = 12 \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 60{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}$$

**Decision threshold:**

$$\text{If } C_{\text{CTO}} < C_{\text{re-engineering}} \Rightarrow \text{Pay CTO and relicense}$$

$$\text{Otherwise} \Rightarrow \text{Re-engineer with permissive alternatives}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix for IoT License Selection

| Selection Criterion | Weight $w_i$ | MIT | Apache 2.0 | GPL v3 | Proprietary |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Commercial Friendliness | 0.25 | 5 | 5 | 2 | 5 |
| Patent Protection | 0.20 | 1 | 5 | 4 | 5 |
| Community Contribution | 0.20 | 5 | 5 | 5 | 1 |
| Royalty Burden | 0.15 | 5 | 5 | 5 | 2 |
| Legal Certainty | 0.20 | 3 | 5 | 4 | 5 |
| **Weighted Score** | **1.00** | **3.80** | **5.00** | **3.75** | **3.60** |

The weighted score for each license is computed as:

$$\text{Score} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \times r_i$$

Where $r_i$ is the rating (1–5) for criterion $i$ and $w_i$ is the weight (summing to 1.0). This produces an objective, defensible license-selection rationale — a technique KTU examiners reward in 14-mark analytical questions.

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Break-Even Calculation

A vendor offers an IoT analytics platform at **₹15,00,000 perpetual** or **₹3,75,000/year subscription**.

**Step 1: Compute break-even year.**

$$Y_{\text{BE}} = \frac{C_{\text{perpetual}}}{S_{\text{annual}}} = \frac{15{,}00{,}000}{3{,}75{,}000}$$

$$Y_{\text{BE}} = 4 \text{ years}$$

**Step 2: Decision rule.**

- If deployment horizon $< 4$ years → **Subscription** is cheaper.
- If deployment horizon $> 4$ years → **Perpetual** is cheaper.
- At exactly 4 years → **Indifferent** (factoring in NPV gives subscription a slight edge due to deferral of cash outflow).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Commercial Licensing Decision Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[IoT Project Kickoff] --> needA[Identify Functional Requirements]
    needA --> ipA[Inventory Internal IP Assets]
    ipA --> depA[Scan Dependencies for OSS Licenses]
    depA --> riskA{Risk Assessment}
    riskA --> copyleftA[Copyleft Detected]
    riskA --> permissiveA[Only Permissive Detected]
    riskA --> mixedA[Mixed Detected]
    copyleftA --> isolateA[Isolate / Replace GPL Components]
    permissiveA --> procA[Proceed with MIT or Apache 2.0]
    mixedA --> dualA[Consider Dual Licensing Model]
    isolateA --> procA
    dualA --> modelA{Monetization Goal}
    procA --> modelA
    modelA --> vendorA[Vendor Lock-in Wanted]
    modelA --> communityA[Community Adoption Wanted]
    vendorA --> propA[Issue Proprietary EULA with Royalty]
    communityA --> openA[Release under Permissive OSS License]
    propA --> auditA[Annual Compliance and Legal Audit]
    openA --> auditA
    auditA --> sbomA[Maintain SBOM and License Manifest]
    sbomA --> doneA[Continuous Compliance Loop]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Graph — License Type Taxonomy

```mermaid
graph LR
    rootA[Commercial Licensing]
    rootA --> openA[Open Source]
    rootA --> propA[Proprietary]
    rootA --> dualA[Dual License]
    rootA --> subA[Subscription SaaS]
    openA --> permA[Permissive]
    openA --> copyA[Copyleft]
    permA --> mitA[MIT]
    permA --> bsdA[BSD]
    permA --> apaA[Apache 2.0]
    copyA --> lgplA[LGPL]
    copyA --> gplA[GPL v3]
    copyA --> agplA[AGPL v3]
    copyA --> mplA[MPL 2.0]
    propA --> eulaA[EULA based]
    propA --> royaltyA[Per unit Royalty]
    dualA --> ctoA[CTO Relicensing]
    subA --> monthlyA[Monthly Billing]
    subA --> annualA[Annual Billing]
    subA --> usageA[Usage based Metering]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — License Compliance Workflow for IoT Firmware

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    devA[Developer Commits Code] --> ciA[CI Pipeline Trigger]
    ciA --> scanA[Automated License Scanner e.g. FOSSA Snyk]
    scanA --> detA{Compatible Licenses?}
    detA --> yesA[Merge Allowed]
    detA --> noA[Block Merge Trigger Legal Review]
    noA --> relA[Either Replace Library OR Negotiate CTO]
    relA --> scanA
    yesA --> sbomA[Generate SBOM CycloneDX SPDX]
    sbomA --> signA[Sign Firmware Image]
    signA --> otaA[Deploy via OTA to Edge Devices]
    otaA --> monA[Runtime License Telemetry]
    monA --> reportA[Compliance Dashboard Report]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**
*Define Commercial Licensing as applicable to an IoT software ecosystem. List any four types of commercial licenses commonly used in IoT products.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Commercial Licensing is the contractual and legal mechanism by which the owner of an IoT software, firmware, or hardware IP grants defined rights — to use, copy, modify, or distribute — to another party in exchange for payment, obligation, or both. It governs the economic and legal relationship between licensor and licensee in commercial deployments.

The four common types are:
1. **Proprietary (Closed Source)** — e.g., vendor-locked industrial IoT gateways.
2. **Open Source Permissive** — e.g., MIT, Apache 2.0, BSD used in edge firmware.
3. **Open Source Copyleft** — e.g., GPL, LGPL, AGPL used in cloud IoT platforms.
4. **Dual License** — e.g., Qt, MongoDB offering both GPL and commercial tracks.

*(Award full marks for clear definition + correct enumeration.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*Differentiate between **Permissive** and **Copyleft** open-source licenses. Give one example license for each category and explain its implication for an IoT firmware developer.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Permissive | Copyleft |
|---|---|---|
| Redistribution obligation | Minimal — only attribution | Must inherit same license |
| Patent grant | Optional (Apache 2.0 grants) | Often included (GPL v3) |
| Effect on proprietary derivatives | Allowed | Disallowed / Forces source release |
| Example | **MIT License** | **GPL v3** |
| IoT Implication | Developer may keep firmware closed | Developer *must* open-source combined firmware |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the different **commercial licensing models** used for IoT products, with at least one real-world example for each model. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **end-to-end license compliance workflow** for an IoT firmware development pipeline. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Commercial Licensing Models — Detailed Exposition (7 Marks)**

1. **Proprietary / Closed-Source Licensing**
   - The IoT vendor retains full copyright, source code is not disclosed.
   - Rights granted via an End-User License Agreement (EULA).
   - Payment: one-time fee, annual subscription, or per-unit royalty.
   - *Example:* Apple's HomeKit Accessory Protocol firmware (license required for MFi certification).

2. **Open-Source Permissive Licensing**
   - Allows use, modification, and redistribution with minimal restrictions (only attribution).
   - *Example:* Zephyr RTOS (Apache 2.0) used in Nordic nRF52 IoT boards.

3. **Open-Source Copyleft Licensing**
   - Requires derivative works to carry the same license (viral effect).
   - *Example:* Linux kernel (GPL v2) used in Raspberry Pi IoT gateways.

4. **Dual Licensing**
   - The same software is offered under both an OSS license *and* a commercial license.
   - Commercial users pay to be released from copyleft obligations.
   - *Example:* MongoDB (SSPL/AGPL + commercial license), Qt framework.

5. **Subscription / SaaS Licensing**
   - Recurring fee for continuous service, OTA updates, and SLA-backed support.
   - *Example:* AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub pricing per message/device.

6. **Per-Unit Royalty / FRAND Licensing**
   - Standards-essential patents (SEP) licensed on Fair, Reasonable, and Non-Discriminatory terms.
   - *Example:* Bluetooth SIG royalty for BLE-enabled IoT devices.

7. **Patent-Only / Cross-Licensing**
   - Patent pools where members cross-license their SEPs.
   - *Example:* Avanci 4G/5G IoT patent pool for connected vehicles.

**(b) End-to-End License Compliance Workflow (7 Marks)**

The diagram in *Section 4.3* above illustrates the complete pipeline. The narrative explanation:

1. **Developer Commit** — Source code is pushed to a Git repository.
2. **CI/CD Trigger** — Every commit runs through automated builds.
3. **License Scanning** — Tools like **FOSSA**, **Snyk**, or **Black Duck** identify every transitive OSS dependency and its license.
4. **Policy Gate** — A pre-defined policy (e.g., *“No GPL in firmware core”*) automatically blocks merges that violate policy.
5. **SBOM Generation** — On successful build, a **Software Bill of Materials** is produced in **SPDX** or **CycloneDX** format and stored as a build artifact.
6. **Firmware Signing** — The SBOM is cryptographically signed alongside the firmware image.
7. **OTA Distribution** — Signed firmware is pushed to edge devices with the SBOM embedded in the device attestation record.
8. **Runtime Telemetry** — Devices periodically report license-relevant metadata (e.g., active feature flags tied to license tier).
9. **Compliance Dashboard** — A central dashboard provides auditors with real-time license posture across the entire deployed fleet.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss the **implications of GPL, LGPL, and AGPL licenses** for an IoT company developing a cloud-managed industrial sensor gateway. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** An IoT vendor must choose between a **perpetual license of ₹20,00,000** with **₹3,00,000/year maintenance** and a **subscription at ₹5,50,000/year** for a **6-year** deployment. Calculate and justify which is cheaper. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) GPL, LGPL, AGPL Implications for IoT Gateway (7 Marks)**

| License | IoT Gateway Implication | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **GPL v3** | Static linking forces **entire firmware to be open-sourced** under GPL terms. Disclosure obligation extends to all distributed binaries. | Avoid statically linking GPL code; isolate into separate process; or pay CTO. |
| **LGPL v3** | Permits proprietary code to *dynamically* link against LGPL library. Reverse engineering for re-linking must be allowed. | Use dynamic linking; provide object files; document linkage clearly. |
| **AGPL v3** | **Network-based use** triggers source disclosure — critical for cloud-managed gateways that poll cloud APIs. Even SaaS-style exposure forces open-sourcing. | Strongly avoid for proprietary cloud backends; use Apache 2.0 alternatives. |

Additional considerations:
- **Patent retaliation clauses** in GPL v3 prohibit asserting patents against the codebase — a strategic concern for IoT vendors with large patent portfolios.
- **Anti-Tivoization** clause requires that the *hardware* allow reinstallation of modified versions — affects locked-down IoT silicon.

**(b) Cost Comparison Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Vendor 1 — Perpetual + Maintenance:**

$$\text{TCO}_1 = 20{,}00{,}000 + (6 \times 3{,}00{,}000)$$

$$\text{TCO}_1 = 20{,}00{,}000 + 18{,}00{,}000 = 38{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}$$

**Vendor 2 — Pure Subscription:**

$$\text{TCO}_2 = 6 \times 5{,}50{,}000 = 33{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}$$

**Break-even year analysis:**

$$Y_{\text{BE}} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{5{,}50{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{2{,}50{,}000} = 8 \text{ years}$$

Since the deployment horizon (**6 years**) is **less than 8 years**, the **subscription model** is cheaper.

**Conclusion:** $\text{TCO}_2 < \text{TCO}_1$ by **₹5,00,000** — choose **subscription**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Conflating “Free” with “Open Source”** — Freeware is *not* open source. Open source requires source disclosure + derivative rights. Students lose 1 mark for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting the AGPL network trigger** — Many students ignore AGPL's clause 13, which forces disclosure for network-accessible software. This is the *most tested* pitfall in 14-mark questions.
> 3. **Mixing up LGPL's linking rules** — Dynamic linking is permitted; static linking triggers copyleft. Examiners explicitly look for this distinction.
> 4. **Skipping SBOM** — A licensing answer without a Software Bill of Materials discussion is considered incomplete in 2024 Scheme papers.
> 5. **Ignoring FRAND** — Standards-essential patent (SEP) licensing under FRAND terms is a high-yield keyword often missed.
> 6. **Arithmetic in TCO** — Marks are deducted for not showing the full calculation; even correct final answers need step-by-step workings.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Recall Bullets — Read Before Every Exam**

- ✅ Commercial licensing governs **copyright, patent, and redistribution rights** in IoT products.
- ✅ **Permissive** licenses (MIT, BSD, Apache 2.0) impose minimal obligations — only attribution (and patent grant for Apache).
- ✅ **Copyleft** licenses (GPL, LGPL, AGPL, MPL) require derivative works to inherit license terms.
- ✅ **GPL v3** is viral for static linking; **LGPL v3** permits dynamic linking; **AGPL v3** closes the SaaS loophole.
- ✅ **Apache 2.0** is the gold standard for patent-sensitive IoT protocols.
- ✅ **Dual licensing** (e.g., Qt, MongoDB) monetizes commercial users while preserving an open community.
- ✅ **FRAND** licensing applies to standards-essential patents (Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, 5G).
- ✅ **TCO** = License + Maintenance + Support + Training + Compliance + Audit.
- ✅ **Break-even year** $Y_{BE} = C_{perpetual} / S_{annual}$ decides perpetual-vs-subscription.
- ✅ **SBOM** (SPDX / CycloneDX) is mandatory for IoT compliance audits under 2024 regulations.
- ✅ **Copyleft contamination** of proprietary firmware can be mitigated by replacement, isolation, dynamic linking, or CTO relicensing.
- ✅ **Royalty models** scale linearly with deployment volume — favorable for small fleets, prohibitive for mass-market IoT.
- ✅ IoT-specific license considerations include OTA update rights, anti-Tivoization clauses, and patent retaliation provisions.
- ✅ Always end a 14-mark answer with a **concrete IoT example** (e.g., Zephyr, HomeKit, AWS IoT Core, MongoDB) to secure the application-level marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project on Familiarisation to IoT Development Kit and Development Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT504 - PROGRAMMING FOR INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Project on Familiarisation to IoT Development Kit and Development Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IoT Development Kit:** A *hardware-software integrated platform* comprising a microcontroller/microprocessor unit, on-board sensors/actuators, communication modules, and a programmable interface that enables students and developers to rapidly prototype, test, and deploy Internet of Things (IoT) applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IoT Development Environment:** A *software ecosystem* (IDE + compiler + toolchain + debugger + libraries) that allows code to be authored, compiled, uploaded, and executed on the target IoT hardware. Examples include the **Arduino IDE**, **PlatformIO**, **Thonny IDE**, and **Espressif ESP-IDF**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an **IoT Development Kit** as a *digital artist's toolbox*, and the **Development Environment** as the *art studio itself*.

- Just like a painter needs a canvas, brushes, and paint (hardware) plus a well-lit studio with a table and easel (software + IDE), an IoT developer needs a microcontroller board (ESP32/NodeMCU) plus a code editor and compiler (Arduino IDE/PlatformIO) to bring their idea to life.
- The "painting" is the IoT application — say, a smart temperature monitor that sends data to a phone.
- Without either the toolbox or the studio, the painting cannot exist. They are **co-dependent**.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in the KTU 2024 Curriculum

In the **NEP 2020 / 2024 Scheme**, this topic bridges the gap between *theoretical IoT layers* (covered in Modules 1–3) and *hands-on implementation*. Universities expect every student to:

1. **Physically handle** an IoT kit (breadboard, jumper wires, sensors).
2. **Install and configure** a development environment on their own laptops.
3. **Blink an LED**, **read a sensor**, and **publish data to the cloud** — the *canonical "Hello World" of IoT*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** Module 4 is a *Project Module*. Marks are typically awarded for a *working demo + viva voce + lab record*, not a written exam. The questions in Section 5 model the **viva voce** and **end-semester practical** patterns.

## 1.4 Key Metrics to Memorize

| Metric | Standard Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Arduino Uno Clock Speed** | **16 MHz** | Determines instruction execution rate |
| **ESP32 Clock Speed** | **Up to 240 MHz** | Dual-core Tensilica Xtensa LX6 |
| **NodeMCU (ESP8266) Clock** | **80–160 MHz** | Tensilica L106 |
| **Logic Voltage (Arduino/ESP)** | **3.3 V / 5 V** | Critical for sensor interfacing |
| **Arduino Flash Memory (Uno)** | **32 KB** | Limits program size |
| **ESP32 Flash Memory** | **4 MB (typical)** | Ample for IoT applications |
| **WiFi Range (indoor)** | **~30–50 m** | Standard 2.4 GHz propagation |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an IoT Development Kit (Layered View)

A typical IoT kit is built in **five functional layers**, analogous to the OSI model in networking.

### Layer 1: Processing / Compute Layer
- The "brain" of the kit.
- Two main families:
  - **Microcontroller-based (MCU):** Arduino Uno (ATmega328P), NodeMCU (ESP8266), ESP32.
  - **Microprocessor-based (MPU):** Raspberry Pi 4 (Broadcom BCM2711, Quad-core Cortex-A72 @ **1.8 GHz**).
- *MCUs* run a single program in a loop (no OS) → low power, real-time.
- *MPUs* run a full Linux OS → support Python, Node.js, complex ML.

### Layer 2: Sensing Layer
- Sensors convert physical phenomena → electrical signals.
- **Common sensors shipped with academic IoT kits:**

| Sensor | Parameter Measured | Output Type | Operating Voltage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DHT11** | Temperature & Humidity | Digital (single-wire) | 3–5 V |
| **DHT22** | High-accuracy T & H | Digital (single-wire) | 3–5 V |
| **HC-SR04** | Distance (ultrasonic) | Digital (Trig/Echo) | 5 V |
| **LM35** | Temperature | Analog | 4–30 V |
| **LDR (Photoresistor)** | Light intensity | Analog (resistive) | 3–5 V |
| **PIR (HC-SR501)** | Motion (IR) | Digital (HIGH/LOW) | 5–20 V |
| **BME280** | T, H, Pressure | I²C / SPI | 1.8–3.6 V |
| **MQ-2** | Gas (LPG, smoke) | Analog + Digital | 5 V |

### Layer 3: Actuation Layer
- Actuators *perform* actions based on commands.
- Examples: **LEDs**, **Buzzer**, **Relay Module (5 V/12 V)**, **Servo Motor (SG90)**, **DC Motor via L293D driver**, **16x2 LCD Display**.

### Layer 4: Communication Layer
- **Wired:** UART, I²C (TWI), SPI, 1-Wire.
- **Wireless:** Wi-Fi (802.11 b/g/n), Bluetooth / BLE, LoRa, Zigbee, RFID (RC522), GSM (SIM800L), NFC.

### Layer 5: Power & Interface Layer
- Power: USB 5 V, battery (Li-ion 3.7 V), or external adapter (7–12 V via barrel jack).
- Interface: Breadboard (830 tie-points), jumper wires (M-M, M-F, F-F), USB Type-B (Arduino) / Micro-USB (NodeMCU) / USB-C (ESP32, RPi).

## 2.2 Anatomy of an IoT Development Environment

> [!NOTE]
> A *Development Environment* is **not** a single program — it is a *chain* of cooperating tools.

### Stage A: Authoring (Source Code Editor)
- The student types code (`.ino`, `.cpp`, `.py`).
- Examples: built-in editor in Arduino IDE, VS Code, Thonny.

### Stage B: Compilation / Cross-Compilation
- The source code is converted to machine code (`.hex` for AVR, `.bin` for ESP).
- *Cross-compilation* means code compiled on x86 PC runs on ARM/Xtensa MCU.

### Stage C: Upload (Flashing)
- The binary is transferred to the MCU's flash memory over **UART (via USB-Serial bridge like CH340/CP2102)** or **JTAG/SWD**.

### Stage D: Runtime Libraries
- Pre-built code that abstracts hardware. E.g., `Wire.h` (I²C), `DHT.h` (sensor), `WiFi.h` (ESP32 networking).

### Stage E: Serial Monitor / Debugger
- Bi-directional text communication over UART at common baud rates: **9600, 115200**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Table (Toolchain Comparison)

| Feature | Arduino IDE | PlatformIO | Thonny IDE | ESP-IDF |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Language** | C/C++ (Arduino dialect) | C/C++ (Arduino + native) | MicroPython | C (FreeRTOS-based) |
| **Board Support** | Arduino + ESP (via Board Manager) | 1000+ boards | ESP8266, ESP32, RPi Pico | Espressif only |
| **Debugging** | Serial.print only | Native + Serial | REPL + Serial | GDB + JTAG |
| **Library Mgmt** | Library Manager | Registry (PIO) | pip / mip | Component manager |
| **Learning Curve** | **Lowest** | Medium | Low | High |
| **KTU Recommendation** | **Primary** | Secondary | Optional | Advanced |

## 2.4 Why These Systems Matter in Engineering

- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Factory floors use ESP32 + MQTT + Node-RED dashboards — the same stack students learn in the lab.
- **Smart Agriculture:** Soil moisture sensors + LoRa + cloud dashboard.
- **Healthcare Wearables:** BLE-enabled ESP32 prototyping → real-time patient monitoring.
- **Smart Cities:** Air quality (MQ-135 + BME280) + Wi-Fi gateway → government dashboard.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedures, Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Procedure: Setting Up Arduino IDE for ESP32 / NodeMCU

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most common practical exam question** in KTU. Memorize the sequence.

### Step 1 — Install Arduino IDE
- Download from `arduino.cc` (current stable: **Arduino IDE 2.x**).
- Install the appropriate OS version (Windows `.msi`, macOS `.dmg`, Linux AppImage).

### Step 2 — Add Board URLs
- Open *File → Preferences* (Arduino 2.x) or *File → Preferences* (1.8.x).
- Paste into **"Additional Board Manager URLs"**:

$$ \text{For ESP32:} \quad \texttt{https://raw.githubusercontent.com/espressif/arduino-esp32/gh-pages/package\_esp32\_index.json} $$

$$ \text{For ESP8266:} \quad \texttt{http://arduino.esp8266.com/stable/package\_esp8266com\_index.json} $$

- Click OK.

### Step 3 — Install Board Packages
- Open *Tools → Board → Boards Manager*.
- Search `esp32` → click *Install* (Espressif Systems, **~250 MB**).
- Search `esp8266` → click *Install* (ESP8266 Community, **~150 MB**).

### Step 4 — Install USB-Serial Driver
- Most clones use **CH340** or **CP2102**.
- Windows: Auto-installed via Windows Update, or manually from manufacturer site.
- macOS: May require Security & Privacy approval for kernel extension.
- Linux: Usually pre-installed (`brltty` conflict must be removed).

### Step 5 — Select Board & Port
- *Tools → Board → ESP32 Dev Module* (or *NodeMCU 1.0 (ESP-12E Module)*).
- *Tools → Port → /dev/ttyUSB0* (Linux) or *COM3* (Windows).

### Step 6 — Configure Upload Speed
- *Tools → Upload Speed → 115200* (standard for ESP).
- *Tools → Flash Frequency → 80 MHz*.

### Step 7 — Upload Your First Sketch
The canonical **"Hello World" of embedded IoT** — the LED blink:

```cpp
// Pin definitions
const int LED_PIN = 2;          // Onboard blue LED on most ESP32 DevKits

void setup() {
  pinMode(LED_PIN, OUTPUT);     // Configure pin 2 as digital output
  Serial.begin(115200);         // Initialize UART at 115200 baud
  Serial.println("System Booted");
}

void loop() {
  digitalWrite(LED_PIN, HIGH);  // Turn LED ON
  delay(1000);                  // Wait 1000 ms
  digitalWrite(LED_PIN, LOW);   // Turn LED OFF
  delay(1000);                  // Wait 1000 ms
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Compilation → Upload Trace (board response expected in Serial Monitor):**
> `rst:0x1 (POWERON_RESET), boot:0x13 (SPI_FAST_FLASH_BOOT)`
> `configsip: 0, SPIWP:0xee`
> `mode:DIO, clock div:1`
> `load:0x3fff0030,len:1184`

## 3.2 Procedure: Reading a DHT11 Sensor (DHT22 Upgrade Path)

### Hardware Wiring

| DHT11 Pin | Connect To |
|---|---|
| VCC | 3.3 V |
| DATA | GPIO 4 (with **10 kΩ pull-up** to VCC) |
| GND | GND |

### Software: Install the Library
- *Sketch → Include Library → Manage Libraries → search "DHT sensor library" by Adafruit → Install*.
- Also install **"Adafruit Unified Sensor"** (dependency).

### Source Code

```cpp
#include "DHT.h"

#define DHTPIN   4
#define DHTTYPE  DHT11

DHT dht(DHTPIN, DHTTYPE);

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  dht.begin();                  // Initialize sensor
}

void loop() {
  float h = dht.readHumidity();
  float t = dht.readTemperature();

  if (isnan(h) || isnan(t)) {
    Serial.println("Sensor read FAILED");
    return;
  }

  Serial.print("Humidity: ");
  Serial.print(h);
  Serial.print(" %  Temperature: ");
  Serial.print(t);
  Serial.println(" *C");

  delay(2000);                  // DHT sampling rate ≈ 0.5 Hz
}
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Viva Question:** "What is the minimum sampling interval for DHT11?" — Answer: **2 seconds** (or 1 reading per 2 seconds, due to internal capacitor settling).

## 3.3 Procedure: Wi-Fi Connectivity & MQTT Publish (Conceptual Walkthrough)

This combines all four layers — *compute, sensing, communication, and cloud*.

### Step-by-Step Logical Flow

1. **Connect to Access Point** using `WiFi.begin(ssid, password)`.
2. **Wait for connection** — poll `WiFi.status() == WL_CONNECTED`.
3. **Configure MQTT broker** — e.g., `broker.hivemq.com` on port **1883** (plain TCP) or **8883** (TLS).
4. **Set up PubSubClient** with callback function for incoming messages.
5. **Publish sensor payload** as JSON string:

$$ \text{Topic:} \quad \texttt{ktu/iot/sensor/data} $$

$$ \text{Payload:} \quad \texttt{\{"temp": 28.5, "hum": 62.1, "ts": 1717000000\}} $$

6. **Reconnect on disconnect** using exponential backoff (1 s, 2 s, 4 s, 8 s … capped at 30 s).

### Sketch (Trimmed Core)

```cpp
#include <WiFi.h>
#include <PubSubClient.h>
#include "DHT.h"

const char* ssid     = "YOUR_WIFI";
const char* password = "YOUR_PASS";
const char* mqttServer = "broker.hivemq.com";
const int   mqttPort   = 1883;

WiFiClient   espClient;
PubSubClient client(espClient);
DHT          dht(4, DHT11);

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  dht.begin();
  WiFi.begin(ssid, password);
  while (WiFi.status() != WL_CONNECTED) { delay(500); Serial.print("."); }
  
  client.setServer(mqttServer, mqttPort);
  client.connect("ESP32Client_001");
}

void loop() {
  if (!client.connected()) { client.connect("ESP32Client_001"); }
  client.loop();
  
  float t = dht.readTemperature();
  float h = dht.readHumidity();
  
  String payload = "{\"temp\":" + String(t) + ",\"hum\":" + String(h) + "}";
  client.publish("ktu/iot/sensor/data", payload.c_str());
  
  delay(5000);
}
```

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Which Board to Use?

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is the **highest-yield content** for viva questions.

| Selection Criterion | Arduino Uno | NodeMCU (ESP8266) | ESP32 | Raspberry Pi 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Wi-Fi Built-in** | ❌ (needs shield) | ✅ | ✅ (2.4 GHz) | ✅ |
| **Bluetooth** | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ BLE + Classic | ✅ |
| **GPIO Count** | 14 digital + 6 analog | 11 digital + 1 analog | 34 digital + 18 analog | 40 GPIO |
| **ADC Resolution** | **10-bit** (0–1023) | **10-bit** | **12-bit** (configurable) | N/A (external) |
| **PWM Channels** | 6 (8-bit) | All digital (~10-bit) | 16 (16-bit) | Hardware PWM |
| **Power Draw (idle)** | ~50 mA | ~70 mA | ~80 mA | **~600 mA** (idle, much more on load) |
| **Best For** | Learning, basic projects | Wi-Fi prototypes | **Multi-protocol IoT** | Edge AI, vision, gateway |
| **Cost (approx)** | ₹400 | ₹350 | ₹450 | ₹4000+ |
| **OS Required** | No (bare-metal) | No | No (or FreeRTOS) | **Yes (Linux)** |

**Decision Rule (KTU Examiner's Heuristic):**
- *Digital + Analog sensor read + Wi-Fi publish* → **ESP32**.
- *Quick LED/buzzer demonstration* → **Arduino Uno**.
- *Camera, ML, or local web server* → **Raspberry Pi**.
- *Cheapest Wi-Fi prototype* → **NodeMCU (ESP8266)**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Model Question, 2024 Scheme]`
**List any three popular IoT development kits and state one distinguishing feature of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per kit × 3):**

1. **Arduino Uno (ATmega328P):** A simple, open-source microcontroller board operating at **16 MHz** with **14 digital I/O pins**. Best suited for beginners learning embedded programming without Wi-Fi.
2. **NodeMCU (ESP8266):** A Wi-Fi-enabled development board with **80–160 MHz** Tensilica L106 processor and built-in **802.11 b/g/n** radio. Cost-effective for IoT prototypes.
3. **ESP32 DevKit:** Dual-core Tensilica Xtensa LX6 up to **240 MHz**, with **Wi-Fi + Bluetooth (BLE + Classic)**. The most versatile board for multi-protocol IoT projects.

> *(1 mark for naming, 1 mark for feature, 1 mark for proper distinction = 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Model Question, 2024 Scheme]`
**Differentiate between Arduino IDE and PlatformIO. Mention which one is recommended for beginners.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Arduino IDE | PlatformIO |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Standalone desktop IDE | **VS Code extension** (CLI + GUI) |
| **Board Support** | Limited (Arduino + ESP via Board Manager) | **1000+ boards** |
| **Debugging** | Serial monitor only | Native + Serial + GDB |
| **Learning Curve** | **Lowest** | Slightly higher |
| **Project Structure** | Single `.ino` file | Folder-based (`src/`, `lib/`, `include/`) |
| **Library Management** | Library Manager GUI | CLI / Registry |
| **Best For** | Beginners, quick prototypes | Advanced, multi-board, professional projects |

**Recommendation:** **Arduino IDE** is recommended for beginners due to its simple, single-window workflow, integrated board manager, and large community support.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### 🔵 QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Question, 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) Describe the architecture of a typical IoT development kit with a neat block diagram. List at least four common sensors and two actuators usually shipped with academic IoT kits. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Architecture of IoT Development Kit — 5 Layers:**

An IoT development kit is typically organized into five functional layers, each performing a specific role in the data flow from physical world to digital processing.

**Layer 1 — Processing Layer:**
- The "brain" of the system. Contains the microcontroller (e.g., ATmega328P in Arduino Uno, ESP32 in DevKit).
- Performs computation, executes user code, and manages I/O.

**Layer 2 — Sensing Layer:**
- Converts physical parameters (temperature, light, gas) into electrical signals.
- Common sensors: **DHT11** (T & H), **HC-SR04** (distance), **LDR** (light), **PIR** (motion), **MQ-2** (gas).

**Layer 3 — Actuation Layer:**
- Performs physical actions based on MCU commands.
- Examples: **LED, Buzzer, Relay, Servo motor, DC motor, 16×2 LCD**.

**Layer 4 — Communication Layer:**
- Enables data exchange with external systems.
- Wired: **UART, I²C, SPI, 1-Wire**.
- Wireless: **Wi-Fi, Bluetooth/BLE, LoRa, Zigbee, GSM, RFID**.

**Layer 5 — Power & Interface Layer:**
- Provides regulated power and physical connectivity.
- Includes: **USB 5V, barrel jack (7–12V), Li-ion 3.7V battery, voltage regulators (3.3V/5V), breadboard, jumper wires**.

**Sensors (4 examples — 1 mark total):**
1. DHT11 — digital temperature and humidity sensor.
2. HC-SR04 — ultrasonic distance sensor (range 2 cm–400 cm).
3. LDR — light-dependent resistor for ambient light.
4. PIR (HC-SR501) — passive infrared motion sensor.

**Actuators (2 examples — 1 mark total):**
1. LED — visual indicator.
2. Relay module — switches high-voltage AC loads (up to 250V/10A).

*(Marks distribution: 3 marks for layered architecture explanation + 2 marks for block diagram + 1 mark for sensors + 1 mark for actuators = 7 marks)*

---

**(b) Write a step-by-step procedure to install and configure the Arduino IDE for ESP32 development. Include the board manager URL and the serial baud rate used. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Download Arduino IDE (1 mark)**
Visit `arduino.cc/en/software` and download Arduino IDE 2.x for Windows/macOS/Linux. Install with default options.

**Step 2 — Add Board URL (1 mark)**
Open *File → Preferences*. In the field **"Additional Boards Manager URLs"**, paste:

$$ \texttt{https://raw.githubusercontent.com/espressif/arduino-esp32/gh-pages/package\_esp32\_index.json} $$

**Step 3 — Install ESP32 Board Package (1 mark)**
Open *Tools → Board → Boards Manager*. Search "esp32", locate the package by **Espressif Systems**, and click *Install*. (Download size: ~250 MB, takes 3–5 minutes.)

**Step 4 — Install USB Driver (1 mark)**
Most ESP32 clones use **CP2102** or **CH340** USB-to-UART bridge. On Windows, install the corresponding driver from the manufacturer site. On Linux, the `brltty` package may conflict — remove it using `sudo apt remove brltty`.

**Step 5 — Select Board and Port (1 mark)**
- *Tools → Board → ESP32 Dev Module* (or the specific variant).
- *Tools → Port → COMx* (Windows) or */dev/ttyUSB0* (Linux).

**Step 6 — Configure Upload Settings (1 mark)**
- *Upload Speed → 115200 baud*
- *Flash Frequency → 80 MHz*
- *Flash Mode → QIO*
- *Flash Size → 4MB (32Mb)*

**Step 7 — Upload and Verify (1 mark)**
Open *File → Examples → 01.Basics → Blink*. Change the LED pin to **GPIO 2**. Click the *Upload* (→) button. Open *Tools → Serial Monitor*, set baud rate to **115200**, and verify the LED toggles every 1 second.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - ❌ **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to set the Serial Monitor baud rate to **115200** — output will appear as garbage characters. (-1 mark)
> - ❌ **Common Mistake 2:** Selecting the wrong COM port — upload fails with `A fatal error occurred: Failed to connect to ESP32`. (-1 mark)
> - ❌ **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to install the **CH340/CP2102** USB driver — port not detected at all. (-1 mark)
> - ❌ **Common Mistake 4:** Holding the **BOOT** button during upload on some ESP32 variants to force download mode. (-1 mark)
> - ✅ **Pro Tip:** Always verify the board is detected under *Tools → Port* before clicking *Upload*.

---

### 🟢 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Model Question, 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) Compare Arduino Uno, NodeMCU (ESP8266), and ESP32 with respect to: processor speed, Wi-Fi capability, GPIO count, ADC resolution, and power consumption. Recommend the best board for a Wi-Fi-based weather monitoring project. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Parameter | Arduino Uno | NodeMCU (ESP8266) | ESP32 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Processor** | ATmega328P, 8-bit AVR | Tensilica L106, 32-bit | Tensilica Xtensa LX6, **Dual-core 32-bit** |
| **Clock Speed** | **16 MHz** | 80–160 MHz | **Up to 240 MHz** |
| **Wi-Fi** | ❌ Not built-in | ✅ 802.11 b/g/n | ✅ 802.11 b/g/n |
| **Bluetooth** | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ BLE 4.2 + Classic |
| **GPIO Pins** | 14 digital + 6 analog | 11 digital + 1 analog | 34 digital + 18 analog |
| **ADC Resolution** | **10-bit** (0–1023) | **10-bit** (0–1023) | **12-bit** (0–4095) |
| **Flash Memory** | 32 KB | 4 MB | 4 MB |
| **Operating Voltage** | 5 V | 3.3 V | 3.3 V |
| **Idle Current** | ~50 mA | ~70 mA | ~80 mA |
| **Cost (approx)** | ₹400 | ₹350 | ₹450 |

**Recommendation for Wi-Fi Weather Monitoring (2 marks):**

For a **Wi-Fi-based weather monitoring project** (which requires temperature, humidity, and pressure sensors, cloud publishing, and possibly future expansion to BLE-based mobile app), the **ESP32 DevKit** is the most suitable choice because:

1. It has **built-in Wi-Fi** (no extra shield needed).
2. **Higher ADC resolution (12-bit)** allows more accurate analog sensor readings.
3. **More GPIO pins** support multiple sensors (DHT22, BMP280, rain sensor, anemometer).
4. **Dual-core processing** handles Wi-Fi stack and sensor sampling concurrently.
5. **Bluetooth co-existence** allows optional mobile dashboard without extra hardware.
6. **Better flash memory (4 MB)** supports OTA (Over-The-Air) firmware updates.

*(NodeMCU is a close second if cost is the only concern; Arduino Uno is unsuitable due to lack of built-in Wi-Fi.)*

---

**(b) Explain the stages involved in compiling and uploading a sketch to an ESP32 board using Arduino IDE. What is the role of the USB-to-UART bridge? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Stage 1 — Authoring (1 mark)**
The developer writes source code in the Arduino IDE editor. Files are saved with the `.ino` extension. The IDE automatically generates a function prototype for every function defined in the sketch.

**Stage 2 — Library Linking (1 mark)**
Pre-built libraries such as `Wire.h`, `DHT.h`, `WiFi.h` are linked at compile time. The IDE scans `#include` directives and resolves paths using the *Library Manager*.

**Stage 3 — Cross-Compilation (1 mark)**
The IDE invokes the **xtensa-esp32-elf-gcc** compiler, which converts the C/C++ source code into ESP32 machine instructions. Output is a `.bin` binary file.

**Stage 4 — Pre-Processing & ELF Generation (1 mark)**
The linker merges all object files and libraries into an **ELF (Executable and Linkable Format)** file, then extracts the binary content.

**Stage 5 — Upload (Flashing) (2 marks)**
The IDE calls **`esptool.py`** (a Python utility) to:
- Reset the ESP32 into **download mode** by toggling GPIO 0 LOW.
- Erase flash if requested.
- Write the binary to flash memory at offset **0x10000** (default app partition).
- Verify the upload by reading back checksum.

**Stage 6 — Execution (1 mark)**
ESP32 reboots, reads the binary from flash, and executes `setup()` once followed by an infinite `loop()`.

**Role of the USB-to-UART Bridge (1 mark):**
- The ESP32 chip uses **UART (TX/RX pins)** for programming, but modern PCs have only USB ports.
- The **USB-to-UART bridge IC** (e.g., **CP2102** from Silicon Labs or **CH340** from WCH) acts as a **protocol converter**, translating USB packets on the host side into serial bytes the ESP32 can understand.
- It also provides **power (5V via VBUS)** and **automatic reset (DTR/RTS lines)** to enter download mode.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - ❌ **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing "UART" with "USB" — students write that ESP32 has a USB port (it does **not**; it has UART, and the *on-board* USB is provided by the bridge IC). (-1 mark)
> - ❌ **Common Mistake 2:** Writing the wrong compiler name — `gcc` is for AVR (Arduino Uno); ESP32 uses `xtensa-esp32-elf-gcc`. (-1 mark)
> - ❌ **Common Mistake 3:** Saying the binary is uploaded to RAM — it is uploaded to **flash memory**. (-1 mark)
> - ❌ **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting to mention that the **bridge IC also provides power** to the ESP32. (-1 mark)
> - ✅ **Pro Tip:** Draw a small diagram showing PC → USB → Bridge IC → UART TX/RX → ESP32 to earn a "diagram" bonus mark.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme Exam / Viva:**

- 🔹 **IoT Kit** = Processing + Sensing + Actuation + Communication + Power layers.
- 🔹 **Development Environment** = Authoring + Compilation + Upload + Runtime + Debugging.
- 🔹 **Most-used academic boards:** Arduino Uno (no Wi-Fi), NodeMCU (Wi-Fi), ESP32 (Wi-Fi + BLE), Raspberry Pi (Linux).
- 🔹 **Arduino IDE 2.x** is the recommended primary IDE for KTU labs.
- 🔹 **ESP32 board URL:** `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/espressif/arduino-esp32/gh-pages/package_esp32_index.json`
- 🔹 **Standard baud rate for ESP32:** **115200** (Serial Monitor must match exactly).
- 🔹 **USB-to-UART bridge ICs:** **CP2102** or **CH340** — required to program ESP32/NodeMCU.
- 🔹 **ADC resolution:** Arduino Uno / NodeMCU = **10-bit**; ESP32 = **12-bit**.
- 🔹 **Clock speeds:** Arduino = 16 MHz; NodeMCU = 80–160 MHz; ESP32 = up to **240 MHz**.
- 🔹 **DHT11 sampling rate:** minimum **2 seconds** between successive reads.
- 🔹 **MQTT broker port:** **1883** (plain) or **8883** (TLS).
- 🔹 **Voltage levels:** Arduino digital pins = **5V**; ESP32 / NodeMCU GPIO = **3.3V** (do not exceed!).
- 🔹 **Flashing offset for ESP32 application binary:** **0x10000**.
- 🔹 **Top 4 sensors to remember:** DHT11, HC-SR04, LDR, PIR.
- 🔹 **Top 2 actuators to remember:** LED, Relay module.
- 🔹 **Top 4 wireless protocols to remember:** Wi-Fi, BLE, LoRa, Zigbee.
- 🔹 **Public MQTT broker for testing:** `broker.hivemq.com` (port 1883).
- 🔹 **Always remember to install USB driver** before expecting the board to appear in *Tools → Port*.
- 🔹 **For any Wi-Fi + multi-sensor project, default choice = ESP32.**
- 🔹 **For any camera / ML / web-server project, default choice = Raspberry Pi.**
- 🔹 **Viva mantra:** "A Development Kit is the *hardware*; a Development Environment is the *software*. Both are needed."

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project on LEDs Controlled over Internet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCIT504 - PROGRAMMING FOR INTERNET OF THINGS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Project on LEDs Controlled over Internet # 💡 Project on LEDs Controlled over Internet — Core Foundations ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Internet-Controlled LED System (IoT Actuation Project):** A real-time embedded system in which Light Emitting Diodes (LEDs) are toggled, dimmed, or patterned remotely through a TCP/IP network (typically the public Internet) using an IoT-capable microcontroller (e.g., **ESP8266 / NodeMCU / ESP32**) as the actuation node, and a smartphone / web client as the control node, with message exchange governed by lightweight application-layer protocols such as **HTTP REST**, **MQTT**, or **WebSockets**. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this project demonstrates the canonical **"Sense → Connect → Cloud → Actuate"** IoT pipeline, and serves as the benchmark application for understanding the practical integration of **WiFi networking, embedded firmware, and a cloud/edge control dashboard**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine the electrical switchboard in your house. Traditionally, to turn on a light you must physically flick a switch on the wall. Now imagine: - The wall switch is replaced by an **invisible "Internet switch"** that you can press from anywhere in the world — your office, an airport, or even another country. - The light bulb in your room listens for a *command message* over the WiFi network. - The **microcontroller** is the "translator" that converts the digital command (1 or 0) into an electrical signal on a General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) pin, which then lights up the LED. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The entire project boils down to ONE core idea:** A button press on your phone travels over the Internet → reaches a cloud broker → reaches the WiFi-enabled microchip → toggles a pin → lights up (or switches off) an LED. This is the **Hello-World equivalent** of the IoT world, just as printing "Hello, World!" is the first program in C. ## 1.3 Key Building Blocks (Hardware & Software) | # | Component | Role in the Project | Typical Choice | |---|-----------|--------------------|----------------| | 1 | Microcontroller (MCU) | WiFi-enabled brain that runs the firmware | **NodeMCU (ESP8266)** or **ESP32** | | 2 | Actuator (Output) | The device being controlled | **LED** (or relay for AC bulbs) | | 3 | Current-Limiting Resistor | Protects LED from over-current | **220 Ω** resistor (for ~3.3 V) | | 4 | Power Supply | Powers the MCU & LED | USB 5 V or external 3.3 V regulator | | 5 | Network | Transport medium for commands | **WiFi (802.11 b/g/n, 2.4 GHz)** | | 6 | Protocol | Language of communication | **HTTP / MQTT / WebSocket** | | 7 | Cloud / Dashboard | Remote user interface | **Blynk / ThingSpeak / Firebase / AWS IoT** | | 8 | Client | The user pressing the button | Smartphone app or web browser | ## 1.4 Project Statement (KTU Standard) > **Design and develop an IoT system to control three (3) LEDs connected to an ESP8266/NodeMCU such that the LEDs can be switched ON/OFF individually from a smartphone over the Internet using the MQTT protocol (or Blynk platform). The status of each LED shall be reflected in real time on the dashboard.** # 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis — Architecture, Protocols & Theory ## 2.1 The Operational Concept (Logic Flow) The end-to-end lifecycle of a single button-press on the smartphone → LED glow can be broken into **7 discrete stages**: 1. **User Intent Capture** — The user taps a virtual button in the mobile app (e.g., Blynk). 2. **Command Serialization** — The app converts the tap into a structured message: e.g., `{"led": 2, "state": "ON"}` (JSON) or a single byte payload over MQTT. 3. **Cloud Broker / Server Hop** — The message is published to a cloud broker (MQTT broker like **HiveMQ / Mosquitto / Blynk Cloud**). 4. **Internet Routing** — The TCP packet traverses the **public Internet** using IP routing (typically IPv4 today, migrating to IPv6). 5. **WiFi Reception at MCU** — The ESP8266's built-in WiFi stack (running **lwIP**) receives the packet, decapsulates the TCP segment, and hands the payload to the application layer. 6. **GPIO Driver Action** — The Arduino-style `digitalWrite(pin, HIGH/LOW)` instruction drives the GPIO pin to 3.3 V (HIGH) or 0 V (LOW). 7. **Visual Feedback** — Current flows through the LED (limited by a 220 Ω resistor), and photons are emitted. ## 2.2 Why ESP8266 / NodeMCU? > [!IMPORTANT] > The **ESP8266** integrates a **32-bit Tensilica L106 Diamond Standard 106Micro** processor with built-in **802.11 b/g/n WiFi**, **GPIO**, **ADC**, **I2C**, **SPI**, and **UART** — all on a single chip costing under ₹200. The **NodeMCU** is a development board that breaks out all ESP8266 pins with on-board USB-UART and a 3.3 V regulator. | Property | Value | Significance | |----------|-------|--------------| | Operating Voltage | **3.3 V** | GPIO is NOT 5 V tolerant — use level shifters for Arduino Uno | | Flash Memory | **1 MB to 4 MB** | Stores program + WiFi credentials | | SRAM | **~50 KB usable** | Holds program variables + TCP buffers | | Clock Speed | **80 MHz (default) / 160 MHz (boost)** | Sufficient for MQTT/HTTP handling | | WiFi Modes | **STA, AP, STA+AP** | Can act as station, access point, or both | | GPIO Current | **Max 12 mA per pin** | LEDs draw ~10–20 mA — use driver transistor for arrays | ## 2.3 Protocol Selection: MQTT vs HTTP | Criterion | HTTP REST | MQTT | |-----------|-----------|------| | Pattern | Request → Response | Publish → Subscribe | | Overhead | High (HTTP headers ~200+ bytes) | Low (fixed 2-byte header) | | Power Efficiency | Poor for battery devices | Excellent (designed for IoT) | | Real-Time Push | Needs polling or WebSockets | Native push from broker | | Typical KTU Choice | Web-server based projects | **Blynk / HiveMQ / Adafruit IO** | > [!NOTE] > For the KTU 2024 board exam, **MQTT is the de-facto recommended protocol** because it is publish-subscribe, lightweight, and aligns with the syllabus' emphasis on "cloud-mediated IoT". ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Electronics & Timing) | Symbol | Formula | Description | Units | |--------|---------|-------------|-------| | $V_{LED}$ | $V_F \approx 2.0\text{ V}$ (red), $3.2\text{ V}$ (blue/white) | Forward voltage of LED | Volts (V) | | $I_{LED}$ | $10\text{ mA} - 20\text{ mA}$ (typical) | Safe forward current | milliampere (mA) | | $R_{series}$ | $\dfrac{V_{supply} - V_{F}}{I_{F}}$ | Current-limiting resistor | Ohms (Ω) | | $R_{calc}$ | $\dfrac{3.3 - 2.0}{0.010} = 130\ \Omega$ | For red LED on 3.3 V at 10 mA | Ω | | $R_{chosen}$ | **220 Ω** (standard nearest value) | Safe & bright | Ω | | $t_{publish}$ | $1\text{ s} \le t \le 60\text{ s}$ | MQTT publish interval (status) | seconds (s) | | $P_{LED}$ | $V_{F} \times I_{F}$ | Power per LED | Watts (W) | | RSSI | $-30\text{ dBm (excellent)} \to -90\text{ dBm (poor)}$ | WiFi signal strength | dBm | > **Note on absolute value symbol:** In LaTeX we use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown tables, e.g., $\vert V_{F} \vert$. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility This exact project architecture scales up to: - **Smart Street Lighting** — Municipal corporations control thousands of LED streetlights from a central dashboard. - **Smart Home Automation** — Philips Hue, Xiaomi Yeelight — all use the same publish/subscribe paradigm. - **Industrial Indicating Panels** — Tower lights in factories with remote status monitoring. - **Agricultural Greenhouses** — LED grow lights controlled based on cloud weather data. # 🛠️ Step-by-Step Implementation, Code & Derivation ## 3.1 Hardware Derivation — Choosing the Resistor Value **Given:** - Supply voltage to LED anode: $V_{CC} = 3.3\ \text{V}$ (NodeMCU GPIO HIGH) - Red LED forward voltage: $V_{F} = 2.0\ \text{V}$ - Desired forward current: $I_{F} = 10\ \text{mA} = 0.010\ \text{A}$ **Derivation using Ohm's Law:** $$V_R = V_{CC} - V_{F}$$ Substituting values: $$V_R = 3.3\ \text{V} - 2.0\ \text{V} = 1.3\ \text{V}$$ $$R = \dfrac{V_R}{I_{F}} = \dfrac{1.3}{0.010}$$ $$R = 130\ \Omega$$ **Practical choice:** The nearest standard E12 resistor value **greater** than 130 Ω is **220 Ω**, which gives a safe current of: $$I_F = \dfrac{3.3 - 2.0}{220} = \dfrac{1.3}{220} = 0.0059\ \text{A} = 5.9\ \text{mA}$$ This is well within the safe operating range of any standard 5 mm LED. ## 3.2 Hardware Circuit — Wiring | NodeMCU Pin | Connects To | Purpose | |-------------|-------------|---------| | **D1 (GPIO5)** | LED1 anode via 220 Ω → GND | LED 1 control | | **D2 (GPIO4)** | LED2 anode via 220 Ω → GND | LED 2 control | | **D3 (GPIO0)** | LED3 anode via 220 Ω → GND | LED 3 control | | **GND** | Common cathode rail | Reference ground | | **Vin / USB** | 5 V supply | Power input | > [!IMPORTANT] > NodeMCU GPIO pins output **3.3 V** when HIGH. They are **NOT 5 V tolerant** as inputs. Never connect them directly to a 5 V Arduino or to AC mains without a relay. ## 3.3 Project Architecture (Data Flow) ``` Smartphone App → Cloud MQTT Broker → Internet (TCP/IP) → Home WiFi Router │ ▼ NodeMCU (ESP8266) │ ▼ GPIO5 / GPIO4 / GPIO0 │ ▼ LED1 / LED2 / LED3 ``` ## 3.4 Firmware Implementation (Arduino IDE, C/C++) Below is the **complete, KTU-style, fully-commented** Arduino sketch for an MQTT-based 3-LED controller using the **PubSubClient** library and a public **HiveMQ** broker. ```cpp /*============================================================= * PROJECT : LEDs Controlled over Internet (MQTT) * MCU : NodeMCU (ESP8266) * IDE : Arduino IDE 2.x * LIBRARIES: ESP8266WiFi.h, PubSubClient.h * KTU 2024 : PBCIT504 - Module 4 Demonstration *===========================================================*/ #include // Step 1: WiFi stack #include // Step 2: MQTT client /* -------- USER CONFIGURATION -------- */ const char* WIFI_SSID = "YourHomeWiFi"; const char* WIFI_PASSWORD = "YourPassword123"; const char* MQTT_BROKER = "broker.hivemq.com"; // Public test broker const int MQTT_PORT = 1883; const char* MQTT_CLIENTID = "ktu_node_led_01"; /* Topic format: ktu/leds/control/L payload: "ON" or "OFF" Status topic: ktu/leds/status payload: JSON */ const char* TOPIC_CTRL[3] = { "ktu/leds/control/L1", "ktu/leds/control/L2", "ktu/leds/control/L3" }; /* -------- GPIO PIN MAPPING -------- */ const int LED_PINS[3] = { D1, D2, D3 }; // GPIO5, GPIO4, GPIO0 WiFiClient wifiClient; PubSubClient mqttClient(wifiClient); /* -------- STEP 3: WiFi INITIALIZATION -------- */ void setupWiFi() { delay(10); Serial.println("\nConnecting to WiFi..."); WiFi.begin(WIFI_SSID, WIFI_PASSWORD); int timeout = 0; while (WiFi.status() != WL_CONNECTED && timeout < 30) { delay(500); Serial.print("."); timeout++; } if (WiFi.status() == WL_CONNECTED) { Serial.println("\nWiFi Connected!"); Serial.print("IP Address: "); Serial.println(WiFi.localIP()); } else { Serial.println("\nWiFi FAILED. Restarting..."); ESP.restart(); } } /* -------- STEP 4: MQTT CALLBACK (COMMAND RECEIVER) -------- */ void mqttCallback(char* topic, byte* payload, unsigned int length) { String msg = ""; for (unsigned int i = 0; i < length; i++) { msg += (char)payload[i]; } msg.toUpperCase(); // accept both "on"/"ON" Serial.print("Topic: "); Serial.println(topic); Serial.print("Msg : "); Serial.println(msg); /* Decode which LED is addressed and toggle it */ for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) { if (String(topic) == TOPIC_CTRL[i]) { if (msg == "ON") { digitalWrite(LED_PINS[i], HIGH); Serial.printf("LED %d switched ON\n", i + 1); } else if (msg == "OFF") { digitalWrite(LED_PINS[i], LOW); Serial.printf("LED %d switched OFF\n", i + 1); } /* Publish the new state back as confirmation */ String statusTopic = "ktu/leds/status"; String statusMsg = "{\"led" + String(i+1) + "\":\"" + msg + "\"}"; mqttClient.publish(statusTopic.c_str(), statusMsg.c_str(), true); } } } /* -------- STEP 5: MQTT RECONNECT (AUTO-RETRY) -------- */ void reconnectMQTT() { while (!mqttClient.connected()) { Serial.print("Attempting MQTT connection..."); if (mqttClient.connect(MQTT_CLIENTID)) { Serial.println("CONNECTED"); for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) { mqttClient.subscribe(TOPIC_CTRL[i]); Serial.printf("Subscribed to %s\n", TOPIC_CTRL[i]); } } else { Serial.print("FAILED, rc="); Serial.print(mqttClient.state()); Serial.println(" → retry in 3 s"); delay(3000); } } } /* -------- STEP 6: SETUP -------- */ void setup() { Serial.begin(115200); /* Configure LED pins as OUTPUT and start in OFF state */ for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) { pinMode(LED_PINS[i], OUTPUT); digitalWrite(LED_PINS[i], LOW); } setupWiFi(); mqttClient.setServer(MQTT_BROKER, MQTT_PORT); mqttClient.setCallback(mqttCallback); } /* -------- STEP 7: MAIN LOOP -------- */ void loop() { if (!mqttClient.connected()) { reconnectMQTT(); } mqttClient.loop(); // MUST be called frequently to process messages } ``` ## 3.5 Companion Python Publisher (Test Client) This small Python script can be run on a laptop to **publish MQTT messages** and verify the LED responses — it is the "virtual phone" pressing the buttons. ```python # test_publisher.py — Run on PC to simulate the mobile app import paho.mqtt.client as mqtt import time BROKER = "broker.hivemq.com" PORT = 1883 TOPICS = ["ktu/leds/control/L1", "ktu/leds/control/L2", "ktu/leds/control/L3"] STATES = ["ON", "OFF"] client = mqtt.Client(client_id="ktu_phone_simulator") client.connect(BROKER, PORT, keepalive=60) print("Publishing test messages. Press Ctrl+C to stop.") try: n = 0 while True: topic = TOPICS[n % 3] state = STATES[(n // 3) % 2] client.publish(topic, state, qos=0, retain=False) print(f"Published {topic} -> {state}") n += 1 time.sleep(2) except KeyboardInterrupt: client.disconnect() ``` ## 3.6 Step-by-Step Execution Trace (Analytical Walk-Through) | Time (s) | Action | Network Packet | GPIO State | |----------|--------|----------------|------------| | $0.0$ | Phone publishes `ON` to `ktu/leds/control/L1` | MQTT PUBLISH (QoS 0) | All LOW | | $0.05$ | Packet reaches HiveMQ broker | TCP ACK returned to phone | All LOW | | $0.15$ | Broker fans out to NodeMCU subscriber | MQTT PUBLISH to NodeMCU | All LOW | | $0.20$ | `mqttCallback()` triggered, `digitalWrite(D1, HIGH)` | — | D1 = HIGH → LED1 ON | | $0.25$ | NodeMCU publishes status `{"led1":"ON"}` | MQTT PUBLISH | D1 = HIGH | | $2.00$ | User taps OFF button | MQTT PUBLISH | — | | $2.20$ | Callback fires, `digitalWrite(D1, LOW)` | — | D1 = LOW → LED1 OFF | ## 3.7 Compilation of the Derivation Chain — Final Summary $$ \begin{aligned} R_{\text{required}} &= \frac{V_{CC} - V_{F}}{I_{F}} \\[4pt] &= \frac{3.3\ \text{V} - 2.0\ \text{V}}{0.010\ \text{A}} \\[4pt] &= 130\ \Omega \\[4pt] R_{\text{used}} &= 220\ \Omega \quad \text{(standard E12)} \\[4pt] I_{F,\text{actual}} &= \frac{3.3 - 2.0}{220} = 5.9\ \text{mA} \;\;(\text{safe}) \\[4pt] P_{LED} &= V_{F} \times I_{F,\text{actual}} = 2.0 \times 0.0059 \\[4pt] &= 0.0118\ \text{W} \;\;(\text{negligible heat}) \end{aligned} $$ # 📊 Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[userDevice
Smartphone App]:::client B[cloudBroker
MQTT Broker HiveMQ]:::cloud C[homeRouter
WiFi Router 2.4GHz]:::net D[edgeNode
NodeMCU ESP8266]:::mcu E[ledBank
3 LEDs plus 220 ohm]:::actuator A -- PUBLISH ktu/leds/control/L1 --> B B -- FAN OUT to subscribers --> C C -- WiFi TCP packet --> D D -- GPIO5 GPIO4 GPIO0 --> E E -- photons emitted --> A D -- PUBLISH ktu/leds/status --> B classDef client fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cloud fill:#9FC5E8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef net fill:#C5E0B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef mcu fill:#F4B183,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef actuator fill:#E6B9D8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Firmware State Machine (Mermaid) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> BootInit BootInit --> WiFiConnect : WiFi begin called WiFiConnect --> WiFiOK : status equals WL CONNECTED WiFiConnect --> WiFiFail : timeout 15 seconds WiFiFail --> ESPReset ESPReset --> BootInit WiFiOK --> MQTTConnect : set server and callback MQTTConnect --> Subscribed : connect success MQTTConnect --> RetryWait : connect failure rc not zero RetryWait --> MQTTConnect : after 3 second delay Subscribed --> IdleListen : subscribe to 3 topics IdleListen --> CommandRecv : incoming PUBLISH packet CommandRecv --> ToggleLED : parse led index and state ToggleLED --> PublishAck : publish status JSON PublishAck --> IdleListen IdleListen --> IdleListen : keep alive ping every 60 s IdleListen --> MQTTConnect : connection lost detected ``` ## 4.3 Hardware Wiring Schematic (Mermaid Functional Map) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PowerRail[Power Supply 3.3 V and GND] VCC[VIN 5 V from USB]:::pwr REG[Onboard 3.3 V LDO AMS1117]:::pwr GND[Common GND]:::pwr end subgraph MCUBlock[NodeMCU ESP8266] GP1[GPIO5 pin D1]:::pin GP2[GPIO4 pin D2]:::pin GP3[GPIO0 pin D3]:::pin WIFI[WiFi radio 2.4 GHz]:::radio end subgraph LEDBank[Actuator Bank] R1[Resistor 220 ohm 1]:::r R2[Resistor 220 ohm 2]:::r R3[Resistor 220 ohm 3]:::r L1[LED 1 red]:::led L2[LED 2 green]:::led L3[LED 3 blue]:::led end VCC --> REG REG --> GP1 REG --> GP2 REG --> GP3 GP1 --> R1 --> L1 --> GND GP2 --> R2 --> L2 --> GND GP3 --> R3 --> L3 --> GND WIFI -. 2.4 GHz .-> AP((Home Router)) classDef pwr fill:#FFE699,stroke:#333,color:#000 classDef pin fill:#F4B183,stroke:#333,color:#000 classDef r fill:#D9D9D9,stroke:#333,color:#000 classDef led fill:#C5E0B4,stroke:#333,color:#000 classDef radio fill:#9FC5E8,stroke:#333,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Data Flow Sequence (Mermaid Sequence Diagram) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User Phone participant B as MQTT Broker participant N as NodeMCU participant L as LED Hardware U->>B: PUBLISH ktu/leds/control/L1 payload ON B-->>U: PUBACK B->>N: Forward PUBLISH to subscribed client N->>N: mqttCallback parses topic and payload N->>L: digitalWrite D1 HIGH L-->>N: LED 1 emits photons N->>B: PUBLISH ktu/leds/status JSON led1 ON B-->>N: PUBACK Note over U,L: Round trip typically 100 to 300 ms on local WiFi ``` # 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Rapid Recap > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls** > 1. Students often **forget to declare `pinMode(ledPin, OUTPUT)`** in `setup()` — the LED will behave erratically (−2 marks). > 2. Using a **5 V Arduino relay module directly on a 3.3 V ESP8266 GPIO** will permanently damage the pin (−3 marks). > 3. Hard-coding WiFi credentials in the question paper is acceptable, but you MUST **wrap them in `const char*`** and place them **outside `loop()`** to avoid stack corruption (−1 mark). > 4. Failing to call `mqttClient.loop()` continuously in `loop()` is the #1 reason MQTT projects "work once then freeze" (−3 marks). > 5. **Not mentioning the current-limiting resistor calculation** in the circuit-design part costs easy marks. Always show the **$R = (V_{CC} - V_{F})/I_{F}$** derivation. --- ## 🅰️ PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **List the major hardware and software components required to implement an "LEDs controlled over the Internet" project using NodeMCU and MQTT. Justify why MQTT is preferred over HTTP for this application.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Hardware:** NodeMCU (ESP8266) dev board, 3 × LEDs (Red/Green/Blue), 3 × 220 Ω current-limiting resistors, breadboard, USB cable, 5 V power source. **Software:** Arduino IDE 2.x with ESP8266 board package, `ESP8266WiFi.h` and `PubSubClient.h` libraries, a public MQTT broker (e.g., `broker.hivemq.com` on port **1883**), and a publisher app (Python `paho-mqtt` or mobile app like **MQTT Dash**). **Why MQTT > HTTP (1.5 marks):** | Feature | HTTP | MQTT | |---------|------|------| | Pattern | Request–Response | Publish–Subscribe | | Header size | Large (~200 B) | Tiny (2 B fixed) | | Power use | High (battery drain) | Low (sleep-friendly) | | Push support | Needs WebSocket/polling | Native | | Suitability for IoT | Medium | **High** ✓ | For low-bandwidth, battery-powered, real-time IoT control, **MQTT's lightweight publish-subscribe model** is the industry standard. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Explain the role of the `mqttCallback()` function in an MQTT-based LED control sketch. What happens if the function `mqttClient.loop()` is not called inside the main `loop()`?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The `mqttCallback(char* topic, byte* payload, unsigned int length)` is a **user-defined function registered via `mqttClient.setCallback(mqttCallback)`**. It is automatically invoked by the PubSubClient library **whenever a new PUBLISH packet arrives on a subscribed topic**. Inside it, we extract the topic name and the payload string, decode the LED index and the desired state (`ON`/`OFF`), and execute `digitalWrite()` accordingly. It may also publish a status acknowledgement back to the broker. If `mqttClient.loop()` is **not** called inside Arduino's `loop()`, the underlying TCP socket will not be polled, incoming MQTT messages will be **buffered in the lwIP stack and never delivered to the application**, the keep-alive PINGREQ packet will not be sent, and the broker will eventually **terminate the connection** (typical MQTT keep-alive is **60 s**). The project will appear "frozen" — earlier commands may execute, but the device silently disconnects. --- ## 🅱️ PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### ✳️ Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024] > **A. (a)** Design and explain the complete architecture of a system to control **three LEDs** over the Internet using **NodeMCU and the MQTT protocol**. Draw the functional block diagram, list the hardware, and identify the topics used for communication. **(7 marks)** > > **A. (b)** Write the complete **Arduino sketch** for the above system.
