Your code must include WiFi connection, MQTT subscription, a callback that handles `ON`/`OFF` messages for each LED, and status publishing. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution ### A.(a) — Architecture & Design (7 marks) **1. Functional Block Diagram (3 marks):** ``` [Smartphone / Web App] │ │ PUBLISH ktu/leds/control/L1..L3 ▼ [MQTT Broker — broker.hivemq.com : 1883] │ │ Forward to subscribed clients ▼ [Home WiFi Router] ◄── 2.4 GHz ──► [NodeMCU ESP8266] │ │ GPIO5, GPIO4, GPIO0 ▼ [220 Ω] → [LED1/2/3] → GND ``` **2. Hardware List (2 marks):** - NodeMCU v3 (ESP8266, 4 MB flash) - 3 × 5 mm LEDs (R, G, B) - 3 × 220 Ω resistors (E12 standard) - Breadboard, jumper wires, USB cable, 5 V/1 A adapter **3. MQTT Topic Design (2 marks):** | Topic | Direction | Payload | QoS | |-------|-----------|---------|-----| | `ktu/leds/control/L1` | App → MCU | `ON` or `OFF` | 0 | | `ktu/leds/control/L2` | App → MCU | `ON` or `OFF` | 0 | | `ktu/leds/control/L3` | App → MCU | `ON` or `OFF` | 0 | | `ktu/leds/status` | MCU → App | JSON `{"led1":"ON", ...}` | 0 (retain) | The **3 control topics** enable independent addressing of each LED, and the **single status topic** carries the aggregated state for dashboard display. --- ### A.(b) — Complete Arduino Code (7 marks) ```cpp #include #include const char* WIFI_SSID = "YourHomeWiFi"; const char* WIFI_PASS = "YourPass123"; const char* BROKER = "broker.hivemq.com"; const int PORT = 1883; const char* CLIENT_ID = "ktuNodeA_01"; const char* TOPIC_CTRL[3] = { "ktu/leds/control/L1", "ktu/leds/control/L2", "ktu/leds/control/L3" }; const char* TOPIC_STATUS = "ktu/leds/status"; const int LED_PINS[3] = { D1, D2, D3 }; WiFiClient espClient; PubSubClient mqtt(espClient); void setupWifi() { WiFi.begin(WIFI_SSID, WIFI_PASS); while (WiFi.status() != WL_CONNECTED) { delay(500); } } void callback(char* topic, byte* payload, unsigned int len) { String msg; for (unsigned int i = 0; i < len; i++) msg += (char)payload[i]; msg.toUpperCase(); for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) { if (String(topic) == TOPIC_CTRL[i]) { bool turnOn = (msg == "ON"); digitalWrite(LED_PINS[i], turnOn ? HIGH : LOW); char json[64]; sprintf(json, "{\"led%d\":\"%s\"}", i + 1, msg.c_str()); mqtt.publish(TOPIC_STATUS, json, true); } } } void reconnect() { while (!mqtt.connected()) { if (mqtt.connect(CLIENT_ID)) { for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) mqtt.subscribe(TOPIC_CTRL[i]); } else { delay(3000); } } } void setup() { Serial.begin(115200); for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) { pinMode(LED_PINS[i], OUTPUT); digitalWrite(LED_PINS[i], LOW); } setupWifi(); mqtt.setServer(BROKER, PORT); mqtt.setCallback(callback); } void loop() { if (!mqtt.connected()) reconnect(); mqtt.loop(); } ``` **Code-Walk-Through Marking (1 mark per correct concept):** - ✅ WiFi initialization with `WiFi.begin()` (1 m) - ✅ MQTT server set + callback registration (1 m) - ✅ `pinMode()` in `setup()` (1 m) - ✅ Topic-to-LED mapping in `callback()` (1 m) - ✅ `digitalWrite()` actuation (1 m) - ✅ Status publish back (1 m) - ✅ `mqtt.loop()` in `loop()` (1 m) --- ### ✳️ Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] > **B. (a)** Compare HTTP and MQTT protocols in the context of IoT. Explain the publish-subscribe model with a suitable diagram. Why is HTTP considered heavier for IoT applications? **(7 marks)** > > **B. (b)** Design the **complete circuit** (with resistor derivation) and write a **step-by-step algorithm** to control two LEDs from a Blynk mobile app using ESP8266. Mention the virtual pins used. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution ### B.(a) — HTTP vs MQTT & Pub-Sub Model (7 marks) **Comparison Table (3 marks):** | Parameter | HTTP (REST) | MQTT | |-----------|-------------|------| | Architecture | Client–Server (1-to-1) | Publish–Subscribe (many-to-many) | | Transport | TCP | TCP | | Header | Hundreds of bytes | 2-byte fixed header | | Method | GET, POST, PUT, DELETE | CONNECT, PUBLISH, SUBSCRIBE | | Security | HTTPS (TLS) | TLS via `mqtts://` port 8883 | | Bandwidth | High | **< 1 KB/s typical** | | Battery | Drains quickly | **Sleeps when idle** | | Push | Polling/WebSocket needed | **Native push** | | KTU 2024 Use | Web dashboards | **Blynk / IoT cloud** ✓ | **Publish-Subscribe Model (3 marks):** ``` ┌────────────┐ publish ┌────────────┐ deliver ┌────────────┐ │ Publisher │ ─────────► │ Broker │ ─────────► │ Subscriber │ │ (Phone App)│ │ (HiveMQ) │ │ (NodeMCU) │ └────────────┘ └────────────┘ └────────────┘ │ │ deliver ▼ ┌────────────┐ │ Subscriber │ │ (Other App)│ └────────────┘ ``` The **Publisher** (e.g., smartphone) does not know who the Subscribers are. It simply sends a message **tagged with a topic** to the **Broker** (central server). The **Broker** is responsible for filtering and forwarding messages to all clients that have **subscribed** to matching topics. This **decouples** publishers from subscribers and is what makes MQTT scalable to millions of IoT devices. **Why HTTP is heavier (1 mark):** Each HTTP request carries verbose headers (User-Agent, Cookies, Accept, etc.) totalling 200–800 bytes even for a tiny payload like `{"led":"ON"}`. MQTT's 2-byte fixed header and topic-based addressing result in **10–50× less bandwidth** for the same logical message, which is critical for cellular/satellite IoT deployments. --- ### B.(b) — Blynk-Based Two-LED Controller (7 marks) **1. Circuit with Resistor Derivation (3 marks):** For a red LED ($V_{F} = 2.0\ \text{V}$) on NodeMCU 3.3 V GPIO at $I_{F} = 10\ \text{mA}$: $$R = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{F}}{I_{F}} = \frac{3.3 - 2.0}{0.010} = 130\ \Omega \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{use } \mathbf{220\ \Omega}$$ **Wiring:** | NodeMCU Pin | Wire To | Connected Component | |-------------|---------|---------------------| | **D1 (GPIO5)** | 220 Ω → LED1 anode | LED1 (Red) | | **D2 (GPIO4)** | 220 Ω → LED2 anode | LED2 (Green) | | **GND** | LED1 cathode + LED2 cathode | Common ground | | **Vin** | 5 V USB | Power | **2. Blynk Setup (2 marks):** - Create a new Blynk template. - Add **two Button widgets**: V0 (mapped to D1), V1 (mapped to D2). Set each button to **"Switch" mode** and pin to **"Digital"** with value range 0–1. - Copy the **Auth Token** from Blynk to the sketch. **3. Step-by-Step Algorithm (2 marks):** ``` Step 1 : Install Blynk library in Arduino IDE Step 2 : Define BLYNK_AUTH_TOKEN from Blynk app Step 3 : Define WIFI_SSID and WIFI_PASSWORD Step 4 : Define BLYNK_TEMPLATE_ID and BLYNK_TEMPLATE_NAME Step 5 : In setup() - Serial.begin(115200) Step 6 : pinMode(D1, OUTPUT) and pinMode(D2, OUTPUT) Step 7 : Blynk.begin(BLYNK_AUTH_TOKEN, ssid, pass) Step 8 : Implement BLYNK_WRITE(V0) to read V0 and digitalWrite(D1, val) Step 9 : Implement BLYNK_WRITE(V1) to read V1 and digitalWrite(D2, val) Step 10: In loop() - call Blynk.run() continuously ``` **Blynk Virtual Pin Mapping:** V0 → D1 (LED1), V1 → D2 (LED2). When the user toggles Button V0 in the app, Blynk cloud sends a value (0 or 1) to the ESP8266, triggering the `BLYNK_WRITE(V0)` handler, which sets the GPIO accordingly. --- ## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Internet-Controlled LED Project** - 🔌 **Hardware MCU:** NodeMCU (ESP8266) — 3.3 V logic, NOT 5 V tolerant. - 💡 **Resistor Rule:** $R = (V_{CC} - V_{F})/I_{F}$ → for 3.3 V & red LED, use **220 Ω**. - 📡 **Protocol:** MQTT (port **1883**) preferred over HTTP for IoT. - 🧠 **MQTT Concepts:** Broker, Topic, Publish, Subscribe, QoS, Retain flag, Keep-alive (default 60 s). - 🔁 **Mandatory in `loop()`:** `mqttClient.loop()` — without it the connection silently dies. - 📍 **Topic Naming:** Use hierarchical, lowercase, slash-separated names (e.g., `ktu/leds/control/L1`). - 🪪 **QoS Levels:** 0 (at most once), 1 (at least once), 2 (exactly once) — use QoS 0 for LED on/off to save bandwidth. - 🛡️ **Retain Flag:** Set `retain=true` on status publishes so a new subscriber immediately sees the last state. - 🆔 **Client ID:** Must be **unique** per device on a public broker; otherwise, the older client gets kicked out. - 🧰 **Library Trio:** `ESP8266WiFi.h`, `PubSubClient.h`, optionally `BlynkSimpleEsp8266.h`. - 🔄 **Auto-Reconnect:** Always wrap `mqttClient.connect()` in a `while(!connected)` loop with `delay(3000)`. - 🚫 **Forbidden:** Never drive AC mains directly from an ESP8266 GPIO — use a **5 V relay module** with optocoupler. - 📊 **Power Formula:** $P_{LED} = V_{F} \times I_{F} \approx 12\ \text{mW}$ — negligible, no heatsink needed. - 🧪 **Testing Tool:** Use **MQTTBox** (Windows/Mac) or **MQTT Dash** (Android) to publish test messages from any device. - 📦 **Payload Format:** Plain `ON`/`OFF` for control; JSON for status — keep it human-readable for easy debugging. - 🌐 **Public Brokers:** `broker.hivemq.com:1883`, `test.mosquitto.org:1883`, `broker.emqx.io:1883` — fine for lab demos, NOT for production.

topics.title: Project on Temperature Logger
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT504 - PROGRAMMING FOR INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Project on Temperature Logger

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **IoT Temperature Logger** is a cyber-physical embedded system that integrates sensing, computation, communication, and storage subsystems to continuously capture ambient thermal measurements, time-stamp them, and transmit them to a remote repository (cloud or local server) for later analysis, visualization, and decision-making. In the context of the KTU 2024 scheme (Course PBCIT504), the project is implemented as a complete **vertical slice of an IoT solution** — spanning the *Perception Layer* (sensor), *Network Layer* (WiFi/MQTT/HTTP), and *Application Layer* (dashboard/database).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Checklist (must appear verbatim in answers):**
> An IoT Temperature Logger is an *event-driven, time-sampled, data-acquisition system* that uses a transducer (sensor) to convert physical thermal energy into a proportional electrical signal, processes it via a microcontroller, and pushes the digital readings over an IP-based protocol to a sink node (cloud broker or web server) at predefined sampling intervals $T_s$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Health Watch" for Rooms

Imagine a patient in a hospital wearing a digital health band. The band continuously records heart rate, timestamps every reading, and silently sends data to the doctor's tablet. A **Temperature Logger** does the same job for a room, a greenhouse, a cold-storage warehouse, or a server rack:

- The **sensor** is the "thermometer sticker."
- The **microcontroller** is the "wrist-watch brain."
- The **WiFi module** is the "wireless signal that reaches the doctor's tablet."
- The **cloud dashboard** is the "tablet screen showing a live graph."

Just as a doctor cannot be physically present 24/7, we cannot deploy a human to read a thermometer every minute. The logger automates this drudgery and provides a *historical trail* — exactly what auditors and scientists need.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core metrics you must memorize for KTU viva:**
> - **Sampling Interval $T_s$** — typical values: **5 s, 30 s, 60 s, 300 s** (industrial default = **60 s**)
> - **ADC Resolution** — typical 10-bit (Arduino UNO) or **12-bit (ESP32)** = $2^{12} = \textbf{4096 levels}$
> - **Operating range of DHT11:** **0 °C – 50 °C**, $\pm$**2 °C accuracy**
> - **Operating range of DS18B20:** **−55 °C to +125 °C**, $\pm$**0.5 °C accuracy**

## 1.3 High-Level Block View of the Project

A Temperature Logger is never a single component — it is a *pipeline*. Memorize the following five-stage pipeline as it appears verbatim in KTU question papers:

$$
\text{Sensor} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Microcontroller (ADC + Firmware)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Connectivity (WiFi/MQTT)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Cloud / Local Sink} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Dashboard}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Examiners often award a full mark simply for naming these five stages in the correct sequence. Skipping any stage (especially the **timestamping** sub-step inside the firmware) is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Perception Layer — Choosing a Temperature Sensor

The project must begin with sensor selection. KTU frequently tests the *trade-offs* between sensors.

| Sensor | Output Type | Range | Accuracy | Protocol to MCU | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LM35** | Analog (voltage) | 0 – 100 °C | $\pm 0.5$ °C | ADC pin | Low |
| **DHT11** | Digital (single-wire) | 0 – 50 °C | $\pm 2$ °C | GPIO + DHTLib | Low |
| **DHT22 / AM2302** | Digital (single-wire) | −40 – 80 °C | $\pm 0.5$ °C | GPIO + DHTLib | Medium |
| **DS18B20** | Digital (1-Wire) | −55 – 125 °C | $\pm 0.5$ °C | GPIO + OneWire | Medium |
| **BME280** | Digital (I²C/SPI) | −40 – 85 °C | $\pm 1.0$ °C | I²C bus | High |

### 2.1.1 LM35 — The Voltage-to-Temperature Equation

LM35 outputs a linear voltage scaled at **10 mV per °C**. The MCU reads the analog pin and converts with the standard Arduino formula:

$$
T_{°C} = \frac{V_{out}}{10\,\text{mV}} = \frac{\text{ADC} \times V_{ref}}{1024 \times 10\,\text{mV}}
$$

Where:
- $\text{ADC}$ = digital value returned by `analogRead()` (0 – 1023 for 10-bit)
- $V_{ref}$ = reference voltage (default **5.0 V** on UNO, **3.3 V** on ESP32)
- 1024 = $2^{10}$ for a 10-bit ADC

### 2.1.2 DS18B20 — The 1-Wire Protocol

DS18B20 is a **digital** sensor: it performs the ADC conversion *internally* and exposes the result as a 9–12 bit two's-complement integer. The MCU talks to it over a single GPIO pin using Maxim's **1-Wire protocol**. Each device has a unique **64-bit ROM code**, allowing multiple sensors on one pin (parasitic power mode).

## 2.2 The Network Layer — Protocols

The Temperature Logger project typically employs one of three communication stacks:

### 2.2.1 HTTP (REST) — Simplest

The MCU acts as an HTTP **client**, sending `GET` or `POST` requests to a cloud endpoint (e.g., ThingSpeak, Firebase REST API). Suitable for *low-frequency* logging (every 1–5 minutes).

### 2.2.2 MQTT — Industry Standard for IoT

MQTT is a **publish/subscribe** protocol operating over TCP port **1883** (or 8883 for TLS). The architecture is:

- **Publisher** = the ESP32 logger
- **Broker** = a server (Mosquitto, HiveMQ, AWS IoT Core)
- **Subscriber** = the dashboard / mobile app
- **Topic** = a string such as `home/livingroom/temperature`

The QoS (Quality of Service) levels are:
- **QoS 0** — at most once (fire-and-forget)
- **QoS 1** — at least once (acknowledged)
- **QoS 2** — exactly once (4-step handshake)

### 2.2.3 CoAP — Constrained Devices

For very low-power nodes, **Constrained Application Protocol** over UDP port 5683 is used. KTU often asks students to *contrast* MQTT vs. CoAP.

## 2.3 The Application Layer — Sinks and Visualization

| Sink Type | Tool | KTU-Mentionable Feature |
|---|---|---|
| Public Cloud | **ThingSpeak** | Free, MATLAB-based analytics |
| Public Cloud | **Firebase Realtime DB** | JSON tree, REST API |
| Public Cloud | **AWS IoT Core** | Enterprise, MQTT over TLS |
| Private Cloud | **Node-RED** | Visual flow, runs on Raspberry Pi |
| Local file | **SD card module** | No internet required |
| Local file | **CSV via Serial** | Lab-friendly, plot with Excel/Python |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When answering KTU questions on data storage, you **must** mention that the *timestamp* (using `NTPClient` library) is critical. A temperature reading without a timestamp is scientifically useless because **time-series analysis requires monotonic time indices**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{out} = 10\,\text{mV} \times T_{°C}$ | LM35 transfer function | V, °C |
| 2 | $T_{°C} = \dfrac{\text{ADC} \times V_{ref}}{2^n \times 10\,\text{mV}}$ | ADC → temperature (analog sensors) | °C |
| 3 | $T_{°F} = T_{°C} \times \dfrac{9}{5} + 32$ | Celsius to Fahrenheit | °F |
| 4 | $T_{K} = T_{°C} + 273.15$ | Celsius to Kelvin | K |
| 5 | $\text{Resolution}_{°C} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n \times S}$ | Smallest detectable $\Delta T$ | °C / LSB |
| 6 | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{T_s}$ | Sampling frequency | Hz |
| 7 | $R_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i$ | Moving-average filter | °C |
| 8 | $\text{Alarm} = (T_i > T_{\max}) \;\lor\; (T_i < T_{\min})$ | Threshold trigger (event-driven) | boolean |
| 9 | $N_{\text{bits}} = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{V_{ref}}{V_{\text{LSB}}}\right)$ | ADC bit-depth from LSB size | bits |
| 10 | $P_{\text{link}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_t}{P_r}\right)$ | Path-loss (dB), for wireless range questions | dB |

> [!NOTE]
> The **pipe symbol prohibition** is enforced above: notice we used $\vert$ implicitly via fraction bars instead of `|x|` notation, so the markdown table renders without corruption.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cold-Chain Logistics** — pharma companies log vaccine temperatures; FDA audits require 1-minute granularity.
- **Smart Greenhouses** — automated ventilation triggers at 30 °C.
- **Data Centers** — ASHRAE standards mandate 18 – 27 °C; HVAC failure triggers alarms within 30 s.
- **Industrial Predictive Maintenance** — bearing temperature trending predicts failure 48 h in advance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Firmware Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix

This section delivers **exhaustive** derivations and an annotated firmware walkthrough. No step is skipped.

## 3.1 Derivation: ADC Reading to Celsius (LM35)

**Given:** Arduino UNO, $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V, 10-bit ADC, LM35 sensor.

**Step 1 — Sensor output equation** (datasheet):
$$
V_{out} = 10\,\text{mV/°C} \times T
$$

**Step 2 — ADC quantization** (any MCU):
$$
\text{ADC value} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{ref}} \times 2^{n} \right\rfloor
$$

**Step 3 — Substituting** $V_{out}$ from Step 1:
$$
\text{ADC} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{10\,\text{mV} \times T}{V_{ref}} \times 2^{n} \right\rfloor
$$

**Step 4 — Inverting** to recover temperature:
$$
T = \dfrac{\text{ADC} \times V_{ref}}{2^{n} \times 10\,\text{mV}}
$$

**Step 5 — Plugging numerical values** for Arduino UNO ($V_{ref}=5.0$ V, $n=10$):
$$
T = \dfrac{\text{ADC} \times 5.0}{1024 \times 0.010}
$$

**Step 6 — Simplification**:
$$
T = \dfrac{\text{ADC} \times 5.0}{10.24} = \text{ADC} \times 0.48828125\,°C/\text{count}
$$

**Step 7 — Worked numeric example** (ADC = 205):
$$
T = 205 \times 0.48828125 = 100.0976\,°C
$$

Since LM35 maxes out at 150 °C (corresponding to 1.5 V = ADC 307), the reading is valid.

## 3.2 Derivation: Sampling Theorem (Nyquist)

For a temperature signal whose highest meaningful frequency is $f_{\max}$, we must sample at:
$$
f_s \geq 2 f_{\max} \quad \text{(Shannon-Nyquist criterion)}
$$

**Worked example:** A greenhouse temperature varies measurably at most every 5 minutes. Then:
$$
f_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{300\,\text{s}} \approx 0.0033\,\text{Hz}
$$
$$
f_s \geq 0.0067\,\text{Hz} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_s \leq 149\,\text{s}
$$

Hence a sampling period of **60 s** comfortably satisfies Nyquist, and a 5-min interval is also fine.

## 3.3 Derivation: Threshold-Trigger Event

Define alert condition:
$$
\text{alert}(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } T(t) \geq T_{\max} \;\text{or}\; T(t) \leq T_{\min} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Worked example:** Refrigerator for vaccines, $T_{\min} = 2\,°C$, $T_{\max} = 8\,°C$.

Suppose the logger records 10 readings:

$$
\{4.1,\; 5.0,\; 6.2,\; 8.1,\; 9.3,\; 7.4,\; 5.5,\; 4.0,\; 1.9,\; 6.0\}
$$

We annotate the alert state:

| Reading # | $T_i$ (°C) | $T_i \geq 8$? | $T_i \leq 2$? | Alert |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4.1 | No | No | 0 |
| 2 | 5.0 | No | No | 0 |
| 3 | 6.2 | No | No | 0 |
| 4 | 8.1 | Yes | No | 1 |
| 5 | 9.3 | Yes | No | 1 |
| 6 | 7.4 | No | No | 0 |
| 7 | 5.5 | No | No | 0 |
| 8 | 4.0 | No | No | 0 |
| 9 | 1.9 | No | Yes | 1 |
| 10 | 6.0 | No | No | 0 |

Total alert events: **3** out of 10 — these trigger the actuator (sms/buzzer) via MQTT publish on a separate topic.

## 3.4 Firmware Walkthrough (ESP32 + DHT22 + ThingSpeak via HTTP)

Below is a **complete, runnable Arduino sketch** for an ESP32-based Temperature Logger. Read the annotations in parallel.

```cpp
#include <WiFi.h>
#include <HTTPClient.h>
#include <DHT.h>

// ---- Configuration constants ----
#define DHTPIN    4        // GPIO 4 on ESP32
#define DHTTYPE   DHT22
const char* ssid     = "YOUR_WIFI_SSID";
const char* password = "YOUR_WIFI_PASS";
const char* apiKey   = "YOUR_THINGSPEAK_WRITE_KEY";

// ---- Sampling period ----
const unsigned long SAMPLE_PERIOD_MS = 60000;  // 60 s
unsigned long lastSample = 0;

// ---- Sensor object ----
DHT dht(DHTPIN, DHTTYPE);

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  dht.begin();

  // Connect to WiFi
  WiFi.begin(ssid, password);
  while (WiFi.status() != WL_CONNECTED) {
    delay(500);
    Serial.print(".");
  }
  Serial.println("\nWiFi connected");
}

void loop() {
  unsigned long now = millis();
  if (now - lastSample < SAMPLE_PERIOD_MS) return;   // 1) Wait for next tick
  lastSample = now;

  // 2) Read sensor (blocking ~2 s for DHT22)
  float temperature = dht.readTemperature();   // °C
  float humidity    = dht.readHumidity();      // %RH

  if (isnan(temperature) || isnan(humidity)) {
    Serial.println("Sensor read FAILED");
    return;                                     // 3) Robustness: skip bad sample
  }

  // 4) Build HTTP URL with query string
  String url = "http://api.thingspeak.com/update";
  url += "?api_key="; url += apiKey;
  url += "&field1="; url += String(temperature, 2);
  url += "&field2="; url += String(humidity, 2);

  // 5) Push to cloud
  if (WiFi.status() == WL_CONNECTED) {
    HTTPClient http;
    http.begin(url);
    int httpCode = http.GET();
    if (httpCode > 0) {
      Serial.printf("Pushed T=%.2f H=%.2f  HTTP=%d\n",
                    temperature, humidity, httpCode);
    } else {
      Serial.printf("HTTP error: %s\n", http.errorToString(httpCode).c_str());
    }
    http.end();
  }
}
```

### 3.4.1 Line-by-Line Logic Map

| Code Region | Engineering Purpose |
|---|---|
| `#include <DHT.h>` | Driver for the single-wire protocol of DHT22. |
| `WiFi.begin(ssid, password)` | Initializes the **Network Layer** (802.11 b/g/n station mode). |
| `dht.readTemperature()` | Issues a start pulse, waits ~2 s, returns float in °C. |
| `isnan(temperature)` | Defensive check: DHT sensors occasionally return `NaN`. |
| `String url = "...update?..."` | ThingSpeak REST endpoint: `GET /update` with query params. |
| `SAMPLE_PERIOD_MS` | Implements the **sampling theorem** constraint from §3.2. |
| `millis()` | Non-blocking delay; preferred over `delay()` in KTU viva answers. |

## 3.5 Mathematical Check for the ADC Resolution (LM35 + ESP32 12-bit)

For ESP32 with 12-bit ADC and 3.3 V reference, attach LM35:

$$
\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n \times S} = \dfrac{3.3}{4096 \times 0.010} = 0.0806\,°C/\text{count}
$$

This means the smallest measurable temperature change is **~0.08 °C**, which is finer than DHT11's $\pm 2\,°C$ accuracy. Use this calculation in viva when justifying sensor selection.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph perception[Perception Layer]
        A1[LM35 / DHT22 / DS18B20 Sensor]
        A2[Signal Conditioning and ADC]
        A1 --> A2
    end

    subgraph edge[Edge / Network Layer]
        B1[ESP32 Microcontroller]
        B2[WiFi 802.11 b/g/n Radio]
        B3[MQTT or HTTP Client Firmware]
        A2 --> B1
        B1 --> B3
        B3 --> B2
    end

    subgraph cloud[Application Layer]
        C1[Cloud Broker - Mosquitto or ThingSpeak]
        C2[Time Series Database]
        C3[Web Dashboard or Mobile App]
        B2 -->|publish payload| C1
        C1 --> C2
        C2 --> C3
    end

    subgraph actuator[Actuator Layer - Optional]
        D1[Buzzer or Relay or SMS Gateway]
        C1 -->|subscribe alert topic| D1
    end
```

## 4.2 Data Flow Sequence (Mermaid Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sensor as DHT22 Sensor
    participant MCU as ESP32 Firmware
    participant WiFi as WiFi AP and Router
    participant Broker as MQTT Broker
    participant Dash as Node-RED Dashboard
    participant Phone as Mobile Subscriber

    loop Every 60 seconds
        Sensor->>MCU: Sends temperature and humidity bits
        MCU->>MCU: ADC convert and timestamp via NTP
        MCU->>WiFi: Connect (if not connected)
        WiFi-->>MCU: DHCP lease
        MCU->>Broker: PUBLISH home/room1/temp QoS 1
        Broker-->>Dash: Forward payload
        Dash->>Dash: Update gauge widget and time-series chart
        Dash-->>Phone: Push notification if threshold breached
    end
```

## 4.3 Hardware Wiring Reference (Block-Level Map)

Since physical drawings are difficult to render in Mermaid, we provide a **functional block map** mapping each pin to its role:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ESP32_DevKit
        VIN[VIN Pin - 5V Input]
        GND1[GND Pin]
        GPIO4[GPIO 4 - DHT DATA]
        GPIO2[GPIO 2 - Status LED]
        GPIO21[GPIO 21 - I2C SDA to OLED]
        GPIO22[GPIO 22 - I2C SCL to OLED]
    end

    subgraph Sensors
        DHT22[DHT22 VCC to 3V3, DATA to GPIO4, GND to GND]
        OLED[0.96 inch SSD1306 OLED - SDA to GPIO21, SCL to GPIO22]
    end

    subgraph Power
        PSU[5V 2A SMPS Adapter]
        PSU --> VIN
    end

    DHT22 --> GPIO4
    OLED --> GPIO21
    OLED --> GPIO22
```

## 4.4 MQTT Topic Hierarchy (KTU-Favorite Topic)

```
home/
   ├── livingroom/
   │     ├── temperature     (°C float)
   │     ├── humidity        (%RH float)
   │     └── battery         (V float)
   ├── bedroom/
   │     ├── temperature
   │     └── humidity
   └── alerts/
         ├── overheat         (string: "Kitchen 38.2C at 14:22")
         └── sensor_offline  (string: "Garage DHT lost at 02:10")
```

> [!TIP]
> When asked to "design the topic structure" in the exam, always use a **hierarchical, slash-separated, lower-case** layout. Examiners explicitly mark this as good practice.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define an IoT Temperature Logger and list its three primary layers.

**Model Answer (verbatim-ready):**

An IoT Temperature Logger is a cyber-physical system that periodically measures ambient temperature using a transducer, processes the reading with a microcontroller, and transmits it over an IP network to a remote sink for storage and visualization.

The three primary layers are:
1. **Perception Layer** — physical sensor (DHT22 / DS18B20) and ADC.
2. **Network Layer** — WiFi module running MQTT or HTTP client.
3. **Application Layer** — cloud broker, database, and dashboard.

*(Self-explanatory keywords: transducer, microcontroller, IP network, remote sink, perception-network-application. Always use these exact terms.)* **(3 marks)**

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Differentiate between MQTT and HTTP for IoT temperature logging. State two advantages of MQTT.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | HTTP | MQTT |
|---|---|---|
| Pattern | Request/Response | Publish/Subscribe |
| Port | 80 / 443 | 1883 / 8883 |
| Header size | Large (KB) | Tiny (2 bytes minimum) |
| Power | Higher | Lower |
| Suitability | Lab, occasional push | Battery nodes, real-time |

**Two advantages of MQTT:**
1. **Low bandwidth** — minimal 2-byte fixed header, ideal for GSM/NB-IoT.
2. **Asynchronous push** — broker fans out to many subscribers; no polling overhead.
3. *(Bonus 1 mark)* **QoS levels** — at-least-once / exactly-once delivery guarantees.

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **Embedded + Networking Track**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, **explain the architecture of an IoT-based Temperature Logger** using ESP32 and DHT22. Describe the role of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the **complete Arduino firmware** to read the DHT22 every 60 seconds and publish the value to the MQTT broker `test.mosquitto.org` on topic `ktu/iot/temp`. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution A(a) — Architecture (7 marks)

The system has four functional blocks:

**Block 1 — Sensor Sub-system:** DHT22 measures air temperature (capacitive polymer) and humidity. It returns calibrated digital data over a single-wire protocol; no ADC required on the MCU side.

**Block 2 — Processing Sub-system:** ESP32 (Xtensa LX6 dual-core, 240 MHz, 520 KB SRAM) reads the sensor via the `DHT` library, attaches a Unix timestamp using the `NTPClient` library (synchronized from `pool.ntp.org`), packages the payload as a JSON string:

```json
{"device":"esp32_01","temp_c":28.4,"hum":62.1,"ts":1719854100}
```

**Block 3 — Communication Sub-system:** ESP32's built-in 2.4 GHz WiFi connects to the campus AP. The `PubSubClient` library opens a TCP socket to `test.mosquitto.org:1883`, subscribes to a control topic, and publishes the JSON payload to `ktu/iot/temp` at QoS 1.

**Block 4 — Cloud / Application Sub-system:** A Node-RED dashboard running on a Raspberry Pi subscribes to the same topic, parses the JSON, and plots the values on a Chart.js time-series widget. Alerts are forwarded to a Telegram bot when the temperature exceeds 35 °C.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark-split rubric (examiner's guide):**
> - Block diagram with labels: 2 marks
> - Sensor + signal path: 1 mark
> - Firmware stages: 2 marks
> - Connectivity (MQTT): 1 mark
> - Application layer: 1 mark

#### Solution A(b) — Firmware (7 marks)

```cpp
#include <WiFi.h>
#include <PubSubClient.h>
#include <DHT.h>

#define DHTPIN   4
#define DHTTYPE  DHT22

const char* ssid       = "CampusWiFi";
const char* password   = "lab12345";
const char* mqttServer = "test.mosquitto.org";
const int   mqttPort   = 1883;
const char* topic      = "ktu/iot/temp";

DHT dht(DHTPIN, DHTTYPE);
WiFiClient   espClient;
PubSubClient client(espClient);

unsigned long last = 0;
const unsigned long PERIOD = 60000UL;

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  dht.begin();
  WiFi.begin(ssid, password);
  while (WiFi.status() != WL_CONNECTED) { delay(500); }
  client.setServer(mqttServer, mqttPort);
}

void reconnect() {
  while (!client.connected()) {
    String clientId = "esp32_" + String(random(0xffff), HEX);
    if (client.connect(clientId.c_str())) {
      client.publish("ktu/iot/status", "online");
    } else {
      delay(2000);
    }
  }
}

void loop() {
  if (!client.connected()) reconnect();
  client.loop();

  if (millis() - last < PERIOD) return;
  last = millis();

  float t = dht.readTemperature();
  if (isnan(t)) return;

  char payload[120];
  snprintf(payload, sizeof(payload),
           "{\"device\":\"esp32_01\",\"temp_c\":%.2f}", t);

  client.publish(topic, payload, true);  // retained
  Serial.println(payload);
}
```

**Mark-split:**
- Header includes + DHT setup: 1
- WiFi/MQTT connect: 2
- Non-blocking `millis()` timing: 1
- JSON payload construction: 1
- Publish + retained flag: 1
- Robustness (`isnan`, `reconnect`): 1

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **Data + Cloud Track**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain **MQTT protocol** in detail. With a diagram, describe the publish-subscribe model and list all three QoS levels. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a **ThingSpeak dashboard URL** that plots temperature from `field1` and humidity from `field2` for channel `123456`. Explain how the REST API call `https://api.thingspeak.com/update` is used to push data. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution B(a) — MQTT Explained (7 marks)

**MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** is a lightweight, TCP-based publish/subscribe messaging protocol standardized as **ISO/IEC 20922**. It was invented by Andy Stanford-Clark (IBM) and Arlen Nipper in 1999 for unreliable satellite links on oil pipelines.

**Architecture (refer to Mermaid sequence diagram in §4.2):**
- **Broker** — central server (e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ) holding topic queues.
- **Publisher** — pushes messages to a topic.
- **Subscriber** — receives messages from a topic it has subscribed to.
- **Topic** — UTF-8 string with hierarchical `/` separators, e.g., `home/livingroom/temp`.

**Three QoS Levels:**

| Level | Name | Guarantee | Handshake | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| QoS 0 | At most once | None | PUB only | Fire-and-forget |
| QoS 1 | At least once | Possible duplicates | PUB + PUBACK | Most sensors |
| QoS 2 | Exactly once | No duplicates | 4-step handshake | Billing / alarms |

**Packet format (minimum 2 bytes):**
- Byte 1: fixed header (control packet type + flags)
- Byte 2+: variable header (topic name, packet ID for QoS > 0)
- Payload: up to 256 MB theoretically (typically < 1 KB for sensor data)

**Mark-split:** Definition + history (1), broker/publisher/subscriber (2), topic structure (1), QoS table (2), packet anatomy (1).

#### Solution B(a) - continued with example of LWT (Last Will and Testament)

A unique MQTT feature is the **Last Will and Testament (LWT)**: when a client connects, it can pre-register a message to be auto-published by the broker if the client disconnects ungracefully. KTU loves this point — for a Temperature Logger, the LWT message is:

```
ktu/iot/status  →  "offline"
```

This lets the dashboard know the device has died (e.g., battery pulled or WiFi lost).

#### Solution B(b) — ThingSpeak REST + Dashboard (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Channel creation:** Create a free ThingSpeak account → *Channels* → *New Channel* → enable Field 1 (Temperature) and Field 2 (Humidity) → save. The system returns:
- `Channel ID = 123456`
- `Write API Key = ABCDEFGH7I` *(example)*

**Step 2 — Data push from ESP32 (HTTP GET):**
```
http://api.thingspeak.com/update?api_key=ABCDEFGH7I&field1=28.40&field2=62.10
```

The broker responds with a numeric **entry ID** (e.g., `7854`). A new entry is allowed only every **15 seconds** (ThingSpeak rate limit).

**Step 3 — Dashboard URL (public view):**
```
https://thingspeak.com/channels/123456
```

This renders two charts: Field 1 as a line graph, Field 2 as a second line graph, both time-aligned. Optional MATLAB Visualizations allow FFT or moving-average overlays.

**Step 4 — Read-back via GET (for verification):**
```
https://api.thingspeak.com/channels/123456/fields/1.json?results=2
```

Returns:
```json
{"channel":{"id":123456,"name":"Lab"},"feeds":[{"field1":"28.40"}, {"field1":"27.95"}]}
```

**Mark-split:** Channel creation (1), REST GET URL syntax (2), rate-limit note (1), public dashboard URL (1), read-back JSON (2).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the timestamp** — every cloud payload MUST include `ts` or `created_at`.
> 2. **Using `delay(60000)` instead of `millis()`** — this is an instant 2-mark deduction for "non-blocking design."
> 3. **Not handling `NaN` sensor returns** — DHT sensors are noisy; missing the `isnan()` check costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing publish and subscribe** — students often write code that subscribes to its own topic and loops.
> 5. **Skipping the rate-limit clause for ThingSpeak** (1 update / 15 s) — explicit syllabus item.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (memorize verbatim before exam):**

- **Definition:** A Temperature Logger = sensor + MCU + network + cloud + dashboard.
- **Default sampling period** in labs: **60 s** (1 min). Nyquist minimum: depends on the slowest expected thermal event.
- **LM35** is analog, **10 mV/°C**, range 0–150 °C, needs ADC.
- **DHT22** is digital, single-wire, range −40 to +80 °C, accuracy $\pm 0.5$ °C.
- **DS18B20** is digital, 1-Wire, waterproof variants available, $\pm 0.5$ °C, range −55 to +125 °C.
- **MQTT** runs on TCP port **1883**, QoS 0/1/2, uses topics like `home/room1/temp`.
- **HTTP** REST is simpler but heavier; ThingSpeak uses `GET /update?api_key=…&field1=…`.
- **ThingSpeak rate limit** = 1 update / 15 s.
- **JSON payload minimum fields:** `device_id`, `temp_c`, `hum`, `ts`.
- **ADC resolution formula:** $\Delta T = V_{ref} / (2^n \times S)$.
- **Celsius ↔ Fahrenheit:** $T_F = T_C \times 9/5 + 32$.
- **Celsius ↔ Kelvin:** $T_K = T_C + 273.15$.
- **NTP** is required for timestamping; use `pool.ntp.org` as the default server.
- **Non-blocking design** uses `millis()` not `delay()`.
- **LWT (Last Will and Testament)** is unique to MQTT — auto-publish on disconnect.
- **Topic naming convention:** lower-case, slash-separated, hierarchical.
- **Block diagram for KTU answer** must show all 5 layers: Sensor → MCU → Connectivity → Cloud → Dashboard → (Actuator).
- **Common viva question:** *"Why not use HTTP polling?"* Answer: wastes bandwidth, higher power, no QoS, no push semantics.
- **Industrial application:** cold-chain pharma (2–8 °C), server rooms (18–27 °C), greenhouse (ventilation at >30 °C).
- **Firmware headers to memorize:** `#include <WiFi.h>`, `<PubSubClient.h>`, `<DHT.h>`, `<HTTPClient.h>`, `<NTPClient.h>`, `<ArduinoJson.h>`.
- **Examiner sanity check:** if your diagram does not contain the words "Perception", "Network", "Application", you have lost 2 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project on Use of Soil Moisture Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT504 - PROGRAMMING FOR INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Project on Use of Soil Moisture Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Soil Moisture Sensor** is an electronic transducer module designed to measure the volumetric water content (VWC) present in soil. It plays a pivotal role in **Internet of Things (IoT)**-based precision agriculture systems by converting the physical property of soil hydration into a quantifiable electrical signal (analog voltage or digital pulse) that can be processed by microcontrollers such as **Arduino Uno, ESP8266 (NodeMCU), ESP32, or Raspberry Pi**.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBCIT504 – Programming for IoT)**, the soil moisture sensor is classified as a **Class-II Environmental Sensor Module** under the broader taxonomy of agricultural IoT sensors. It belongs to the family of **resistive/capacitive transducers** and operates on the principle of variation in electrical permittivity or resistance with respect to soil water content.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The course mandates the implementation of at least one **end-to-end IoT project** using sensors, actuators, and a communication protocol (Wi-Fi/MQTT/HTTP). The "Soil Moisture Sensor Project" is the **most frequently asked and board-recommended** project under this module because it encompasses all four layers of the IoT architecture: **Perception (Sensor), Network (Wi-Fi/MQTT), Processing (Cloud/Local Server), and Application (Mobile/Web Dashboard).**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Plant's Thirst Meter"**
> Imagine you are a farmer in Kerala during the summer. You walk into your cardamom plantation and want to know: *"Does this patch of soil need watering right now?"* Instead of digging up the soil and squeezing it (the traditional, manual method), you insert a small probe that acts like the plant's **"thirst nerve."** The probe instantly tells you — via an LED, an LCD, or even a WhatsApp alert on your phone — whether the soil is *bone-dry*, *perfectly moist*, or *waterlogged*. This probe, in engineering terms, is the **Soil Moisture Sensor**.

### Intuitive Breakdown
| Component in Analogy | Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Farmer's hand checking soil | Manual tensiometer (old method) |
| "Thirst nerve" probe | Soil Moisture Sensor (v1.2 / v2.0 capacitive) |
| LED/LCD showing "Dry/Wet" | Microcontroller output (Arduino/NodeMCU) |
| WhatsApp alert | IoT cloud notification (Blynk/ThingSpeak/MQTT) |
| The plant | The end-application domain (smart irrigation) |

## 1.3 Types of Soil Moisture Sensors

There are **three principal variants** encountered in KTU lab examinations and project work:

1. **Resistive Soil Moisture Sensor (Two-Probe Type)**
   - Measures soil resistance between two exposed electrodes.
   - **Higher water content → Lower resistance → Higher analog reading.**
   - *Drawback:* Electrodes corrode (electrolysis) over 2–6 months in moist soil.

2. **Capacitive Soil Moisture Sensor (Corrosion-Free Type v1.2/v2.0)**
   - Measures the **dielectric permittivity** of soil using a coplanar capacitor etched on the PCB.
   - The soil acts as the dielectric; water (εᵣ ≈ **80**) drastically shifts capacitance.
   - *Advantage:* No exposed metal contacts, hence **long operational life (3–5 years).**

3. **Volumetric Water Content (VWC) Sensor — Industrial Grade (e.g., TEROS 12, EC-5)**
   - Uses **Time Domain Reflectometry (TDR)** or **Frequency Domain Reflectometry (FDR)**.
   - Used in research-grade precision agriculture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Lab Tip:** For all academic projects, the **capacitive v1.2 sensor** is **strongly recommended** by examiners due to its longevity, ease of interfacing (single analog pin), and absence of corrosion-related drift — a frequent viva question.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Dielectric Constant of Dry Soil:** εᵣ ≈ **3 to 5**
- **Dielectric Constant of Pure Water:** εᵣ ≈ **80** (at 20 °C, 1 MHz)
- **Dielectric Constant of Wet Soil:** εᵣ ≈ **10 to 40** (depends on water content)
- **Standard Operating Voltage:** **3.3 V to 5 V DC**
- **Output Voltage Range (Analog):** **0 V to Vcc** (proportional to moisture)
- **ADC Resolution (Arduino Uno):** **10-bit** (values 0–1023)
- **ADC Resolution (ESP32):** **12-bit** (values 0–4095)
- **Typical Module Current Draw:** **5 mA to 35 mA**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Working Principle

The capacitive soil moisture sensor v1.2 operates on the principle of **dielectric sensing**. The onboard circuitry generates a high-frequency AC signal (typically ~550 kHz to 5 MHz, generated by a **NE555 timer IC**) that charges and discharges the coplanar capacitor formed by the etched copper traces on the sensor PCB.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Excitation Signal Generation:** The onboard **NE555 astable multivibrator** produces a square wave of fixed frequency.
2. **Capacitive Charge-Discharge Cycle:** This signal is applied to the sensing capacitor (the exposed copper pads). The soil surrounding these pads acts as the **dielectric medium**.
3. **Capacitance Variation:** As water content in soil changes, the effective dielectric constant (εᵣ) of the medium changes, which in turn **modulates the capacitance** C of the sensor.

$$ C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot A}{d} $$

where $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$ is the vacuum permittivity, $\varepsilon_r$ is the relative permittivity of the soil, $A$ is the effective plate area, and $d$ is the separation between conductive traces.

4. **Frequency-to-Voltage Conversion:** The change in capacitance alters the charge-discharge time constant, which the NE555 circuit translates into a **variable output voltage**.
5. **Rectification & Smoothing:** The AC signal is rectified and filtered, producing a **DC analog voltage** at the AOUT pin.
6. **Threshold Comparison:** A **comparator IC (LM393)** compares the voltage against a preset threshold (set via onboard potentiometer) to provide a clean **digital output (DOUT)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind the Physics:** Water has a dielectric constant of **80**, which is roughly **20 to 30 times higher** than that of dry soil. So even a small addition of moisture causes a disproportionately large shift in capacitance, making this method highly sensitive.

## 2.2 ADC Conversion and Digital Interpretation

The microcontroller (Arduino/NodeMCU) reads the analog voltage and converts it to a digital value using its **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**.

### Universal ADC-to-Voltage Formula

$$ V_{out} = \frac{ADC_{value}}{ADC_{resolution}} \times V_{ref} $$

where:
- $V_{out}$ = measured analog voltage at the sensor output
- $ADC_{value}$ = raw digital reading (0 to 1023 for Arduino, 0 to 4095 for ESP32)
- $ADC_{resolution} = 2^n - 1$, where $n$ is the number of bits
- $V_{ref}$ = reference voltage (typically 3.3 V or 5.0 V)

### Universal Voltage-to-Moisture Percentage Formula (after calibration)

$$ M_{\%} = \frac{V_{dry} - V_{measured}}{V_{dry} - V_{wet}} \times 100 $$

where:
- $M_{\%}$ = soil moisture content in percentage
- $V_{dry}$ = sensor output voltage when soil is **completely dry** (calibrated baseline)
- $V_{wet}$ = sensor output voltage when soil is **fully saturated** (calibrated maximum)
- $V_{measured}$ = current sensor reading

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Typical Value / Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Parallel Plate Capacitance | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | Farads (F) | Sensor physics |
| 2 | Time Constant (RC) | $\tau = R \times C$ | Seconds (s) | NE555 timing |
| 3 | ADC to Voltage | $V_{out} = \dfrac{ADC}{2^n - 1} \times V_{ref}$ | Volts (V) | Microcontroller read |
| 4 | Voltage to Moisture % | $M_{\%} = \dfrac{V_{dry} - V_m}{V_{dry} - V_{wet}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) | Calibration |
| 5 | Current Divider (Resistive Type) | $V_{out} = V_{cc} \times \dfrac{R_{soil}}{R_{soil} + R_{ref}}$ | Volts (V) | Resistive sensor model |
| 6 | Threshold Trigger (Comparator) | $V_{out} = V_{cc}$ if $V_{in+} > V_{in-}$ | Boolean | LM393 logic |
| 7 | NE555 Frequency | $f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2) \times C}$ | Hertz (Hz) | Excitation source |
| 8 | Sensor Sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta M_{\%}}$ | V/% | Performance metric |
| 9 | Power Consumption | $P = V_{cc} \times I_{avg}$ | mW | Battery life estimation |
| 10 | Cloud Update Interval | $T_{tx} = \dfrac{1}{f_{sampling}}$ | Seconds (s) | MQTT publish rate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This sensing principle is not limited to agriculture. It is used in **concrete curing monitoring (civil engineering), pharmaceutical powder moisture analysis, food industry (grain storage humidity), and environmental monitoring of landfills** (leachate detection). The same dielectric principle underpins **capacitive touchscreens** in smartphones.

## 2.4 System Architecture of the IoT Project

The complete project comprises **four functional layers** mapped to the standard **IoT Reference Model (ISO/IEC 30141)**:

1. **Perception Layer:** Soil Moisture Sensor + DHT11 (temperature/humidity) + ESP8266
2. **Network Layer:** Wi-Fi (802.11 b/g/n) + MQTT/HTTPS protocol
3. **Processing Layer:** Cloud platform (ThingSpeak / Blynk / AWS IoT / Firebase)
4. **Application Layer:** Mobile dashboard + Automated irrigation pump relay control
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Raw ADC Value to Volumetric Water Content

### Given
- Arduino Uno with **10-bit ADC** (n = 10, range 0–1023)
- Reference voltage $V_{ref}$ = **5.0 V** (default)
- Sensor reading at dry soil: $ADC_{dry}$ = **650**
- Sensor reading at wet soil: $ADC_{wet}$ = **280**
- Current sensor reading: $ADC_{m}$ = **450**

### Step 1: Convert all ADC values to equivalent voltages using the universal formula.

$$ V_{out} = \frac{ADC}{2^n - 1} \times V_{ref} $$

Substituting for dry soil:

$$ V_{dry} = \frac{650}{2^{10} - 1} \times 5.0 = \frac{650}{1023} \times 5.0 $$

$$ V_{dry} = 0.6354 \times 5.0 = 3.177 \text{ V} $$

### Step 2: Substituting for wet soil.

$$ V_{wet} = \frac{280}{1023} \times 5.0 = 0.2737 \times 5.0 = 1.369 \text{ V} $$

### Step 3: Substituting for the current measured value.

$$ V_m = \frac{450}{1023} \times 5.0 = 0.4399 \times 5.0 = 2.199 \text{ V} $$

### Step 4: Apply the moisture percentage formula.

$$ M_{\%} = \frac{V_{dry} - V_m}{V_{dry} - V_{wet}} \times 100 $$

$$ M_{\%} = \frac{3.177 - 2.199}{3.177 - 1.369} \times 100 $$

$$ M_{\%} = \frac{0.978}{1.808} \times 100 $$

$$ M_{\%} = 54.09\% $$

### Conclusion
The soil currently contains approximately **54.09% volumetric moisture content**, which is in the **optimal range** for most crops (ideal: 50%–70%).

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Inversion Logic:** In capacitive sensors, **higher ADC values correspond to drier soil** (higher voltage output) and **lower ADC values correspond to wetter soil**. This is because increased water content raises the capacitance, which **lowers** the output voltage of the NE555-based rectifier. This counterintuitive behavior is a **favourite viva question** — examiners expect students to explain it.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Threshold Setting for Automated Irrigation Pump

### Given
- Crops require moisture above **45%** for healthy growth.
- $V_{dry}$ = 3.177 V, $V_{wet}$ = 1.369 V.
- We need to find the **voltage threshold $V_{th}$** below which the pump should turn ON.

### Step 1: Set up the inequality.

The pump turns ON when $M_{\%} \leq 45\%$, i.e.,

$$ \frac{V_{dry} - V_{th}}{V_{dry} - V_{wet}} \times 100 \leq 45 $$

### Step 2: Divide both sides by 100.

$$ \frac{V_{dry} - V_{th}}{V_{dry} - V_{wet}} \leq 0.45 $$

### Step 3: Cross-multiply (note the denominator is positive since $V_{dry} > V_{wet}$).

$$ V_{dry} - V_{th} \leq 0.45 \times (V_{dry} - V_{wet}) $$

### Step 4: Substitute numerical values.

$$ 3.177 - V_{th} \leq 0.45 \times (3.177 - 1.369) $$

$$ 3.177 - V_{th} \leq 0.45 \times 1.808 $$

$$ 3.177 - V_{th} \leq 0.8136 $$

### Step 5: Rearrange to solve for $V_{th}$.

$$ V_{th} \geq 3.177 - 0.8136 $$

$$ V_{th} \geq 2.3634 \text{ V} $$

### Step 6: Convert the voltage threshold back to ADC value for code implementation.

$$ ADC_{th} = \frac{V_{th}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^n - 1) $$

$$ ADC_{th} = \frac{2.3634}{5.0} \times 1023 $$

$$ ADC_{th} = 0.4727 \times 1023 = 483.55 \approx 484 $$

### Conclusion
In the Arduino code, the condition should be:

```cpp
if (analogRead(SENSOR_PIN) > 484) {
    digitalWrite(PUMP_PIN, HIGH);  // Pump ON
} else {
    digitalWrite(PUMP_PIN, LOW);   // Pump OFF
}
```

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Power Budget for Solar-Powered Deployment

### Given
- Sensor current: $I_{sensor}$ = 25 mA
- NodeMCU (ESP8266) Wi-Fi active current: $I_{wifi}$ = 170 mA
- NodeMCU deep-sleep current: $I_{sleep}$ = 10 µA = 0.01 mA
- Transmission duty cycle: 1 transmission every 5 minutes (300 s)
- Single transmission (active) duration: $t_{on}$ = 3 s
- Battery: 3.7 V, 2500 mAh Li-Ion

### Step 1: Average current consumption (per 300 s window).

$$ I_{avg} = \frac{(I_{sensor} + I_{wifi}) \times t_{on} + I_{sleep} \times (T - t_{on})}{T} $$

$$ I_{avg} = \frac{(25 + 170) \times 3 + 0.01 \times (300 - 3)}{300} $$

$$ I_{avg} = \frac{195 \times 3 + 0.01 \times 297}{300} $$

$$ I_{avg} = \frac{585 + 2.97}{300} $$

$$ I_{avg} = \frac{587.97}{300} = 1.9599 \text{ mA} $$

### Step 2: Battery life in hours.

$$ T_{life} = \frac{C_{battery}}{I_{avg}} = \frac{2500 \text{ mAh}}{1.9599 \text{ mA}} = 1275.5 \text{ hours} $$

### Step 3: Convert to days.

$$ T_{life} = \frac{1275.5}{24} = 53.15 \text{ days} $$

### Conclusion
A single 2500 mAh battery will power the system for approximately **53 days** before requiring recharge, making solar trickle-charging with a 6V/2W panel an ideal pairing.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of the Complete IoT System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Soil Moisture Sensor v1.2 Capacitive] --> B[NodeMCU ESP8266 ADC Pin A0]
    C[DHT11 Temperature Humidity Sensor] --> B
    B --> D{Threshold Logic Firmware}
    D -->|Moisture less than 45 percent| E[Relay Module 5V SPDT]
    E --> F[Submersible Water Pump 12V]
    D -->|Always| G[WiFi Module 802.11 bgn]
    G --> H[MQTT Broker HiveMQ Cloud]
    H --> I[ThingSpeak Dashboard]
    H --> J[Blynk Mobile App]
    I --> K[Farmer Smartphone Browser]
    J --> K
```

```mermaid

    start([Power ON System Boot]) --> init[Initialize Serial 115200 baud]
    init --> wifi[Connect to WiFi SSID and Password]
    wifi -->|Failure| wifiR[Retry 5 times with exponential backoff]

    dhtcfg --> loop{Loop Start every 5 seconds}
```

## 4.4 Reference Arduino/NodeMCU Sketch

```cpp
// =============================================
// Smart Irrigation using Soil Moisture Sensor
// Compatible with NodeMCU ESP8266 + Capacitive v1.2 sensor
// =============================================
#include <ESP8266WiFi.h>
#include <PubSubClient.h>
#include "DHT.h"

#define MOISTURE_PIN    A0
#define RELAY_PIN       D1
#define DHT_PIN         D2
#define DHTTYPE         DHT11
#define THRESHOLD_PCT   45   // Pump ON if moisture < 45%

// Calibration values (replace with your own)
const int DRY_VALUE = 650;  // ADC reading in dry air
const int WET_VALUE = 280;  // ADC reading in distilled water

const char* ssid      = "YOUR_WIFI_SSID";
const char* password  = "YOUR_WIFI_PASS";
const char* mqttServer = "broker.hivemq.com";
const int   mqttPort   = 1883;
const char* mqttTopic  = "farm/soil/moisture";

WiFiClient espClient;
PubSubClient client(espClient);
DHT dht(DHT_PIN, DHTTYPE);

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  pinMode(RELAY_PIN, OUTPUT);
  digitalWrite(RELAY_PIN, LOW);  // Pump initially OFF
  dht.begin();

  WiFi.begin(ssid, password);
  while (WiFi.status() != WL_CONNECTED) {
    delay(500);
    Serial.print(".");
  }
  Serial.println("\nWiFi Connected");

  client.setServer(mqttServer, mqttPort);
}

float getMoisturePercent(int adcValue) {
  int pct = map(adcValue, DRY_VALUE, WET_VALUE, 0, 100);
  return constrain(pct, 0, 100);
}

void loop() {
  if (!client.connected()) {
    while (!client.connected()) {
      if (client.connect("SoilNodeMCU")) break;
      delay(2000);
    }
  }
  client.loop();

  int rawADC   = analogRead(MOISTURE_PIN);
  float pct    = getMoisturePercent(rawADC);
  float temp   = dht.readTemperature();
  float humid  = dht.readHumidity();

  Serial.print("Moisture: "); Serial.print(pct); Serial.println(" %");
  Serial.print("Temp: ");     Serial.print(temp);  Serial.println(" C");

  // Irrigation control logic
  if (pct < THRESHOLD_PCT) {
    digitalWrite(RELAY_PIN, HIGH);
    Serial.println("Pump: ON");
  } else {
    digitalWrite(RELAY_PIN, LOW);
    Serial.println("Pump: OFF");
  }

  // Publish to MQTT
  char payload[120];
  snprintf(payload, sizeof(payload),
           "{\"moisture\":%.1f,\"temp\":%.1f,\"humidity\":%.1f}",
           pct, temp, humid);
  client.publish(mqttTopic, payload);

  ESP.deepSleep(295e6);  // Sleep 295 seconds (5 min - active time)
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Adaptation Note for Arduino Uno:** Replace `analogRead` resolution, Wi-Fi, MQTT, and `ESP.deepSleep` with serial LCD display and remove cloud functions. The threshold logic remains identical.
