# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]**

### Question 1: Differentiate between resistive and capacitive soil moisture sensors. Which one is preferred for long-term IoT deployments and why?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Resistive Sensor | Capacitive Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Measures resistance between two metal probes | Measures dielectric permittivity of soil |
| Exposed metal? | Yes (direct soil contact) | No (encapsulated PCB traces) |
| Corrosion rate | High (3–6 months) | Negligible (3–5 years) |
| Sensitivity | Moderate | High (due to water's εᵣ ≈ 80) |
| Cost | Low (₹80–₹150) | Moderate (₹200–₹400) |
| Output | Analog voltage | Analog + Digital (via LM393) |

**Preferred choice:** The **capacitive soil moisture sensor v1.2** is preferred for long-term IoT deployments because it **does not suffer from electrochemical corrosion**, has a longer operational lifespan, and provides more stable readings over time. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]**

### Question 2: List any four applications of soil moisture sensors in IoT-based smart agriculture.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Automated Drip Irrigation Control** — Triggers water pumps only when soil moisture falls below a threshold, conserving water by up to 60%.
2. **Greenhouse Climate Management** — Integrated with temperature/humidity sensors to maintain optimal microclimate for hydroponic crops.
3. **Crop Yield Prediction** — Long-term moisture data fed into ML models (e.g., LSTM) for yield forecasting in KTU final-year projects.
4. **Landslide Early Warning** — Soil moisture combined with rain gauge data to predict slope instability in hilly regions of Wayanad/Idukki.
5. **Sports Turf & Golf Course Maintenance** — Ensures uniform grass health in large recreational areas.

*(Any four correctly explained applications carry 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]**

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — *Project Design & Implementation*

**(a)** Design an IoT-based smart irrigation system using a soil moisture sensor and NodeMCU ESP8266. Draw the complete block diagram, list the hardware components, and explain the working principle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the complete Arduino/NodeMCU sketch to read the soil moisture sensor, compare it with a threshold of 45%, and automatically control a relay-connected water pump. Include Wi-Fi connectivity and MQTT publishing. **(7 marks)**

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Theoretical & Analytical Depth*

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a capacitive soil moisture sensor v1.2. Derive the relationship between ADC value and volumetric water content (VWC) percentage, given $V_{dry}$ = 3.18 V, $V_{wet}$ = 1.37 V, and a current ADC reading of 450 on a 5V, 10-bit system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A farmer requires the pump to turn ON when moisture falls below 40%. Calculate the voltage threshold $V_{th}$ and the corresponding ADC value. Discuss the role of MQTT protocol in this system and explain the Publish-Subscribe model with a suitable diagram. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Model Solutions for Question A

### Solution to A(a) — System Design (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

The block diagram should show:

```
[Soil Moisture Sensor] → [NodeMCU ESP8266 (ADC + WiFi)] 
       → [MQTT Broker] → [ThingSpeak/Blynk Cloud]
       → [Relay Module] → [Submersible Pump]
```

**Hardware Components List (2 marks):**
- Capacitive soil moisture sensor v1.2 (1 unit)
- NodeMCU ESP8266 development board (1 unit)
- 5V single-channel relay module (1 unit)
- 12V DC submersible water pump (1 unit)
- 12V 2A SMPS adapter (1 unit)
- LM7805 voltage regulator IC (1 unit)
- DHT11 temperature/humidity sensor (optional, 1 unit)
- Connecting wires, breadboard, project enclosure

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
The capacitive sensor detects changes in soil dielectric permittivity due to varying water content. The onboard NE555 IC converts this to an analog voltage, which the NodeMCU reads via its ADC. Based on the calibrated threshold, the firmware triggers a relay to switch ON/OFF the pump. Simultaneously, data is published to an MQTT broker and visualized on a cloud dashboard accessible via smartphone.

---

### Solution to A(b) — Code (7 marks)

```cpp
#include <ESP8266WiFi.h>
#include <PubSubClient.h>

#define MOISTURE_PIN  A0
#define RELAY_PIN     D1
#define THRESHOLD     45   // Percent
#define DRY_VAL       650
#define WET_VAL       280

const char* ssid = "HomeWiFi";
const char* pass = "password123";
const char* mqttBroker = "broker.hivemq.com";
const char* topic = "farm/soil/data";

WiFiClient espClient;
PubSubClient client(espClient);

float getMoisture(int adc) {
  int pct = map(adc, DRY_VAL, WET_VAL, 0, 100);
  return constrain(pct, 0, 100);
}

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  pinMode(RELAY_PIN, OUTPUT);
  digitalWrite(RELAY_PIN, LOW);

  WiFi.begin(ssid, pass);
  while (WiFi.status() != WL_CONNECTED) { delay(500); }

  client.setServer(mqttBroker, 1883);
}

void loop() {
  if (!client.connected()) client.connect("NodeMCU_01");
  client.loop();

  int adc = analogRead(MOISTURE_PIN);
  float moisture = getMoisture(adc);

  if (moisture < THRESHOLD) {
    digitalWrite(RELAY_PIN, HIGH);
  } else {
    digitalWrite(RELAY_PIN, LOW);
  }

  char msg[60];
  snprintf(msg, sizeof(msg), "{\"moisture\":%.1f}", moisture);
  client.publish(topic, msg);

  delay(5000);
}
```

**Key Evaluation Points (7 marks breakdown):**
- Correct header inclusion & pin definitions: 1 mark
- Calibration logic with `map()` function: 2 marks
- Threshold-based pump control: 1 mark
- Wi-Fi connection logic: 1 mark
- MQTT publish with JSON payload: 1 mark
- Code indentation and comments: 1 mark

---

## 5.4 Model Solutions for Question B

### Solution to B(a) — Sensor Working & VWC Derivation (7 marks)

**Working (3 marks):**
A capacitive soil moisture sensor v1.2 contains a 555 timer IC that generates a high-frequency AC signal applied to a coplanar capacitor etched on the PCB. When the sensor is inserted into soil, the soil acts as the dielectric. The dielectric constant of water (≈80) is much greater than that of dry soil (3–5), so any change in moisture drastically alters the capacitance. The 555 circuit converts this capacitance change into a proportional DC voltage at the AOUT pin. An LM393 comparator provides a digital threshold output.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

**Step 1:** Convert ADC 450 to voltage.

$$ V_m = \frac{450}{1023} \times 5.0 = 2.199 \text{ V} $$

**Step 2:** Apply the moisture formula.

$$ M_{\%} = \frac{3.18 - 2.199}{3.18 - 1.37} \times 100 = \frac{0.981}{1.81} \times 100 = 54.2\% $$

**Conclusion:** The soil currently has approximately **54.2% moisture content**, indicating **adequate hydration** — no irrigation needed at this moment.

---

### Solution to B(b) — Threshold Calculation & MQTT (7 marks)

**Threshold Calculation (3 marks):**

**Step 1:** Set up inequality for 40% threshold.

$$ \frac{V_{dry} - V_{th}}{V_{dry} - V_{wet}} \times 100 = 40 $$

**Step 2:** Solve for $V_{th}$.

$$ V_{dry} - V_{th} = 0.40 \times (V_{dry} - V_{wet}) $$

$$ V_{th} = V_{dry} - 0.40 \times (V_{dry} - V_{wet}) $$

$$ V_{th} = 3.18 - 0.40 \times (3.18 - 1.37) $$

$$ V_{th} = 3.18 - 0.40 \times 1.81 $$

$$ V_{th} = 3.18 - 0.724 = 2.456 \text{ V} $$

**Step 3:** Convert to ADC value.

$$ ADC_{th} = \frac{2.456}{5.0} \times 1023 = 502.5 \approx 503 $$

**MQTT Explanation (4 marks):**
**MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** is a lightweight, publish-subscribe messaging protocol designed for low-bandwidth, high-latency, or unreliable networks — ideal for IoT.

**Publish-Subscribe Model:**
- The NodeMCU (publisher) sends data with a **topic label** (e.g., `farm/soil/moisture`) to a central **MQTT broker** (e.g., HiveMQ, Mosquitto).
- The cloud dashboard and mobile app (subscribers) register interest in that topic.
- The broker forwards messages to all interested subscribers.
- The publisher and subscribers **never communicate directly**, enabling decoupled, scalable architectures.

**Topic Hierarchy Example:**
```
farm/zone1/soil/moisture
farm/zone1/air/temperature
farm/zone2/soil/moisture
```

This hierarchical naming allows fine-grained subscription filtering using wildcards `+` and `#`.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Inverted Logic Trap (–2 marks):** Students often write the moisture formula as $(V_m - V_{dry})/(V_{wet} - V_{dry})$ which is the **standard formula for sensors where higher voltage = higher moisture**. But for capacitive v1.2 sensors, the relationship is **inverted**. Always use $(V_{dry} - V_m)/(V_{dry} - V_{wet})$.
>
> 2. **ADC Resolution Confusion (–1 mark):** Using `1024` instead of `1023` in the denominator. Remember, a 10-bit ADC has values from **0 to 1023**, which is $2^{10} - 1 = 1023$ distinct levels.
>
> 3. **Corrosion Ignorance in Viva (–1 mark):** Failing to mention that **resistive sensors corrode** and are unsuitable for long-term deployment. This is a **favourite viva question** worth 1 mark.
>
> 4. **Missing MQTT Explanation (–2 marks):** In Part B, examiners expect at least 2–3 lines on **why MQTT over HTTP** (lower bandwidth, lower power, push-based). Skipping this costs dearly.
>
> 5. **Pin Number Mismatch (–1 mark):** Writing `A1` for NodeMCU — it has **only one analog pin (A0)**. Many students confuse it with Arduino Uno.
>
> 6. **No Calibration Mention (–2 marks):** Jumping straight to deployment without explaining the **two-point calibration procedure** (dry + wet) is a major deduction point. Always show the calibration step.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Soil Moisture Sensor Project**

### 🔬 Sensor Fundamentals
- ☐ Capacitive v1.2 sensors are **preferred over resistive** for IoT due to corrosion resistance.
- ☐ **Water's dielectric constant ≈ 80**; dry soil ≈ 3–5. This large contrast enables sensitive detection.
- ☐ The **NE555 timer IC** generates the excitation frequency, and **LM393** provides digital threshold output.
- ☐ In capacitive sensors, **higher ADC value = drier soil** (inverted relationship).

### 🔢 Key Formulas to Memorize
- ☐ $V_{out} = \dfrac{ADC}{2^n - 1} \times V_{ref}$
- ☐ $M_{\%} = \dfrac{V_{dry} - V_m}{V_{dry} - V_{wet}} \times 100$
- ☐ Arduino ADC range = **0–1023**; ESP32 ADC range = **0–4095**.

### 🔌 Hardware Essentials
- ☐ NodeMCU has **only one analog pin (A0)** — cannot read multiple analog sensors without an external **MUX (CD4051)**.
- ☐ Relay module requires a **separate 5V supply** for the coil; do NOT power it from the NodeMCU 3.3V pin.
- ☐ Use a **flyback diode (1N4007)** across the pump terminals to protect against back-EMF.

### 📡 Communication Protocol
- ☐ **MQTT** is preferred over HTTP for IoT due to **lower overhead, push-based delivery, and bandwidth efficiency**.
- ☐ Three MQTT QoS levels: **0 (at most once), 1 (at least once), 2 (exactly once)**.
- ☐ Common brokers: **HiveMQ, Mosquitto, AWS IoT Core, Google Cloud IoT Core**.

### 🧪 Calibration Steps
- ☐ **Step 1:** Read sensor in **open air** → record $ADC_{dry}$.
- ☐ **Step 2:** Dip sensor in **distilled water** up to marked line → record $ADC_{wet}$.
- ☐ **Step 3:** Apply map() function in code using these two values.

### 🎯 KTU Project Presentation Tips
- ☐ Always include a **calibration graph** (ADC vs. actual moisture %) in your report.
- ☐ Mention **power budget analysis** for battery/solar deployment.
- ☐ Discuss **security** — use **TLS/SSL** (mqtts://) and **username/password authentication** for MQTT.
- ☐ Include a **future scope** section mentioning **LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, or AI-based predictive irrigation**.
- ☐ Common viva questions: *"Why not use a resistive sensor?"*, *"Why MQTT over HTTP?"*, *"How do you handle sensor drift over time?"*

### 🏆 Bonus Knowledge (Beyond Syllabus but Viva Gold)
- ☐ **TDR (Time Domain Reflectometry)** is the gold standard for VWC measurement in research.
- ☐ **FDR (Frequency Domain Reflectometry)** sensors are used in commercial precision agriculture.
- ☐ **Soil moisture is classified** into three zones: **Saturation (≈100%), Field Capacity (≈30%), Permanent Wilting Point (≈15%)**.
- ☐ The **global smart agriculture market** is projected to reach **$34 billion by 2027**, with soil moisture sensing being a key driver.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project on Controlling the Color of Light
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT504 - PROGRAMMING FOR INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Project on Controlling the Color of Light

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **IoT-Based RGB Color Control Project:** A capstone embedded-IoT system that uses a Wi-Fi-enabled microcontroller (NodeMCU ESP8266 / ESP32) to drive a tri-color **RGB LED** through three independent **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** channels, allowing a remote user to select and emit any color in the visible additive spectrum through a mobile application (Blynk), web dashboard, or MQTT broker.

### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus-Aligned)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **RGB LED** | A single package containing three LEDs (Red, Green, Blue) whose combined light produces any visible color by additive mixing. |
| **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** | A digital signalling technique that fakes an analog voltage by rapidly toggling a pin; the **duty cycle** determines average brightness. |
| **Duty Cycle ($D$)** | The fraction of one period for which the signal is HIGH. Expressed as a percentage: $D = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON}+T_{OFF}} \times 100\%$. |
| **Color Hex Code** | A 24-bit representation of color: `#RRGGBB` where each pair is a hexadecimal intensity from `00` to `FF` (0–255 decimal). |
| **NodeMCU / ESP8266** | A low-cost Wi-Fi SoC with a Tensilica L106 32-bit core, 4 MB flash, and 11 GPIO pins (1 used for TX, leaving 10 usable). |
| **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** | A lightweight publish/subscribe IoT messaging protocol over TCP port 1883. |
| **Blynk** | A drag-and-drop IoT platform that links mobile widgets (sliders, color picker) to hardware pins via the Blynk Cloud. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Painter's Palette"

Imagine an artist who has three magic paint tubes — **Red, Green, and Blue**. By squeezing each tube with different pressure, the artist can mix any color of the rainbow on a canvas. The **microcontroller** is the artist, the **PWM duty cycle** is the pressure on the tube, the **RGB LED** is the canvas, and the **smartphone app** is the remote control telling the artist how hard to squeeze each tube. The Internet is the invisible thread connecting your finger on the phone to the artist's hands.

### 1.4 Why This Project Matters in KTU 2024

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This project is the **integrative assessment** for Module 4 of PBCIT504. It demonstrates mastery across:
> - GPIO programming (Module 1–2)
> - Wi-Fi & TCP/IP stack (Module 3)
> - Cloud protocols — MQTT / HTTP / Blynk (Module 4)
> - PWM and analog signal emulation (Module 2)
> - Real-time sensor-actuator feedback (Module 4)
>
> Together they satisfy **CO4**: *"Design and develop IoT applications using appropriate protocols and cloud platforms."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The RGB Additive Color Model

Human eyes contain three types of cone cells sensitive roughly to **red ($\lambda \approx 700$ nm)**, **green ($\lambda \approx 546$ nm)**, and **blue ($\lambda \approx 436$ nm)** wavelengths. By stimulating these three receptors at different intensities, the brain perceives millions of distinct colors — this is the **CIE 1931 RGB** color space foundation.

For an RGB LED with 8-bit resolution per channel, the total reproducible color space is:

$$N_{colors} = 2^{bits/ch} \times 2^{bits/ch} \times 2^{bits/ch}$$

Substituting the standard 8 bits per channel:

$$N_{colors} = 2^{8} \times 2^{8} \times 2^{8} = 256 \times 256 \times 256 = 16{,}777{,}216$$

This is the famous **"16.7 million colors"** specification of a TrueColor display.

### 2.2 PWM-to-Intensity Translation

The microcontroller cannot output a true analog voltage. Instead, it switches the GPIO pin ON and OFF so fast that the LED — being a slow thermal/chemical emitter — integrates the energy and appears continuously dimmer or brighter. The mathematical relationship between the digital PWM register value and the perceived intensity is **linear** for LEDs:

$$I_{perceived} = I_{max} \times \frac{V_{PWM}}{V_{FS}}$$

where $V_{PWM}$ is the average voltage and $V_{FS}$ is the full-scale (logic HIGH) voltage, typically **3.3 V** for ESP8266/ESP32.

The **duty cycle** in percent is:

$$D (\%) = \frac{V_{PWM}}{V_{FS}} \times 100\% = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_{period}} \times 100\%$$

For the ESP8266, the PWM register uses a 10-bit value (0–1023). The conversion from an 8-bit color channel ($c \in [0,255]$) is:

$$PWM_{register} = c \times \frac{1023}{255} = 4c + \frac{3c}{255} \approx 4.012c$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Board-Specific PWM Ranges** — *remember this for viva:*
> - **Arduino Uno / Nano:** 8-bit, range `0–255`, default frequency $\approx 490$ Hz (pin 5 & 6: 980 Hz).
> - **NodeMCU / ESP8266:** 10-bit, range `0–1023`, default `analogWriteRange(1023)`, frequency $100$ Hz – $1$ kHz.
> - **ESP32:** 8-bit, range `0–255`, **LEDC peripheral** with configurable channels (0–15), frequency up to 40 MHz.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_{period}} \times 100\%$ | Duty cycle as percentage | Converting PWM register to % |
| 2 | $PWM_{10bit} = 4.012 \times c_{8bit}$ | 8-bit color → 10-bit PWM | ESP8266 `analogWrite()` |
| 3 | $N_{colors} = (2^{b})^{3}$ | Total color combinations | Spec calculation |
| 4 | $R_{LED} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{F}}{I_{F}}$ | Current-limiting resistor | Hardware design |
| 5 | $P_{LED} = V_{F} \times I_{F}$ | Power per LED die | Power budget |
| 6 | $T = \frac{1}{f_{PWM}}$ | PWM period from frequency | Frequency selection |
| 7 | $HSV \to RGB$: $C = V \cdot S$ | Chroma in HSV conversion | Color picker app |
| 8 | $m = V - C$ | HSV match value | HSV → RGB final step |

### 2.4 Hardware Design — Current-Limiting Resistor Derivation

An RGB LED has three internal dies. Each die has a forward voltage $V_F$ and a maximum continuous forward current $I_F$. To prevent burnout, a series resistor is mandatory:

$$R_{series} = \frac{V_{supply} - V_{F}}{I_{F}}$$

For a typical 5 mm common-cathode RGB LED driven from a 3.3 V NodeMCU pin:
- $V_F$ (Red) $\approx 2.0$ V, $V_F$ (Green/Blue) $\approx 3.0$ V
- $I_F$ (typical) $= 20$ mA $= 0.020$ A

Substituting for the red channel:

$$R_{red} = \frac{3.3 - 2.0}{0.020} = \frac{1.3}{0.020} = 65\ \Omega$$

The nearest standard E12 value is **68 Ω**. For green/blue (higher $V_F$):

$$R_{green} = \frac{3.3 - 3.0}{0.020} = 15\ \Omega$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In practice, designers use a **single 220 Ω resistor** on the common cathode/anode line, or three **330 Ω** resistors (one per channel) when running from 5 V. This safely limits current to $\approx 9$ mA per channel — enough to perceive bright colors without burning the LED.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

This exact architecture is used in:
- **Philips Hue** smart bulbs (ZigBee + PWM driver IC)
- **LIFX** Wi-Fi LED bulbs (ESP32-class SoC + constant-current driver)
- **Automotive ambient lighting** (CAN-bus + PWM)
- **Stage lighting DMX controllers** (RS-485 + PWM)
- **Agricultural grow lights** (tunable spectrum via PWM)

Mastering this small project scales directly to **commercial smart-lighting firmware**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hex Color Code to PWM Register Values

A user inputs the color **"Teal"** with the hex code `#008080` in the Blynk app. The Arduino sketch must convert this 24-bit value into three PWM register writes.

**Step 1: Parse the hex string into three 2-character substrings.**

$$C_{hex} = \texttt{"008080"} \implies R_{hex} = \texttt{"00"},\ G_{hex} = \texttt{"80"},\ B_{hex} = \texttt{"80"}$$

**Step 2: Convert each pair from base-16 to base-10 using positional weights.**

For the green channel "80":

$$G_{dec} = (8 \times 16^{1}) + (0 \times 16^{0}) = 8 \times 16 + 0 \times 1 = 128$$

**Step 3: Map the 8-bit value (0–255) to the ESP8266's 10-bit PWM range (0–1023) using the standard scale factor.**

$$PWM_{G} = G_{dec} \times \frac{1023}{255}$$

$$PWM_{G} = 128 \times 4.01176 = 513.5 \approx 514$$

**Step 4: Repeat for red and blue.**

$$PWM_{R} = 0 \times 4.01176 = 0$$

$$PWM_{B} = 128 \times 4.01176 \approx 514$$

**Step 5: Write to GPIOs.**

```cpp
analogWrite(redPin, 0);      // Red OFF
analogWrite(greenPin, 514);  // Green half-brightness
analogWrite(bluePin, 514);   // Blue half-brightness
```

The LED emits **teal**. ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hue, Saturation, Value (HSV) to RGB Conversion

Mobile color pickers (e.g., the Blynk `zeRGBa` widget) often send color as **Hue, Saturation, Value** instead of RGB. The mathematical conversion is required on the microcontroller.

**Step 1: Normalize all inputs to $[0,1]$.**

$$H' = \frac{H}{60}, \quad S' = \frac{S}{100}, \quad V' = \frac{V}{100}$$

**Step 2: Compute the chroma (colorfulness).**

$$C = V' \cdot S'$$

**Step 3: Compute the intermediate value $X$ that depends on hue sector.**

$$X = C \cdot \left(1 - \left\vert \left(H' \bmod 2\right) - 1 \right\vert \right)$$

**Step 4: Determine the $(R_1, G_1, B_1)$ tuple by hue sector.**

$$\begin{aligned}
(R_1, G_1, B_1) &=
\begin{cases}
(C, X, 0) & \text{if } 0 \le H' < 1 \\
(X, C, 0) & \text{if } 1 \le H' < 2 \\
(0, C, X) & \text{if } 2 \le H' < 3 \\
(0, X, C) & \text{if } 3 \le H' < 4 \\
(X, 0, C) & \text{if } 4 \le H' < 5 \\
(C, 0, X) & \text{if } 5 \le H' < 6
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Add the match value $m = V' - C$ to each component.**

$$R = (R_1 + m) \times 255, \quad G = (G_1 + m) \times 255, \quad B = (B_1 + m) \times 255$$

**Worked Example:** $H = 180°$ (cyan), $S = 100\%$, $V = 100\%$.

$$H' = 3.0, \quad S' = 1.0, \quad V' = 1.0$$

$$C = 1.0 \times 1.0 = 1.0$$

$$X = 1.0 \times (1 - \vert (3.0 \bmod 2) - 1 \vert) = 1.0 \times (1 - \vert 1.0 - 1 \vert) = 1.0$$

Sector $2 \le H' < 3$ gives $(R_1, G_1, B_1) = (0, C, X) = (0, 1.0, 1.0)$.

$$m = V' - C = 1.0 - 1.0 = 0$$

$$R = 0 \times 255 = 0, \quad G = 1.0 \times 255 = 255, \quad B = 1.0 \times 255 = 255$$

Result: **Cyan `#00FFFF`** ✓

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Duty Cycle to Perceived Brightness (Logarithmic Eye Response)

Human brightness perception is **logarithmic** (Weber-Fechner law), not linear. A PWM duty cycle of 50% does not appear "half as bright" — it appears roughly 73% as bright.

The perceived brightness $L$ relates to actual luminous intensity $I$ by:

$$L = k \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)$$

For LED control, we use **gamma correction** with $\gamma \approx 2.2$:

$$PWM_{corrected} = \left(\frac{PWM_{input}}{PWM_{max}}\right)^{\gamma} \times PWM_{max}$$

**Example:** Set brightness to "50%" (i.e., 512 of 1023 on ESP8266):

$$PWM_{corrected} = \left(\frac{512}{1023}\right)^{2.2} \times 1023$$

$$= (0.5005)^{2.2} \times 1023 = 0.2295 \times 1023 \approx 235$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why high-end libraries like **FastLED** and **Adafruit NeoPixel** offer `setBrightness()` and gamma-correction tables. Skipping gamma makes colors look "washed out" at low PWM values.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Component Selection Table

| Subsystem | Component | Specification | Qty | Unit Cost (₹ approx) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Controller | NodeMCU V3 (ESP8266) | 80 MHz, 4 MB flash, Wi-Fi b/g/n | 1 | 250 |
| Actuator | RGB LED (common cathode) | $V_F$ 2.0/3.0/3.0 V, $I_F$ 20 mA | 1 | 15 |
| Passives | Resistor 330 Ω, ¼ W | Carbon film, ±5% | 3 | 1 |
| Passives | Resistor 10 kΩ (optional) | Pull-down for boot button | 1 | 1 |
| Power | USB cable / 5 V adapter | 1 A minimum | 1 | 50 |
| Connectivity | Wi-Fi router, 2.4 GHz | 802.11 b/g/n | 1 | — |
| Software | Arduino IDE + ESP8266 Board Package | v2.7+ | — | Free |
| Software | Blynk App / MQTT Broker (HiveMQ) | iOS/Android | — | Free |

**Total BOM cost: ≈ ₹ 320** (₹ 4 USD) — making this an ideal KTU lab project.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **end-to-end data path** from the user's finger on the phone to photons emitted by the LED. It is drawn in the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** style as a fallback for physical schematics.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    userA[User Finger]:::user --> phoneA[Smartphone App]:::ui
    phoneA -->|HTTPS over WiFi| cloudA[Blynk Cloud Server]:::cloud
    cloudA -->|MQTT Publish| brokerA[MQTT Broker Port 1883]:::cloud
    brokerA -->|Subscribe Topic led1/rgb| mcuA[NodeMCU ESP8266 Firmware]:::mcu
    mcuA -->|PWM GPIO D1| redA[Red LED Die 2.0V]:::led
    mcuA -->|PWM GPIO D2| greenA[Green LED Die 3.0V]:::led
    mcuA -->|PWM GPIO D3| blueA[Blue LED Die 3.0V]:::led
    redA --> mixA[Additive Color Mix in Free Space]:::opt
    greenA --> mixA
    blueA --> mixA
    mixA --> eyeA[Human Eye L cones M cones S cones]:::sensor

    classDef user fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ui fill:#B4D8FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cloud fill:#D4B4FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mcu fill:#B4FFB4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef led fill:#FFB4B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef opt fill:#FFFAB4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sensor fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram (left → right):**
1. **User Finger** taps a color on the Blynk `zeRGBa` widget.
2. **Smartphone App** packages the color as a 24-bit integer.
3. **Blynk Cloud** receives the value and pushes it to the device.
4. **NodeMCU ESP8266** receives the value over MQTT or Blynk's proprietary protocol.
5. Three independent **PWM signals** (on GPIOs D1, D2, D3) drive the three LED dies.
6. The **additive mix** in air is perceived by the three types of cone cells in the human eye.

---

### 4.2 Firmware State Machine — Connection Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> bootA
    bootA: Boot and Init GPIO
    wifiA: Connect to WiFi AP
    mqttA: Connect to MQTT Broker
    runA: Subscribe to led/rgb Topic
    colorA: Apply PWM Values
    errA: Retry With Backoff

    bootA --> wifiA: WiFi.begin
    wifiA --> mqttA: WL_CONNECTED
    wifiA --> errA: Timeout 30s
    mqttA --> runA: CONNACK rc=0
    mqttA --> errA: Connection Failed
    errA --> wifiA: After 5s
    runA --> colorA: Message Arrived
    colorA --> runA: Loop Forever
    runA --> [*]: OTA Update or Reset
```

---

### 4.3 Pin Connection Table (Wiring Reference)

| NodeMCU Pin | GPIO # | Connects To | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| D1 | GPIO5 | Red anode via 330 Ω | PWM channel 0 |
| D2 | GPIO4 | Green anode via 330 Ω | PWM channel 1 |
| D3 | GPIO0 | Blue anode via 330 Ω | PWM channel 2 |
| GND | — | Common cathode | Current return |
| VIN | — | 5 V USB (optional) | Powers NodeMCU |
| 3V3 | — | Pull-up for CH_PD | Enable chip |

> [!WARNING]
> Avoid using **GPIO0 (D3)** as a PWM output for the blue channel if you also rely on it for boot-mode selection. The LED may flicker at reset. Use **D4 (GPIO2)** for blue instead if you observe this issue.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**
*Explain the RGB additive color model. How does PWM enable color mixing in an LED driven by a digital microcontroller?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **RGB additive color model** represents every visible color as an additive combination of three primary light sources — **Red, Green, and Blue** — at intensities from 0 to 255 (8-bit). White is produced by $R=G=B=255$, while black is $R=G=B=0$.

A digital microcontroller cannot output a true analog voltage. Instead, it uses **PWM** — rapidly switching each GPIO pin ON and OFF at a fixed frequency (e.g., 1 kHz) and varying the **duty cycle**. The LED integrates this rapid switching visually and appears at a stable brightness proportional to the duty cycle $D = T_{ON} / T_{period} \times 100\%$.

For an ESP8266, a 10-bit register value (0–1023) is written via `analogWrite(pin, value)`. By controlling three independent PWM channels, the firmware can synthesize any of $2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216$ colors. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*List the hardware and software components required to build an IoT project that controls the color of an RGB LED via WiFi.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Hardware:** NodeMCU ESP8266 board, common-cathode RGB LED, three 330 Ω current-limiting resistors, breadboard, jumper wires, 5 V USB power supply, and a Wi-Fi router (2.4 GHz).

**Software:** Arduino IDE with ESP8266 board package, Blynk library (or PubSubClient for MQTT), Blynk IoT app on smartphone, optional HiveMQ public broker, and an MQTT client app (e.g., MQTT Dash) for testing.

**Protocol:** MQTT over TCP port 1883 (or Blynk's proprietary cloud protocol on port 8442) over a 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi link. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — IoT Control via Blynk Mobile App [14 Marks]**

**Design an IoT system using NodeMCU ESP8266 and the Blynk platform to control the color of an RGB LED from a smartphone. Explain the hardware connections, Blynk app configuration, and complete Arduino sketch.**

#### Part (a) — Hardware Setup and Blynk App Configuration (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Hardware Wiring (2 marks)**
- Connect the **common cathode** of the RGB LED to **GND** of NodeMCU.
- Connect the **red anode** through a **330 Ω resistor** to pin **D1 (GPIO5)**.
- Connect the **green anode** through a **330 Ω resistor** to pin **D2 (GPIO4)**.
- Connect the **blue anode** through a **330 Ω resistor** to pin **D3 (GPIO0)**.
- Power the NodeMCU via USB.

**Step 2: Blynk App Configuration (2 marks)**
- Install Blynk IoT app, create a new template, and copy the **Auth Token**.
- Add a **`zeRGBa` widget** (Color Picker) on virtual pin **V1**. Set its output mode to **MERGE** so it writes three integers: $R$, $G$, $B$ on the same pin.
- Optionally add a **Slider** widget on V2 for global brightness (0–100).
- Tap "Play" to deploy.

**Step 3: Wi-Fi Credentials (1 mark)**
Configure `ssid[]`, `pass[]`, and `BLYNK_AUTH_TOKEN` in the Arduino sketch as `char` arrays.

**Step 4: Safety & Power (2 marks)**
- Total current if all three channels fully ON: $3 \times 20$ mA $= 60$ mA — within the 200 mA budget of the NodeMCU 3.3 V rail.
- Add a **100 µF decoupling capacitor** across VIN–GND to suppress Wi-Fi burst transients.

---

#### Part (b) — Complete Arduino Sketch and Working (7 Marks)

```cpp
#define BLYNK_PRINT Serial
#include <ESP8266WiFi.h>
#include <BlynkSimpleEsp8266.h>

char auth[] = "YOUR_BLYNK_TOKEN";
char ssid[] = "YourWiFiName";
char pass[] = "YourWiFiPassword";

const int RED_PIN   = D1;   // GPIO5
const int GREEN_PIN = D2;   // GPIO4
const int BLUE_PIN  = D4;   // GPIO2 (avoid GPIO0 for stability)

int globalBrightness = 100;  // %

// BLYNK_WRITE fires when the zeRGBa widget sends new color
BLYNK_WRITE(V1) {
  int r = param[0].asInt();  // 0-255
  int g = param[1].asInt();
  int b = param[2].asInt();

  // Apply brightness scaling
  r = (r * globalBrightness) / 100;
  g = (g * globalBrightness) / 100;
  b = (b * globalBrightness) / 100;

  // ESP8266 analogWrite is 10-bit (0-1023)
  analogWrite(RED_PIN,   r * 4);
  analogWrite(GREEN_PIN, g * 4);
  analogWrite(BLUE_PIN,  b * 4);

  Serial.print("Color set to R="); Serial.print(r);
  Serial.print(" G="); Serial.print(g);
  Serial.print(" B="); Serial.println(b);
}

BLYNK_WRITE(V2) {
  globalBrightness = param.asInt();
}

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  pinMode(RED_PIN, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(GREEN_PIN, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(BLUE_PIN, OUTPUT);
  Blynk.begin(auth, ssid, pass);
}

void loop() {
  Blynk.run();
}
```

**Explanation of code (3 marks):**
- `BLYNK_WRITE(V1)` is an **interrupt-style callback** automatically called whenever the `zeRGBa` widget sends a new color tuple.
- `param[0].asInt()` extracts the first integer (Red) from the MERGE-mode array.
- Brightness scaling uses $(R \times B)/100$ to keep math in the integer domain — important on 8-bit AVR-class MCUs.
- The multiplication by 4 converts 8-bit color to 10-bit ESP8266 PWM resolution.
- `Blynk.run()` in `loop()` maintains the MQTT-over-WS connection to Blynk Cloud.

**Working (2 marks):** The user opens Blynk, taps a color, Blynk Cloud forwards it as a write event to the device, the callback scales and writes the PWM values, and the LED emits the chosen color within ~150 ms.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Common Mistakes that cost marks:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert 8-bit color to 10-bit PWM on ESP8266 — LED will look dim.
> 2. Using `D3 (GPIO0)` — boot-mode interference; LED flickers at every reset.
> 3. Forgetting to declare `pinMode(..., OUTPUT)` — `analogWrite` will silently fail on some pins.
> 4. Hard-coding Wi-Fi credentials as `String` instead of `char[]` — causes heap fragmentation on ESP8266.
> 5. Not adding a current-limiting resistor — burns the LED within seconds at full white.

---

### **Question B — MQTT-Based Web Control [14 Marks]**

**Develop a web-dashboard-controlled RGB LED system using NodeMCU, the MQTT protocol, and a public broker. Draw the architecture, write the firmware, and explain the message flow.**

#### Part (a) — System Architecture and MQTT Topic Design (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Architecture (3 marks)**
1. **Web Dashboard** (HTML/JS hosted on GitHub Pages or local) sends an MQTT **PUBLISH** message to broker.
2. **MQTT Broker** (HiveMQ public `broker.hivemq.com:1883`) routes the message.
3. **NodeMCU Subscribes** to the topic and applies the color.
4. **Acknowledgement** is published back to a status topic for UI update.

**Step 2: Topic Design (2 marks)**

| Topic | Direction | Payload (JSON) | QoS |
|---|---|---|---|
| `ktu/iot/led1/cmd` | Dashboard → NodeMCU | `{"r":255,"g":0,"b":0}` | 1 |
| `ktu/iot/led1/status` | NodeMCU → Dashboard | `{"color":"RED","r":255}` | 1 |
| `ktu/iot/led1/brightness` | Dashboard → NodeMCU | `{"level":75}` | 1 |
| `ktu/iot/led1/heartbeat` | NodeMCU → Dashboard | `{"uptime":12345}` | 0 |

**Step 3: QoS Levels (2 marks)**
- **QoS 0** — "at most once", used for heartbeats (loss OK).
- **QoS 1** — "at least once", used for color commands (must arrive, duplicates acceptable).
- **QoS 2** — "exactly once", overkill for an LED, not used.

---

#### Part (b) — Firmware and Web Dashboard Implementation (7 Marks)

**NodeMCU Firmware (4 marks):**

```cpp
#include <ESP8266WiFi.h>
#include <PubSubClient.h>

const char* ssid     = "KTU_LAB_WIFI";
const char* password = "lab12345";
const char* mqtt_server = "broker.hivemq.com";

WiFiClient espClient;
PubSubClient client(espClient);

const int RED_PIN   = D1;
const int GREEN_PIN = D2;
const int BLUE_PIN  = D4;

void callback(char* topic, byte* payload, unsigned int length) {
  String message;
  for (int i = 0; i < length; i++) message += (char)payload[i];

  // Very small JSON parser (for demo only)
  if (String(topic) == "ktu/iot/led1/cmd") {
    int r = valueFromJson(message, "r");
    int g = valueFromJson(message, "g");
    int b = valueFromJson(message, "b");
    analogWrite(RED_PIN,   r * 4);
    analogWrite(GREEN_PIN, g * 4);
    analogWrite(BLUE_PIN,  b * 4);
  }
}

void reconnect() {
  while (!client.connected()) {
    if (client.connect("nodeMCU_led1")) {
      client.subscribe("ktu/iot/led1/cmd");
    } else {
      delay(5000);
    }
  }
}

void setup() {
  pinMode(RED_PIN, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(GREEN_PIN, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(BLUE_PIN, OUTPUT);
  WiFi.begin(ssid, password);
  client.setServer(mqtt_server, 1883);
  client.setCallback(callback);
}

void loop() {
  if (!client.connected()) reconnect();
  client.loop();
}
```

**Web Dashboard (3 marks):**

```html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
  <title>KTU IoT Color Picker</title>
  <script src="https://unpkg.com/mqtt/dist/mqtt.min.js"></script>
</head>
<body>
  <h2>KTU PBCIT504 — RGB LED Control</h2>
  <input type="color" id="cp" value="#ff0000">
  <button onclick="publish()">Set Color</button>

  <script>
    const client = mqtt.connect('wss://broker.hivemq.com:8884/mqtt');
    client.on('connect', () => console.log('Connected to HiveMQ'));
    function publish() {
      const hex = document.getElementById('cp').value;
      const r = parseInt(hex.substr(1,2), 16);
      const g = parseInt(hex.substr(3,2), 16);
      const b = parseInt(hex.substr(5,2), 16);
      const payload = JSON.stringify({r, g, b});
      client.publish('ktu/iot/led1/cmd', payload, {qos: 1});
    }
  </script>
</body>
</html>
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Common Mistakes on MQTT-based questions:**
> 1. **Confusing QoS levels** — saying "QoS 2 is best" without explaining the 4-step handshake overhead.
> 2. **Hard-coding a single global topic** — multiple devices will collide. Always include a **device ID** in the topic (e.g., `led1`, `led2`).
> 3. **Using `String` concatenation inside the callback** — leads to heap fragmentation. Use a `char[]` buffer.
> 4. **Forgetting the broker port** — WebSockets use `8884`, plain MQTT uses `1883`. Mixing them causes silent failure.
> 5. **No `client.loop()` in `loop()`** — connection drops after ~30 s.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — "Project on Controlling the Color of Light"**
>
> ✅ RGB model produces $2^{24} = 16.7$ million colors via additive mixing of Red, Green, Blue.
>
> ✅ PWM duty cycle $D = T_{ON}/T_{period} \times 100\%$ controls perceived LED brightness.
>
> ✅ ESP8266 PWM is **10-bit (0–1023)**; ESP32 and Arduino are **8-bit (0–255)**. Conversion factor $= 4.012$.
>
> ✅ Each LED die needs a series resistor: $R = (V_{CC} - V_F)/I_F$. For 3.3 V and 20 mA, use **68 Ω (red)** and **15 Ω (green/blue)** or a single 220 Ω for safety.
>
> ✅ Blynk uses virtual pins `V0`–`V255` and the `zeRGBa` widget sends an RGB tuple via `BLYNK_WRITE(V1)`.
>
> ✅ MQTT topics must be **hierarchical and device-unique** (e.g., `ktu/iot/led1/cmd`).
>
> ✅ QoS 0 = fire-and-forget, QoS 1 = at-least-once, QoS 2 = exactly-once.
>
> ✅ Gamma correction ($\gamma = 2.2$) is required for perceptually linear brightness.
>
> ✅ Hex code `#RRGGBB` to decimal: each pair is a base-16 number from 00 (0) to FF (255).
>
> ✅ HSV → RGB conversion uses **chroma $C = V \cdot S$** and the **match value $m = V - C$**.
>
> ✅ Avoid using **GPIO0 / D3** for PWM if it is also a boot-mode strap pin.
>
> ✅ The NodeMCU 3.3 V rail can safely source **~200 mA total**; an RGB LED at full white draws ~60 mA.
>
> ✅ Add a **100 µF decoupling capacitor** across VIN–GND to suppress Wi-Fi TX current spikes.
>
> ✅ In the Arduino sketch, always declare `pinMode(pin, OUTPUT)` *before* `analogWrite(pin, val)`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project on Home Automation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT504 - PROGRAMMING FOR INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Project on Home Automation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Home Automation (HA)**, in the context of the Internet of Things (IoT), is defined as the **automated, remote, and intelligent control of household appliances, environmental parameters, and security systems** through a network of interconnected sensors, actuators, communication protocols, and computing devices that exchange data over the Internet.

Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBCIT504 - Programming for IoT)**, the "Project on Home Automation" is treated as a **capstone integrative module** that synthesizes the four prior modules:
- **Module 1:** IoT Architecture & Sensors
- **Module 2:** Communication Protocols (Wi-Fi, BLE, MQTT, CoAP, HTTP)
- **Module 3:** Cloud Platforms, Data Analytics & IoT Security
- **Module 4 (current):** **End-to-end application engineering** — implementing a working smart-home system.

### 1.1.1 Standard Terminology Used by KTU Examiners

| Term | KTU-Standard Definition |
|---|---|
| **Thing** | Any physical object embedded with a sensor/actuator and connectivity (e.g., a smart bulb). |
| **Smart Object** | A "thing" augmented with processing, memory, and a communication interface. |
| **Gateway** | A device that bridges local IoT nodes (often over short-range protocols) to the Internet. |
| **Edge Node** | A resource-constrained device (e.g., ESP8266, Arduino) performing sensing/actuation. |
| **Cloud Back-end** | Server-side infrastructure handling storage, analytics, dashboards, and user auth. |
| **Actuator** | A transducer that converts an electrical signal into a physical action (relay, servo, MOSFET). |

> [!NOTE]
> A home automation system is essentially a **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** — software decisions (cyber) directly drive physical state changes (physical) in the home environment.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Smart Home as a Human Nervous System

Imagine your home is a **human body**:

- The **sensors** (temperature, motion, gas, light) act as the **senses** — they perceive the world.
- The **microcontroller** (ESP32/NodeMCU/Arduino) is the **brain stem** — it processes incoming signals and issues reflexive commands.
- The **cloud server** is the **cerebrum** — it does higher-order reasoning, long-term memory (logs), and decision making across days/weeks.
- The **actuators** (relays, motors, solenoids) are the **muscles** — they actually move things.
- The **mobile app / dashboard** is the **conscious mind** of the user — it issues intent ("turn off lights at 11 PM") and reads feedback.
- The **communication protocol (MQTT/HTTP)** is the **nervous system** carrying signals between all these parts.

> [!TIP]
> When a KTU examiner asks, *"Justify why MQTT is preferred over HTTP for a home automation system,"* the answer is: **HTTP is like shouting across a room (heavy, no continuous channel); MQTT is like a private walkie-talkie line (lightweight, persistent, broker-mediated).**

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics to Remember

These standard metrics are frequently asked in KTU exams and **must be memorized**:

- **Latency:** typically **< 500 ms** for comfortable user experience in toggling appliances.
- **Power budget of a sensor node:** **3.3 V × ~200 mA = 660 mW** (active Wi-Fi).
- **MQTT packet overhead:** as low as **2 bytes** (vs. ~200+ bytes for HTTP headers).
- **Standard operating voltage for hobby boards:** **3.3 V** (ESP8266/ESP32) or **5 V** (Arduino Uno).
- **Relay coil current:** **~70 mA at 5 V** — must be driven via a transistor/MOSFET, never directly from a GPIO pin.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners explicitly test whether you can articulate the **difference between local (edge) automation and cloud-mediated automation** — a 3-mark favorite.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Architecture of a Home Automation System

A KTU-grade home automation project is analyzed in **four functional layers**:

### Layer 1 — Perception (Sensing) Layer
- **Function:** Convert physical phenomena → electrical signals.
- **Components:** DHT11/DHT22 (temp+humidity), PIR (motion), MQ-2/MQ-135 (gas), LDR (light), reed switch (door), current sensor (ACS712).
- **KTU exam cue:** "Explain the role of a transducer in IoT."

### Layer 2 — Network (Communication) Layer
- **Function:** Move data between edge, gateway, and cloud.
- **Short-range:** Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), BLE, ZigBee, LoRa.
- **Application-layer protocols:** **MQTT**, **HTTP/REST**, **CoAP**.
- **KTU exam cue:** "Compare MQTT with HTTP for IoT."

### Layer 3 — Processing (Edge/Cloud) Layer
- **Function:** Store, analyze, decide, trigger.
- **Edge processing:** rule-based thresholds on the microcontroller (e.g., "if temp > 35 °C → fan ON").
- **Cloud processing:** dashboards (Node-RED, Blynk, AWS IoT), analytics, user authentication.

### Layer 4 — Application (Interface) Layer
- **Function:** Human-machine interaction.
- **Forms:** Mobile app (Blynk/MIT App Inventor), web dashboard (Node-RED), voice assistant (Alexa/Google Home).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Choosing MQTT — KTU's Favourite Question

| Parameter | HTTP/REST | MQTT |
|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | Client–Server (request/response) | Publish–Subscribe (broker-mediated) |
| **Header overhead** | ~200–800 bytes | **2 bytes** (minimum) |
| **Connection** | Short-lived | **Persistent** (keep-alive) |
| **Power efficiency** | Poor (re-establishes TCP) | Excellent (low keep-alive) |
| **Push semantics** | Needs polling or WebSockets | **Native** |
| **QoS levels** | None (TCP reliability only) | **QoS 0, 1, 2** |
| **Use case in HA** | One-shot commands, firmware updates | **Continuous telemetry & control** |

> [!NOTE]
> **MQTT Topic Naming Convention** (asked in viva): `home/<room>/<device>/<action>`
> Example: `home/livingroom/fan/set` → payload `{"state":"ON","speed":3}`

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **complete, exam-ready reference** for the mathematical and electrical quantities in this module. Every row is a potential 1–2 mark sub-question.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Value | Units | Where Used in Project |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Power consumed by a load** | $P = V \times I$ | Watts (W) | Sizing PSU & relays |
| 2 | **Energy over time** | $E = P \times t$ | Watt-hours (Wh) | Monthly billing, energy monitoring |
| 3 | **Energy in kWh (tariff)** | $E_{kWh} = \dfrac{V \times I \times t}{1000}$ | kWh | Electricity bill calculation |
| 4 | **Ohm's Law (for sensor/LED current-limit resistor)** | $V = I \times R$ | V, A, Ω | LED resistor calculation |
| 5 | **Current-limit resistor for LED** | $R = \dfrac{V_{supply} - V_{LED}}{I_{LED}}$ | Ω | Driving indicator LEDs |
| 6 | **MQTT keep-alive** | Typically **60 s** | seconds | Preventing broker disconnect |
| 7 | **MQTT packet length encoding** | Variable byte (1–4 bytes) | bytes | Knowing why header is "2 bytes" |
| 8 | **ADC resolution (ESP32)** | $V_{in} = \dfrac{ADC_{value}}{4095} \times V_{ref}$ | Volts | Reading analog sensors (LDR, ACS712) |
| 9 | **PIR detection range** | ~**3–7 m**, 120° cone | m, ° | Room layout planning |
| 10 | **DHT11 accuracy** | Temp ±**2 °C**, Humidity ±**5% RH** | °C, % | Setting reliable thresholds |
| 11 | **Relay contact rating (typical)** | **10 A @ 250 V AC** | A, V | Maximum load switching |
| 12 | **Wi-Fi TX current (ESP8266)** | ~**170 mA** peak (802.11b) | mA | Power-supply headroom |
| 13 | **Sleep current (ESP8266 deep sleep)** | ~**10 µA** | µA | Battery-operated nodes |
| 14 | **Latency budget (user-perceptible)** | **< 500 ms** | ms | UX requirement |
| 15 | **Steinhart–Hart (thermistor)** | $\dfrac{1}{T} = a + b\ln(R) + c[\ln(R)]^{3}$ | Kelvin | Optional — NTC sensors |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

A home automation system is **not just a hobby project** — it is a vertical application of:
- **Smart Grid & Demand Response** (utilities).
- **Ambient Assisted Living (AAL)** — elderly fall detection, medication reminders.
- **Insurance Telematics** — water-leak alerts reduce claim sizes.
- **Hospitality** — hotel room automation (HVAC, lighting, curtains).

> [!TIP]
> In a 14-mark question, KTU examiners reward students who mention **at least one industry deployment** (e.g., "This architecture is similar to **Tuya Smart's** white-label IoT platform used in 200,000+ device models worldwide").

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Sizing the Current-Limit Resistor for an Indicator LED

> **Problem (KTU Part-B style):** A home automation PCB uses a red indicator LED ($V_{LED} = 2.0$ V, $I_{LED} = 20$ mA) connected to a 3.3 V GPIO of an ESP32. Calculate the required series resistor.

**Step 1 — State the goal.**
We must limit current through the LED to a safe value so that the GPIO is not overloaded and the LED is not destroyed.

**Step 2 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL).**
The supply voltage equals the sum of voltage drops across the resistor and the LED.

$$V_{supply} = V_{R} + V_{LED}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the resistor voltage drop.**
Rearranging the KVL equation for $V_R$:

$$V_{R} = V_{supply} - V_{LED}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$V_{R} = 3.3 - 2.0 = 1.3\ \text{V}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Ohm's Law.**
We want exactly $I_{LED} = 20$ mA through the series resistor (and therefore through the LED).

$$R = \dfrac{V_{R}}{I_{LED}}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute values with SI units.**
Convert 20 mA to 0.020 A:

$$R = \dfrac{1.3}{0.020}$$

**Step 7 — Compute.**

$$R = 65\ \Omega$$

**Step 8 — Choose the nearest standard E12 value.**
The next standard value above 65 Ω is **68 Ω**. This is the final design choice.

> **Result:** Use a **68 Ω, ¼ W resistor** in series with the LED.

**Step 9 — Sanity check (power dissipation).**
Power dissipated in the resistor:

$$P_{R} = I^{2} \times R = (0.020)^{2} \times 68 = 0.0272\ \text{W}$$

A ¼ W (0.25 W) resistor is more than sufficient (derating factor of ~9×).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Energy & Cost Calculation for a Smart Plug

> **Problem:** A smart plug monitors a 230 V, 750 W air-conditioner. The plug uses an ACS712 current sensor to read RMS current. The MQTT broker publishes current every 10 s. Calculate: (a) the hourly energy in kWh, and (b) the daily energy cost at ₹8.50/unit.

**Step 1 — Identify given quantities.**
- Voltage: $V = 230$ V (RMS, AC mains)
- Power rating: $P = 750$ W
- Tariff: $r = 8.50$ ₹/kWh

**Step 2 — Hourly energy calculation.**

$$E_{hourly} = P \times t = 750 \times 1 = 750\ \text{Wh}$$

Convert to kWh:

$$E_{hourly\ (kWh)} = \dfrac{750}{1000} = 0.75\ \text{kWh}$$

**Step 3 — Extrapolate to a full day (24 hours) — assuming continuous operation.**

$$E_{daily} = 0.75 \times 24 = 18\ \text{kWh}$$

**Step 4 — Compute daily cost.**

$$\text{Cost}_{daily} = E_{daily} \times r = 18 \times 8.50$$

$$\text{Cost}_{daily} = 153\ ₹$$

**Step 5 — Monthly cost (30 days).**

$$\text{Cost}_{monthly} = 153 \times 30 = 4{,}590\ ₹$$

**Step 6 — Engineering insight.**
If a rule is added — *"turn AC OFF when room temp < 24 °C and no motion detected for 10 minutes"* — assume a **30 % duty cycle**:

$$E_{monthly\ (optimized)} = 18 \times 0.30 \times 30 = 162\ \text{kWh} \rightarrow 1{,}377\ ₹$$

> **Savings: ₹3,213 per month** — this is the kind of "value-add" KTU examiners award bonus credit for.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Reading an Analog LDR Sensor with ESP32 ADC

> **Problem:** An LDR is wired as a voltage divider with a 10 kΩ fixed resistor between 3.3 V and the ESP32's GPIO34 (ADC1_CH6). The ADC reads a value of 2,800 (out of 4,095). Calculate the LDR's resistance. The default attenuation of ESP32 ADC gives full-scale at ~3.1 V.

**Step 1 — Write the ADC formula.**

$$V_{in} = \dfrac{ADC_{value}}{4095} \times V_{ref}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$V_{in} = \dfrac{2800}{4095} \times 3.1$$

**Step 3 — Compute.**

$$V_{in} = 0.6838 \times 3.1 = 2.1198\ \text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the voltage-divider formula.**
With $V_{in}$ as the voltage across the LDR (the lower half of the divider, connected to GND):

$$V_{in} = V_{supply} \times \dfrac{R_{LDR}}{R_{LDR} + R_{fixed}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute knowns and solve for $R_{LDR}$.**

$$2.1198 = 3.3 \times \dfrac{R_{LDR}}{R_{LDR} + 10{,}000}$$

$$\dfrac{2.1198}{3.3} = \dfrac{R_{LDR}}{R_{LDR} + 10{,}000}$$

$$0.6424 \times (R_{LDR} + 10{,}000) = R_{LDR}$$

$$6{,}424 + 0.6424 \cdot R_{LDR} = R_{LDR}}$$

$$6{,}424 = R_{LDR} \times (1 - 0.6424)$$

$$6{,}424 = 0.3576 \times R_{LDR}$$

**Step 6 — Final answer.**

$$R_{LDR} = \dfrac{6{,}424}{0.3576} \approx 17{,}960\ \Omega \approx 18\ \text{k}\Omega$$

> **Interpretation:** A high LDR resistance (~18 kΩ) corresponds to **low ambient light** — the system would interpret this as "night" and trigger lights ON.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Table for Component Selection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners love a **selection justification** question. Use this table to answer, *"Which microcontroller would you choose for a battery-powered door sensor that must run for 1 year on a single coin cell?"*

| Criteria | Arduino Uno | ESP8266 | ESP32 | Raspberry Pi Pico W |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Wi-Fi built-in** | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Deep-sleep current** | ~**45 mA** (no Wi-Fi) | **~10 µA** | **~10 µA** | **~1.3 mA** |
| **ADC channels** | 6 (10-bit) | 1 (10-bit) | 18 (12-bit) | 3 (12-bit) |
| **Operating voltage** | 5 V | 3.3 V | 3.3 V | 3.3 V |
| **Cost (approx.)** | ₹400 | ₹180 | ₹350 | ₹600 |
| **Battery suitability (coin cell)** | Poor | **Excellent** | Excellent | Good |
| **Verdict for door sensor** | Not suitable | **Best choice** | Good | Acceptable |

> **Conclusion for a 1-year battery life coin-cell design: ESP8266 in deep-sleep with wake-on-interrupt from a reed switch.**

## 4.2 MQTT Publish–Subscribe Data Flow (Logical View)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sensor as ESP32 Sensor
    participant Broker as MQTT Broker
    participant Cloud as Node-RED Dashboard
    participant Mobile as Mobile App
    participant Relay as Relay Actuator

    Note over Sensor: Reads DHT11 every 5 s
    Sensor->>Broker: PUBLISH home/livingroom/temp {"value": 28.4}
    Broker-->>Cloud: Forward message (subscribed)
    Cloud->>Cloud: Plot graph, log to DB
    Cloud-->>Mobile: Push notification if > 32 deg C

    Note over Mobile: User taps Fan ON
    Mobile->>Broker: PUBLISH home/livingroom/fan/set {"state":"ON"}
    Broker-->>Relay: Forward to ESP32 (subscribed)
    Relay->>Relay: Energize coil, close contact
    Relay-->>Broker: PUBLISH home/livingroom/fan/status {"state":"ON"}
    Broker-->>Mobile: Update UI to ON
    Broker-->>Cloud: Log event
```

## 4.3 Edge-Decision State Machine (Rule Engine on ESP32)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle

    Idle --> ReadingSensors: Every 5 s
    ReadingSensors --> CheckRules: Sensor data ready
    CheckRules --> MotionDetected: PIR HIGH AND time is night
    CheckRules --> TempHigh: DHT11 temp above 32 deg C
    CheckRules --> GasLeak: MQ2 above 400 ppm
    CheckRules --> Idle: No rule matched

    MotionDetected --> ActuateLight: Turn ON hallway light
    TempHigh --> ActuateFan: Turn ON fan, send alert
    GasLeak --> EmergencyShutoff: Turn OFF all appliances, siren ON
    ActuateLight --> PublishStatus
    ActuateFan --> PublishStatus
    EmergencyShutoff --> PublishStatus
    PublishStatus --> Idle
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the term "Home Automation" in the context of IoT. List any four common sensors used in a smart-home system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Home Automation, in the IoT context, refers to the **automatic and remote control of household devices** through sensors, actuators, and Internet-connected controllers, enabling intelligent decisions based on environmental data and user preferences. **(2 marks)**
>
> Four common sensors are: **(1)**
> 1. **DHT11/DHT22** — temperature & humidity,
> 2. **PIR (HC-SR501)** — motion/occupancy,
> 3. **MQ-2** — gas/smoke,
> 4. **LDR (GL55)** — ambient light.
>
> *(Optional 4th: reed switch for door, ACS712 for current.)* **(1 mark)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Compare MQTT and HTTP as communication protocols for a battery-powered IoT device. Which is more suitable and why?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> HTTP is a **request–response, stateless** protocol with a large header (~200–800 bytes) and short-lived TCP connections, making it **power-hungry and bandwidth-wasteful** for IoT. MQTT is a **lightweight publish–subscribe** protocol with broker mediation, **2-byte minimum headers**, persistent TCP connection, and built-in **QoS 0/1/2** delivery guarantees. **(2 marks)**
>
> Therefore, **MQTT is more suitable** for battery-powered devices because it reduces air-time, supports keep-alive at **~60 s** intervals, and offers **push semantics** without polling. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates that each Part-B question have sub-parts (a) and (b), typically **7 + 7 marks**, with an **internal choice** between two questions of equal marks.

---

### **Q3A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
*Design an IoT-based Home Automation system using ESP32 to control three appliances (light, fan, socket) based on temperature, motion, and time-of-day. Answer the following:*

***(a) Draw and explain the system architecture. List the hardware and software components. (7 marks)***
***(b) Write the pseudo-code / algorithm for the ESP32 firmware. Explain the MQTT topic structure used. (7 marks)***

#### **Model Solution (a):**

The system has **four layers** as shown in the architecture diagram (refer to **Section 4.1**).

| Layer | Components | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Perception** | DHT11, PIR, LDR, Reed switch | Sense temperature, motion, light, door state. |
| **Edge** | ESP32, 3-channel 5V relay module, BC547 drivers, 1N4007 flyback diodes | Run rule engine, drive actuators. |
| **Network** | Wi-Fi 2.4 GHz, MQTT broker (Mosquitto on Raspberry Pi) | Bidirectional transport. |
| **Application** | Blynk app + Node-RED dashboard | User control and visualization. |

**Software stack:** Arduino IDE (C++), PubSubClient MQTT library, Blynk library, ArduinoJson for payload encoding, Node-RED flows, InfluxDB + Grafana for time-series.

**Justification of ESP32 (1 mark):** Dual-core 240 MHz, built-in Wi-Fi + BLE, 18×12-bit ADC, deep-sleep at ~10 µA, ample GPIO (30+), industry-grade support.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

```text
ALGORITHM: SmartHome_Loop
  1.  BEGIN
  2.    Connect to Wi-Fi SSID using SSID, PASS
  3.    Connect to MQTT broker at broker.local:1883
  4.    Subscribe to topics:
            home/livingroom/light/set
            home/livingroom/fan/set
            home/livingroom/socket/set
  5.    INITIALIZE pins: light=25, fan=26, socket=27 as OUTPUT
  6.    INITIALIZE sensors: dht.begin(); pirSetup(); ldrSetup()
  7.    LOOP every 5 seconds:
  7.1     temp = dht.readTemperature()
  7.2     hum  = dht.readHumidity()
  7.3     motion = digitalRead(PIR_PIN)
  7.4     lux    = analogRead(LDR_PIN)
  7.5     publish("home/livingroom/temp", temp)   // QoS 1
  7.6     publish("home/livingroom/hum",  hum)
  7.7     publish("home/livingroom/motion", motion)
  7.8     RULE_ENGINE(temp, motion, lux, hourOfDay)
  7.9     if (mqtt.connected() == false) mqtt.reconnect()
  8.  END
```

**RULE_ENGINE function (selected rules):**

```text
IF temp > 32 AND hourOfDay IN [09:00..22:00] THEN fan ON
IF motion == HIGH AND lux < 1500 AND hourOfDay > 18:00 THEN light ON
ELSE IF no motion for 10 minutes THEN light OFF
IF gas sensor > 400 ppm THEN all OFF, publish alert, sound buzzer
```

**MQTT Topic Structure (1 mark):**

```text
home/<room>/<device>/<action>
Examples:
  home/livingroom/temp         (telemetry)
  home/livingroom/light/set    (command)
  home/livingroom/light/status (acknowledgement)
```

**Payload format (1 mark):** JSON — `{"value": 28.4}` for telemetry, `{"state": "ON"}` for commands.

**Security (1 mark):** Use TLS port 8883, username/password auth on broker, JWT tokens for app users, OTA updates signed with SHA-256.

---

### **Q3B. [Alternative — KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*You are tasked with deploying a home automation system in a 3-BHK apartment with priority on **energy monitoring and security**. Answer the following:*

***(a) Design the sensor and actuator placement for the apartment. Justify each placement. (7 marks)***
***(b) Explain how the ACS712 current sensor is interfaced with ESP32. Show the calculation to compute real power from voltage and current samples. (7 marks)***

#### **Model Solution (a):**

| Room | Sensor | Actuator | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Living Room** | DHT22, PIR, LDR, ACS712 on AC socket | Smart plug (relay), curtain motor | Highest occupancy, energy hogs (TV, AC). |
| **Master Bedroom** | DHT11, PIR | Light, fan relays | Sleep-cycle automation, AC control. |
| **Kitchen** | MQ-2 (gas), flame sensor | Exhaust fan relay, gas-valve solenoid | **Safety critical** — leak detection. |
| **Bathroom** | Humidity sensor, water-leak sensor | Exhaust fan, auto-valve | Mold prevention, flood prevention. |
| **Main Door** | Reed switch, RFID reader | Smart lock, buzzer | Intrusion detection, keyless entry. |
| **Balcony** | DHT22, rain sensor | Awning motor | Weather-responsive. |

**Justification (1 mark):** Centralized MQTT broker in the **hallway utility area** ensures line-of-sight to all rooms, with **star topology** Wi-Fi.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

The **ACS712** is a Hall-effect based current sensor with **bidirectional** output and a sensitivity of **185 mV/A** (for the 5 A variant) or **100 mV/A** (20 A variant) or **66 mV/A** (30 A variant). The output is centered at $V_{CC}/2$ (≈1.65 V for 3.3 V supply).

**Interfacing (2 marks):**
- VCC → 3.3 V
- GND → GND
- OUT → ESP32 GPIO35 (ADC1_CH7, 12-bit)
- The load is wired in series with the IP+ and IP− pins of the ACS712.

**RMS current calculation (3 marks):**

```text
1. Sample N = 1000 ADC values at fs = 4 kHz.
2. Convert each sample: i_k = (adc_k - 2048) / sensitivity
   (For 5 A variant, sensitivity = 0.185 V/A
    But ADC counts per A = (4095 / 3.3) * 0.185 = 229.6 counts/A
    So: i_k = (adc_k - 2048) / 229.6
3. Compute RMS: I_rms = sqrt( (1/N) * sum(i_k^2) )
4. Compute real power:
   P_real = (1/N) * sum(v_k * i_k)
   where v_k is the line-voltage sample (from ZMPT101B or assumed 230 V RMS).
5. Energy over time t: E = sum(P_real * delta_t)
```

**Sample calculation (2 marks):** For an AC socket reading 230 V RMS and a kettle drawing 4 A RMS:

$$P = V_{rms} \times I_{rms} \times \cos\phi = 230 \times 4 \times 0.95 = 874\ \text{W}$$

Daily energy at 30 minutes of use:

$$E = 874 \times 0.5 = 437\ \text{Wh} = 0.437\ \text{kWh}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do not** omit the **offset subtraction** `(adc - 2048)` in the ACS712 formula — it accounts for the 0-A bias. Forgetting this gives **non-zero current when the load is OFF**. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Do not** assume sinusoidal unity power factor in a real home. Mentioning **cos φ** earns an extra mark.
> 3. **Do not** power the relay coil directly from the ESP32 GPIO. The GPIO sources ~12 mA; the relay coil needs ~70 mA. You **must** use a **transistor driver + flyback diode**. Omission = –2 marks.
> 4. **Always** mention **MQTT QoS 1** for state commands and **QoS 0** for high-frequency telemetry.
> 5. **Never** connect mains (230 V AC) directly to the breadboard or to a sensor input. Use proper **opto-isolation** for the relay module (a "high-level trigger" relay with built-in opto-coupler is expected).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and read it the night before the exam.**

- [ ] **Home Automation = CPS** (Cyber-Physical System) bridging sensors → cloud → actuators.
- [ ] **Standard 4-layer architecture:** Perception → Network → Processing → Application.
- [ ] **Preferred microcontroller:** **ESP32** (Wi-Fi + BLE + dual-core + 12-bit ADC).
- [ ] **Preferred protocol:** **MQTT** (lightweight, pub-sub, 2-byte header, QoS 0/1/2).
- [ ] **MQTT topic convention:** `home/<room>/<device>/<action>` — payload in **JSON**.
- [ ] **MQTT broker:** **Mosquitto** (local) or **HiveMQ / AWS IoT Core** (cloud).
- [ ] **Sensor portfolio:** DHT11/22 (climate), PIR (motion), MQ-2 (gas), LDR (light), ACS712 (current), reed switch (door).
- [ ] **Actuator interface rule:** Always use a **transistor driver + flyback diode** for relays; never drive a relay coil directly from a GPIO.
- [ ] **Key power formula:** $P = V \times I$; **Energy:** $E = P \times t$; **kWh:** divide by 1000.
- [ ] **LED resistor formula:** $R = (V_{supply} - V_{LED}) / I_{LED}$.
- [ ] **ESP32 ADC formula:** $V_{in} = (\text{ADC} / 4095) \times V_{ref}$ (default 12-bit, 0–3.1 V).
- [ ] **ACS712 offset:** Always subtract `Vcc/2` (≈ 2048 counts) before computing current.
- [ ] **Latency target:** **< 500 ms** for user-perceptible control.
- [ ] **Deep-sleep current:** ~**10 µA** for ESP8266/ESP32 — essential for battery nodes.
- [ ] **Security essentials:** TLS on port 8883, broker authentication (username/password or cert-based), device-unique credentials, **OTA** firmware updates.
- [ ] **Cloud platforms:** **Blynk** (rapid prototyping), **Node-RED** (flows), **AWS IoT Core** (production), **ThingSpeak** (academic).
- [ ] **Most common viva question:** *"What happens if Wi-Fi goes down?"* — Answer: **edge rules on the microcontroller MUST continue to work locally** (e.g., gas-leak shutoff is a local rule, not a cloud rule).
- [ ] **Industry deployment mention (bonus):** Tuya Smart, Xiaomi Aqara, Samsung SmartThings — all use a **gateway + cloud + app** architecture similar to the KTU-recommended model.
- [ ] **Capstone project deliverable checklist:** (1) Working prototype, (2) Architecture diagram, (3) Circuit diagram, (4) Source code with comments, (5) MQTT topic table, (6) Test results table (latency, false-trigger rate), (7) Cost-of-implementation table, (8) Future-enhancement section (voice, ML-based anomaly detection).

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Project on Home Automation.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project on Security Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCIT504 - PROGRAMMING FOR INTERNET OF THINGS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Project on Security Systems # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition An **IoT-Based Security System Project** is a comprehensive embedded application that integrates heterogeneous sensors, microcontroller units, wireless communication protocols, and cloud platforms to deliver real-time **perimeter intrusion detection**, **environmental hazard monitoring**, and **remote situational awareness** for residential, industrial, and commercial premises. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Verbatim Style):** > A security system in the IoT paradigm is a *cyber-physical system (CPS)* where physical-world events (motion, gas leakage, door breach, fire) are sensed, digitized, processed at the edge (microcontroller), and transmitted over IP-based networks to a remote user interface, triggering alerts via actuators (buzzers, sirens) and digital channels (email, SMS, Telegram, Blynk notifications). The project is expected to demonstrate the **four pillars of IoT**: 1. **Sensing** – Data acquisition from physical phenomena. 2. **Processing** – Edge computation on NodeMCU/ESP32/Raspberry Pi. 3. **Connectivity** – Wi-Fi/MQTT/HTTP-based communication. 4. **Actuation & Alerting** – Local buzzers + cloud push notifications. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy – "The Smart Watchman"** > Imagine hiring a security guard who **never sleeps, never blinks, and works in 12 places at once**. He has a *thermal vision camera* (PIR), a *smell sensor* (MQ-2 gas sensor), *magnetic locks* (reed switches), and a *loud voice* (buzzer). The moment he detects anything unusual, he **instantly dials your phone** (cloud notification), **screams loudly** (local alarm), and **logs the event** in a register (cloud database). An **IoT Security System is exactly this digital watchman**, just faster, cheaper, and tireless. | Feature | Human Watchman | IoT Security System | |---|---|---| | Response Time | 5–30 seconds | < **500 ms** | | Working Hours | 8-hour shifts | **24/7/365** | | Cost (per year) | ₹1,50,000+ | **₹2,000 – ₹5,000** | | Remote Access | Walkie-Talkie only | **Global via Internet** | | Data Logging | Manual register | **Cloud timestamped logs** | ## 1.3 Scope of the Project (KTU Module 4 Alignment) The KTU 2024 Scheme **PBCIT504 Module 4** expects students to build an end-to-end prototype covering: - **Sensor Layer**: PIR (HC-SR501), Gas/Smoke (MQ-2), Reed Switch, Temperature (DHT11), LDR, ESP32-CAM. - **Network Layer**: Wi-Fi 802.11 b/g/n, MQTT (Mosquitto Broker), HTTP REST APIs. - **Cloud Layer**: ThingSpeak / Blynk / Firebase / Telegram Bot API. - **Application Layer**: Mobile dashboard + Web dashboard + SMS gateway (Twilio). > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** When asked to "design an IoT security system," always begin with a **block diagram**, list **hardware specifications**, provide the **circuit topology**, and then move to **firmware logic**. Marks are awarded for *systematic design*, not random code dumps. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Specifications ## 2.1 System Architecture – Layered Breakdown ### Layer 1: **Perception / Sensor Layer** Sensors convert physical phenomena into analog/digital electrical signals. | Sensor | Physical Quantity | Operating Voltage | Output Type | Threshold (Typical) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **HC-SR501 (PIR)** | Infrared motion (human body) | **5V DC** | Digital (HIGH/LOW) | Trigger range: **3–7 m** | | **MQ-2** | LPG, Methane, Smoke | **5V DC** | Analog (0–1023) | Rs clean air: **≥ 10 kΩ** | | **Reed Switch** | Magnetic field (door open) | **3.3V – 5V** | Digital (HIGH/LOW) | Activation gap: **≤ 20 mm** | | **DHT11** | Temperature + Humidity | **3.3V – 5V** | Digital (single-wire) | Temp: **0–50 °C** (±2 °C) | | **ESP32-CAM** | Visual evidence (image) | **5V DC** | JPEG stream over Wi-Fi | Resolution: **UXGA (1632×1232)** | | **Buzzer (Piezo)** | Acoustic alarm | **3.3V – 5V** | Digital actuation | SPL: **≥ 85 dB @ 10 cm** | ### Layer 2: **Network / Communication Layer** The **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** protocol is the KTU-preferred IoT messaging standard due to its lightweight publish/subscribe model. $$\text{MQTT Packet Size} \approx 2 \text{ bytes (minimum header)}$$ **MQTT Quality of Service (QoS) Levels:** | QoS Level | Guarantee | Use Case | |---|---|---| | **QoS 0** | At most once (fire and forget) | Periodic sensor data (non-critical) | | **QoS 1** | At least once (PUBACK) | Security alerts (recommended) | | **QoS 2** | Exactly once (4-way handshake) | Banking-grade transactions | ### Layer 3: **Processing Layer (Edge Computing)** The **ESP32** (dual-core Tensilica Xtensa LX6, 240 MHz) handles: - Sensor data fusion using **weighted averaging**: $$D_{fused} = w_1 \cdot d_{PIR} + w_2 \cdot d_{Gas} + w_3 \cdot d_{Reed}$$ where the constraint $\sum_{i=1}^{3} w_i = 1$ ensures normalized decision-making. - **Debounce filtering** for reed switch: $$t_{debounce} \geq 50 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(to eliminate mechanical chatter)}$$ ### Layer 4: **Application Layer (Cloud + Dashboard)** Cloud platforms used in KTU projects: > [!IMPORTANT] > - **ThingSpeak** – MathWorks, free tier, ideal for time-series plots. > - **Blynk IoT** – Mobile-first dashboard, drag-and-drop widgets. > - **Firebase Realtime DB** – Google's NoSQL JSON tree. > - **Telegram Bot API** – Free push notifications to smartphones. ## 2.2 High-Yield Hardware Specification Sheet | Component | Quantity | Unit Price (INR) | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | ESP32 DevKit V1 | 1 | 450 | Main controller + Wi-Fi | | HC-SR501 PIR | 2 | 120 | Motion detection | | MQ-2 Sensor | 1 | 180 | Gas/smoke detection | | Magnetic Reed Switch | 2 | 60 | Door/window intrusion | | DHT11 | 1 | 150 | Env. monitoring | | Active Buzzer 5V | 1 | 30 | Local alarm | | Red/Green LED | 2 | 5 | Status indication | | Breadboard + Jumpers | 1 set | 200 | Prototyping | | 5V 2A SMPS Adapter | 1 | 250 | Power supply | | **Total Estimated Cost** | — | **≈ ₹1,855** | — | ## 2.3 Why This Project Matters (Engineering Utility) > [!TIP] > **Real-World Deployment Scenarios:** > 1. **Smart Homes** – Integration with Google Home / Alexa routines. > 2. **Industrial Safety** – Detecting LPG leaks in chemical plants. > 3. **Banking Vaults** – Multi-sensor intrusion logging for forensics. > 4. **Healthcare** – Fall detection in elderly care homes (PIR + accelerometer). > 5. **Smart Cities** – Perimeter security of public infrastructure. # 3. Step-by-Step Project Design, Firmware Logic & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Circuit Design – Pin Allocation Table The **ESP32** GPIO pins must be carefully selected to avoid **bootstrapping pins** (GPIO 0, 2, 15) at startup. | Component | ESP32 GPIO Pin | Pin Type | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | PIR Sensor 1 | **GPIO 13** | Input | Internal pull-down enabled | | PIR Sensor 2 | **GPIO 12** | Input | Avoid (TDI) strapping pin during flash | | MQ-2 (Analog) | **GPIO 34 (ADC1_CH6)** | Analog Input | ADC2 conflicts with Wi-Fi – use ADC1 | | MQ-2 (Digital) | **GPIO 27** | Digital Input | Optional, threshold via onboard pot | | Reed Switch (Door) | **GPIO 14** | Input | Hardware debounce via 100nF cap | | Reed Switch (Window) | **GPIO 26** | Input | Software debounce in code | | DHT11 Data | **GPIO 4** | Bidirectional | Requires 10kΩ pull-up | | Buzzer | **GPIO 25** | Output (PWM) | Use NPN BC547 driver if >20 mA | | Red LED (Alarm) | **GPIO 16** | Output | Active HIGH | | Green LED (Status) | **GPIO 17** | Output | Active HIGH | > [!WARNING] > **ESP32 ADC Non-Linearity Notice:** The ESP32 ADC is non-linear below 0.1V and above 3.1V. Always apply the calibration correction: > $$\text{ADC}_{corrected} = \text{ADC}_{raw} \times 3.3 / 4095 \quad \text{(for 12-bit default)}$$ ## 3.2 Firmware Logic – Algorithmic Derivation The complete firmware follows a **state-machine paradigm** with three discrete states: $$ S = \{S_{IDLE},\ S_{ALERT},\ S_{SENTINEL}\} $$ where: - $S_{IDLE}$ → System armed, no intrusion detected. - $S_{ALERT}$ → Intrusion confirmed, local alarm ON, cloud push triggered. - $S_{SENTINEL}$ → Continuous monitoring after alert (no false re-trigger within 30 s). ### Step-by-Step Firmware Derivation: **Step 1 – Initialization Phase** ```cpp void setup() { Serial.begin(115200); pinMode(PIR1, INPUT); pinMode(BUZZER, OUTPUT); dht.begin(); WiFi.begin(ssid, password); while (WiFi.status() != WL_CONNECTED) { delay(500); } client.setServer(mqttServer, 1883); } ``` **Step 2 – Sensor Fusion & Threshold Check** The motion decision variable is derived as: $$ M(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } \text{PIR}_{1}(t) \lor \text{PIR}_{2}(t) = \text{HIGH} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ The gas hazard decision is derived as: $$ G(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } V_{\text{MQ2}}(t) > V_{\text{threshold}} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ **Step 3 – Composite Threat Index** $$ T(t) = \alpha \cdot M(t) + \beta \cdot G(t) + \gamma \cdot R(t) + \delta \cdot F(t) $$ where $R(t)$ is the reed switch state, $F(t)$ is the flame indicator, and the weights satisfy: $$ \alpha + \beta + \gamma + \delta = 1.0 $$ **Recommended weighting for KTU demo project:** | Weight | Value | Justification | |---|---|---| | $\alpha$ (Motion) | **0.30** | Most common intrusion type | | $\beta$ (Gas) | **0.35** | Highest life-safety risk | | $\gamma$ (Reed) | **0.20** | Physical breach indicator | | $\delta$ (Fire) | **0.15** | Detected via flame sensor or temp spike | **Step 4 – Alert Escalation Logic** $$ \text{Action}(t) = \begin{cases} \text{Buzzer ON + MQTT Publish} & \text{if } T(t) \geq 0.50 \\ \text{Log Only} & \text{if } 0.20 \leq T(t) < 0.50 \\ \text{No Action} & \text{if } T(t) < 0.20 \end{cases} $$ **Step 5 – Main Loop Pseudocode Derivation** $$ \text{Loop} \rightarrow \text{ReadSensors} \rightarrow \text{Compute } T(t) \rightarrow \text{Decision}(T) $$ $$ \rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{Buzzer ON, publish(JSON payload)} & \text{if } T \geq 0.50 \\ \text{Publish status (QoS 1)} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ **Step 6 – MQTT Payload Construction (JSON)** ```json { "deviceId": "SEC_ESP32_01", "timestamp": "2026-05-14T10:23:45Z", "pir1": 1, "pir2": 0, "mq2_ppm": 412, "door": "OPEN", "window": "CLOSED", "temperature": 28.4, "humidity": 62, "threatIndex": 0.65, "alarmState": "ACTIVE" } ``` ## 3.3 Cloud Dashboard – ThingSpeak Field Mapping | ThingSpeak Field | Data | Update Interval | |---|---|---| | Field 1 | PIR Motion Count (cumulative) | 15 s | | Field 2 | MQ-2 Gas Concentration (ppm) | 15 s | | Field 3 | Temperature (°C) | 30 s | | Field 4 | Humidity (%) | 30 s | | Field 5 | Threat Index (0–1) | 15 s | | Field 6 | Door State (0/1) | 15 s | | Status | System Health string | On change | ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Sensor Failure Modes & FMEA Table > [!NOTE] > **FMEA** = Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (Industry-standard reliability tool). | Sensor | Failure Mode | Effect | RPN (Risk Priority Number) | |---|---|---|---| | PIR | False trigger (heat source) | False alarm | **6** (S=2, O=3, D=1) | | MQ-2 | Sensor poisoning (silicon) | Permanent zero output | **12** (S=4, O=3, D=1) | | Reed Switch | Magnetic tampering | Bypass detection | **15** (S=5, O=3, D=1) | | Wi-Fi Module | Router outage | No cloud alert | **18** (S=3, O=6, D=1) | | Power Supply | Voltage drop | ESP32 brownout reset | **16** (S=4, O=4, D=1) | The RPN formula is: $$ \text{RPN} = S \times O \times D $$ where $S$ = Severity, $O$ = Occurrence, $D$ = Detection difficulty (each 1–10 scale). # 4.
