Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SENS["SENSOR LAYER (Perception)"] S1[PIR HC-SR501] S2[MQ-2 Gas Sensor] S3[Reed Switch Door] S4[DHT11 Temp Humidity] S5[ESP32 CAM Module] end subgraph CTRL["PROCESSING LAYER (Edge)"] ESP[ESP32 Microcontroller] ADC[12 bit ADC Converter] FSM[State Machine Logic] end subgraph NET["NETWORK LAYER (Wi Fi)"] ROUTER[Wi Fi Router 802.11n] MQTT[MQTT Broker Mosquitto] HTTP[HTTP REST API] end subgraph CLOUD["CLOUD LAYER"] TS[ThingSpeak Dashboard] BLYNK[Blynk Mobile App] TG[Telegram Bot Alert] FB[Firebase Realtime DB] end subgraph ACT["ACTUATION LAYER"] BUZ[Buzzer 85 dB] LEDR[Red LED Alarm] LEDG[Green LED Status] end S1 --> ESP S2 --> ADC S3 --> ESP S4 --> ESP S5 --> ESP ADC --> ESP ESP --> FSM FSM --> ESP ESP --> ROUTER ROUTER --> MQTT ROUTER --> HTTP MQTT --> TS MQTT --> BLYNK MQTT --> TG HTTP --> FB FSM --> BUZ FSM --> LEDR FSM --> LEDG ``` ## 4.2 Firmware State Machine Flow ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> INIT INIT --> WIFI_CONNECT : boot complete WIFI_CONNECT --> IDLE : WiFi OK WIFI_CONNECT --> WIFI_RETRY : WiFi FAIL WIFI_RETRY --> WIFI_CONNECT : after 5 sec IDLE --> ALERT : threatIndex >= 0.50 IDLE --> LOG_ONLY : 0.20 <= threatIndex < 0.50 LOG_ONLY --> IDLE : next cycle ALERT --> SENTINEL : after 30 sec SENTINEL --> IDLE : cooldown complete SENTINEL --> ALERT : re trigger within 10 sec ALERT --> [*] : user reset ``` ## 4.3 MQTT Publish/Subscribe Topology ```mermaid flowchart TB PUB1[ESP32 Sensor Node 1] PUB2[ESP32 Sensor Node 2] PUB3[Raspberry Pi Gateway] BROKER[(Mosquitto MQTT Broker
Broker Address 192.168.1.10
Port 1883)] SUB1[Mobile App Blynk] SUB2[Cloud ThingSpeak] SUB3[Telegram Bot] SUB4[Firebase Logger] PUB1 -->|home security pir
QoS 1| BROKER PUB2 -->|home security gas
QoS 1| BROKER PUB3 -->|home security cam
QoS 0| BROKER BROKER -->|subscribe +| SUB1 BROKER -->|subscribe +| SUB2 BROKER -->|subscribe +| SUB3 BROKER -->|subscribe +| SUB4 ``` ## 4.4 Data Flow Sequence Diagram ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant PIR as PIR Sensor participant ESP as ESP32 MCU participant BR as MQTT Broker participant CL as Cloud Dashboard participant USR as User Mobile PIR->>ESP: Digital HIGH detected ESP->>ESP: Compute threatIndex = 0.65 ESP->>ESP: Activate Buzzer GPIO 25 ESP->>BR: PUBLISH home/security/alert BR->>CL: Forward payload JSON BR->>USR: Push notification CL->>USR: Web dashboard update USR->>BR: PUBLISH home/security/ack BR->>ESP: Acknowledge received ESP->>ESP: Move to SENTINEL state ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]** **List any six sensors commonly used in an IoT-based home security system and state the physical quantity each one measures.** **Model Answer:** | # | Sensor | Physical Quantity Measured | |---|---|---| | 1 | **PIR (HC-SR501)** | Passive Infrared radiation (human motion) | | 2 | **MQ-2** | LPG / Methane / Smoke gas concentration | | 3 | **Magnetic Reed Switch** | Magnetic field (door/window open/close) | | 4 | **DHT11** | Air Temperature and Relative Humidity | | 5 | **LDR (Photoresistor)** | Ambient light intensity | | 6 | **ESP32-CAM (OV2640)** | Visible light (image/video capture) | | 7 | **Piezo Buzzer** | (Actuator) Acoustic alarm output | | 8 | **Flame Sensor (IR)** | Infrared emission from fire | *(Any six with correct physical quantity = 3 marks)* ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Differentiate between the MQTT and HTTP protocols in the context of IoT communication. State two advantages of MQTT over HTTP.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | MQTT | HTTP | |---|---|---| | Architecture | Publish/Subscribe | Request/Response | | Header size | **2 bytes (fixed)** | Hundreds of bytes | | Transport | TCP (port **1883**) | TCP (port 80) | | Power efficiency | **High** (low overhead) | Low | | Real-time push | Native (server-initiated) | Polling required | | Security | TLS via port **8883** | HTTPS / TLS | **Two advantages of MQTT:** 1. **Lightweight** – 2-byte fixed header makes it ideal for low-bandwidth, battery-powered IoT nodes. 2. **Asynchronous push** – Broker pushes data instantly to subscribers, avoiding client polling overhead. ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – Module Internal Choice > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry **14 marks** with optional internal choice. Solve both sub-parts fully to score full marks. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **"Design and implement an IoT-based smart home security system that detects motion, gas leakage, and door intrusion. The system should send real-time alerts to a mobile dashboard."** #### Part (a) – System Architecture & Hardware Design [7 Marks] **Model Solution:** **(i) Functional Requirements (1 mark):** - Detect unauthorized human motion (PIR). - Detect LPG/smoke leakage (MQ-2). - Detect door/window breach (Reed switch). - Local acoustic alarm (buzzer). - Remote mobile notification (Blynk/Telegram). **(ii) Hardware Components (3 marks):** | Component | Specification | Justification | |---|---|---| | ESP32 DevKit V1 | Dual-core, 240 MHz, Wi-Fi 802.11n | Built-in ADC, Wi-Fi, sufficient GPIO | | HC-SR501 PIR | 5V, 3–7m range, 3-pin | Industry-standard motion sensor | | MQ-2 | 5V, analog + digital output | Detects LPG, CO, smoke simultaneously | | Reed Switch | 3.3V, NO/NC, magnetic actuation | Reliable door breach detection | | Buzzer | 5V active, 85 dB | Loud audible alarm | | 5V/2A Adapter | SMPS regulated | Stable ESP32 power supply | **(iii) Circuit Topology (2 marks):** - PIR1 → GPIO 13, PIR2 → GPIO 12 (with 10 kΩ pull-down). - MQ-2 analog → GPIO 34 (ADC1_CH6 to avoid Wi-Fi conflict). - Reed switches → GPIO 14, 26 (with 100 nF hardware debounce). - Buzzer → GPIO 25 via NPN BC547 driver transistor. - Common GND rail; 3.3V for logic, 5V for sensors via Vin. **(iv) Power Budget Calculation (1 mark):** - ESP32 peak current: **240 mA** @ 3.3V. - PIR: **65 mA** × 2 = 130 mA @ 5V. - MQ-2 preheat: **150 mA** @ 5V. - Buzzer: **30 mA** @ 5V. - **Total ≈ 550 mA** → Use **5V/2A SMPS** (1000 mA headroom). #### Part (b) – Firmware Logic & Cloud Integration [7 Marks] **Model Solution:** **(i) Firmware Flow (3 marks):** ``` INITIALIZE SENSORS → CONNECT WI-FI → CONNECT MQTT BROKER ↓ LOOP: READ PIR + MQ-2 + REED → COMPUTE T(t) ↓ IF T(t) ≥ 0.50 → ACTIVATE BUZZER + PUBLISH MQTT (QoS 1) ELSE → PUBLISH TELEMETRY (QoS 0) ↓ REPEAT every 2 seconds ``` **(ii) Threat Index Computation (2 marks):** $$ T(t) = 0.30 \cdot M(t) + 0.35 \cdot G(t) + 0.20 \cdot R(t) + 0.15 \cdot F(t) $$ Where: - $M(t) = 1$ if PIR triggered, else $0$. - $G(t) = 1$ if $V_{MQ2} > V_{th}$, else $0$. - $R(t) = 1$ if reed switch open, else $0$. - $F(t) = 1$ if temp > 60 °C, else $0$. **(iii) Cloud MQTT Payload (1 mark):** JSON format published to topic `home/security/status` every 2 s; alert published to `home/security/alert` only on $T \geq 0.50$. **(iv) Blynk Mobile Dashboard (1 mark):** - Virtual Pin V0: Threat Index (Gauge widget 0–1). - V1: PIR Status (LED widget). - V2: Gas Level (Gauge widget in ppm). - V3: Door State (Label widget). - V4: Notification trigger on V0 > 0.50. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **"Explain the design of an IoT-based fire and gas leakage detection system. Discuss the sensors used, microcontroller selection, communication protocol, and cloud dashboard design."** #### Part (a) – Sensor Selection & Microcontroller Justification [7 Marks] **Model Solution:** **(i) Hazard Analysis (1 mark):** LPG (C₃H₈) is heavier than air → accumulates near floor. CO is colorless/odorless → requires electrochemical sensor. **(ii) Sensor Specifications (3 marks):** | Sensor | Target Gas | Sensitivity | Response Time | |---|---|---|---| | **MQ-2** | LPG, H₂, CH₄, CO | Rs (300 ppm LPG) = 1–5 kΩ | < **10 s** | | **MQ-7** | Carbon Monoxide | 10–300 ppm range | < **30 s** | | **MQ-135** | NH₃, NOₓ, Benzene | Air quality (VOCs) | < **15 s** | | **DHT11** | Temp/Humidity | ±2 °C, ±5% RH | **2 s** sampling | | **Flame Sensor (KY-026)** | IR 760–1100 nm | 60° detection cone | < **1 s** | **(iii) Microcontroller Selection (2 marks):** - **Chosen: ESP32-WROOM-32** (Xtensa LX6, 240 MHz, 520 KB SRAM). - **Rationale:** Built-in Wi-Fi 802.11 b/g/n, dual-core for parallel sensor + cloud tasks, 18× 12-bit ADC channels, hardware PWM for buzzer, ultra-low-power deep-sleep (**10 µA**) for battery operation. - **Rejected alternatives:** Arduino Uno (no Wi-Fi), Raspberry Pi (over-spec, high power). **(iv) Calibration Methodology (1 mark):** - MQ-2 preheat for **24 hours** before first calibration. - Set $R_L = 5\text{ kΩ}$, measure $R_s$ in clean air, compute $R_s/R_0$ ratio for ppm conversion via log-log curve. #### Part (b) – Communication Protocol & Cloud Dashboard [7 Marks] **Model Solution:** **(i) Protocol Justification (2 marks):** MQTT is chosen over HTTP because: - **Bandwidth efficiency** – 2-byte header vs 500+ bytes HTTP. - **Push-based alerts** – No client polling required. - **QoS guarantees** – Critical alerts use QoS 1 (at-least-once). - **Topic-based routing** – Multiple subscribers (mobile, cloud, logging) from one publish. **(ii) ThingSpeak Dashboard Design (2 marks):** - **Channel ID:** Private, with Write API Key in ESP32 firmware. - **Field 1:** LPG concentration (ppm) → Line Chart widget. - **Field 2:** Temperature → Gauge widget. - **Field 3:** Humidity → Numeric widget. - **Field 4:** Threat level (HIGH/MED/LOW) → String/LED widget. - **React App:** Triggers MATLAB visualization if Field 1 > 1000 ppm. **(iii) Telegram Bot Integration (2 marks):** - Create bot via **@BotFather**, obtain **HTTP API token**. - ESP32 sends HTTP POST to `https://api.telegram.org/bot/sendMessage` with chat_id and text. - On alert: message format → `"🚨 GAS LEAK ALERT! Sensor: MQ-2, PPM: 1200, Location: Kitchen, Time: 14:23 IST"`. **(iv) Reliability & Redundancy (1 mark):** - Watchdog timer: **3000 ms** – resets ESP32 if firmware hangs. - Local SD card logging: Logs every event with RTC timestamp. - Fail-safe: If Wi-Fi down for > 60 s, local buzzer still rings. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Pin Conflict:** Using ESP32 ADC2 pins (GPIO 0, 2, 4, 12–15, 25–27) with active Wi-Fi causes **ADC read errors**. Always use **ADC1 (GPIO 32–39)**. > 2. **MQ-2 Preheat:** Students forget the 24-hour preheat requirement → sensor reads garbage. **Mention this in viva.** > 3. **No Debounce:** Reed switch mechanical chatter causes 5–10 false triggers/minute. Always add **100 nF cap + 50 ms software debounce**. > 4. **Single Point of Failure:** Relying only on cloud notifications → if Wi-Fi fails, user is blind. Always include a **local buzzer** as primary alarm. > 5. **Missing Pull-down:** PIR outputs float when idle → always use **internal pull-down** on ESP32 GPIO input pins. > 6. **Cost Estimation Omission:** Examiners award marks for **component cost table + power budget** calculation. > 7. **No FMEA / Reliability Analysis:** KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes **failure analysis** – always include an FMEA table. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist – KTU Module 4 Project (Security Systems):** - ✅ **System = 4 Layers:** Sensors → Edge MCU → Network → Cloud/Actuator. - ✅ **Controller = ESP32** (preferred) for built-in Wi-Fi + dual-core. - ✅ **Communication = MQTT** (topic-based, 2-byte header, QoS 0/1/2). - ✅ **Cloud = ThingSpeak / Blynk / Firebase / Telegram Bot API.** - ✅ **Sensor trio = PIR + MQ-2 + Reed Switch** (minimum viable product). - ✅ **Pin Rule:** Use **ADC1 (GPIO 32–39)** for analog sensors to avoid Wi-Fi conflict. - ✅ **Threat Index formula:** $T(t) = \sum w_i \cdot d_i$ with $\sum w_i = 1$. - ✅ **Escalation:** $T < 0.20$ silent, $0.20 \leq T < 0.50$ log only, $T \geq 0.50$ ALARM. - ✅ **MQ-2 requires 24 h preheat** before reliable calibration. - ✅ **FMEA formula:** RPN = Severity × Occurrence × Detection. - ✅ **Power budget:** Calculate sum of all sensor currents; provide **30% headroom**. - ✅ **Local buzzer = MANDATORY** even if cloud is the primary notification. - ✅ **Watchdog timer** must be enabled in firmware for production reliability. - ✅ **Data format:** JSON with timestamp + deviceId for forensic logging. - ✅ **Cost target:** Aim for under **₹2,000** for a B.Tech prototype to score presentation marks. - ✅ **Documentation:** Always include **block diagram, circuit diagram, FMEA table, cost table** in the report.

topics.title: Project on Parking Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT504 - PROGRAMMING FOR INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Project on Parking Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **IoT-based Parking Sensor System** is an embedded cyber-physical system that integrates ultrasonic/acoustic ranging sensors with a microcontroller and wireless communication modules to detect, classify, and report the real-time occupancy status of a parking slot. As per the KTU 2024 syllabus, this project belongs to the *Smart City / Smart Infrastructure* application vertical of IoT, wherein physical sensor data is converted into structured digital telemetry, transmitted over a network layer (typically MQTT/HTTP over Wi-Fi), and rendered on a cloud dashboard or mobile interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A Parking Sensor is a **proximity-aware embedded node** that uses the *Time-of-Flight (ToF)* principle to measure the distance between a vehicle and a physical barrier, and forwards the processed information to a central decision-making unit (gateway or cloud) using standard IoT communication protocols.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bat and the Blind Cane"

Imagine you are blindfolded in a corridor. You clap your hands and listen for the echo. The faster the echo returns, the closer the wall is. A parking sensor works on the **exact same principle**:

- The **clap** → the **trigger pulse** sent by the ultrasonic transducer
- The **echo** → the **reflected ultrasonic wave** bouncing off the car
- Your **brain calculating the time gap** → the **microcontroller computing the distance**
- You **calling out the distance to a friend** → the **IoT module transmitting data to the cloud**

## 1.3 Core Components Vocabulary

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terms You Must Memorize for KTU Exam:**

| Term | Meaning | Role in Project |
|------|---------|-----------------|
| **HC-SR04** | Ultrasonic ranging module | Primary distance sensor (2 cm – 400 cm) |
| **ESP32 / ESP8266** | Wi-Fi enabled SoC | Acts as MCU + IoT transceiver |
| **MQTT** | Message Queuing Telemetry Transport | Lightweight IoT pub-sub protocol |
| **Blynk / ThingSpeak** | Cloud dashboard | Remote visualization |
| **ToF (Time-of-Flight)** | Distance measurement method | Foundation of ultrasonic sensing |
| **Trigger Pulse** | 10 µs digital HIGH input | Initiates ultrasonic burst |
| **Echo Pulse** | Variable-width digital HIGH output | Width ∝ distance to object |

## 1.4 Physical Constants to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **speed of sound in air at 20 °C** = **343 m/s** (≈ 0.0343 cm/µs). This constant is the heart of every parking sensor calculation.
>
> Also, remember the **factor of 2** — sound travels *to* the object AND *back*, so the measured time must be halved.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline (Step-by-Step Logic)

The parking sensor system can be decomposed into **six sequential logic blocks**. Each block performs a specific transformation:

1. **Triggering Block** — The MCU sends a **10 µs HIGH pulse** to the `TRIG` pin of the HC-SR04. The sensor internally generates **eight 40 kHz ultrasonic bursts**.

2. **Propagation Block** — The ultrasonic wavefront travels through air at the speed of sound $v_s \approx 343 \text{ m/s}$. It hits the nearest reflecting surface (typically a car's bumper or chassis).

3. **Reflection & Reception Block** — The reflected wavefront returns and strikes the receiver diaphragm of the HC-SR04. The `ECHO` pin is driven HIGH for a duration proportional to the round-trip time $t$.

4. **Time-Measuring Block** — The MCU uses `pulseIn()` (Arduino) or hardware timer (ESP32) to capture $t$ in microseconds with microsecond resolution.

5. **Distance Computation Block** — The MCU applies the ToF formula to convert time into distance in centimeters.

6. **Decision & Actuation Block** — The distance is thresholded:
   - $d < 15$ cm → **RED LED** + **Buzzer ON** (Critical / Stop)
   - $15 \le d < 40$ cm → **YELLOW LED** (Caution)
   - $d \ge 40$ cm → **GREEN LED** (Safe)
   
   Simultaneously, the value is published to the MQTT broker / Blynk cloud.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why 40 kHz?** — It is above human hearing (>20 kHz) and offers a good trade-off between attenuation in air and resolution for short ranges.
- **Why 10 µs trigger?** — The HC-SR04 datasheet mandates a minimum of 10 µs HIGH to reliably start the burst sequence.
- **Why a minimum delay between cycles?** — The sensor needs ~60 ms to complete one measurement cycle; otherwise, "echo collisions" cause erroneous readings.
- **Why publish via MQTT?** — HTTP is heavy; MQTT is **publish/subscribe**, runs over TCP port **1883**, and is optimized for low-bandwidth, high-latency networks.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear almost every KTU exam cycle!**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $d = \dfrac{v_s \cdot t}{2}$ | Distance from round-trip time | cm |
| 2 | $t = \dfrac{2d}{v_s}$ | Time taken for round trip | s |
| 3 | $v_s = 331 + 0.6T$ | Speed of sound at temperature $T$ °C | m/s |
| 4 | $d_{\text{cm}} = \dfrac{t_{\mu s}}{58}$ | Empirical short-form (HC-SR04 calibration) | cm |
| 5 | $d_{\text{inch}} = \dfrac{t_{\mu s}}{148}$ | Imperial calibration | inch |
| 6 | $f_{\text{sample}} \le \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{cycle}}} = \dfrac{1}{60 \text{ ms}} \approx 16.67$ | Maximum reliable sampling rate | Hz |
| 7 | $P_{\text{LED}} = V \cdot I$ | Power dissipated by indicator LED | W |
| 8 | $R_{\text{current-limit}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{supply}} - V_{f}}{I_{f}}$ | LED series resistor value | Ω |
| 9 | $\theta_{\text{cone}} = 15^{\circ}$ | Beam angle of HC-SR04 | degrees |
| 10 | $t_{\text{MQTT}} = \frac{L_{\text{payload}}}{B_{\text{link}}} + 2 \cdot t_{\text{prop}}$ | End-to-end MQTT latency | s |

> **Note on calibration:** The constants 58 and 148 in formulas (4) and (5) are derived from $v_s = 343$ m/s converted to cm/µs and halved, then inverted.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Parking sensor IoT systems are deployed in:
- **Smart Malls** — Real-time slot availability on mobile apps
- **Airport Multi-level Parking** — Automated gate integration
- **Smart City Roadside** — Finding the nearest available slot (Bayes-optimized routing)
- **Truck Fleet Yards** — Reverse-assist safety for large vehicles (ADAS Level 1)
- **Underground Basements** — Indoor localization for autonomous valet parking

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The ToF Distance Equation

The HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor emits a sound wave, which travels to an object, reflects, and returns. The MCU measures the **round-trip time** $t$. We need to derive the formula that converts this time into a physical distance $d$.

**Given:**
- Speed of sound in air at 20 °C: $v_s = 343 \text{ m/s}$
- Round-trip time: $t$ (in seconds, measured by MCU)
- One-way distance from sensor to object: $d$ (meters)

**Step 1 — Write the fundamental kinematic relation:**

In uniform motion, distance equals speed multiplied by time:

$$\text{Total distance travelled by wave} = v_s \cdot t$$

**Step 2 — Account for the round-trip geometry:**

The wave starts at the sensor, travels distance $d$ to the object, reflects, and travels distance $d$ back to the sensor. Therefore, the total distance is:

$$\text{Total distance} = d + d = 2d$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions:**

$$2d = v_s \cdot t$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $d$ (one-way distance):**

$$d = \frac{v_s \cdot t}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Convert units to centimeters (1 m = 100 cm) and microseconds (1 s = $10^6$ µs):**

$$d_{\text{cm}} = \frac{343 \times 100}{10^6} \cdot \frac{t_{\mu s}}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify the numerical constant:**

$$d_{\text{cm}} = \frac{34300}{2 \times 10^6} \cdot t_{\mu s} = 0.01715 \cdot t_{\mu s}$$

**Step 7 — Recognize the empirical inverse form:**

$$d_{\text{cm}} = \frac{t_{\mu s}}{58.31} \approx \frac{t_{\mu s}}{58}$$

This is the **famous "divide by 58"** rule used in every Arduino parking sensor sketch.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example

> **Problem:** A car is parked in front of a sensor. The MCU measures a pulse width of **2350 µs** on the ECHO pin. What is the distance to the car in centimeters and meters?

**Step 1 — Apply the empirical formula:**

$$d_{\text{cm}} = \frac{t_{\mu s}}{58} = \frac{2350}{58}$$

**Step 2 — Perform the division:**

$$d_{\text{cm}} \approx 40.52 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to meters:**

$$d_{\text{m}} = 0.4052 \text{ m} \approx 0.41 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check using the fundamental formula:**

$$d = \frac{343 \times 0.002350}{2} = \frac{0.80605}{2} = 0.4030 \text{ m} = 40.3 \text{ cm}$$

The small difference (~0.5%) is due to the empirical constant 58 being a rounded value of 58.31. In practice, students are expected to use **0.01715** for higher accuracy.

## 3.3 Temperature Compensation Derivation

The speed of sound varies linearly with air temperature:

$$v_s(T) = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T \quad \text{(m/s, where } T \text{ is in °C)}$$

If the ambient temperature is 35 °C (common in Kerala summers):

$$v_s = 331 + 0.6 \times 35 = 331 + 21 = 352 \text{ m/s}$$

The corrected distance equation becomes:

$$d = \frac{352 \cdot t}{2} = 176 \cdot t \quad \text{(m, with } t \text{ in s)}$$

In centimeters with $t$ in µs:

$$d_{\text{cm}} = \frac{352 \times 100}{2 \times 10^6} \cdot t_{\mu s} = 0.0176 \cdot t_{\mu s}$$

**Inference:** A 12 °C temperature change from the calibration point (20 °C) introduces approximately a **2.6% distance error** — a significant bias for precise ADAS applications.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | HC-SR04 (Ultrasonic) | IR Sensor (Infrared) | LiDAR | Camera + CV |
|---------|----------------------|----------------------|-------|-------------|
| Range | 2–400 cm | 10–80 cm | Up to 40 m | Variable |
| Accuracy at 30 cm | ±0.3 cm | ±1–2 cm | ±0.1 cm | ±0.5 cm |
| Cost | ₹80–120 | ₹50–80 | ₹3000+ | ₹1500+ |
| Works in dark | ✅ Yes | ⚠️ Partial | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| Works in sunlight | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Affected by temperature | ⚠️ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| Beam angle | 15° | Narrow cone | 0.5–2° | Field of view |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion for KTU Viva:** HC-SR04 offers the best **price-to-performance ratio** for indoor parking lots where ambient light and temperature are controlled. For outdoor smart-city deployments, LiDAR or vision-based systems are preferred.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Power Supply 5V DC] --> B[Microcontroller ESP32]
    C[HC-SR04 Ultrasonic Sensor] --> B
    D[Red LED Indicator] --> B
    E[Yellow LED Indicator] --> B
    F[Green LED Indicator] --> B
    G[Buzzer Piezo] --> B
    H[16x2 LCD Display] --> B
    B --> I[WiFi Module Built-in]
    I --> J[MQTT Broker Mosquitto]
    I --> K[Cloud Dashboard Blynk]
    I --> L[Mobile App]
    J --> M[Subscriber Node]
    K --> N[Analytic Dashboard]
    B --> O[Serial Monitor Debug]
```

## 4.2 Detailed Circuit Schematic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Sensor_Stage ["Sensor Acquisition Stage"]
        S1[HC-SR04 VCC to 5V] 
        S2[HC-SR04 GND to GND]
        S3[HC-SR04 TRIG to GPIO 5]
        S4[HC-SR04 ECHO via Voltage Divider to GPIO 18]
    end
    
    subgraph Controller_Stage ["Processing Stage ESP32"]
        C1[Trigger Pulse 10us]
        C2[pulseIn Echo Capture]
        C3[Distance Calculation]
        C4[Threshold Logic]
        C5[MQTT Publish]
    end
    
    subgraph Output_Stage ["Actuator Stage"]
        O1[Red LED GPIO 25]
        O2[Yellow LED GPIO 26]
        O3[Green LED GPIO 27]
        O4[Buzzer GPIO 32]
        O5[LCD SDA GPIO 21]
        O6[LCD SCL GPIO 22]
    end
    
    S3 --> C1
    S4 --> C2
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> O1
    C4 --> O2
    C4 --> O3
    C4 --> O4
    C4 --> C5
    C4 --> O5
    C4 --> O6
```

## 4.3 Software Layered Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Application Layer Dashboard] --> L2[Middleware MQTT Broker]
    L2 --> L3[Network Layer WiFi TCP IP]
    L3 --> L4[Edge Layer ESP32 MCU]
    L4 --> L5[Perception Layer HC-SR04 IR Camera]
    
    L1 -.Feedback.-> L2
    L2 -.Commands.-> L3
    L3 -.Telemetry.-> L4
    L4 -.Raw Data.-> L5
```

## 4.4 State Machine for Distance Classification

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Init
    Init --> Measure: Setup Complete
    Measure --> Safe: distance >= 40 cm
    Measure --> Caution: 15 <= distance < 40
    Measure --> Critical: distance < 15
    Safe --> Measure: 100 ms later
    Caution --> Measure: 100 ms later
    Critical --> Measure: 100 ms later
    Critical --> Critical: Object Still Close
```

## 4.5 Pin Connection Table (ESP32 Reference Design)

| Component | Pin Label | ESP32 GPIO | Notes |
|-----------|-----------|------------|-------|
| HC-SR04 | VCC | 5V | Use external 5V if USB weak |
| HC-SR04 | GND | GND | Common ground mandatory |
| HC-SR04 | TRIG | GPIO 5 | Output from MCU |
| HC-SR04 | ECHO | GPIO 18 | **Voltage divider required** (5V → 3.3V) |
| Red LED | Anode → 220 Ω → GPIO 25 | GPIO 25 | Cathode to GND |
| Yellow LED | Anode → 220 Ω → GPIO 26 | GPIO 26 | Cathode to GND |
| Green LED | Anode → 220 Ω → GPIO 27 | GPIO 27 | Cathode to GND |
| Buzzer | + | GPIO 32 | Active buzzer with NPN driver |
| LCD (I2C) | SDA | GPIO 21 | 4.7 kΩ pull-up to 3.3V |
| LCD (I2C) | SCL | GPIO 22 | 4.7 kΩ pull-up to 3.3V |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake (costs marks):** Connecting the HC-SR04 ECHO pin *directly* to an ESP32 GPIO. The ECHO line outputs 5V, but ESP32 GPIOs are **3.3V tolerant only**. Always use a voltage divider (1 kΩ + 2 kΩ) or a level shifter.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the Time-of-Flight (ToF) principle as applied to ultrasonic parking sensors. Why is the speed of sound in air treated as a constant in most Arduino implementations?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Time-of-Flight (ToF) principle** is a non-contact ranging technique in which a wave pulse is emitted, reflected by a target object, and the round-trip time $t$ is measured to compute the distance $d$ as $d = \dfrac{v_s \cdot t}{2}$, where $v_s$ is the wave propagation speed.

The speed of sound in air ($v_s \approx 343$ m/s at 20 °C) is treated as a constant in Arduino implementations because:
1. Indoor parking environments have **stable temperatures** (within ±2 °C), introducing <1% error.
2. Real-time computation requires **deterministic timing** — variable compensation adds floating-point overhead unsuitable for 8-bit MCUs.
3. The **empirical calibration constant 58** is derived assuming $v_s = 343$ m/s, so the formula $d = t/58$ holds only under this assumption.

*(1 mark: definition, 1 mark: derivation link, 1 mark: justification)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any three advantages of using MQTT over HTTP for transmitting parking sensor data in an IoT system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Lightweight Protocol** — MQTT has a fixed 2-byte header vs. HTTP's variable headers, reducing payload overhead for tiny sensor packets (e.g., 12-byte distance value).

2. **Publish/Subscribe Model with Persistent Connection** — Once connected via TCP, the broker pushes new readings instantly; HTTP requires repeated polling.

3. **Quality of Service (QoS) Levels** — MQTT offers three QoS levels (0, 1, 2) ensuring message delivery guarantees critical for safety applications like collision alerts.

4. **Low Power Consumption** — Suitable for battery-operated nodes; HTTP keep-alive cycles drain ESP32 batteries rapidly.

*(1 mark each for any three correct advantages)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of an IoT-based smart parking system** using ultrasonic sensors. Clearly identify the perception, network, and application layers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An HC-SR04 sensor returns an ECHO pulse width of **1875 µs**. Calculate the distance to the obstacle. If the ambient temperature is **40 °C**, recalculate the distance and comment on the error. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

The IoT-based smart parking system follows a **three-layer architecture** as mandated by the KTU 2024 syllabus:

**1. Perception Layer (Sensor + Actuator Layer):**
- **HC-SR04 Ultrasonic Sensor** mounted on the ceiling above each parking slot
- **IR Break-beam sensors** as backup to detect vehicle presence
- **RGB LED Indicators** (Red/Yellow/Green) for local visual feedback
- **Piezo Buzzer** for audio alert on critical proximity
- **ESP32-CAM** (optional) for license plate capture

**2. Network Layer (Communication Layer):**
- **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11 b/g/n)** — The ESP32 connects to a local router
- **MQTT Protocol over TCP port 1883** — Each sensor publishes to topic `/parking/slot/{id}/distance`
- **TLS Encryption (port 8883)** for production security
- **Gateway/Router** bridges to the internet

**3. Application Layer (Cloud + User Interface):**
- **MQTT Broker (Mosquitto / HiveMQ Cloud)** receives and routes messages
- **Node-RED / Blynk / ThingSpeak** dashboard renders real-time slot maps
- **Mobile App (Flutter / Blynk IoT)** shows nearest free slot
- **Database (InfluxDB / Firebase)** for historical analytics

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Perception_Layer
        P1[HC-SR04 Sensors] 
        P2[LED Indicators] 
        P3[Buzzer]
    end
    subgraph Network_Layer
        N1[ESP32 WiFi]
        N2[MQTT Broker]
    end
    subgraph Application_Layer
        A1[Cloud Dashboard]
        A2[Mobile App]
        A3[Database]
    end
    P1 --> N1
    P2 --> N1
    P3 --> N1
    N1 --> N2
    N2 --> A1
    N2 --> A2
    N2 --> A3
```

*(2 marks: layer names, 2 marks: components, 2 marks: protocol, 1 mark: diagram)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Use the standard empirical formula at 20 °C:**

$$d_1 = \frac{t}{58} = \frac{1875}{58}$$

$$d_1 \approx 32.33 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the temperature-corrected speed of sound formula at 40 °C:**

$$v_s(40) = 331 + 0.6 \times 40 = 331 + 24 = 355 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to cm/µs:**

$$v_s = 355 \times \frac{100}{10^6} = 0.0355 \text{ cm/µs}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the corrected distance:**

$$d_2 = \frac{v_s \cdot t}{2} = \frac{0.0355 \times 1875}{2} = \frac{66.5625}{2} = 33.28 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the percentage error:**

$$\text{Error} = \frac{\vert d_2 - d_1 \vert}{d_2} \times 100 = \frac{\vert 33.28 - 32.33 \vert}{33.28} \times 100$$

$$\text{Error} \approx 2.85\%$$

**Comment:**
The uncompensated formula under-reads by approximately 0.95 cm. While this is acceptable for **general parking** (a 32 cm vs 33 cm distinction), it is **significant for ADAS reverse-assist** where ±1 cm precision is mandated. Hence, production parking IoT systems incorporate **DHT22 temperature sensors** and apply real-time compensation.

*(1 mark: standard formula, 1 mark: temperature formula, 1 mark: substitution, 1 mark: error computation, 1 mark: 33.28 cm answer, 1 mark: comment, 1 mark: conclusion)*

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the **working principle of the HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor** with a timing diagram. How is the distance calculated from the echo pulse width? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design an **Arduino/ESP32 code skeleton** for a parking sensor that:
- Triggers the HC-SR04 every 100 ms
- Computes distance
- Activates RED LED + Buzzer if distance < 15 cm, YELLOW LED if 15–40 cm, GREEN LED if > 40 cm
- Publishes the value to a Blynk virtual pin V0

State the libraries used and explain the role of `pulseIn()`. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Working Principle:**

The HC-SR04 has **two ultrasonic transducers** — a transmitter (T) and a receiver (R) — plus an onboard STM8 microcontroller.

**Phase 1 — Triggering:**
The MCU sends a 10 µs HIGH pulse to the TRIG pin. Upon detecting this rising edge, the HC-SR04 internally fires **eight 40 kHz ultrasonic bursts** from the transmitter.

**Phase 2 — Wave Propagation:**
The 40 kHz burst travels through air at $v_s = 343$ m/s. It hits a reflecting surface (vehicle) and scatters.

**Phase 3 — Echo Reception:**
A portion of the reflected wave returns to the receiver. The onboard circuit amplifies this signal and drives the ECHO pin HIGH. The ECHO pin remains HIGH for a duration **exactly equal to the round-trip time** of the ultrasonic wave.

**Phase 4 — Measurement:**
The host MCU measures the ECHO pulse width using `pulseIn(ECHO, HIGH)`, which returns $t$ in microseconds.

**Timing Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TRIG_Signal
        T1[LOW Idle] 
        T2[HIGH 10 us Trigger Pulse]
        T3[LOW Wait for Echo]
    end
    
    subgraph ECHO_Signal
        E1[LOW Waiting] 
        E2[HIGH for duration t us]
        E3[LOW Cycle Complete]
    end
    
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3
    E1 --> E2
    E2 --> E3
```

**Distance Calculation:**

$$d = \frac{v_s \cdot t}{2} = 0.01715 \cdot t_{\mu s} \text{ cm}$$

The factor of 2 is critical — the sound wave traverses the path **twice**.

*(2 marks: phases, 2 marks: timing diagram, 2 marks: formula, 1 mark: factor of 2 explanation)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Libraries Required:**

```cpp
#include <WiFi.h>          // ESP32 WiFi connectivity
#include <BlynkSimpleEsp32.h> // Blynk IoT platform
#include <NewPing.h>       // Optimized HC-SR04 library
```

**Complete Code Skeleton:**

```cpp
#define TRIG_PIN 5
#define ECHO_PIN 18
#define RED_LED  25
#define YEL_LED  26
#define GRN_LED  27
#define BUZZER   32
#define MAX_DIST 200
#define SOUND_SPEED 0.01715  // cm/usec

NewPing sonar(TRIG_PIN, ECHO_PIN, MAX_DIST);

char auth[] = "YourBlynkAuthToken";
char ssid[] = "YourWiFiName";
char pass[] = "YourWiFiPassword";

unsigned long lastTrigger = 0;
const int INTERVAL = 100; // 100 ms sampling

void setup() {
    pinMode(RED_LED, OUTPUT);
    pinMode(YEL_LED, OUTPUT);
    pinMode(GRN_LED, OUTPUT);
    pinMode(BUZZER, OUTPUT);
    Serial.begin(115200);
    Blynk.begin(auth, ssid, pass);
}

void loop() {
    Blynk.run();
    if (millis() - lastTrigger >= INTERVAL) {
        lastTrigger = millis();
        delay(50);  // HC-SR04 stabilization
        unsigned int t = sonar.ping();  // returns time in us
        float distance = t * SOUND_SPEED;  // cm
        
        // Reset all indicators
        digitalWrite(RED_LED, LOW);
        digitalWrite(YEL_LED, LOW);
        digitalWrite(GRN_LED, LOW);
        digitalWrite(BUZZER, LOW);
        
        // Threshold logic
        if (distance < 15.0) {
            digitalWrite(RED_LED, HIGH);
            digitalWrite(BUZZER, HIGH);
        } else if (distance <= 40.0) {
            digitalWrite(YEL_LED, HIGH);
        } else {
            digitalWrite(GRN_LED, HIGH);
        }
        
        // Publish to Blynk
        Blynk.virtualWrite(V0, distance);
        Serial.print("Distance: ");
        Serial.print(distance);
        Serial.println(" cm");
    }
}
```

**Role of `pulseIn()`:**

The `pulseIn(pin, value)` function **pauses program execution** and measures the duration (in microseconds) for which the given pin remains at the specified logic level. In the parking sensor context, `pulseIn(ECHO_PIN, HIGH)` blocks until the ECHO pin goes HIGH, then waits for it to go LOW, and returns the elapsed time — which is precisely the round-trip time $t$. This is then halved and multiplied by $v_s$ to yield distance.

The `NewPing` library used above is an optimized replacement that returns time directly via `sonar.ping()` in microseconds, eliminating the blocking delay of the default `pulseIn()`.

*(1 mark: libraries, 2 marks: trigger logic, 2 marks: threshold logic, 1 mark: Blynk publish, 1 mark: pulseIn explanation)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the ToF formula → -2 marks
> 2. **Wrong unit conversion** (using 343 m/s directly without converting to cm/µs) → -1 mark
> 3. **Confusing TRIG and ECHO pin roles** in the timing diagram → -2 marks
> 4. **Not mentioning temperature compensation** in long answers → -1 mark
> 5. **Drawing block diagrams without labeling layers** (Perception/Network/Application) → -2 marks
> 6. **Writing HTTP code in place of MQTT** when asked for IoT — KTU specifically tests the protocol understanding

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Speed of sound** at 20 °C = **343 m/s** = **0.0343 cm/µs** → divide by 2 for one-way
- ✅ **Empirical formula**: $d_{cm} = t_{\mu s} / 58$
- ✅ **HC-SR04 Range**: 2 cm to 400 cm, Beam angle: 15°, Frequency: 40 kHz
- ✅ **Trigger pulse**: Minimum 10 µs HIGH on TRIG pin
- ✅ **Sampling period**: ≥ 60 ms (max ~16 Hz)
- ✅ **Voltage divider mandatory** for ECHO pin on 3.3V MCUs
- ✅ **Three layers of IoT**: Perception → Network → Application
- ✅ **MQTT port**: 1883 (plaintext) / 8883 (TLS) over TCP
- ✅ **Blynk Virtual Pin V0–V255** used for data publishing
- ✅ **Threshold logic**: <15 cm Critical / 15–40 cm Caution / >40 cm Safe
- ✅ **Temperature correction**: $v_s = 331 + 0.6T$ (m/s)
- ✅ **Libraries**: `WiFi.h`, `PubSubClient.h`, `BlynkSimpleEsp32.h`, `NewPing.h`
- ✅ **Pull-up resistors** (4.7 kΩ) required for I2C LCD on ESP32
- ✅ **Common ground** mandatory between sensor, MCU, and actuators

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Project on Parking Sensors | PBCIT504**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project on Controlling Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCIT504 - PROGRAMMING FOR INTERNET OF THINGS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Project on Controlling Motor ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) An **IoT-based Motor Control Project** is an embedded system application in which a microcontroller (such as **ESP32**, **NodeMCU (ESP8266)**, or **Arduino UNO** with a Wi-Fi shield) is interfaced with a motor driver circuit to regulate the **speed**, **direction**, and **ON/OFF state** of an electric motor through a network-accessible interface (Web Dashboard, Mobile App, or MQTT Broker). The system follows the canonical **Sense → Process → Actuate → Communicate** paradigm of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS), where the motor becomes the *actuator node* in a distributed IoT network. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus:** The emphasis is on **programming the microcontroller**, **interfacing the motor driver**, and **establishing bidirectional cloud communication** — not on heavy electrical machine theory. Examiners look for code quality, protocol understanding, and pin-mapping logic. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Garage Gate" Imagine a **remote-controlled garage gate**: - You press a button on your phone → the gate opens/closes (motor rotates) - The gate knows when it is fully open (sensor feedback) - If your phone loses internet, the gate still responds to a local switch (fallback control) The IoT motor control project is the **digital twin** of this idea, applied to: | Real World | IoT Project Equivalent | |---|---| | Remote control | Mobile App / Web Dashboard | | Motor physically moving gate | DC / Servo / Stepper Motor | | Manual override switch | Physical push-button (GPIO) | | Indicator light | LED + Serial Monitor feedback | | Gate position sensor | IR sensor / Encoder | ### 1.3 Types of Motors Used in IoT Projects | Motor Type | Control Variable | Typical Driver | Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | **DC Motor** | Speed (PWM) + Direction | L298N, L293D | Fans, pumps, wheels | | **Servo Motor (SG90)** | Angular position (0°–180°) | Direct PWM pin | Robotic arm, door lock | | **Stepper Motor (28BYJ-48)** | Precise step angle | ULN2003, A4988 | 3D printer, CNC | | **BLDC Motor** | Speed + Commutation | ESC module | Drones, EV | ### 1.4 Key Project Objectives (Measurable) - **Latency:** End-to-end command response ≤ **500 ms** on local Wi-Fi. - **Reliability:** Maintain control even if cloud drops (local fallback). - **Security:** Use **MQTT with TLS** or **HTTP Basic Auth** (avoid plain text credentials). - **Scalability:** Architecture should allow adding more motors with minimal firmware changes. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever a question mentions "IoT project", always describe it using a **3-layer architecture** — *Perception Layer (sensors/motor), Network Layer (Wi-Fi/MQTT), Application Layer (dashboard/app)*. This phrase alone fetches 1–2 marks. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 System Architecture — Layered View The motor control IoT project is broken into **three functional layers** (mandatory for any KTU 14-mark question): **Layer 1 — Perception / Hardware Layer** - Microcontroller (NodeMCU / ESP32) - Motor + Driver (L298N) - Power supply (typically 5V/2A for logic, 12V for motor) - Feedback sensors (optional: current sensor ACS712, encoder, IR) **Layer 2 — Network / Communication Layer** - Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11 b/g/n) at **2.4 GHz** - Protocol choice: - **HTTP/REST** → simple, browser-based - **MQTT** → lightweight, ideal for constrained devices (uses publish/subscribe over TCP port **1883**, TLS on **8883**) - **WebSocket** → real-time bi-directional - IoT Platform: **Blynk, ThingSpeak, Firebase, AWS IoT Core** **Layer 3 — Application Layer** - Web Dashboard (HTML/JS hosted on ESP or external server) - Mobile App (Blynk / MIT App Inventor) - Voice control (Google Assistant via IFTTT webhook) ### 2.2 Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) — The Core of Speed Control > [!NOTE] > **Most tested concept in Module 4.** KTU frequently asks: *"How does Arduino control DC motor speed?"* — the answer is **PWM**. **Working Principle:** A DC motor's speed is approximately proportional to the **average voltage** across its terminals. Since microcontrollers cannot output analog voltages, they simulate it using **PWM** — a square wave where the *duty cycle* is varied. - **Duty Cycle** = (T_on / T_period) × 100 % - **Average Voltage** = V_supply × (Duty Cycle / 100) For ESP8266/NodeMCU: - PWM frequency: default **1 kHz**, can be set up to **1 kHz–1 MHz** via `analogWriteFreq()` - Resolution: **10-bit** (0–1023) on ESP8266, **8-bit** (0–255) on Arduino UNO - PWM-capable pins on NodeMCU: **D1, D2, D3, D4** (all GPIOs except D0) ### 2.3 H-Bridge Motor Driver Theory (L298N) A DC motor needs an **H-bridge** to run in both directions. The **L298N** is a dual H-bridge IC that accepts **logic-level inputs (5V)** from a microcontroller and switches a **higher voltage motor supply (up to 46V)**. **Truth Table for One Channel (Motor A):** | IN1 | IN2 | ENA | Motor Behaviour | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | 0 | x | Stop (coast) | | 1 | 0 | 1 | Rotate Forward | | 0 | 1 | 1 | Rotate Reverse | | 1 | 1 | 1 | Brake (fast stop) | | x | x | 0 | Free run (PWM = 0) | By applying **PWM to the ENA pin**, speed is controlled while direction is set by IN1/IN2 logic levels. ### 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet Table > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorize this table — it covers 90 % of numerical/factual questions. | Concept | Formula / Value | Unit | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | PWM Duty Cycle | $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{period}} \times 100$ | % | $T_{period} = \dfrac{1}{f_{PWM}}$ | | Average Motor Voltage | $V_{avg} = V_{supply} \times \dfrac{D}{100}$ | V | Linear approximation | | Motor RPM (no load) | $N \approx K_v \times V_{avg}$ | RPM | $K_v$ is motor constant | | MQTT QoS Levels | 0 / 1 / 2 | — | At most once / At least once / Exactly once | | HTTP Response Code (Success) | **200 OK** | — | 404 = Not Found, 500 = Server Error | | NodeMCU ADC Range | 0 – 3.3 V → 0 – 1023 | — | 10-bit resolution | | Arduino UNO PWM Range | 0 – 255 | — | 8-bit, $\Delta V \approx 19.6$ mV/step | | Wi-Fi Frequency Band | 2.4 GHz | GHz | ESP does NOT support 5 GHz | | L298N Logic Current | $< 36$ mA | mA | Per channel | | L298N Motor Current (peak) | 2 A (continuous) | A | With heatsink | | Blynk Virtual Pin | V0 – V255 | — | Used to send commands from app | | ESP8266 Operating Voltage | 3.3 V | V | Applying 5V damages the chip | ### 2.5 Real-World Utility - **Industry 4.0:** Smart conveyor belts, automated valves. - **Agriculture:** Remotely controlled irrigation pumps. - **Healthcare:** Motorized patient beds, ventilator blowers. - **Smart Homes:** Curtain openers, exhaust fans, smart locks. - **Robotics:** Differential-drive wheeled robots. ## 3. Step-by-Step Implementation & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > This is a **complete end-to-end project walkthrough**. Examine it block by block. ### 3.1 Hardware Bill of Materials (BoM) | # | Component | Quantity | Approx. Cost (INR) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | NodeMCU ESP8266 | 1 | ₹250 | | 2 | L298N Motor Driver Module | 1 | ₹150 | | 3 | DC Gear Motor (BO / 100 RPM) | 1 | ₹120 | | 4 | 12V / 2A DC Adapter | 1 | ₹180 | | 5 | Breadboard + Jumper Wires | 1 set | ₹100 | | 6 | 1N4007 Flyback Diode (optional) | 2 | ₹10 | | 7 | 220Ω Resistor + LED (status) | 1 | ₹5 | ### 3.2 Pin Mapping (NodeMCU ↔ L298N ↔ Motor) | NodeMCU Pin | L298N Pin | Function | |---|---|---| | **D1 (GPIO5)** | IN1 | Direction bit 1 | | **D2 (GPIO4)** | IN2 | Direction bit 2 | | **D3 (GPIO0)** | ENA | PWM (Speed) | | **Vin / 5V** | 5V (logic) | Power for IC | | **GND** | GND | Common ground | | External 12V | +12V terminal | Motor supply | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget the **common ground** between NodeMCU and the external 12V supply's GND. Without it, the L298N inputs float and the motor behaves erratically. ### 3.3 Firmware Development (Arduino IDE / C++) The following code demonstrates a **Wi-Fi + Blynk-controlled DC motor** with manual fallback and live feedback. **Step 1 — Include libraries and define credentials:** ```cpp #include #include // Wi-Fi credentials char ssid[] = "YourWiFiName"; char pass[] = "YourWiFiPassword"; // Blynk auth token (from Blynk app / web console) char auth[] = "YourBlynkAuthToken"; // Pin definitions (NodeMCU pin numbers) #define IN1 D1 // Direction bit 1 #define IN2 D2 // Direction bit 2 #define ENA D3 // PWM speed pin #define BTN D5 // Physical push-button (local fallback) #define LED D6 // Status LED int speedVal = 0; // 0 – 1023 for ESP8266 int direction = 0; // 0 = stop, 1 = forward, 2 = reverse ``` **Step 2 — Setup function:** ```cpp void setup() { pinMode(IN1, OUTPUT); pinMode(IN2, OUTPUT); pinMode(ENA, OUTPUT); pinMode(BTN, INPUT_PULLUP); pinMode(LED, OUTPUT); analogWriteFreq(1000); // 1 kHz PWM — sweet spot for DC motors analogWriteRange(1023); // 10-bit resolution Serial.begin(115200); Blynk.begin(auth, ssid, pass); // Auto-connects Wi-Fi + Blynk cloud // Default motor state: STOPPED stopMotor(); } ``` **Step 3 — Main loop with non-blocking logic:** ```cpp void loop() { Blynk.run(); // Handles Blynk cloud events // Local push-button override (fallback if cloud drops) if (digitalRead(BTN) == LOW) { direction = (direction == 1) ? 2 : 1; // Toggle direction applyMotor(direction, 800); // Run at 78% speed delay(300); // Software debounce } } ``` **Step 4 — Motor control helper functions (the *core* logic):** ```cpp void applyMotor(int dir, int pwm) { speedVal = constrain(pwm, 0, 1023); switch (dir) { case 1: // FORWARD digitalWrite(IN1, HIGH); digitalWrite(IN2, LOW); analogWrite(ENA, speedVal); digitalWrite(LED, HIGH); break; case 2: // REVERSE digitalWrite(IN1, LOW); digitalWrite(IN2, HIGH); analogWrite(ENA, speedVal); digitalWrite(LED, HIGH); break; default: // STOP stopMotor(); } // Push telemetry to Blynk virtual pin V1 Blynk.virtualWrite(V1, speedVal); } void stopMotor() { digitalWrite(IN1, LOW); digitalWrite(IN2, LOW); analogWrite(ENA, 0); digitalWrite(LED, LOW); Blynk.virtualWrite(V1, 0); } ``` **Step 5 — Blynk app widget handlers (called when user moves slider / presses button):** ```cpp // Slider on V0 (0 – 1023) controls speed BLYNK_WRITE(V0) { int pwm = param.asInt(); applyMotor(direction, pwm); } // Button on V2 (momentary) sets direction = FORWARD BLYNK_WRITE(V2) { direction = 1; applyMotor(direction, speedVal); } // Button on V3 (momentary) sets direction = REVERSE BLYNK_WRITE(V3) { direction = 2; applyMotor(direction, speedVal); } // Button on V4 (momentary) stops motor BLYNK_WRITE(V4) { direction = 0; stopMotor(); } ``` ### 3.4 Mathematical Analysis of PWM Output Let us analytically derive the **average voltage** seen by the motor. $$ V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_{period}} \int_{0}^{T_{on}} V_{supply} \, dt $$ $$ V_{avg} = V_{supply} \times \frac{T_{on}}{T_{period}} $$ Since $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{period}}$: $$ \boxed{V_{avg} = V_{supply} \times D} $$ **Example:** If $V_{supply} = 12\text{ V}$ and `analogWrite(ENA, 512)` is executed on a 10-bit NodeMCU: $$ D = \frac{512}{1023} \approx 0.500 $$ $$ V_{avg} = 12 \times 0.500 = 6.0 \text{ V} $$ The motor therefore "sees" an effective **6 V**, even though the supply is 12 V. Hence speed ≈ 50 % of no-load RPM. ### 3.5 Alternate Implementation — Pure HTTP Web Server (No Cloud) For **offline / local-network** control, the NodeMCU can host its own web page: ```cpp #include ESP8266WebServer server(80); const char index_html[] PROGMEM = R"rawliteral( IoT Motor Control

Motor Control Panel

)rawliteral"; void setup() { pinMode(IN1, OUTPUT); pinMode(IN2, OUTPUT); pinMode(ENA, OUTPUT); WiFi.begin(ssid, pass); while (WiFi.status() != WL_CONNECTED) delay(500); server.on("/", [](){ server.send(200, "text/html", index_html); }); server.on("/forward", [](){ applyMotor(1, 800); server.send(200, "text/plain", "OK FWD"); }); server.on("/reverse", [](){ applyMotor(2, 800); server.send(200, "text/plain", "OK REV"); }); server.on("/stop", [](){ stopMotor(); server.send(200, "text/plain", "STOPPED"); }); server.begin(); } void loop() { server.handleClient(); } ``` > [!TIP] > **Why this is asked in exams:** This block demonstrates **RESTful endpoint design** — examiners love seeing `/forward`, `/reverse`, `/stop` as semantic URLs returning HTTP 200 responses. ### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Protocol to Use? | Criteria | HTTP | MQTT | WebSocket | |---|---|---|---| | Bandwidth | High | Very Low | Medium | | Real-time | No (polling) | Yes (push) | Yes | | Browser support | Native | Needs JS lib | Native | | Code complexity | Low | Medium | Medium | | Best for KTU project | Quick demos | Industrial | Live dashboards | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Complete System Architecture (Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph APP["APPLICATION LAYER"] UI["Web Dashboard / Blynk App / Voice Assistant"] end subgraph NET["NETWORK LAYER"] WIFI["Wi-Fi Router 2.4 GHz"] PROTOCOL["Protocol: HTTP or MQTT over TCP"] end subgraph PER["PERCEPTION / HARDWARE LAYER"] MCU["NodeMCU ESP8266 Microcontroller"] DRIVER["L298N H-Bridge Driver"] MOTOR["DC Motor 12V"] FB["Feedback Sensor: Current / RPM Encoder"] end UI -->|User Command: Speed Direction| WIFI WIFI -->|Encrypted TCP Packet| PROTOCOL PROTOCOL -->|Parsed JSON or MQTT Payload| MCU MCU -->|PWM Signal on ENA Pin| DRIVER MCU -->|Logic HIGH or LOW on IN1 IN2| DRIVER DRIVER -->|Switched 12V Power| MOTOR MOTOR -->|Mechanical Rotation| FB FB -->|Analog or Digital Read| MCU MCU -->|Telemetry via MQTT Publish| WIFI WIFI -->|Status Update| UI ``` ### 4.2 Motor Direction State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> STOPPED STOPPED --> FORWARD: command FWD received STOPPED --> REVERSE: command REV received FORWARD --> STOPPED: command STOP received REVERSE --> STOPPED: command STOP received FORWARD --> REVERSE: brake then reverse REVERSE --> FORWARD: brake then forward FORWARD --> FORWARD: speed slider changed REVERSE --> REVERSE: speed slider changed FORWARD --> FAULT: overcurrent detected REVERSE --> FAULT: overcurrent detected FAULT --> STOPPED: reset by user ``` ### 4.3 Firmware Execution Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD BOOT["Boot: setup runs once"] --> WIFI_CONN["Connect to Wi-Fi SSID"] WIFI_CONN --> CLOUD_CONN["Connect to Blynk or MQTT Broker"] CLOUD_CONN --> LISTEN["loop: Listen for Blynk Virtual Pin Events"] LISTEN --> CHECK_BTN{"Physical Button Pressed?"} CHECK_BTN -->|Yes| TOGGLE_DIR["Toggle Direction Variable"] TOGGLE_DIR --> APPLY["applyMotor: Set IN1 IN2 and PWM on ENA"] CHECK_BTN -->|No| LISTEN APPLY --> TELEMETRY["Send speed back to cloud via virtualWrite"] TELEMETRY --> LISTEN ``` ### 4.4 Block Diagram — Hardware Interconnection ```mermaid flowchart LR NODE["NodeMCU ESP8266"] -->|D1 IN1| L298["L298N Driver"] NODE -->|D2 IN2| L298 NODE -->|D3 ENA PWM| L298 PWR12["12V DC Adapter"] -->|+12V| L298 PWR12 -->|GND Common| NODE L298 -->|OUT1 OUT2| MOTOR["DC Motor"] L298 -->|5V Logic| NODE NODE -->|D5 BTN| PUSHBTN["Push Button to GND"] NODE -->|D6 LED| STATUSLED["Status LED to GND via 220 ohm"] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Explain the role of an H-Bridge motor driver in an IoT-based DC motor control project. Why is the L298N preferred for low-power IoT prototypes?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **H-Bridge** is a circuit topology using four switching elements (transistors/MOSFETs) arranged in an "H" shape around the motor. It allows **bidirectional current flow**, enabling the motor to rotate in **both clockwise and counter-clockwise directions** using only logic-level signals from a microcontroller, which cannot directly source motor current. **Why L298N is preferred (1 mark):** 1. Accepts **logic-level inputs (5 V TTL)** directly from ESP8266/Arduino. 2. Can switch **higher voltages (up to 46 V)** and currents **up to 2 A continuous** per channel. 3. Has **two channels** — controls two motors independently. 4. **Built-in flyback diodes** protect against back-EMF. 5. **ENA pin** accepts PWM, enabling speed control in the same module. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Differentiate between HTTP and MQTT communication protocols in the context of an IoT motor control system. Which is more suitable and why?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | HTTP (REST) | MQTT | |---|---|---| | Architecture | Request/Response | Publish/Subscribe | | Header size | Large (overhead) | Tiny (2 bytes fixed header) | | Power consumption | High | Very low | | Best for | Browser-based UI | Sensor-actuator telemetry | | Real-time push | No (needs polling) | Yes (broker pushes) | **Conclusion (1 mark):** For IoT motor control, **MQTT is more suitable** because it is **lightweight, supports push-based real-time control, uses minimal bandwidth**, and works reliably over unreliable Wi-Fi. However, **HTTP is preferred for quick browser demos** where no broker setup is desired. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of an IoT-based DC motor speed and direction control system** using NodeMCU and L298N driver. Explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write the **Arduino firmware** to control a DC motor through Blynk app using three virtual pins (V0 = speed slider, V2 = forward button, V3 = reverse button, V4 = stop button). Include the circuit pin connections. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Block Diagram (5 marks) + Explanation (2 marks):** The system consists of **three layers**: 1. **Application Layer — Blynk Mobile App / Web Dashboard:** The user interacts with GUI widgets (slider, buttons) which send values to a Blynk cloud server over Wi-Fi. 2. **Network Layer — Blynk Cloud Server (MQTT/HTTP):** Receives widget events from the app and forwards them to the assigned virtual pin on the NodeMCU. Authentication is via a unique 32-character **auth token**. 3. **Perception Layer — NodeMCU + L298N + Motor:** - **NodeMCU** parses incoming virtual pin values. - It sets logic levels on **IN1, IN2** for direction and outputs **PWM on ENA** for speed. - **L298N** switches the 12 V motor supply accordingly. **Pin Connections Table (include in answer):** | NodeMCU Pin | L298N Pin | Purpose | |---|---|---| | D1 | IN1 | Direction bit 1 | | D2 | IN2 | Direction bit 2 | | D3 | ENA | PWM speed | | Vin | 5V | Logic supply | | GND | GND | Common ground | **(b) Firmware (7 marks):** ```cpp #include #include char ssid[] = "KTU_IoT_Lab"; char pass[] = "lab@1234"; char auth[] = "AbCdEfGhIjKlMnOpQrStUvWxYz123456"; #define IN1 D1 #define IN2 D2 #define ENA D3 int speedPWM = 0; void setup() { pinMode(IN1, OUTPUT); pinMode(IN2, OUTPUT); pinMode(ENA, OUTPUT); analogWriteFreq(1000); analogWriteRange(1023); Blynk.begin(auth, ssid, pass); stopMotor(); } BLYNK_WRITE(V0) { // Speed slider 0–1023 speedPWM = param.asInt(); analogWrite(ENA, speedPWM); } BLYNK_WRITE(V2) { // Forward digitalWrite(IN1, HIGH); digitalWrite(IN2, LOW); analogWrite(ENA, speedPWM); } BLYNK_WRITE(V3) { // Reverse digitalWrite(IN1, LOW); digitalWrite(IN2, HIGH); analogWrite(ENA, speedPWM); } BLYNK_WRITE(V4) { stopMotor(); } // Stop void stopMotor() { digitalWrite(IN1, LOW); digitalWrite(IN2, LOW); analogWrite(ENA, 0); speedPWM = 0; } void loop() { Blynk.run(); } ``` **Flow Explanation (for remaining marks):** - `Blynk.run()` is the heart of the loop — it must be called frequently to service cloud events. - `BLYNK_WRITE` callbacks are **interrupt-like** functions triggered only when the corresponding virtual pin is updated. - Speed value is preserved in a **global variable `speedPWM`** so direction changes do not reset speed. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain **PWM-based speed control of a DC motor** with the help of relevant waveforms and equations. How is it implemented on an ESP8266 NodeMCU? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Design a **local web-server based motor control** (no cloud) using NodeMCU. The webpage should have buttons for FORWARD, REVERSE, STOP, and a slider for speed. Provide the complete code and explain how a request like `http://192.168.1.45/forward?pwm=700` is handled. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) PWM Theory (7 marks):** A **Pulse Width Modulated** signal is a digital square wave with a fixed period $T_{period}$ but a variable high-time $T_{on}$. The **duty cycle** is: $$ D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{period}} \times 100 $$ **Waveform (verbal description in answer):** A series of pulses where the *ON duration* widens (right edge shifts right) as duty cycle increases from 0 % to 100 %. The motor, being an **inductive load**, integrates these pulses and behaves as if a lower DC voltage were applied. **Average voltage equation:** $$ V_{avg} = V_{supply} \times D $$ For a 12 V supply at 70 % duty cycle: $$ V_{avg} = 12 \times 0.70 = 8.4 \text{ V} $$ **ESP8266 implementation (3 marks):** - Use `analogWrite(pin, value)` where `value ∈ [0, 1023]` (10-bit). - Set PWM frequency via `analogWriteFreq(1000)` — 1 kHz is ideal for DC motors (above audible range, below switching losses). - `analogWriteRange(1023)` ensures full 10-bit resolution. ```cpp analogWriteFreq(1000); analogWriteRange(1023); analogWrite(D3, 700); // ~68% duty cycle ``` **(b) Web-Server Design (7 marks):** **Code:** ```cpp #include #define IN1 D1 #define IN2 D2 #define ENA D3 ESP8266WebServer server(80); const char* ssid = "KTU_WiFi"; const char* pass = "project2024"; void setup() { pinMode(IN1, OUTPUT); pinMode(IN2, OUTPUT); pinMode(ENA, OUTPUT); WiFi.begin(ssid, pass); while (WiFi.status() != WL_CONNECTED) delay(500); server.on("/forward", HTTP_GET, handleForward); server.on("/reverse", HTTP_GET, handleReverse); server.on("/stop", HTTP_GET, handleStop); server.on("/", HTTP_GET, handleRoot); server.begin(); } void handleForward() { int pwm = server.arg("pwm").toInt(); if (pwm == 0) pwm = 800; digitalWrite(IN1, HIGH); digitalWrite(IN2, LOW); analogWrite(ENA, pwm); server.send(200, "text/plain", "Motor FORWARD at " + String(pwm)); } void handleReverse() { int pwm = server.arg("pwm").toInt(); if (pwm == 0) pwm = 800; digitalWrite(IN1, LOW); digitalWrite(IN2, HIGH); analogWrite(ENA, pwm); server.send(200, "text/plain", "Motor REVERSE at " + String(pwm)); } void handleStop() { digitalWrite(IN1, LOW); digitalWrite(IN2, LOW); analogWrite(ENA, 0); server.send(200, "text/plain", "Motor STOPPED"); } void handleRoot() { String html = "

IoT Motor Control

" "" "" ""; server.send(200, "text/html", html); } void loop() { server.handleClient(); } ``` **Request Handling Explanation (2 marks):** When the user clicks FWD, the browser issues `GET /forward?pwm=700`. ESP8266WebServer's internal router invokes `handleForward()`. The query parameter `pwm` is read via `server.arg("pwm")`, converted to integer, and used as the PWM value. The function sets the H-Bridge direction bits and writes PWM, then returns a **200 OK** plain-text response to the client. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting common ground** between NodeMCU and 12 V adapter — motor "twitches" or does not run. **−1 mark.** > 2. **Not calling `Blynk.run()` inside `loop()`** — app controls become unresponsive. **−1 mark.** > 3. **Using `analogWrite` value > 255 on Arduino UNO** — silently caps at 255. Write 0–1023 only for ESP8266. **−0.5 mark.** > 4. **Drawing pin diagrams with crossed wires** — examiner deducts 1 mark for unclear diagram. > 5. **Writing only `digitalWrite` for speed control** — students forget PWM; you get **0/2** for the speed-control section. > 6. **Skipping the `analogWriteFreq(1000)` line** — without it, ESP8266 defaults to a different frequency; motor will whine audibly and may overheat. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-revision checklist — memorize before every KTU exam.** - ☐ **Three-layer IoT architecture** (Perception / Network / Application) — must appear in every 14-mark answer. - ☐ **NodeMCU PWM is 10-bit (0–1023)**; Arduino UNO PWM is 8-bit (0–255). Mixing these loses marks. - ☐ **L298N truth table** — IN1=1, IN2=0 → Forward; IN1=0, IN2=1 → Reverse; both 0 → Stop; both 1 → Brake. - ☐ **Common ground** is mandatory between NodeMCU and external motor supply. - ☐ **PWM frequency** for DC motors: **1 kHz** (use `analogWriteFreq(1000)`). - ☐ **Blynk auth token** is unique per project and must be embedded in firmware. - ☐ **MQTT broker** default port: **1883** (TLS: **8883**). - ☐ **Wi-Fi** on ESP8266/ESP32 works only on **2.4 GHz**, never 5 GHz. - ☐ **Flyback diodes** across motor terminals protect against back-EMF spikes. - ☐ **H-bridge** allows direction reversal using only logic-level signals. - ☐ **HTTP 200** = success; **404** = wrong URL; **500** = server crash. - ☐ **Local fallback button** ensures the system works even when Wi-Fi/cloud is down — a common 2-mark sub-question. - ☐ **Virtual pins V0–V255** in Blynk are used as logical channels between app and firmware. - ☐ **Telemetry direction**: motor → sensor → MCU → cloud → app (this is *reverse* control flow and is the *feedback path*). - ☐ The phrase **"Sense → Process → Actuate → Communicate"** impresses examiners — use it in introductions. - ☐ Mention **Power budget**: NodeMCU ≈ 70 mA, L298N logic ≈ 36 mA, motor up to 2 A. Total adapter must supply ≥ 2.5 A. - ☐ Always include a **circuit diagram OR functional block diagram** in 14-mark answers — it alone is worth 2–3 marks. - ☐ **NEVER** apply 5 V to a NodeMCU data pin — GPIO is **3.3 V tolerant** only. Use a logic-level shifter if needed. - ☐ For servo control, the PWM pulse width is **1 ms (0°) – 2 ms (180°)** at **50 Hz**, NOT the same as DC motor PWM. - ☐ **OTA (Over-the-Air) updates** can be mentioned as a future enhancement — it earns bonus appreciation marks. - ☐ Final line of any answer should mention **"real-world deployment challenges: EMI, power conditioning, enclosure design"** — shows examiner you understand beyond code.

topics.title: Project on Water Level Reaching Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT504 - PROGRAMMING FOR INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Project on Water Level Reaching Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Project on Water Level Reaching Control

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> An *IoT-based Water Level Reaching Control System* is a cyber-physical embedded system that uses non-contact/contact level sensors, a microcontroller with network connectivity, and electro-mechanical actuators to **autonomously monitor, transmit, and regulate the liquid level inside an overhead tank (OHT), sump, or reservoir** in real time, thereby preventing overflow (dry-running) and water wastage.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Watchman" of a Tank

Imagine a small security guard standing on top of your overhead water tank. He carries:
1. A **measuring tape** (the **Ultrasonic / Float Sensor**)
2. A **walkie-talkie** (the **Wi-Fi Module / ESP8266 / ESP32**)
3. A **hand on a switch** that starts or stops the motor (the **Relay + Pump**)
4. A **clipboard** that logs every reading (the **Cloud / Blynk / ThingSpeak Dashboard**)

When the water level drops below 25 %, the guard shouts "ON" to the motor. When it reaches 95 %, he shouts "OFF". He also calls the cloud every 2 seconds with the new level. That guard is your **microcontroller firmware running on NodeMCU/Arduino**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Syllabus Highlights (KTU 2024 — Module 4):**
> - Sensor interfacing (HC-SR04 Ultrasonic, Float Switch, Waterproof Probes)
> - Actuator control via **Relay Module (5 V coil, 230 V contact)**
> - **Blynk / ThingSpeak / MQTT** cloud integration
> - Threshold-based decision logic & hysteresis
> - Real-time **Serial + Web dashboard** visualization

### 1.2 Key Hardware & Software Components

| Block | Component | Typical Rating | Purpose |
|------|----------|----------------|---------|
| Sensing | HC-SR04 Ultrasonic | 5 V, 15 mA, 2–400 cm range | Non-contact distance measurement |
| Sensing | Float Switch (Magnetic) | 10 W, 250 V AC | Mechanical high/low level cut-off |
| Control | NodeMCU ESP8266 | 3.3 V, 80 mA | MCU + Wi-Fi in one chip |
| Switching | Single-Channel Relay | 5 V coil, 10 A NO/NC | Isolated 230 V motor control |
| Actuation | Submersible / Mono-block Pump | 0.5–1 HP, 230 V AC | Water fill / drain |
| Power | SMPS / Hi-Link HLK-PM01 | 230 V → 5 V, 3 W | Logic supply |
| Cloud | Blynk 2.0 / ThingSpeak | HTTPS/MQTT | Remote dashboard |
| Local UI | 16×2 LCD + I²C Backpack | 5 V, 20 mA | On-site display |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System Architecture (Layered Model)

Following the canonical **3-layer IoT reference model** prescribed in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

1. **Perception Layer (Sensors + Actuators)** → Raw analog/digital data acquisition.
2. **Network Layer (ESP8266 Wi-Fi + MQTT Broker)** → Secure transport using **TCP/IP port 1883 (MQTT)** or **port 80/443 (HTTP)**.
3. **Application Layer (Blynk / ThingSpeak / Custom Web App)** → Visualization, alerts, analytics.

## 2.2 Working Principle — Step by Step

1. The **HC-SR04** emits a 40 kHz ultrasonic burst via the *Trig* pin (≥ 10 µs HIGH pulse).
2. The burst travels through air, reflects off the water surface, and returns to the *Echo* pin.
3. The MCU measures the **pulse width** of the echo, which is proportional to round-trip time.
4. Distance is computed, then subtracted from the known **tank depth (D)** to obtain the water level.
5. The level (in cm and %) is published to the cloud **every T seconds** (typically T = 2 s).
6. **If** level < 25 % → Relay ON, pump starts.
7. **If** level > 95 % → Relay OFF, pump stops (anti-overflow safeguard).
8. **Hysteresis** is introduced (e.g., ON at 25 %, OFF at 95 %) to prevent relay *chattering*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Hysteresis?**
> Without a gap between ON and OFF thresholds, the relay would toggle 50+ times per second when the water level hovers around the threshold — burning out the contacts within hours. A 70 % gap is the **industry standard for domestic pumps**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---------|---------|------|---------|
| 1 | Speed of sound in air (20 °C) | $v = 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot T$ | m/s | **T** in °C |
| 2 | One-way distance (HC-SR04) | $d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$ | cm | Divide by 2 for round-trip |
| 3 | Water level (cm) | $L = D - d$ | cm | **D** = empty-tank distance |
| 4 | Water level (%) | $\% = \dfrac{L}{H} \times 100$ | % | **H** = total tank height |
| 5 | Tank volume | $V = \pi r^2 H$ (cylindrical) | m³ or L | **r** = radius |
| 6 | Pump power | $P = \rho g Q h$ | W | **Q** = discharge, **h** = head |
| 7 | MQTT keep-alive | $\text{KA} = 1.5 \times \text{latency}$ | s | Default 60 s |
| 8 | Sampling frequency | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Hz | **T** = publish interval |

> [!WARNING]
> The HC-SR04's **maximum reliable range is 4 m**; the **minimum blind zone is 2 cm**. For deeper tanks, use the **JSN-SR04T waterproof transducer** or a hydrostatic pressure sensor (e.g., *MPX5700AP*).

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Domestic:** Apartment overhead tanks, apartment sump automation.
- **Industrial:** Chemical reactor level control, cooling-tower make-up water.
- **Agricultural:** Drip-irrigation reservoirs, greenhouse hydroponics.
- **Smart City:** Storm-water sump monitoring, flood-alert systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Distance from Echo Pulse Width

The HC-SR04 works on the **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** principle.

**Step 1 — Trigger the sensor**

The MCU sends a HIGH pulse of duration $\geq 10\,\mu s$ on the *Trig* pin. The sensor internally fires **8 cycles of 40 kHz** ultrasound.

**Step 2 — Echo generation**

The reflected wavefront strikes the receiver, and the sensor sets the *Echo* pin HIGH for a duration equal to the round-trip time $t_r$.

**Step 3 — Relate time to distance**

The sound wave travels a **total round-trip distance** of $2d$ in time $t_r$ at speed $v$:

$$2d = v \cdot t_r$$

**Step 4 — Solve for one-way distance**

$$d = \dfrac{v \cdot t_r}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to cm (commonly used in code)**

If $t_r$ is measured in microseconds and $v \approx 0.0343\,\text{cm/µs}$ (speed of sound at 20 °C), then:

$$d_{\text{cm}} = \dfrac{t_r}{2 \times 29.1} = \dfrac{t_r}{58.2}$$

> The constant $58.2$ is the empirical divisor used in the popular `NewPing` and `HCSR04` Arduino libraries.

**Step 6 — Compensate for temperature**

Substituting the general expression:

$$d_{\text{cm}} = \dfrac{t_r \cdot (331.4 + 0.6 T)}{2 \times 10^{4}}$$

where $T$ is air temperature in °C and $t_r$ is in µs.

**Step 7 — Compute water level**

Given the empty-tank reference distance $D$ (sensor-to-bottom distance when tank is dry):

$$L_{\text{cm}} = D - d_{\text{cm}}$$

**Step 8 — Convert to percentage**

$$\%_{\text{full}} = \left(\dfrac{D - d_{\text{cm}}}{H}\right) \times 100$$

where $H$ is the *usable* tank height (e.g., excluding 10 cm air gap at the top).

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Given:**
- Tank total height $H = 150$ cm
- Empty-tank reference $D = 160$ cm (sensor is 10 cm above tank top)
- Air temperature $T = 30$ °C
- Measured echo pulse width $t_r = 4200$ µs

**Step 1 — Speed of sound at 30 °C**

$$v = 331.4 + 0.6 \times 30 = 349.4 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Convert speed to cm/µs**

$$v = \dfrac{349.4 \times 100}{10^{6}} = 0.03494 \text{ cm/µs}$$

**Step 3 — One-way distance**

$$d_{\text{cm}} = \dfrac{0.03494 \times 4200}{2} = 73.37 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 4 — Water level in cm**

$$L_{\text{cm}} = 160 - 73.37 = 86.63 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 5 — Percentage full**

$$\%_{\text{full}} = \left(\dfrac{86.63}{150}\right) \times 100 = 57.75 \,\%$$

**Step 6 — Control decision**

Since $25\% \leq 57.75\% \leq 95\%$, the pump state is **held unchanged** (within the dead-band).

## 3.3 Control Logic Truth Table

| Current Level (%) | Previous Pump State | Pump State (New) | Reason |
|-------------------|--------------------|------------------|--------|
| < 25              | OFF                | **ON**           | Below LOW threshold |
| 25 – 95           | ON / OFF           | **Unchanged**    | Inside hysteresis band |
| > 95              | ON                 | **OFF**          | Above HIGH threshold |
| > 95              | OFF                | OFF              | No change |
| Sensor timeout    | *                  | **OFF (fail-safe)** | Watchdog triggered |

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Sensor Choices)

| Criterion | HC-SR04 | JSN-SR04T | Float Switch | Pressure (MPX5700) |
|-----------|---------|-----------|--------------|--------------------|
| Contact with water | No | **Yes (IP67)** | Yes | Yes |
| Range / Span | 2–400 cm | 25–450 cm | Binary (2 points) | 0–7 m |
| Accuracy | ±3 mm | ±3 mm | ±5 mm | ±1.5 % FS |
| Cost (INR) | 80 | 350 | 60 | 600 |
| Corrosion risk | None | Low | Medium | High (stainless needed) |
| IoT-friendly analog output | No (digital) | No (digital) | No (digital) | **Yes (0–5 V analog)** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level System Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HC-SR04 Ultrasonic Sensor] -->|Trig/Echo Digital| B[NodeMCU ESP8266]
    C[Float Switch HIGH] -->|GPIO D6| B
    D[Float Switch LOW] -->|GPIO D7| B
    B -->|GPIO D1| E[5V Relay Module]
    E -->|230V AC Contact| F[Submersible Pump]
    B -->|I2C SDA/SCL| G[16x2 LCD Display]
    B -->|Wi-Fi 2.4 GHz| H[Home Router]
    H -->|MQTT 1883| I[Cloud Broker Blynk / ThingSpeak]
    I --> J[Mobile App Dashboard]
    I --> K[SMS / Email Alert]
    B -->|Serial 115200 baud| L[PC Serial Monitor for Debug]
```

## 4.2 Circuit Wiring Schematic (Logical View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph POWER[Power Supply Section]
        PS[230V AC Mains] --> SMPS[HLK-PM01 5V SMPS]
        SMPS --> V5[5V Rail]
        SMPS --> V33[3.3V LDO for ESP]
    end

    subgraph SENSE[Sensor Section]
        US[HC-SR04 VCC] --> V5
        US[Trig Pin] --> P1[NodeMCU D2 GPIO4]
        US[Echo Pin via 1k/2k divider] --> P2[NodeMCU D1 GPIO5]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL[Control Section]
        P3[NodeMCU D5 GPIO14] --> REL[Relay IN pin]
        REL --> COIL[5V Relay Coil]
        COIL --> V5
        REL --> NO[Normally Open Contact]
        NO --> MOTOR[230V Pump]
        MOTOR --> N[AC Neutral]
        PS --> L[AC Live] --> NO
    end

    subgraph CLOUD[Cloud Section]
        P4[NodeMCU TX/RX] --> USB[USB to PC for flashing]
        WIFI[ESP8266 Wi-Fi Radio] --> NET[Home Router 2.4 GHz]
        NET --> MQTT[MQTT Broker]
        MQTT --> APP[Mobile Dashboard]
    end
```

## 4.3 Data-Flow Sequence (Ultrasonic Read → Cloud Publish)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant MCU as NodeMCU
    participant US as HC-SR04
    participant REL as Relay
    participant PUMP as Water Pump
    participant MQTT as Blynk/ThingSpeak

    Note over MCU: setup() — connect Wi-Fi & MQTT
    loop Every 2 seconds
        MCU->>US: Trig HIGH for 10 µs
        US-->>MCU: Echo HIGH for t_r µs
        MCU->>MCU: Compute distance d = t_r/58.2
        MCU->>MCU: Level(%) = (D - d)/H * 100
        alt Level < 25%
            MCU->>REL: GPIO HIGH
            REL->>PUMP: Energize coil — motor ON
        else Level > 95%
            MCU->>REL: GPIO LOW
            REL->>PUMP: De-energize — motor OFF
        end
        MCU->>MQTT: Publish level to topic "tank/level"
        MQTT-->>MCU: PUBACK
    end
    Note over MCU,PUMP: If Wi-Fi lost → fail-safe OFF, log to EEPROM
```

## 4.4 State Machine of the Pump Controller

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IDLE
    IDLE --> FILLING: level < 25% AND pumpFault = false
    FILLING --> FULL: level > 95%
    FILLING --> IDLE: level between 25% and 95% (manual stop)
    FULL --> IDLE: level drops below 90% (hysteresis release)
    FILLING --> FAULT: motor current sensor trips
    FULL --> FAULT: level sensor timeout > 10s
    FAULT --> IDLE: reset button pressed
    FAULT --> FAULT: SMS alert sent every 5 min
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**List the major hardware and software components required to build an IoT-based water level reaching control system. Briefly state the role of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1.5 per component category):**

A complete IoT water level control system consists of the following blocks:

1. **Sensing block:** HC-SR04 ultrasonic transducer (or JSN-SR04T waterproof variant) and/or float switches — convert the physical water level into an electrical signal proportional to the distance from the sensor.
2. **Processing & communication block:** NodeMCU ESP8266 (or ESP32) microcontroller — executes the control algorithm (threshold comparison + hysteresis) and publishes data over Wi-Fi using **MQTT** or **HTTP** to the Blynk/ThingSpeak cloud.
3. **Actuation block:** 5 V single-channel relay module driving a 230 V AC submersible pump — electrically isolates the low-voltage logic from the high-voltage motor and switches the pump ON/OFF based on the MCU's GPIO command.
4. **Cloud / Application block:** Blynk 2.0 / ThingSpeak dashboard for remote monitoring, threshold configuration, and SMS/email alerts.
5. **Local UI & power:** 16×2 LCD (I²C) for on-site display and an SMPS (HLK-PM01) for 5 V regulated supply.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**Explain the concept of hysteresis in a water level control system. Why is it essential?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Definition (1 mark):* **Hysteresis** in a level-control system is the deliberate introduction of a *dead-band* (a gap) between the **LOW threshold** at which the pump turns ON and the **HIGH threshold** at which the pump turns OFF. For example, pump turns ON at 25 % and OFF at 95 %.

*Why essential (2 marks):*
1. **Prevents relay chattering** — without hysteresis, when the water level hovers around a single threshold (say 50 %), the relay would toggle ON/OFF dozens of times per second, welding the contacts within hours.
