2. **Reduces motor inrush wear** — each start draws 5–7× rated current; hysteresis limits starts to ≤ 4 per hour, extending motor life.
3. **Provides safety margin** — the upper threshold (95 %) leaves a 5 % air gap, preventing overflow.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**(a) Describe the working principle of the HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor with a neat labelled diagram. Derive the formula used to compute distance from the echo pulse width. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working principle (3 marks):**

The HC-SR04 operates on the **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** of ultrasonic waves:

1. The MCU drives the *Trig* pin HIGH for **≥ 10 µs**. Internally, the module emits **8 cycles of 40 kHz** ultrasound from the transmitter.
2. The sound wave propagates through air, strikes the water surface, and reflects back.
3. The receiver detects the echo and drives the *Echo* pin HIGH for a duration $t_r$ equal to the round-trip time.
4. The MCU measures $t_r$ using `pulseIn()` or hardware timer.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Let $v$ be the speed of sound in air, $d$ the one-way distance, and $t_r$ the round-trip time.

Round-trip distance equals $2d$:

$$2d = v \cdot t_r$$

Solving for $d$:

$$d = \dfrac{v \cdot t_r}{2}$$

For air at 20 °C, $v = 343\,\text{m/s} = 0.0343\,\text{cm/µs}$, giving the empirical working formula used in Arduino code:

$$d_{\text{cm}} = \dfrac{t_r}{58.2}$$

The water level inside the tank is then:

$$L_{\text{cm}} = D - d_{\text{cm}}$$

where $D$ is the calibrated empty-tank distance (sensor to bottom).

> [!NOTE]
> **Labelled block diagram:** Refer to *Section 4.2* — the *HC-SR04* node shows VCC, Trig, Echo, GND pins with the formula $d = vt_r/2$ annotation.

---

**(b) Write the complete Arduino/NodeMCU firmware to (i) read distance from HC-SR04, (ii) compute water level in %, and (iii) control a relay-connected pump with 25 % ON and 95 % OFF thresholds. Assume ESP8266, Blynk library, and a 150 cm tank. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```cpp
/* ---- IoT Water Level Reaching Control ---- */
/* Course: PBCIT504 - Programming for IoT (KTU 2024) */

#include <ESP8266WiFi.h>
#include <BlynkSimpleEsp8266.h>

#define TRIG_PIN   D2      // GPIO4
#define ECHO_PIN   D1      // GPIO5 (use 1k/2k voltage divider!)
#define RELAY_PIN  D5      // GPIO14
#define TANK_H     150.0   // cm — total usable height
#define EMPTY_D    160.0   // cm — sensor-to-bottom (empty)
#define LOW_TH     25      // % below this -> pump ON
#define HIGH_TH    95      // % above this -> pump OFF

char auth[] = "YOUR_BLYNK_TOKEN";
char ssid[] = "YourWiFi";
char pass[] = "YourPassword";

BlynkTimer timer;

float distanceCm = 0.0, levelPct = 0.0;
bool  pumpState  = false;

float readDistance() {
    digitalWrite(TRIG_PIN, LOW);  delayMicroseconds(2);
    digitalWrite(TRIG_PIN, HIGH); delayMicroseconds(10);
    digitalWrite(TRIG_PIN, LOW);
    long t = pulseIn(ECHO_PIN, HIGH, 30000UL);   // µs, timeout 30 ms
    if (t == 0) return -1.0;                     // out of range
    return (float)t / 58.2;                      // cm
}

void controlPump() {
    distanceCm = readDistance();
    if (distanceCm < 0) {                         // sensor fail-safe
        digitalWrite(RELAY_PIN, LOW);
        pumpState = false;
        Blynk.logEvent("sensor_fault", "HC-SR04 timeout!");
        return;
    }
    float levelCm = EMPTY_D - distanceCm;
    levelPct = constrain((levelCm / TANK_H) * 100.0, 0.0, 100.0);

    if (levelPct < LOW_TH && !pumpState) {
        digitalWrite(RELAY_PIN, HIGH);  pumpState = true;
    } else if (levelPct > HIGH_TH && pumpState) {
        digitalWrite(RELAY_PIN, LOW);   pumpState = false;
    }

    Blynk.virtualWrite(V0, levelPct);
    Blynk.virtualWrite(V1, pumpState ? 1 : 0);
    Serial.printf("Dist: %.1f cm | Level: %.1f %% | Pump: %s\n",
                  distanceCm, levelPct, pumpState ? "ON" : "OFF");
}

void setup() {
    Serial.begin(115200);
    pinMode(TRIG_PIN, OUTPUT);
    pinMode(ECHO_PIN, INPUT);
    pinMode(RELAY_PIN, OUTPUT);
    digitalWrite(RELAY_PIN, LOW);            // fail-safe OFF
    Blynk.begin(auth, ssid, pass);
    timer.setInterval(2000L, controlPump);   // every 2 s
}

void loop() {
    Blynk.run();
    timer.run();
}
```

**Mark-splitting rubric (7 marks):**

| Sub-task | Marks |
|---------|-------|
| Header, includes, pin definitions | 1 |
| `readDistance()` with pulseIn + 58.2 formula | 2 |
| Percentage conversion + threshold logic | 2 |
| Blynk publish + relay actuation + fail-safe | 1 |
| `setup()`/`loop()` + comments | 1 |

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4]`

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the 3-layer IoT reference architecture for the water level control project. Identify the protocols used in each layer. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The KTU 2024 syllabus prescribes the canonical **3-layer IoT architecture**:

**Layer 1 — Perception / Sensing Layer (2 marks):**
- Physical devices: HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor, float switches, relay, 230 V pump.
- Function: Convert physical water level into electrical signals.
- Protocols: **GPIO**, **PWM** (if analog sensors used), **I²C** (for LCD).

**Layer 2 — Network / Communication Layer (2.5 marks):**
- Gateway: **NodeMCU ESP8266** with **Wi-Fi 802.11 b/g/n** (2.4 GHz).
- Transport: **TCP/IP** stack; application protocols **MQTT (port 1883)** for lightweight pub/sub or **HTTP/HTTPS (port 80/443)** for REST.
- QoS levels: MQTT QoS 0/1/2 — at least QoS 1 recommended for control commands.

**Layer 3 — Application / Cloud Layer (2.5 marks):**
- Platforms: **Blynk 2.0**, **ThingSpeak**, **AWS IoT Core**, or self-hosted **Node-RED + Mosquitto**.
- Functions: real-time dashboard, threshold configuration, historical analytics, **SMS/email alerts via Twilio**.
- Data model: time-series (timestamp, level %, pump state, RSSI).

> Refer to the **system block diagram in Section 4.1** for the visual representation.

---

**(b) Design a complete project report outline for the IoT water level control system. Include the proposed circuit, BOM, cost estimate, and expected test results. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution Outline:**

1. **Title & Abstract** (0.5 mark) — 150-word synopsis of problem, solution, key result (e.g., 38 % water saving).
2. **Block Diagram & Working Principle** (1 mark) — reuse the diagrams in Section 4.1 and 4.2.
3. **Circuit Schematic** (1 mark) — include: 230 V live → SMPS → 5 V rail; sensor wiring with voltage divider on Echo; relay coil snubber (1N4007 flyback diode).
4. **Bill of Materials (BOM)** (1.5 marks):

| # | Item | Qty | Unit Price (INR) | Total (INR) |
|---|------|-----|------------------|-------------|
| 1 | NodeMCU ESP8266 V3 | 1 | 350 | 350 |
| 2 | HC-SR04 | 1 | 80 | 80 |
| 3 | 5V Single-Channel Relay | 1 | 70 | 70 |
| 4 | HLK-PM01 5V SMPS | 1 | 180 | 180 |
| 5 | 16×2 LCD + I²C | 1 | 220 | 220 |
| 6 | Jumper wires, terminal blocks, enclosure | — | — | 250 |
| 7 | Float switch (mechanical back-up) | 2 | 60 | 120 |
| | **Total** | | | **1270** |

5. **Firmware Flowchart** (1 mark) — Sequence diagram in Section 4.3.
6. **Test Plan & Expected Results** (1 mark):

| Test # | Scenario | Input | Expected Output | Pass Criteria |
|--------|---------|-------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | Empty tank | 150 cm | Level = 0 % | Pump ON |
| 2 | Half full | 75 cm | Level = 50 % | Pump unchanged |
| 3 | Full tank | 5 cm | Level = 100 % | Pump OFF |
| 4 | Wi-Fi outage | — | Fail-safe OFF, EEPROM log | No overflow |
| 5 | Cloud latency | — | < 3 s | Dashboard updates |

7. **Applications & Future Scope** (0.5 mark) — extend to multi-tank SCADA, solar-powered variant, LoRaWAN for rural deployment.

8. **Conclusion** (0.5 mark) — re-state outcome.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the voltage divider on the Echo pin** — the HC-SR04 outputs 5 V but ESP8266 GPIOs tolerate only **3.3 V**. Use a 1 kΩ + 2 kΩ divider or you will silently fry the chip. Many students lose 1–2 marks here.
> 2. **No fail-safe in the loop** — if `pulseIn()` returns 0 (out of range), the pump must default to OFF. A student who omits this loses 1 mark on "robustness".
> 3. **Using `delay()` inside the main loop** — this blocks the Blynk.run() call and disconnects the cloud. Use `BlynkTimer` instead.
> 4. **Confusing D2/D4 pin labels on NodeMCU** — D2 is GPIO4, D1 is GPIO5. Pin-mapping mistakes are the #1 reason code fails to compile in the lab.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **HC-SR04** measures distance via **Time-of-Flight** of 40 kHz ultrasound; formula: $d = vt_r/2$ ⇒ in cm: $d = t_r/58.2$.
- Water level $L = D - d$; Percentage $= (L/H) \times 100$.
- **Hysteresis thresholds**: 25 % ON, 95 % OFF — prevents relay chattering and motor inrush wear.
- **Voltage divider (1 kΩ + 2 kΩ)** is mandatory on the Echo pin to protect the **3.3 V** ESP8266 GPIO from the **5 V** sensor output.
- **Flyback diode (1N4007)** across the relay coil is mandatory to protect the MCU from back-EMF.
- **MQTT** is the preferred IoT protocol (port 1883, QoS ≥ 1) over HTTP for low-bandwidth, high-frequency telemetry.
- **Blynk virtual pins** V0 = level %, V1 = pump state, V2 = manual override button.
- **Fail-safe principle**: if sensor times out or Wi-Fi drops, the relay must default to **OFF** to prevent dry-run damage.
- **3-layer architecture**: Perception → Network (Wi-Fi/MQTT) → Application (Blynk/ThingSpeak).
- **Sampling interval** of 2 s is optimal; < 500 ms causes cloud throttling on free tiers.
- **Calibration step**: measure $D$ with the tank empty before deployment; hard-code into `EEPROM` for portability.
- **Optional enhancements**: solar power, LoRaWAN for off-grid, Node-RED dashboard, predictive ML-based water-usage forecasting.
- **Safety**: isolate the 230 V section from the 5 V logic using the relay's **optical isolation**; use a properly rated **MCB** in series with the pump.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project on Street Light Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT504 - PROGRAMMING FOR INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Project on Street Light Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Smart Street Light Control System** is an IoT-enabled embedded project that uses ambient light sensors, motion detectors, and a microcontroller to automatically switch ON/OFF street lights based on environmental conditions, while transmitting real-time status data to a cloud platform for remote monitoring, fault detection, and energy analytics.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for *PBCIT504 – Programming for Internet of Things*, this project is a **Model Implementation** of the **Sense → Process → Actuate → Communicate** IoT paradigm using a microcontroller (Arduino/NodeMCU/ESP32), physical sensors, actuators, and a **Wi-Fi-based cloud dashboard**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Night Watchman"**
> Imagine a watchman standing on a street at night. He carries a small lamp (sensor) that detects the sunlight level. When it gets dark AND he hears footsteps (motion), he switches ON the big street light. When the sun rises OR no one is around, he turns it OFF. The IoT version of this watchman reports everything back to a control room (cloud dashboard) so the city engineer can monitor all streetlights from his laptop — exactly like **MagicBricks** or **Surya Smart City** solutions deployed in modern smart cities.

### 1.3 Block-Level Functional Summary

The project integrates four IoT layers:

| Layer | Function | Component Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Perception Layer** | Sense light & motion | **LDR**, **PIR / IR Sensor** |
| **Network Layer** | Connect to internet | **ESP8266 / ESP32 Wi-Fi** |
| **Processing Layer** | Decision making | **Arduino / NodeMCU MCU** |
| **Application Layer** | Visualize data | **Blynk / ThingSpeak / Firebase** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Examiner Vocabulary:** You must use the exact phrases — *Ambient Light Sensing (ALS)*, *Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) for dimming*, *Cloud Telemetry*, and *Edge Decision Making* — to score full marks in the descriptive section.

### 1.4 Key Project Specifications (Standard Metrics)

- **Operating Voltage:** **5 V DC** (logic) / **230 V AC** (load via relay)
- **LDR Dark Resistance:** ~**1 MΩ**, Light Resistance: ~**10–20 kΩ**
- **Communication Protocol:** **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)**
- **Cloud Latency Tolerance:** < **2 seconds**
- **Energy Saving Target:** **40–60%** compared to conventional always-ON lights

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — Step-by-Step Logic

The system follows a closed-loop IoT control flow. Each step is justified for a *Why* and *How* understanding:

1. **Step 1 — Sense Ambient Light**
   - *Why:* Conventional streetlights waste power by running during daylight.
   - *How:* An **LDR** forms a **voltage divider** with a fixed resistor. As light intensity (Lux) increases, LDR resistance falls, changing the analog output voltage read by the **ADC** of the microcontroller.

2. **Step 2 — Sense Presence (Vehicle / Pedestrian)**
   - *Why:* To enable *dynamic dimming* and prevent lighting empty roads at full brightness.
   - *How:* A **PIR sensor** detects infrared radiation change (body heat). An **IR proximity sensor** detects vehicle proximity. Output is a **digital HIGH/LOW** signal.

3. **Step 3 — Edge Processing (MCU Decision)**
   - *Why:* Reduces cloud dependency and response latency.
   - *How:* The NodeMCU executes an `if-else` logic gate:
     - IF (Lux < threshold) **AND** (motion == HIGH) → **LED ON**
     - IF (Lux > threshold) **OR** (motion == LOW for *t* seconds) → **LED OFF / DIM**

4. **Step 4 — Actuation**
   - *Why:* To physically switch the AC street lamp.
   - *How:* The MCU drives a **5V Relay Module** through a **transistor (BC547)** and **flyback diode (1N4007)**. For dimming, a **MOSFET (IRF540N)** driven by **PWM** is used.

5. **Step 5 — Cloud Telemetry**
   - *Why:* Enables remote monitoring and fault alerts.
   - *How:* The NodeMCU publishes JSON payloads `{lamp_id, lux, motion, status, timestamp}` over **MQTT** to a broker (e.g., HiveMQ, Adafruit IO). A dashboard subscribes and renders the data.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table verbatim — KTU 2024 Part B (14-mark) questions frequently test these exact relations.

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{out} = V_{cc} \times \dfrac{R_{fixed}}{R_{fixed} + R_{LDR}}$ | Voltage divider output | Volts (V) | LDR analog read |
| 2 | $R_{LDR} \approx R_0 \left(\dfrac{L}{L_0}\right)^{-n}$ | LDR resistance vs Lux | Ω | LDR modeling |
| 3 | $Lux = \dfrac{ADC_{value} \times V_{ref}}{1024} \times Calibration\_Factor$ | ADC → Lux | Lux | Light calibration |
| 4 | $P_{saved} = \left(1 - \dfrac{T_{ON\_smart}}{T_{ON\_normal}}\right) \times 100\%$ | % Power saved | % | Energy audit |
| 5 | $E_{saved} = P_{rated} \times H_{saved} \times N_{lamps} \times 365$ | Annual energy saved | kWh/year | ROI calculation |
| 6 | $f_{PWM} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{N \times (TOP+1)}$ | PWM frequency | Hz | LED dimming |
| 7 | $Duty\_Cycle = \dfrac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON}+T_{OFF}} \times 100\%$ | PWM duty | % | Brightness control |
| 8 | $I_{coil} = \dfrac{V_{cc} - V_{CE(sat)}}{R_{base} \times \beta}$ | Transistor base current | mA | Relay driver |
| 9 | $T_{latency} = T_{sense} + T_{process} + T_{comm}$ | End-to-end delay | ms | Real-time guarantee |
| 10 | $MQTT\_Payload_{size} = N_{fields} \times Avg\_field\_size + Header$ | MQTT packet size | bytes | Bandwidth planning |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitfall Note for KTU Exam:** Never use the symbol `|` (vertical bar) inside the table. Use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX when writing absolute values, e.g., write $\vert V_{out} \vert$ instead of $|V_{out}|$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Cities Mission (India):** Deployed in **Pune, Jaipur, Bhopal** under the SCM platform.
- **ROI Payback Period:** Typically **18–24 months** for a 100-lamp cluster.
- **Industrial Use:** Adaptive lighting in **tunnels, airports, parking lots**.
- **Standards:** Conforms to **IS 1944 (India)** and **IEC 60598** for luminaire safety.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — LDR Voltage Divider Output

**Problem:** Given $V_{cc} = 5\,V$, $R_{fixed} = 10\,k\Omega$, and LDR resistance in dark = $1\,M\Omega$, find the analog output voltage in dark and bright (LDR = $10\,k\Omega$) conditions.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Dark Condition: } R_{LDR} &= 1 \times 10^6 \, \Omega \\
V_{out,dark} &= V_{cc} \times \frac{R_{fixed}}{R_{fixed} + R_{LDR}} \\
&= 5 \times \frac{10 \times 10^3}{10 \times 10^3 + 1 \times 10^6} \\
&= 5 \times \frac{10^4}{1.01 \times 10^6} \\
&= 5 \times 9.9009 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 0.0495 \, V \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bright Condition: } R_{LDR} &= 10 \times 10^3 \, \Omega \\
V_{out,bright} &= 5 \times \frac{10 \times 10^3}{10 \times 10^3 + 10 \times 10^3} \\
&= 5 \times \frac{1}{2} = 2.5 \, V \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic Row:** The voltage is low in dark (sensor reads ~0.05 V) and rises to ~2.5 V in bright sunlight. The MCU threshold is set at **~1.2 V** (Lux ≈ 200) to trigger lamp activation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Annual Energy Savings Calculation

**Problem:** A colony has **50 streetlamps** rated at **150 W** each. Conventional system runs **12 hours/night × 365 days**. Smart system runs **6 hours full + 4 hours dimmed (50%) + 2 hours OFF**. Compute annual kWh saved.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{conventional} &= 50 \times 150 \, W \times 12 \, h \times 365 \\
&= 50 \times 0.15 \, kW \times 4380 \, h \\
&= 32{,}850 \, kWh/year \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{smart} &= 50 \times [150 \times 6 + 75 \times 4 + 0 \times 2] \times 365 / 1000 \\
&= 50 \times [900 + 300 + 0] \times 365 / 1000 \\
&= 50 \times 1200 \times 365 / 1000 \\
&= 21{,}900 \, kWh/year \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{saved} &= E_{conventional} - E_{smart} = 32{,}850 - 21{,}900 = 10{,}950 \, kWh/year \\
\% \text{ Saving} &= \frac{10{,}950}{32{,}850} \times 100 = 33.33\% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always show units in every line. Examiners deduct 1 mark for missing units in derivations.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — PWM Frequency for LED Dimming

**Problem:** NodeMCU ESP8266 timer is 16-bit, prescaler $N=1$, clock $f_{clk}=80\,MHz$. Find PWM frequency for $TOP=999$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{PWM} &= \frac{f_{clk}}{N \times (TOP+1)} \\
&= \frac{80 \times 10^6}{1 \times (999 + 1)} \\
&= \frac{80 \times 10^6}{1000} \\
&= 80{,}000 \, Hz = 80 \, kHz \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic Row:** 80 kHz is above the human flicker fusion threshold (~100 Hz), giving smooth dimming. The duty cycle is then varied between 0–100% using `analogWrite(pin, value)` where value ranges 0–1023.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Component Selection Trade-Off

| Component | Alternative A | Alternative B | Why A Wins for This Project |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MCU** | Arduino Uno + ESP-01 | NodeMCU ESP8266 (single board) | Built-in Wi-Fi, fewer modules, lower BOM cost |
| **Light Sensor** | LDR (Analog) | BH1750 (Digital I²C) | LDR is cheaper; BH1750 is more accurate |
| **Motion Sensor** | PIR HC-SR501 | Ultrasonic HC-SR04 | PIR detects humans, ultrasonic detects any object |
| **Switching** | Electromagnetic Relay | Solid State Relay (SSR) | SSR has longer life; Relay is cheaper for prototype |
| **Cloud** | Blynk | ThingSpeak | Blynk gives real-time control; ThingSpeak gives analytics |

## 3.5 Arduino Sketch (NodeMCU ESP8266)

```cpp
// Street Light Control - KTU PBCIT504 Module 4 Project
#include <ESP8266WiFi.h>
#include <PubSubClient.h>

const char* ssid     = "KTU_IoT_Lab";
const char* password = "project2024";
const char* mqtt_server = "broker.hivemq.com";

WiFiClient espClient;
PubSubClient client(espClient);

#define LDR_PIN    A0
#define PIR_PIN    D6
#define RELAY_PIN  D1
#define LUX_THRESH 500

void setup() {
  pinMode(LDR_PIN, INPUT);
  pinMode(PIR_PIN, INPUT);
  pinMode(RELAY_PIN, OUTPUT);
  Serial.begin(115200);
  WiFi.begin(ssid, password);
  while (WiFi.status() != WL_CONNECTED) delay(500);
  client.setServer(mqtt_server, 1883);
}

void loop() {
  if (!client.connected()) reconnect();
  client.loop();

  int ldrRaw   = analogRead(LDR_PIN);
  int motion   = digitalRead(PIR_PIN);
  int lux      = map(ldrRaw, 0, 1023, 0, 1000);
  bool lampOn  = (lux < LUX_THRESH && motion == HIGH);

  digitalWrite(RELAY_PIN, lampOn ? HIGH : LOW);

  char payload[120];
  snprintf(payload, sizeof(payload),
           "{\"lamp\":\"SL_01\",\"lux\":%d,\"motion\":%d,\"state\":\"%s\"}",
           lux, motion, lampOn ? "ON" : "OFF");
  client.publish("ktu/streetlight/SL_01", payload);
  delay(2000);
}

void reconnect() {
  while (!client.connected()) {
    if (client.connect("NodeMCU_SL_01")) {
      client.subscribe("ktu/streetlight/cmd");
    } else { delay(5000); }
  }
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> The code is intentionally written in **C/C++ for Arduino IDE** since PBCIT504 is a programming course. No Python is used in non-CS subjects elsewhere.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sunlight Source] --> B[LDR Sensor]
    P[Human / Vehicle] --> C[PIR Sensor]
    B --> D[NodeMCU ESP8266 MCU]
    C --> D
    D --> E{Relay Driver BC547}
    E --> F[230V AC Street Lamp]
    D -- MQTT over WiFi --> G[HiveMQ Cloud Broker]
    G --> H[Blynk Mobile Dashboard]
    G --> I[ThingSpeak Analytics]
    D --> J[Status LED Indicator]
```

## 4.2 Project Development Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Project Init]) --> A1[Define Requirements]
    A1 --> A2[Select Components]
    A2 --> A3[Circuit Design on Tinkercad]
    A3 --> A4{Simulation OK?}
    A4 -- No --> A3
    A4 -- Yes --> A5[Write Embedded C Code]
    A5 --> A6[Upload to NodeMCU]
    A6 --> A7[Configure MQTT Broker]
    A7 --> A8[Build Blynk Dashboard]
    A8 --> A9[Hardware Assembly on Breadboard]
    A9 --> A10[Field Test at Night]
    A10 --> A11[Document & Submit Report]
    A11 --> A12([Project Complete])
```

## 4.3 Circuit Schematic (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Power_Supply [Power Supply 5V DC]
        V1[USB 5V] --> V2[AMS1117 3.3V Regulator]
        V2 --> V3[NodeMCU VIN Pin]
    end

    subgraph Sensor_Array [Sensor Array]
        L1[LDR] --> J1[Voltage Divider 10k ohm]
        J1 --> A1[Analog Pin A0]
        P1[PIR HC-SR501] --> A2[Digital Pin D6]
    end

    subgraph Control_Unit [NodeMCU ESP8266]
        A1 --> MCU1[10-bit ADC]
        A2 --> MCU1
        MCU1 --> LOGIC[Decision Engine]
        LOGIC --> PWM[PWM Generator]
    end

    subgraph Actuator_Stage [Actuator Stage]
        LOGIC --> T1[Transistor BC547 Base]
        T1 --> R1[Relay Coil 5V]
        R1 --> D1[Flyback Diode 1N4007]
        R1 --> K1[Relay Common Pole]
        K1 --> LAMP[230V AC LED Lamp]
    end

    subgraph Cloud_Link [Cloud Telemetry]
        MCU1 --> WIFI[Wi-Fi Radio]
        WIFI --> MQTT[MQTT Publish]
        MQTT --> BROKER[HiveMQ Broker]
        BROKER --> APP[Blynk / ThingSpeak App]
    end
```

## 4.4 State Transition Diagram (Lamp State Machine)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> OFF
    OFF --> DIMMING: Lux < 500 AND motion detected
    DIMMING --> ON: Motion persists > 5 sec
    ON --> DIMMING: No motion for 30 sec
    ON --> OFF: Lux > 500 (Sunrise)
    DIMMING --> OFF: Lux > 500 (Sunrise)
    OFF --> [*]
```

## 4.5 Component Placement Table (Pin Map)

| NodeMCU Pin | Connected To | Mode | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A0** | LDR Voltage Divider | Analog In | Ambient Light Read |
| **D6** | PIR OUT | Digital In | Motion Detection |
| **D1** | Relay IN | Digital Out | Lamp Switch |
| **D2** | Status LED | Digital Out | Visual Indicator |
| **D5** | PWM MOSFET Gate | PWM Out | Dimming Control |
| **VIN** | 5V DC Supply | Power | Main Supply |
| **GND** | Common Ground | Power | Reference |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** 
*List the major components required to build an IoT-based smart street light control system and state the role of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **LDR (Light Dependent Resistor):** Acts as ambient light sensor. (1 mark)
2. **PIR / IR Sensor:** Detects presence of vehicles/pedestrians. (1 mark)
3. **NodeMCU ESP8266:** Acts as microcontroller with built-in Wi-Fi for processing & cloud communication. (1 mark)

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Differentiate between MQTT and HTTP protocols in the context of IoT communication.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | MQTT | HTTP |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture | Publish-Subscribe | Request-Response |
| Header Size | 2 bytes | ~500+ bytes |
| Power Use | Very Low | High |
| Suitability | Ideal for IoT (low bandwidth) | Heavy web apps |

MQTT is preferred for IoT streetlight telemetry due to low overhead, push-based delivery, and reliable QoS levels. (3 marks distributed across explanation)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
> 
> **(a)** Design an IoT-based street light control system using NodeMCU. Draw the system block diagram and explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Write the Arduino sketch to read LDR and PIR sensor data, and publish it to an MQTT broker. Explain each section. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The system consists of **5 functional blocks**:

1. **Sensing Layer:** LDR and PIR sensors capture ambient light and motion. Output: analog voltage (LDR) and digital HIGH/LOW (PIR). (1.5 marks)

2. **Processing Layer:** NodeMCU ESP8266 runs the decision algorithm using `analogRead()` and `digitalRead()`. (1.5 marks)

3. **Communication Layer:** Built-in 802.11 b/g/n Wi-Fi module uses MQTT protocol to publish JSON data. (1.5 marks)

4. **Actuation Layer:** A 5V relay (driven by BC547 transistor) switches the 230V AC lamp. A flyback diode protects against back-EMF. (1.5 marks)

5. **Application Layer:** Blynk/ThingSpeak dashboard receives data and displays real-time lamp status, lux value, and motion events. (1 mark)

*(See Section 4.3 for the full circuit diagram referenced.)*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

```cpp
#include <ESP8266WiFi.h>
#include <PubSubClient.h>
// [Full code from Section 3.5 — awarded 4 marks for code]
```
- **`setup()`:** Initializes pins, Wi-Fi, and MQTT broker connection — **1 mark**
- **`loop()`:** Reads LDR via ADC, PIR via digital pin, applies threshold logic, drives relay, publishes JSON — **1.5 marks**
- **`reconnect()`:** Handles MQTT reconnection for resilience — **0.5 marks**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
> 
> **(a)** Explain the working principle of LDR. Derive the voltage divider equation used to interface it with the NodeMCU ADC. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A streetlight colony has **80 lamps** rated at **100 W** each. The smart system runs the lamps for **8 hours at 50% dimmed (50 W)** and **4 hours at full (100 W)** per day, instead of **12 hours full ON** in the conventional system. Calculate the annual energy saved in kWh and the percentage savings. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

LDR (Light Dependent Resistor) is a passive component whose resistance **decreases** as incident light intensity **increases** (photoconductive principle). The relationship is given by:

$$
R_{LDR} = R_0 \left(\frac{L}{L_0}\right)^{-n}
$$

where $R_0$ is resistance at reference Lux $L_0$, and $n$ is a material constant (~0.7–0.9 for CdS). (2 marks)

The voltage divider equation derivation (as in Section 3.1) is reproduced. (3 marks)

ADC reading converts analog voltage to 10-bit digital value (0–1023), then mapped to Lux using:

$$
Lux = \frac{ADC \times V_{ref}}{1024} \times K_{cal}
$$

where $V_{ref} = 3.3\,V$ for NodeMCU and $K_{cal}$ is the sensor-specific calibration constant. (2 marks)

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{conventional} &= 80 \times 100\,W \times 12\,h \times 365 / 1000 \\
&= 80 \times 0.1 \times 4380 \\
&= 35{,}040 \, kWh/year \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{smart} &= 80 \times [50 \times 8 + 100 \times 4] \times 365 / 1000 \\
&= 80 \times [400 + 400] \times 365 / 1000 \\
&= 80 \times 800 \times 365 / 1000 \\
&= 23{,}360 \, kWh/year \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{saved} &= 35{,}040 - 23{,}360 = 11{,}680 \, kWh/year \\
\% \text{ Saving} &= \frac{11{,}680}{35{,}040} \times 100 = 33.33\% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

(7 marks distributed: 2 + 2 + 1.5 + 1.5)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones — Read Carefully Before Exam:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting Units:** Writing "150 W × 12 h" without converting to kWh costs 0.5–1 mark. Always write `150 W × 12 h / 1000 = 1.8 kWh`.
> 
> 2. **Missing Block Diagram:** In 7-mark design questions, examiners allocate **2–3 marks purely for the diagram**. Drawing the system block diagram is **mandatory** — not optional.
> 
> 3. **Conflating Sensors:** Writing "PIR measures light" or "LDR detects motion" is an instant 0.5-mark deduction. Be precise: **LDR = light, PIR = motion**.
> 
> 4. **No Edge vs Cloud Distinction:** The KTU 2024 scheme specifically tests the difference between *edge processing* (decision on MCU) and *cloud analytics* (visualization on dashboard). Mention both explicitly.
> 
> 5. **Incorrect MQTT Port:** Students often write port `80` (HTTP) instead of `1883` (default MQTT). Examiners catch this.
> 
> 6. **Not Mentioning Safety:** 230V AC handling requires a **flyback diode, optocoupler isolation, and proper enclosure**. Skipping safety aspects in the design part loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Project Goal:** Automatic, sensor-driven, IoT-monitored street lighting.
- ✅ **Sensors:** LDR (light) + PIR/IR (motion) — never interchange them.
- ✅ **MCU Choice:** NodeMCU ESP8266 (built-in Wi-Fi) preferred over Arduino Uno + ESP-01.
- ✅ **Voltage Divider:** $V_{out} = V_{cc} \times \dfrac{R_{fixed}}{R_{fixed} + R_{LDR}}$ — must derive in exam.
- ✅ **Threshold:** Lux < 500 (dark) + motion HIGH → Lamp ON.
- ✅ **Relay Driver:** Always use **transistor + flyback diode** to protect MCU.
- ✅ **Dimming:** Use **PWM** with MOSFET (IRF540N), not relay.
- ✅ **Protocol:** **MQTT (port 1883)** for IoT, not HTTP.
- ✅ **Cloud Options:** Blynk (control), ThingSpeak (analytics), Firebase (database).
- ✅ **Energy Saving:** Typically **30–60%** over conventional systems.
- ✅ **Safety:** Optocoupler isolation for 230V, IP65 enclosure for outdoor use.
- ✅ **Standards:** IS 1944, IEC 60598, IEEE 1451 (smart transducer).
- ✅ **Exam Pattern:** Part A = 3 marks (definition/component), Part B = 14 marks (design + calculation).

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Project on Street Light Control**
